<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition and Necessity (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT). List any **four** limitations of conventional secondary treatment that necessitate the deployment of AWT systems.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT) is defined as the ensemble of physical, chemical, biological, and membrane-based processes applied **subsequent to conventional secondary treatment** to produce a polished effluent that meets stringent discharge standards or reuse requirements.

**Limitations of Conventional Treatment (4 × 0.5 = 2 Marks):**
1. **Inadequate Nutrient Removal:** Conventional activated sludge removes only 20–40% of total nitrogen and 10–30% of total phosphorus, failing to prevent eutrophication.
2. **No Removal of Refractory Organics:** Micropollutants like pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors, and dyes pass through secondary treatment undegraded.
3. **Ineffective TDS Reduction:** Conventional treatment does not remove dissolved solids, restricting reuse in industrial and potable applications.
4. **Limited Pathogen Destruction:** Disinfection is typically partial; viruses and protozoan cysts may survive secondary treatment.

---

### Question 2: Classification (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Classify Advanced Wastewater Treatment technologies on the basis of **(a) the principle of operation** and **(b) the target contaminant class**. Give **two examples** for each category.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification by Principle of Operation (1.5 Marks):**
| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| Physical / Mechanical | Microfiltration, Dissolved Air Flotation |
| Chemical | Chemical Precipitation, Advanced Oxidation Processes |
| Biological | Membrane Bioreactor (MBR), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR) |
| Membrane-based | Reverse Osmosis (RO), Nanofiltration (NF) |

**(b) Classification by Target Contaminant (1.5 Marks):**
| Target Contaminant | Examples |
|---|---|
| Suspended Solids / Colloids | MF, UF, Multimedia Filtration |
| Dissolved Organics | Activated Carbon Adsorption, AOPs |
| Dissolved Inorganics / TDS | Reverse Osmosis, Ion Exchange |
| Microorganisms | UV, Ozonation, Chlorination |
| Nutrients (N & P) | Biological Nutrient Removal, Chemical Precipitation |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: Multi-Barrier AWT Train Design (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** A secondary effluent from a domestic STP has the following characteristics:
- BOD₅ = 30 mg/L
- COD = 120 mg/L
- TSS = 35 mg/L
- Total Nitrogen (TN) = 40 mg/L
- Total Phosphorus (TP) = 6 mg/L
- Fecal Coliform = 1 × 10⁵ MPN/100 mL

The water is to be reused for industrial cooling tower makeup. The target quality is:
- BOD₅ < 5 mg/L
- TSS < 1 mg/L
- TN < 10 mg/L
- TP < 0.5 mg/L
- Fecal Coliform < 2.2 MPN/100 mL

**(a)** Propose a **multi-barrier AWT train** of at least 3 stages. Justify the choice of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the individual removal efficiencies of the chosen stages for BOD₅ are 60%, 90%, and 99.5%, calculate the **overall removal efficiency and the final effluent BOD₅**. Also compute the **Log Removal Value (LRV)** for BOD. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Proposed AWT Train (7 Marks):**

| Stage | Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coagulation-Flocculation followed by Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | Removes residual TSS and a major fraction of TP via chemical precipitation with alum/poly-aluminum chloride (PACl). Reduces TSS to ~10 mg/L and TP to ~1 mg/L. |
| 2 | **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** with UF | Combines biological BOD reduction (activated sludge at high MLSS ~8000–12000 mg/L) with physical UF membrane. Achieves TSS < 1 mg/L, BOD < 5 mg/L, and partial nitrification (NH₄⁺-N to NO₃⁻-N). |
| 3 | **Biological Denitrification + Anammox** (or MBBR with anoxic zone) | Converts NO₃⁻-N to N₂ gas, achieving TN < 10 mg/L. |
| 4 | **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | Final polishing barrier — removes residual TDS, refractory organics, and remaining nutrients. |
| 5 | **UV Disinfection (40 mJ/cm²)** | Provides 4-log virus and 3-log bacterial inactivation, meeting the Fecal Coliform target. |

**Engineering Justification (3 marks):** The cooling tower makeup requires low TDS, low hardness, low silica, and microbiological control. The above train uses **multi-barrier redundancy** ensuring that failure of any single stage does not breach the reuse standard. RO concentrate can be sent to a brine recovery unit (Module 4 / 5 topic).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\eta_1 = 60\% = 0.60$ (Coagulation-DAF)
- $\eta_2 = 90\% = 0.90$ (MBR)
- $\eta_3 = 99.5\% = 0.995$ (RO)
- $C_0 = 30$ mg/L (Initial BOD₅)

**Step 1: Overall Removal Efficiency**

Using the multi-stage formula:
$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)(1 - \eta_3)$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.60)(1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.995)$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - (0.40)(0.10)(0.005)$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 1 - 0.0002 = 0.9998$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 99.98\%}$$

**Step 2: Final Effluent BOD₅**

$$C_{final} = C_0 \times (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)(1 - \eta_3)$$

$$C_{final} = 30 \times 0.40 \times 0.10 \times 0.005$$

$$C_{final} = 30 \times 0.0002 = 0.006 \; \text{mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{C_{final} = 0.006 \; \text{mg/L} \ll 5 \; \text{mg/L} \; \text{target}}$$

**Step 3: Log Removal Value (LRV)**

$$LRV = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_{final}}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{30}{0.006}\right)$$

$$LRV = \log_{10}(5000) = 3.699$$

$$\boxed{LRV = 3.7}$$

**Conclusion:** The proposed AWT train achieves 99.98% BOD removal with a 3.7 log reduction, comfortably meeting the < 5 mg/L target with a safety margin of nearly 3 orders of magnitude.

---

### Question B: Membrane Technology Classification and Application (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** **(a)** Classify membrane-based advanced wastewater treatment technologies based on **pore size and operating pressure**. Tabulate the **rejection characteristics, operating pressure range, and target contaminants** for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A textile industry wastewater has TDS = 8000 mg/L and COD = 1500 mg/L. The desired reuse is **process water with TDS < 200 mg/L** and **COD < 50 mg/L**. Propose a suitable **membrane-based AWT train**. If an RO membrane has a **rejection coefficient R = 0.99**, calculate the **permeate TDS**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Membrane Classification Table (7 Marks):**

| Membrane Process | Pore Size (µm) | Operating Pressure (bar) | Target Contaminants | Typical Rejection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 | 0.1 – 0.5 | TSS, Bacteria, Turbidity | TSS 90–99% |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 0.01 – 0.1 | 0.5 – 5 | Colloids, Viruses, Macromolecules | Colloids 99–99.9% |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 0.001 – 0.01 | 5 – 20 | Divalent ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻), Dyes, Organics | Multivalent ions 80–95% |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.001 | 10 – 80 | Monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻), TDS, Salts | TDS 95–99.8% |

**Engineering Notes (to be added by student for full marks):**
- MF/UF use **sieving mechanism**; NF/RO use **solution-diffusion** with charge effects.
- The order of increasing pressure is **MF < UF < NF < RO**, with corresponding decrease in pore size.

**Part (b) — Proposed Membrane AWT Train (4 Marks):**

For textile wastewater with high TDS and COD, the train would be:

**Coagulation-Flocculation (for color and colloidal COD reduction) → MBR with UF (for BOD/COD reduction to < 50 mg/L) → Cartridge Filter (5 µm) → High-Pressure RO (for TDS reduction to < 200 mg/L) → UV Disinfection**

**Step 1: Calculate Permeate TDS Using the Rejection Formula (3 Marks)**

The rejection coefficient is defined as:
$$R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{permeate}}{C_{feed}}$$

Rearranging:
$$C_{permeate} = C_{feed} (1 - R)$$

Substituting values: $C_{feed} = 8000$ mg/L, $R = 0.99$:

$$C_{permeate} = 8000 \times (1 - 0.99)$$

$$C_{permeate} = 8000 \times 0.01 = 80 \; \text{mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{C_{permeate} = 80 \; \text{mg/L} \ll 200 \; \text{mg/L} \; \text{target}}$$

**Conclusion:** The RO membrane with 99% rejection produces a permeate TDS of 80 mg/L, well below the 200 mg/L target. For COD, the preceding MBR-UF stage must reduce COD from 1500 mg/L to < 50 mg/L (i.e., > 96.7% removal) — typically achievable with a well-designed MBR.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU 2024 Examiners Penalize (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing AWT with Tertiary Treatment:** Tertiary treatment is a *subset* of AWT. AWT includes tertiary + quaternary + sludge/residue treatment. Examiners deduct marks for using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Wrong Formula Application:** Many students compute overall removal efficiency as $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_3$. This is **INCORRECT**. The correct formula is multiplicative: $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod(1 - \eta_i)$.
> 3. **Mixing Units:** Always express concentrations in mg/L (or ppm), flux in LMH, and pressure in bar — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Omitting Justification:** When proposing an AWT train, students list processes without justifying the choice. Always tie the technology to the **target contaminant** and **removal mechanism**.
> 5. **Forgetting Byproducts:** RO generates brine (~20–30% of feed); DAF/Coagulation generates sludge. Failure to mention waste streams in design answers leads to loss of 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Topic: Classification of AWT Technologies**

- ✅ **Definition of AWT:** Treatment **beyond conventional secondary** to meet stringent discharge/reuse standards.
- ✅ **Five Classification Axes:** Mechanism, Target Contaminant, Treatment Stage, Phase, and Mode of Operation.
- ✅ **Four Membrane Processes (in order):** MF (0.1–10 µm) → UF (0.01–0.1 µm) → NF (0.001–0.01 µm) → RO (< 0.001 µm).
- ✅ **Key AWT Targets:** Refractory organics, nutrients (N & P), TDS, pathogens, heavy metals.
- ✅ **Core Formula 1 (Removal Efficiency):** $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Core Formula 2 (Multi-Stage):** $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - \eta_i)$
- ✅ **Core Formula 3 (Rejection):** $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}$
- ✅ **Core Formula 4 (LRV):** $LRV = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$
- ✅ **Multi-Barrier Philosophy:** No single technology can address all contaminants; real AWT plants use 2–5 processes in series.
- ✅ **Biological AWT Examples:** MBR, MBBR, SBR, BAF, Anammox, Constructed Wetland.
- ✅ **Chemical AWT Examples:** Chemical precipitation, Fenton, Ozonation, Ion Exchange, Electrodialysis, Electrocoagulation.
- ✅ **Physical AWT Examples:** MF, UF, DAF, Multimedia Filtration, GAC Adsorption.
- ✅ **Major Byproducts:** RO brine (~20–30% feed), chemical sludge (coagulation), spent GAC, backwash water.
- ✅ **Module 1 CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO1: Classify advanced wastewater treatment technologies based on principle, target contaminant, and removal mechanism.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nutrient Removal - Nitrogen Removal: Nitrification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Nutrient Removal - Nitrogen Removal: Nitrification ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Nitrification** is a chemoautotrophic biological oxidation process in which ammonia nitrogen (NH₄⁺-N) present in wastewater is sequentially oxidized to nitrite (NO₂⁻-N) and subsequently to nitrate (NO₃⁻-N) under aerobic conditions by two distinct groups of autotrophic bacteria, using inorganic carbon (CO₂/HCO₃⁻) as the carbon source for cell synthesis. The process is executed in two discrete steps: **Step 1 — Ammonia Oxidation (Nitritation):** $$NH_4^+ + 1.5\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas, AOB}} NO_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ **Step 2 — Nitrite Oxidation (Nitratation):** $$NO_2^- + 0.5\,O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter, NOB}} NO_3^-$$ **Overall Stoichiometric Reaction:** $$NH_4^+ + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nitrification is the prerequisite for advanced nitrogen removal (denitrification, anammox). Without complete nitrification, downstream processes like biological denitrification cannot function because NO₃⁻ (the electron acceptor) is absent. ### 1.2 Microbial Consortia Involved | Group | Representative Genera | Function | Yield (Y) | |-------|----------------------|----------|-----------| | **AOB** (Ammonia-Oxidizing Bacteria) | *Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrosococcus*, *Nitrosospira* | NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ | **0.15 – 0.33 mg VSS/mg N** | | **NOB** (Nitrite-Oxidizing Bacteria) | *Nitrobacter*, *Nitrococcus*, *Nitrospira* | NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ | **0.04 – 0.13 mg VSS/mg N** | AOB are the rate-limiting step; they grow slower than NOB and are more sensitive to environmental stress, which is why designers always size reactors for AOB kinetics. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person Relay Race" Imagine nitrification as a relay race on a track: - The **first runner (AOB / Nitrosomonas)** is *slower* and *more delicate*. He picks up the "ammonia baton" and converts it into a "nitrite baton." He drinks a lot of oxygen and consumes alkalinity (his energy drink). - The **second runner (NOB / Nitrobacter)** is *faster*. He picks up the nitrite baton almost instantly and converts it to the "nitrate baton." If the first runner gets tired (low DO, low pH, low temperature), the whole team collapses — because the second runner is never handed the baton. Therefore, **engineers design the system to satisfy the slower runner (AOB)**, not the faster one. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** Nitrification is essentially a *two-step bio-oxidation* carried out by *two specialized microbial teams*, where the first team (AOB) is the bottleneck and dictates reactor sizing. ### 1.4 Real-World Significance - **Discharge Compliance:** KTU-referenced CPHEEO norms and most Indian states (Kerala State Pollution Control Board) require effluent NH₄⁺-N < 5 mg/L and total N < 10 mg/L for discharges into surface waters. - **Public Health:** Prevents methemoglobinemia ("blue baby syndrome") caused by NO₃⁻ in drinking water and reduces oxygen depletion in receiving streams. - **Eutrophication Control:** Nitrification combined with denitrification removes the nitrogen that triggers algal blooms in lakes and reservoirs. - **Alkalinity Buffering:** A nitrified effluent has lower alkalinity, which is why denitrification (alkalinity recovery) is paired downstream. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stoichiometric Breakdown — The Mass Balance Logic Starting with the overall reaction: NH₄⁺ + 2O₂ → NO₃⁻ + 2H⁺ + H₂O **Component-by-Component Mass Check:** | Component | Nitrogen Oxidation State Change | Oxygen Required | Electrons Transferred | |-----------|--------------------------------|-----------------|----------------------| | NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ | −3 → +3 (loss of 6 e⁻) | **3.43 g O₂/g N** | 6 e⁻ per N atom | | NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ | +3 → +5 (loss of 2 e⁻) | **1.14 g O₂/g N** | 2 e⁻ per N atom | | **Overall** | −3 → +5 | **4.57 g O₂/g N** | **8 e⁻ per N atom** | **Verification of 4.57 g O₂/g N:** 1. Molar mass of N = **14 g/mol** 2. Molar mass of O₂ = **32 g/mol** 3. From overall stoichiometry: 1 mole N requires 2 moles O₂ 4. Mass ratio = (2 × 32) / 14 = **64/14 ≈ 4.57 g O₂/g N** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Rule of Thumb (Board-Examiner Favorite):** 1 kg of NH₄⁺-N oxidized requires 4.57 kg of O₂ and destroys 7.14 kg of CaCO₃ alkalinity. Always quote this in KTU answers for full marks. ### 2.2 Alkalinity Consumption — Derivation Logic For every mole of NH₄⁺ oxidized to NO₃⁻, **2 moles of H⁺** are produced. To maintain pH buffering, these protons must be neutralized by bicarbonate alkalinity (HCO₃⁻): $$2H^+ + 2HCO_3^- \rightarrow 2H_2CO_3 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2CO_2$$ - Moles of HCO₃⁻ consumed per mole N = 2 - Molar mass of CaCO₃ equivalent = **100 g/mol**; of HCO₃⁻ = **61 g/mol** - Therefore: Alkalinity destroyed = (2 × 100) / 14 = **14.28 mg CaCO₃ per mg N (theoretical)** - Empirically observed (with biomass synthesis) = **7.14 mg CaCO₃ per mg N** The 50% reduction from theoretical comes from the fact that biomass synthesis regenerates part of the alkalinity (CO₂ released during cell growth reacts with water to form HCO₃⁻). ### 2.3 Microbial Growth Kinetics — The Monod Framework Nitrifier growth follows classical Monod kinetics: $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot \frac{DO}{K_O + DO} - k_d$$ Where: - μ = net specific growth rate (day⁻¹) - μ_max = maximum specific growth rate at 20°C (day⁻¹) - S = substrate concentration (NH₄⁺-N, mg/L) - K_S = half-saturation constant (mg/L NH₄⁺-N) - DO = dissolved oxygen (mg/L) - K_O = DO half-saturation constant (mg/L) - k_d = endogenous decay coefficient (day⁻¹) ### 2.4 Temperature Correction $$\mu_{max,T} = \mu_{max,20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$$ Typical value: **θ ≈ 1.07 – 1.10** (use 1.08 for KTU problems unless specified). ### 2.5 Sludge Retention Time (SRT) — Critical Design Parameter For complete nitrification, the mean cell residence time (SRT or θ_c) must exceed the inverse of the net growth rate: $$\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{\mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_0}{K_S + S_0} \cdot \frac{DO}{K_O + DO} - k_d}$$ **Design Safety Factor:** SRT_design = **SF × θ_c^min**, where SF = 1.5 to 3.0 (typical KTU value = 2.0). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** Using SRT < 2 days in winter (T < 15°C) results in washout of AOB. Always apply the temperature-corrected μ_max and the design safety factor. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | O₂ demand = 4.57 × ΔN | Oxygen consumed per unit N oxidized | g O₂/g N | | 2 | Alkalinity destroyed = 7.14 × ΔN | CaCO₃ consumed per unit N oxidized | mg CaCO₃/mg N | | 3 | Biomass produced = 0.15 × ΔN (AOB) | Sludge yield from AOB | mg VSS/mg N | | 4 | μ = μ_max · S/(K_S + S) | Monod growth | day⁻¹ | | 5 | μ_max(T) = μ_max(20) · θ^(T-20) | Temperature correction | day⁻¹ | | 6 | SRT_min = 1/(μ_max − k_d) | Minimum sludge age | day | | 7 | F/M = (Q · S₀)/(V · X) | Food-to-microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | | 8 | q = (μ_max/Y) · S/(K_S + S) | Specific substrate removal rate | g N/g VSS·d | | 9 | V = Q(S₀ − S)/(q · X) | Reactor volume | m³ | | 10 | Eff. NO₂⁻ < 1.0 | NOB must be complete | mg N/L | | 11 | pH optimum = 7.0 – 8.0 | Operational range | – | | 12 | DO optimum ≥ 2.0 | Aerobic requirement | mg/L | | 13 | Free NH₃ inhibition at FA > 10 | Substrate inhibition | mg/L as NH₃ | | 14 | Free HNO₂ inhibition at FNA > 0.2 | Product inhibition | mg/L as HNO₂ | ### 2.7 Inhibitory Substances & Environmental Constraints | Parameter | Inhibitory Threshold | Mechanism | |-----------|---------------------|-----------| | **Free Ammonia (FA)** | > 10 mg/L (pH-dependent) | Passes through cell membrane → pH disruption | | **Free Nitrous Acid (FNA)** | > 0.2 mg/L | Toxic protonated form of NO₂⁻ | | **pH** | < 6.5 or > 9.5 | Enzyme denaturation, HNO₂/FA imbalance | | **DO** | < 0.5 mg/L | Limits electron acceptor | | **Temperature** | < 5°C or > 40°C | Microbial activity halts | | **Heavy Metals** | Cr, Cu, Ni, Zn | Block enzyme active sites | **FA Calculation Formula (Key KTU Equation):** $$FA = \frac{[NH_4^+-N] \times 10^{pH}}{e^{6344/(T+273)} + 10^{pH}}$$ This appears frequently in KTU numerical problems on nitrification design. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand (4.57 g O₂/g N) **Given:** Overall reaction is NH₄⁺ + 2O₂ → NO₃⁻ + 2H⁺ + H₂O **Step 1:** Identify molar ratio between N and O₂ from stoichiometry. From the balanced equation, 1 mole of NH₄⁺ reacts with 2 moles of O₂. Therefore the mole ratio is: $$\frac{\text{moles } O_2}{\text{moles } N} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$ **Step 2:** Convert molar ratio into mass ratio using molecular weights. The molecular weight of N (as atomic N) is 14 g/mol, and the molecular weight of O₂ is 32 g/mol. Substituting these values: $$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{2 \text{ mol} \times 32 \text{ g/mol}}{1 \text{ mol} \times 14 \text{ g/mol}}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the fraction. $$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{64}{14}$$ **Step 4:** Perform the final division. $$\text{Mass ratio} = 4.5714... \approx 4.57 \text{ g } O_2 \text{ per g } N$$ **Conclusion:** The theoretical oxygen demand for complete nitrification of 1 g of NH₄⁺-N is 4.57 g of O₂. This is the gold-standard number universally used in KTU solutions. ### 3.2 Derivation: Alkalinity Consumption (7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N) **Given:** The overall nitrification reaction produces 2 moles of H⁺ per mole of N oxidized. **Step 1:** Write the alkalinity neutralization equation. For every mole of NH₄⁺ oxidized, 2 moles of H⁺ are produced, which must be neutralized by 2 moles of bicarbonate: $$2H^+ + 2HCO_3^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2CO_2$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the theoretical alkalinity consumption in CaCO₃ equivalents. Two moles of HCO₃⁻ (mol wt = 61 g/mol) corresponds to two moles of CaCO₃ equivalent (mol wt = 100 g/mol). The conversion factor is: $$\text{Conversion factor} = \frac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 61} = 1.64$$ This means every g of HCO₃⁻ consumed equals 1.64 g of CaCO₃ equivalent. **Step 3:** Compute the theoretical mass ratio. The mass of HCO₃⁻ neutralized per gram of N is (2 × 61)/14 = 8.71 g HCO₃⁻ per g N. Converting this to CaCO₃ equivalent: $$\frac{8.71 \text{ g HCO}_3^-}{g\,N} \times 1.64 = 14.28 \text{ mg CaCO}_3 \text{ per mg N}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the empirical correction. In practice, biomass synthesis regenerates approximately 50% of this alkalinity. The accepted design value is therefore: $$\text{Alkalinity destroyed} = 0.5 \times 14.28 = 7.14 \text{ mg CaCO}_3 \text{ per mg N}$$ **Conclusion:** For every 1 mg of NH₄⁺-N nitrified, 7.14 mg of CaCO₃ alkalinity is destroyed. This is critical when designing alkalinity supplementation (lime, NaHCO₃ dosing) for low-alkalinity industrial wastewaters. ### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum SRT for Nitrification **Given:** Monod kinetics for AOB at 20°C with K_S = 1.0 mg/L, μ_max = 0.45 d⁻¹, k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹, and influent NH₄⁺-N (S₀) = 30 mg/L. **Step 1:** Apply the Monod growth rate expression. $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S_0}{K_S + S_0} - k_d$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$\mu = 0.45 \cdot \frac{30}{1.0 + 30} - 0.05$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the substrate term. The fraction S₀/(K_S + S₀) = 30/31 = 0.9677. This shows that for S₀ >> K_S, the growth rate approaches μ_max. **Step 4:** Calculate the net growth rate. $$\mu = 0.45 \times 0.9677 - 0.05 = 0.4355 - 0.05 = 0.3855 \text{ d}^{-1}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the minimum SRT. The minimum SRT is the inverse of the net growth rate: $$\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.3855} = 2.59 \text{ days}$$ **Step 6:** Apply a safety factor of 2.0 for design. $$\theta_c^{design} = 2.0 \times 2.59 = 5.18 \text{ days}$$ **Conclusion:** The designed SRT should be approximately 5.2 days at 20°C to ensure complete nitrification with operational margin against temperature drops and shock loads. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Temperature on Nitrifier Growth Let μ_max(20) = 0.45 d⁻¹ and θ = 1.08. The temperature-corrected maximum growth rate is computed as μ_max(T) = 0.45 × 1.08^(T−20). | Temperature T (°C) | μ_max(T) (d⁻¹) | θ_c^min (d) at S₀ >> K_S | SRT_design (×2) (d) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 5 | 0.45 × 1.08^(−15) = 0.1158 | 8.64 | 17.3 | | 10 | 0.45 × 1.08^(−10) = 0.1703 | 5.87 | 11.7 | | 15 | 0.45 × 1.08^(−5) = 0.2506 | 3.99 | 8.0 | | 20 | 0.45 × 1.08^(0) = 0.4500 | 2.22 | 4.4 | | 25 | 0.45 × 1.08^(5) = 0.6606 | 1.51 | 3.0 | | 30 | 0.45 × 1.08^(10) = 0.9698 | 1.03 | 2.1 | **Interpretation:** As temperature drops, μ_max decreases sharply, requiring longer SRT. This is why winter operation in Kerala (cooler mornings, ~22°C) and northern India (~10°C) demands larger aeration tanks. ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style **Problem:** A municipal wastewater treatment plant treats Q = 10,000 m³/d. Influent NH₄⁺-N = 30 mg/L; required effluent NH₄⁺-N ≤ 2 mg/L. MLVSS = 2,000 mg/L. Y = 0.15 mg VSS/mg N. k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹. μ_max = 0.45 d⁻¹; K_S = 1.0 mg/L. Calculate: (a) Oxygen demand, (b) Alkalinity to be added, (c) Aeration tank volume for SRT = 6 days. **Solution:** **(a) Oxygen Demand:** Mass of N oxidized per day = Q × (S₀ − S) = 10,000 × (30 − 2) = 280,000 g/d = 280 kg/d. $$O_2 \text{ required} = 4.57 \times 280 = 1,279.6 \text{ kg O}_2\text{/d}$$ **(b) Alkalinity Consumption:** $$\text{Alkalinity destroyed} = 7.14 \times 280 = 1,999.2 \text{ kg CaCO}_3\text{/d}$$ This is the amount that must be supplemented (typically as lime, Ca(OH)₂, or NaHCO₃). **(c) Aeration Tank Volume:** For SRT = 6 days and μ_max = 0.45 d⁻¹, we must verify feasibility: $$\theta_c \geq \frac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d} = \frac{1}{0.45 - 0.05} = 2.5 \text{ days}$$ Since 6 > 2.5, complete nitrification is feasible. Using SRT = V·X / (Q·X_e) approximation for a CSTR with negligible effluent solids (X_e ≈ 0): $$V = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Y}{X \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}$$ Step-by-step substitution: $$V = \frac{6 \times 10,000 \times 28 \times 0.15}{2,000 \times (1 + 0.05 \times 6)}$$ Numerator = 6 × 10,000 × 28 × 0.15 = 252,000. Denominator = 2,000 × 1.30 = 2,600. $$V = \frac{252,000}{2,600} = 96.9 \approx 97 \text{ m}^3$$ **Conclusion:** The aeration tank volume should be approximately 97 m³. This is a typical KTU 14-mark numerical answer. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Two-Stage Nitrification ```mermaid flowchart LR INF["INFLUENT
NH4+ N 30 mg per L
Alkalinity 200 mg per L
DO 0 mg per L"] --> AERA["AERATION TANK 1
AOB Zone
DO 2 to 4 mg per L
SRT 6 days
pH 7.5"] AERA --> TRANS["TRANSITION
NO2 accumulates
as AOB active"] TRANS --> AERB["AERATION TANK 2
NOB Zone
DO 2 to 4 mg per L
SRT 6 days
pH 7.5"] AERB --> SETT["FINAL CLARIFIER
Sludge Recycle
Wastage"] SETT --> EFF["NITRIFIED EFFLUENT
NO3 N 28 mg per L
NH4 N less than 2 mg per L
Alkalinity 0 mg per L"] SETT --> SLUDGE["WASTE SLUDGE
Biosolids to digester"] AERA -.-> AIR1["Air Supply Blower 1"] AERB -.-> AIR2["Air Supply Blower 2"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Nitrification System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT STREAMS"] I1["Raw Wastewater
NH4+ N 30 mg per L"] I2["Return Activated Sludge
RAS 100 percent Q"] I3["Air Blower Supply
O2 21 percent"] end subgraph REACTOR["BIOLOGICAL REACTOR"] R1["Mixing Zone
Complete Mix CSTR"] R2["AOB Population
Nitrosomonas
Y 0.15 g VSS per g N"] R3["NOB Population
Nitrobacter
Y 0.13 g VSS per g N"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STREAMS"] O1["Treated Effluent
NO3 N 28 mg per L"] O2["Waste Sludge
Calculated from SRT"] O3["Off Gas CO2
Alkalinity Loss Indicator"] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL PARAMETERS"] C1["DO greater than 2 mg per L"] C2["pH 7.0 to 8.0"] C3["Temperature 20 to 30 C"] C4["SRT greater than 5 days"] end I1 --> R1 I2 --> R1 I3 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R1 --> R3 R2 --> R3 R2 --> O1 R3 --> O1 R1 --> O2 R1 --> O3 C1 -.controls.- R1 C2 -.controls.- R1 C3 -.controls.- R1 C4 -.controls.- R2 ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Nitrification Cascade — Substrate Transformations ```mermaid flowchart LR A["NH4 plus
Ammoniacal Nitrogen
Oxidation state minus 3"] -->|Step 1 AOB
3.43 g O2 per g N| B["NO2 minus
Nitrite
Oxidation state plus 3"] B -->|Step 2 NOB
1.14 g O2 per g N| C["NO3 minus
Nitrate
Oxidation state plus 5"] A -.->|Alkalinity 7.14
mg CaCO3 per mg N| D["H plus
Proton Release
pH Drop"] C --> E["Effluent to Denitrification
Anoxic Zone
Carbon Source CH3OH"] ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Nitrification Reactor Configurations ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUSP["SUSPENDED GROWTH SYSTEMS"] S1["Conventional Activated Sludge
SRT 5 to 10 d
MLVSS 1500 to 3000 mg per L"] S2["Separate Stage Nitrification
Two Tanks in Series
Higher SRT 10 to 20 d"] S3["Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR
Time Cycled Fill Draw

High MLVSS 5000 to 10000
Complete Biomass Retention"] end subgraph ATT["ATTACHED GROWTH SYSTEMS"] A1["Trickling Filter

Discs Rotate 40 percent Submerged"] A3["Biological Aerated Filter BAF

Free Floating Plastic Carriers"] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 ``` ### 4.5 pH–Temperature Inhibition Envelope for AOB ```mermaid flowchart LR SAFE["SAFE OPERATING ENVELOPE
pH 7.0 to 8.5
Temperature 15 to 30 C
DO greater than 2 mg per L"] INH1["FA INHIBITION ZONE

AOB Reversibly Inhibited"] INH2["FNA INHIBITION ZONE
