Both AOB and NOB Affected"] INH3["COLD SHOCK ZONE
Temperature less than 10 C
Growth Rate less than 0.2 per d
Washout Risk"] INH4["DO LIMITATION ZONE
DO less than 0.5 mg per L
Incomplete NO2 Oxidation
NOB Suppressed"] SAFE --> INH1 SAFE --> INH2 SAFE --> INH3 SAFE --> INH4 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Set A] **Q: Define nitrification. Name the two groups of bacteria responsible and write the overall reaction.** **Model Answer:** Nitrification is the biological oxidation of ammonia to nitrate in two sequential steps under aerobic conditions, mediated by chemoautotrophic bacteria that use CO₂ as their carbon source. The two bacterial groups are: 1. **Ammonia-Oxidizing Bacteria (AOB)** — e.g., *Nitrosomonas* — oxidize NH₄⁺ to NO₂⁻. 2. **Nitrite-Oxidizing Bacteria (NOB)** — e.g., *Nitrobacter* — oxidize NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻. The overall reaction is: $$NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ The oxygen demand is **4.57 g O₂/g N** and the alkalinity consumed is **7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N**. --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sessional Model] **Q: List the major environmental factors affecting the rate of nitrification in activated sludge systems.** **Model Answer:** The major factors are: 1. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Must be ≥ 2.0 mg/L; below 0.5 mg/L, nitrification ceases. 2. **pH:** Optimum range 7.0 – 8.0; below 6.5 or above 9.5, severe inhibition occurs. 3. **Temperature:** Optimum 25 – 30°C; below 10°C, growth rate becomes too slow to retain biomass. 4. **Free Ammonia (FA):** At FA > 10 mg/L, AOB activity is inhibited. 5. **Free Nitrous Acid (FNA):** At FNA > 0.2 mg/L, NOB activity is inhibited. 6. **Sludge Retention Time (SRT):** Must exceed θ_c^min to prevent AOB washout (typically ≥ 5 days at 20°C). 7. **C/N ratio and toxic substances** (heavy metals, phenols, cyanides). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the microbiology and stoichiometry of nitrification with balanced equations. Discuss the effect of temperature and DO on nitrification rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wastewater treatment plant receives 15,000 m³/d of municipal wastewater with NH₄⁺-N = 35 mg/L. The effluent NH₄⁺-N is to be reduced to 2 mg/L. Calculate: - (i) The daily oxygen requirement. - (ii) The alkalinity consumption per day. - (iii) The design SRT at 25°C, given μ_max(20) = 0.45 d⁻¹, θ = 1.08, K_S = 1.0 mg/L, k_d = 0.05 d⁻¹, and S₀ = 35 mg/L. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Microbiology and Stoichiometry (7 marks):** Nitrification is a two-step autotrophic bio-oxidation. The reactions, bacteria involved, and key parameters are: Step 1 (Nitritation by AOB, *Nitrosomonas*): $$NH_4^+ + 1.5O_2 \rightarrow NO_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ Step 2 (Nitratation by NOB, *Nitrobacter*): $$NO_2^- + 0.5O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^-$$ Overall: $$NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ Stoichiometric oxygen demand = 4.57 g O₂/g N. Alkalinity consumption = 7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N. The bacterial yield (Y) for AOB is 0.15 g VSS/g N and for NOB is 0.13 g VSS/g N. **Effect of Temperature:** Microbial growth follows an Arrhenius-type relationship. μ_max(T) = μ_max(20)·θ^(T-20). Below 10°C, growth slows dramatically, requiring longer SRT. Above 30°C, μ_max continues to rise but at diminishing returns; above 40°C, enzymes denature. **Effect of DO:** Below 2.0 mg/L, the rate-limiting step becomes oxygen transfer. The Monod expression for DO is μ = μ_max · DO/(K_O + DO), with K_O ≈ 0.5 – 2.0 mg/L. At DO < 0.5 mg/L, AOB activity drops sharply, and NO₂⁻ accumulates because NOB are more DO-tolerant than AOB in some systems. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Daily Oxygen Requirement:** Mass of N oxidized = Q × (S₀ − S) = 15,000 × (35 − 2) = 15,000 × 33 = 495,000 g/d = 495 kg/d. $$O_2 = 4.57 \times 495 = 2,262.15 \text{ kg O}_2\text{/d}$$ **(ii) Daily Alkalinity Consumption:** $$\text{Alkalinity} = 7.14 \times 495 = 3,534.3 \text{ kg CaCO}_3\text{/d}$$ **(iii) Design SRT at 25°C:** First, temperature-correct μ_max: $$\mu_{max}(25) = 0.45 \times 1.08^{5} = 0.45 \times 1.4693 = 0.6612 \text{ d}^{-1}$$ Apply Monod for substrate: $$\mu = 0.6612 \times \frac{35}{1 + 35} - 0.05 = 0.6612 \times 0.9722 - 0.05 = 0.6428 - 0.05 = 0.5928 \text{ d}^{-1}$$ Minimum SRT: $$\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{0.5928} = 1.69 \text{ days}$$ Design SRT (with SF = 2.0): $$\theta_c^{design} = 2.0 \times 1.69 = 3.37 \text{ days} \approx 3.4 \text{ days}$$ A practical design would adopt **θ_c = 4 days** to account for hydraulic transients and winter cooling. --- #### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Compare suspended growth and attached growth nitrification systems. Explain why separate-stage nitrification is sometimes preferred over single-sludge systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a completely mixed activated sludge nitrification system for the following data: - Flow Q = 8,000 m³/d - Influent NH₄⁺-N S₀ = 25 mg/L - Effluent NH₄⁺-N S = 1 mg/L - MLVSS X = 2,500 mg/L - μ_max = 0.40 d⁻¹, K_S = 0.8 mg/L, k_d = 0.04 d⁻¹ - Y = 0.17 mg VSS/mg N - Adopt safety factor SF = 2.5 Compute: (i) Aeration tank volume, (ii) Oxygen required, (iii) Sludge wasting rate. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Suspended vs. Attached Growth Systems (7 marks):** **Suspended Growth Systems (e.g., Conventional Activated Sludge, SBR, MBR):** The biomass is maintained in suspension by mechanical mixing and aeration. Hydraulic retention time (HRT) and SRT are decoupled through sludge recycle. SRT is the primary control parameter. Suitable for large flows with consistent influent characteristics. MBRs achieve very high MLVSS (5,000 – 10,000 mg/L) and complete biomass retention. **Attached Growth Systems (e.g., Trickling Filter, RBC, BAF, MBBR):** The biomass grows as a biofilm on inert media. The SRT is effectively infinite (biomass sloughs naturally). Nitrifiers are retained even at low temperatures because they are protected within the biofilm. Less sensitive to shock loads and toxic compounds. MBBR combines advantages of both — high specific surface area and suspended carrier operation. **Why Separate-Stage Nitrification is Preferred:** In a single-sludge system, heterotrophs (BOD-removing bacteria) compete with autotrophic nitrifiers for DO and space. Heterotrophs have higher yield (Y = 0.4 – 0.6) and grow faster, often dominating the MLVSS, which suppresses AOB. In a separate-stage configuration, the first stage removes carbonaceous BOD, and the second stage is dedicated to nitrification with optimized SRT (10 – 20 days) and high DO (2 – 4 mg/L) for nitrifiers. This prevents competition and produces reliable, complete nitrification. The trade-off is higher capital cost (additional tank) but better effluent quality and operational stability. **Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):** **(i) Aeration Tank Volume:** Net specific growth rate: $$\mu = 0.40 \times \frac{25}{0.8 + 25} - 0.04 = 0.40 \times 0.9690 - 0.04 = 0.3876 - 0.04 = 0.3476 \text{ d}^{-1}$$ Minimum SRT: $$\theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{0.3476} = 2.88 \text{ days}$$ Design SRT: $$\theta_c = 2.5 \times 2.88 = 7.2 \text{ days}$$ Volume calculation using the steady-state biomass equation: $$V = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Q \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{X \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}$$ $$V = \frac{7.2 \times 8,000 \times 0.17 \times (25 - 1)}{2,500 \times (1 + 0.04 \times 7.2)}$$ Numerator = 7.2 × 8,000 × 0.17 × 24 = 7.2 × 32,640 = 235,008. Denominator = 2,500 × 1.288 = 3,220. $$V = \frac{235,008}{3,220} = 73.0 \text{ m}^3$$ **(ii) Oxygen Required:** Mass of N oxidized = 8,000 × 24 = 192,000 g/d = 192 kg/d. $$O_2 = 4.57 \times 192 = 877.4 \text{ kg O}_2\text{/d}$$ **(iii) Sludge Wasting Rate:** Waste sludge production per day: $$P_X = \frac{Q \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{8,000 \times 0.17 \times 24}{1.288} = \frac{32,640}{1.288} = 25,341 \text{ g VSS/d} \approx 25.3 \text{ kg VSS/d}$$ The wasting flow rate (assuming waste MLVSS = 8,000 mg/L from underflow): $$Q_w = \frac{25,341}{8,000} = 3.17 \text{ m}^3\text{/d}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 0.5 × 14.28 correction for alkalinity** — Many students write 14.28 mg CaCO₃/mg N as the design value, losing 1 full mark. The empirical 7.14 is the standard. > > 2. **Using only μ_max without Monod substrate term** — AOB kinetics are substrate-influenced; you must apply μ = μ_max · S/(K_S + S) − k_d, not just 1/μ_max. > > 3. **Ignoring temperature correction** — μ_max(20) is not μ_max at 25°C. Apply θ^(T-20) with θ = 1.08. > > 4. **Omitting the safety factor for SRT** — A design SRT must be 1.5× to 3× the theoretical minimum. Using θ_c^min alone risks operational failure. > > 5. **Confusing Y for AOB and heterotrophs** — Nitrifier yield is 0.15 (AOB) or 0.13 (NOB), NOT 0.6 (heterotrophs). Using 0.6 in nitrification design is a 2-mark deduction. > > 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always write mg/L, kg/d, m³, day⁻¹. Board examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. > > 7. **No pH/alkalinity discussion in descriptive answers** — Always mention the 7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N alkalinity loss and the need for supplementation in low-alkalinity wastewater. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Nitrification** is a two-step autotrophic bio-oxidation: NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻. - The two microbial groups are **AOB** (*Nitrosomonas*) and **NOB** (*Nitrobacter*). - **Overall reaction:** NH₄⁺ + 2O₂ → NO₃⁻ + 2H⁺ + H₂O. - **Oxygen demand** = **4.57 g O₂/g N** oxidized. - **Alkalinity destroyed** = **7.14 mg CaCO₃/mg N** oxidized. - **Y_AOB** = 0.15 mg VSS/mg N; **Y_NOB** = 0.13 mg VSS/mg N (much lower than heterotrophs). - **pH optimum:** 7.0 – 8.0; outside 6.5 – 9.5, severe inhibition. - **DO requirement:** ≥ 2.0 mg/L. - **Temperature optimum:** 25 – 30°C; growth rate halves for every 8 – 10°C drop (θ = 1.08). - **SRT** must exceed **θ_c^min = 1/(μ_net)**; design SRT = SF × θ_c^min, with SF = 1.5 to 3.0. - **FA inhibition** above 10 mg/L (pH-dependent); **FNA inhibition** above 0.2 mg/L. - **Two configurations:** single-sludge (combined BOD + N removal) and separate-stage (dedicated nitrification tank). - **Attached growth systems** (RBC, BAF, MBBR, TF) offer biomass retention advantages and resilience to low temperatures. - **Free ammonia (FA)** formula: FA = [NH₄⁺-N] × 10^pH / (e^(6344/(T+273)) + 10^pH). - **Effluent quality targets:** NH₄⁺-N ≤ 2 mg/L for downstream denitrification; NO₂⁻-N < 1 mg/L (NOB must be complete). - **Post-nitrification:** Effluent has low alkalinity, low BOD, and high NO₃⁻ — ideal for subsequent denitrification with methanol or wastewater-borne carbon.

topics.title: Denitrification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Denitrification # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Denitrification** is the microbiological process by which oxidized nitrogenous compounds — principally **nitrate (NO₃⁻)** and to a lesser extent **nitrite (NO₂⁻)** — are biochemically reduced under **anoxic conditions** (dissolved oxygen, DO < 0.2 mg/L) to **nitrogen gas (N₂)**, which then escapes harmlessly to the atmosphere. It is the terminal, gaseous-elimination step of the biological nitrogen removal (BNR) train, succeeding the aerobic nitrification step. In KTU parlance, denitrification is classified as an **anoxic, heterotrophic, dissimilatory respiratory process** where nitrate acts as the *terminal electron acceptor* in the bacterial electron transport chain, replacing molecular oxygen. > [!NOTE] > **Why "anoxic" and not "anaerobic"?** > In strict anaerobic digestion, *no* oxygen species (free or bound) are present. In anoxic denitrification, although free DO is absent, **bound oxygen** within the NO₃⁻ ion is still bio-available to the microbes. This distinction is a frequent KTU viva question. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Back-Up Generator" Imagine a city's power grid. Under normal conditions, factories run on grid electricity (analogous to **aerobic respiration** with O₂ as the electron acceptor). When the grid fails (analogous to the absence of free O₂), the factories switch to their **diesel back-up generator**. The generator is *less efficient* and produces a different exhaust, but it keeps operations alive. Similarly, **facultative heterotrophic bacteria** (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Paracoccus*, *Alcaligenes*, *Bacillus*, *Micrococcus*) cannot use O₂ when it is depleted in the anoxic zone, so they "switch on" their **dissimilatory nitrate reductase enzyme system** and use NO₃⁻ as a back-up oxidant. The "exhaust" in this case is benign **N₂ gas** that bubbles out of the water. ## 1.3 Position of Denitrification in the N-Removal Train | Stage | Process | Condition | Electron Acceptor | Product | |:------|:--------|:----------|:------------------|:--------| | 1 | Ammonification | Aerobic/Anoxic | O₂ | NH₄⁺ | | 2 | **Nitrification** | Aerobic (DO > 2.0 mg/L) | O₂ | NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ | | 3 | **Denitrification** | **Anoxic (DO < 0.2 mg/L)** | **NO₃⁻** | **N₂ ↑** | ## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember - **Target effluent total nitrogen (TN)** : < **10–15 mg/L** (KTU 2024 design standard for advanced treatment) - **Anoxic zone DO threshold** : < **0.2 mg/L**, ideally **< 0.5 mg/L** - **Optimum pH** : **7.0 – 8.5** (inhibition below 6.0 and above 9.5) - **Optimum temperature** : **25 – 35 °C** - **C : N ratio (as COD : NO₃⁻-N)** : minimum **3 : 1**, preferred **4 : 1** to 6 : 1 - **Return Activated Sludge (RAS) recycle ratio** : **0.5 – 1.0 Q** (typical) - **Mixed Liquor Recycle (MLR) ratio** : **1.0 – 4.0 Q** (for Modified Ludzack-Ettinger / MLE) - **Endogenous decay rate (Kₐ)** : **0.04 – 0.06 day⁻¹** at 20 °C > [!TIP] > KTU examiners often award partial marks for correctly stating these standard operating windows. Memorize the **DO threshold of 0.2 mg/L** — it is the single most-tested numerical value in this module. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Microbial Mechanism — Step by Step Denitrification is mediated by a consortium of **facultative heterotrophic bacteria** that possess the genetic capacity to express a four-enzyme denitrifying cascade. Each enzyme step reduces nitrogen by two electrons, sequentially liberating bound oxygen: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four-Step Reductive Cascade (Dissimilatory Pathway)** > > 1. **Nar** (Nitrate reductase, membrane-bound) : NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ > 2. **Nir** (Nitrite reductase) : NO₂⁻ → NO > 3. **Nor** (Nitric oxide reductase) : NO → N₂O > 4. **Nos** (Nitrous oxide reductase) : N₂O → N₂ The intermediate **N₂O (nitrous oxide)** is a potent greenhouse gas (Global Warming Potential ≈ 298 × CO₂) — a key reason why KTU emphasizes *complete* denitrification, not partial NO₂⁻ accumulation. ## 2.2 Stoichiometry — The Heart of Design ### 2.2.1 With Methanol (CH₃OH) as External Carbon Source $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Oxidation–reduction half reactions, summed:} \\ \text{Nitrate reduction:} \quad & 6 \, \text{NO}_3^- + 6 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{N}_2 + 12 \, \text{OH}^- \\ \text{Carbon oxidation:} \quad & 5 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 5 \, \text{CO}_2 + 30 \, \text{H}^+ + 30 \, e^- \\ \hline \text{Net stoichiometric equation:} \quad & 6 \, \text{NO}_3^- + 5 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{N}_2 + 5 \, \text{CO}_2 + 7 \, \text{OH}^- + 6 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.2.2 With Raw Domestic Wastewater as the Carbon Source (BOD-basis) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{19}\text{O}_3\text{N} + 10 \, \text{NO}_3^- &\rightarrow 5 \, \text{N}_2 + 10 \, \text{CO}_2 + 3 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 + 10 \, \text{OH}^- \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] > All values are at standard reference conditions of **T = 20 °C** unless otherwise noted. Multiply heterotrophic yield (Y) and decay (Kₐ) by **θ^(T-20)** for temperature correction, where θ ≈ 1.05 for denitrifiers. | # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks | |:-:|:----------|:----------------|:------|:--------| | 1 | Methanol dose | $D_{MeOH} = 2.47 \times (\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}_{removed})$ | g CH₃OH / d | Stoichiometric demand | | 2 | COD demand (methanol) | $C_{COD} = 1.90 \times (\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}_{removed})$ | g COD / d | 1.5 g CH₃OH = 1.90 g COD | | 3 | COD demand (wastewater) | $C_{COD,ww} = 3.0 \times (\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}_{removed})$ | g COD / d | Empirical, includes cell decay | | 4 | Sludge yield (anoxic) | $Y_{anoxic} = 0.45 \times Y_{aer}$ (≈ 0.20 g VSS/g COD) | g VSS / g COD | Lower than aerobic yield | | 5 | Alkalinity recovered | $Alk_{rec} = 3.57 \times (\text{NO}_3^-\text{-N}_{removed})$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Half of nitrification consumed | | 6 | Temperature correction | $K_{d,T} = K_{d,20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$, θ ≈ 1.05 | — | Apply to all rate constants | | 7 | Denitrification rate (suspended growth) | $K_{DN} = 0.03 \text{ to } 0.06$ | g NO₃⁻-N / g VSS·d | At 20 °C | | 8 | Specific denitrification rate (SDNR) | $SDNR = \dfrac{Q \cdot (N_{in} - N_{out})}{V_{anox} \cdot X}$ | g N / g VSS·d | Used in MLE design | | 9 | Anoxic HRT | $HRT_{anox} = \dfrac{V_{anox}}{Q}$ | hours | Typical 1 – 3 h | | 10 | Recycle ratio (MLR for MLE) | $R_{MLR} = \dfrac{N_{in} - N_{eff}}{(N_{eff} - 0)}$ ≈ 2 – 4 | — | Forwarded to aeration tank | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters 1. **Effluent TN compliance** for discharge into rivers, lakes, and coastal waters. 2. **Alkalinity recovery** — denitrification regenerates ≈ **50 %** of the alkalinity consumed during nitrification, partially offsetting lime/NaOH dosing costs. 3. **Aeration energy savings** — eliminating BOD in the anoxic zone reduces downstream aeration demand by **25 – 40 %**. 4. **Sludge reduction** — anoxic yield is *lower* than aerobic yield, decreasing waste activated sludge (WAS) production. 5. **Mitigation of eutrophication** in receiving water bodies. > [!TIP] > KTU 2024 questions frequently ask: *"List two advantages of pre-anoxic denitrification over post-anoxic denitrification."* Always cite (i) alkalinity recovery and (ii) reduced aeration cost. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Methanol Dose from Electron-Balance Stoichiometry **Problem:** A wastewater treatment plant must remove **N = 50 mg/L** of NO₃⁻-N from a flow of **Q = 10,000 m³/d**. Determine the stoichiometric methanol requirement and the mass of methanol needed per day. ### Step-by-Step Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Identify molecular weights (MW):} \\ \text{MW of NO}_3^-\text{-N} &= 14 \text{ g/mol (the N atom)} \\ \text{MW of CH}_3\text{OH (methanol)} &= 32 \text{ g/mol} \\ \text{From net equation: 6 mol NO}_3^-\text{-N} \text{ requires 5 mol CH}_3\text{OH} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2 — Compute molar ratio:} \\ \dfrac{n_{CH_3OH}}{n_{NO_3^-\text{-N}}} &= \dfrac{5}{6} = 0.833 \text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH per mol NO}_3^-\text{-N} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3 — Convert to mass ratio:} \\ \dfrac{m_{CH_3OH}}{m_{NO_3^-\text{-N}}} &= \dfrac{0.833 \times 32}{1 \times 14} = \dfrac{26.67}{14} = 1.905 \text{ g CH}_3\text{OH per g NO}_3^-\text{-N} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **1.9 g CH₃OH / g NO₃⁻-N** factor is the textbook "stoichiometric" ratio. The **practical design ratio is 2.47 g/g** to account for cell synthesis and endogenous decay. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4 — Apply practical factor (with 30 % safety for cell synthesis):} \\ D_{MeOH} &= 2.47 \times 50 = 123.5 \text{ mg/L of methanol} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 5 — Convert to daily mass:} \\ M_{MeOH} &= D_{MeOH} \times Q = 123.5 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \\ M_{MeOH} &= 1{,}235{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 1235 \text{ kg/d} \approx \mathbf{1.24 \text{ tonnes/d}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anoxic Tank Volume (Modified Ludzack-Ettinger, MLE) **Problem:** Design the anoxic volume of an MLE system. - Flow Q = 20,000 m³/d - Influent NO₃⁻-N (after nitrification) = 35 mg/L - Target effluent NO₃⁻-N = 5 mg/L - MLVSS in anoxic zone X = 2500 mg/L - Specific denitrification rate SDNR = 0.05 g N / g VSS·d ### Step-by-Step Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Mass of NO}_3^-\text{-N to be removed per day:} \\ \Delta N &= Q \times (N_{in} - N_{out}) \\ \Delta N &= 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times (35 - 5) \text{ g/m}^3 \\ \Delta N &= 20{,}000 \times 30 = 600{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 600 \text{ kg N/d} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2 — Mass of denitrifier biomass in the anoxic zone:} \\ M_{bio} &= V_{anox} \times X \\ \text{(unknown volume } V_{anox} \text{ is what we solve for)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3 — Apply the SDNR definition:} \\ SDNR &= \dfrac{\Delta N}{M_{bio}} = \dfrac{Q (N_{in} - N_{out})}{V_{anox} \cdot X} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 4 — Solve for } V_{anox} \text{ (note: convert mg/L → g/m}^3 \text{ = 1:1):} \\ V_{anox} &= \dfrac{Q (N_{in} - N_{out})}{SDNR \cdot X} \\ V_{anox} &= \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 30}{0.05 \times 2500} \\ V_{anox} &= \dfrac{600{,}000}{125} = 4800 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 5 — Check HRT:} \\ HRT &= \dfrac{V_{anox}}{Q} = \dfrac{4800}{20{,}000} \text{ d} = 0.24 \text{ d} = \mathbf{5.76 \text{ h}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This HRT falls within the **typical 1–3 h** KTU reference range, but is on the higher end because denitrification is partly limited by the SDNR assumed. Always check that the anoxic HRT is between **1 and 3 hours** for a realistic design. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Internal Recycle (MLR) Ratio for MLE **Problem:** For the MLE system above, compute the internal mixed-liquor recycle (MLR) ratio required to achieve the desired effluent NO₃⁻-N. ### Step-by-Step Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Mass balance on NO}_3^-\text{-N at the anoxic tank inlet:} \\ \text{NO}_3^- \text{ into anoxic zone} &= (Q + R \cdot Q) \cdot N_{aer} + Q \cdot N_{inf} \\ \text{where } R &= \text{MLR ratio}, N_{aer} = \text{NO}_3^-\text{-N leaving aeration zone} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2 — Steady-state: All N entering anoxic zone is denitrified.} \\ (Q + R \cdot Q) \cdot N_{aer} &= (Q + R \cdot Q) \cdot N_{eff} \quad \text{(using MLE assumption)} \\ \text{Since } N_{aer} = N_{eff} \text{ in a well-mixed aeration tank:} \\ \text{Re-arranged: } N_{eff} = \dfrac{N_{inf}}{1 + R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \dfrac{N_{inf} - N_{eff}}{N_{eff}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (using } N_{inf} = 35 \text{ mg/L}, N_{eff} = 5 \text{ mg/L):} \\ R &= \dfrac{35 - 5}{5} = \dfrac{30}{5} = \mathbf{6.0} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU mistake:** Students frequently apply $R = (N_{in} - N_{eff})/N_{eff}$ but forget that $N_{in}$ here is the **effluent of the nitrification stage**, not the raw influent TKN. Read the problem statement carefully — a 2-mark trap in the KTU 2024 university exam. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Configuration Comparison | Configuration | Anoxic Position | C-Source | Internal Recycle | Alkalinity Recovery | Typical Removal | |:--------------|:----------------|:---------|:-----------------|:--------------------|:----------------| | **MLE** (Modified Ludzack-Ettinger) | Pre-anoxic | Influent BOD | MLR = 1–4 Q | Full | 60–80 % | | **4-Stage Bardenpho** | Pre + Post anoxic | Influent + Endogenous | MLR + Post recirc. | Full + extra | 90–95 % | | **SBR** (Sequential Batch) | Time-sequenced | Influent | None (batch) | Full | 80–95 % | | **MBBR/MBR** | Integrated carriers | Influent | MLR | Full | 85–95 % | | **Post-anoxic (tertiary)** | Downstream of aeration | External (methanol/acetate) | None | None | 95+ % | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Architecture — Pre-Anoxic MLE Configuration ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent
Wastewater
Q with NH4+ and BOD] --> B[Anoxic Zone
DO less than 0.2 mg per L
Denitrification] R1[RAS Recycle
R equals 0.5 to 1.0 Q
Return Sludge] --> B B --> C[Aerobic Zone
DO greater than 2.0 mg per L
Nitrification] R2[Internal Recycle
MLR equals 2 to 4 Q
Nitrate-rich ML] --> B C --> D[Secondary Clarifier
Solids Liquid Separation] D --> E[Treated Effluent
NO3-N less than 5 mg per L
BOD less than 10 mg per L] D --> R1 D --> F[Waste Activated Sludge
WAS to Thickener] ``` ## 4.2 Microbial Electron Flow — Why DO Must Be Low ```mermaid flowchart TB H[Carbon Source
CH3OH or BOD] --> E1[Microbial Cell
Electron Transport Chain] O1[Oxygen Available
Aerobic Condition] -->|Preferred path| E1 E1 --> P1[Aerobic Respiration
High ATP yield
O2 to H2O] O2[Oxygen Absent
Anoxic Condition] -->|Back-up path| E1 E1 --> P2[Denitrification
Lower ATP yield
NO3 to N2 gas] P2 --> G1[N2 gas released
to atmosphere] P1 --> G2[CO2 and H2O] ``` ## 4.3 4-Stage Bardenpho Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1
Anaerobic
P Release] --> S2[Stage 2
Anoxic
Pre-Denitrification] S2 --> S3[Stage 3
Aerobic
Nitrification and N Uptake] S3 --> S4[Stage 4
Anoxic
Post-Denitrification] S4 --> S5[Stage 5
Re-aeration
DO Stripping and Polishing] S5 --> S6[Final Clarifier
Effluent Discharge] RS[RAS Recycle
R equals 0.5 to 1.0 Q] --> S1 MLR[Internal Recycle
MLR equals 2 to 4 Q
Stage 3 to Stage 2] --> S2 PR[Post Recycle
Stage 4 to Stage 2
Improves TN] --> S2 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Carbon Source Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Influent
BOD to N
ratio?} -->|Greater than 4| P1[Use Influent
BOD as carbon
No external dose] Q1 -->|Between 2 and 4| P2[Consider
Primary Effluent
Fermentation or VFA] Q1 -->|Less than 2| P3[Add External
Carbon Source] P3 --> Q2{Type of
external source?} Q2 -->|Methanol| R1[CH3OH
2.47 g per g N
Lowest sludge yield] Q2 -->|Acetate| R2[CH3COONa
2.0 g per g N
Higher cost] Q2 -->|Ethanol| R3[C2H5OH
1.8 g per g N
Flammable storage] Q2 -->|Industrial waste| R4[Free or low cost
BOD check for toxics] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The decision flow above is a **KTU-style design heuristic**. If the influent already carries sufficient biodegradable COD relative to nitrate-N, **never dose external carbon** — it inflates sludge and operating cost. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define denitrification. List the four sequential enzymes involved and the gaseous intermediates produced.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Denitrification is the **anoxic biological reduction of nitrate (NO₃⁻) to nitrogen gas (N₂)** by facultative heterotrophic bacteria, using NO₃⁻ as the terminal electron acceptor in place of molecular oxygen. It requires DO < 0.2 mg/L. **Four-enzyme cascade:** 1. **Nitrate reductase (Nar)** — NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ 2. **Nitrite reductase (Nir)** — NO₂⁻ → NO 3. **Nitric oxide reductase (Nor)** — NO → N₂O 4. **Nitrous oxide reductase (Nos)** — N₂O → N₂ **Gaseous intermediates:** Nitric oxide (NO) and Nitrous oxide (N₂O), with the end product being inert **N₂**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **State the stoichiometric equation for denitrification using methanol and the practical methanol dose required per gram of NO₃⁻-N removed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The balanced stoichiometric equation is: $$ 6 \, \text{NO}_3^- + 5 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{N}_2 + 5 \, \text{CO}_2 + 7 \, \text{OH}^- + 6 \, \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ The **stoichiometric** demand is **1.9 g CH₃OH per g NO₃⁻-N**. The **practical design** dose, accounting for cell synthesis and endogenous decay, is **2.47 g CH₃OH per g NO₃⁻-N removed**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, every 14-mark question carries internal choice. Both options below are independent full-credit alternatives. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE)** process for biological nitrogen removal. Explain the role of the anoxic and aerobic zones. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A municipal STP treats **Q = 25,000 m³/d** of wastewater. The influent TKN is **40 mg/L** and the required effluent NO₃⁻-N is **6 mg/L**. Calculate: - (i) the theoretical **methanol dose** in kg/d, - (ii) the **internal mixed-liquor recycle (MLR) ratio**, - (iii) the **alkalinity recovered** in mg/L as CaCO₃. Assume complete nitrification and that methanol is the sole external carbon source. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Block Diagram & Process Description (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the MLE block diagram. The descriptive points are: - **Influent + RAS** enter the **anoxic zone** (DO < 0.2 mg/L). Here, heterotrophic denitrifiers use influent BOD as the carbon source to reduce NO₃⁻ (from the recycle stream) to N₂ gas. This **pre-anoxic** arrangement consumes BOD *before* it reaches the aeration tank, saving aeration energy. - The mixed liquor flows into the **aerobic zone** where **nitrification** occurs (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻) at DO > 2.0 mg/L. - A portion of the **nitrate-rich mixed liquor** is recycled back to the anoxic zone via the **MLR pump** (typically 2–4 Q). The recycle stream supplies the electron acceptor (NO₃⁻) for denitrification. - **Sludge settles** in the secondary clarifier; a fraction returns as **RAS** (0.5–1.0 Q) to the anoxic zone, maintaining biomass. - **Advantages:** alkalinity recovery, lower aeration cost, lower sludge yield. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Methanol dose:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NO}_3^-\text{-N to be removed} &= N_{in} - N_{eff} = 40 - 6 = 34 \text{ mg/L} \\ \text{Practical methanol dose} &= 2.47 \times 34 = 83.98 \text{ mg/L} \\ \text{Daily methanol mass} &= 83.98 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 25{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \\ &= 2{,}099{,}500 \text{ g/d} = \mathbf{2099.5 \text{ kg/d} \approx 2.1 \text{ tonnes/d}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) MLR ratio:** $$ R = \dfrac{N_{in} - N_{eff}}{N_{eff}} = \dfrac{40 - 6}{6} = \dfrac{34}{6} = \mathbf{5.67} $$ **(iii) Alkalinity recovered:** $$ \begin{aligned} Alk_{rec} &= 3.57 \times (N_{in} - N_{eff}) \\ &= 3.57 \times 34 \\ &= \mathbf{121.4 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on Question A:** > 1. Confusing **stoichiometric** (1.9 g/g) with **practical** (2.47 g/g) methanol dose — full mark requires both values. > 2. Using **influent TKN directly as N_in** without stating the assumption of complete nitrification. Always write the assumption explicitly. > 3. Computing alkalinity recovery using the *influent* N (40) instead of the *removed* N (34). This error costs 1 mark. > 4. Drawing the MLE block diagram without arrows for **both** the MLR and RAS — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the direction of recycle streams. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **(a)** Compare **pre-anoxic** and **post-anoxic** denitrification systems in terms of carbon source, alkalinity recovery, and effluent nitrogen quality. Mention at least one example configuration for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the anoxic tank volume of an MLE system for the following data: - Q = 15,000 m³/d - NO₃⁻-N to be removed = 30 mg/L - MLVSS, X = 3000 mg/L - Specific denitrification rate (SDNR) at 25 °C = 0.06 g N / g VSS·d - Temperature correction factor θ = 1.05; SDNR at 20 °C = 0.04 g N / g VSS·d Determine the volume and the anoxic HRT. Comment on whether the design is acceptable per KTU norms. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Comparison (7 marks):** | Feature | Pre-Anoxic (e.g., MLE) | Post-Anoxic (e.g., Tertiary Filter) | |:--------|:-----------------------|:------------------------------------| | **Carbon source** | Influent BOD (free, no chemical cost) | External — methanol, acetate, ethanol | | **Alkalinity recovery** | Yes — 3.57 mg/L as CaCO₃ per mg N removed | No (NO₃⁻ already converted downstream) | | **Effluent TN** | 5–8 mg/L (limited by recycle) | < 3 mg/L (achievable) | | **BOD competition** | Yes — high BOD favours denitrifiers | No — BOD already removed | | **Sludge yield** | Lower (anoxic yield) | Higher due to external C | | **Operating cost** | Lower (no chemical) | Higher (chemical + storage) | | **Aeration saving** | Yes (25–40 % less) | No | **Conclusion:** Pre-anoxic is preferred when influent C/N is sufficient (COD:N > 6); post-anoxic is preferred for very low effluent TN requirements (< 3 mg/L). **(b) Design of Anoxic Volume (7 marks):** > [!NOTE] > The SDNR is already given at 25 °C, so we use it directly without temperature correction. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{anox} &= \dfrac{Q \cdot (N_{in} - N_{out})}{SDNR_{25} \cdot X} \\ &= \dfrac{15{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times 30 \text{ g/m}^3}{0.06 \text{ g N/g VSS·d} \times 3000 \text{ g/m}^3} \\ &= \dfrac{450{,}000}{180} = \mathbf{2500 \text{ m}^3} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} HRT_{anox} &= \dfrac{V_{anox}}{Q} = \dfrac{2500}{15{,}000} \text{ d} = 0.1667 \text{ d} = \mathbf{4.0 \text{ h}} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment:** The design HRT of **4.0 hours** is *slightly higher* than the typical KTU norm of **1–3 hours** for MLE. This suggests the system is **over-designed** for safety, or the SDNR value is conservative. For optimization, the designer may reduce X or increase MLR. The design is **acceptable** but not optimal — recommend reducing MLVSS to 2000 mg/L to bring HRT into the 2.5–3 h range. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks on Question B:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting that mg/L = g/m³. Many students divide by 3000 instead of multiplying, losing 2 marks. > 2. **Temperature confusion:** When SDNR is given at 20 °C and T = 25 °C, *apply* the correction. When SDNR is given at the design T directly, *do not* apply it again — that's a double-correction trap. > 3. **HRT comment:** Students often compute the volume correctly but skip the **acceptance comment**, losing 1 mark. Always validate against KTU norms. > 4. **Forgetting the X unit conversion:** X = 3000 mg/L = 3 g/L = 3000 g/m³ — all numerically the same in this case but explicitly state the unit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):** - **Definition:** Anoxic reduction of NO₃⁻ → N₂ by facultative heterotrophs. - **DO threshold:** **< 0.2 mg/L** in anoxic zone; > 2 mg/L in aerobic (nitrification) zone. - **Optimum pH:** 7.0 – 8.5; **Optimum T:** 25 – 35 °C. - **Net methanol equation:** $6 \, \text{NO}_3^- + 5 \, \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3 \, \text{N}_2 + 5 \, \text{CO}_2 + 7 \, \text{OH}^- + 6 \, \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - **Practical methanol dose:** **2.47 g CH₃OH / g NO₃⁻-N** (book value). - **COD demand:** **1.9 g COD / g N** (methanol) or **3.0 g COD / g N** (wastewater). - **Anoxic yield (Y):** **0.20 g VSS / g COD** (≈ 0.45 × aerobic yield). - **Alkalinity recovery:** **3.57 mg/L as CaCO₃ per mg NO₃⁻-N removed** (offsets ~50 % of nitrification loss). - **Four enzymes (in order):** Nar → Nir → Nor → Nos. - **Two intermediates to remember:** NO and N₂O (greenhouse gas — complete denitrification mandatory). - **C : N ratio:** minimum 3 : 1, preferred 4–6 : 1. - **MLE recycle formula:** $R = (N_{in} - N_{eff})/N_{eff}$, typically 2 – 4. - **Anoxic HRT:** 1 – 3 h (pre-anoxic), 2 – 4 h (post-anoxic with external C). - **Five key reactor configurations:** MLE, 4-Stage Bardenpho, SBR, MBBR/MBR, post-anoxic tertiary. - **Two engineering benefits to always cite:** (i) alkalinity recovery and (ii) aeration energy saving. - **Temperature correction:** $K_{d,T} = K_{d,20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$, $\theta = 1.05$ for denitrifiers. - **Key genera:** *Pseudomonas*, *Paracoccus*, *Alcaligenes*, *Bacillus*, *Micrococcus*. - **Distinguish:** anoxic = bound O₂ present (NO₃⁻); anaerobic = no O₂ at all. - **Common N₂O cause:** insufficient carbon source or low pH → incomplete Nos activity. ---

topics.title: Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND)** is a single-reactor biological nitrogen removal process in which the oxidation of ammonia to nitrate (nitrification, mediated by autotrophic aerobic bacteria) and the reduction of nitrate/nitrite to nitrogen gas (denitrification, mediated by heterotrophic facultative bacteria) occur concurrently within the same reaction vessel, under unified operating conditions — most commonly at a controlled low **dissolved oxygen (DO)** concentration in the range of **0.5 – 1.5 mg/L**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Anchor:**
> *"SND is a compact, energy-efficient biological nitrogen removal configuration in which aerobic and anoxic micro-environments coexist within a single bioreactor, enabling ammonia oxidation and nitrate reduction to proceed in parallel rather than in sequentially separated tanks."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **single large office kitchen** during lunch hour:

- The **stove tops** (high-oxygen, hot zones) are like the **outer surface of activated sludge flocs** — here, ammonia is "cooked" (oxidized) into nitrate by nitrifying bacteria.
- The **walk-in cold storage room** (low-oxygen, cool zones) is like the **inner core of the floc** — here, the nitrate produced is "cooled down" (reduced) into harmless nitrogen gas by denitrifying bacteria.
- A **chef's helper runs between the two** — this represents the **diffusion gradient** of oxygen and substrates. The kitchen produces a complete meal (nitrogen removal) in *one* location, instead of needing two separate buildings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** SND eliminates the need for a *separate* anoxic tank (pre- or post-denitrification) by engineering a **gradient of oxygen** within microscopic floc structures.

## 1.3 Governing Microbial Populations

| Microbial Group | Metabolism | Oxygen Requirement | Function in SND |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ammonia-Oxidizing Bacteria (AOB)** | Chemolithoautotrophic | Strictly aerobic | NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ |
| **Nitrite-Oxidizing Bacteria (NOB)** | Chemolithoautotrophic | Strictly aerobic | NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ |
| **Heterotrophic Denitrifiers** | Chemoorganoheterotrophic | Facultative (anoxic) | NO₃⁻ → N₂ |
| **Nitrifier-Denitrifiers** (e.g., *Nitrosomonas* sp.) | Mixotrophic | Microaerobic | NH₄⁺ → N₂O / N₂ directly |
| **Comammox Bacteria** (*Nitrospira*) | Chemolithoautotrophic | Aerobic | Complete NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Microbial Logic of SND

### Step 1 — Substrate Diffusion into the Floc
- Ammonia-NH₄⁺, oxygen, and organic carbon diffuse from the bulk liquid **toward the floc surface**.
- Diffusion rates follow **Fick's First Law**: $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ where $D$ is the molecular diffusivity.

### Step 2 — Outer Aerobic Shell (Nitrification)
- At the floc periphery where **DO > 1.0 mg/L**, **AOB** oxidize NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻:
$$\text{NH}_4^+ + 1.5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{AOB}} \text{NO}_2^- + 2\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **NOB** further oxidize NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻:
$$\text{NO}_2^- + 0.5\,\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NOB}} \text{NO}_3^-$$

### Step 3 — Inner Anoxic Core (Denitrification)
- Oxygen cannot penetrate deep into large flocs (D_O ≈ 2.09 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s at 25 °C).
- Within the **anoxic core** (DO < 0.2 mg/L), denitrifiers reduce NO₃⁻ to N₂:
$$\text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$
- They use **organic carbon** as the electron donor:
$$\text{NO}_3^- + 1.25\,\text{CH}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 0.5\,\text{N}_2 + 1.25\,\text{CO}_2 + 0.75\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{OH}^-$$

### Step 4 — The "Bridge": Nitrifier-Denitrification
- Under DO < 0.5 mg/L, certain AOB (e.g., *Nitrosomonas europaea*) bypass NOB and reduce NO₂⁻ directly:
$$\text{NH}_4^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2\text{O} / \text{N}_2$$
- This contributes to **~30%** of total N-removal in well-engineered SND systems.

### Step 5 — Why This Works: Reactor Engineering
- Macroscale DO heterogeneity (e.g., airlift reactor circulation, SBR mixing) creates **alternating micro-environments** every few seconds.
- Aerobic granules (Ø 0.2 – 3.0 mm) sustain stable co-existence of nitrifiers and denitrifiers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{SND} = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{NO3,eff}}{N_{NH4,inf} - N_{NH4,eff}}\right) \times 100\%$ | SND efficiency based on nitrate balance | % |
| 2 | $r_{N} = \mu_{max,N}\cdot\dfrac{1}{Y_N}\cdot\dfrac{N_{NH4}}{K_{NH4} + N_{NH4}}\cdot\dfrac{DO}{K_{O,N}+DO}\cdot X_N$ | Nitrification rate (Monod) | mg N/(L·d) |
| 3 | $r_{DN} = \mu_{max,D}\cdot\dfrac{1}{Y_D}\cdot\dfrac{N_{NO3}}{K_{NO3}+N_{NO3}}\cdot\dfrac{K_{O,D}}{K_{O,D}+DO}\cdot X_D$ | Denitrification rate (Monod with DO inhibition) | mg N/(L·d) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta\text{N}}{\Delta t} = -r_N - r_{DN}$ | Net nitrogen removal in SND | mg N/(L·d) |
| 5 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot (1 - 0.43\cdot X_v^{0.51})$ | Effective diffusivity in biofilm/floc | m²/s |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max,N} - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT for nitrifier retention | days |
| 7 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0 \cdot Q}{V \cdot X_v}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | d⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{NH}_4^+ \text{ oxidized to N}_2}{\text{Total NH}_4^+ \text{ oxidized}} \times 100\%$ | Fractional N-removal by SND | % |
| 9 | $\Delta\text{NO}_3 = r_{DN} \cdot \dfrac{2.86}{1 - Y_H}$ | COD required per unit NO₃-N reduced | mg COD/mg N |
| 10 | $L_{floc}^{crit} \approx 2\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D_{O,eff} \cdot C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}}$ | Critical floc thickness for anoxic core | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Note:** In all rate equations, when using a switch/inhibition factor like $\dfrac{K_O}{K_O + DO}$, **never** use the vertical bar `|` character (e.g., `|DO|`). Use `\vert DO \vert` or the rational Monod form $\dfrac{K_O}{K_O+DO}$ to preserve table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Municipal STPs in Kerala (KTU 2024 Case Context):** Compact SND units handle **500 – 50,000 m³/d** flows with **> 85% N-removal** at **40% less aeration cost** versus conventional Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE) configurations.
2. **Industrial Effluents (Fertilizer, Landfill Leachate):** High-strength NH₄⁺ (200 – 2000 mg/L) treated efficiently in SBR-based SND.
3. **Net-Zero Wastewater Plants:** Reduces **carbon footprint** by ~25% (less external methanol dosing, less aeration energy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SND Efficiency (η_SND) from Mass Balance

### Step 1: Define the Inlet and Outlet Nitrogen Species
Let:
- $N_{NH_4,inf}$ = Influent ammonia-N concentration (mg/L)
- $N_{NH_4,eff}$ = Effluent ammonia-N concentration (mg/L)
- $N_{NO_3,eff}$ = Effluent nitrate-N concentration (mg/L)
- $N_{org,inf}$ = Influent organic-N (mineralizes to NH₄⁺)

### Step 2: Write a Steady-State Nitrogen Mass Balance Over the Reactor
Total nitrogen entering = Total nitrogen leaving + N₂ gas stripped out.

$$Q \cdot (N_{NH_4,inf} + N_{org,inf}) = Q \cdot (N_{NH_4,eff} + N_{NO_3,eff} + N_{NO_2,eff}) + Q \cdot N_{N_2,stripped}$$

### Step 3: Define Ammonia Oxidized to Nitrate
The amount of NH₄⁺ actually oxidized through nitrification is:

$$\Delta N_{NH_4 \to NO_3} = N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff} \quad \text{(Eq. A)}$$

### Step 4: Define Nitrate Actually Denitrified
The nitrate that has been denitrified (gone to N₂) is the difference between nitrate produced and nitrate leaving:

$$N_{DN} = (N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}) - N_{NO_3,eff} \quad \text{(Eq. B)}$$

### Step 5: Form the SND Efficiency Ratio
By definition, $\eta_{SND}$ is the **fraction of oxidized ammonia that is denitrified within the same reactor**:

$$\eta_{SND} = \dfrac{N_{DN}}{\Delta N_{NH_4 \to NO_3}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 6: Substitute (B) into (A)
Substituting Eq. B and Eq. A into the SND ratio gives:

$$\eta_{SND} = \dfrac{(N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}) - N_{NO_3,eff}}{N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 7: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\eta_{SND} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{N_{NO_3,eff}}{N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}} \right) \times 100\%}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Oxygen Penetration Depth into Activated Sludge Floc

### Step 1: Steady-State Diffusion-Reaction Equation
Inside a spherical floc, with oxygen consumption following zero-order kinetics $r_O$ (mg O₂/m³·s):

$$D_{O,eff}\left(\dfrac{d^2 C}{dr^2} + \dfrac{2}{r}\dfrac{dC}{dr}\right) - r_O = 0$$

### Step 2: Boundary Conditions
- At floc surface $r = R_f$: $C(R_f) = C_{O,bulk}$
- At floc center $r = 0$: $\dfrac{dC}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0} = 0$ (symmetry)

### Step 3: Integrate Twice
First integration:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dr} = \dfrac{r_O}{3 D_{O,eff}}\left(R_f - \dfrac{r^2}{R_f}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{r} \cdot r \quad \text{... applying symmetry}$$

After two integrations and applying the surface boundary condition:

$$C(r) = C_{O,bulk} - \dfrac{r_O}{6 D_{O,eff}}\left(R_f^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 4: Find the Critical Radius Where DO = 0
Set $C(r_{crit}) = 0$:

$$0 = C_{O,bulk} - \dfrac{r_O}{6 D_{O,eff}}\left(R_f^2 - r_{crit}^2\right)$$

### Step 5: Solve for r_crit

$$r_{crit}^2 = R_f^2 - \dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} \cdot C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}$$

$$\boxed{r_{crit} = \sqrt{R_f^2 - \dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} \cdot C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}}}$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation
- If $R_f^2 < \dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}$ → **no anoxic core** (small flocs) → SND fails.
- For typical $D_{O,eff} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $C_{O,bulk} = 1.0$ g/m³, $r_O = 2.5$ g/(m³·s):
  - Threshold $R_f = \sqrt{6 \cdot 1.2\times10^{-9} \cdot 1.0 / 2.5\times10^{-3}} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **1.7 mm**.
  - Flocs > 1.7 mm will host an internal anoxic core — **prerequisite for SND**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum SRT for Nitrifier Retention in SND

### Step 1: Monod Growth Kinetics for Nitrifiers
$$\mu_N = \mu_{max,N} \cdot \dfrac{N_{NH_4}}{K_{NH_4} + N_{NH_4}} \cdot \dfrac{DO}{K_{O,N} + DO}$$

### Step 2: At Steady State, $\mu_N = 1/\theta_c$ (Inverse of SRT)
$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu_{max,N} \cdot \dfrac{N_{NH_4}}{K_{NH_4} + N_{NH_4}} \cdot \dfrac{DO}{K_{O,N} + DO} - k_{d,N}$$

### Step 3: Assume Substrate-Saturated, High-DO (Worst Case for Washout is Low DO; here, design for DO ≈ 1.0 mg/L)
With $N_{NH_4} \gg K_{NH_4}$ and $DO \gg K_{O,N}$, both Monod terms → 1:

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} \approx \mu_{max,N} - k_{d,N}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Minimum SRT

$$\boxed{\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max,N} - k_{d,N}}}$$

For *Nitrosomonas* at 20 °C: $\mu_{max,N} = 0.75$ d⁻¹, $k_{d,N} = 0.05$ d⁻¹ → $\theta_c^{min} \approx 1.43$ days.
Design SRT is typically **2 – 3× this** to ensure robust SND, i.e., **3 – 5 days** for warm climates (Kerala).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of N-Removal Configurations

| Parameter | Conventional Nitrification + Separate Denitrification | Pre-Denitrification (MLE) | **SND (Single Reactor)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactor count | ≥ 2 | ≥ 2 | **1** |
| Aeration energy | High | Moderate | **Low (30–40% saving)** |
| External C-source | Often required | Optional | **Often not required** |
| Footprint | Large | Moderate | **Compact (40% reduction)** |
| N-removal efficiency | 70 – 80% | 80 – 90% | **75 – 95%** |
| Sludge yield | Higher | Moderate | **Lower** |
| Control complexity | Low | Moderate | **High (DO-sensitive)** |
| Effluent NO₃⁻ | 5 – 10 mg/L | 5 – 8 mg/L | **2 – 6 mg/L** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SND Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Influent Wastewater NH4 plus Organics] --> B1[Single Bioreactor DO 0.5 to 1.5 mg per L]
    B1 --> C1[Aerobic Zone Outer Floc Shell]
    B1 --> C2[Anoxic Core Inner Floc Zone]
    C1 --> D1[AOB Oxidation NH4 to NO2]
    C1 --> D2[NOB Oxidation NO2 to NO3]
    D1 --> E1[NO2 minus and NO3 minus Diffusion Inward]
    E1 --> C2
    C2 --> D3[Heterotrophic Denitrification NO3 to N2 gas]
    C2 --> D4[Nitrifier Denitrification NH4 to N2O or N2]
    D3 --> F1[Treated Effluent Low NH4 and Low NO3]
    D4 --> F1
    D3 --> G1[Off gas N2 release to atmosphere]
    D4 --> G1
    F1 --> H1[Sludge Recycle Loop to Maintain SRT 3 to 5 days]
    H1 --> B1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mass Flux Pathways Within a Floc

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Bulk Liquid DO 1.0 mg per L] --> B2[Floc Outer Shell Aerobic]
    B2 --> C2a[AOB NOB Activity]
    C2a --> D2a[NH4 plus consumed O2 consumed]
    C2a --> E2a[NO3 minus produced]
    E2a --> F2a[Diffusion to Anoxic Core]
    F2a --> G2a[Denitrifiers NO3 to N2]
    A2 --> H2a[Organic Carbon Substrate C]
    H2a --> G2a
    G2a --> I2a[N2 gas bubble release]
    B2 --> J2a[DO gradient DO 0 to 1.0 mg per L]
    J2a --> K2a[Anoxic Core DO less than 0.2 mg per L]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — SND Reactor Type Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification Systems] --> B3[Suspended Growth]
    A3 --> C3[Attached Growth]
    A3 --> D3[Hybrid Systems]
    B3 --> B3a[Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR]
    B3 --> B3b[Aerobic Granular Sludge Reactor]
    B3 --> B3c[Orbal Oxidation Ditch]
    C3 --> C3a[Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor MBBR]
    C3 --> C3b[Integrated Fixed Film Activated Sludge IFAS]
    C3 --> C3c[Fluidized Bed Bioreactor]
    D3 --> D3a[Membrane Bioreactor MBR with DO gradient]
    D3 --> D3b[Biofilm Airlift Suspension BAS Reactor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B3c`, `D3a`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels use plain uppercase text only — no bold/italic markdown inside the quoted strings — to ensure clean rendering across browsers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification (SND). Mention any two mechanisms responsible for its occurrence in a single reactor."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** SND is a biological nitrogen removal process in which nitrification (NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻) and denitrification (NO₃⁻ → N₂) occur concurrently in the same reactor under controlled low DO conditions (0.5 – 1.5 mg/L).
2. **Mechanism 1 (1 mark):** **Microenvironment (floc theory):** DO gradient within sludge flocs creates aerobic outer shell and anoxic inner core, supporting both microbial groups simultaneously.
3. **Mechanism 2 (1 mark):** **Nitrifier denitrification:** Certain AOB (e.g., *Nitrosomonas europaea*) at low DO directly convert NH₄⁺ via NO₂⁻ to N₂O/N₂, bypassing NOB.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List any three factors that affect SND efficiency in a single-tank bioreactor."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Concentration (1 mark):** Optimal range 0.5 – 1.5 mg/L. Below 0.3 mg/L nitrification fails; above 2.0 mg/L anoxic zones collapse.
2. **Floc Size / SVI (1 mark):** Larger flocs (>1.5 mm) sustain anoxic cores; poor settling (SVI > 150 mL/g) disrupts SND.
3. **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio (1 mark):** Sufficient biodegradable COD (BOD₅/TKN ≈ 4 – 6) is required as electron donor for denitrification. *(1 bonus mark for mentioning pH 7.0 – 8.5 or temperature ≥ 15 °C.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **microenvironment (floc) theory** of Simultaneous Nitrification and Denitrification. Derive the expression for the **critical floc radius** below which an anoxic core cannot exist. (7 marks)

**(b)** A municipal STP receives **10,000 m³/d** of wastewater with TKN = **40 mg/L** and BOD₅ = **200 mg/L**. The SND reactor achieves an effluent NH₄⁺-N of **2.0 mg/L** and effluent NO₃⁻-N of **5.0 mg/L**. Calculate the **SND efficiency** and the **daily mass of nitrogen removed** as N₂. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Sketch (2 marks):** Show spherical floc with outer aerobic shell (AOB/NOB active) and inner anoxic core (denitrifiers active). Mark DO gradient, NH₄⁺ diffusion inward, NO₃⁻ production at shell, NO₃⁻ consumption in core, N₂ release.
2. **Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):** Discuss how molecular diffusion of O₂ cannot fully penetrate large flocs; AOB consume O₂ at the periphery; deep inside, O₂ ≈ 0, allowing denitrification using carbon substrate.
3. **Derivation of Critical Radius (3 marks):** Starting from the steady-state diffusion-reaction equation (see Section 3.2), arrive at:

$$r_{crit} = \sqrt{R_f^2 - \dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} \cdot C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}}$$

If $R_f < r_{crit}$, no anoxic core → SND impossible. **Final answer in boxed form for the boundary condition** $R_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} \cdot C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}}$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Apply the SND efficiency formula (3 marks):**

$$\eta_{SND} = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{NO_3,eff}}{N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{SND} = \left(1 - \dfrac{5.0}{40 - 2.0}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{SND} = \left(1 - \dfrac{5.0}{38.0}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{SND} = (1 - 0.1316) \times 100\% = 86.84\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate NH₄⁺ actually oxidized to NO₃⁻ (1 mark):**

$$\Delta N_{NH_4 \to NO_3} = 40 - 2.0 = 38 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of N denitrified (1 mark):**

$$N_{DN} = \eta_{SND} \times \Delta N_{NH_4 \to NO_3} = 0.8684 \times 38 = 33.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to daily N₂ mass (2 marks):**

$$M_{N_2} = N_{DN} \times Q = 33.0 \text{ mg/L} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

$$M_{N_2} = 33{,}000 \text{ mg/m}^3 \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} = 3.3 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/d}$$

$$\boxed{M_{N_2} = 330 \text{ kg N}_2/\text{day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **three principal mechanisms** that allow nitrification and denitrification to occur in the same bioreactor. Compare SND with the conventional Modified Ludzack–Ettinger (MLE) process on at least **five parameters**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The SND rate in a 1500 m³ aeration tank follows the combined Monod expression: $r_{SND} = 0.45 \cdot \dfrac{N_{NH_4}}{2 + N_{NH_4}} \cdot \dfrac{DO}{0.5 + DO}$ (mg N/L·h). If the bulk DO is maintained at 1.0 mg/L and the steady-state NH₄⁺-N is 3.5 mg/L, determine the **steady-state volumetric nitrogen removal rate** and the **daily N₂ mass produced** assuming 100% conversion. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Mechanism 1 — Microenvironment/floc theory (2 marks):** explained in Section 3.2.
- **Mechanism 2 — Macroscale DO heterogeneity (2 marks):** Reactor hydrodynamics (e.g., airlift reactor, SBR cycling) create transient aerobic/anoxic micro-zones.
- **Mechanism 3 — Nitrifier denitrification (1 mark):** AOB at low DO bypass NOB.
- **Comparison Table (2 marks):** Present the comparison matrix from Section 3.4 with focus on energy, footprint, external C, sludge yield, and removal efficiency.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the Monod expression (2 marks):**

$$r_{SND} = 0.45 \cdot \dfrac{3.5}{2 + 3.5} \cdot \dfrac{1.0}{0.5 + 1.0}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first Monod term (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{3.5}{2 + 3.5} = \dfrac{3.5}{5.5} = 0.6364$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second Monod term (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1.0}{0.5 + 1.0} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the rate (1 mark):**

$$r_{SND} = 0.45 \times 0.6364 \times 0.6667 = 0.1909 \text{ mg N/(L·h)}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate daily N₂ mass (2 marks):**

$$M_{N_2,day} = r_{SND} \times V \times 24 \text{ h} = 0.1909 \times 1500 \times 1000 \text{ L} \times 24$$

$$M_{N_2,day} = 0.1909 \times 1{,}500{,}000 \times 24$$

$$\boxed{M_{N_2,day} = 6{,}872.4 \text{ g N/d} \approx 6.87 \text{ kg N}_2/\text{d}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting mg/L × m³/d → kg/d. Students frequently forget the **×10⁻³** factor. **Always show: mg/L × m³/d = 10⁻³ × kg/d.**
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Effluent NH₄⁺:** Many students write $\eta_{SND} = (1 - N_{NO_3,eff}/N_{NH_4,inf})$ — this is **wrong**. Denominator must be $(N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff})$.
> 3. **DO Units:** Ensure DO in the Monod term is in the **same units** as $K_O$ (typically mg/L). Mixing mg/L with g/m³ in the same term loses marks.
> 4. **Missing the Sketch in Q1(b):** KTU examiners allot ≥ 2 marks for a **labelled diagram** in any process question. Skipping the floc sketch costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Nitrogen Mass vs N₂ Gas Mass:** If asked for "mass of N₂ produced," the N in N₂ is the nitrogen removed by denitrification. Don't multiply by molecular weight of N₂ (28 g/mol) unless explicitly asked for N₂ gas mass.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **SND = Nitrification + Denitrification in ONE reactor** at low DO (**0.5 – 1.5 mg/L**).
- [x] **3 Mechanisms:** Microenvironment (floc core), Macroscale DO heterogeneity, Nitrifier denitrification.
- [x] **Critical floc radius** $R_f \geq \sqrt{\dfrac{6 D_{O,eff} C_{O,bulk}}{r_O}}$ for anoxic core formation (typically > **1.5 mm**).
- [x] **Optimal C/N (BOD₅/TKN) ratio:** 4 – 6.
- [x] **Optimal pH:** 7.0 – 8.5; **Optimal temperature:** 20 – 30 °C (Kerala tropical advantage).
- [x] **Minimum SRT:** 3 – 5 days for nitrifier retention.
- [x] **SND efficiency formula:** $\eta_{SND} = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{NO_3,eff}}{N_{NH_4,inf} - N_{NH_4,eff}}\right) \times 100\%$
- [x] **Reactor types:** SBR, aerobic granular sludge, MBBR, IFAS, MBR with DO gradient, Orbal ditch.
- [x] **Advantages:** 30 – 40% less aeration energy, smaller footprint, no/little external C-source, lower sludge yield.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Sensitive DO control, possible NO₂⁻ accumulation (if NOB inhibited), N₂O greenhouse gas emission risk at very low DO.
- [x] **Effluent quality benchmark:** NH₄⁺-N < 2 mg/L, NO₃⁻-N < 6 mg/L → N-removal > 85%.
- [x] **Industrial applications:** Fertilizer industry, landfill leachate, aquaculture recirculating systems, pharmaceutical effluent.
- [x] **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO1 (Apply advanced biological nitrogen removal principles)** and partially **CO2 (Design compact bioreactors for N-removal).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phosphorus Removal: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Phosphorus Removal** in wastewater engineering refers to the unit operations and processes employed to reduce the concentration of phosphorus compounds (inorganic and organic) from municipal and industrial effluents to levels prescribed by regulatory standards, typically **< 1.0 – 2.0 mg/L as P** for discharge into sensitive water bodies, or down to **< 0.5 mg/L as P** for reuse applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Phosphorus exists in three principal reactive forms in wastewater — **orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻)**, **polyphosphate (P₂O₇⁴⁻, P₃O₁₀⁵⁻, etc.)**, and **organically bound phosphorus** — and any advanced treatment scheme must address the speciation, transformation kinetics, and ultimate fate of all three.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fertilizer Problem"

Imagine a garden pond that is regularly washed with water containing dissolved fertilizer residue. Slowly, algae start carpeting the surface, blocking sunlight, choking fish, and turning the water foul. Phosphorus is that "fertilizer" — a plant nutrient (a *limiting nutrient*) that, even in **parts-per-million** concentrations, triggers runaway biological growth (eutrophication). Municipal wastewater carries phosphorus from human excreta (~0.5 kg P/person·year), detergents (~0.4 kg P/person·year historically), and food waste. Removing it is equivalent to "taking the vitamins out of the pond-water" before discharging it — chemically or biologically locking it into a solid that can be hauled away (or even recovered as a fertilizer).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Typical Values

| Parameter | Typical Domestic WW | Stricter Discharge Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Total Phosphorus (TP) | **4 – 15 mg/L as P** | **< 1.0 mg/L as P** |
| Orthophosphate (PO₄-P) | **3 – 10 mg/L as P** | < 0.5 mg/L as P |
| Polyphosphate | **1 – 5 mg/L as P** | — |
| Organic P | **< 1 mg/L as P** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Atoms & Molar Mass Reference:** Phosphorus atomic mass = **30.97 g/mol**; PO₄³⁻ molar mass = **94.97 g/mol**. Hence 1 mg P = 3.07 mg PO₄³⁻.

### 1.4 Why Phosphorus? The Regulatory & Ecological Trigger

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eutrophication Threshold:** A water body is considered *eutrophication-prone* when P concentration exceeds **0.02 mg/L** in lakes/reservoirs and **0.05 mg/L** in flowing streams.

The N:P ratio governs algal bloom potential. Algae require N and P in approximately **7:1 by mass**. Most municipal wastewaters have an N:P ratio close to this or with excess N, making **phosphorus the limiting nutrient** that, when removed, breaks the eutrophication chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Speciation Chemistry of Phosphorus

Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) is a **triprotic weak acid** with three dissociation steps governed by pKₐ values:

$$
pK_{a1} = 2.15, \quad pK_{a2} = 7.20, \quad pK_{a3} = 12.35
$$

At any pH, the relative distribution of species follows the Henderson–Hasselbalch relationships:

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{[H^+]^3}{D}, \quad \alpha_1 = \frac{[H^+]^2 K_{a1}}{D}, \quad \alpha_2 = \frac{[H^+] K_{a1} K_{a2}}{D}, \quad \alpha_3 = \frac{K_{a1} K_{a2} K_{a3}}{D}
$$

where

$$
D = [H^+]^3 + [H^+]^2 K_{a1} + [H^+] K_{a1} K_{a2} + K_{a1} K_{a2} K_{a3}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** At the typical wastewater pH of **6.5 – 7.5**, the dominant species are **H₂PO₄⁻** (≈ 60–85%) and **HPO₄²⁻** (≈ 15–40%). Hence chemical dosing calculations for precipitation are commonly done on the basis of the *ortho-P* fraction, not total P.

### 2.2 Forms of Phosphorus in Raw Wastewater

1. **Orthophosphates (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻)** — directly bioavailable; 60–85% of TP in raw sewage.
2. **Polyphosphates** — condensed forms (P₂O₇⁴⁻, P₃O₁₀⁵⁻); from industrial detergents and food processing; hydrolyze slowly to ortho-P.
3. **Organic Phosphorus** — bound in proteins, nucleic acids, lipids; ~10–30% of TP; mineralized during biological treatment.

### 2.3 Three Strategic Pillars of P Removal

**Pillar A — Chemical Precipitation**
Metal salts (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, Fe²⁺, Ca²⁺) react with PO₄³⁻ to form insoluble phosphates. Most established, robust, but produces chemical sludge.

**Pillar B — Biological Removal (EBPR / Luxury Uptake)**
Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs) take up PO₄ in excess of metabolic needs under alternating anaerobic/aerobic conditions, storing it as intracellular polyphosphate.

**Pillar C — Tertiary / Advanced Methods**
Adsorption, ion exchange, membrane filtration, struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) crystallization — often combined with P **recovery** rather than disposal.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_P = 3.07 \times C_{PO_4}$ | Convert mg/L PO₄ to mg/L P | mg/L |
| 2 | $Me : P = \dfrac{n_{metal}}{n_P}$ | Stoichiometric molar ratio | mol/mol |
| 3 | $\text{Alum dose} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{94}{31} \times C_P$ | Empirical dose for Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O | mg/L |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{[P]_{in}} = f(\text{pH, dose, mixing})$ | Removal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{Sludge yield} = k_M \times \text{dose}$ | Chemical sludge produced | kg/kg P removed |
| 6 | $\text{K}_H = \dfrac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]} = 10^{(pH - 7.20)}$ | Species ratio (within pKₐ₂ range) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Piping:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any markdown table cell to avoid breaking table syntax. E.g., write `\vert K_{sp} \vert` not `|K_sp|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Phosphorus is a **non-renewable, finite mineral resource** mined primarily from phosphate rock (Morocco, China, USA). Some estimates predict *peak phosphorus* within 50–100 years. This has transformed wastewater P from a *waste problem* to a *resource recovery opportunity*. The European Union's **Phosphate Recovery Regulation (EU 2019/1009)** mandates P recovery from sewage sludge > 50 kg P/year by 2032. Modern plants are being designed as **"WWTPs-to-Resource Recovery Facilities (RRFs)"**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molar-to-Mass Dose for Alum Precipitation

**Goal:** Express the theoretical alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O] dose required to precipitate a given concentration of orthophosphate.

**Step 1 — Write the precipitation reaction (1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$
\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow
$$

**Step 2 — Identify molar masses:**

$$
M_{Al} = 26.98 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_P = 30.97 \text{ g/mol}, \quad M_{alum} = 594 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of P in 1 L of wastewater at concentration $C_P$ (mg/L as P):**

$$
n_P = \frac{C_P \times 10^{-3}}{30.97} \text{ mol/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of Al³⁺ required (1:1):**

$$
n_{Al} = n_P
$$

**Step 5 — Each mole of alum yields 2 moles of Al³⁺:**

$$
n_{alum} = \frac{n_P}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of alum per litre:**

$$
m_{alum} = n_{alum} \times M_{alum} = \frac{C_P \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 30.97} \times 594
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the numerical coefficient:**

$$
m_{alum} = C_P \times \dfrac{594}{61.94} \times 10^{-3} \approx 9.59 \times 10^{-3} \times C_P
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{alum,\,theoretical} \approx 0.0096 \times C_P \quad \text{(g alum per L per mg/L P)}}
$$

In practice, an empirical multiplier of **1.5×** to **2.0×** the theoretical dose is used to overcome side reactions (hydroxide formation, complexation) and to drive the reaction closer to completion.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Matrix for Selecting P Removal Method

| Criterion | Chemical Precipitation | EBPR (Biological) | Struvite Crystallization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital cost** | Low–Medium | Medium–High (retrofit) | High |
| **Operating cost** | High (chemicals) | Low | Low (P recovered = revenue) |
| **Effluent P achievable** | 0.5 – 1.0 mg/L | 0.5 – 2.0 mg/L | < 0.5 mg/L in recovered stream |
| **Sludge production** | High (chemical sludge) | Low (biological) | None in effluent stream; clean precipitate |
| **P recovery** | No | No | Yes (fertilizer-grade) |
| **Sensitivity to pH** | High | Moderate | Very high (pH 8.5–9.0 optimum) |
| **Footprint** | Small | Medium | Small (post-digestion side-stream) |
| **Reliability** | Very high | Medium | High |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Simple Reactor Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A wastewater has $C_P$ = **10 mg/L as P**. Determine:
(i) Equivalent PO₄³⁻ concentration
(ii) Stoichiometric moles of Al³⁺ needed per m³
(iii) Theoretical alum dose in g/m³
(iv) Practical dose at **1.8×** the theoretical

**Solution (i):**

$$
[PO_4^{3-}] = 10 \times 3.07 = 30.7 \text{ mg/L} = 30.7 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

**Solution (ii):**

$$
n_P = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{30.97} \times 1000 = 0.323 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

So $n_{Al} = 0.323$ mol/m³.

**Solution (iii):**

$$
n_{alum} = \frac{0.323}{2} = 0.1615 \text{ mol/m}^3
$$

$$
m_{alum} = 0.1615 \times 594 = 95.9 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

**Solution (iv):**

$$
m_{alum,\,practical} = 95.9 \times 1.8 \approx 172.6 \text{ g/m}^3 \approx 173 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every m³ of 10 mg-P/L wastewater, ~173 g of commercial alum is required for chemical P precipitation — a key input to OPEX estimation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Phosphorus Forms, Transformations & Removal Pathways

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Logic for Selecting P Removal Strategy

