> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is read top-to-bottom. Each coloured block represents a process unit; arrows show the possible fate of each P form. The *architecture diagram* above helps plant engineers decide which removal strategy to deploy based on influent characteristics, plant configuration, and end-use of recovered P.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**List the three main forms of phosphorus present in municipal wastewater. Which form is biologically most available and dominant at neutral pH?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three forms are (i) **orthophosphate** (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻), (ii) **polyphosphate**, and (iii) **organic phosphorus**. At the typical wastewater pH of 6.5–7.5, **H₂PO₄⁻** is the dominant orthophosphate species (~60–85%) and is **directly bioavailable** to microorganisms. Hence ortho-P governs the immediate eutrophication potential.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is eutrophication, and how does phosphorus trigger it in receiving water bodies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Eutrophication is the nutrient-driven excessive growth of algae and aquatic plants in surface waters. Phosphorus acts as the **limiting nutrient** because the N:P ratio in most municipal wastewaters (≈ 7:1) closely matches algal demand; **even minor P additions trigger exponential algal blooms**. These blooms block sunlight, deplete dissolved oxygen upon decay, and disrupt aquatic ecosystems. Regulatory P limits of **< 1 mg/L** are imposed to prevent this cascade.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Phosphorus Removal:**
> - Students frequently **confuse PO₄³⁻ concentration with P concentration** → causes a 3× numerical error. Always clarify the basis (mg/L as P vs mg/L as PO₄).
> - Forgetting to convert **moles of metal to moles of salt** (alum yields 2 Al³⁺ per mole) costs 1–2 marks.
> - Writing "removes 100% P" without acknowledging residual equilibrium concentration is a common deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain with reactions the **chemical precipitation of phosphorus using ferric chloride (FeCl₃)**. Discuss the role of **pH**, **molar ratio (Fe:P)**, and **sludge volume** produced. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** highlighting the role of **Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)**, the function of the **anaerobic–aerobic cycle**, and the role of **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Reaction (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{FePO}_4 \downarrow \quad (\log K_{sp} \approx -23)
$$
Side reaction: $\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \downarrow$

**pH effect (2 marks):** Optimal pH is **5.0 – 7.0**. Below pH 5, Fe³⁺ stays soluble; above pH 8, Fe(OH)₃ dominates and PO₄ remains in solution, reducing removal.

**Fe:P ratio (2 marks):** Stoichiometric ratio is **1:1 molar**, but practical dose is **1.5 – 3.0× stoichiometric** to overcome competing hydrolysis reactions. Typical commercial FeCl₃ dose: **15 – 30 mg Fe per mg P removed**.

**Sludge volume (2 marks):** Each kg of P removed produces **~3 – 6 kg dry chemical sludge** (Fe(OH)₃ + FePO₄ + bound water). This increases sludge-handling costs by 30–50% over a non-precipation plant.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Anaerobic Stage (2 marks):** PAOs take up **short-chain VFAs** (acetate, propionate) and convert them to **polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs)** stored intracellularly. Hydrolysis of stored polyphosphate releases **PO₄** to bulk liquid (effluent P rises here).

**Step 2 — Aerobic Stage (3 marks):** PAOs use stored **PHAs** as carbon/energy source to **luxury-uptake PO₄** from the bulk liquid, re-storing it as intracellular polyphosphate. Net P removal = P in wasted sludge.

**Step 3 — Sludge Wasting (2 marks):** P-rich PAO biomass is wasted; this sludge contains **4 – 6% P by dry mass**, ~3× conventional activated sludge.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) crystallization process** for phosphorus recovery from anaerobically digested supernatant. State the **stoichiometry, optimum pH, and operating conditions**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **chemical precipitation** and **biological (EBPR)** methods of phosphorus removal under the following heads: **(i) Capital & operating cost, (ii) Effluent quality, (iii) Sludge handling, (iv) P recovery potential.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Reaction (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{PO}_4^{3-} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{MgNH}_4\text{PO}_4 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \downarrow
$$
Molar ratio **Mg : NH₄ : PO₄ = 1 : 1 : 1**. Struvite has low solubility ($K_{sp} \approx 10^{-13.3}$).

**Process sketch & description (3 marks):** Side-stream from anaerobic digester centrate (rich in NH₄⁺ and PO₄³⁻) is pumped into a fluidized-bed or air-driven **crystallizer/reactor**. MgCl₂ or Mg(OH)₂ is dosed; pH is raised to **8.0 – 8.5** using NaOH. Crystals nucleate on seed material (e.g., sand), grow to **1–3 mm**, and settle.

**Optimum conditions (2 marks):**
- pH: **8.0 – 9.0**
- Mg:P molar ratio: **1.05 – 1.5**
- HRT: **2 – 6 hours**
- Recovery efficiency: **70 – 90%** of P → as a slow-release fertilizer.

**Model Solution (b):** Tabulated comparison (1 mark per cell, 4 marks for narrative synthesis):

| Parameter | Chemical Precipitation | EBPR |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Capital / OPEX** | Low capex, high opex (chemicals) | Moderate capex, low opex |
| **(ii) Effluent quality** | 0.5–1.0 mg/L P reliably | 0.5–2.0 mg/L P, can fluctuate |
| **(iii) Sludge handling** | High chemical sludge (3–6 kg/kg P) | Low sludge, but P-rich (4–6% P) |
| **(iv) P recovery** | Difficult (P locked in sludge) | Possible via sludge incineration ash |

**Synthesis (3 marks):** Chemical precipitation is preferred for **retrofit upgrades** and where **reliability > cost**. EBPR is preferred for **greenfield plants** with adequate carbon (BOD:P > 20:1). Increasingly, **EBPR + struvite recovery** is chosen as a hybrid sustainable solution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three forms of P:** ortho-, poly-, organic — ortho-P dominates at neutral pH (H₂PO₄⁻).
- ✅ **Concentration unit:** Always state **"as P"** vs **"as PO₄"** — they differ by a factor of **3.07**.
- ✅ **Eutrophication trigger:** > **0.02 mg/L P** in lakes, > **0.05 mg/L P** in rivers.
- ✅ **pKₐ values:** 2.15, 7.20, 12.35 — govern speciation and dosing calculations.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Al:P = Fe:P = 1:1 (molar)**; practical dose = 1.5–2× theoretical.
- ✅ **EBPR key organisms:** PAOs (e.g., *Accumulibacter*); need **anaerobic–aerobic cycle + VFAs**.
- ✅ **Struvite formula:** MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O; optimal pH 8.0–9.0; 1:1:1 molar stoichiometry.
- ✅ **Sludge burden:** Chemical P removal adds **3–6 kg dry sludge per kg P removed**.
- ✅ **Recovery angle:** Struvite crystallization is the **future of P management** — a fertilizer, not a waste.
- ✅ **Decision rule of thumb:** BOD:P > 20:1 → EBPR viable; BOD:P < 15:1 → chemical or hybrid.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphorus Removal by Chemical Precipitation: Principles of Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phosphorus Removal by Chemical Precipitation: Principles of Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Chemical Precipitation for Phosphorus Removal** is a physico-chemical unit process in advanced wastewater treatment wherein soluble orthophosphate ions (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻) present in municipal and industrial effluents are converted into **insoluble metallic phosphate salts** through the addition of **multivalent metal cation coagulants** (typically Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, Fe²⁺, or Ca²⁺), followed by **coagulation, flocculation, and solid–liquid separation** (sedimentation/floatation/filtration).

The process is governed by the **solubility product principle (Ksp)** and operates within specific **pH envelopes** to maximise the yield of the desired precipitate. It is widely classified as a **tertiary (polishing) treatment step** because it follows conventional biological treatment, although it can also be deployed as **pre-precipitation (primary)** or **simultaneous precipitation (biological stage)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 OBE framework, students must be able to (i) write the **balanced stoichiometric reactions** for the three standard precipitants, (ii) compute **molar and mass dosage requirements** given an influent TP concentration, and (iii) justify the **choice of metal salt** based on pH, alkalinity, and sludge production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Magnet and Dust" Model

Imagine your wastewater as a glass of **muddy water** where the dissolved phosphorus compounds are invisible "specks of dust" floating around — they pass right through ordinary filters because they are dissolved at the molecular level. If you sprinkle a special chemical "magnet" (a metal salt like alum or ferric chloride) into the water, these tiny invisible specks are **captured and bundled together** into heavy, sand-like clumps (precipitates like AlPO₄ or FePO₄) that sink to the bottom. This is exactly what chemical precipitation does:

| Real-World Object | Wastewater Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Invisible dust in water | Dissolved PO₄³⁻ ions (~1–10 mg/L typical) |
| Magnetic powder sprinkled | Alum / FeCl₃ / Lime (precipitant) |
| Heavy sand clumps that sink | AlPO₄ / FePO₄ / Hydroxyapatite sludge |
| Pouring off clean water | Clarifier overflow (effluent with P < 0.5–1 mg/L) |

## 1.3 Forms of Phosphorus Targeted

Wastewater phosphorus exists in three principal forms. Chemical precipitation is **most effective against orthophosphate**; hence biological processes are often placed upstream to convert poly-P and organic-P to ortho-P first.

- **Orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻, HPO₄²⁻, H₂PO₄⁻, H₃PO₄)** — directly precipitable. **pH-dependent speciation.**
- **Polyphosphate (P₂O₇⁴⁻, P₃O₁₀⁵⁻ …)** — must be hydrolysed first (biologically or by acid).
- **Organic Phosphorus** — bound in proteins, nucleic acids; released as ortho-P during biological oxidation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speciation reminder:** At pH 7, H₂PO₄⁻ (~61 %) and HPO₄²⁻ (~39 %) dominate. At pH > 12.4, PO₄³⁻ dominates. The choice of precipitant often dictates the operating pH.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics (Bold per V10 Norms)

- **Solubility product of AlPO₄ at 25 °C: Ksp ≈ 9.84 × 10⁻²¹**
- **Solubility product of FePO₄ at 25 °C: Ksp ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻²²**
- **Stoichiometric metal-to-phosphorus molar ratio (Me:P): 1:1** for Al³⁺ and Fe³⁺; **5:3** for Ca²⁺ (as apatite).
- **Typical design dose:** **100–200 mg/L of Alum (as Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O)** or **45–90 mg/L of FeCl₃** for tertiary polishing.
- **Residual target:** **≤ 0.5 mg P/L** (discharge norm for sensitive recipients) and **≤ 1.0 mg P/L** (general KTU/KSPCB standard).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Big Picture" Operational Logic

The chemical precipitation process follows a **5-stage cascade** that mirrors the broader coagulation–flocculation chemistry taught in CET/MET modules:

1. **Dosage & Rapid Mixing (G ≈ 700–1000 s⁻¹, 30–60 s)** — Disperses the metal-salt hydrolysing species uniformly; allows formation of initial mono-/polynuclear hydrolysis products such as Al(H₂O)₆³⁺ → Al(OH)²⁺ → polymeric Al₁₃.
2. **Precipitation / Nucleation (T ≈ 1–5 min)** — Metal ion reacts with orthophosphate to form the insoluble Meₓ(PO₄)ᵧ nucleus once the **ionic product [Me³⁺]ⁿ[PO₄³⁻]ᵐ > Ksp**.
3. **Coagulation (Slow; G ≈ 20–75 s⁻¹, 20–30 min)** — Sweep flocculation by hydroxide co-precipitate (e.g., Al(OH)₃(s) or Fe(OH)₃(s)) enmeshes fine phosphate colloids.
4. **Flocculation (G ≈ 10–30 s⁻¹, 30 min)** — Gentle bridging by long-chain polymers or natural floc aids (e.g., polyacrylamide).
5. **Solid–Liquid Separation** — Settling in clarifier (most common), DAF (for aluminium and iron flocs), or filtration (for residual colloids).

## 2.2 Why & How — The Chemistry Behind Each Step

- **Why a pH window?** The metal phosphate salt is least soluble within a narrow pH band. Outside this band, the metal converts to its amphoteric hydroxide (Al(OH)₃ dissolves at pH > 9 to Al(OH)₄⁻) and re-dissolution of phosphate occurs.
- **Why is the *actual* dose higher than the *stoichiometric* dose?** Co-precipitation, surface adsorption on Me(OH)₃ floc, and competing side reactions (carbonates, hydroxides, NOM complexation) consume metal ions that are *not* delivered to phosphate. Typical **applied-to-stoichiometric dose ratio = 1.5 to 2.5** for alum and iron.
- **Why lime at high pH?** Lime does not directly form Ca₃(PO₄)₂ — it forms **hydroxyapatite (HAP) Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH**, the most thermodynamically stable calcium phosphate, which requires alkaline conditions (pH > 9.5) to precipitate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** and appear repeatedly in KTU Part A and Part B questions. Master these and you can answer any numerical on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Conditions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Me}^{n+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{MePO}_4 \downarrow$ | Generic 1:1 precipitation for Al³⁺ and Fe³⁺ | — |
| 2 | $5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \downarrow$ | Hydroxyapatite (HAP) — for lime | — |
| 3 | $D_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{salt}}}{M_{\text{P}}} \times [\text{P}]_{\text{in}} \times R$ | Stoichiometric dose of salt; $R$ = required mol Me/mol P (1 for Al/Fe, 1.67 for Ca) | mg/L |
| 4 | $D_{\text{actual}} = \beta \cdot D_{\text{stoich}}$ | Actual dose; safety factor $\beta = 1.5$–$2.5$ (Al/Fe) or $1.05$–$1.5$ (lime) | mg/L |
| 5 | $\text{Alum dose (mg/L)} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{594}{31} \cdot [\text{P}] \approx 19.2\,\beta \cdot [\text{P}]$ | Using Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O (MW 594) and P (MW 31) | mg/L |
| 6 | $\text{FeCl}_3 \text{ dose (mg/L)} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{162.2}{31} \cdot [\text{P}] \approx 5.23\,\beta \cdot [\text{P}]$ | Using FeCl₃ (MW 162.2) and P | mg/L |
| 7 | $\text{Lime dose as Ca(OH)}_2 \text{ (mg/L)} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 74}{3 \times 31} \cdot [\text{P}] \approx 3.98\,\beta \cdot [\text{P}]$ | HAP stoichiometry, Ca(OH)₂ MW 74 | mg/L |
| 8 | $\text{Alkalinity destroyed (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = 5.4 \cdot [\text{Alum}]$ | For alum — accounts for bicarbonate neutralisation | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 9 | $\text{Sludge produced (kg/d)} = Q \cdot (D \cdot Y_{\text{Me(OH)}_3} + 3.07\,[\Delta\text{P}])$ | $Q$ = flow (m³/d); $Y$ = yield of Me(OH)₃ per g of salt; $[\Delta P]$ = P removed | kg/d |
| 10 | $K_{sp} = [\text{Me}^{n+}]^a [\text{PO}_4^{3-}]^b$ | For equilibrium residual P at pH 7: $[\text{P}]_{\text{res}} \approx \dfrac{K_{sp}}{[\text{Me}^{n+}] \cdot \alpha_3}$ | mol/L |

> Note on the table: in line 10, **$\alpha_3$** is the **fractional dissociation factor** converting total soluble P to PO₄³⁻ at operating pH (e.g., at pH 7, $\alpha_3 \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-6}$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs in Kerala (e.g., Brahmapuram, Cochin; STP at Trivandrum Corporation):** Alum-based simultaneous precipitation is the most common upgrade for eutrophication control of Vembanad-type backwaters.
- **Industrial applications:** Poultry & dairy effluent (high P), fertilizer plant effluents, semiconductor (uses NH₄⁺ + PO₄³⁻ waste streams) — lime precipitation is preferred due to low cost.
- **Reuse / Struvite Recovery:** Mg²⁺ + NH₄⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → struvite (MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O) is an emerging variant used in sludge-streams from anaerobic digesters.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Stoichiometry of the Three Standard Precipitants

### 3.1.1 Aluminium Sulphate (Alum) — Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O

**Balanced molecular reaction (synthetic water, pH ≈ 6.0–7.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3\cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} &\longrightarrow 2\text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 14\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Per mole of orthophosphate-P removed:
- Moles of Al required = 1 → moles of Alum = 1/2
- Mass of Alum per mg of P = $\dfrac{594 \, \text{g/mol}}{2 \times 31 \, \text{g/mol}} = 9.58$ mg Alum per mg P
- With applied-to-stoichiometric factor $\beta = 1.5$: **Alum dose ≈ 14.4 mg per mg P**.

> [!NOTE]
> In real wastewater, bicarbonate alkalinity consumes part of the alum. Each mole of Al³⁺ hydrolyses:
> $$\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{HCO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3\downarrow + 3\text{CO}_2$$
> Hence a fraction of the dose is "wasted" on alkalinity destruction. The **effective dose for P removal** must be computed by:
> $$D_{\text{eff}} = D_{\text{applied}} - 0.4 \cdot \text{Alk}\ (\text{as mg/L CaCO}_3) \cdot \dfrac{594}{300}$$

### 3.1.2 Ferric Chloride — FeCl₃

**Balanced reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{PO}_4^{3-} &\longrightarrow \text{FePO}_4\downarrow + 3\text{Cl}^-
\end{aligned}
$$

Per mg of P:
- Mass of FeCl₃ per mg P = $\dfrac{162.2}{31} = 5.23$ mg FeCl₃ per mg P
- With $\beta = 1.5$: **FeCl₃ dose ≈ 7.85 mg per mg P**.

**Fe²⁺ (ferrous) pathway** — used when alkalinity is low and pH 7–8 is acceptable:
$$
\begin{aligned}
3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} &\longrightarrow \text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2\downarrow \\
\text{but also: } 4\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 10\text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3 + 8\text{H}^+
\end{aligned}
$$
The Fe²⁺ route is rarely used for **tertiary polishing** because it requires oxidation; common in **biological** P-removal.

### 3.1.3 Lime — Ca(OH)₂

**Hydroxyapatite (HAP) pathway (most common, pH > 9.5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}\downarrow
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative dicalcium phosphate (DCPD) at pH 7–8 (less common):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{HPO}_4^{2-} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{CaHPO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\downarrow
\end{aligned}
$$

Per mg of P for HAP:
- Moles of Ca(OH)₂ per mole of P = 5/3 → mass ratio = $\dfrac{(5/3)\times 74}{31} = 3.98$ mg Ca(OH)₂ per mg P
- With $\beta = 1.05$: **Ca(OH)₂ dose ≈ 4.18 mg per mg P**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark KTU Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Model):** A municipal STP handles **$Q = 50{,}000$ m³/d** of wastewater with **influent TP = 8 mg/L**. Tertiary chemical precipitation is to be designed using **(a) Alum, (b) FeCl₃, (c) Lime**. Compute the **stoichiometric dose**, **actual dose (with $\beta$)**, **daily chemical requirement in kg/d**, and **estimate sludge mass produced**. Use $\beta$ = 1.5, 1.5, 1.05 for alum, FeCl₃, lime respectively.

### Step 1 — Stoichiometric mass ratios (we derived above)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alum} &: \dfrac{594}{2 \times 31} = 9.58\ \text{mg per mg P} \\
\text{FeCl}_3 &: \dfrac{162.2}{31} = 5.23\ \text{mg per mg P} \\
\text{Ca(OH)}_2 &: \dfrac{5\times 74}{3\times 31} = 3.98\ \text{mg per mg P}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric dose (mg/L) for each precipitant

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{stoich, alum}} &= 9.58 \times 8 = 76.64\ \text{mg/L} \\
D_{\text{stoich, FeCl}_3} &= 5.23 \times 8 = 41.84\ \text{mg/L} \\
D_{\text{stoich, lime}} &= 3.98 \times 8 = 31.84\ \text{mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Actual dose (mg/L)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{act, alum}} &= 1.5 \times 76.64 = 114.96 \approx 115\ \text{mg/L} \\
D_{\text{act, FeCl}_3} &= 1.5 \times 41.84 = 62.76 \approx 63\ \text{mg/L} \\
D_{\text{act, lime}} &= 1.05 \times 31.84 = 33.43 \approx 33.5\ \text{mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Daily chemical requirement (kg/d)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alum} &: 115 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 5{,}750\ \text{kg/d} \\
\text{FeCl}_3 &: 63 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 3{,}150\ \text{kg/d} \\
\text{Lime} &: 33.5 \times 50{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 1{,}675\ \text{kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sludge mass estimate (per kg Chemical Engineering convention)

For **alum**, sludge ≈ dry solids per kg Alum = 0.26 kg Al(OH)₃ + 0.32 kg AlPO₄ + biomass contribution. A quick rule:
- Alum sludge ≈ $2.5 \times$ **mass of Alum applied** ≈ $2.5 \times 5{,}750 = 14{,}375$ kg dry solids/d.

For **FeCl₃**:
- FeCl₃ sludge ≈ $2.0 \times$ mass applied ≈ $2.0 \times 3{,}150 = 6{,}300$ kg/d.

For **Lime (HAP + CaCO₃):**
- Lime sludge ≈ $3.0 \times$ mass applied ≈ $3.0 \times 1{,}675 = 5{,}025$ kg/d.

### Step 6 — Tabulated Comparison (analytical matrix)

| Parameter | Alum (Al³⁺) | FeCl₃ (Fe³⁺) | Lime (Ca²⁺) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating pH | 5.5 – 7.0 | 4.5 – 5.5 (Fe³⁺) | > 9.5 |
| Stoich dose (mg/L) | 76.6 | 41.8 | 31.8 |
| Actual dose (mg/L) | 115 | 63 | 33.5 |
| Daily chemical (kg/d) | 5,750 | 3,150 | 1,675 |
| Sludge (kg/d dry) | ~14,400 | ~6,300 | ~5,000 |
| Alkalinity impact | High consumption | Moderate | Re-carbonation needed |
| Equipment corrosion | Mild | Severe (Cl⁻) | Mild |
| Cost (relative) | Moderate | High | Lowest |
| Residual P achievable | 0.3–0.5 mg/L | 0.2–0.5 mg/L | 0.5–1.0 mg/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Take-away:** Among the three, **FeCl₃** delivers the lowest residual P but is the most expensive; **lime** is cheapest chemically but the high-pH operation increases O&M complexity (re-carbonation, scaling); **alum** is the most widely deployed in India for the cost–performance balance.

## 3.3 Equilibrium Residual P Derivation (the $\alpha_3$ Method)

The residual soluble P at equilibrium with a metal phosphate solid of $K_{sp}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{P}]_{\text{res}} \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{Me}^{n+}]_{\text{res}} \, K_{sp}}{\alpha_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha_3$ is obtained from the triprotic phosphoric acid equilibria ($K_1 = 7.1 \times 10^{-3}$, $K_2 = 6.2 \times 10^{-8}$, $K_3 = 4.4 \times 10^{-13}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_3 = \dfrac{K_1 K_2 K_3}{[\text{H}^+]^3 + K_1[\text{H}^+]^2 + K_1 K_2 [\text{H}^+] + K_1 K_2 K_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

At pH 7 ($[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-7}$), substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_3 &= \dfrac{(7.1\times10^{-3})(6.2\times10^{-8})(4.4\times10^{-13})}{(10^{-7})^3 + (7.1\times10^{-3})(10^{-7})^2 + (4.4\times10^{-20})(10^{-7}) + (1.94\times10^{-22})} \\
&\approx \dfrac{1.94\times10^{-22}}{1.0\times10^{-21} + 7.1\times10^{-17} + 1.94\times10^{-27} + 1.94\times10^{-22}} \\
&\approx \dfrac{1.94\times10^{-22}}{7.1\times10^{-17}} \approx 4.7\times10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **alum** with residual $[\text{Al}^{3+}] = 10^{-6}$ M (typical at pH 7, mostly as Al(OH)₃):

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{P}]_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{(10^{-6})(9.84\times10^{-21})}{4.7\times10^{-6}} \approx 2.1\times10^{-21}\ \text{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

This extremely low value is the *theoretical* floor; practical residual is governed by **kinetics, mixing, and competing ligands**, not by Ksp.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List the three principal chemical precipitants used for phosphorus removal from wastewater. State the operating pH window and the typical residual P achieved by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal chemical precipitants are:

1. **Aluminium sulphate (Alum, Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O):** Operating pH **5.5 – 7.0**; residual P of **0.3 – 0.5 mg/L** achievable.
2. **Ferric chloride (FeCl₃):** Operating pH **4.5 – 5.5** (for Fe³⁺) or **7 – 8** (Fe²⁺ with oxidation); residual P of **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L**.
3. **Lime (Ca(OH)₂) → Hydroxyapatite:** Operating pH **> 9.5**; residual P of **0.5 – 1.0 mg/L** achievable with recarbonation.

**Mark-split tip:** 1 mark for naming the three precipitants + 1 mark for pH windows + 1 mark for residuals.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Why is the actual dose of metal salt always greater than the stoichiometric dose in chemical P-precipitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The actual dose exceeds stoichiometric for **three reasons**: (1) **side reactions** of the metal ion with wastewater **alkalinity (HCO₃⁻)** and **natural organic matter (NOM)**, which consume a portion of the metal without precipitating P; (2) **co-precipitation and adsorption losses** of P onto Me(OH)₃ flocs, which require a higher dissolved metal concentration than the simple 1:1 stoichiometry implies; (3) **mixing inefficiencies** and **kinetic limitations** at low residual P, which necessitate a safety factor β (typically 1.5–2.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU 2024 Model, 14 marks]**
**(a)** [7 marks] With balanced chemical equations, explain the principle of phosphorus removal by **chemical precipitation using aluminium sulphate (alum) and lime**. State the operating pH and identify the precipitate formed in each case.

**(b)** [7 marks] A wastewater treatment plant receives **40,000 m³/d** of secondary effluent containing **TP = 6 mg/L**. Design a **tertiary chemical precipitation unit using FeCl₃** to achieve a residual TP of **1 mg/L**. Compute (i) actual dose, (ii) daily FeCl₃ requirement in kg/d, and (iii) estimated daily dry sludge mass. Assume $\beta = 1.5$.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Principle with Alum and Lime — 7 marks

**Alum pathway:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3\cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} &\longrightarrow 2\text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow + 3\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 14\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Operating pH window: **5.5 – 7.0** (slightly acidic to neutral)
- Precipitate formed: **Aluminium phosphate (AlPO₄)** — a white, gelatinous solid; co-precipitate Al(OH)₃ assists sweep flocculation.
- Side reaction (alkalinity destruction):
  $$\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{HCO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3\downarrow + 3\text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

**Lime pathway:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5\text{Ca}^{2+} + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{OH}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}\downarrow \quad \text{(Hydroxyapatite, HAP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Operating pH: **> 9.5** (typically 10.5–11.5 for efficient P removal)
- Precipitate: **Hydroxyapatite (Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH)** — dense, crystalline, easy to thicken.
- **Re-carbonation** with CO₂ is required downstream to lower pH before discharge.
- **Mark distribution:** 3 marks for alum reaction + 2 marks for lime reaction + 1 mark pH + 1 mark precipitate identity.

#### (b) FeCl₃ Design — 7 marks

**Given:** $Q = 40{,}000$ m³/d, TP in = 6 mg/L, TP out = 1 mg/L, $\beta = 1.5$.

**Step 1 — P removed:**
$$[\Delta\text{P}] = 6 - 1 = 5\ \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric FeCl₃ dose (1:1 mol ratio, MW FeCl₃ = 162.2, P = 31):**
$$
D_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{162.2}{31}\times 6 = 5.23 \times 6 = 31.38\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual dose:**
$$
D_{\text{act}} = \beta \times D_{\text{stoich}} = 1.5 \times 31.38 = 47.07 \approx 47.1\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily FeCl₃ requirement:**
$$
M_{\text{FeCl}_3} = 47.1 \times 40{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 1{,}884\ \text{kg/d}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily dry sludge mass** (using factor ≈ 2.0 × mass of FeCl₃ applied, since FePO₄ + Fe(OH)₃ both contribute):
$$
M_{\text{sludge}} \approx 2.0 \times 1{,}884 = 3{,}768\ \text{kg dry solids/d}
$$

A more refined estimate:
- FePO₄ sludge = $\dfrac{151}{31} \times 5 \times 40{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 974$ kg/d
- Fe(OH)₃ from excess Fe = $(47.1 - 31.4) \times 40{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{107}{162.2} = 414$ kg/d
- **Total ≈ 1,388 kg dry/d** (after P removal only, before considering Fe(OH)₃ from alkalinity).

**Mark-split tip:** 1 mark setup + 2 marks stoichiometry + 1 mark $\beta$ + 1 mark kg/d + 2 marks sludge.

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice — [KTU 2024 Model, 14 marks]**
**(a)** [7 marks] Compare **alum, ferric chloride, and lime** as chemical precipitants for phosphorus removal. Your answer must cover operating pH, sludge volume, alkalinity impact, cost, and residual P achievable. *(Use a comparison table for clarity.)*

**(b)** [7 marks] The **Brahmapuram STP (Cochin)** is to be upgraded with a tertiary chemical P-removal step. The design flow is **30 MLD**, influent TP = **7 mg/L**, target effluent TP = **< 1 mg/L**, available wastewater alkalinity = **120 mg/L as CaCO₃**. Determine the **alum dose (mg/L) and kg/d**, accounting for the alkalinity consumption. Take $\beta = 1.5$ and use the formula:
$$\text{Alum effective} = \text{Alum applied} - 0.4 \times \text{Alk}\times \dfrac{594}{300}$$

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Comparison Table — 7 marks

| Criterion | Alum (Al³⁺) | FeCl₃ (Fe³⁺) | Lime (Ca²⁺) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating pH | 5.5 – 7.0 | 4.5 – 5.5 | > 9.5 |
| Sludge mass (relative) | 2.5× dose | 2.0× dose | 3.0× dose |
| Sludge settleability | Light, gelatinous | Better than alum | Heavy, good settling |
| Alkalinity impact | High consumption | Moderate | Alkalinity produced; recarbonation needed |
| Corrosion to equipment | Mild | Severe (chloride) | Mild |
| Relative cost (chemical) | Moderate | Highest | Lowest |
| Achievable residual P | 0.3–0.5 mg/L | 0.2–0.5 mg/L | 0.5–1.0 mg/L |
| Tolerance to low T | Poor | Moderate | Good |

**Verdict:** FeCl₃ gives the cleanest effluent; lime is cheapest; alum is the **best balance** for tropical Indian conditions.

#### (b) Alum Dose Accounting for Alkalinity — 7 marks

**Given:** $Q = 30{,}000$ m³/d, TP = 7 mg/L, Alk = 120 mg/L as CaCO₃, $\beta = 1.5$.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric alum dose:**
$$
D_{\text{stoich}} = 9.58 \times 7 = 67.06\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Uncorrected actual dose:**
$$
D_{\text{uncorr}} = 1.5 \times 67.06 = 100.6\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Alkalinity correction** (per the given formula):
$$
D_{\text{corr}} = 100.6 - 0.4 \times 120 \times \dfrac{594}{300} = 100.6 - 95.04 = 5.56\ \text{mg/L}
$$

This indicates that the wastewater alkalinity would consume almost all of the alum if not supplemented. In practice, we **increase the dose** to overcome alkalinity:

**Step 4 — Re-apply the actual dose with the corrected residual target:**
The **applied dose required to leave enough Al for P precipitation** is:
$$
D_{\text{applied}} = D_{\text{stoich}} + 0.4 \times \text{Alk} \times \dfrac{594}{300} = 67.06 + 95.04 = 162.1\ \text{mg/L}
$$

(equivalently, $\beta_{\text{eff}} = 162.1 / 67.06 = 2.42$)

**Step 5 — Daily alum requirement:**
$$
M_{\text{alum}} = 162.1 \times 30{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 4{,}863\ \text{kg/d} \approx 4.86\ \text{tonnes/d}
$$

**Mark split:** 2 marks for stoichiometry + 2 marks for alkalinity correction + 2 marks for daily kg/d + 1 mark for engineering interpretation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing *stoichiometric* and *applied* doses** — Many students write "alum dose = 9.58 × TP" without multiplying by $\beta$. This loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the molecular weight of the *salt*, not the *metal*** — Alum is 594 g/mol, not 27 g/mol. A very common KTU mistake.
> 3. **Writing "PO₄³⁻" at pH 7** — At neutral pH, the dominant species is H₂PO₄⁻, not PO₄³⁻. Examiners expect you to mention pH-dependent speciation.
> 4. **Ignoring alkalinity consumption in real wastewater** — Bookwork says 1 mol Al per mol P; field reality is $\beta = 1.5$–$2.5$ due to bicarbonate. KTU questions from 2022–2024 specifically test this.
> 5. **Not balancing the equations properly** — Lime gives HAP (Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH), *not* Ca₃(PO₄)₂. Writing the wrong product is a 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Units slip** — Mixing mg/L and g/L, or forgetting ×10⁻³ when converting mg/L × m³/d to kg/d. Lose 1–2 marks per numerical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These 12 Bullets Before Walking into the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ Chemical precipitation works **only on orthophosphate**; biological conversion of poly-P and organic-P is essential upstream.
- ✅ **Three standard precipitants:** Alum (pH 5.5–7), FeCl₃ (pH 4.5–5.5), Lime (pH > 9.5 → HAP).
- ✅ **1:1 molar Me:P** for Al³⁺ and Fe³⁺; **5:3 Ca:P** for lime (HAP).
- ✅ **Mass ratios to remember (per mg P):** Alum → 9.58; FeCl₃ → 5.23; Ca(OH)₂ → 3.98.
- ✅ **Safety factor β** = 1.5–2.5 (Al/Fe) and 1.05–1.5 (lime); always multiply stoichiometric dose by β.
- ✅ **Alkalinity consumption:** Each mole of Al³⁺ destroys 3 moles of HCO₃⁻ → check alkalinity before sizing the dose.
- ✅ **Sludge production factor:** ≈ 2.0–3.0 × mass of salt applied (for Al ≈ 2.5×, for Fe ≈ 2.0×, for lime ≈ 3.0×).
- ✅ **Re-carbonation step** is mandatory after lime precipitation (to lower pH from ~11 to ~7).
- ✅ **FeCl₃** gives the lowest residual P but is the most expensive and corrosive.
- ✅ **Lime** is cheapest and produces the heaviest, best-settling sludge, but the highest O&M complexity.
- ✅ **Solubility products:** Ksp(AlPO₄) ≈ 9.84 × 10⁻²¹; Ksp(FePO₄) ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻²² — HAP is even less soluble.
- ✅ **Design residual target:** ≤ 0.5 mg P/L for sensitive water bodies (Vembanad Lake, KSPCB norm) and ≤ 1.0 mg P/L for general discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-link to other modules:** Chemical precipitation is often coupled with **Module 2 (Enhanced Biological P Removal, EBPR)** — the sludge produced can be co-thickened with EBPR waste activated sludge. **Module 3 (Membrane processes)** may follow for polishing residual P to < 0.1 mg/L for water reuse applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemicals Applied
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemicals Applied # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT)**, the term **"Chemicals Applied"** refers to the systematic addition of specific inorganic or organic chemical reagents at predetermined unit operations to achieve targeted objectives such as coagulation–flocculation, precipitation, disinfection, pH adjustment, oxidation–reduction, nutrient (Nitrogen & Phosphorus) removal, defoaming, and sludge conditioning. These chemicals act as primary process agents that alter the **physico-chemical state** of the wastewater matrix, enabling the removal of colloidal, suspended, dissolved, and microbial constituents that cannot be removed effectively by physical means alone. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Framework** > The KTU 2024 scheme classifies applied chemicals into functional groups: > (i) **Coagulants & Flocculants** (e.g., Alum, PAC, Polyelectrolytes) > (ii) **Disinfectants** (e.g., Chlorine, Ozone, Chlorine Dioxide) > (iii) **pH Adjusters / Alkalinity Controllers** (e.g., Lime, Caustic Soda, Sulfuric Acid) > (iv) **Oxidants** (e.g., H₂O₂, KMnO₄, ClO₂) > (v) **Nutrient Removal Reagents** (e.g., Ferric Chloride for P, Methanol for N) > (vi) **Antifoaming / Sludge Conditioning Agents** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detergent Principle" Imagine washing a heavily greased cloth plate with plain water — the grease simply smears around. Add a small quantity of **dish soap (a chemical agent)** and the grease breaks into tiny droplets that get trapped in the water. The soap is the *chemical*, and the action is *coagulation/oxidation*. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Parallel:** In water treatment, an oily industrial effluent behaves like the greasy plate. Adding **Aluminium Sulphate (Alum)** acts exactly like the soap — it neutralizes the electrostatic repulsion on colloidal particles, allowing them to clump together (flocculate) and settle out. The same "detergent principle" applies to **chlorine killing bacteria** (disrupting cell walls) and **lime precipitating phosphates** (forming insoluble calcium phosphate sludge). ## 1.3 Why Chemicals are Indispensable in AWT - **Colloidal Stability:** Suspended particles (< 1 μm) carry surface charges (typically negative) and **Brownian motion** keeps them in permanent suspension. Only chemicals can destabilize them. - **Solubility Barriers:** Dissolved species like PO₄³⁻, NH₃, and heavy metals require **chemical precipitation** to convert them into separable solids. - **Microbial Threat:** Pathogens (E. coli, coliforms, viruses) require **disinfectant residuals** to meet KTU-mandated reuse/recreational standards. - **Reuse Standards:** Tertiary treated water for irrigation/industrial reuse demands chemical polishing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Dosing Metric Used in KTU Examinations** > Chemical dose is universally reported in **mg/L** (milligrams per litre) or **kg/d** (kilograms per day) when the plant flow rate is known. Always remember: **1 mg/L = 1 g/m³**. ## 1.4 Standard Classification Table — Chemicals Applied in AWT | Functional Group | Primary Purpose | Typical Chemical Examples | |------------------|----------------|---------------------------| | Coagulants | Destabilize colloids | Alum, Ferric Chloride, PAC | | Flocculants | Aggregate destabilized particles | Anionic/Cationic Polyacrylamides | | Disinfectants | Pathogen inactivation | Cl₂, NaOCl, O₃, ClO₂ | | pH Adjusters | Maintain optimal pH | Lime [Ca(OH)₂], H₂SO₄, NaOH | | Precipitants | Nutrient removal | Lime, FeCl₃, Alum (for P) | | Oxidants | Break complex organics | H₂O₂, KMnO₄, O₃ | | Antifoaming Agents | Suppress biological foam | Silicone-based defoamers | | Sludge Conditioners | Improve dewatering | Polyelectrolytes, Lime | # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Chemical Action in Wastewater The addition of chemicals in AWT follows a **four-stage mechanistic chain** that every KTU student must internalize: ### Stage 1 — **Dosing & Rapid Mixing (Flash Mixing)** - The chemical is injected into the wastewater at a **highly turbulent zone** with **velocity gradient (G) = 700 to 1000 s⁻¹**. - Purpose: Uniform dispersion of the chemical before any reaction proceeds. - Typical detention time: **20 to 60 seconds**. ### Stage 2 — **Reaction (Coagulation)** - Chemical species (e.g., Al³⁺ from Alum) hydrolyze to form **cationic hydrolysis products** like $\text{Al}^{3+}$, $\text{Al(OH)}^{2+}$, $\text{Al(OH)}_2^+$, and polynuclear species $\text{Al}_{13}\text{O}_4(\text{OH})_{24}^{7+}$. - These neutralize the negative surface charge of colloids (**Zeta potential → 0 mV**). - Time: **1 to 5 minutes**. ### Stage 3 — **Flocculation** - Slow mixing at **G = 20 to 75 s⁻¹** allows micro-flocs to collide and grow into settleable flocs. - Flocculants (polymers) bridge the destabilized particles via **inter-particle bridging**. ### Stage 4 — **Separation** - Flocs settle (clarifier), float (DAF), or are filtered out. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Charge Neutralization** > Colloids in wastewater carry negative charges (~ -20 mV Zeta potential). The **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)** theory explains that Al³⁺ ions (trivalent) compress the EDL three times more effectively than monovalent ions — this is why **alum is preferred over NaCl** for coagulation. ## 2.2 Detailed Chemistry of Major Chemicals ### 2.2.1 Aluminium Sulphate (Alum) — $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ The **stoichiometric hydrolysis** of alum in wastewater: $$\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Al(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$ **Key Insight:** The reaction produces **H₂SO₄** (acid), which **lowers pH**. Therefore, **lime is often co-applied** to buffer alkalinity. ### 2.2.2 Ferric Chloride — $\text{FeCl}_3$ $$\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{HCl}$$ - Effective across a **wider pH range (4 to 11)** than alum. - Excellent for **phosphate precipitation**: $$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{FePO}_4 \downarrow$$ ### 2.2.3 Lime — $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ Used for **pH elevation**, **softening**, and **phosphorus removal**: $$5\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + 3\text{HPO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \downarrow + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\text{OH}^-$$ ### 2.2.4 Chlorine — $\text{Cl}_2$ In water, chlorine forms **hypochlorous acid**, the active disinfectant: $$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$ $$\text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^-$$ **HOCl is 80 times more effective than OCl⁻** as a disinfectant, so **lower pH (≈ 7) is preferred**. ### 2.2.5 Ozone — $\text{O}_3$ A **powerful oxidant** that decomposes to produce hydroxyl radicals: $$\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HO}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$ - Standard Reduction Potential: **E⁰ = +2.07 V** (versus +1.36 V for Cl₂). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following formulas are TESTED repeatedly in KTU ESE examinations. Memorize the units.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Engineering Use | |---|-----------|---------|------|-----------------| | 1 | Chemical Feed Rate (dry) | $M = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6}$ | kg/d | Total mass to be dosed per day | | 2 | Chemical Feed Rate (solution) | $M_s = \dfrac{Q \times D \times 100}{C \times 10^6}$ | kg/d | For liquid chemicals of strength C % | | 3 | Solution Feed Rate (volume) | $Q_s = \dfrac{M_s}{\rho_s \times 1000}$ | m³/d | Liquid chemical volumetric feed | | 4 | Stoichiometric Alum Dose (Turbidity) | $D_{\text{alum}} = 0.18 \times T$ (approx.) | mg/L | Empirical rule of thumb | | 5 | Chlorine Demand | $\text{Cl}_2 \text{ demand} = \text{Cl}_2 \text{ applied} - \text{Cl}_2 \text{ residual}$ | mg/L | Design of contact chamber | | 6 | Lime Requirement (for P) | $L = 1.5 \times P$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Empirical for P removal | | 7 | CT Value (Disinfection) | $\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times t$ | mg·min/L | KTU/US EPA standard | | 8 | Velocity Gradient | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$ | s⁻¹ | Flocculator design | | 9 | Camp–Steele G·t value | $G \cdot t$ | dimensionless | Flocculation performance | | 10 | Coagulant dose in moles | $n = \dfrac{D}{M_w \times 1000}$ | mmol/L | Molar stoichiometry | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Formula 1 — The "10⁶ Divisor" Logic** > If $Q$ is in m³/d and $D$ in mg/L, the unit conversion is: > $\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{d}} \times \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ mg}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ g}} = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{d}} \times \dfrac{1}{10^6}$ (i.e., $\dfrac{1}{10^6}$ is the cumulative unit conversion factor). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Municipal STPs in Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Trivandrum):** Use **PAC (Polyaluminium Chloride)** due to its low alkalinity consumption — ideal for **soft, low-alkalinity water** typical of Western Ghats catchments. - **Industrial ETPs (Textile, Dairy):** Use **lime + alum** for colour removal and **H₂O₂** for COD polishing. - **Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETPs):** Use **polyelectrolytes** to enhance settling of recalcitrant colloids. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation I — Chemical Feed Rate from First Principles ### Problem Context Given a wastewater flow rate $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{d}$ and a desired dose $D$ in mg/L, derive the **mass feed rate of chemical per day**. ### Step 1 — Start with the definitions Let: - $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate of wastewater - $D$ = Required dose of chemical (mg/L) ### Step 2 — Convert mg/L to a mass-per-volume equivalent in SI By definition: $$1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$$ Because: $$1 \text{ mg/L} = \dfrac{10^{-3} \text{ g}}{10^{-3} \text{ m}^3} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$$ ### Step 3 — Multiply by flow rate to get mass per day The total mass of chemical to be added per day is: $$M = Q \times D \quad \text{(in g/d, if Q is in m}^3\text{/d and D is in g/m}^3\text{)}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert grams to kilograms $$M = \dfrac{Q \times D}{1000} \quad \text{(kg/d)}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute D in mg/L (more common in KTU papers) Since $1 \text{ mg} = 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$, we have: $$M = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6} \quad \text{(kg/d)}$$ ### Final Equation $$\boxed{M = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6} \text{ kg/d}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Feed Rate for Commercial (Impure) Chemicals ### Problem Context The commercial chemical available in the market is **not 100% pure**. For a chemical with purity $P$ (as a fraction, e.g., 0.96 for 96% pure alum), the **commercial mass required** is: ### Step 1 — Start with the pure mass required $$M_{\text{pure}} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6} \text{ kg/d}$$ ### Step 2 — Account for impurities If the chemical is only $P$ fraction pure, the mass of the commercial product must be **scaled up** by $\dfrac{1}{P}$: $$M_{\text{commercial}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{pure}}}{P} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{P \times 10^6} \text{ kg/d}$$ ### Step 3 — Account for moisture content $M_c$ (optional) If the chemical contains moisture fraction $M_c$ (e.g., 0.05 for 5% moisture), the wet mass is: $$M_{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{commercial}}}{1 - M_c}$$ ### Final General Equation $$\boxed{M_{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{P \times (1 - M_c) \times 10^6} \text{ kg/d}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Worked Numerical Example (Alum Dose) ### Problem A wastewater treatment plant receives a flow of **$Q = 25,000$ m³/d**. The jar test indicates an optimum **alum dose of $D = 30$ mg/L**. The alum is available as **commercial aluminium sulphate with 96% purity**. Calculate: (a) The mass of pure alum required per day. (b) The mass of commercial alum required per day. ### Step (a) — Pure Alum Mass Using the equation: $$M_{\text{pure}} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6}$$ Substituting: $$M_{\text{pure}} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 30}{10^6} = \dfrac{750{,}000}{10^6}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{pure}} = 0.75 \text{ kg/d}}$$ ### Step (b) — Commercial Alum Mass Using the purity correction: $$M_{\text{commercial}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{pure}}}{P} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.96}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{commercial}} = 0.78125 \text{ kg/d} \approx 0.78 \text{ kg/d}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Stoichiometric Lime Dose for Phosphorus Removal ### Step 1 — Reaction $$5\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + 3\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH} \downarrow + 9\text{OH}^-$$ ### Step 2 — Molar mass basis - Molecular weight of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ = $74 \text{ g/mol}$ - Molecular weight of $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ (as P) = $95 \text{ g/mol}$ (or 31 g/mol as P) ### Step 3 — Stoichiometric ratio From the balanced equation: $$\dfrac{5 \text{ mol Ca(OH)}_2}{3 \text{ mol PO}_4^{3-}} = \dfrac{5 \times 74}{3 \times 95} = \dfrac{370}{285} = 1.298$$ ### Step 4 — Lime dose equation $$L = 1.5 \times P_{\text{concentration}} \text{ (as mg/L CaCO}_3\text{ equivalent)}$$ The factor **1.5** is the empirical multiplier used in practice (above the stoichiometric 1.298) to ensure complete precipitation. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Chemical | Wastewater Type | Primary Objective | Recommended Chemical | Dose Range (mg/L) | KTU Exam Frequency | |-----------------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------| | Municipal | Turbidity removal | Alum | 10 – 150 | Very High | | Industrial | Colour removal | PAC + Polymer | 20 – 80 | High | | Effluent with high P | P precipitation | Lime or FeCl₃ | 50 – 200 | High | | High ammonia | Nitrification | (Biological, not chemical) | — | — | | Pathogen control | Disinfection | Cl₂ / NaOCl | 2 – 10 (residual) | Very High | | Refractory organics | Advanced oxidation | O₃ or H₂O₂ | 5 – 25 | Medium | | Foaming sludge | Foam control | Silicone defoamer | 0.1 – 5 | Low | > [!NOTE] > **Tip for KTU Numerical Problems** > When a question states "**optimum dose from jar test**", the value is the **final answer for D** — do not multiply by additional empirical factors. Apply purity correction only if explicitly stated. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Chemical Application Points in an AWT Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent Wastewater
Flow Q m3 per d] --> B[Preliminary Treatment
Screening and Grit Removal] B --> C[Primary Clarifier] C --> D[Secondary Biological Reactor
Activated Sludge] D --> E[Secondary Clarifier] E --> F{Tertiary
Chemical
Polishing} F --> F1[Coagulation Tank
Alum or PAC] F1 --> F2[Flocculation Tank
Polyelectrolyte] F2 --> F3[Settling or DAF Unit] F3 --> G[Disinfection Unit
Chlorine or Ozone] G --> H[Treated Effluent
Reuse or Discharge] F --> X1[Sludge Line
Lime Conditioning] X1 --> X2[Sludge Dewatering
Filter Press] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Decision Tree for Chemical Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start
Select Chemical] --> S2{Is the contaminant
colloidal or suspended?} S2 -- Yes --> S3{Is alkalinity
low below 50 mg per L?} S2 -- No --> S4{Is the contaminant
dissolved?} S3 -- Yes --> S5[Use PAC
Polyaluminium Chloride] S3 -- No --> S6[Use Alum
Al2 SO4 3] S4 -- Yes --> S5b{Is target
Phosphorus?} S4 -- No --> S6b[Use oxidants
H2O2 or KMnO4] S5b -- Yes --> S7[Use Lime or Ferric Chloride] S5b -- No --> S8{Is target
Ammonia Nitrogen?} S8 -- Yes --> S9[Biological process
Not chemical dosing] S8 -- No --> S10[Use breakpoint chlorination] S5 --> END1[Selected Chemical
PAC] S6 --> END2[Selected Chemical
Alum] S7 --> END3[Selected Chemical
Lime or FeCl3] S6b --> END4[Selected Chemical
Oxidant] S9 --> END5[Process Path
Biological Nitrification] S10 --> END6[Selected Chemical
Chlorine] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Jar Test Apparatus (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR J1[Raw Wastewater
Sample 1 L] --> JT[Jar Test Apparatus
6 Beakers at 200 rpm] J2[Chemical Dose 1
10 mg per L] --> JT J3[Chemical Dose 2
20 mg per L] --> JT J4[Chemical Dose 3
30 mg per L] --> JT J5[Chemical Dose 4
40 mg per L] --> JT J6[Chemical Dose 5
50 mg per L] --> JT J7[Control
0 mg per L] --> JT JT --> RM[Rapid Mix 1 min
G equals 1000 per s] RM --> SM[Slow Mix 30 min
G equals 50 per s] SM --> ST[Settling 30 min] ST --> OBS[Observe
Residual Turbidity
and Floc Size] OBS --> OPT[Optimum Dose
Selected for Plant] ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — Coagulation–Flocculation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD CHEM[Chemical Storage
Tank] --> PUMP[Chemical Metering Pump] PUMP --> RMIX[Rapid Mix Chamber
G equals 800 per s
t equals 30 s] RMIX --> FLOC[Flocculation Basin
G equals 30 per s
t equals 30 min] FLOC --> CLAR[Clarifier
Surface Overflow Rate 30 m3 per m2 per d] CLAR --> SLUDGE[Sludge Blanket
to Thickener] CLAR --> EFF[Clarified Effluent
to Disinfection] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a coagulant and a flocculant. State one example of each and explain their functional difference.** **Model Answer:** - **Coagulant:** A chemical reagent added to wastewater to **destabilize the electrostatic charges** on colloidal particles, neutralizing the zeta potential and promoting particle aggregation. **Example:** Aluminium Sulphate (Alum), $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$. - **Flocculant:** A chemical reagent (typically a **long-chain polymer**) added after coagulation to **bridge the destabilized micro-flocs into larger, settleable flocs**. **Example:** Anionic Polyacrylamide (PAM). - **Functional Difference:** Coagulants work on **charge neutralization** (chemical destabilization), while flocculants work via **inter-particle bridging** (physical aggregation). Coagulants are inorganic salts of multivalent cations; flocculants are organic polymers of very high molecular weight (10⁶ to 10⁷ Da). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Why is chlorine preferred over other disinfectants in municipal wastewater treatment? List two disadvantages.** **Model Answer:** - **Reasons for preference:** 1. **Low cost** and **wide availability** — economical for large flow rates. 2. **Persistent residual** — provides disinfection protection throughout the distribution system. 3. **Ease of application** and **proven track record** in municipal plants. - **Disadvantages:** 1. Formation of **harmful Disinfection By-Products (DBPs)** such as trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs) — carcinogenic. 2. **Ineffective against certain cysts** (e.g., *Cryptosporidium parvum*) unless very high doses are used. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Convention:** Each Part B question presents a choice between OR options. Students answer **either Option A OR Option B**, not both. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** List and briefly explain **five major categories of chemicals** applied in advanced wastewater treatment. For each category, provide **one specific chemical example** and its **reaction mechanism**. (7 Marks) **(b)** A wastewater treatment plant handles a flow of **$Q = 50,000$ m³/d**. Jar tests indicate an optimum **alum dose of $D = 40$ mg/L**. The commercial alum has a **purity of 97%** and **moisture content of 3%**. Calculate: (i) Mass of pure alum required per day. (ii) Mass of commercial alum required per day. (iii) Mass of wet commercial alum required per day. (iv) If the alum is supplied as a **10% solution** (by mass, density 1.05 kg/L), calculate the **solution feed rate in L/h**. (7 Marks) #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Five Major Categories of Chemicals:** 1. **Coagulants** — Destabilize colloidal charge. *Example:* $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ (Alum). *Reaction:* $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Al(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ 2. **Flocculants** — Aggregate destabilized particles via polymer bridging. *Example:* Cationic Polyacrylamide. *Reaction:* Long-chain polymer backbone adsorbs onto multiple particles, forming visible flocs. 3. **Disinfectants** — Inactivate pathogens. *Example:* Chlorine ($\text{Cl}_2$). *Reaction:* $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$, where HOCl penetrates microbial cell walls and oxidizes enzymes. 4. **pH Adjusters** — Maintain optimum pH range. *Example:* Lime $[\text{Ca(OH)}_2]$. *Reaction:* $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^-$, raising pH. 5. **Nutrient Removal Reagents** — Precipitate nitrogen or phosphorus. *Example:* Ferric Chloride ($\text{FeCl}_3$) for phosphorus. *Reaction:* $\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{FePO}_4 \downarrow$ **(b) Step-by-step Calculations:** **(i) Mass of pure alum:** $$M_{\text{pure}} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 40}{10^6} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{10^6}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{pure}} = 2.0 \text{ kg/d}}$$ **(ii) Mass of commercial alum (corrected for purity only):** $$M_{\text{commercial}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{pure}}}{P} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.97}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{commercial}} = 2.062 \text{ kg/d}}$$ **(iii) Mass of wet commercial alum (corrected for moisture):** $$M_{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{commercial}}}{1 - M_c} = \dfrac{2.062}{1 - 0.03} = \dfrac{2.062}{0.97}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{wet}} = 2.126 \text{ kg/d}}$$ **(iv) Solution feed rate:** The solution is 10% by mass, so each kg of solution contains 0.10 kg of alum. $$\text{Solution mass} = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}}}{0.10} = \dfrac{2.126}{0.10} = 21.26 \text{ kg/d}$$ Convert to volume using density: $$V_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{21.26}{\rho} = \dfrac{21.26}{1.05} = 20.25 \text{ L/d}$$ Convert to L/h: $$Q_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{20.25}{24}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{\text{solution}} = 0.844 \text{ L/h}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of coagulation** using the **Electrical Double Layer (EDL) theory** and the role of **zeta potential**. State **three factors** that affect coagulation efficiency. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the chemistry of **chlorination** in wastewater. A treatment plant applies chlorine at a dose of **$D = 5$ mg/L** to a flow of **$Q = 30,000$ m³/d**. The plant requires a **CT value of 6 mg·min/L** for 2-log inactivation of coliforms. Determine: (i) The mass of chlorine applied per day in kg/d. (ii) The required **residual chlorine concentration** at the end of the contact tank. (iii) The **contact time** corresponding to the CT value. (iv) State the **standard equation** for chlorine demand. (7 Marks) #### **Model Solution:** **(a) EDL Theory & Zeta Potential:** - Every colloidal particle in wastewater carries a **surface charge** (typically negative due to adsorbed OH⁻ and organic acids). - Around the particle, counter-ions (cations) arrange in two layers: - **Stern Layer** — ions tightly bound to particle surface. - **Diffuse Layer (Gouy Layer)** — loosely held counter-ions in the bulk. - The boundary between the moving particle and the stationary fluid is the **Slipping Plane**. - The **Zeta potential** is the electrical potential at the slipping plane. - A high absolute zeta potential (e.g., $-30$ mV) indicates a **stable suspension**. - **Coagulant cations (e.g., Al³⁺, Fe³⁺) compress the EDL** and reduce the zeta potential toward zero, enabling **van der Waals attractive forces** to dominate and cause flocculation. **Three factors affecting coagulation efficiency:** 1. **pH of wastewater** — affects speciation of coagulant hydrolysis products. 2. **Coagulant dose** — under-dose or over-dose both cause poor flocculation. 3. **Initial turbidity / colloidal concentration** — determines optimum dose (jar test). **(b) Chlorination Chemistry & Numerical Solution:** **(i) Mass of chlorine applied per day:** $$M_{\text{Cl}_2} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 5}{10^6}$$ $$\boxed{M_{\text{Cl}_2} = 0.15 \text{ kg/d}}$$ **(ii) CT Equation:** The CT (Concentration × Time) value is defined as: $$\text{CT} = C_{\text{residual}} \times t$$ Given CT = 6 mg·min/L and contact time $t = 30$ min (typical), solving: $$C_{\text{residual}} = \dfrac{\text{CT}}{t} = \dfrac{6}{30}$$ $$\boxed{C_{\text{residual}} = 0.2 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **(iii) If the contact time is taken as the standard 30 min, the CT value is 6 mg·min/L.** If the question requires the contact time given a residual of, say, 0.5 mg/L: $$t = \dfrac{\text{CT}}{C_{\text{residual}}} = \dfrac{6}{0.5} = 12 \text{ min}$$ **(iv) Chlorine Demand Equation:** $$\boxed{\text{Cl}_2 \text{ Demand} = \text{Cl}_2 \text{ Applied} - \text{Cl}_2 \text{ Residual}}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 1–2 Marks Each in KTU Evaluations:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 10⁶ divisor** in chemical feed rate calculations. Always write the full unit conversion chain in the answer sheet. > 2. **Not applying purity correction** when the question explicitly states "commercial grade" or provides a purity percentage. This is a **3-mark loss in numerical questions**. > 3. **Confusing "alum dose" with "aluminium ion dose"** — they are not equivalent. Alum formula is $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (MW = 594 g/mol), and the active Al³⁺ fraction must be calculated separately if asked. > 4. **Mixing up pH effect of alum vs. lime** — alum is **acidic** (lowers pH); lime is **alkaline** (raises pH). KTU questions frequently test this. > 5. **Not writing balanced chemical equations** in descriptive answers. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for **balanced stoichiometry**. > 6. **Skipping the "real-world application" sentence** at the end of descriptive answers — KTU rubric allocates 0.5–1 mark for engineering relevance statements. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: Chemicals Applied** - [ ] The **four-stage mechanism** of chemical dosing: **Rapid Mix → Reaction → Flocculation → Separation**. - [ ] **Alum** hydrolyzes to form **$\text{Al(OH)}_3$ floc** + $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (acid-producing). - [ ] **PAC (Polyaluminium Chloride)** is preferred for **low-alkalinity water** because it does not consume as much alkalinity. - [ ] **Ferric Chloride** is effective across a **wider pH range** (4–11) and is excellent for **phosphate removal**. - [ ] **Lime $[\text{Ca(OH)}_2]$** raises pH and precipitates **phosphates** as **hydroxyapatite** $\text{Ca}_5(\text{PO}_4)_3\text{OH}$. - [ ] **Chlorine** forms **HOCl** (80× stronger than $\text{OCl}^-$); lower pH favours HOCl. - [ ] **Ozone** is the strongest oxidant used (E⁰ = **+2.07 V**), but has **no residual** — must be used on-site. - [ ] The **master formula** for chemical feed rate is $M = \dfrac{Q \times D}{10^6}$ (kg/d) when $Q$ in m³/d and $D$ in mg/L. - [ ] **Purity and moisture** corrections: $M_{\text{wet}} = \dfrac{Q \times D}{P \times (1 - M_c) \times 10^6}$. - [ ] **EDL theory** explains why trivalent ions ($\text{Al}^{3+}$, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$) are superior coagulants — they compress the double layer more effectively. - [ ] **Zeta potential** approaching **0 mV** indicates destabilized colloids — the goal of coagulation. - [ ] **CT value** = $C_{\text{residual}} \times t$ is the design parameter for **disinfection contact chambers** (USEPA / KTU benchmark). - [ ] **Jar test** is the **standard laboratory procedure** to determine optimum coagulant dose. - [ ] **Velocity Gradient (G)** values: Rapid Mix = 700–1000 s⁻¹; Flocculation = 20–75 s⁻¹. - [ ] Always **convert mg/L to g/m³** explicitly in derivations to demonstrate unit consistency.

topics.title: Chemistry of Phosphorus Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemistry of Phosphorus Precipitation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phosphorus Precipitation** is a physico-chemical unit operation in advanced wastewater treatment wherein dissolved orthophosphate ions ($PO_4^{3-}$, $HPO_4^{2-}$, $H_2PO_4^-$) are converted into sparingly soluble metallic phosphate salts through the addition of multivalent metal cations (typically $Al^{3+}$, $Fe^{3+}$, or $Ca^{2+}$), thereby removing phosphorus from the aqueous phase to satisfy stringent effluent discharge norms (**≤ 1 mg/L TP** in sensitive receiving waters under the KTU/CPCB framework). **Forms of Phosphorus in Municipal Wastewater:** | Fraction | Chemical Speciation | Typical Proportion | |----------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Orthophosphate** | $H_2PO_4^-$, $HPO_4^{2-}$, $PO_4^{3-}$ | **4–12 mg/L** (70%) | | **Polyphosphate** | $P_2O_7^{4-}$, $P_3O_{10}^{5-}$ (linear/cyclic) | **1–4 mg/L** (10%) | | **Organic Phosphorus** | Phospholipids, nucleic acids, ATP | **0.5–2 mg/L** (10%) | ### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **"The Magnet & Iron-Filing Analogy"** — Imagine phosphorus as microscopic "invisible fish" swimming in wastewater. Adding alum, lime, or ferric chloride is like dropping a giant chemical "fishing net" into the water. The metal cations (the net frame) instantly grab the phosphate ions (the fish) and lock them together into a heavy solid "snowball" (the precipitate), which then sinks to the bottom. What floats above is clean water — that is **phosphorus precipitation** in essence! **Why is Phosphorus Removal Critical?** - Eutrophication of lakes and reservoirs - algal blooms, fish kills, oxygen depletion - Stringent discharge limits: **< 0.5 to 1 mg/L TP** in KTU/CPCB regulated zones - Recovery potential as **struvite ($MgNH_4PO_4 \cdot 6H_2O$)** — a slow-release fertilizer --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Governing Principle — Solubility Product (Ksp) Phosphorus precipitation is fundamentally governed by the **solubility product principle**. A metallic phosphate salt $M_x(PO_4)_y$ precipitates when the **ionic activity product (IAP)** exceeds the **thermodynamic solubility product ($K_{sp}$)**. For a generic precipitation reaction: $$M_x(PO_4)_y \rightleftharpoons xM^{n+} + yPO_4^{3-}$$ $$K_{sp} = \{M^{n+}\}^x \cdot \{PO_4^{3-}\}^y$$ **Precipitation occurs ONLY when IAP > $K_{sp}$**. Otherwise, the system is undersaturated and phosphorus remains dissolved. ### 2.2 The Three Principal Precipitants #### (A) Aluminum-Based Precipitation (Alum) - Common reagent: **Alum** — $Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O$ (molar mass ≈ **594 g/mol**) - Active species: $Al^{3+}$ - Reaction: $$Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O + 2PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2AlPO_4 \downarrow + 3SO_4^{2-} + 14H_2O$$ - Molar ratio required: **1 mol $Al^{3+}$ : 1 mol P** (stoichiometric) - Practical dose: **1.5 – 3.0 mol $Al^{3+}$ / mol P** (excess for kinetics & side reactions) #### (B) Iron-Based Precipitation (Ferric Chloride) - Common reagent: **Ferric Chloride** — $FeCl_3 \cdot 6H_2O$ (molar mass ≈ **270.3 g/mol**) - Active species: $Fe^{3+}$ - Reaction: $$FeCl_3 + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow FePO_4 \downarrow + 3Cl^-$$ - Molar ratio: **1 mol $Fe^{3+}$ : 1 mol P** (stoichiometric) - Practical dose: **1.5 – 3.0 mol $Fe^{3+}$ / mol P** #### (C) Lime-Based Precipitation (Calcium Hydroxide) - Common reagent: **Lime** — $Ca(OH)_2$ (molar mass ≈ **74 g/mol**) - Active species: $Ca^{2+}$ - Reaction forming **Hydroxyapatite (HAP)** — the thermodynamically stable form: $$5Ca^{2+} + 3PO_4^{3-} + OH^- \rightarrow Ca_5(PO_4)_3OH \downarrow$$ - Molar ratio: **1.67 mol $Ca^{2+}$ : 1 mol P** (stoichiometric) - Practical dose: **5 – 20 mol $Ca^{2+}$ / mol P** (much higher due to pH requirement and side reactions with alkalinity) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU Board Exam** | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Engineering Use | |---|-----------|---------|------|-----------------| | 1 | Solubility Product | $K_{sp} = [M^{n+}]^x[PO_4^{3-}]^y$ | dimensionless (or $M^{(x+y)}$) | Determines if precipitation will occur | | 2 | Residual P (Alum/Fe) | $[P]_{eq} = \sqrt{K_{sp}/[M^{n+}]}$ | mol/L | Predicts minimum achievable P | | 3 | Stoichiometric Al dose | $D_{Al} = Q \times [P] \times \frac{MW_{Al}}{MW_P} \times R$ | g/d | Design of chemical feed system | | 4 | Sludge Volume | $V_{sludge} = Q \times [P]_{rem} \times \frac{Y_{sludge}}{\rho_{sludge}}$ | $m^3/d$ | Sludge handling design | | 5 | Lime Demand (mg/L as $CaCO_3$) | $D_{Ca(OH)_2} = [Alk] + 1.5[P] + 50$ | mg/L | Buffer neutralization design | | 6 | pH for Lime Process | $pH \geq 9.5$ (HAP stability) | – | Critical operational parameter | | 7 | Henderson-Hasselbalch (P speciation) | $pH = pK_{a2} + \log\frac{[HPO_4^{2-}]}{[H_2PO_4^-]}$ | – | Determines dominant P species | | 8 | $pK_{a2}$ of $H_3PO_4$ | **7.20** | – | Reference constant | | 9 | $pK_{a3}$ of $H_3PO_4$ | **12.35** | – | Reference constant | > **Convention Used in Table:** $[M^{n+}]$ = molar concentration of metal cation, $[P]$ = molar concentration of phosphorus as P (atomic mass = **31 g/mol**), $Q$ = wastewater flow rate, $R$ = practical-to-stoichiometric mole ratio, $Y_{sludge}$ = sludge yield coefficient. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **WWTP Discharge Compliance** — Prevents eutrophication penalties - **Struvite Recovery** — Precipitation under controlled $Mg^{2+}:NH_4^+:PO_4^{3-}$ ratio - **EBPR Polishing** — Chemical precipitation as backup for biological P removal - **Industrial Effluents** — Fertilizer, dairy, semiconductor industries --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Theoretical Alum Dose Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Board-Style Problem):** A wastewater treatment plant receives **$Q = 10,000 \ m^3/d$** with influent total phosphorus of **[TP] = 10 mg/L as P**. The plant uses **alum** ($Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O$, MW = **594 g/mol**) at a **mole ratio R = 2.0** (Al:P). Calculate: (i) theoretical chemical dose in kg/d, and (ii) sludge (AlPO₄) produced in kg/d. **Step 1 — Convert influent P to molar concentration:** $$[P] = \frac{10 \ mg/L}{31 \ g/mol} = 0.3226 \ mmol/L = 0.3226 \ mol/m^3$$ **Step 2 — Daily molar loading of P:** $$n_P = Q \times [P] = 10,000 \ m^3/d \times 0.3226 \ mol/m^3$$ $$\boxed{n_P = 3,226 \ mol \ P/d}$$ **Step 3 — Moles of $Al^{3+}$ required using mole ratio R = 2.0:** $$n_{Al^{3+}} = n_P \times R = 3,226 \times 2.0 = 6,452 \ mol \ Al^{3+}/d$$ **Step 4 — Moles of alum required (1 mole of alum provides 2 moles of $Al^{3+}$):** $$n_{alum} = \frac{n_{Al^{3+}}}{2} = \frac{6,452}{2} = 3,226 \ mol \ alum/d$$ **Step 5 — Mass of alum required per day:** $$m_{alum} = n_{alum} \times MW_{alum} = 3,226 \ mol/d \times 594 \ g/mol$$ $$\boxed{m_{alum} = 1,916,244 \ g/d = 1,916.24 \ kg/d \approx 1.92 \ t/d}$$ **Step 6 — Sludge (AlPO₄, MW = 121.95 g/mol) produced per day:** $$m_{AlPO_4} = n_P \times MW_{AlPO_4} = 3,226 \ mol/d \times 121.95 \ g/mol$$ $$\boxed{m_{AlPO_4} = 393,411 \ g/d \approx 393.4 \ kg/d}$$ ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Residual Soluble Phosphorus > [!NOTE] > **Derivation of equilibrium [P] for AlPO₄ system** For the reaction $AlPO_4 \rightleftharpoons Al^{3+} + PO_4^{3-}$, with $K_{sp} = 9.84 \times 10^{-21}$ (at **25°C**): $$K_{sp} = [Al^{3+}][PO_4^{3-}]$$ Assuming residual $Al^{3+}$ is at saturation, and total P equals $PO_4^{3-}$ (at high pH): $$[PO_4^{3-}]_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{K_{sp}}{[Al^{3+}]}}$$ **Numerical substitution** (with residual $[Al^{3+}] = 1 \times 10^{-5} \ M$): $$[PO_4^{3-}]_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{9.84 \times 10^{-21}}{1 \times 10^{-5}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{9.84 \times 10^{-16}} = 9.92 \times 10^{-8} \ M$$ Convert to mg/L as P: $$[P]_{eq} = 9.92 \times 10^{-8} \ mol/L \times 31 \ g/mol \times 1000 \ mg/g$$ $$\boxed{[P]_{eq} \approx 3.07 \times 10^{-3} \ mg/L \ (\approx 3 \ \mu g/L)}$$ **Conclusion:** Theoretically, alum can reduce P to **ppb levels**, but in practice **side reactions with alkalinity and co-precipitation kinetics** limit it to **0.5 – 1 mg/L**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: pH vs. Precipitant Performance | pH Range | Dominant P Species | Alum Effectiveness | Lime Effectiveness | Ferric Effectiveness | |----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------| | **4.0 – 5.5** | $H_2PO_4^-$ | Poor (soluble Al) | None (CaHPO₄) | Good | | **5.5 – 7.0** | $H_2PO_4^-$ / $HPO_4^{2-}$ | **Excellent** | Fair | **Excellent** | | **7.0 – 8.5** | $HPO_4^{2-}$ | Good | Fair | Good | | **8.5 – 9.5** | $HPO_4^{2-}$ | Poor (Al(OH)₃ dominates) | Good | Fair | | **9.5 – 11.0** | $HPO_4^{2-}$ / $PO_4^{3-}$ | None | **Excellent (HAP)** | None | | **> 11.0** | $PO_4^{3-}$ | None | Excellent (HAP) | None | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Phosphorus Speciation Diagram (pH-dependent) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[H3PO4
pH less than 2.0] -->|pKa1 = 2.15| B[H2PO4-
pH 2 to 7.2] B -->|pKa2 = 7.20| C[HPO4 2-
pH 7.2 to 12.35] C -->|pKa3 = 12.35| D[PO4 3-
pH greater than 12.35] B -.->|Optimal for Alum
pH 5.5 to 7.0| E[AlPO4 Precipitate] C -.->|Optimal for Lime
pH greater than 9.5| F[Ca5 PO4 3 OH HAP] B -.->|Optimal for FeCl3
pH 4.5 to 7.0| G[FePO4 Precipitate] ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Chemical Precipitation Train ```mermaid flowchart TD Inf[Influent Wastewater
TP = 10 mg/L] --> Mix1[Rapid Mix Tank
G = 700 to 1000 s-1
t = 30 to 60 s] Chem[Chemical Feed System
Alum or FeCl3 or Lime] --> Mix1 Mix1 --> Floc[Flocculation Basin
G = 20 to 75 s-1
t = 15 to 30 min] Floc --> Sed[Secondary Clarifier
ORP less than 2 mg/L TP
Sludge to Digester] Sed --> Eff[Treated Effluent
TP less than 1 mg/L] Sed -->|Recycle| RAS[RAS / WAS
Sludge Handling] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Precipitation Efficiency ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Phosphorus Removal Efficiency] --> A1[Chemical Dose] P --> A2[Solution pH] P --> A3[Alkalinity / Bicarbonate] P --> A4[Mixing Intensity and Time] P --> A5[Temperature] P --> A6[Initial P Concentration] P --> A7[Co-ions: Ca, Mg, NH4] A2 --> B1[Alum: optimum 5.5 to 7.0] A2 --> B2[Fe: optimum 4.5 to 7.0] A2 --> B3[Lime: optimum greater than 9.5] A1 --> C1[Stoichiometric Ratio 1:1] A1 --> C2[Pract: 1.5 to 3.0 mol M per mol P] A1 --> C3[Lime: 5 to 20 mol Ca per mol P] ``` ### 4.4 Molar Stoichiometry Flow (Per 1 mole P) ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[1 mole P] --> P1[Alum Path] P0 --> P2[Iron Path] P0 --> P3[Lime Path] P1 --> D1[1 mole Al3+] D1 --> D2[0.5 mole Al2 SO4 3 . 14 H2O] D2 --> D3[1 mole AlPO4 sludge] P2 --> E1[1 mole Fe3+] E1 --> E2[1 mole FeCl3] E2 --> E3[1 mole FePO4 sludge] P3 --> F1[1.67 mole Ca2+] F1 --> F2[1.67 mole Ca OH 2] F2 --> F3[0.33 mole Ca5 PO4 3 OH HAP sludge] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **List the three principal chemical precipitants used for phosphorus removal and write the stoichiometric precipitation reaction for any one of them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three principal precipitants are: 1. **Aluminum salts** — e.g., Alum $Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O$ 2. **Iron salts** — e.g., Ferric chloride $FeCl_3$ 3. **Lime** — $Ca(OH)_2$ **Reaction (Alum):** $$Al^{3+} + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow AlPO_4 \downarrow$$ **Stoichiometric ratio = 1 mol Al : 1 mol P**. *(1 mark for each precipitant = 2 marks; 1 mark for balanced reaction)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Explain why lime precipitation requires a much higher Ca:P mole ratio (~10:1) than the stoichiometric 1.67:1.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The stoichiometric reaction requires only **1.67 mol Ca : 1 mol P** to form hydroxyapatite $Ca_5(PO_4)_3OH$. **(1 mark)** - However, lime must also neutralize the wastewater's **natural alkalinity** ($HCO_3^-$) before raising pH above 9.5. **(1 mark)** - Additional lime is consumed in side reactions forming $CaCO_3$ and in excess $Ca(OH)_2$ to maintain the high pH. Hence a practical **5–20 mol Ca : 1 mol P** is needed. **(1 mark)** --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With balanced chemical equations, explain the chemistry of **phosphorus precipitation using (i) alum and (ii) ferric chloride**. State the optimal pH range for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wastewater flow of **$50,000 \ m^3/d$** has **[TP] = 8 mg/L**. Design a **ferric chloride ($FeCl_3$, MW = 162.2 g/mol)** dosing system using mole ratio **R = 2.5** (Fe:P). Calculate: (i) Fe dose in kg/d, (ii) sludge ($FePO_4$, MW = 150.82 g/mol) produced in kg/d. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:** **Alum Chemistry:** - Reagent: $Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O$, MW = 594 g/mol - Reaction: $Al_2(SO_4)_3 \cdot 14H_2O + 2PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow 2AlPO_4 \downarrow + 3SO_4^{2-} + 14H_2O$ **(2 marks)** - Stoichiometric ratio: 1 mol Al : 1 mol P - Optimal pH: **5.5 – 7.0** **(1 mark)** **Ferric Chloride Chemistry:** - Reagent: $FeCl_3$, MW = 162.2 g/mol - Reaction: $FeCl_3 + PO_4^{3-} \rightarrow FePO_4 \downarrow + 3Cl^-$ **(2 marks)** - Stoichiometric ratio: 1 mol Fe : 1 mol P - Optimal pH: **4.5 – 7.0** **(1 mark)** **Conceptual Note (1 mark):** Both reactions are governed by the solubility product; the trivalent cation forms an insoluble phosphate precipitate. The slight pH difference is due to $Fe(OH)_3$ precipitating at lower pH than $Al(OH)_3$. --- **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Convert TP to molar concentration: $$[P] = \frac{8 \ mg/L}{31 \ g/mol} = 0.2581 \ mmol/L = 0.2581 \ mol/m^3 \quad (1 \ mark)$$ **Step 2:** Daily molar loading of P: $$n_P = 50,000 \times 0.2581 = 12,903 \ mol \ P/d \quad (1 \ mark)$$ **Step 3:** Moles of $Fe^{3+}$ required (R = 2.5): $$n_{Fe^{3+}} = 12,903 \times 2.5 = 32,258 \ mol \ Fe^{3+}/d \quad (1 \ mark)$$ **Step 4:** Mass of $FeCl_3$ required: $$m_{FeCl_3} = 32,258 \times 162.2 = 5,232,236 \ g/d = 5,232.2 \ kg/d \quad (2 \ marks)$$ **Step 5:** Mass of $FePO_4$ sludge: $$m_{FePO_4} = 12,903 \times 150.82 = 1,946,029 \ g/d \approx 1,946 \ kg/d \quad (2 \ marks)$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternate]** **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting chemical phosphorus precipitation** with reference to pH, alkalinity, dose, and mixing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A municipality plans to treat **$20,000 \ m^3/d$** wastewater using **lime** ($Ca(OH)_2$, MW = 74 g/mol) for P removal. The TP is **12 mg/L**, alkalinity is **250 mg/L as $CaCO_3$**, and target pH = **9.8**. Using a Ca:P mole ratio of **10:1** (to account for alkalinity neutralization), calculate the **lime dose in kg/d**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:** 1. **pH (2 marks):** Each precipitant has a characteristic optimum pH (Alum: 5.5–7.0, Fe: 4.5–7.0, Lime: > 9.5). Outside this range, the metal precipitates as hydroxide instead of phosphate, reducing P removal efficiency. 2. **Alkalinity (2 marks):** Natural bicarbonate alkalinity consumes added metal coagulants by forming metal hydroxides/carbonates. Lime doses must overcome alkalinity before pH rises. 3. **Chemical Dose (1.5 marks):** Insufficient dose leaves soluble P; overdosing wastes chemicals and increases sludge volume. Practical dose is **1.5 – 3 × stoichiometric** for Al/Fe and **5 – 20 × stoichiometric** for lime. 4. **Mixing (1.5 marks):** Rapid mix (G = 700–1000 s⁻¹ for 30–60 s) ensures uniform dispersion of coagulant; slow flocculation (G = 20–75 s⁻¹ for 15–30 min) builds settleable flocs. --- **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Convert TP to molar basis: $$[P] = \frac{12 \ mg/L}{31 \ g/mol} = 0.3871 \ mmol/L = 0.3871 \ mol/m^3 \quad (1 \ mark)$$ **Step 2:** Daily moles of P: $$n_P = 20,000 \times 0.3871 = 7,742 \ mol \ P/d \quad (1 \ mark)$$ **Step 3:** Moles of $Ca^{2+}$ required (R = 10:1): $$n_{Ca^{2+}} = 7,742 \times 10 = 77,420 \ mol \ Ca^{2+}/d \quad (1.5 \ marks)$$ **Step 4:** Moles of $Ca(OH)_2$ (1:1 stoichiometry): $$n_{Ca(OH)_2} = 77,420 \ mol/d \quad (0.5 \ mark)$$ **Step 5:** Mass of lime: $$m_{Ca(OH)_2} = 77,420 \times 74 = 5,729,080 \ g/d = 5,729.1 \ kg/d \quad (2 \ marks)$$ **Step 6 (commentary):** Compare with alkalinity demand: $$\text{Alkalinity as } CaCO_3 = \frac{250 \ mg/L}{50 \ g/mol} \times 100 = 5 \ mmol/L$$ This consumes a substantial portion of the lime — explaining the high 10:1 ratio. **(1 mark)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting MW of P (= 31 g/mol)** — students often use 95 g/mol (PO₄), causing 3× error.
