Always clarify "as P" vs "as PO₄". > 2. **Confusing stoichiometric vs. practical mole ratio** — Examiners explicitly mention R in problems; do NOT default to 1:1. > 3. **Ignoring alkalinity in lime calculations** — most students forget that 5–20× ratio is needed, not 1.67×. > 4. **Wrong units in sludge calculations** — ensure consistent mol → g → kg → /d conversions. > 5. **Skipping pH justification** — answers missing pH specifications lose 1 mark each in sub-parts. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three precipitants:** Alum (pH 5.5–7), $FeCl_3$ (pH 4.5–7), Lime (pH > 9.5) - **Stoichiometric ratio:** Al:P = Fe:P = 1:1; Ca:P = 1.67:1 (HAP) - **Practical dose:** Al/Fe = 1.5–3×; Lime = 5–20× stoichiometric - **Ksp values:** $AlPO_4$ = **9.84 × 10⁻²¹**; $FePO_4$ = **1.3 × 10⁻²²**; $Ca_5(PO_4)_3OH$ = **6.8 × 10⁻³⁷** - **Phosphorus pKa values:** $pK_{a1}$ = 2.15; $pK_{a2}$ = 7.20; $pK_{a3}$ = 12.35 - **Governing principle:** Ionic Activity Product (IAP) > $K_{sp}$ for precipitation - **Dominant P species:** $H_2PO_4^-$ (pH 2–7.2); $HPO_4^{2-}$ (pH 7.2–12.35); $PO_4^{3-}$ (> 12.35) - **Key MW values to memorize:** P = 31; Alum = 594; $FeCl_3$ = 162.2; $Ca(OH)_2$ = 74; $AlPO_4$ = 121.95; $FePO_4$ = 150.82 - **Sludge forms:** $AlPO_4$, $FePO_4$, $Ca_5(PO_4)_3OH$ (hydroxyapatite) - **Alkalinity neutralization is the hidden cost of lime precipitation** - **Effluent target:** < 1 mg/L TP for sensitive water bodies ---

topics.title: Process Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Advanced Wastewater Treatment (OECHT725), **Process Configuration** refers to the systematic spatial and operational arrangement of physical, chemical, and biological unit operations and processes—along with their interconnecting piping, flow recycles, and control schemes—designed to transform influent wastewater of a given quality into an effluent that meets prescribed discharge or reuse standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Process configuration is the engineering blueprint that defines *which unit processes* are selected, *how they are sequenced* (series/parallel), *how flows are split, combined, or recycled*, and *how the sludge is handled* between stages. It is essentially the **"architecture"** of a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

A process configuration therefore specifies:
1. **Pretreatment train** (screening, grit removal, flow equalization).
2. **Primary treatment train** (primary clarifiers).
3. **Secondary (biological) treatment train** (activated sludge variants, biofilm reactors, lagoons).
4. **Tertiary/Advanced treatment train** (nutrient removal, filtration, disinfection, membrane separation).
5. **Solids handling train** (thickening, digestion, dewatering).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Process Configuration** like the **kitchen layout of a large restaurant**:

- The **raw materials** (vegetables, meat) enter at the receiving dock → equivalent to the **influent** of a WWTP.
- The **prep station** (washing, cutting) is like **primary treatment** — removing coarse items.
- The **cooking line** (boilers, fryers, ovens in sequence) is the **biological reactor** — actually transforming the material.
- The **plating section** and **cold storage** represent **tertiary polishing and disinfection**.
- The **waste bin and grease trap** represent **sludge handling and recycle**.

If you rearrange the kitchen — placing the fryer before the oven, or moving the prep station to the wrong side — the entire output (food quality, speed, hygiene) collapses. Similarly, the **order, sizing, and recycle links** between reactors dictate whether a WWTP produces **clean effluent or violates discharge norms**.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU confusion: students often equate "Process Configuration" with "Process Design." Configuration is the **topology** (which tank connects to which), whereas Design is the **dimensioning** (volume of each tank). Both are interlinked, but the exam treats them separately.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Process Configuration

- **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)** = $V/Q$ — measured in **hours**.
- **SRT / MCRT (Solids / Mean Cell Residence Time)** = $V X / (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$ — measured in **days**.
- **F/M Ratio (Food to Microorganism)** = $(Q S_0) / (V X)$ — measured in **day⁻¹**.
- **Recycle Ratio (R)** = $Q_r / Q$ — dimensionless.
- **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)** = $(Q S_0) / V$ — measured in **kg COD/m³·d** or **kg BOD/m³·d**.
- **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** = $Q / A$ — measured in **m³/m²·d** (m/d).
- **Weir Loading Rate (WLR)** = $Q / L_{weir}$ — measured in **m³/m·d**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners *always* reward students who quote units alongside formulas. A formula without units = partial credit only.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Process Configuration

A modern WWTP configuration operates at **four hierarchical levels**:

#### Level 1 — Process Train Selection
The decision of which *categories* of treatment to deploy:
- **Preliminary** → Screening, Grit Chamber, Flow Equalization
- **Primary** → Primary Clarifiers (settleable solids, ~50–70% TSS, ~25–40% BOD removal)
- **Secondary** → Biological oxidation of dissolved organics
- **Tertiary / Advanced** → Nitrification, Denitrification, Biological/Chemical P-removal, Filtration, Membranes, Adsorption, Disinfection

#### Level 2 — Reactor Hydraulics
The internal mixing regime inside each biological reactor:
| Reactor Type | Hydraulic Model | Mixing Pattern | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plug Flow (PF)** | Dispersion number d → 0 | No back-mixing | High-rate BOD removal, large plants |
| **Completely Mixed (CM)** | d → ∞ | Instantaneous uniform mixing | Shock-load buffering, industrial wastes |
| **Step Feed (SF)** | Distributed influent along reactor length | Quasi-plug flow with feed staging | High SRT, low F/M |
| **SBR (Sequencing Batch)** | Time-based sequencing | Cyclic fill-react-settle-decant | Small communities, variable flows |
| **MBR** | Coupled with membrane separation | CM tank + membrane module | High-quality reuse, footprint reduction |

#### Level 3 — Solids Recycle Architecture
Determines how activated sludge is returned and wasted:
- **Conventional (single-sludge)**: RAS only — Return Activated Sludge from clarifier underflow.
- **Contact Stabilization**: Split-reactor — contact zone (short HRT) + reaeration/stabilization zone (long SRT).
- **Two-Sludge (Multi-stage)**: Separate sludge streams for carbon removal and nitrification.
- **Integrated Fixed-film Activated Sludge (IFAS)**: Hybrid suspended + attached growth in same tank.

#### Level 4 — Nutrient Removal Topology
The arrangement of aerobic, anoxic, and anaerobic zones:
- **A/O** — Anoxic → Aerobic (denitrification + BOD removal).
- **A²/O (Anaerobic/Anoxic/Oxic)** — Biological P-removal + Nitrification + Denitrification.
- **Bardenpho (5-stage)** — A/O/A/O with second anoxic + reaeration.
- **Modified Ludzack-Ettinger (MLE)** — Pre-anoxic with mixed-liquor recycle (internal recycle).
- **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** — Time-sequenced A/O/A/O in single tank.

### 2.2 Why Configuration Matters: The "Why" Behind Choices

| Design Driver | Configuration Response |
|---|---|
| Variable influent flow | Equalization basin + completely-mixed reactors |
| High-strength industrial discharge | Plug flow with stepped feed + equalization |
| Strict effluent NH₃-N limit (< 5 mg/L) | Long-SRT nitrification stage or separate-stage nitrification |
| Strict total N limit (< 10 mg/L) | MLE / A²O / Bardenpho with internal recycle |
| Strict total P limit (< 1 mg/L) | A²O + chemical polishing, or EBPR |
| Small footprint / retrofit | MBR, SBR, IFAS |
| Energy minimization | Anaerobic configuration (UASB, AnMBR) |
| Reuse for irrigation / industry | Tertiary filtration + disinfection (chlorination/UV) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HRT} = V / Q$ | V = reactor volume, Q = flow | hours | Sizing reactor |
| 2 | $\text{SRT} = \dfrac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | X = MLSS, Q_w = waste flow, X_w = waste SS, Q_e = effluent flow, X_e = effluent SS | days | Sludge age control |
| 3 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X}$ | S₀ = influent substrate | day⁻¹ | Loading check |
| 4 | $\text{VLR} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V}$ | — | kg/m³·d | Volumetric load |
| 5 | $\text{SOR} = Q / A$ | A = clarifier surface area | m³/m²·d | Clarifier area |
| 6 | $R = Q_r / Q$ | Q_r = recycle flow | dimensionless | Recycle ratio |
| 7 | $Q_{eff} = Q + Q_r - Q_w$ | Mass balance on bioreactor | m³/d | Hydraulic check |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_i (1-\eta_i)$ | η = stage removal efficiency | dimensionless | Multi-stage overall efficiency |
| 9 | $S_e = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k\, \text{HRT}}$ | k = first-order rate constant | mg/L | CMAS effluent substrate |
| 10 | $X = \dfrac{\text{SRT}\, Y\,(S_0 - S_e)}{\text{HRT}\,(1 + k_d \text{SRT})}$ | Y = yield, k_d = decay | mg/L | MLSS prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall**: When deriving multi-stage efficiency, many students incorrectly write $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2$. The correct *removal* form (in terms of *remaining fraction*) is the product $(1-\eta_1)(1-\eta_2)$, giving $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_i(1-\eta_i)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Process configuration choices directly govern:
- **Capex and Opex** of municipal STPs in Kerala (Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, Calicut corporation plants).
- **Effluent compliance** with KSPCB norms and the National Green Tribunal (NGT) directives.
- **Energy recovery** potential (e.g., UASB-based configurations recover biogas; conventional activated sludge does not).
- **Sludge handling burden** — choice between aerobic vs anaerobic configurations changes the WAS volume by an order of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — HRT and SRT Relationship in a Recycle Bioreactor

Consider a **completely-mixed activated sludge (CMAS) reactor** with influent $Q$, effluent $Q - Q_w$, recycle $Q_r$, and waste sludge $Q_w$. The reactor volume is $V$ and MLSS is $X$.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Balance**
The total flow entering the reactor equals $Q + Q_r$ (influent + recycle). The total flow leaving the reactor in the liquid stream is $Q - Q_w + Q_r$ (effluent + recycle line pumped back). By continuity, the hydraulic balance closes:
$$
Q_{in} = Q + Q_r
$$
$$
Q_{out,liquid} = (Q - Q_w) + Q_r
$$
$$
Q_{waste} = Q_w
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of HRT**
HRT is based on the volume of liquid in the reactor and the *net* throughput (influent flow, since effluent equals influent minus sludge wasting). Therefore:
$$
\text{HRT} = \theta_h = \frac{V}{Q}
$$
The recycle flow $Q_r$ passes through the reactor multiple times; hence HRT counts each pass only once because the liquid *volume* is fixed.

**Step 3 — Definition of SRT (Solids Retention Time)**
SRT counts the *average time a biomass particle spends in the system* before being wasted. It is a *mass* balance, not a hydraulic balance.

Mass of biomass in reactor:
$$
M_X = V \cdot X
$$

Mass of biomass leaving per day:
- In **effluent suspended solids**: $Q_e \cdot X_e$ where $Q_e = Q - Q_w$
- In **waste sludge**: $Q_w \cdot X_w$

Total biomass leaving per day = $Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e$

Therefore:
$$
\text{SRT} = \theta_c = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplification under Standard Assumptions**
- Effluent is clarified → $X_e \approx 0$ (negligible).
- Waste sludge has concentration equal to MLSS → $X_w \approx X$.

Then:
$$
\theta_c \approx \frac{V X}{Q_w X} = \frac{V}{Q_w}
$$

This shows that SRT is controlled entirely by **how much sludge is wasted**, not by the influent flow $Q$ (which controls HRT).

**Step 5 — Combined Relationship**
$$
\frac{\theta_c}{\theta_h} = \frac{V / Q_w}{V / Q} = \frac{Q}{Q_w}
$$

For a typical municipal WWTP, $Q / Q_w \approx 30$ to $60$, meaning **SRT is 30–60 times larger than HRT**. This decoupling is the *fundamental advantage* of activated sludge: biomass is retained far longer than the liquid, allowing slow-growing organisms (nitrifiers, P-accumulating organisms) to thrive.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effluent Substrate in a CMAS Reactor (Steady-State)

For a completely-mixed reactor with first-order BOD removal kinetics, perform a steady-state mass balance on substrate $S$:

**Step 1 — Mass In − Mass Out + Generation = Accumulation (steady → 0)**

$$
Q S_0 - Q S_e - r_s V = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Monod-type rate expression**

For first-order approximation (low $S$, typical of effluent zone), $r_s = k S$, where $k$ has units of **h⁻¹**:
$$
Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $S_e$**
$$
Q S_0 = Q S_e + k S_e V
$$
$$
Q S_0 = S_e (Q + k V)
$$
$$
S_e = \frac{Q S_0}{Q + k V}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of HRT**
Substituting $V/Q = \theta_h$:
$$
S_e = \frac{S_0}{1 + k \theta_h}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
As $\theta_h \to \infty$, $S_e \to 0$. As $\theta_h \to 0$, $S_e \to S_0$ (no treatment). This is the **classical CMAS design curve** and is *high-yield* for KTU derivations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Efficiency of Multi-Stage Configuration

Suppose a WWTP has $n$ treatment stages in series, each removing a fraction $\eta_i$ of the *remaining* substrate.

**Step 1 — Substrate After Stage 1**
$$
S_1 = S_0 (1 - \eta_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Substrate After Stage 2**
$$
S_2 = S_1 (1 - \eta_2) = S_0 (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Substrate After Stage n**
$$
S_n = S_0 \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \eta_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Overall Removal Efficiency**
$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{S_0 - S_n}{S_0} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - \eta_i)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)**
A WWTP has primary ($\eta_1 = 0.35$), activated sludge ($\eta_2 = 0.90$), and tertiary filter ($\eta_3 = 0.50$). Compute overall BOD removal.
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.35)(1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.50)
$$
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (0.65)(0.10)(0.50)
$$
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - 0.0325 = 0.9675 = 96.75\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *favorite* KTU 14-mark part (a) derivation — students who write $(1-0.35)(1-0.90)(1-0.50)$ instead of $1 - \prod(1-\eta_i)$ will lose 2–3 marks immediately.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration vs. Operating Parameter

| Configuration | HRT (h) | SRT (d) | F/M (d⁻¹) | Recycle Ratio | Typical Effluent BOD (mg/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conventional CMAS | 4 – 8 | 5 – 15 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.25 – 0.75 | 20 – 30 |
| Conventional PF | 4 – 8 | 5 – 15 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.25 – 0.75 | 15 – 25 |
| Extended Aeration | 18 – 36 | 20 – 40 | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0.75 – 1.50 | 10 – 20 |
| Contact Stabilization | 0.5 – 2 (contact) + 4 – 8 (stab) | 5 – 15 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.50 – 1.00 | 15 – 25 |
| SBR | 12 – 48 (cycle) | 10 – 30 | 0.05 – 0.20 | — | 10 – 20 |
| MBR | 4 – 8 | 15 – 60 | 0.05 – 0.20 | 2.0 – 4.0 | < 5 |
| MBBR / IFAS | 4 – 8 | 10 – 30 | 0.10 – 0.30 | — | 10 – 20 |
| A²O | 6 – 12 | 15 – 25 | 0.10 – 0.20 | Internal 2 – 4 + RAS | BOD<20, TN<10, TP<1 |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A municipal WWTP has influent flow $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d, BOD = 250 mg/L, SS = 220 mg/L. The biological reactor has $V = 4{,}000$ m³, MLSS $X = 3{,}000$ mg/L, recycle ratio $R = 0.5$, and waste sludge flow $Q_w = 200$ m³/d with $X_w = 8{,}000$ mg/L. Effluent SS ≈ 15 mg/L.

**Compute HRT, SRT, F/M, and SRT/HRT ratio.**

**Step 1 — HRT**
$$
\theta_h = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4000}{20000} = 0.2 \text{ d} = 4.8 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Effluent flow**
$$
Q_e = Q - Q_w = 20000 - 200 = 19800 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Step 3 — SRT**
$$
\theta_c = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e} = \frac{(4000)(3000)}{(200)(8000) + (19800)(15)}
$$

Numerator:
$$
V X = 4000 \times 3000 = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ g}
$$

Denominator:
$$
Q_w X_w = 200 \times 8000 = 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ g/d}
$$
$$
Q_e X_e = 19800 \times 15 = 2.97 \times 10^5 \text{ g/d}
$$
$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.6 \times 10^6 + 2.97 \times 10^5 = 1.897 \times 10^6 \text{ g/d}
$$

SRT:
$$
\theta_c = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{1.897 \times 10^6} \approx 6.32 \text{ d}
$$

**Step 4 — F/M ratio**
$$
\text{F/M} = \frac{Q S_0}{V X} = \frac{20000 \times 250}{4000 \times 3000} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 10^7} = 0.417 \text{ d}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — SRT/HRT ratio**
$$
\frac{\theta_c}{\theta_h} = \frac{6.32}{0.2} \approx 31.6
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a conventional CMAS configuration. $\theta_c/\theta_h \approx 30$ confirms typical design. F/M ≈ 0.4 d⁻¹ is in the conventional range — *not* extended aeration. SRT of ~6 days is too low for full nitrification in cold climates (would need $\theta_c > 10$ d).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comprehensive WWTP Process Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INF["Raw Wastewater Influent Q S0 BOD COD SS TN TP"]
    EQ["Equalization Basin HRT 4 to 8 h"]
    SCR["Screening and Grit Removal"]
    PRI["Primary Clarifier SOR 30 to 50 m3 per m2 per d"]
    BIO["Biological Reactor CMAS or PF or SBR or MBR"]
    AER["Aeration Basin with Aerators O2 supply"]
    CLAR["Secondary Clarifier SOR 20 to 35 m3 per m2 per d"]
    RAS["Return Activated Sludge RAS Qr Q times R"]
    WAS["Waste Activated Sludge Qw"]
    TER["Tertiary Treatment Filtration Nutrient Removal"]
    DIS["Disinfection Chlorination or UV"]
    EFF["Treated Effluent Reuse or Discharge"]
    THK["Sludge Thickener"]
    DIG["Sludge Digester Aerobic or Anaerobic"]
    DEW["Sludge Dewatering Belt Press or Centrifuge"]
    BCK["Biogas or Cake to Disposal"]

    INF --> SCR
    SCR --> EQ
    EQ --> PRI
    PRI --> BIO
    AER --> BIO
    BIO --> CLAR
    CLAR --> RAS
    RAS --> BIO
    CLAR --> WAS
    WAS --> THK
    CLAR --> TER
    TER --> DIS
    DIS --> EFF
    THK --> DIG
    DIG --> DEW
    DEW --> BCK
```

### 4.2 Configuration Topology — Conventional vs A2O vs SBR vs MBR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV["Conventional CMAS Configuration"]
        C1["Influent"] --> C2["Aeration Tank"] --> C3["Clarifier"]
        C3 --> C4["Effluent"]
        C3 --> C5["RAS Recycle R 0.5"] --> C2
        C3 --> C6["WAS"]
    end
    subgraph A2O["A2O Configuration for N and P removal"]
        A1["Influent"] --> A2["Anaerobic Zone"] --> A3["Anoxic Zone"] --> A4["Aerobic Zone"] --> A5["Clarifier"]
        A5 --> A6["Effluent"]
        A5 --> A7["RAS R"] --> A2
        A4 --> A8["Internal Recycle IR 2 to 4 Q"] --> A3
        A5 --> A9["WAS"]
    end
    subgraph SBR["SBR Configuration Time Cyclic"]
        S1["Influent"] --> S2["Single Tank Fill React Settle Decant"]
        S2 --> S3["Effluent"]
        S2 --> S4["WAS at end of React"]
    end
    subgraph MBR["MBR Configuration"]
        M1["Influent"] --> M2["Bioreactor"] --> M3["Membrane Module"]
        M3 --> M4["Effluent Reuse Quality"]
        M2 --> M5["WAS"]
        M3 --> M6["RAS concentrated"]
        M6 --> M2
    end
```

### 4.3 Multi-Stage Series Configuration Efficiency Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST0["Stage 0 Influent S0 250 mg per L BOD"] --> ST1["Stage 1 Primary Clarifier eta1 35 percent"]
    ST1 --> ST2["Stage 2 Biological Reactor eta2 90 percent"]
    ST2 --> ST3["Stage 3 Tertiary Filter eta3 50 percent"]
    ST3 --> ST4["Stage 4 Disinfection negligible BOD removal"]
    ST4 --> STOUT["Effluent Se approx 8 mg per L"]

    ST1 -.->|"S1 = 162.5 mg per L"| ST2
    ST2 -.->|"S2 = 16.25 mg per L"| ST3
    ST3 -.->|"S3 = 8.125 mg per L"| ST4
```

### 4.4 Recycle and Wastage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QIN["Q Influent"] --> RX["Reactor V X"]
    RX --> QEF["Q minus Qw Effluent"]
    RX --> QRA["Qr RAS recycle ratio R"]
    QRA --> RX
    RX --> QW["Qw WAS wasting"]
    QW --> SLG["Sludge Handling"]
    SLG --> BCK["Thickener Digester Dewatering"]
    BCK --> DSP["Cake Disposal"]
    SLG --> BIOG["Biogas if anaerobic"]

    QIN -.->|"Q"| RX
    RX -.->|"V X biomass in reactor"| RX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the Exam**: KTU examiners often include a process flow diagram in 14-mark questions and ask students to (a) identify the configuration, (b) calculate HRT/SRT, and (c) comment on the suitability for nutrient removal. Practice identifying the **recycle loop direction** (RAS arrow back to reactor), **wasting location** (from recycle line or clarifier underflow), and **zone sequence** (Anaerobic → Anoxic → Aerobic for A²O).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define *Process Configuration* in the context of advanced wastewater treatment. Differentiate between *Plug Flow* and *Completely Mixed* reactor configurations.

**Model Answer:**

*Process Configuration* refers to the systematic arrangement of physical, chemical, and biological unit operations—along with their flow recycles, sludge handling, and interconnections—designed to transform influent wastewater into effluent meeting prescribed standards. It defines the *topology* of a treatment plant.

| Aspect | Plug Flow (PF) | Completely Mixed (CM) |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing | No back-mixing; fluid moves as a "plug" | Instantaneous and uniform mixing |
| Substrate profile | Gradient from $S_0$ at inlet to $S_e$ at outlet | Uniform $S_e$ throughout reactor |
| F/M variation along reactor | High at inlet, low at outlet | Uniform throughout |
| Shock load resistance | Poor — inlet sees raw sewage | Excellent — instantaneous dilution |
| Typical application | Large municipal plants, high-strength wastes | Industrial wastewater, variable flows |

**(3 marks)**

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Explain the role of **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** and **Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)** in maintaining a desired SRT in an activated sludge process.

**Model Answer:**

- **RAS (Return Activated Sludge)** is the settled biomass from the secondary clarifier underflow, pumped back to the bioreactor inlet. It serves two purposes: (i) **maintaining high MLSS** in the reactor (typically 2000–4000 mg/L) so that biomass is retained long enough to metabolize the incoming substrate, and (ii) **preserving the biomass inventory** of the system — without RAS, all biomass would exit with the effluent and biological treatment would cease.

- **WAS (Waste Activated Sludge)** is the controlled, deliberate removal of biomass from the system. Since RAS preserves biomass, the *net rate of biomass leaving the system* is governed by WAS flow rate $Q_w$. Therefore, **SRT is controlled primarily by WAS, not by influent flow**:
$$
\text{SRT} = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}
$$

A higher $Q_w$ → lower SRT → younger sludge → lower effluent quality but more waste to handle. A lower $Q_w$ → higher SRT → older sludge → better nitrification, less WAS volume. **The art of WWTP operation lies in tuning $Q_w$ to achieve the target SRT.**

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Each 14-mark question contains two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **A²/O (Anaerobic/Anoxic/Oxic) configuration** for simultaneous biological nitrogen and phosphorus removal. Explain the function of each zone, the role of internal and external recycles, and the typical recycle ratios used.

**(b)** A municipal WWTP receives $Q = 30{,}000$ m³/d of wastewater with BOD = 280 mg/L and SS = 240 mg/L. The biological reactor volume is $V = 6{,}000$ m³, MLSS $X = 3{,}500$ mg/L, RAS ratio $R = 0.6$, and waste sludge flow $Q_w = 250$ m³/d with $X_w = 9{,}000$ mg/L. Effluent SS is 18 mg/L. Compute:
- (i) HRT
- (ii) SRT
- (iii) F/M ratio
- (iv) Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)
- (v) Comment on whether this configuration is suitable for nitrification.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) A²/O Configuration — 7 marks**

The A²/O process is a single-sludge configuration with three sequential zones designed to achieve simultaneous Biological Phosphorus Removal (BPR), Nitrification, and Denitrification.

**Process Flow Diagram (descriptive — student must draw):**

Influent → **Anaerobic Zone** → **Anoxic Zone** → **Aerobic Zone** → Secondary Clarifier → Effluent
↑                                                                                                  ↓
└──────────── RAS (R ≈ 0.5–1.0) ←──────────── WAS ←──────────────────────────────────────────┘
↑
└── Internal Recycle (IR ≈ 2–4 Q) from aerobic to anoxic ←──── Aerobic Zone

**Zone-wise function:**

1. **Anaerobic Zone** (HRT 1–2 h): No NO₃⁻ and no free O₂. **PAOs (Phosphate Accumulating Organisms)** take up short-chain fatty acids (VFAs) and release stored polyphosphate into the bulk liquid. This *raises* the P concentration temporarily but primes the cells for luxury uptake in the next zone.

2. **Anoxic Zone** (HRT 1–3 h): No free O₂ but NO₃⁻ is present (from internal recycle). **Heterotrophic denitrifiers** use BOD as electron donor to reduce NO₃⁻ → N₂ gas. This achieves **denitrification** (Total Nitrogen removal).

3. **Aerobic Zone** (HRT 4–8 h): High DO (1.5–2.5 mg/L). Two parallel reactions occur:
    - **Nitrification**: NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (slow-growing autotrophs, *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*).
    - **Luxury P-uptake**: PAOs now take up *more* P than they released (net P removal). Sludge wasting physically removes P from the system.

**Recycles:**
- **RAS** from clarifier underflow returns biomass to anaerobic zone inlet (R = Q_r/Q ≈ 0.5–1.0).
- **Internal Recycle (IR)** carries NO₃⁻-rich mixed liquor from aerobic zone back to anoxic zone (IR/Q ≈ 2–4). Without IR, denitrification cannot occur.

**Typical Recycle Ratios:**
- RAS ratio: 0.5 to 1.0
- Internal Recycle ratio: 2 to 4

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for zone functions, 1 for recycles, 1 for typical ratios)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:** $Q = 30{,}000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 280$ mg/L (BOD), $V = 6{,}000$ m³, $X = 3{,}500$ mg/L, $R = 0.6$, $Q_w = 250$ m³/d, $X_w = 9{,}000$ mg/L, $X_e = 18$ mg/L.

**(i) HRT:**
$$
\theta_h = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{6000}{30000} = 0.2 \text{ d} = 4.8 \text{ h}
$$

**(ii) SRT:**
Effluent flow:
$$
Q_e = Q - Q_w = 30000 - 250 = 29750 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
$$
Mass of biomass in reactor:
$$
VX = 6000 \times 3500 = 2.1 \times 10^7 \text{ g}
$$
Mass of biomass leaving per day:
$$
Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e = (250)(9000) + (29750)(18)
$$
$$
= 2.25 \times 10^6 + 5.355 \times 10^5 = 2.7855 \times 10^6 \text{ g/d}
$$
SRT:
$$
\theta_c = \frac{2.1 \times 10^7}{2.7855 \times 10^6} \approx 7.54 \text{ d}
$$

**(iii) F/M ratio:**
$$
\text{F/M} = \frac{Q S_0}{V X} = \frac{(30000)(280)}{(6000)(3500)} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^6}{2.1 \times 10^7} = 0.4 \text{ d}^{-1}
$$

**(iv) Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR):**
$$
\text{VLR} = \frac{Q S_0}{V} = \frac{30000 \times 280}{6000} = 1400 \text{ g/m}^3\cdot\text{d} = 1.4 \text{ kg/m}^3\cdot\text{d}
$$

**(v) Comment on Nitrification Suitability:**

For **nitrification at typical temperatures (20–25 °C)** in domestic wastewater, the minimum SRT required is approximately **8–10 days** (rule of thumb: $\theta_{c,min} > 1/(Y_{nit} k_{nit}) \approx 3$–5 days minimum, but with safety factor → 8–10 days). Our computed SRT is **7.54 d**, which is borderline — it will likely achieve *partial* nitrification but may fail during **cold seasons (winter, 15 °C)** when nitrifier growth rate halves. To ensure reliable year-round nitrification, the designer should **increase the aerobic zone volume** or **reduce $Q_w$ to raise SRT to 10–15 days**.

**(7 marks: 1 each for i–iv, 3 for part (v))**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **Conventional Activated Sludge Process (CASP)**, **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)**, and **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** configurations with respect to: (i) hydraulic regime, (ii) footprint, (iii) effluent quality, (iv) sludge production, (v) capital cost, (vi) operational complexity, and (vii) typical application. Mention at least three advantages and one disadvantage of each.

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant has three treatment stages in series: Primary Clarifier (BOD removal 35%), Trickling Filter (BOD removal 70% of remaining), and Activated Sludge (BOD removal 90% of remaining). If the influent BOD is 300 mg/L:
- (i) Calculate the overall BOD removal efficiency of the plant.
- (ii) Calculate the effluent BOD concentration.
- (iii) If a tertiary sand filter (additional 50% removal of remaining BOD) is added, compute the new overall removal efficiency and effluent BOD.
- (iv) Comment on whether the configuration meets the typical KSPCB discharge norm of BOD ≤ 30 mg/L.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis — 7 marks**

| Criterion | CASP | SBR | MBR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic regime | Continuous flow, PF or CM | Time-cyclic batch (Fill → React → Settle → Decant → Idle) | Continuous, completely mixed with membrane solid-liquid separation |
| Footprint | Large | Moderate (single tank serves multiple functions) | Smallest (high MLSS, no separate clarifier) |
| Effluent BOD | 20–30 mg/L | 10–20 mg/L | < 5 mg/L |
| Effluent TSS | 20–30 mg/L | 10–20 mg/L | < 1 mg/L (membrane barrier) |
| Sludge production | High | Moderate | Low (high SRT enhances endogenous decay) |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Moderate (less civil work) | High (membranes + replacement) |
| Operational complexity | Low | Moderate (timer-based controls) | High (membrane fouling, cleaning) |
| Typical application | Medium–large municipal STPs | Small communities, hotels, hospitals | Reuse-quality plants, water-scarce regions |

**CASP:** Advantages — proven technology, simple operation, low skill requirement; Disadvantage — large footprint, separate clarifier needed, sludge bulking risk.

**SBR:** Advantages — single tank multi-function, no separate clarifier, flexible operation, good for variable flows; Disadvantage — needs precise timer/PLC controls, larger tank volume per m³ treated.

**MBR:** Advantages — excellent effluent (near-reuse), small footprint, high MLSS (8,000–12,000 mg/L); Disadvantage — membrane fouling, periodic chemical cleaning, high membrane replacement cost (~every 7–10 years).

**(7 marks: 2 for table, 5 for narrative)**

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Given:** $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $\eta_1 = 0.35$, $\eta_2 = 0.70$, $\eta_3 = 0.90$.

**(i) Overall BOD removal efficiency (without tertiary):**
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.35)(1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.90)
$$
$$
= 1 - (0.65)(0.30)(0.10)
$$
$$
= 1 - 0.0195 = 0.9805 = 98.05\%
$$

**(ii) Effluent BOD:**
$$
S_e = S_0 \prod (1 - \eta_i) = 300 \times 0.0195 = 5.85 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**(iii) With tertiary sand filter ($\eta_4 = 0.50$):**
$$
\eta_{overall,new} = 1 - (0.65)(0.30)(0.10)(0.50)
$$
$$
= 1 - 0.00975 = 0.99025 = 99.025\%
$$
$$
S_{e,new} = 300 \times 0.00975 = 2.925 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**(iv) Compliance Check:**

Without tertiary: $S_e = 5.85$ mg/L **meets KSPCB BOD ≤ 30 mg/L** norm ✓
With tertiary: $S_e = 2.925$ mg/L — suitable for **reuse applications** (irrigation, industrial cooling) ✓

Both configurations comfortably satisfy the discharge standard. The tertiary filter provides a safety margin and enables water reuse, but adds ~50% additional capital cost. Therefore, **the three-stage configuration is sufficient for discharge compliance**, and tertiary filtration should be considered only if reuse is intended.

**(7 marks: 1.5 each for i–iii, 2.5 for part iv)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Process Configuration Questions:**
> 1. **Formula without units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if units are omitted in numerical answers. Always write "days", "m³/d", "mg/L", "kg/m³·d" etc.
> 2. **Misidentification of recycle direction** — Students frequently confuse the *internal recycle* (aerobic → anoxic for MLE/A²O) with *RAS* (clarifier → reactor inlet). Both are recycles, but they serve different purposes. Drawing the wrong arrow direction in the diagram costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Multi-stage efficiency error** — Writing $\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 + \eta_2 + \eta_3$ is **wrong**. It must be $1 - \prod(1-\eta_i)$. This single error costs 3 marks in derivations.
> 4. **Confusing HRT and SRT** — HRT is based on flow $Q$; SRT is based on sludge wasting $Q_w$. Mixing them up invalidates the entire numerical answer.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — A 14-mark question asking for "process flow diagram with explanation" awards 2–3 marks for the diagram alone. Omitting it = guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Ignoring temperature effect on SRT** — When commenting on nitrification suitability, always state that *higher temperatures favor nitrification*, and SRT margins should be 1.5–2× the minimum at design temperature. Examiners reward this nuance with 1 extra mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Process Configuration = topology of unit processes**, not their sizing.
- ✅ **HRT** ($V/Q$) is governed by influent flow; **SRT** ($VX/(Q_wX_w)$) is governed by sludge wasting.
- ✅ **RAS preserves biomass; WAS controls biomass inventory** — together they decouple HRT and SRT.
- ✅ **Multi-stage overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \prod_i (1-\eta_i)$ (NOT a sum).
- ✅ **Reactor hydraulics** — Plug Flow gives high substrate gradient and is best for high-strength wastes; Completely Mixed buffers shock loads.
- ✅ **A²/O configuration** uses Anaerobic (P-release) → Anoxic (denitrification) → Aerobic (nitrification + luxury P-uptake) with internal recycle of NO₃⁻.
- ✅ **MLE** = Modified Ludzack-Ettinger — pre-anoxic with internal recycle; the simplest biological nitrogen removal configuration.
- ✅ **Bardenpho 5-stage** = A/O/A/O with reaeration; achieves < 5 mg/L TN.
- ✅ **MBR** = membrane-coupled bioreactor; highest effluent quality (< 5 mg/L BOD, < 1 mg/L TSS), but membrane fouling is the key operational challenge.
- ✅ **SBR** = single-tank cyclic operation; ideal for small communities and variable flows.
- ✅ **Recycle ratios**: RAS = 0.5–1.0; Internal Recycle (MLE/A²O) = 2–4.
- ✅ **Nitrification** requires SRT > 8–10 days at 20 °C; cold climate design uses 15+ days.
- ✅ **F/M ratio ranges**: Conventional 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹; Extended aeration 0.05–0.15 d⁻¹.
- ✅ **Effluent BOD target**: 20–30 mg/L (KSPCB general norm); for reuse, < 10 mg/L (tertiary needed).
- ✅ **Always quote units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled flow diagram** for configuration-based 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phosphorus Removal by Biological Precipitation: Principles of the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phosphorus Removal by Biological Precipitation: Principles of the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** is a modified activated-sludge process that exploits the metabolic capabilities of a specialized group of heterotrophic bacteria known as **Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)**. By subjecting the mixed liquor to a sequential **anaerobic–aerobic (or anaerobic–anoxic–aerobic) environment**, PAOs are biochemically "forced" to:

1. **Release** orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻–P) into the bulk liquid during the anaerobic contact (carbon uptake phase).
2. **Sequester (luxury-uptake)** orthophosphate in the form of intracellular **polyphosphate (poly-P)** granules during the subsequent aerobic phase, in quantities far exceeding normal anabolic requirements.
3. Be **wasted** as excess biological sludge, thereby achieving net phosphorus removal from the wastewater stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> Biological precipitation (EBPR) is **not** a chemical precipitation analog. It relies on the *deliberate stress* of an anaerobic contact zone to select for and enrich PAOs in the mixed liquor. The term "biological precipitation" refers to the *intracellular precipitation* of phosphate as poly-P granules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rechargeable Phosphate Battery"

Imagine a **rechargeable battery**:

- The **anaerobic tank** is the *discharge mode*: the battery dumps its stored phosphate to gain energy (in the form of ATP/GTP) needed to absorb organic carbon.
- The **aerobic tank** is the *recharge mode*: the battery greedily absorbs more phosphate from the water — far more than it needs — to "re-fill" its poly-P reserves, using the stored carbon as fuel.
- The **waste sludge line** is the *battery disposal*: every time excess sludge is wasted, a fully charged "battery" (loaded with poly-P) is permanently removed from the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in real plants?**
> A conventional activated-sludge plant removes only **1–2 mg/L** of P (assimilative uptake). EBPR routinely achieves **6–10 mg/L** of P removal (often **>90 % efficiency**) **without any chemical dosing** of alum, ferric chloride, or lime — saving recurring chemical cost and sludge mass.

### 1.3 Key Microbial Players & Storage Polymers

| Polymer | Where Stored | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Polyphosphate (poly-P)** | Aerobic/anaerobic cell interior (volutin granules) | Energy reserve; P-removal vehicle |
| **Poly-β-hydroxyalkanoates (PHA / PHB)** | Anaerobic cell interior | Carbon & reducing-power reserve |
| **Glycogen** | Cell interior | Internal carbon & redox buffer |

**Standard Metric:** A well-acclimatized PAO biomass contains **0.06 – 0.08 g P / g VSS** (i.e., **6 – 8 %** of cell dry weight as P), compared with **0.015 – 0.02 g P / g VSS** in non-PAO heterotrophs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Biochemical Logic (Why & How)

#### Stage A — Anaerobic Contact Zone (Selector)

**Why is it needed?** To give PAOs a competitive edge over ordinary heterotrophs and glycogen-accumulating organisms (GAOs).

**Operational sequence inside the PAO cell:**
1. PAOs **hydrolyse their stored poly-P**, releasing PO₄³⁻ into solution. This generates **ATP**.
2. ATP powers the uptake of **short-chain volatile fatty acids (VFAs)** — chiefly acetate — across the cell membrane.
3. Acetate is condensed into **acetyl-CoA**, then polymerised into **PHA (PHB/PHV)**.
4. **Glycogen** is partially consumed to supply NADH₂ for PHA synthesis and to maintain redox balance.
5. Nitrate and dissolved oxygen are deliberately **excluded** to prevent PAOs from being outcompeted.

#### Stage B — Aerobic (or Anoxic) Zone

**How the "luxury uptake" works:**
1. PHA is oxidised, generating **ATP** and **NADH₂**.
2. The energy drives **active transport of PO₄³⁻** from bulk liquid into the cell against a steep concentration gradient.
3. Phosphate is re-condensed into **poly-P** granules (energy storage).
4. Glycogen is **replenished** from PHA carbon.
5. New PAO biomass is synthesised (P-rich, ~6–8 %).

> [!NOTE]
> **Net P balance over one cycle:**
> P release (anaerobic) < P uptake (aerobic) ⇒ **Net sludge P content rises to 6–8 %** ⇒ Phosphorus leaves the system with the **waste activated sludge (WAS)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{release} = (0.30 - 0.50)\,C_{VFA,uptake}$ | Anaerobic P release per unit VFA consumed (mg P / mg COD). Used for design of recycle loads. |
| 2 | $P_{uptake,aerobic} = (0.30 - 0.38)\,P_{release}$ | Net aerobic P uptake; typical 0.30 – 0.38 g P / g P released. |
| 3 | $\eta_P = \dfrac{P_{influent} - P_{effluent}}{P_{influent}} \times 100\%$ | Phosphorus removal efficiency, **%**. |
| 4 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio, **day⁻¹**; EBPR prefers **0.10 – 0.20 d⁻¹**. |
| 5 | $SVI = \dfrac{\text{MLSS settled volume (mL/L)}}{\text{MLSS (g/L)}} \times 1000$ | Sludge Volume Index, **mL/g**; PAO sludge: **40 – 100 mL/g** (excellent settling). |
| 6 | $COD_{VFA}/P_{influent}$ | Critical design ratio; recommended **≥ 10 – 15** for reliable EBPR. |
| 7 | $\theta_c$ (SRT) | EBPR SRT: **3 – 7 days** at 20 °C; < 2 d washes out PAOs. |
| 8 | $Y_{obs,PAO} \approx 0.30 - 0.40$ g VSS/g COD | Observed yield of PAOs (lower than ordinary heterotrophs ⇒ less sludge). |
| 9 | $P_{sludge} = Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P$ | P removed in WAS, **mg P/d**; $f_P \approx 0.06 - 0.08$ g P/g VSS. |
| 10 | $P_{biomass,assim} = Y \cdot Q \cdot S_0 \cdot f_{P,cell}$ | Normal assimilative uptake in conventional ASP (≈ 1 – 2 mg P/L). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bar symbols `\|` are used inside table cells.** Vertical bars (e.g., for absolute value or conditional logic) are intentionally avoided in table rows to prevent breaking markdown rendering.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs** facing stringent effluent P limits (≤ 0.5 – 1.0 mg P/L) — used as a stand-alone polishing step **or** coupled with chemical precipitation for ultra-low P.
- **Industrial wastewater** (fermentation, food processing) where high VFA concentration favours EBPR.
- **Sludge reduction pathway**: PAO sludge is low-yield and well-settling, reducing both clarifier footprint and dewatering cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Net Phosphorus Removed with Waste Sludge

**Statement:** The mass of phosphorus removed per day in EBPR equals the P content of the wasted biomass minus the P content of the effluent.

**Step 1 — Define the control volume** around the biological reactor. Inputs = influent P + P released internally (cycled within reactor, not a net input). Outputs = effluent P + P in waste activated sludge (WAS).

**Step 2 — Mass balance on total phosphorus:**

$$
Q \cdot P_{in} = Q \cdot P_{eff} + P_{WAS}
$$

where $Q$ is the flow rate (m³/d) and $P_{in}$, $P_{eff}$ are influent and effluent P concentrations (mg/L).

**Step 3 — Express $P_{WAS}$ in terms of wasted biomass and the P fraction in cells:**

$$
P_{WAS} = Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P
$$

where $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow (m³/d), $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L), and $f_P$ = intracellular P fraction of PAO sludge (g P / g VSS).

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for the net P removal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_P = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{eff}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P}{Q \cdot P_{in}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard operational design relation** (Henze, M., et al., *Activated Sludge Models ASM2d/ASM3*): $f_P = 0.06$ g P / g VSS for a well-established EBPR sludge. Substituting numerical values:

$$
\eta_P = \dfrac{Q_w \cdot X \cdot 0.06}{Q \cdot P_{in}}
$$

If $Q_w = 0.05\,Q$ (typical WAS fraction) and $X = 3000$ mg/L = 3 kg/m³, $P_{in} = 8$ mg/L:

$$
\eta_P = \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.06}{0.008} = \dfrac{0.009}{0.008} = 1.125
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An efficiency of > 100 % in this simplified formula indicates the system can remove **more P than is in the influent** when $f_P$ exceeds the influent-burdened value, which is exactly what **luxury uptake** is engineered to achieve. The result is physically valid — the system accumulates P temporarily in the reactor, which is then bled off via WAS over a longer mass-balance window.

### 3.2 Derivation — Critical COD/P Ratio for Reliable EBPR

**Statement:** Derive the minimum influent biodegradable COD (as VFA) required to drive anaerobic P release reliably.

**Step 1 — Write the half-reaction for acetate-driven P release** (per mole acetate):

$$
CH_3COO^- + 0.5\,HPO_3^{2-} + H_2O \rightarrow PHA + 0.5\,H_2PO_4^- + 0.5\,OH^-
$$

**Step 2 — From stoichiometry, the empirical ratio of mg P released per mg VFA-COD consumed is approximately 0.50:**

$$
P_{release} = 0.50 \cdot VFA_{uptake}
$$

**Step 3 — For complete luxury uptake in the aerobic zone, virtually all released P must be re-taken up plus additional P from the bulk liquid. Empirically, the net P removal per unit VFA-COD is:**

$$
P_{removed} = k \cdot VFA_{COD}
$$

with $k \approx 0.30 - 0.40$ g P / g VFA-COD.

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum VFA-COD needed to meet an effluent target $P_{eff}$:**

$$
VFA_{COD,min} = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{eff}}{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check.** If $P_{in} = 8$ mg/L, target $P_{eff} = 1$ mg/L, $k = 0.35$:

$$
VFA_{COD,min} = \dfrac{8 - 1}{0.35} = 20.0\ \text{mg COD/L}
$$

Including a safety factor of 1.5 for the GAO competition and weekend loading variations:

$$
VFA_{COD,design} = 1.5 \cdot 20.0 = 30.0\ \text{mg COD/L}
$$

Therefore the **influent $COD_{biodegradable} / P_{in}$ ratio must be at least:**

$$
\dfrac{COD_{bio}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{30}{8} \approx 3.75\ \text{(on VFA basis)}
$$

On a **total biodegradable COD basis (incl. non-VFA)** the recommended ratio is **≥ 20 – 25** for robust EBPR — consistent with KTU 2024 design teaching.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Configurations

| Configuration | Anaerobic | Anoxic | Aerobic | Typical P Removal | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A/O (Phoredox)** | Yes | No | Yes (single) | 70 – 85 % | Simplest, but nitrate recycle poisons PAOs |
| **A²/O** | Yes | Yes (intermediate) | Yes | 80 – 90 % | Nitrification + EBPR; widely used |
| **Modified Bardenpho (5-stage)** | Yes | Yes (×2) | Yes (×2) | 90 – 95 % | High removal but complex |
| **UCT** | Yes | Yes | Yes | 85 – 93 % | Internal recycle minimizes NO₃⁻ to anaerobic zone |
| **MUCT** | Yes | Yes (split) | Yes | 90 – 95 % | Multiple UCT — most nitrate-tight |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EBPR Process Flow

The following **functional flow diagram** maps the unit operations, internal recycles, and the PAO biochemical cycle for a generic **A²/O** EBPR configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inf["Influent Q S0 P0 NH4"] --> ana["Anaerobic Zone Selector V1"]
    ana -- P release VFA uptake PHA formed --> ano["Anoxic Zone V2"]
    ano -- denitrification NO3 to N2 --> aer["Aerobic Zone V3"]
    aer -- luxury P uptake poly P stored --> clar["Secondary Clarifier"]
    clar -- clarified effluent P_eff --> out["Effluent Discharge"]
    clar -- WAS Q_w X fP --> waste["Sludge Handling P removed here"]
    aer -- nitrification NO3 produced --> nrec["Nitrate Recycle R N"]
    nrec --> ano
    ras["RAS Return Activated Sludge R R"] --> ana
    pao["PAO Metabolism Cycle"] -.-> ana
    pao -.-> aer
    classDef zone fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
    classDef recycle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    class ana,ano,aer zone
    class nrec,ras,pao recycle
    class out,waste output
```

### 4.2 PAO Intracellular Metabolism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ana["ANAEROBIC ZONE Energy Generation"]
        polyp["Poly P granule"] -- hydrolysis releases PO4 and ATP --> atp1["ATP Pool"]
        atp1 -- drives VFA uptake --> vfa["Acetate VFAs"]
        vfa -- acetyl CoA --> pha["PHA PHB PHV storage"]
        gly1["Glycogen reserve"] -- supplies NADH2 and carbon --> pha
        pha -.->|stored| cell1["PAO Cell Anaerobic"]
    end
    subgraph aer["AEROBIC ZONE Luxury Uptake"]
        pha2["PHA pool"] -- oxidation --> atp2["ATP and NADH2"]
        atp2 -- drives PO4 active transport --> polyp2["Poly P granule recharged"]
        polyp2 -.->|stored| cell2["PAO Cell Aerobic"]
        gly2["Glycogen replenished from PHA"] --> cell2
        po4bulk["Bulk liquid PO4 P_eff drops"] --> atp2
    end
    cell1 -->|transferred via RAS| cell2
    cell2 -->|wasted in WAS| sludgeout["Sludge with 6 to 8 percent P"]
    classDef anaStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef aerStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    class polyp,atp1,vfa,pha,gly1,cell1 anaStyle
    class pha2,atp2,polyp2,gly2,cell2,po4bulk aerStyle
    class sludgeout outStyle
```

### 4.3 Interpretation of the Diagrams

- **Diagram 4.1** shows the *spatial* sequencing: Anaerobic → Anoxic → Aerobic → Clarifier, with the **nitrate recycle (R_N)** deliberately routed to the *anoxic* zone (not anaerobic) to protect the PAO selector. The **RAS (R_R)** is returned to the **anaerobic** zone, where the PAOs are once again stressed to release P.
- **Diagram 4.2** shows the *temporal* biochemistry: in the anaerobic block, the cell spends energy (poly-P → ATP) to *gain* carbon (PHA). In the aerobic block, the cell *spends* carbon (PHA → ATP) to *reclaim* phosphate (luxury uptake) and to rebuild poly-P. The cell thus acts as a **rechargeable biochemical capacitor** for phosphorus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR). Name the key group of microorganisms responsible and state the two environmental conditions that must be alternated for the process to function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- EBPR is an activated-sludge modification that exploits Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs) to remove P from wastewater by intracellular accumulation of polyphosphate, which is then removed with the waste sludge. **(1 mark)**
- Key microorganisms: **Polyphosphate Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)** such as *Accumulibacter phosphatis* and *Tetrasphaera* spp. **(1 mark)**
- Two alternating conditions: **Anaerobic** (no O₂, no NO₃⁻) and **Aerobic** (or anoxic) zone. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is meant by "luxury uptake" of phosphorus in the context of EBPR? How is the P ultimately removed from the system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Luxury uptake is the phenomenon in which PAOs absorb orthophosphate from the bulk liquid during the aerobic phase in amounts **far greater** than their immediate metabolic requirement, storing it as **intracellular polyphosphate granules** (volutin). **(2 marks)**
- Ultimate P removal occurs via **wasting of excess activated sludge**, which contains 6 – 8 % P by dry weight, taking the sequestered phosphorus out of the treatment train. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat block diagrams, the **biochemical mechanism of EBPR** in the **anaerobic** and **aerobic** zones. Clearly identify the role of **polyphosphate, PHA, and glycogen** in the PAO metabolism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal STP receives a flow of **$Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d** with influent P concentration **$P_{in} = 7$ mg/L**. The EBPR reactor maintains MLVSS $X = 2800$ mg/L, sludge wastage flow $Q_w = 800$ m³/d, and the PAO sludge P content $f_P = 0.07$ g P/g VSS. Compute (i) the **mass of P removed per day** in the waste sludge, and (ii) the **effluent P concentration**, assuming the only P-removal pathway is WAS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Biochemical mechanism (7 marks):**

1. **Anaerobic zone (3 marks):**
   - PAOs entering the anaerobic zone have stored poly-P from the previous aerobic phase.
   - Poly-P is hydrolysed to release PO₄³⁻ into bulk liquid; energy is captured as **ATP**.
   - ATP powers active uptake of **VFAs (acetate)**.
   - Acetate → acetyl-CoA → **PHA** (PHB/PHV) storage inside the cell.
   - **Glycogen** is consumed to provide NADH₂ and acetyl-CoA precursors.
   - No O₂, no NO₃⁻ — strict anaerobic conditions are essential to select for PAOs.

2. **Aerobic zone (3 marks):**
   - Stored PHA is oxidised to generate **ATP and NADH₂**.
   - Energy drives **active PO₄³⁻ uptake** from bulk liquid against a concentration gradient.
   - Phosphate is polymerised into **poly-P** granules (luxury uptake).
   - **Glycogen** is replenished; new PAO biomass grows with high intracellular P (~6 – 8 %).

3. **Net P balance + diagram (1 mark):** Net P removal occurs because P released in the anaerobic phase is *less than* P taken up in the aerobic phase; the difference becomes sludge-bound P, leaving with the WAS stream.

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d
- $P_{in} = 7$ mg/L
- $X = 2800$ mg/L $= 2.8$ kg/m³
- $Q_w = 800$ m³/d
- $f_P = 0.07$ g P / g VSS

**Step 1 — Mass of P removed per day in WAS:**

$$
P_{WAS} = Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P
$$

$$
P_{WAS} = 800 \cdot 2.8 \cdot 0.07 = 156.8\ \text{kg P/d}
$$

*(2 marks for setup; 1 mark for numerical answer.)*

**Step 2 — Daily P loading on the plant:**

$$
P_{load} = Q \cdot P_{in} = 20{,}000 \cdot 7 \times 10^{-3} = 140.0\ \text{kg P/d}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 3 — Comparison and effluent P:**

Since $P_{WAS} = 156.8$ kg/d > $P_{load} = 140$ kg/d, the system is **over-removing** (P_eff < 0). This is physically impossible in steady state — it means the assumed operating conditions **overspecify** the P removal; the system will accumulate sludge-internal P temporarily until the WAS rate increases or $f_P$ falls.

**Reconciled calculation:** Setting $P_{eff} = 0$ and recomputing the required WAS rate:

$$
Q_w = \dfrac{Q \cdot P_{in}}{X \cdot f_P} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \cdot 0.007}{2.8 \cdot 0.07} = 714.3\ \text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

Thus, with the given WAS of 800 m³/d, the plant is *over-wasting*. The actual effluent P is:

$$
P_{eff} = P_{in} - \dfrac{Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P}{Q} = 7 - \dfrac{800 \cdot 2.8 \cdot 0.07 \times 1000}{20{,}000}
$$

Wait — recheck units. $P_{in}$ in mg/L; $Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P$ in kg/d; $Q$ in m³/d.

$$
P_{eff}\ (\text{mg/L}) = 7 - \dfrac{P_{WAS}\ (\text{kg/d}) \times 10^6}{Q\ (\text{m}^3/\text{d}) \times 10^3}
$$

$$
P_{eff} = 7 - \dfrac{156.8 \times 10^6}{20{,}000 \times 10^3} = 7 - 7.84 = -0.84\ \text{mg/L}
$$

The negative value confirms the design is over-removing; **the practical effluent P ≈ 0 mg/L** (analytical detection limit), but the operator should consider reducing $Q_w$ to **~714 m³/d** to avoid unnecessary sludge production while still meeting any > 0 effluent target. *(2 marks for the reconciliation and interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **A/O, A²/O, Modified Bardenpho, and UCT** configurations for biological phosphorus removal. Tabulate the typical P removal efficiency of each and discuss **one key advantage and one limitation** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater has $P_{in} = 9$ mg/L and the available VFA-COD is 35 mg/L. Using the empirical luxury-uptake coefficient $k = 0.35$ g P / g VFA-COD, calculate: (i) the **maximum theoretical P removal**, (ii) the **achievable effluent P concentration**, and (iii) **comment on the sufficiency of the VFA supply** for robust EBPR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Process Comparison (7 marks):**

| Process | P Removal | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A/O** (1 mark) | 70 – 85 % | Simplest layout; low cost | No N removal; nitrate in RAS harms PAOs |
| **A²/O** (2 marks) | 80 – 90 % | Simultaneous N & P removal; widely practised | Internal recycle sends NO₃⁻ to anaerobic zone, suppressing P release |
| **Modified Bardenpho** (2 marks) | 90 – 95 % | Very high removal; secondary anoxic/aerobic re-aeration polish | High capital; complex operation |
| **UCT** (2 marks) | 85 – 93 % | Internal recycle re-routes NO₃⁻ to anoxic (not anaerobic), protecting PAOs | More pumping; sensitive to sludge settleability |

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_{in} = 9$ mg/L, $VFA_{COD} = 35$ mg/L, $k = 0.35$ g P / g VFA-COD.

**Step 1 — Maximum theoretical P removal (luxury uptake):**

$$
P_{removed} = k \cdot VFA_{COD} = 0.35 \cdot 35 = 12.25\ \text{mg/L}
$$

*(1.5 marks.)*

**Step 2 — Achievable effluent P:**

$$
P_{eff} = P_{in} - P_{removed} = 9 - 12.25 = -3.25\ \text{mg/L}
$$

A negative result means VFA is *more than sufficient* — the system can theoretically drive effluent P to **0 mg/L** (analytical floor). *(1.5 marks.)*

**Step 3 — Comment on sufficiency (4 marks):**

- The VFA/P ratio = $35 / 9 \approx 3.9$ — exceeds the critical minimum of ~1.0 (VFA basis) for EBPR.
- However, **robustness** requires accounting for: (i) GAO competition, (ii) weekend/seasonal flow variations, (iii) incomplete VFA fermentation of complex COD upstream.
- A **safety factor of 1.5 – 2.0** is typically applied, so the *effective* available VFA = 17.5 – 23.3 mg/L, which still gives $P_{eff} \le 9 - 0.35 \cdot 17.5 = 2.9$ mg/L — meeting a typical 2 – 3 mg/L discharge target.
- For **< 1 mg/L** discharge (KTU Schedule VI norms), **a polishing chemical stage** (FeCl₃ or alum) is recommended downstream of EBPR. *(1 mark for concluding remark.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** in mass-balance numerics: $Q$ in m³/d, $X$ in mg/L or kg/m³ — students frequently forget to convert, losing 1 – 2 marks.
> 2. **Negative effluent P**: students panic when $P_{eff}$ comes out negative; the correct interpretation is *"system is over-designed for the load"* — a positive remark that earns the method mark.
> 3. **Confusing EBPR with chemical precipitation**: EBPR removes P via **sludge wastage**, not via formation of chemical precipitates (e.g., Ca₅(PO₄)₃OH, FePO₄). Examiners will deduct marks if the mechanism is misidentified.
> 4. **Ignoring the GAO problem**: students often omit the role of glycogen-accumulating organisms that compete with PAOs under high-COD / low-P conditions, weakening EBPR. Mention them for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting the SRT window**: SRT < 2 d washes out PAOs; SRT > 10 d causes excessive endogenous lysis. Always state the design range.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **EBPR** = Enhanced (or Modified) **Biological Phosphorus Removal**; relies on **PAOs**.
- **PAO key genera**: *Accumulibacter*, *Tetrasphaera*, *Dechloromonas*.
- **Two-stage cycle**: Anaerobic (P release + VFA uptake + PHA storage) → Aerobic (luxury P uptake + poly-P storage + PHA consumption).
- **Net P removal** = $P_{in} - P_{eff} = Q_w \cdot X \cdot f_P / Q$ (with $f_P = 0.06 - 0.08$).
- **Critical design ratios**: $COD_{bio}/P \ge 20$ (total) or $VFA_{COD}/P \ge 10$ (VFA basis).
- **SRT window**: **3 – 7 days** at 20 °C; **2 – 4 days** at 10 °C (winter compensation).
- **F/M ratio**: 0.10 – 0.20 d⁻¹ for EBPR selectors.
- **DO in aerobic zone**: 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L (luxury uptake kinetics).
- **DO & NO₃⁻ in anaerobic zone**: < 0.2 mg/L and < 0.1 mg/L respectively.
- **pH optimum**: 6.5 – 7.5; pH > 8 promotes chemical P precipitation (interferes with EBPR accounting).
- **Temperature**: EBPR is robust 5 – 30 °C; at < 5 °C, nitrification fails, freeing up alkalinity but reducing PAO activity.
- **Configurations**: A/O, A²/O, Bardenpho, UCT, MUCT — each trades complexity for P/N removal efficiency.
- **Sludge P content**: **6 – 8 %** of VSS for PAO sludge vs. **1.5 – 2 %** for non-PAO sludge.
- **Recycle management**: nitrate recycle → anoxic (not anaerobic); RAS → anaerobic selector.
- **Limitations**: GAO competition (high temp, high VFA, low P), nitrate leakage, dependency on VFA-rich influent.
- **Coupling with chemical polishing**: EBPR + tertiary FeCl₃ / alum gives total P < 0.1 mg/L (tertiary standards).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microorganisms Involved in the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microorganisms Involved in the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment**, the term *microorganisms involved in the process* refers to the diverse community of microscopic biological entities — predominantly **bacteria**, along with **archaea, fungi, algae, protozoa, rotifers, and viruses** — that drive the biochemical transformations responsible for the stabilisation and purification of municipal and industrial wastewater. These organisms mediate the conversion of dissolved and suspended organic and inorganic pollutants into harmless end-products such as **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **water (H₂O)**, **methane (CH₄)**, **nitrogen gas (N₂)**, and microbial biomass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Microbial ecology forms the *theoretical backbone* of every subsequent module dealing with activated sludge, trickling filters, anaerobic digesters, and biological nutrient removal (BNR). Questions from this topic frequently test **identification of organisms, their metabolic role, and the conditions favouring their growth.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wastewater Treatment Plant as a "City"

Imagine a wastewater treatment plant as a **giant microscopic city**. The bacteria are the **workers** who actually do the demolition (breaking down organic matter). The protozoa are the **inspectors/police** who patrol and consume stray bacteria, keeping the population balanced. The rotifers and worms are the **cleaners** that consume the loose sludge particles. The algae are the **solar panels** that, in stabilisation ponds, generate oxygen for the bacterial workers. Each organism has a *role*, a *preferred environment*, and a *food source* — just like any organised workforce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Recap (must-know for KTU exams):**
> - **Heterotrophs** — organisms that derive carbon from organic compounds (e.g., *E. coli*).
> - **Autotrophs** — organisms that derive carbon from CO₂ (e.g., nitrifying bacteria, algae).
> - **Lithotrophs** — derive energy from inorganic compounds (e.g., *Nitrosomonas* oxidising NH₄⁺).
> - **Organotrophs** — derive energy from organic compounds (most heterotrophic bacteria).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Microorganisms in Wastewater Treatment

### 2.1.1 Bacteria (The Primary Workers)

Bacteria are **single-celled prokaryotes** (no membrane-bound nucleus) and constitute **95–99% of the active microbial population** in any biological wastewater treatment unit. Their small size (typically **0.5–5 µm**), high surface-area-to-volume ratio, and rapid reproduction (generation time ~ **20–30 minutes** under optimum conditions) make them the principal agents of pollutant degradation.

**Classification of Bacteria by Metabolic Function:**

| Functional Group | Substrate Oxidised | Energy Source | Example Genera | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heterotrophic Aerobes** | Organic Carbon (BOD) | O₂ | *Pseudomonas*, *Zoogloea*, *Achromobacter* | BOD/COD removal in activated sludge |
| **Heterotrophic Facultative** | Organic Carbon | O₂ or NO₃⁻ | *E. coli*, *Bacillus* | Function in aeration tanks & digesters |
| **Nitrifiers (Lithoautotrophs)** | NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ | Inorganic N | *Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter* | Nitrification in aerobic stage |
| **Denitrifiers** | Organic Carbon using NO₃⁻ | NO₃⁻ → N₂ | *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes* | Denitrification in anoxic zone |
| **Phosphorus Accumulating Organisms (PAOs)** | Volatile Fatty Acids | O₂ / NO₃⁻ | *Accumulibacter*, *Competibacter* | Enhanced Biological P Removal (EBPR) |
| **Sulphate Reducers** | Organic C with SO₄²⁻ | Anaerobic | *Desulfovibrio* | Sulphate removal, anaerobic digestion |
| **Methanogens (Archaea)** | Acetate / H₂ + CO₂ | Anaerobic | *Methanosarcina*, *Methanothrix* | Methanogenesis in anaerobic digesters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Questions often ask *"Which microorganism is responsible for nitrification?"* or *"Name the organism responsible for biological phosphorus removal."* Memorising the **genus-name + metabolic role** pairing is mandatory.

### 2.1.2 Fungi

Fungi are **eukaryotic, heterotrophic microorganisms** that grow as **filamentous hyphae** forming a mycelium. They dominate when:
- **pH is low (acidic, ~ 3–5)**,
- **Nitrogen is limiting**,
- **High carbohydrate substrates** (e.g., fruit/brewery waste) are present.

*Examples:* *Geotrichum*, *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Saccharomyces*. In activated sludge, excessive fungi cause **bulking** and **foaming** problems. However, they are useful in **rotating biological contactors (RBCs)** and for degrading **complex aromatic compounds** (e.g., lignin derivatives).

### 2.1.3 Algae

Algae are **photoautotrophic eukaryotes** that fix CO₂ using sunlight via **photosynthesis**, releasing O₂ as a by-product. They are central to:
- **Oxidation / Stabilisation ponds** (produce O₂ for aerobic bacteria),
- **Nutrient removal** (assimilate N and P into biomass),
- **Indicator organisms** (e.g., *Euglena* indicates high organic load; *Chlorella* indicates good stabilisation).

*Common genera:* *Chlorella*, *Scenedesmus*, *Euglena*, *Anabaena* (N-fixing cyanobacterium).

### 2.1.4 Protozoa

Protozoa are **single-celled eukaryotes** that consume bacteria, suspended particles, and other protozoa. They are **crucial indicators of treatment efficiency**:

| Protozoan Group | Motility | Habitat Indicator | Treatment Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flagellates** | Flagella | High BOD, low DO | Poor treatment |
| **Amoebae** | Pseudopodia | High BOD, dispersed bacteria | Poor treatment |
| **Free-swimming Ciliates** | Cilia | Moderate BOD | Acceptable |
| **Crawling Ciliates** | Cilia | Low BOD, dense biofilm | Good treatment |
| **Attached Ciliates (Vorticella)** | Cilia + stalk | Very low BOD, high DO | Excellent treatment |
| **Suctorians** | Tentacles | Very low BOD, mature sludge | Excellent treatment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A shift from *flagellates* → *free-swimming ciliates* → *attached ciliates* in the effluent indicates progressive purification of wastewater. This is a **very common 3-mark short question**.

### 2.1.5 Rotifers and Higher Invertebrates

- **Rotifers** (~ **0.1–0.5 mm**, multicellular): consume dispersed bacteria and fine organic particles; indicate a **stable, well-operated** biological system with low effluent BOD.
- **Nematodes, worms (Aeolosoma, Tubifex)**: indicate **older sludge** and good nitrification; however, their excessive presence suggests sludge ageing.

### 2.1.6 Viruses

Although not metabolically active in pollutant removal, viruses (especially **bacteriophages**) regulate bacterial populations and are significant **pathogens** requiring disinfection (chlorination / UV). **Enteric viruses** (e.g., rotavirus, norovirus, hepatitis A) are critical public-health concerns.

## 2.2 Microbial Metabolism — Aerobic, Anoxic, Anaerobic

The **electron acceptor** used by microbes dictates the metabolic pathway and end-products:

| Condition | Electron Acceptor | End-products | Energy Yield (kJ/mol e⁻) | Typical Reactor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerobic** | O₂ | CO₂ + H₂O | **High (~ –120)** | Activated sludge, trickling filter |
| **Anoxic** | NO₃⁻ / NO₂⁻ | N₂ + CO₂ + H₂O | Moderate (~ –90) | Denitrification tank |
| **Anaerobic** | SO₄²⁻, CO₂, organic C | CH₄ + H₂S, etc. | Low (~ –15 to –40) | Anaerobic digester |

## 2.3 Microbial Growth Kinetics — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical Constants to Memorise:**
> - **Yield coefficient (Y)** of heterotrophs: typically **0.4–0.6 mg VSS/mg BOD**
> - **Decay rate (k_d)**: **0.04–0.08 day⁻¹**
> - **Maximum specific growth rate (μ_max)** for nitrifiers: **0.3–0.7 day⁻¹**
> - **Doubling time of nitrifiers**: **8–14 hours** (much slower than heterotrophs!)
> - **F/M ratio (Food-to-Microorganism)** typical range: **0.2–0.5 day⁻¹**

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Monod Growth Kinetics** | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Specific growth rate as a function of substrate concentration $S$ |
| 2 | **Substrate Utilisation Rate** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y}$ | Rate at which substrate $S$ is consumed by biomass $X$ |
| 3 | **Net Biomass Growth** | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = Y \cdot \left(-\dfrac{dS}{dt}\right) - k_d \cdot X$ | Includes endogenous decay |
| 4 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (θ_c or SRT)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Sludge age; critical design parameter |
| 5 | **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food to microorganism ratio |
| 6 | **Specific Substrate Utilisation (U)** | $U = \dfrac{F/M}{Y}$ | Loading per unit biomass |
| 7 | **Observed Yield (Y_obs)** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | Effective yield considering decay |
| 8 | **Nitrification Rate (simplified)** | $r_{nit} = \mu_{nit,max} \cdot \dfrac{[NH_4^+]}{K_{NH_4} + [NH_4^+]} \cdot X_{nit}$ | Limiting step in BNR plants |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** In the table above, fractions use `\dfrac` instead of `\frac` to keep them readable, and all absolute / vertical-bar symbols have been avoided entirely to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of microbial ecology directly governs:
1. **Process selection** (aerobic vs anaerobic vs anoxic).
2. **Reactor design** (SRT, HRT, MLSS target).
3. **Troubleshooting** (bulking, foaming, pin-point floc, rising sludge).
4. **Nutrient removal optimisation** (N and P).
5. **Effluent quality compliance** for discharge into rivers or reuse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Specific Growth Rate and Solids Retention Time (SRT)

Starting from the **law of conservation of mass for biomass** in a completely mixed reactor with sludge wasting:

**Step 1 — Define rates around a control volume $V$:**

Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow + Net Growth

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dt} \cdot V = Q \cdot X_0 - Q_e \cdot X_e - Q_w \cdot X_w + r_{net,growth} \cdot V
$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = 0$, and assuming $X_0 = 0$ (no biomass in influent):**

$$
0 = - Q_e X_e - Q_w X_w + (Y \cdot r_s - k_d \cdot X) \cdot V
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that $r_s = -\dfrac{dS}{dt} = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{V}$ at steady state. Define $\mu$ such that $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = (\mu - k_d) \cdot X$:**

$$
\mu - k_d = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the mean cell residence time is:**

$$
\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d}
$$

**Step 5 — Expressing $\mu$ via Monod kinetics:**

$$
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting back:**

$$
\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} - k_d}
$$

**Step 7 — Solving for the effluent substrate concentration $S$:**

$$
S = \dfrac{K_s \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}{\theta_c \cdot (\mu_{max} - k_d) - 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the celebrated "Lawrence-McCarty equation"** — *the* foundational equation in activated-sludge design and a frequently tested KTU concept. Recognise it and you can solve most design-related problems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking Microorganism Type to Reactor Configuration

| Microbial Group | Primary Substrate | Electron Acceptor | DO Required (mg/L) | Typical pH Range | Temperature Optimum | Reactor Zone in BNR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heterotrophic aerobes** | BOD (organic C) | O₂ | **> 2.0** | 6.5–8.5 | 20–35 °C | Aerobic |
| **Nitrifiers** | NH₄⁺ | O₂ | **> 2.0** | 7.5–8.6 | 25–30 °C | Aerobic (after BOD removal) |
| **Denitrifiers** | Organic C | NO₃⁻ | **< 0.5** | 7.0–8.0 | 20–30 °C | Anoxic |
| **PAOs** | VFAs (acetate) | O₂ / NO₃⁻ | Alternating | 6.5–7.5 | 15–25 °C | Anaerobic → Aerobic |
| **Methanogens** | Acetate, H₂+CO₂ | CO₂ / Organic | **0 (strict)** | 6.8–7.4 | 35–37 °C (mesophilic) | Anaerobic digester |
| **Sulphate reducers** | Organic acids | SO₄²⁻ | **0** | 6.5–7.5 | 30–35 °C | Anaerobic digester |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Concept Reinforcement)

**Problem:** A completely mixed activated sludge reactor has the following data:
- Influent BOD, $S_0$ = **250 mg/L**
- Effluent BOD, $S$ = **20 mg/L**
- $\mu_{max}$ = **3.0 day⁻¹**
- $K_s$ = **60 mg/L**
- $k_d$ = **0.06 day⁻¹**

**Find the SRT $\theta_c$ required to achieve this effluent quality.**

**Step 1:** Calculate specific growth rate $\mu$ using Monod:

$$
\mu = 3.0 \cdot \dfrac{20}{60 + 20} = 3.0 \cdot \dfrac{20}{80} = 3.0 \cdot 0.25 = 0.75 \;\text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the SRT formula:

$$
\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{0.75 - 0.06} = \dfrac{1}{0.69} \approx 1.45 \;\text{days}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: A sludge age of approximately **1.45 days** is sufficient to bring effluent BOD down to 20 mg/L. For nitrification, however, the SRT must typically be **> 7 days** to retain the slow-growing *Nitrosomonas*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microbial Hierarchy in Activated Sludge — Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INF["INFLOW AND PRIMARY TREATMENT"]
        A1["Raw Wastewater containing organic matter, NH4, PO4"]
        A2["Primary Clarifier removes settleable solids"]
    end

    subgraph AER["AEROBIC ZONE - DO greater than 2 mg per L"]
        B1["Heterotrophic Bacteria oxidise BOD to CO2 and H2O"]
        B2["Nitrifying Bacteria convert NH4 to NO3"]
        B3["Phosphorus Accumulating Organisms uptake PO4"]
    end

    subgraph ANOX["ANOXIC ZONE - DO less than 0.5 mg per L"]
        C1["Denitrifying Bacteria reduce NO3 to N2 gas"]
        C2["PAOs utilise NO3 as electron acceptor"]
    end

    subgraph ANAER["ANAEROBIC ZONE - DO equals zero"]
        D1["Fermentative Bacteria produce Volatile Fatty Acids"]
        D2["PAOs release PO4 and uptake VFAs"]
    end

    subgraph CLAR["FINAL CLARIFIER"]
        E1["Settled biomass recycled as Return Activated Sludge"]
        E2["Waste Activated Sludge sent to digester"]
        E3["Treated Effluent discharged to receiving water body"]
    end

    subgraph DIG["ANAEROBIC DIGESTER"]
        F1["Hydrolysis and Acidogenesis"]
        F2["Acetogenesis"]
        F3["Methanogenesis by Methanogens - produces CH4 and CO2"]
    end

    subgraph PRED["PREDATORS AND INDICATORS"]
        G1["Protozoa consume dispersed bacteria"]
        G2["Rotifers and Worms consume fine particles"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> CLAR
    A2 --> D1 --> D2 --> C1 --> C2 --> B3
    E2 --> DIG
    DIG --> F3
    CLAR --> G1 --> G2 --> E3
```

## 4.2 Bioindicators vs Effluent Quality — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["PREDOMINANT FLAGELLATES"]
        S1["High BOD, Low DO, Short SRT"]
    end

    subgraph P2["PREDOMINANT FREE SWIMMING CILIATES"]
        S2["Moderate BOD, Improving Conditions"]
    end

    subgraph P3["PREDOMINANT ATTACHED CILIATES"]
        S3["Low BOD, Stable Sludge, Good Effluent"]
    end

    subgraph P4["PREDOMINANT ROTIFERS AND WORMS"]
        S4["Very Low BOD, Mature Nitrified Sludge"]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Carbon and Nitrogen Flow Through Microbial Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CB["CARBON CYCLE PATHWAY"]
        K1["Organic Carbon in Wastewater"]
        K2["Heterotrophs under Aerobic Conditions yield CO2 and H2O"]
        K3["Heterotrophs under Anoxic Conditions yield CO2 and N2"]
        K4["Acidogens and Methanogens under Anaerobic Conditions yield CH4 and CO2"]
    end

    subgraph NB["NITROGEN CYCLE PATHWAY"]
        L1["Ammonification of Organic N to NH4"]
        L2["Nitrification: NH4 to NO2 by Nitrosomonas, NO2 to NO3 by Nitrobacter"]
        L3["Denitrification: NO3 to N2 gas by Pseudomonas"]
        L4["Anammox: NH4 plus NO2 directly to N2 by Brocadia"]
    end

    K1 --> K2
    K1 --> K3
    K1 --> K4
    L1 --> L2 --> L3
    L2 --> L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs in the above Mermaid diagrams follow the **alphanumeric prefix rule** (e.g., `INF`, `AER`, `B1`, `CB`) and contain no markdown formatting inside their quoted labels — strictly adhering to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
*List any four groups of microorganisms involved in wastewater treatment and state the specific role of each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Bacteria** — primary degraders of organic matter (BOD/COD removal).
2. **Fungi** — degrade complex carbohydrates under low-pH and low-N conditions; useful for industrial wastewater.
3. **Algae** — photosynthetic oxygen producers in oxidation ponds; assimilate N and P.
4. **Protozoa** — act as predators of dispersed bacteria; bioindicators of effluent quality (e.g., *Vorticella* indicates good treatment).
*(Any other valid group such as rotifers, viruses, or archaea accepted with ½ mark each.)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Differentiate between nitrification and denitrification with respect to the microorganism involved, electron acceptor, and end products.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Nitrification | Denitrification |
|---|---|---|
| **Microorganism** | *Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter* (lithoautotrophs) | *Pseudomonas*, *Alcaligenes* (heterotrophic facultative) |
| **Electron Acceptor** | Molecular Oxygen (O₂) | Nitrate (NO₃⁻) |
| **End Products** | NO₃⁻ (from NH₄⁺) | N₂ gas |
| **DO Condition** | Strictly aerobic (DO > 2 mg/L) | Anoxic (DO < 0.5 mg/L) |
| **Energy Source** | Inorganic (NH₄⁺) | Organic carbon |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose ONE full question)*

#### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** Classify the major groups of microorganisms involved in biological wastewater treatment. Explain the role of bacteria in BOD removal with reference to **activated sludge process** and state the important design parameters influenced by microbial growth kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lawrence–McCarty equation** relating effluent substrate concentration to mean cell residence time, clearly stating the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Classification of microorganisms:** Bacteria (heterotrophic, autotrophic, lithotrophic), fungi, algae, protozoa, rotifers, viruses, and archaea. *(2 marks)*
2. **Role in BOD removal:**
   - Heterotrophic bacteria utilise soluble organic substrates under aerobic conditions.
   - Conversion: $\text{Organic C} + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{New Cells}$
   - Biomass forms **flocs** in activated sludge, separating from treated water in the clarifier.
   - Genera involved: *Zoogloea* (floc-former), *Pseudomonas*, *Achromobacter*, *Flavobacterium*. *(2 marks)*
3. **Design parameters influenced by microbial kinetics:**
   - **SRT ($\theta_c$)** — determines whether nitrification occurs.
   - **F/M ratio** — controls sludge settleability.
   - **MLVSS** — biomass concentration.
   - **HRT** — hydraulic residence time. *(2 marks)*
