Density of slurry ≈ 1.0 g/mL (dilute assumption). $$\text{Slurry mass required} = \frac{724.9}{0.05} = 14498 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$\text{Slurry volume} = \frac{14498 \text{ kg/day}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3} \approx 14.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Slurry volume} \approx 14.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$ A typical design assumes a holding time of 8 hours in the slurry tank: $$V_{tank} = 14.5 \times \frac{8}{24} = 4.83 \text{ m}^3$$ **Design recommendation:** Provide a slurry tank of volume ≈ **5 m³** with a mechanical agitator running at 30 – 60 rpm. --- #### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2] **(a)** Compare strong acid–strong base and weak acid–strong base neutralization under the following heads: pH at equivalence point, titration curve shape, conductivity behaviour, and reagent demand. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Strong Acid + Strong Base | Weak Acid + Strong Base | |---|---|---| | **Equivalence pH** | Exactly 7.0 | > 7 (typically 8 – 9) due to hydrolysis of conjugate base | | **Titration curve shape** | Sharp, near-vertical jump around equivalence; symmetrical | Gradual slope; prominent buffer plateau centred at $pH = pK_a$ | | **Conductivity** | High at both extremes (free $H^+$ or free $OH^-$); minimum at equivalence | Lower at equivalence; rises again with excess $OH^-$ | | **Reagent demand (g per L)** | Predictable by simple stoichiometry | Higher near the buffer region; controlled by $K_a$ | | **Indicator suitable** | Phenolphthalein, methyl orange — both work | Phenolphthalein preferred (transition range 8.2 – 10) | | **Example reaction** | $HCl + NaOH \rightarrow NaCl + H_2O$ | $CH_3COOH + NaOH \rightarrow CH_3COONa + H_2O$ | | **Heat of reaction** | ~ –55.84 kJ/mol (constant) | Variable, generally lower than strong system | --- **(b)** A dairy industry discharges an effluent with the following characteristics: $Q = 2 \text{ L/s}$, $pH = 4.2$, alkalinity negligible. Estimate the daily requirement of **commercial $NaOH$ (purity 98%)** to bring the pH to 7.0. Molecular weight of NaOH = 40. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial $[H^+]$.** $$[H^+]_i = 10^{-4.2} = 6.310 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 2 — Final $[H^+]$.** $$[H^+]_f = 10^{-7} = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 3 — Net $[H^+]$ to be neutralized.** $$\Delta[H^+] \approx 6.310 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$ (Negligible contribution from $10^{-7}$.) **Step 4 — Moles of NaOH required per litre.** From $H^+ + OH^- \rightarrow H_2O$, the stoichiometry is 1:1. $$n_{NaOH} = 6.310 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 5 — Mass of pure NaOH per litre.** $$m_{pure} = 6.310 \times 10^{-5} \times 40 = 2.524 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L}$$ **Step 6 — Apply purity correction (98%).** $$m_{commercial} = \frac{2.524 \times 10^{-3}}{0.98} = 2.576 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L}$$ **Step 7 — Daily mass of NaOH.** $$Q_{daily} = 2 \times 86400 = 172800 \text{ L/day}$$ $$\text{Mass}_{daily} = 2.576 \times 10^{-3} \times 172800 = 445.1 \text{ g/day} \approx 0.445 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Daily NaOH requirement} \approx 0.445 \text{ kg/day}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** Because the influent is only mildly acidic (pH 4.2) and the flow is moderate, the absolute NaOH demand is small. This justifies selection of NaOH (precise dosing) over lime (slurry handling would be uneconomical at this low load). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Ignoring purity correction** — Students write $m = N \times Eq.wt.$ but forget the *commercial purity* factor, losing 1 full mark. > 2. **Confusing normality with molarity** — Always multiply molarity by the *number of replaceable $H^+$ or $OH^-$* to obtain normality. For $H_2SO_4$, $N = 2M$; for $Ca(OH)_2$, $N = 2M$. > 3. **Using moles instead of equivalents** — Neutralization stoichiometry *must* be balanced in equivalents, not moles, for polyprotic systems. > 4. **Assuming pH 7 equivalence for weak acids** — Examiner specifically deducts 1 mark if you state "the equivalence pH is always 7." > 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — Flow rate $Q$ is typically given in L/s or m³/h; reagent mass comes out in g or kg. Unit-inconsistency errors cost 1 mark. > 6. **No control-loop discussion in design answers** — A complete neutralization design answer *must* mention pH sensor → controller → dosing-pump feedback loop. Omission = –1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Sheet — Pin This to Your Wall** - **Definition:** Neutralization = adjusting pH to **6.0 – 9.0** by dosing acid or base. - **Core ionic equation:** $H^+ + OH^- \rightarrow H_2O$ (only valid for strong acid + strong base). - **Master parameters:** $pH = -\log[H^+]$, $pH + pOH = 14$, $K_w = 10^{-14}$ at **25 °C**. - **Equivalent weight = Molecular weight / n**, where n = number of replaceable $H^+$ or $OH^-$. - **Common acidic reagents:** $H_2SO_4$ (Eq wt 49), HCl (Eq wt 36.5), $HNO_3$ (Eq wt 63). - **Common basic reagents:** $NaOH$ (Eq wt 40), $Ca(OH)_2$ (Eq wt 37), $Na_2CO_3$ (Eq wt 53). - **Lime is cheapest; NaOH is most accurate; soda ash is for weak systems.** - **Equivalence point pH = 7** only for **strong acid + strong base**; weak systems deviate. - **Henderson–Hasselbalch:** $pH = pK_a + \log\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ — governs buffer-region behaviour. - **Design must include:** Equalization basin → mixing tank → pH sensor → PLC control loop → clarifier. - **CPCB discharge norm:** **pH 6.0 – 9.0** for inland surface water; **5.5 – 9.0** for marine outfalls. - **Purity correction always** — divide theoretical mass by purity fraction (e.g., 0.90 for 90% lime). - **Daily reagent mass** = (mass per litre) × (daily flow in litres). - **Reagent dose order of magnitude:** Strongly acidic effluent → 0.1 – 0.3 kg lime per m³. - **Common KTU mistake:** Using molarity instead of normality in stoichiometric calculations.

topics.title: Equalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Equalization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equalization** is a unit operation in **preliminary/primary wastewater treatment** that utilizes a specially designed holding basin (called an **equalization basin** or **equalization tank**) to dampen the fluctuations in **hydraulic flow rate (Q)** and/or **pollutant concentration (C)** of incoming wastewater, thereby providing a **uniform, homogeneous, and quasi-steady influent** to the downstream biological and tertiary treatment units. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, equalization is classified as a **hydraulic shock-load buffering process** that mitigates the adverse effects of: - Diurnal/seasonal flow variations - Toxic slug discharges from industries - Variable organic loading (BOD/COD spikes) - pH and temperature excursions It functions on the principle of **hydraulic detention** combined with **complete-mix fluid dynamics**, governed by the **Mass Conservation Law (Continuity Equation)** and the **Ideal Completely-Mixed Reactor (CMR) mass balance model**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tower"** > Think of a **municipal water supply overhead tank**. During the day, your locality's taps release water in irregular bursts (high flow in the morning for bathing/cooking, low flow at night). However, the pump station supplies water at a near-constant rate. The overhead tank acts as a **buffer** — it fills up when demand is low and supplies the deficit when demand is high. The pump (treatment plant) sees only a smooth, steady load. > > **An equalization basin in a wastewater treatment plant works exactly the same way** — the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) is the "pump," and the equalization basin is the "overhead tank" that absorbs the **peaks** and feeds the **troughs**, allowing the downstream biological reactors to operate under a **stable, optimized regime**. ### 1.3 Why Equalization is Indispensable in Advanced Wastewater Treatment | Engineering Problem Without Equalization | Consequence at Downstream Unit | |---|---| | Hydraulic surge (peak flow) | Washout of biomass in activated sludge process | | BOD/COD shock load | Loss of nitrification, poor sludge settling, bulking | | pH excursions | Inhibition of nitrifying bacteria, struvite formation | | Toxic slug discharge | Complete biological process failure | | Variable F/M ratio | Poor BOD removal efficiency, fluctuating effluent quality | ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics - **Volumetric Capacity (V):** typically in **m³** - **Detention Time (t):** usually **2 to 24 hours** (industrial), **4 to 8 hours** (municipal) - **Mixing Power Density (P/V):** **0.005 to 0.040 kW/m³** (**5 to 40 W/m³**) - **BOD Removal in Basin (E):** typically **10 to 35 %** when aerated ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Equalization Systems Equalization basins are broadly classified based on the parameter being equalized: **A. Flow Equalization Basin (FEB)** - Equalizes only the **hydraulic flow rate (Q)**. - Concentrations are allowed to fluctuate naturally. - Used where flow is the dominant problem (e.g., domestic WWTPs serving tourist towns). **B. Concentration Equalization Basin (CEB)** - Equalizes only the **pollutant concentration (C)**. - Flow is allowed to fluctuate, but the basin is designed to dampen concentration spikes. - Achieved by feeding the basin at variable rates such that the **mass loading rate (Q·C)** becomes constant. **C. Combined Flow & Concentration Equalization Basin (CFEB)** - The most common design. - Equalizes **both Q and C simultaneously** by providing **adequate retention time (t)** and **continuous mixing**. - Operates as a **Completely-Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR)**. ### 2.2 Configurations Based on Basin Geometry | Configuration | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **In-line (Series)** | Basin placed in the main flow line, all flow passes through | Small flow, strict uniformity needed | | **Off-line (Parallel/Bypass)** | Basin receives only the excess flow above the average; bypass for normal flow | Large flows, cost optimization | | **Variable Discharge Off-line** | Variable-speed pump modulates outflow from basin | Industrial complexes | | **In-line with Recirculation** | Basin effluent is recirculated to blend with incoming waste | High-concentration industrial waste | ### 2.3 Operational Requirements of an Equalization Basin For an equalization basin to function correctly, the following auxiliaries are mandatory: 1. **Mixing System** — either **mechanical mixers (propeller/turbine type)** or **diffused air aeration**. 2. **Aeration System** — required if BOD reduction is desired; maintains dissolved oxygen (DO ≥ 1.0 to 2.0 mg/L) to prevent **septicization, odor (H₂S), and septicity**. 3. **Variable-Speed Effluent Pumps** — to draw the basin down at the **average flow rate** (Q_avg). 4. **Scum and Sludge Removal Facilities** — because the basin traps floatables and settleables. 5. **Baffles / Flow Distribution Weirs** — to prevent short-circuiting. 6. **Instrumentation** — flow meters, pH probes, DO probes, level sensors. ### 2.4 Mass Balance Foundation For a **completely-mixed equalization basin** at steady state, the well-known **CMFR mass balance** is: $$V \frac{dC}{dt} = Q_{in} C_{in} - Q_{out} C_{out}$$ When the basin is **well-mixed**, **C_out = C** (the basin concentration equals the outflow concentration). Also, if the basin level is allowed to vary, **Q_out ≠ Q_in** instantaneously. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equations to Memorize for KTU 2024 University Examinations** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = Q_{avg} \cdot t$ | Basin volume from design detention time | m³ | | 2 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (Q_i \cdot t_i)}{\sum t_i}$ | Time-weighted average flow | m³/h | | 3 | $V = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ Q_{in}(t) - Q_{avg} \right] dt$ | Continuous form (positive part only) | m³ | | 4 | $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \left( Q_i - Q_{avg} \right) \cdot \Delta t_i \right]_{positive}$ | Discrete form (positive deviations only) | m³ | | 5 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (Q_i \cdot C_i \cdot \Delta t_i)}{\sum (Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i)}$ | Average mass loading | kg/d | | 6 | $V = \dfrac{M_{peak} - M_{avg}}{\Delta C_{allowable}} \cdot t$ | Concentration equalization volume | m³ | | 7 | $C_{out}(t) = C_0 e^{-t/\tau} + \dfrac{1}{\tau}\int_0^t C_{in}(s)\, e^{-(t-s)/\tau} ds$ | Convoluted CMFR response | mg/L | | 8 | $P = (0.005 \text{ to } 0.040) \cdot V$ | Aeration/mixing power requirement | kW | | 9 | $BOD_{removed} = E \cdot BOD_{in} \cdot Q \cdot t$ | BOD reduction in aerated basin | kg | | 10 | $SVI_{after} = f(SVI_{before}, \sigma_{Q}^{2}, \sigma_{C}^{2})$ | SVI improvement (qualitative) | mL/g | > **Where:** > - $\tau = V/Q_{avg}$ = hydraulic residence time (h) > - $\sigma_Q, \sigma_C$ = standard deviations of flow and concentration > - $E$ = BOD removal efficiency in basin (typically 10–35 %) > - $\Delta C_{allowable}$ = allowable concentration fluctuation downstream ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Equalization is a **pre-requisite** for: - **Sequencing Batch Reactors (SBR)** — to ensure uniform fill phase - **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** — to prevent membrane fouling from shock loads - **Reverse Osmosis (RO) pretreatment** — to protect membranes from osmotic shock - **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** — to maintain stable F/M ratio (0.2–0.5 kg BOD/kg MLSS·d) - **Industrial Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)** — common in **dairy, tannery, textile, pharmaceutical** sectors ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Volume for Flow Equalization (Cumulative Inflow vs. Outflow Method) **Starting Principle:** The basin must store water during periods of inflow exceeding the average outflow, and release it during periods of inflow below the average. Therefore, the **required volume equals the maximum cumulative deviation** of the inflow curve from the **straight-line average outflow curve**. **Step 1 — Define the average outflow rate:** $$Q_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total volume of wastewater during time } T}{\text{Total time } T} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}$$ **Step 2 — Define the cumulative volume of inflow at any time t_k:** $$V_{in}(t_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i$$ **Step 3 — Define the cumulative volume of average outflow (a straight line) at time t_k:** $$V_{out}(t_k) = Q_{avg} \cdot t_k$$ **Step 4 — The deviation at time t_k is:** $$\Delta V(t_k) = V_{in}(t_k) - V_{out}(t_k)$$ **Step 5 — The required basin volume is the maximum of the positive cumulative deviations:** $$V_{req} = \max_{k} \left[ \Delta V(t_k) \right] - \min_{k} \left[ \Delta V(t_k) \right]$$ **Step 6 — Geometric interpretation:** On a graph of *cumulative volume (V) vs. time (t)*, plot the inflow curve and the average outflow line. The required storage is the **maximum vertical distance** between the two curves on the **upper side**, OR equivalently, the **maximum vertical distance** on the **lower side** — both are equal in magnitude (mirror image). **Final Compact Form:** $$\boxed{V_{req} = \left[ \max_{k} \left( V_{in}(t_k) - Q_{avg} \cdot t_k \right) \right] - \left[ \min_{k} \left( V_{in}(t_k) - Q_{avg} \cdot t_k \right) \right]}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass-Balance Derivation of Basin Concentration Under Variable Influent Consider a **CMFR** with influent concentration $C_{in}(t)$ varying with time, basin volume $V$ held constant, and outflow $Q_{avg}$ held constant. The mass balance is: **Step 1 — Write the unsteady-state CMFR mass balance:** $$V \frac{dC}{dt} = Q_{in} \cdot C_{in}(t) - Q_{out} \cdot C(t)$$ **Step 2 — Assume basin is well-mixed, hence $C_{out} = C$ and $Q_{out} = Q_{avg}$ (constant outflow). Also $V$ is constant:** $$V \frac{dC}{dt} + Q_{avg} \cdot C(t) = Q_{in}(t) \cdot C_{in}(t)$$ **Step 3 — Divide through by V to obtain standard first-order linear ODE form:** $$\frac{dC}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} C(t) = \frac{Q_{in}(t) \cdot C_{in}(t)}{V}$$ where $\tau = V / Q_{avg}$ is the **hydraulic residence time**. **Step 4 — Multiply both sides by the integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:** $$e^{t/\tau} \frac{dC}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} e^{t/\tau} C = \frac{d}{dt}\left[ C \cdot e^{t/\tau} \right] = \frac{Q_{in}(t) \cdot C_{in}(t)}{V} \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from 0 to t:** $$C(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} - C(0) = \frac{1}{V} \int_0^t Q_{in}(s) \cdot C_{in}(s) \cdot e^{s/\tau} \, ds$$ **Step 6 — Solve for C(t):** $$C(t) = C(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{1}{V} \int_0^t Q_{in}(s) \cdot C_{in}(s) \cdot e^{-(t-s)/\tau} \, ds$$ **Step 7 — Express $1/V$ as $1/(Q_{avg} \cdot \tau)$:** $$\boxed{C(t) = C(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{1}{Q_{avg} \cdot \tau} \int_0^t Q_{in}(s) \cdot C_{in}(s) \cdot e^{-(t-s)/\tau} \, ds}$$ **Interpretation:** This is the **convolution integral** showing that the basin output concentration is a **time-smoothed, exponentially-weighted moving average** of the input loading. Larger $\tau$ → greater smoothing. --- ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Board Exam Style) > **[Worked Example]** > An industrial wastewater treatment plant receives flow over a 12-hour shift as given in the table below. The downstream biological unit requires a **steady flow of 2.0 m³/h**. Determine: > (i) The required volume of the equalization basin (using the cumulative deviation method). > (ii) The corresponding hydraulic detention time. > (iii) The mixing power required (assume P/V = 0.020 kW/m³). | Time Interval (h) | Duration $\Delta t$ (h) | Inflow $Q_{in}$ (m³/h) | |---|---|---| | 00:00 – 02:00 | 2 | 1.0 | | 02:00 – 04:00 | 2 | 1.5 | | 04:00 – 06:00 | 2 | 2.5 | | 06:00 – 08:00 | 2 | 4.0 | | 08:00 – 10:00 | 2 | 3.0 | | 10:00 – 12:00 | 2 | 1.5 | **Step 1 — Compute average inflow rate over the 12-h cycle:** $$Q_{avg} = \frac{\sum Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i} = \frac{(1.0+1.5+2.5+4.0+3.0+1.5) \times 2}{12}$$ $$Q_{avg} = \frac{13.5 \times 2}{12} = \frac{27.0}{12} = 2.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ *(Note: This is slightly different from the constant downstream demand of 2.0 m³/h. The actual constant outflow that the basin should provide to the downstream unit is the **process demand** $= 2.0$ m³/h, while $Q_{avg} = 2.25$ m³/h is the time-weighted average inflow. The basin must accommodate the mismatch.)* **Step 2 — Compute cumulative inflow and cumulative average outflow at the end of each interval:** | Time (h) | $V_{in}$ (m³) | $V_{out} = 2.0 \times t$ (m³) | Deviation $\Delta V$ (m³) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | | 2 | 2.0 | 4.0 | $-2.0$ | | 4 | 5.0 | 8.0 | $-3.0$ | | 6 | 10.0 | 12.0 | $-2.0$ | | 8 | 18.0 | 16.0 | $+2.0$ | | 10 | 24.0 | 20.0 | $+4.0$ | | 12 | 27.0 | 24.0 | $+3.0$ | **Step 3 — Identify maximum and minimum deviations:** $$\max(\Delta V) = +4.0 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at t = 10 h)}$$ $$\min(\Delta V) = -3.0 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at t = 4 h)}$$ **Step 4 — Compute required basin volume:** $$V_{req} = \max(\Delta V) - \min(\Delta V) = 4.0 - (-3.0) = 7.0 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 5 — Compute hydraulic detention time using the average inflow:** $$t = \frac{V_{req}}{Q_{avg}} = \frac{7.0}{2.25} = 3.11 \text{ hours} \approx 3.11 \text{ h}$$ **Step 6 — Compute mixing power:** $$P = 0.020 \times V_{req} = 0.020 \times 7.0 = 0.14 \text{ kW} = 140 \text{ W}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Equalization Strategies | Parameter | Flow EQ | Concentration EQ | Combined EQ | |---|---|---|---| | Volume required | Lower | Higher | Highest | | Buffering of BOD spikes | Poor | Good | Excellent | | Aeration required | Optional | Recommended | Strongly recommended | | Capital cost | Low | Medium | High | | Complexity | Simple | Moderate | High | | Typical detention time | 2–6 h | 6–12 h | 8–24 h | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Equalization Basin System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Influent Sewer
Variable Q and C] --> B[Bar Screen
Coarse Screening] B --> C[Grit Chamber
Solids Removal] C --> D[Flow Splitter Box
Diversion to EQ Basin] D --> E1[Equalization Basin
Holding and Mixing] D --> E2[Bypass Channel
During Maintenance] E1 --> F1[Submersible Mixer
Complete Mixing] E1 --> F2[Diffused Air System
Aeration and Odour Control] E1 --> F3[Level Sensor
L1 L2 L3 L4] E1 --> F4[Scum and Sludge Draw-off] F1 --> G[Effluent Pump
Variable Speed VFD] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Downstream Biological
Treatment Unit] E2 --> H F4 --> I[Sludge Handling
SBR / Thickener] ``` ### 4.2 Mass-Flow Path of an In-Line Equalization Basin ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Variable Q_in
t1 t2 t3 t4] --> B[Equalization Basin V] B -->|Mixing Aeration| C[Constant Q_out equals Q_avg] C --> D[Biological Reactor] subgraph BASIN B end H[BOD removal
10 to 35 percent] -.-> B S[Sludge withdrawal] -.-> B ``` ### 4.3 Cumulative-Deviation Method Schematic (S-P Curve Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Plot Inflow Hydrograph Q_in vs t] --> S2[Compute Q_avg] S2 --> S3[Plot Straight Line
Q_avg multiplied by t] S3 --> S4[Compute Cumulative
V_in minus V_out] S4 --> S5[Find max positive deviation] S4 --> S6[Find min negative deviation] S5 --> S7[V_req equals max minus min] S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8[Final Design Volume V] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Layout — In-Line vs Off-Line Equalization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INLINE[In-Line Configuration] A1[Inflow] --> B1[Equalization Basin] B1 --> C1[Outflow to Treatment] end subgraph OFFLINE[Off-Line Configuration] A2[Inflow] --> B2[Flow Splitter] B2 -->|Excess flow| C2[Equalization Basin] B2 -->|Average flow| D2[Bypass to Treatment] C2 -->|Variable speed pump| D2 end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Q1. Define equalization in wastewater treatment. List any three objectives of providing an equalization basin.** **Model Answer:** Equalization is the process of holding wastewater in a specially designed basin to dampen fluctuations in hydraulic flow rate and pollutant concentration, so that the downstream treatment units receive a uniform, quasi-steady feed. Three objectives: 1. To provide a constant hydraulic loading rate to downstream biological units, preventing washout and hydraulic shock. 2. To dampen variations in influent BOD/COD concentration, stabilizing the F/M ratio and improving process efficiency. 3. To control pH, temperature, and toxic substance fluctuations, protecting microbial populations from inhibitory shocks. > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q2. Differentiate between in-line and off-line equalization basins. State one advantage of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | In-Line EQ | Off-Line EQ | |---|---|---| | Flow path | All flow passes through basin | Only excess flow diverted | | Volume required | Larger | Smaller | | Capital cost | Higher | Lower | | Advantage | Provides both flow + concentration equalization simultaneously; better effluent uniformity | Cost-effective for high-rainfall / highly variable flows; basin remains idle during dry weather | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** > **Q. (A) Discuss the design of an equalization basin in detail. With a neat sketch, explain the hydraulic profile method for determining the volume of the basin. A wastewater treatment plant receives the following variable inflows over a 24-hour period. Determine the required volume of the equalization basin.** | Time (h) | 0–2 | 2–4 | 4–6 | 6–8 | 8–10 | 10–12 | 12–14 | 14–16 | 16–18 | 18–20 | 20–22 | 22–24 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Flow (m³/h) | 80 | 100 | 150 | 250 | 300 | 280 | 200 | 150 | 180 | 220 | 170 | 120 | **Model Solution:** **(a) Detailed Discussion of Equalization Basin Design (7 marks):** **Definition:** Equalization is a unit operation designed to dampen variations in flow and pollutant concentration of wastewater. **Design considerations:** 1. **Type** — In-line (all flow through) or Off-line (excess flow only). 2. **Volume calculation** — by graphical S-P curve method, mass balance method, or by rule of thumb using detention time (2–24 h). 3. **Mixing** — mechanical mixers (0.005–0.040 kW/m³) or diffused air (1.0–2.0 m³ air/m³ wastewater·h). 4. **Aeration** — required if BOD reduction is desired; DO must be ≥ 1.5 mg/L. 5. **Basin geometry** — square / circular / rectangular; depth 3–6 m; freeboard 0.5 m. 6. **Auxiliaries** — variable-speed pumps, level sensors, scum removal, sludge drawoff. 7. **Detention time** — 2 h (municipal peak), 4–8 h (industrial), up to 24 h (toxic discharges). 8. **BOD removal** — 10–35% achievable in aerated basin; reduces aerator load downstream. **Sketch (verbal description):** A rectangular basin with inlet at one end, baffle wall creating plug-flow-like retention, multiple submersible mixers distributed along the floor, a diffused air grid near the bottom, a scum collection trough at the top, a sludge hopper at the base, and a variable-speed effluent pump at the outlet. **(b) Volume Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Average flow:** $$Q_{avg} = \frac{\sum Q_i \cdot \Delta t_i}{\sum \Delta t_i} = \frac{(80+100+150+250+300+280+200+150+180+220+170+120) \times 2}{24}$$ $$\sum Q_i = 2200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$Q_{avg} = \frac{2200 \times 2}{24} = \frac{4400}{24} = 183.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 2 — Cumulative inflow and cumulative average outflow:** | Time (h) | $V_{in}$ (m³) | $V_{out} = 183.33 \times t$ (m³) | $\Delta V$ (m³) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 160 | 366.67 | $-206.67$ | | 4 | 360 | 733.33 | $-373.33$ | | 6 | 660 | 1100.00 | $-440.00$ | | 8 | 1160 | 1466.67 | $-306.67$ | | 10 | 1760 | 1833.33 | $-73.33$ | | 12 | 2320 | 2200.00 | $+120.00$ | | 14 | 2720 | 2566.67 | $+153.33$ | | 16 | 3020 | 2933.33 | $+86.67$ | | 18 | 3380 | 3300.00 | $+80.00$ | | 20 | 3820 | 3666.67 | $+153.33$ | | 22 | 4160 | 4033.33 | $+126.67$ | | 24 | 4400 | 4400.00 | 0 | **Step 3 — Maximum and minimum deviations:** $$\max(\Delta V) = +153.33 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at t = 14 h and t = 20 h)}$$ $$\min(\Delta V) = -440.00 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at t = 6 h)}$$ **Step 4 — Required volume:** $$\boxed{V_{req} = 153.33 - (-440.00) = 593.33 \text{ m}^3 \approx 594 \text{ m}^3}$$ **Step 5 — Detention time:** $$t = \frac{V_{req}}{Q_{avg}} = \frac{593.33}{183.33} = 3.24 \text{ h}$$ --- > **Q. (B) Alternative Choice (14 marks)** > **Explain the principles of mass balance and completely-mixed flow reactor (CMFR) theory as applied to equalization. Derive the convolution integral expression for outlet concentration from a constant-volume equalization basin. An equalization basin has $V = 500$ m³ and receives wastewater at $Q_{in} = 200$ m³/h with sudden slug concentration $C_{slug} = 800$ mg/L lasting for 30 minutes. Determine the peak outlet concentration (assume basin initially clean).** **Model Solution:** **(a) CMFR Mass Balance Theory (7 marks):** The mass balance on a completely-mixed equalization basin is governed by the **continuity equation** for a control volume: $$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation}$$ For a non-reactive species (conservative pollutant like chloride), generation = 0: $$V \frac{dC}{dt} = Q_{in} C_{in}(t) - Q_{out} C_{out}(t)$$ For a **well-mixed** basin: $C_{out} = C$ (basin concentration). For a **constant-volume** basin: $Q_{out} = Q_{avg}$ at all times. Therefore: $$V \frac{dC}{dt} + Q_{avg} C(t) = Q_{in} C_{in}(t)$$ This is a **first-order linear ODE** with the integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$ where $\tau = V/Q_{avg}$ is the residence time. The general solution (as derived in Section 3.2) is: $$C(t) = C(0) e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{1}{Q_{avg} \tau} \int_0^t Q_{in}(s) C_{in}(s) e^{-(t-s)/\tau} ds$$ This is the **convolution integral** of the input loading with the **exponential kernel $e^{-t/\tau}$**. Physically, it shows that the basin output is a **time-weighted, exponentially-decaying average** of the input — a low-pass filter in the time domain. **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **Given:** $V = 500$ m³, $Q_{in} = Q_{out} = Q_{avg} = 200$ m³/h, slug $C_{slug} = 800$ mg/L for $t_{slug} = 0.5$ h, $C(0) = 0$. **Residence time:** $$\tau = \frac{V}{Q_{avg}} = \frac{500}{200} = 2.5 \text{ h}$$ **For a constant input over the slug period (0 to 0.5 h):** The convolution integral evaluates to: $$C(t) = \frac{C_{slug}}{\tau} \int_0^{t} e^{-(t-s)/\tau} ds \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq 0.5 \text{ h}$$ **Step 1 — Evaluate the integral:** $$\int_0^t e^{-(t-s)/\tau} ds = \tau \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right]$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$C(t) = \frac{800}{2.5} \times 2.5 \left[ 1 - e^{-t/2.5} \right] = 800 \left[ 1 - e^{-t/2.5} \right]$$ **Step 3 — Peak concentration occurs at end of slug ($t = 0.5$ h):** $$C_{peak} = 800 \left[ 1 - e^{-0.5/2.5} \right] = 800 \left[ 1 - e^{-0.2} \right]$$ **Step 4 — Compute $e^{-0.2}$:** $$e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$ **Step 5 — Final peak:** $$C_{peak} = 800 \times (1 - 0.8187) = 800 \times 0.1813 = 145.04 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Interpretation:** The basin has attenuated the **800 mg/L slug** to a **peak of only ~145 mg/L** at the outlet — a **damping ratio of ~5.5×**. This is the protective effect of equalization. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the absolute-value sign** in the deviation formula — students often write only $\max(\Delta V)$ instead of $\max - \min$. This loses **3 to 4 marks**. > 2. **Confusing $Q_{avg}$ (inflow average) with $Q_{out}$ (constant downstream demand)** — both are different in the general case; the design must use the **process demand** as $Q_{out}$. > 3. **Mixing up $\tau = V/Q$ and $t = V/Q$** units — students must state the unit (hours) clearly. > 4. **Skipping the integration factor step** in the CMFR derivation — KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for showing this. > 5. **Not mentioning the "well-mixed" assumption** explicitly — this is a **mandatory 1-mark point** in 14-mark design questions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2: Equalization** - ✅ Equalization = **dampening Q and/or C variations** using a holding basin. - ✅ **Three types:** Flow EQ, Concentration EQ, Combined EQ. - ✅ **Two configurations:** In-line (series) vs Off-line (parallel/bypass). - ✅ **Volume calculation** uses the **S-P curve / cumulative deviation method**: $V_{req} = \max(\Delta V) - \min(\Delta V)$. - ✅ **CMFR mass balance** yields the **convolution integral**: $C(t) = C(0)e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{1}{Q_{avg}\tau}\int_0^t Q_{in}C_{in}e^{-(t-s)/\tau}ds$. - ✅ **Residence time $\tau = V/Q_{avg}$** governs damping; larger $\tau$ → better smoothing. - ✅ **Detention time design range:** **2–24 hours** (industrial) and **4–8 hours** (municipal). - ✅ **Mixing power density (P/V):** **0.005 to 0.040 kW/m³** (= 5 to 40 W/m³). - ✅ **Aeration requirement:** diffused air **1.0–2.0 m³ air/m³ wastewater·h**; DO ≥ **1.5 mg/L**. - ✅ **BOD removal in basin:** typically **10–35 %** if aerated — this reduces downstream aeration cost. - ✅ **Auxiliaries:** baffles, mixers, level sensors (L1–L4), scum trough, sludge hopper, variable-speed pumps. - ✅ **Real-world applications:** ASP, SBR, MBR, RO pretreatment, dairy/tannery/textile/pharma ETPs. - ✅ **Key benefit:** stabilizes **F/M ratio**, prevents **biomass washout**, and improves **effluent quality consistency**.

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sedimentation** is a physical unit operation in which suspended solids heavier than water (or lighter, in the case of floatation) are removed from a wastewater stream by gravitational settling under quiescent or low-velocity flow conditions. It is the **second most widely used treatment process** (after screening) and serves as the primary solid–liquid separation mechanism in both primary and secondary wastewater treatment plants.

In strict KTU terminology, sedimentation is classified as a **gravity separation process** governed by the balance between:

- **Gravitational force** ($F_g$) acting downward on the particle.
- **Buoyant force** ($F_b$) acting upward (equal to weight of displaced fluid).
- **Drag force** ($F_d$) resisting motion through the fluid.

The terminal, constant downward velocity attained when these forces balance is termed the **settling velocity** ($V_s$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Teacup Test" Analogy ☕**
>
> Imagine pouring a spoonful of stirred muddy water into a transparent teacup and letting it sit still on a table. Within minutes, the heavier silt grains drop to the bottom, the lighter clay particles take longer, and the clear water remains on top. That is sedimentation in its purest form!
>
> In a real wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), the **teacup** becomes a massive **settling tank (clarifier)** that holds millions of liters of mixed wastewater just long enough for the suspended solids to "fall out" of the water column under gravity, leaving clarified effluent on top and a thick sludge blanket on the bottom.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard physical constants and design metrics used in sedimentation engineering:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|-----------|:------:|:---------------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (at 20 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **998.2 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (at 20 °C) | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ N·s/m²** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (at 20 °C) | $\nu$ | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
| Typical overflow rate | $V_o$ | **24 – 48 m³/(m²·d)** (primary) |
| Typical detention time | $t_d$ | **1.5 – 2.5 hours** (primary) |
| Typical weir loading rate | — | **125 – 500 m³/(m·d)** |
| Particle density (organic) | $\rho_s$ | **1.01 – 1.05 g/cm³** |
| Particle density (inorganic) | $\rho_s$ | **2.50 – 2.65 g/cm³** |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Sedimentation (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 explicitly classifies sedimentation into four types.** Examiners frequently ask this classification directly for 3-mark questions:

1. **Type I — Discrete (Free) Settling**: Individual, non-flocculating particles settle independently. Governed by **Stokes' Law**.
2. **Type II — Flocculant Settling**: Particles coalesce during settling; mass and effective size increase with depth/time.
3. **Type III — Hindered (Zone) Settling**: High solids concentration; particles settle as a blanket with a clear interface above.
4. **Type IV — Compression Settling**: At very high concentrations, the settled sludge compresses under its own weight (occurs in sludge thickeners).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

Sedimentation tanks (clarifiers) achieve separation through a meticulously designed hydraulic regime:

- **Step 1 — Inlet Zone (Energy Dissipation):** Influent enters through a stilling well/baffle that destroys kinetic energy and distributes flow uniformly across the tank cross-section.
- **Step 2 — Settling Zone:** The flow moves horizontally at a velocity $\leq 0.3$ m/min, allowing particles to fall vertically under gravity at their terminal velocity $V_s$.
- **Step 3 — Sludge Zone:** Settled particles accumulate as a sludge blanket on the sloped floor. Mechanical scrapers slowly move sludge to a central hopper.
- **Step 4 —Effluent Zone (Outlet):** Clarified supernatant overflows over **V-notch weirs** into launders and exits the tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's "Why":** The tank must provide enough **retention time (detention time)** for the smallest target particle (typically **100 µm** for primary clarifiers) to reach the sludge zone before water exits. This links **$V_s$** with **tank depth** and **overflow rate**.

---

## 2.2 The Three Settling Regimes (Reynolds-Number Based)

The drag coefficient ($C_D$) on a settling particle depends on the **particle Reynolds number**:

$$Re_p = \frac{V_s \cdot d \cdot \rho_w}{\mu}$$

| Regime | $Re_p$ Range | Governing Equation | Drag Coefficient |
|--------|:-----------:|:-------------------|:----------------:|
| **Stokes (Laminar)** | $Re_p < 1$ | $V_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$ |
| **Transition** | $1 \le Re_p \le 10^4$ | Empirical (curve-fit) | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ |
| **Newton (Turbulent)** | $Re_p > 10^4$ | $V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.33 \cdot g(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d}{\rho_w}}$ | $C_D \approx 0.4$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) derivations and numericals. Memorize all formulas.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Units | Application |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:--------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Terminal settling velocity** | $V_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{3 C_D \rho_w}}$ | m/s | All regimes (general) |
| 2 | **Stokes' Law** (Type I) | $V_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^{2}}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | $Re_p < 1$, small particles |
| 3 | **Newton's Law** (Type I) | $V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{\dfrac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{\rho_w}}$ | m/s | $Re_p > 10^3$, large particles |
| 4 | **Overflow rate (Surface loading)** | $V_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | m/d or m/h | Ideal tank design |
| 5 | **Detention time** | $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h or s | Hydraulic residence time |
| 6 | **Critical (cut-off) settling velocity** | $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} = V_o$ | m/s | 100% removal threshold |
| 7 | **Removal efficiency (fractional)** | $R = 1 - X_o + \dfrac{X_o}{V_o} \int_0^{V_o} V_s \, dV_s$ | — | Fractional settling |
| 8 | **Weir loading rate** | $WLR = \dfrac{Q}{L_{weir}}$ | m³/(m·d) | Outlet design |
| 9 | **Hazen's efficiency criterion** | $V_s \ge V_o$ | — | 100% removal condition |
| 10 | **Sludge volume rate** | $Q_{sludge} = \dfrac{Q \cdot X \cdot R}{\rho_{sludge}}$ | m³/d | Sludge pumping |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $V_s$ = settling velocity (m/s)
> - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
> - $\rho_s$ = particle density (kg/m³)
> - $\rho_w$ = water density (kg/m³)
> - $d$ = particle diameter (m)
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²)
> - $C_D$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
> - $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s)
> - $A_s$ = surface area of settling zone (m²)
> - $V$ = tank volume (m³)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- 🏭 **Municipal WWTPs** — Primary clarifiers remove **50–70%** of total suspended solids (TSS) and **25–40%** of BOD before biological treatment.
- ⚗️ **Drinking-water treatment** — Pre-chlorination sedimentation removes turbidity.
- 🧪 **Industrial Effluent Treatment** — Coal washeries, tanneries, and pulp/paper industries use sedimentation for grit, fiber, and lime recovery.
- 🌊 **Stormwater Management** — Sedimentation basins trap sediments in urban runoff before discharge to receiving waters.
- 🧬 **Tertiary Treatment** — Polishing clarifiers remove residual solids after secondary biological reactors.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Terminal Settling Velocity (General Form)

A particle falling under gravity experiences three forces. At **terminal velocity** the net force equals zero.

**Force 1 — Gravitational force (downward):**

$$F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^{3} \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$$

**Force 2 — Buoyant force (upward):**

$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^{3} \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$$

**Force 3 — Drag force (opposing motion, upward when settling downward):**

$$F_d = C_D \cdot A_p \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot V_s^{2}}{2}$$

where the projected area of a spherical particle is $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$.

**Equilibrium condition at terminal velocity:**

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

Substituting all three expressions:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} \rho_s g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^{3} \rho_w g + C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^{2}}{2}$$

Dividing through by $\dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$:

$$\rho_s g = \rho_w g + \frac{3 C_D \rho_w V_s^{2}}{4 d}$$

Rearranging:

$$(\rho_s - \rho_w) g = \frac{3 C_D \rho_w V_s^{2}}{4 d}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{3 C_D \rho_w}}}$$

This is the **General Settling Velocity Equation** valid for all Reynolds-number regimes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stokes' Law (Laminar Regime)

For very small particles settling at low velocities, flow around the particle is laminar, and the drag coefficient takes the form derived analytically by George Gabriel Stokes:

$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p} = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_w V_s d}$$

Substituting $C_D$ into the general equation:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{3 \rho_w} \cdot \frac{1}{C_D}}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d}{3 \rho_w} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s d}{24 \mu}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$V_s^{2} = \frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d \cdot \rho_w V_s d}{3 \rho_w \cdot 24 \mu}$$

Cancel $\rho_w$:

$$V_s^{2} = \frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^{2} V_s}{72 \mu}$$

Solving for $V_s$:

$$V_s = \frac{4 g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^{2}}{72 \mu}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = \frac{g (\rho_s - \rho_w) d^{2}}{18 \mu}} \quad \text{(Stokes' Law)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overflow Rate (Hazen Theory for Ideal Basin)

Consider an ideal settling tank:

- Surface area $= A_s$ (m²)
- Volumetric flow rate $= Q$ (m³/s)
- Horizontal flow velocity $= V_h = Q / A_x$ where $A_x$ is the cross-sectional area.
- Vertical settling velocity $= V_s$

A particle entering at the water surface at the inlet must travel **horizontal distance $L$** in time **$t = L / V_h$** during which it falls a depth $H = V_s \cdot t$.

For **100% removal**, the particle must reach the bottom before reaching the outlet:

$$H = V_s \cdot \frac{L}{V_h}$$

Using $V_h = Q / A_x$ and $L \cdot A_x = V$ (tank volume):

$$H = V_s \cdot \frac{L \cdot A_x}{Q} = V_s \cdot \frac{V}{Q}$$

But more importantly, if the particle enters at the surface with settling velocity $V_s$, the condition $V_s \ge Q / A_s$ is required. Let us derive this:

The time taken by the particle to fall from surface to bottom is:

$$t_{settle} = \frac{H}{V_s}$$

The hydraulic retention time in the tank is:

$$t_{detention} = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A_s \cdot H}{Q}$$

For the particle to be removed:

$$t_{settle} \le t_{detention}$$

$$\frac{H}{V_s} \le \frac{A_s \cdot H}{Q}$$

$$V_s \ge \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

Defining the **surface overflow rate** $V_o = Q / A_s$:

$$\boxed{V_s \ge V_o = \frac{Q}{A_s}}$$

**Hazen's Principle (1904):** *The only design parameter that governs removal in an ideal basin is the surface overflow rate, independent of depth and detention time.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fractional Removal Efficiency (Discrete Settling)

For particles with a distribution of settling velocities, the fraction removed equals:

$$R = 1 - X_o + \frac{1}{V_o} \int_{0}^{V_o} X_s(V_s) \, dV_s$$

where:

- $X_o$ = mass fraction of particles with $V_s < V_o$ (NOT removed)
- $X_s(V_s) \, dV_s$ = incremental mass fraction with settling velocity between $V_s$ and $V_s + dV_s$
- $\int_{0}^{V_o}$ integrates over all particle classes with $V_s \le V_o$ (these ARE removed).

**Derivation logic:**
- Particles with $V_s \ge V_o$ are **100% removed** (mass fraction $= 1 - X_o$).
- Particles with $V_s < V_o$ are **partially removed** proportional to $V_s / V_o$.
- Weighting by their mass distribution $X_s$ and integrating gives total fractional removal.

The overall removal in percentage form:

$$\boxed{R(\%) = \left[1 - X_o + \frac{1}{V_o}\int_{0}^{V_o} X_s \, dV_s\right] \times 100}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sedimentation Design Equations Summary

| Design Parameter | Governing Equation | Critical Constraint |
|:-----------------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| Surface Area | $A_s = \dfrac{Q}{V_o}$ | $A_s \ge \dfrac{Q}{V_{s, \min}}$ |
| Tank Volume | $V = Q \cdot t_d$ | $t_d \ge 1.5$ h (primary) |
| Tank Depth | $H = V / A_s$ | $3.0 \le H \le 5.0$ m |
| Width (rectangular) | $W = A_s / L$ | $L/W = 4:1$ to $5:1$ |
| Weir Length | $L_{weir} = Q / WLR$ | $WLR \le 500$ m³/(m·d) |
| Sludge Hopper Volume | $V_{sludge} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_o \cdot R \cdot t_{storage}}{\rho_{sludge} \cdot X_{sludge}}$ | $t_{storage} \ge 24$ h |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Sedimentation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater Q m³/s] --> B[Inlet Distribution Channel]
    B --> C[Stilling Well / Baffle Zone]
    C --> D[Settling Zone - Quiescent Region]
    D --> E{Particle Settling Path}
    E -- Type I Discrete --> F1[Stokes Law Region]
    E -- Type II Flocculant --> F2[Floc Growth Region]
    E -- Type III Hindered --> F3[Sludge Blanket Interface]
    E -- Type IV Compression --> F4[Compression Zone]
    F1 --> G[Sludge Hopper - Bottom]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Sludge Scraper Mechanism]
    H --> I[Sludge Pump to Digester]
    D --> J[Clarified Effluent]
    J --> K[V-Notch Weir Launder]
    K --> L[Effluent Outflow to Next Stage]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Settling Tanks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRCULAR_CLA
        A1[Center Feed Well] --> A2[Radial Flow Settling Zone]
        A2 --> A3[Sludge Hopper - Center Bottom]
        A2 --> A4[Effluent Peripheral Weir]
    end
    subgraph RECT_CLA
        B1[End Inlet Baffle] --> B2[Longitudinal Flow Zone]
        B2 --> B3[Sludge Hopper - End]
        B2 --> B4[Effluent V-notch Weirs]
    end
    CIRCULAR_CLA --- TITLE1[Type A: Circular Clarifier]
    RECT_CLA --- TITLE2[Type B: Rectangular Clarifier]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Vertical Particle Path in Ideal Basin (Hazen Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Particle at Inlet - Surface] -->|Falls at Vs| P2[Particle at Mid-depth]
    P2 -->|Continues settling| P3[Particle Reaches Sludge Zone - Removed]
    P4[Faster Particle at Inlet] -->|Falls quickly| P5[Reaches Bottom - Removed Earlier]
    P6[Slower Particle] -->|V_s less than V_o| P7[Escapes with Effluent - Lost]
    P1 -. Critical Particle .-> P8[V_s equals V_o - Just Removed]
```

---

## 4.4 Force-Diagram (Schematic Description)

For students visualizing forces on a settling particle:

| Force | Direction | Magnitude Formula |
|:------|:---------:|:------------------|
| $F_g$ | ↓ Downward | $\dfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g$ |
| $F_b$ | ↑ Upward | $\dfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g$ |
| $F_d$ | ↑ Upward (resists fall) | $C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$ |
| **Net** | **= 0** at terminal $V_s$ | Equilibrium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Decreasing water temperature increases $\mu$ (viscosity), which **decreases** $V_s$ for fine particles. This is why sedimentation efficiency drops in winter — a frequent viva question!

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Type I (Discrete) settling and Type II (Flocculant) settling in wastewater treatment. State one example where each type predominates.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Type I — Discrete Settling | Type II — Flocculant Settling |
|:--------|:---------------------------|:------------------------------|
| Particle behaviour | Settle independently | Coalesce during descent |
| Size change | Constant | Increases with depth |
| Governing law | **Stokes' Law** | Empirical / lab column tests |
| Concentration | Low to moderate | Moderate to high |
| Predominates in | Grit chambers, primary clarifiers for sand/silt | Secondary clarifiers after biological treatment |

**Example for Type I:** Removal of sand particles in a grit chamber.
**Example for Type II:** Settling of activated-sludge flocs in a secondary clarifier.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define "Surface Overflow Rate" and "Weir Loading Rate". Write their standard units and typical design values for a primary clarifier.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Surface Overflow Rate (SOR / $V_o$):** The volumetric flow rate applied per unit surface area of the settling zone. It is the **single most critical design parameter** governing discrete-particle removal (Hazen theory).

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} \quad \text{[units: m}^3\text{/(m}^2\text{·d) or m/h]}$$

- **Typical value for primary clarifier:** **30 – 48 m³/(m²·d)**.

**Weir Loading Rate (WLR):** The volumetric flow rate per unit length of the effluent weir. It controls the rate at which clarified water leaves the tank, minimizing upward currents that could re-suspend settled solids.

$$WLR = \frac{Q}{L_{weir}} \quad \text{[units: m}^3\text{/(m·d)]}$$

- **Typical value for primary clarifier:** **≤ 500 m³/(m·d)** (preferably ≤ 300 for municipal plants).

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Students must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **terminal settling velocity of a discrete particle** falling through a fluid under Stokes' law conditions. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A primary sedimentation tank handles a flow of **25,000 m³/d** for a municipal wastewater with a mixture of discrete particles having density **1.25 g/cm³**. Laboratory column analysis yields the following settling-velocity distribution:

| $V_s$ (mm/s) | Mass Fraction $X_i$ |
|:------------:|:-------------------:|
| 0.10 | 0.20 |
| 0.30 | 0.30 |
| 0.50 | 0.25 |
| 0.70 | 0.15 |
| 0.90 | 0.10 |

Determine the **overall fractional removal efficiency** if the tank is designed with a surface overflow rate of **20 m³/(m²·d)** (= **0.231 mm/s**). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**

1. Particle is spherical and rigid.
2. Particle settles alone (no flocculation) — Type I.
3. Reynolds number $Re_p < 1$ — laminar flow around particle.
4. Fluid is infinite, quiescent, and Newtonian.
5. Particle reaches **terminal velocity** (forces balanced).

**Derivation:**

The three forces acting on the particle are:

$$F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g$$

$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g$$

$$F_d = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$$

At terminal velocity, $F_g = F_b + F_d$:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_s g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_w g + C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$$

For Stokes regime, $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24 \mu}{\rho_w V_s d}$.

Substituting and simplifying (as shown in Section 3.2):

$$\boxed{V_s = \frac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert overflow rate to mm/s:**

$$V_o = 20 \, \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}) = \frac{20}{86400} \, \text{m/s} = 2.31 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s} = 0.231 \, \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hazen principle:** Particles with $V_s \ge V_o$ are 100 % removed.

**Step 3 — Tabulate particle classification:**

| $V_s$ (mm/s) | $X_i$ | $V_s / V_o$ | Removal fraction | Contribution $X_i \cdot (V_s/V_o)$ |
|:------------:|:-----:|:-----------:|:----------------:|:------------------------------------:|
| 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.433 | 0.433 | 0.0866 |
| 0.30 | 0.30 | 1.299 | 1.000 | 0.3000 |
| 0.50 | 0.25 | 2.165 | 1.000 | 0.2500 |
| 0.70 | 0.15 | 3.030 | 1.000 | 0.1500 |
| 0.90 | 0.10 | 3.896 | 1.000 | 0.1000 |

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\text{Overall Removal} = \sum X_i \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{V_s}{V_o}\right)$$

$$R = 0.0866 + 0.3000 + 0.2500 + 0.1500 + 0.1000 = 0.8866$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 88.7\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a rectangular sedimentation tank**. Discuss the function of each zone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular sedimentation tank has a diameter of **20 m** and a side-water depth of **3.5 m**. It treats a wastewater flow of **10,000 m³/d**. Determine:
   (i) Surface overflow rate
   (ii) Detention time
   (iii) Weir loading rate, assuming effluent is collected over the full periphery.
   **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Working Principle:** A rectangular sedimentation tank is a long, narrow basin through which wastewater flows **horizontally** at a very low velocity (typically $\le 0.3$ m/min). Suspended solids settle by gravity onto the sloped bottom while clarified water exits over V-notch weirs at the outlet end.

**Zones (functional description):**

1. **Inlet Zone:** Influent enters via an inlet channel with a baffle/stilling well. The baffle distributes flow evenly across the tank width and dissipates inlet kinetic energy.

2. **Settling Zone:** The largest zone; here quiescent horizontal flow allows particles to settle vertically at their terminal velocity $V_s$. Depth is 3 – 5 m and length-to-width ratio is **4:1 to 5:1** for plug-flow behaviour.

3. **Sludge Zone:** Sloped floor (1:100 typical) where settled solids accumulate. A travelling-bridge or chain-and-flight **scraper** slowly moves sludge to a hopper at the inlet end.

4. **Effluent Collection Zone:** Clarified water passes over **V-notch weirs** (which give uniform draw-off at all elevations) into a collecting launder/trough for onward flow.

**Schematic Block (functional architecture):**

```
INFLOW → [INLET BAFFLE] → [SETTLING ZONE] → [V-NOTCH WEIRS] → EFFLUENT
                                  ↓
                            [SLUDGE ZONE]
                                  ↓
                          [SCRAPER → HOPPER → SLUDGE PUMP]
```

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 20$ m $\Rightarrow$ Area $A_s = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$
- Side-water depth $H = 3.5$ m
- Flow rate $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d
- Weir length $L_{weir} = \pi D$ (peripheral)

**(i) Surface overflow rate:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi (20)^{2}}{4} = 314.16 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{10{,}000}{314.16} = 31.83 \, \text{m}^{3}/(\text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{d})$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx 31.83 \, \text{m/d}}$$

**(ii) Detention time:**

$$V = A_s \cdot H = 314.16 \times 3.5 = 1099.56 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

$$t_d = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1099.56}{10{,}000} = 0.1100 \, \text{d}$$

$$\boxed{t_d \approx 2.64 \, \text{hours}}$$

**(iii) Weir loading rate:**

$$L_{weir} = \pi D = \pi \times 20 = 62.83 \, \text{m}$$

$$WLR = \frac{Q}{L_{weir}} = \frac{10{,}000}{62.83} = 159.15 \, \text{m}^{3}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{d})$$

$$\boxed{WLR \approx 159.2 \, \text{m}^{3}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{d})}$$

**Check:** All three values fall within standard design ranges ($V_o$: 24 – 48 m/d, $t_d$: 1.5 – 2.5 h, $WLR$: $\le 500$ m³/(m·d)). ✅ Design is acceptable.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 3–5 marks in 14-mark questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $V_o$** — Students mix m/d and mm/s without converting. Always show the conversion: $1$ m/d $= 1/86400$ m/s $\approx 0.01157$ mm/s.
> 2. **Forgetting drag-force direction** — Many students write $F_d$ downward. Remember, drag ALWAYS opposes relative motion, so it points **upward** when the particle settles downward.
> 3. **Using Stokes' Law for large particles** — Stokes applies only for $Re_p < 1$. For larger particles, use the general equation with appropriate $C_D$.
> 4. **Confusing $V_o$ with $V_s$** — $V_o$ is the tank's overflow rate (hydraulic), while $V_s$ is a particle property. The design criterion is $V_s \ge V_o$.
> 5. **Ignoring Hazen efficiency criterion** — In design problems, you must state and verify that the smallest target particle's $V_s \ge Q/A_s$ for 100% removal.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ Sedimentation = **gravity-driven** solid–liquid separation; primary treatment step.
- ⭐ Four types: **Discrete, Flocculant, Hindered (Zone), Compression**.
- ⭐ **Stokes' Law:** $V_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ — applies for $Re_p < 1$.
- ⭐ **Newton's Law:** $V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{\dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d}{\rho_w}}$ — applies for $Re_p > 10^3$.
- ⭐ **Hazen's Principle:** Design is governed by **surface overflow rate** $V_o = Q/A_s$, not tank depth.
- ⭐ For **100% removal**, $V_s \ge V_o$ must be satisfied.
- ⭐ **Overflow rate** units: m³/(m²·d) or m/h. **Weir loading rate** units: m³/(m·d).
- ⭐ Typical design values (primary clarifier): $V_o = 30$–$48$ m/d, $t_d = 1.5$–$2.5$ h, $WLR \le 500$ m³/(m·d).
- ⭐ **Fractional removal** for polydisperse suspensions uses the integral $R = 1 - X_o + \dfrac{1}{V_o}\int_0^{V_o} X_s \, dV_s$.
- ⭐ **Winter effect:** Cold water → higher $\mu$ → lower $V_s$ → poorer sedimentation.
- ⭐ **Force balance at terminal velocity:** $F_g = F_b + F_d$.
- ⭐ **V-notch weirs** ensure uniform effluent draw-off and prevent re-suspension of settled sludge.
- ⭐ Tank shapes: **Rectangular** (length:width = 4:1 to 5:1) and **Circular** (centre-feed or peripheral-feed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal) # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flotation** is a **gravity separation** process used in advanced wastewater treatment wherein **suspended matter** (such as oil droplets, grease, fine solids, fibers, and biological flocs) is **lifted to the surface** of a liquid body by artificially generated **fine gas bubbles** (typically **air bubbles of 10–100 µm diameter**) that attach themselves to (or become trapped within) the suspended particles, after which the floating layer (the **float**) is mechanically skimmed from the surface. In the specific context of **oil and grease (O&G) removal**, flotation exploits the **density differential** between the oily phase ($\rho_{oil} \approx 850\text{–}900~\text{kg/m}^3$) and the aqueous phase ($\rho_{water} \approx 1000~\text{kg/m}^3$), accelerating the otherwise slow natural coalescence of emulsified oil droplets that would take hours or days in a quiescent settling tank down to a residence time of **20–60 minutes** in a flotation unit. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2** > The course outcome mapping places Flotation under **CO2**: *"Design and analyse unit operations for removal of colloidal, suspended, and floating pollutants from wastewater."* This topic directly contributes to **Bloom Level 4 (Analyse)** and **Level 5 (Evaluate)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Champagne Cork Principle" Imagine pouring a glass of cold soda and dropping in a few **raisins**. Within seconds, the raisins — which are *denser* than water and would normally sink — become coated with bubbles and **pop to the surface**, where they float until the bubbles pop, then they sink again. This is exactly how flotation works: - The **soda** is your wastewater (containing suspended oil/grease/solids). - The **bubbles** are your injected fine air bubbles. - The **raisins** are the oil droplets or suspended solids. - The **surface** is where the skimmed "float" accumulates. The engineering goal is to **maximize the bubble–particle collisions** and the **adhesion strength** so that particles rise rapidly and remain at the surface long enough to be scraped off. ## 1.3 Why Oil & Grease Cannot Be Removed Efficiently by Plain Sedimentation | Pollutant Property | Typical Value | Implication for Settling | |---|---|---| | Oil droplet diameter | 5 – 50 µm (emulsified) | Settling velocity follows **Stokes' Law** → extremely slow | | Specific gravity of oil | 0.85 – 0.95 | Low density → slow rise by buoyancy | | Emulsion stability | Stabilized by surfactants | Surfactants **prevent coalescence** | | Required removal efficiency | 70 – 95% for O&G | Settling alone rarely achieves <50 mg/L | Flotation's effectiveness comes from **attaching a much larger, lower-density object (the air bubble)** to each oil droplet, effectively reducing the **apparent specific gravity** of the combined bubble-particle agglomerate to near **0.1 – 0.3**, causing rapid buoyant rise. ## 1.4 Standard Performance Benchmarks for O&G Removal > [!IMPORTANT] > **Target Effluent Quality After Flotation (Typical Industrial Discharge):** > - Oil & Grease: **< 10 mg/L** (sometimes < 5 mg/L) > - Total Suspended Solids (TSS): **< 30 mg/L** > - Free oil removal efficiency: **95 – 99%** > - Emulsified oil removal efficiency: **70 – 90%** (with chemical conditioning) # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle Flotation separates particles from a liquid phase based on differences in **buoyancy** rather than density alone. The fundamental condition for a particle to float is: $$F_{\text{net upward}} = F_{\text{buoyancy}} - F_{\text{gravity}} > 0$$ In classical flotation theory, when a spherical air bubble of radius $r_b$ attaches to a spherical particle of radius $r_p$, the **apparent density** of the agglomerate is reduced, and the **rise velocity** $v_r$ (in the laminar regime) is given by: $$v_r = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot d_a^2}{18 \mu}$$ where: - $\rho_p$ = density of particle - $\rho_a$ = density of particle–bubble agglomerate - $d_a$ = effective diameter of agglomerate - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) > [!NOTE] > By attaching a single bubble of just **50 µm** to a **10 µm oil droplet**, the effective diameter of the agglomerate can grow by **5–10×**, and the rise velocity can increase by **25–100×** compared to natural Stokes rise. ## 2.2 The Three Sub-Processes Inside a Flotation Tank Flotation is not a single physical event but a **sequence of three coupled sub-processes** that occur in series: ### Sub-Process 1 — Particle–Bubble Collision The wastewater particle and the rising/forming gas bubble must physically **collide**. This is governed by: - **Fluid flow regime** (laminar vs turbulent) - **Bubbles per unit volume** (a high concentration of fine bubbles maximizes collision probability) - **Particle size** (very small particles, $d_p < 5~\mu m$, have low collision efficiency due to hydrodynamic streaming around bubbles) ### Sub-Process 2 — Particle–Bubble Attachment Once in contact, the particle must **attach** to the bubble. This is governed by: - **Surface tension** of the liquid (lower is better for attachment) - **Contact angle** $\theta$ between particle and bubble (hydrophobic particles attach easily; hydrophilic particles may require chemical conditioning) - **Induction time** — the time required for the thin liquid film between particle and bubble to thin and rupture ### Sub-Process 3 — Agglomerate Buoyant Rise The particle–bubble agglomerate must rise faster than the **downflow velocity** in the flotation tank to reach the surface. This is governed by: - The **aggregate size and density** (larger, lighter aggregates rise faster) - **Hydraulic retention time (HRT)** of the tank - **Surface overflow rate (SOR)** in m³/m²·h ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical problems on flotation. Memorize the variables and the **governing equations**. | # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Typical Range / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stokes' Rise Velocity** (for free-floating oil droplet) | $v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_w - \rho_o) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | $v_s \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ m/s for 5–50 µm droplets | | 2 | **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)** | $\dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{s \cdot a_f \cdot Q_a}{Q \cdot X}$ | 0.005 – 0.06 (dimensionless) | | 3 | **Air Mass Saturation (Henry's Law)** | $C_{sat} = \dfrac{P}{H} \cdot x_a$ | At $P_{sat} = 4$ atm, $C_{sat} \approx 80$ g/m³ | | 4 | **Mass of Air Released** | $m_a = s \cdot C_{sat} \cdot Q_r \cdot \dfrac{P - 1}{P}$ | kg air / h | | 5 | **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** | $SOR = \dfrac{Q}{A_{tank}}$ | 5 – 25 m³/m²·h | | 6 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | 20 – 60 minutes | | 7 | **Recycle Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$ | 5% – 50% (typically 20–30%) | | 8 | **Rise Velocity Requirement** | $v_{rise} \geq \dfrac{H_{tank}}{HRT}$ | Generally ≥ 5 m/h | | 9 | **Solids Loading Rate** | $SLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot C}{A_{tank}}$ | 2 – 10 kg/m²·h | | 10 | **Bubbly Flow Reynolds Number** | $Re_b = \dfrac{\rho_b u_b d_b}{\mu}$ | Often < 1 (laminar bubble rise) | ### Explanation of Key Variables - $s$ = fraction of dissolved air released at the pressure-reduction point (usually 0.5 – 1.0) - $a_f$ = mass fraction of air in pressurized recycle flow (dimensionless, = saturation mass ratio) - $Q_a$ = pressurized recycle flow rate (m³/s) - $Q$ = influent wastewater flow (m³/s) - $X$ = influent suspended solids concentration (mg/L or g/m³) - $P$ = saturator pressure (absolute, in atm) - $C_{sat}$ = saturated dissolved-air concentration at $P$ - $Q_r$ = recycle flow rate - $H$ = Henry's law constant for air in water ## 2.4 Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > Flotation for oil & grease removal is **industry-critical** in the following real-world installations: > - **Petroleum refineries** (API separator polishing, desalter effluent treatment) > - **Petrochemical complexes** and **oil tank farm** runoff treatment > - **Food processing industries** (dairy, edible oil, meat processing) > - **Steel mills** (rolling mill emulsion, lubricoolant recovery) > - **Vehicle service stations** and **transportation depots** > - **Marine oil spill response** (compact skid-mounted units) > > In Indian context, the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** discharge norms under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** mandate **O&G < 10 mg/L** for inland surface water discharges — flotation is the **standard engineering solution**. ## 2.5 Chemical Conditioning — The "Bridge" for Stable Emulsions Stable oil-in-water emulsions (e.g., from metal cutting fluids or petrochemical plant wash water) often **resist** bubble attachment because the oil droplets are negatively charged and stabilized by surfactants. The KTU syllabus highlights these conditioning chemicals: | Chemical Type | Common Examples | Function | Typical Dose | |---|---|---|---| | **Coagulants** | Alum, Ferric Chloride, PAC (Poly-Aluminium Chloride) | Neutralize surface charge, destabilize emulsion | 20 – 100 mg/L | | **Flocculants** | Anionic / Cationic Polyacrylamide | Bridge fine particles into larger flocs | 0.5 – 5 mg/L | | **pH Adjusters** | Lime (Ca(OH)₂), H₂SO₄ | Optimize coagulant performance | pH 6 – 8.5 | | **Demulsifiers** | Polyamines, surfactants | Break oil-in-water emulsions | 1 – 10 mg/L | | **Polymers / Coagulant Aids** | Activated silica, starch | Strengthen floc | 1 – 5 mg/L | # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S) The **Air-to-Solids Ratio** is the **single most important design parameter** in DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation) and is frequently asked in KTU numerical problems. **Starting Definitions:** Let: - $s$ = fraction of dissolved air that comes out of solution at the pressure-reduction point (usually $s \approx 0.5$ for effective systems) - $f$ = mass ratio of air dissolved in pressurized water at saturation (kg air / kg water) - $Q_a$ = pressurized recycle flow (m³/h) - $Q$ = influent flow rate (m³/h) - $X$ = influent suspended solids concentration (g/m³ or mg/L) - $\gamma_w$ = specific weight of water = 1000 kg/m³ (for SI) The mass of **air actually released** per hour from the recycle stream: $$\text{Mass of air released} = s \cdot f \cdot Q_a \cdot \gamma_w \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$ The mass of **solids entering** per hour: $$\text{Mass of solids} = Q \cdot X \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$ Therefore, by definition: $$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{s \cdot f \cdot Q_a \cdot \gamma_w}{Q \cdot X}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check (dimensional analysis):** Numerator has units of kg air/h, denominator has units of kg solids/h → A/S is **dimensionless**, as it should be. ### KTU-Style Worked Example — A/S Ratio Computation > **Problem:** A DAF unit treats **5000 m³/d** of refinery wastewater containing **280 mg/L** of suspended solids and **60 mg/L** of emulsified oil. The recycle ratio is **25%**, saturator pressure is **500 kPa (≈ 5 bar, absolute)**, and the fraction of dissolved air released is **0.6**. The saturation mass ratio of air in water at 5 bar absolute is **0.067 kg air / kg water**. Compute the **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)**. **Solution:** $$Q = \dfrac{5000}{24} = 208.33~\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$X = 280 + 60 = 340~\text{mg/L} = 340~\text{g/m}^3 = 0.340~\text{kg/m}^3$$ Recycle flow: $$Q_a = 0.25 \times 208.33 = 52.08~\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ Mass of air released per hour: $$m_{air} = s \cdot f \cdot Q_a \cdot \gamma_w = 0.6 \times 0.067 \times 52.08 \times 1000$$ Step-by-step arithmetic: $$0.6 \times 0.067 = 0.0402$$ $$0.0402 \times 52.08 = 2.094~\text{kg/h (per m}^3\text{)}$$ Multiplying by $\gamma_w = 1000~\text{kg/m}^3$: $$m_{air} = 2.094 \times 1000 = 2094~\text{kg/d (in terms of equivalent flux)}$$ But cleaner: directly compute per-hour numerator: $$\text{Numerator} = 0.6 \times 0.067 \times 52.08 \times 1000 = 2093.6~\text{kg/h}$$ Mass of solids per hour: $$\text{Denominator} = 208.33 \times 0.340 = 70.83~\text{kg/h}$$ Final A/S ratio: $$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{2093.6}{70.83} = 29.56}$$ > [!NOTE] > A/S = 29.56 is **extremely high**. In practice, the **A/S ratio is typically maintained between 0.01 and 0.10 (1%–10%)** for DAF. The above numbers indicate an over-inflated recycle; the design would be re-evaluated to achieve $A/S \approx 0.03\text{–}0.06$ by **reducing the recycle ratio** or **increasing the saturator pressure** to a more efficient operating point. **This kind of result is exactly the type of trap KTU examiners test.** ## 3.2 Derivation of the Saturation Mass Ratio (Henry's Law Form) The amount of air that can be dissolved in water at pressure $P$ (absolute) follows **Henry's Law**. The dissolved-air concentration at saturation is: $$C_{sat} = k_H \cdot P$$ For air at 20 °C, the **Henry's constant** $k_H \approx 25~\text{mg/L per atm}$ (this is a standard reference value for KTU problems). The mass ratio (mass of air per mass of water) at saturation: $$f = \dfrac{C_{sat}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{k_H \cdot P}{1000}$$ At $P = 4~\text{atm}$ (≈ 4 bar absolute, a typical DAF saturator pressure): $$f = \dfrac{25 \times 4}{1000} = 0.10~\text{kg air / kg water}$$ When this pressurized water is released to atmospheric pressure ($P_{atm} = 1~\text{atm}$), the mass of air released per kg of water is: $$\Delta f = \dfrac{k_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{25 \times (4 - 1)}{1000} = 0.075~\text{kg air / kg water}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Raising the saturator pressure from **4 atm to 6 atm** increases dissolved air per kg of water by **50%** — this is why **higher pressures** permit **smaller recycle ratios** to achieve the same A/S, saving pumping energy. ## 3.3 Surface Overflow Rate and Tank Sizing The required **tank surface area** for a flotation unit is: $$A_{tank} = \dfrac{Q}{SOR}$$ where $SOR$ = surface overflow rate in m³/m²·h (or equivalently m/h). **Worked Example — Tank Sizing:** > **Problem:** Design a DAF tank for $Q = 200~\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ of oily wastewater. Target $SOR = 15~\text{m/h}$, $HRT = 30~\text{min}$. Compute the **tank surface area**, **tank volume**, and **water depth**. **Step 1 — Surface area:** $$A_{tank} = \dfrac{Q}{SOR} = \dfrac{200}{15} = 13.33~\text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Volume from HRT:** $$V = Q \times HRT = 200 \times 0.5 = 100~\text{m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Depth check:** $$H = \dfrac{V}{A_{tank}} = \dfrac{100}{13.33} = 7.5~\text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Verify rise velocity:** $$v_{rise} \geq \dfrac{H}{HRT} = \dfrac{7.5}{0.5} = 15~\text{m/h}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Since typical bubble–oil agglomerate rise velocities are **30 – 60 m/h**, the design is **acceptable** — particles will reach the surface long before hydraulic residence ends. Practical DAF tanks are **2 – 3 m deep** (not 7.5 m), so the area should be increased by **widening the tank**, not deepening it. # 4. Influent** | Feed pipe from API separator or primary clarifier | Brings oil-laden wastewater into flotation train | | **2. Coagulation** | Flash mixer with coagulant | Neutralizes surface charges on oil droplets and colloidal solids | | **3. Flocculation** | Slow-stir tank with polymer | Aggregates destabilized droplets into large flocs (100 – 500 µm) | | **4. Pressurization Loop** | Effluent pump + air injection + saturator | Dissolves air under high pressure into a side-stream (recycle) | | **5. Pressure Release** | Nozzle / needle valve | Releases pressure → fine bubbles nucleate and grow | | **6. Bubble–Floc Contact** | Contact zone | Bubbles attach to flocs → agglomerates form | | **7. Separation Zone** | Main flotation chamber | Agglomerates rise to surface under buoyancy | | **8. Skimming** | Chain-and-flight scraper | Removes floating sludge ("float") continuously | | **9. Effluent Collection** | Launder weir | Clear subnatant liquid exits as polished effluent | | **10. Sludge Disposal** | Float pit + dewatering | Concentrated scum is dewatered and disposed | ## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — DAF vs IAF vs CAF | Feature | **DAF** (Dissolved Air Flotation) | **IAF** (Induced Air Flotation) | **CAF** (Cavitation Air Flotation) | |---|---|---|---| | **Bubble Generation** | Pressure reduction of recycled saturated water | Mechanical rotor–stator dispersion | Hydraulic cavitation chambers | | **Bubble Size** | 10 – 100 µm | 100 – 1000 µm | 50 – 200 µm | | **Air Source** | External compressor + saturator | Atmospheric via rotor | Dissolved air + cavitation | | **Energy Demand** | Moderate | Lower | High | | **Best for** | Emulsified oils, fine colloids | Free oils, coarse separation | High solids, sludges | | **Recycle Ratio** | 10 – 50% | < 5% | 10 – 30% | | **Typical Removal** | 70 – 95% emulsified O&G | 80 – 95% free O&G | 75 – 95% O&G + TSS | # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Define flotation as applied in advanced wastewater treatment. Why is it preferred over plain sedimentation for oil and grease removal?** **Model Answer:** Flotation is a **gravity separation unit operation** in which fine gas bubbles (typically air, 10–100 µm in diameter) are introduced into wastewater, attaching to suspended oil droplets, grease particles, or flocs. The bubble-particle agglomerates have an effective density lower than water and **rise to the surface**, where they are skimmed off. Flotation is preferred over plain sedimentation for oil and grease removal because: 1. Oil droplets (especially emulsified, 5–50 µm) have very low **Stokes' settling velocities** (10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁴ m/s), making sedimentation impractical within reasonable tank volumes. 2. **Air bubbles drastically reduce the apparent density** of the agglomerate (effective specific gravity 0.1 – 0.3), giving rise velocities **25 – 100× higher** than natural buoyancy. 3. Flotation completes O&G removal in **20 – 60 minutes** versus **2 – 8 hours** for sedimentation. 4. It produces a **concentrated scum layer (2–5% solids)** for easy sludge handling. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **List any three types of flotation systems used for oil and grease removal. State the bubble size range for each.** **Model Answer:** | Type of Flotation | Bubble Size Range | |---|---| | **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | 10 – 100 µm | | **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** | 100 – 1000 µm | | **Cavitation Air Flotation (CAF)** | 50 – 200 µm | | **Electroflotation** | 10 – 60 µm | | **Vacuum Flotation** | 30 – 100 µm | > **[3 Marks Mapping: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for any two types + 1 mark for bubble sizes]** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *DAF Design and Air-to-Solids Ratio* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the **components and working principle of a Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) unit** for oil and grease removal from refinery wastewater. Discuss the role of the **pressurization loop**, **saturator**, and **bubble–floc contact zone**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A DAF unit is to treat **3000 m³/d** of dairy industry effluent containing **180 mg/L TSS** and **95 mg/L O&G**. The design parameters are: **saturator pressure = 5 bar absolute**, **recycle ratio = 30%**, **fraction of dissolved air released = 0.55**, and **saturation mass ratio of air in water at 5 bar = 0.082 kg air/kg water**. Compute the **Air-to-Solids Ratio**. State whether the design is operationally acceptable. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Solution to Question A(a):** **Working Principle of DAF:** 1. **Influent** flows into a **rapid mix tank** where coagulant (e.g., alum or FeCl₃) is dosed to neutralize the surface charge of oil droplets and colloidal solids. 2. The stream passes to a **flocculation tank** where an anionic polyacrylamide flocculant bridges the destabilized particles into macro-flocs (100 – 500 µm). 3. Simultaneously, a **pressurization loop** takes a side-stream (10 – 50%) of the clarified effluent, pumps it through a **saturator tank** pressurized at **4 – 6 bar absolute** with compressed air. Henry's Law ensures that the saturation mass ratio of air in water (typically 0.05 – 0.10 kg air/kg water) is achieved. 4. The pressurized recycle is then injected through **pressure-release nozzles** into the inlet zone of the flotation tank. The sudden pressure drop causes **fine air bubbles (10 – 100 µm)** to nucleate and disperse throughout the tank. 5. These bubbles **attach** to the pre-formed flocs and oil droplets via hydrophobic and electrostatic attraction. The resulting **bubble–floc agglomerates** have a much lower effective density than water and **rise rapidly** to the surface. 6.
