- Kinetic constants: $\mu_{max} = 3.0$ d⁻¹, $K_s = 80$ mg/L, $Y = 0.65$ mg VSS/mg BOD, $k_d = 0.04$ d⁻¹

**(a)** Derive the relationship between SRT ($\theta_c$) and effluent substrate concentration $S$ from a mass balance on a CSTR. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Using the derived relationship, determine (i) the minimum SRT to prevent washout, (ii) the design SRT (using a safety factor of 5), (iii) the resulting effluent BOD, and (iv) the biomass concentration $X$ in the aeration tank. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

Biomass mass balance on CSTR at steady state (assuming $X_0 = 0$):
$$0 = -Q X + V X(\mu - k_d) \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Dividing by $VX$:
$$\mu - k_d = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Substituting Monod kinetics: $\mu = \mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$:
$$\mu_{max} \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} - k_d = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} = \dfrac{1 + k_d \theta_c}{\mu_{max} \theta_c} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Cross-multiplying and solving for $S$:
$$S \mu_{max} \theta_c = K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c) + S(1 + k_d \theta_c) \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$S[\mu_{max} \theta_c - 1 - k_d \theta_c] = K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c) \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{\theta_c(\mu_{max} - k_d) - 1}} \quad \text{[1 mark for final boxed answer]}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**(i) Minimum SRT (Washout):**

$$\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{3.0 - 0.04} = \dfrac{1}{2.96} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c^{min} = 0.338 \text{ days} \approx 8.1 \text{ hours}}$$

**(ii) Design SRT (with safety factor 5):**

$$\theta_c = 5 \times \theta_c^{min} = 5 \times 0.338 = \boxed{1.69 \text{ days} \approx 2 \text{ days}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(iii) Resulting Effluent BOD:**

Substituting into the SRT-Substrate equation:

$$S = \dfrac{80 \times (1 + 0.04 \times 1.69)}{1.69 \times (3.0 - 0.04) - 1}$$

$$S = \dfrac{80 \times 1.0676}{1.69 \times 2.96 - 1} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$S = \dfrac{85.41}{5.0024 - 1} = \dfrac{85.41}{4.0024} = \boxed{21.34 \text{ mg/L}}$$

*Note: Slightly above 20 mg/L standard. To meet the standard, $\theta_c$ should be slightly increased to ~1.85 days.* **[1 mark for comment]**

**(iv) Biomass Concentration $X$:**

Using the substrate utilization balance: $S_0 - S = \dfrac{\mu_{max} S X \theta}{Y(K_s + S)}$, with $\theta = \theta_c$:

$$X = \dfrac{Y(S_0 - S)(K_s + S)}{\mu_{max} S \theta_c} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$X = \dfrac{0.65 \times (300 - 21.34) \times (80 + 21.34)}{3.0 \times 21.34 \times 1.69}$$

$$X = \dfrac{0.65 \times 278.66 \times 101.34}{108.19} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$X = \dfrac{18,354.7}{108.19} = \boxed{169.6 \text{ mg VSS/L}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the **yield coefficient ($Y$)** and **endogenous decay coefficient ($k_d$)** in biological wastewater treatment. How does the **observed yield ($Y_{obs}$)** differ from the true yield, and why is this distinction important in sludge production calculations? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CSTR with recycle (MLSS = 2,500 mg/L, recycle ratio $R = 0.5$, flow $Q = 15,000$ m³/d) treats wastewater with $S_0 = 200$ mg/L. If $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, and SRT $\theta_c = 8$ days, calculate:
(i) Observed yield $Y_{obs}$
(ii) Excess sludge production rate (kg VSS/d)
(iii) Specific substrate utilization rate $U$
(iv) Check whether the SRT is safe against washout. Assume $\mu_{max} = 2.0$ d⁻¹, $K_s = 50$ mg/L. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Answer [7 marks]**

- **Yield Coefficient ($Y$)**: Mass of new biomass synthesized per unit mass of substrate consumed (typically 0.4–0.8 mg VSS/mg BOD for heterotrophs) **[1 mark]**.
- **Endogenous Decay ($k_d$)**: Accounts for biomass loss due to maintenance metabolism, cell lysis, and predation; typically 0.04–0.08 d⁻¹ **[1 mark]**.
- $Y$ predicts *potential* sludge production; $k_d$ reduces this by simulating the natural die-off of biomass **[1 mark]**.
- **Observed yield** $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ accounts for the cumulative effect of decay over the SRT **[1 mark]**.
- At short SRT, $Y_{obs} \approx Y$; at long SRT (e.g., extended aeration, $\theta_c = 20$–30 d), $Y_{obs}$ can be 30–50% lower **[1 mark]**.
- **Significance in design**: Sludge production $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q(S_0 - S)$ directly affects sludge handling costs, digester sizing, and disposal economics **[1 mark]**.
- Distinction matters in selecting treatment processes — extended aeration plants have lower $Y_{obs}$ (less waste sludge) than conventional plants **[1 mark]**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**(i) Observed Yield [1 mark]:**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 8} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.48} = \boxed{0.405 \text{ mg VSS/mg BOD}}$$

**(ii) Excess Sludge Production [2 marks]:**

First find $S$ using the SRT equation:

$$\mu_{net} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d = 0.125 + 0.06 = 0.185 \text{ d}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} = \dfrac{\mu_{net}}{\mu_{max}} = \dfrac{0.185}{2.0} = 0.0925$$

$$S = \dfrac{0.0925 \times 50}{1 - 0.0925} = \dfrac{4.625}{0.9075} = 5.10 \text{ mg/L} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Sludge production:
$$P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q (S_0 - S) = 0.405 \times 15,000 \times (200 - 5.10)$$

$$P_X = 0.405 \times 15,000 \times 194.9 = \boxed{1.184 \times 10^6 \text{ g VSS/d} = 1,184 \text{ kg VSS/d}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(iii) Specific Substrate Utilization Rate [2 marks]:**

$$U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{X \theta} \quad \text{where } \theta = HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

For a recycle system: HRT = $\dfrac{V}{Q}$, and SRT = $\dfrac{VX}{Q_w X + Q_e X_e}$.

Using F/M approximation with $\theta = \theta_c / (1+R) = 8/1.5 = 5.33$ days (approximation for design):

$$U = \dfrac{194.9}{2500 \times 5.33} = \dfrac{194.9}{13,325} = \boxed{0.0146 \text{ d}^{-1}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Washout Check [2 marks]:**

$$\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{2.0 - 0.06} = \dfrac{1}{1.94} = 0.515 \text{ days}$$

Since $\theta_c = 8$ days $\gg \theta_c^{min} = 0.515$ days, the system is **safe against washout** (Safety factor = 8/0.515 = 15.5). **[2 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This Before the Exam**

- ✅ The **Basic Kinetic Equation** links microbial growth, substrate consumption, and biomass decay.
- ✅ **Monod equation**: $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S/(K_s + S)$ — *always hyperbolic, never linear*.
- ✅ **SRT-Substrate equation**: $S = K_s(1 + k_d\theta_c) / [\theta_c(\mu_{max} - k_d) - 1]$ — *the most-tested formula*.
- ✅ **Washout SRT**: $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_{max} - k_d)$ — design with safety factor 3–10.
- ✅ **Observed yield**: $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d\theta_c)$ — *used for sludge production, not Y directly*.
- ✅ **F/M ratio**: Typical range 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹ for conventional activated sludge.
- ✅ For a **CSTR without recycle**: SRT = HRT.
- ✅ For a **CSTR with recycle**: SRT ≠ HRT; SRT = $\dfrac{VX}{Q_w X + Q_e X_e}$.
- ✅ Substrate balance on CSTR: $Q(S_0 - S) = V \cdot \mu_{max} S X / [Y(K_s + S)]$.
- ✅ At low $S \ll K_s$: **first-order kinetics** ($\mu \propto S$).
- ✅ At high $S \gg K_s$: **zero-order kinetics** ($\mu = \mu_{max}$).
- ✅ **Nitrification** requires $\theta_c \geq 3$–5 days at 20°C (much higher than carbonaceous BOD removal).
- ✅ **High SRT** → more complete treatment but more energy for aeration and potential pinpoint floc.
- ✅ **Low SRT** → poor treatment efficiency, dispersed growth, risk of washout.
- ✅ **Units check**: $\mu_{max}$ in d⁻¹, $K_s$ in mg/L, $Y$ in mg/mg, $k_d$ in d⁻¹, $X$ in mg VSS/L, $S$ in mg BOD/L.
- ✅ **Sludge production** $P_X = Y_{obs} Q (S_0 - S)$ — *always use $Y_{obs}$, never raw Y*.
- ✅ **Oxygen demand** $O_2 = Q(S_0 - S) - 1.42 P_X$ (factor 1.42 = oxygen equivalent of biomass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick — "K-M-Y-D":** **K**ₛ, **M**u max, **Y**ield, **D**ecay. These four letters are your whole universe for the Basic Kinetic Equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Flow Treatment Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Flow Treatment Models # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, **Continuous Flow Treatment Models** (also termed **Continuous-Flow Reactors** or **Flow-Through Reactals**) refer to the mathematical and engineering idealizations of treatment units in which influent wastewater enters and effluent exits the system **continuously and simultaneously**, as opposed to batch reactors where flow is intermittent. The two fundamental idealized continuous-flow models universally adopted in environmental engineering are: 1. **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — also called **Tubular Flow Reactor** or **Longitudinal Dispersion Model (n → ∞)**. The fluid moves through the reactor as discrete "plugs," each plug remaining distinct with no longitudinal mixing. Every fluid element spends an identical residence time equal to the theoretical detention time. 2. **Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR)** — also called **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** or **Backmix Reactor**. The contents of the reactor are assumed to be **completely homogeneous** at all times due to vigorous mechanical or hydraulic mixing; the composition of the effluent is therefore identical to the composition within the reactor at every point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Real treatment units (activated sludge aeration tanks, stabilization ponds, equalization basins, biofilm reactors) behave as **hybrids** of the PFR and CMFR extremes. Mastery of these two ideal models is mandatory for analyzing, designing, and rating actual wastewater treatment plants. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway vs. The Bathtub" Imagine two extreme ways to wash clothes in a river: - **PFR Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt River":** Picture a long, narrow channel where a drop of dye entering at the upstream end travels through the channel as a tight, unmixed slug. The water at any cross-section is **different from** the water at the next cross-section. The drop never mixes with the water ahead of it or behind it. The dye at the exit has had exactly the same treatment time as every other drop. - **CMFR Analogy — "The Bathtub on a Mixer":** Imagine a bathtub with a powerful agitator. You pour a drop of dye in the inlet. The instant it enters, it is **uniformly distributed throughout the entire bathtub volume**. The water leaving the outlet has exactly the same concentration as the water in any corner of the bathtub. The dye in the tub has a *distribution* of ages — some drops just arrived, some have been there for a long time. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** > - **PFR** = maximum efficiency for a given volume (best for most biochemical reactions of order *n > 0*). > - **CMFR** = lower efficiency for a given volume, but provides **damping of shock loads** and **uniform environmental conditions** for biomass (e.g., activated sludge). ## 1.3 Significance in Wastewater Engineering Continuous flow models are the **backbone of design** for: - **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** — a train of CMFRs in series. - **Trickling Filters** — modeled as PFRs with first-order substrate removal. - **Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC)** — closely approximate PFR behavior. - **Stabilization Ponds / Lagoons** — modeled as dispersed-flow reactors (PFR ↔ CMFR spectrum). - **Equalization Tanks** — almost perfectly CMFR in hydraulic performance. The governing physical metrics are: - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**, $\theta = V/Q$, in **days (d)** or **hours (h)**. - **Volumetric Flow Rate, $Q$**, in **m³/d**. - **Reactor Volume, $V$**, in **m³**. - **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)**, $\text{VLR} = Q \cdot S_0 / V$, in **kg BOD/m³·d**. - **Substrate (or Pollutant) Concentration, $S$**, in **mg/L**. - **Reaction Rate, $r$**, in **mg/L·d** (or g/m³·d). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General Mass Balance Framework Every reactor model in wastewater engineering is derived from the **Conservation of Mass** principle applied to a control volume (the reactor): $$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} \pm \text{Reaction}$$ In differential form for a generic substrate of concentration $S$: $$V \frac{dS}{dt} = Q S_{in} - Q S_{out} \pm r_S \cdot V$$ Where: - $V$ = reactor liquid volume (m³) - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/d) - $S_{in}, S_{out}$ = influent and effluent substrate concentrations (mg/L) - $r_S$ = rate of substrate utilization (mg/L·d); negative for consumption. > [!NOTE] > Under **steady-state conditions**, which is the standard design assumption in KTU 2024 continuous-flow problems, $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = 0$. The mass balance simplifies and the equation becomes algebraic. ## 2.2 Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) — Step-by-Step Logic 1. The reactor is conceptualized as an infinite series of infinitesimally thin "plugs." 2. Each plug is a **closed system** — no mixing in the longitudinal (flow) direction. 3. Substrate is consumed by reaction kinetics (typically first-order in wastewater treatment: $r_S = -kS$). 4. The mass balance is written on a **differential element** of volume $dV$. 5. Integration over the entire reactor volume yields the PFR performance equation. The steady-state mass balance on a differential slice is: $$0 = Q \cdot S - Q(S + dS) + r_S \, dV$$ $$Q \, dS = r_S \, dV$$ For **first-order kinetics** $r_S = -kS$, with boundary condition $S = S_0$ at $V = 0$: $$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{Q} \int_0^V dV$$ $$\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp(-k\theta) = \exp\left(-\frac{kV}{Q}\right)}$$ ## 2.3 Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR) — Step-by-Step Logic 1. The contents of the reactor are **completely homogeneous**. 2. The effluent concentration $S_e$ is **equal** to the concentration at any point inside the reactor. 3. A single algebraic mass balance on the entire reactor volume suffices. 4. The standard hydraulic model is the **completely stirred tank**. The steady-state mass balance over the whole reactor: $$0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e + r_S V$$ Solving for $S_e$: $$S_e = S_0 + \frac{r_S V}{Q} = S_0 + r_S \theta$$ For **first-order kinetics** $r_S = -k S_e$ (note: rate is evaluated at the *effluent* concentration, since the entire reactor is at $S_e$): $$0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V$$ $$Q S_0 = S_e (Q + kV)$$ $$\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{1}{1 + kV/Q}}$$ ## 2.4 The Wehner-Wilhelm Equation (PFR + CMFR Combined) When $n$ identical CMFRs are connected **in series**, the overall fractional removal is: $$\boxed{\frac{S_n}{S_0} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{k\theta}{n}\right)^{n}}}$$ This is the **Wehner-Wilhelm equation**, which is the KTU 2024 workhorse for designing multi-stage activated-sludge systems. In the limit as $n \to \infty$, the Wehner-Wilhelm equation reduces smoothly to the PFR equation $\exp(-k\theta)$, mathematically proving the conceptual analogy that "PFR = infinite series of CMFRs." ## 2.5 Real Reaction Kinetics in Wastewater In Module 2 of KTU 2024, the following kinetic models are emphasized: - **First-order kinetics:** $r_S = -kS$ — used for BOD removal, disinfection (Chick's law), and trickling filters. - **Zero-order kinetics:** $r_S = -k$ — used for high-substrate environments, certain biological reactions. - **Monod / Michaelis-Menten kinetics:** $r_S = -\dfrac{kXS}{K_s + S}$ — for substrate-limited microbial growth. - **Second-order kinetics:** $r_S = -kS^2$ — for some advanced oxidation processes (AOPs). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **complete formula kit** required for any Continuous Flow Treatment Model problem in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). | # | Reactor / Concept | Performance Equation | Conversion $\eta$ | Typical Use Case | |---|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | **PFR (First-order)** | $S_e / S_0 = \exp(-k\theta)$ | $1 - \exp(-k\theta)$ | Trickling filter, long narrow tank | | 2 | **CMFR (First-order)** | $S_e / S_0 = 1 / (1 + k\theta)$ | $k\theta / (1 + k\theta)$ | Activated sludge, equalization | | 3 | **CMFR (Zero-order)** | $S_e = S_0 - k\theta$ | $k\theta / S_0$ | High-strength industrial | | 4 | **CMFR (Monod)** | Solve numerically: $\theta = (K_s + S_e)\ln(S_0/S_e)/kX + (S_0 - S_e)/kX$ | $1 - S_e/S_0$ | Biological nutrient removal | | 5 | **n CMFRs in series (Wehner-Wilhelm)** | $S_n / S_0 = 1 / (1 + k\theta/n)^{n}$ | $1 - 1/(1 + k\theta/n)^{n}$ | Multi-stage aeration tanks | | 6 | **PFR + CMFR Hybrid** | $S_e / S_0 = \exp\bigl[-k\theta_1 \bigr] / (1 + k\theta_2)$ | See expression | Primary clarifier + aeration | | 7 | **Hydraulic Retention Time** | $\theta = V / Q$ | — | Universal design parameter | | 8 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT)** | $\theta_c = VX / QX_e$ | — | Activated sludge design | | 9 | **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = Q S_0 / V X$ | — | Activated sludge operating param | | 10 | **Volumetric Loading Rate** | $\text{VLR} = Q S_0 / V$ | — | All biological reactors | > [!NOTE] > **Units Convention (KTU 2024):** $k$ in **d⁻¹** when $\theta$ in **d**; $Q$ in **m³/d**; $V$ in **m³**; concentrations in **mg/L** or **g/m³** (numerically equivalent). ## 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Plant Operators** use the CMFR equation to predict effluent BOD/COD after the aeration tank. - **Design Engineers** use the Wehner-Wilhelm equation to size multi-cell activated-sludge reactors. - **Process Modellers** use PFR and CMFR as the boundary conditions for the **Dispersion Model** that quantifies real reactor behavior. - **Regulatory Compliance** calculations (e.g., Kerala State Pollution Control Board consents) require demonstrating that $S_e < S_{\text{standard}}$ using these models. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFR Performance Equation (First-Order Kinetics) **Starting Point:** Steady-state mass balance on a differential slice of a PFR of cross-sectional area $A$ and elemental length $dL$: $$0 = Q \cdot S - Q \cdot (S + dS) + r_S \cdot dV$$ **Step 1 — Cancel the flow term** (note the negative sign when expanding the outflow): $$0 = Q S - Q S - Q \, dS + r_S \, dV$$ $$0 = -Q \, dS + r_S \, dV$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to separate variables:** $$Q \, dS = r_S \, dV$$ **Step 3 — Substitute first-order rate law** $r_S = -kS$: $$Q \, dS = -k S \, dV$$ **Step 4 — Separate variables** (move all $S$ terms to LHS, all $V$ terms to RHS): $$\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{Q} \, dV$$ **Step 5 — Apply integration limits.** At the inlet $V = 0$, $S = S_0$. At the outlet $V = V$, $S = S_e$: $$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{Q} \int_{0}^{V} dV$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals:** $$\ln(S_e) - \ln(S_0) = -\frac{kV}{Q}$$ **Step 7 — Exponentiate and substitute $\theta = V/Q$:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = -k\theta$$ $$\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k\theta} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{kV}{Q}\right)}$$ **Step 8 — Conversion (fractional removal):** $$\eta_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - \frac{S_e}{S_0} = 1 - e^{-k\theta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** As $k\theta \to \infty$, $S_e \to 0$ (perfect treatment). As $k\theta \to 0$, $S_e \to S_0$ (no treatment). Behaviour is correct for a PFR. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMFR Performance Equation (First-Order Kinetics) **Starting Point:** Steady-state mass balance on the **entire** CMFR volume $V$: $$0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e + r_S V$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the first-order rate law** $r_S = -k S_e$ (use $S_e$ because contents are completely mixed): $$0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V$$ **Step 2 — Group all $S_e$ terms on the right-hand side:** $$Q S_0 = Q S_e + k S_e V$$ **Step 3 — Factor $S_e$:** $$Q S_0 = S_e (Q + kV)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $S_e$:** $$S_e = \frac{Q S_0}{Q + kV}$$ **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $Q$:** $$S_e = \frac{S_0}{1 + kV/Q}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $\theta = V/Q$:** $$\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta}}$$ **Step 7 — Fractional removal:** $$\eta_{\text{CMFR}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{k\theta}{1 + k\theta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** At $k\theta = 4$, $S_e/S_0 = 1/5 = 0.20$ (80% removal). Compare to PFR at $k\theta = 4$: $S_e/S_0 = e^{-4} = 0.0183$ (98.2% removal). The CMFR is **dramatically less efficient** for first-order reactions — this is the classical KTU 2024 result. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wehner-Wilhelm Equation (n CMFRs in Series) **Starting Point:** Consider $n$ equal-volume CMFRs in series, each of volume $V/n$. Each reactor has HRT $\theta/n$. Let the intermediate concentrations be $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$ with $S_n = S_e$. **Step 1 — Apply the single-CFMR equation to the first reactor:** $$\frac{S_1}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta/n}$$ **Step 2 — For the second reactor** (with influent $S_1$ and effluent $S_2$): $$\frac{S_2}{S_1} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta/n}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply the $n$ ratios** (telescoping product): $$\frac{S_2}{S_0} = \frac{S_1}{S_0} \cdot \frac{S_2}{S_1} = \left(\frac{1}{1 + k\theta/n}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Generalize to the $n$-th reactor (effluent):** $$\boxed{\frac{S_n}{S_0} = \left(\frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{k\theta}{n}}\right)^{\!n} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{k\theta}{n}\right)^{n}}}$$ **Step 5 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$** (proving PFR equivalence): $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{k\theta}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{k\theta}$$ Therefore: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{S_n}{S_0} = \frac{1}{e^{k\theta}} = e^{-k\theta}$$ This is **identical** to the PFR equation derived in §3.1. The limit proves mathematically that a PFR is the limiting case of an infinite cascade of CMFRs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Result:** The efficiency ordering for first-order reactions is: > $$\eta_{\text{PFR}} > \eta_{n\text{-CMFR series}} > \eta_{\text{single CMFR}}$$ > for any $n \geq 1$ and any $k\theta > 0$. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | PFR | Single CMFR | n CMFRs in Series ($n=4$) | |-----------|-----|-------------|---------------------------| | $S_e / S_0$ at $k\theta = 1$ | $0.368$ | $0.500$ | $0.316$ | | $S_e / S_0$ at $k\theta = 2$ | $0.135$ | $0.333$ | $0.198$ | | $S_e / S_0$ at $k\theta = 4$ | $0.018$ | $0.200$ | $0.063$ | | $\eta$ at $k\theta = 2$ | $86.5\%$ | $66.7\%$ | $80.2\%$ | | Required $V$ for $S_e/S_0 = 0.1$ | $2.303/k$ | $9/k$ | $3.51/k$ | | Volume ratio relative to PFR | $1.00$ | $3.91$ | $1.52$ | | Shock-load resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good (improves with $n$) | | Effluent quality variability | High | Low | Moderate-Low | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A wastewater with influent BOD $S_0 = 300$ mg/L is treated in a CMFR of volume $V = 1500$ m³. The flow rate is $Q = 500$ m³/d and the first-order BOD removal rate constant is $k = 0.40$ d⁻¹. Calculate the effluent BOD and the fractional removal. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate the HRT:** $$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1500}{500} = 3.0 \text{ d}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the dimensionless group $k\theta$:** $$k\theta = 0.40 \times 3.0 = 1.20$$ **Step 3 — Apply the CMFR performance equation:** $$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.20} = \frac{1}{2.20} = 0.4545$$ **Step 4 — Compute effluent BOD:** $$S_e = 0.4545 \times 300 = 136.4 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the fractional removal:** $$\eta = 1 - 0.4545 = 0.5455 = 54.6\%$$ **Step 6 — Cross-check with PFR for the same conditions:** $$\frac{S_e^{\text{PFR}}}{S_0} = e^{-1.20} = 0.3012 \implies S_e^{\text{PFR}} = 90.4 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\eta_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - 0.3012 = 69.9\%$$ The PFR delivers **15.3 percentage points more removal** for the same volume — confirming the KTU 2024 conclusion that PFRs are more efficient for first-order reactions. > [!TIP] > **Key insight for students:** If the same CMFR is split into **four CMFRs in series** of volume 375 m³ each, the Wehner-Wilhelm equation gives: > $$\frac{S_4}{S_0} = \frac{1}{(1 + 0.4 \times 3.0/4)^{4}} = \frac{1}{(1.30)^{4}} = \frac{1}{2.8561} = 0.3502$$ > $$S_4 = 105.1 \text{ mg/L}, \quad \eta = 65.0\%$$ > The 4-stage CMFR recovers most of the PFR performance (69.9%) with only a marginal increase in cost. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Continuous-Flow Reactor Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent Wastewater
Q, S0, T] --> B{Continuous-Flow
Treatment Unit} B -->|Reactor Type 1| C[Plug Flow Reactor
PFR Model] B -->|Reactor Type 2| D[Completely Mixed
Flow Reactor CMFR] B -->|Reactor Type 3| E[n CMFRs in Series
Wehner-Wilhelm] C --> F[Effluent Stream
Q, Se] D --> F E --> F F --> G{Quality Check
Se vs Standard} G -->|Compliant| H[Discharge to
Environment] G -->|Non-Compliant| I[Return to
Secondary Stage] H --> J[Treated
Effluent] I --> B ``` ## 4.2 PFR — Longitudinal Plug-Flow Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet
S = S0] --> B[Plug 1
S = S0] B --> C[Plug 2
S = S1] C --> D[Plug 3
S = S2] D --> E[Plug 4
S = S3] E --> F[Plug n
S = Sn-1] F --> G[Outlet
S = Se] H((Each plug is
an unmixed batch
in time sequence)) -.-> B H -.-> C H -.-> D H -.-> E H -.-> F ``` ## 4.3 CMFR — Homogeneous Mixing Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet
Q, S0] --> B((Reactor Vessel
V, perfectly mixed)) C[Stirrer /
Diffuser] --> B B --> D[Outlet
Q, Se = Sreactor] E((Sreactor = Se
at every point
and every instant)) -.-> B ``` ## 4.4 Wehner-Wilhelm Cascade — n CMFRs in Series ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent
S0] --> B[CMFR 1
V/n] B --> C[CMFR 2
V/n] C --> D[CMFR 3
V/n] D --> E[CMFR 4
V/n] E --> F[More Reactors
up to n] F --> G[Effluent
Sn] H((Each stage
reduces S by
factor 1 plus k theta over n)) -.-> B H -.-> C H -.-> D H -.-> E H -.-> F ``` ## 4.5 Efficiency Comparison Flow — Reactor Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Choose
Reactor Type] --> B{Is reaction
order greater than zero?} B -->|Yes| C{Need shock-load
damping?} B -->|No, Zero-order| D[PFR and CMFR
give identical
results] C -->|Yes, yes| E[Select Single CMFR
or few-stage cascade] C -->|No| F{Does effluent
variability matter?} F -->|No| G[Select PFR
maximum efficiency] F -->|Yes| H[Select n-stage
CMFR cascade
n greater than 3] E --> I[Final Design] G --> I H --> I D --> I ``` ## 4.6 Mass Balance — General Reactor Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Stream
Q, S0] --> B((Control Volume
V, dS/dt)) C[Reaction Term
rS times V] --> B B --> D[Output Stream
Q, Se] B --> E[Accumulation
V dS/dt] F[Equation:
V dS/dt = Q S0 minus Q Se plus rS V] -.-> B ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR). State the steady-state mass balance equation for a CMFR treating a wastewater with first-order substrate removal kinetics.** **Model Answer:** A **Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR)** is an idealized continuous-flow treatment unit in which the contents are **completely and instantaneously homogeneous** at all times due to vigorous mixing. The concentration of any constituent in the effluent is therefore identical to its concentration at any point inside the reactor. The steady-state mass balance equation for a CMFR with first-order substrate removal kinetics ($r_S = -k S_e$) is: $$Q S_0 = Q S_e + k S_e V$$ or equivalently: $$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} \quad \text{where} \quad \theta = \frac{V}{Q}$$ **[3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for mass balance derivation]** --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) and a Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR) in terms of mixing pattern and treatment efficiency for first-order reactions.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | PFR | CMFR | |---------|-----|------| | **Mixing pattern** | No longitudinal mixing; fluid moves as discrete "plugs" | Complete mixing; contents uniform at all times | | **Concentration profile** | Varies along the length of the reactor | Constant throughout the reactor | | **Residence time distribution** | All fluid elements have identical residence time $\theta$ | Distributed; some elements leave immediately, others stay long | | **Treatment efficiency for 1st-order reaction** | Higher (lower $S_e$ for same $V$) | Lower (higher $S_e$ for same $V$) | | **Shock-load resistance** | Poor — shock propagates to effluent | Excellent — incoming shock is diluted | | **Effluent variability** | Mirrors influent variability | Smoothed and dampened | | **Performance equation** | $S_e/S_0 = e^{-k\theta}$ | $S_e/S_0 = 1/(1 + k\theta)$ | For the same $k\theta$, $e^{-k\theta} \leq 1/(1+k\theta)$, with strict inequality for $k\theta > 0$. Thus, **PFR > CMFR in efficiency for first-order kinetics**. **[3 marks: 1 mark per row of difference with at least 3 valid points]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** A municipal wastewater treatment plant receives a flow of $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d with an influent BOD of $S_0 = 250$ mg/L. The plant uses a single completely mixed aeration tank of volume $V = 5000$ m³. The first-order BOD removal rate constant at the operating temperature is $k = 0.30$ d⁻¹. Determine: 1. The hydraulic retention time $\theta$. 2. The effluent BOD $S_e$. 3. The overall BOD removal efficiency $\eta$. **(b) [7 Marks]** The plant engineer proposes to retrofit the same total volume into **four equal CMFRs in series**. Recalculate the new effluent BOD and the new efficiency using the Wehner-Wilhelm equation. Comment on the improvement achieved. **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic retention time:** $$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{5000}{10000} = 0.5 \text{ d}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless group $k\theta$:** $$k\theta = 0.30 \times 0.5 = 0.15$$ **Step 3 — Apply the CMFR performance equation:** $$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.15} = \frac{1}{1.15} = 0.8696$$ **Step 4 — Compute effluent BOD:** $$S_e = 0.8696 \times 250 = 217.4 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the overall removal efficiency:** $$\eta = 1 - 0.8696 = 0.1304 = 13.04\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to compute $\theta$ and use $V$ directly in the exponent, leading to a dimensionally incorrect result. Always compute $k\theta$ as a **dimensionless** product before applying the formula. **[7 marks: 1 mark for θ, 1 mark for kθ, 2 marks for S_e, 1 mark for η, 2 marks for correctly stating and applying the CMFR equation]** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Volume of each sub-reactor:** Each of the 4 CMFRs has volume $V/4 = 5000/4 = 1250$ m³, so its HRT is: $$\theta_{\text{each}} = \frac{V/4}{Q} = \frac{1250}{10000} = 0.125 \text{ d}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Wehner-Wilhelm equation:** $$\frac{S_4}{S_0} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{k\theta}{n}\right)^{\!n}} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{0.15}{4}\right)^{4}} = \frac{1}{(1.0375)^{4}}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate $(1.0375)^4$:** $$(1.0375)^2 = 1.07641$$ $$(1.0375)^4 = (1.07641)^2 = 1.15866$$ **Step 4 — Compute new effluent BOD:** $$\frac{S_4}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1.15866} = 0.8630$$ $$S_4 = 0.8630 \times 250 = 215.8 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step 5 — Compute new efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{new}} = 1 - 0.8630 = 0.1370 = 13.70\%$$ **Step 6 — Improvement analysis:** $$\Delta \eta = 13.70\% - 13.04\% = 0.66 \text{ percentage points}$$ $$\text{Relative improvement} = \frac{0.66}{13.04} \times 100\% = 5.06\%$$ **Comment:** At the low $k\theta = 0.15$ of this plant, the Wehner-Wilhelm retrofit provides only a marginal improvement because the reactor is operating in the **low-efficiency regime** where both the PFR and the CMFR produce similar results. To gain a substantial benefit, the operator should either **increase the reactor volume** (raise $\theta$) or **rebuild as a true PFR** (e.g., a long narrow plug-flow aeration ditch). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students will incorrectly apply $(1 + k\theta/n)^n$ as a *denominator* with the *sum* $k\theta$ not divided by $n$. Always verify that the Wehner-Wilhelm equation has $k\theta/n$ inside the parenthesis, **not** $k\theta$. A common error is $\bigl(1 + k\theta\bigr)^{n}$ in the denominator — this is **wrong**. **[7 marks: 1 mark θ_each, 2 marks correct Wehner-Wilhelm substitution, 1 mark arithmetic, 1 mark S_4, 1 mark η, 1 mark valid engineering comment]** --- ### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive, from first principles, the steady-state performance equation for a **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** assuming first-order substrate removal kinetics. Clearly state the boundary conditions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A wastewater with $S_0 = 400$ mg/L is treated in a PFR of volume $V = 2000$ m³ with $Q = 1000$ m³/d and $k = 0.50$ d⁻¹. Calculate $S_e$ and $\eta$. If the **same volume and flow** are reconfigured as a **single CMFR**, recalculate $S_e$ and $\eta$. Discuss the engineering implications. **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Define the control volume.** Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of the PFR of cross-section $A$ and thickness $dL$ (so $dV = A\,dL$). **Step 2 — Write the steady-state mass balance on the slice.** Inflow at concentration $S$, outflow at concentration $S + dS$, reaction rate $r_S = -kS$: $$0 = Q \cdot S - Q \cdot (S + dS) + r_S \, dV$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the first-order rate law:** $$0 = Q S - Q S - Q \, dS - kS \, dV$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$Q \, dS = -k S \, dV$$ **Step 5 — Separate variables:** $$\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{Q} \, dV$$ **Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions.** At the inlet, $V = 0$ and $S = S_0$. At the outlet, $V = V$ and $S = S_e$: $$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{k}{Q} \int_{0}^{V} dV$$ **Step 7 — Integrate:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = -\frac{kV}{Q} = -k\theta$$ **Step 8 — Exponentiate to obtain the PFR performance equation:** $$\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k\theta} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{kV}{Q}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Boundary conditions stated explicitly:** (i) At $V = 0$, $S = S_0$ (inlet concentration); (ii) At $V = V$, $S = S_e$ (outlet concentration); (iii) Plug flow assumes no radial or longitudinal dispersion; (iv) Steady state means $dS/dt = 0$. **[7 marks: 1 mark setup, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark separation of variables, 1 mark BCs, 1 mark integration, 1 mark final form, 1 mark physical interpretation or assumption statement]** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic retention time** (same for both configurations): $$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2000}{1000} = 2.0 \text{ d}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $k\theta$:** $$k\theta = 0.50 \times 2.0 = 1.0$$ **Step 3 — Apply the PFR performance equation:** $$\frac{S_e^{\text{PFR}}}{S_0} = e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$$ $$S_e^{\text{PFR}} = 0.3679 \times 400 = 147.2 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\eta_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321 = 63.21\%$$ **Step 4 — Apply the single-CMFR performance equation:** $$\frac{S_e^{\text{CMFR}}}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.0} = 0.5000$$ $$S_e^{\text{CMFR}} = 0.5000 \times 400 = 200.0 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\eta_{\text{CMFR}} = 1 - 0.5000 = 0.5000 = 50.00\%$$ **Step 5 — Comparison table:** | Metric | PFR | CMFR | Difference | |--------|-----|------|-----------| | $S_e$ (mg/L) | 147.2 | 200.0 | 52.8 mg/L | | $\eta$ (%) | 63.21% | 50.00% | 13.21 pp | | Volume required for $\eta = 80\%$ | $V_{\text{PFR}} = 3.219 Q/k$ | $V_{\text{CMFR}} = 16.0 Q/k$ | 4.97× larger | **Step 6 — Engineering discussion:** - For **first-order BOD removal**, a PFR requires **significantly less volume** to achieve the same effluent quality. - However, a CMFR provides **superior resistance to hydraulic and organic shock loads**, which is critical for industrial or combined-sewer systems. - A **compromise solution** is to use **4–6 CMFRs in series** (e.g., compartmentalized oxidation ditch) to capture 80–95% of the PFR benefit while retaining moderate shock damping. - For a plant with **highly variable influent** (typical of Kerala's small-to-medium municipalities), the engineer should consider staged CMFRs; for a plant with **stable influent** and tight land availability, a true PFR (e.g., long narrow aeration tank) is preferable. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > 1. Using $V$ directly in the exponent for CMFR (the CMFR has $V$ only in the $\theta$ term, **not** in an exponential). > 2. Forgetting to convert $S_0$ from mg/L when the rate constant $k$ is in different units. > 3. Reporting $S_e$ with too many significant figures (use **3 significant figures**, e.g., 147 mg/L, not 147.1567). > 4. Confusing the **Wehner-Wilhelm exponent** $(1 + k\theta/n)^n$ with the **PFR exponential** $e^{-k\theta}$. The two coincide only in the limit $n \to \infty$. **[7 marks: 1 mark θ, 1 mark kθ, 1 mark PFR S_e, 1 mark PFR η, 1 mark CMFR S_e, 1 mark CMFR η, 1 mark valid engineering discussion]** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Continuous Flow Treatment Models (KTU 2024, Module 2)** - **Two ideal continuous-flow models:** **PFR** (no longitudinal mixing) and **CMFR** (complete mixing). All real reactors lie between these extremes. - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $\theta = V/Q$. Always in consistent units with $k$. - **PFR performance equation (1st order):** $S_e/S_0 = e^{-k\theta}$. - **CMFR performance equation (1st order):** $S_e/S_0 = 1/(1 + k\theta)$. - **Wehner-Wilhelm equation (n CMFRs in series):** $S_n/S_0 = 1/(1 + k\theta/n)^{n}$. - **Mathematical limit:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 + k\theta/n)^{n} = e^{k\theta}$, so a PFR is mathematically equivalent to an infinite cascade of CMFRs. - **Efficiency ordering (1st-order reactions):** PFR > n-stage CMFR cascade > single CMFR. PFR always wins for the same total volume and $k\theta > 0$. - **CMFR advantages:** Shock-load damping, uniform environmental conditions (favours biomass growth), simpler construction. - **PFR advantages:** Maximum removal efficiency, smaller reactor volume for a given $S_e$. - **In CMFR, use $S_e$ (not $S_0$) in the rate law** because the entire reactor is at $S_e$. In PFR, use $S$ because concentration varies with position. - **Steady-state assumption** ($dS/dt = 0$) is the default for KTU 2024 ESE problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. - **Common KTU exam trap:** confusing $k\theta$ for the dimensionless Damköhler number; the student should be able to derive both PFR and CMFR equations from the general mass balance within 5 minutes. - **Units to memorize:** $k$ in d⁻¹, $Q$ in m³/d, $V$ in m³, $\theta$ in d, $S$ in mg/L (numerically = g/m³). - **Real-world reactors:** Activated sludge ≈ CMFR or n-stage CMFR; trickling filter ≈ PFR; stabilization pond ≈ PFR–CMFR hybrid; oxidation ditch ≈ n-stage CMFR. - **For zero-order reactions**, $S_e = S_0 - k\theta$ in both PFR and CMFR — the two models give **identical** results.

topics.title: Oxygen Requirement in Aerobic Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oxygen Requirement in Aerobic Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Oxygen Requirement in an Aerobic Process** is the total mass of molecular oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) that must be supplied per unit time to a biological reactor so that heterotrophic and autotrophic microorganisms can simultaneously (i) oxidize the dissolved and suspended organic substrate (carbonaceous BOD), (ii) synthesize new cellular biomass, and (iii) perform endogenous respiration, while maintaining the bulk-liquid **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** concentration above the critical limit of **2 mg/L** (typically maintained at **1.5 – 2.0 mg/L** in activated sludge systems).

Mathematically, the **Total Oxygen Demand (TOD)** of an aerobic biological reactor is the sum of three independent oxygen sinks:

$$
\text{TOD} = \underbrace{\text{O}_2^{(\text{C})}}_{\text{Carbonaceous}} + \underbrace{\text{O}_2^{(\text{N})}}_{\text{Nitrification}} + \underbrace{\text{O}_2^{(\text{D})}}_{\text{Endogenous Decay}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Board Examiner expects the student to clearly distinguish between **Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD)**, **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)**, **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)**, and the **Actual Oxygen Transfer Rate (AOTR)**. A common conflation of these terms results in the loss of 2 – 3 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung-Breathing" City

Imagine a densely populated city (the **biomass** in the aeration tank) where every resident eats food (the **substrate/BOD**) and burns it in a furnace to live. The city cannot survive without a steady, forced supply of fresh air pumped in through huge ventilation shafts. The "ventilation rate" of the city is the **oxygen requirement**. 

If the city is hit by a sudden influx of food (high influent BOD), it needs more air. If a special subgroup of residents (the **nitrifiers**) appears and starts processing a different kind of fuel (ammonia), they too need additional air. As the residents get older, they consume oxygen just to maintain their bodies (**endogenous decay**). The air-conditioning system's job is to deliver exactly the sum of all three needs — never more (wasted energy), never less (the city suffocates and crashes — the classic **filamentous bulking** event).

## 1.3 The Three Oxygen Sinks — Quick Overview

| Sink | Triggered by | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbonaceous ($\text{O}_2^{(C)}$)** | Influent soluble + particulate BOD | **40 – 60 %** of total |
| **Nitrification ($\text{O}_2^{(N)}$)** | Influent $\text{NH}_3$-N | **4.57 g O₂ per g N oxidized** |
| **Endogenous Decay ($\text{O}_2^{(D)}$)** | Sludge age (SRT) | **0.04 – 0.10 mg O₂/mg VSS·d** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Memorize:** *4.57 mg of $\text{O}_2$ are required to oxidize 1 mg of ammonia-nitrogen ($\text{NH}_4^+ \to \text{NO}_3^-$)*. This is non-negotiable in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Stoichiometry of Aerobic Oxidation

Aerobic metabolism can be decomposed into **two coupled sub-processes**:

### A. Carbonaceous Synthesis + Energy Reaction
The general oxidation of an organic molecule $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w\text{S}_u\text{P}_v$ by biomass ($\text{C}_5\text{H}_7\text{NO}_2$, the classical McCarty formula for active cells) is:

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4} - \tfrac{z}{2} - \tfrac{3w}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y-3w}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + w\,\text{NH}_3
$$

For **glucose** ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$, MW = 180):
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\text{ThOD}_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{6 \times 32}{180} = \mathbf{1.067 \; \text{g O}_2/\text{g glucose}}
$$

For **acetate** ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$, MW = 60):
$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
$$
\text{ThOD}_{\text{acetate}} = \frac{2 \times 32}{60} = \mathbf{1.067 \; \text{g O}_2/\text{g acetate}}
$$

### B. Nitrification Stoichiometry
$$
\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$
The stoichiometric oxygen demand = **4.57 g O₂/g N-NH₃** (Nitrosomonas + Nitrobacter combined).

## 2.2 Why is Pure Stoichiometry Insufficient?

The ThOD represents the **maximum possible** oxygen demand assuming **complete oxidation to $\text{CO}_2$**. In a real bioreactor, a fraction of the substrate is *diverted* into new cell mass (synthesis), not oxidized. This reduces the *actual* oxygen demand below ThOD. KTU therefore uses empirical **yield + decay coefficients**.

## 2.3 The Two Reference Frameworks

### (i) McKinney's Framework (Substrate-Based)
$$
\text{O}_2 = a' \cdot S_r \cdot Q + b' \cdot V \cdot X
$$
- $a'$ = oxygen used per unit substrate removed (mg O₂/mg BOD)
- $b'$ = endogenous oxygen uptake rate (mg O₂/mg VSS·d)
- $S_r$ = substrate removed (mg/L BOD)
- $Q$ = flow (m³/d)
- $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³)
- $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L)

### (ii) Eckenfelder's Framework (SRT-Based — more common for KTU design)
$$
\text{O}_2 \;(\text{kg/d}) = a \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) + b \cdot V \cdot X
$$
- $a \approx 0.40 - 0.60$ kg O₂/kg BOD removed
- $b \approx 0.04 - 0.10$ kg O₂/kg VSS·d (at 20 °C, corrected via $\theta = 1.04$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction that earns marks:** In Eckenfelder's form, $a$ is the *true oxygen coefficient* (lower than unity because part of the substrate becomes biomass), while in McKinney's $a'$ often equals the ThOD. KTU examiner will test this with a numerical problem.

## 2.4 Temperature Correction (Arrhenius-Type)
Any rate constant or $b$-coefficient at temperature $T$ is corrected from 20 °C as:
$$
k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)} \qquad \text{with } \theta \approx \mathbf{1.04}
$$

## 2.5 The Oxygen Transfer Equation (Clean Water vs. Process Water)

The **volumetric oxygen transfer rate** in the aeration tank is:
$$
\text{OTR} = K_L a \, (C_s^* - C_L)
$$
- $K_L a$ = overall mass-transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $C_s^*$ = saturation DO at field conditions (mg/L)
- $C_L$ = operating DO in bulk liquid (mg/L)

To account for the **dirty process water** and **non-ideal geometry**, the **Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate (SOTR)** is converted to the **Actual Oxygen Transfer Rate (AOTR)** required by the diffusers/blowers:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{AOTR} = \text{SOTR} \cdot \left[\frac{\alpha \cdot \left(\beta \cdot C_{s,20} - C_L\right)}{C_{s,20}}\right] \cdot 1.024^{(T-20)}\;}
$$
- $\alpha = K_L a_{\text{waste}} / K_L a_{\text{clean}}$ (typically **0.6 – 0.9**)
- $\beta = C_{s,\text{waste}} / C_{s,\text{clean}}$ (typically **0.9 – 0.99**)
- $C_{s,20}$ = 9.17 mg/L (standard clean-water saturation at 20 °C, sea level)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{O}_2 = a Q (S_0 - S_e) + b V X$ | $a$ (kg O₂/kg BOD), $Q$ (m³/d), $S$ (kg/m³), $b$ (kg O₂/kg VSS·d), $V$ (m³), $X$ (kg/m³) | Eckenfelder design |
| 2 | $\text{O}_2^{(\text{N})} = 4.57 \, Q \, [\text{NH}_3\text{-N oxidized}]$ | $Q$ (m³/d), N in mg/L | Nitrification demand |
| 3 | $\text{OUR} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2^{(\text{total})}}{V \cdot 24}$ | mg O₂/L·h | Respirometry, control |
| 4 | $\text{SOTR} = \text{AOTR} \cdot \dfrac{C_{s,20}}{\alpha(\beta C_{s,20} - C_L)} \cdot 1.024^{(20-T)}$ | kg O₂/h | Blower sizing |
| 5 | $k_T = k_{20} \, \theta^{(T-20)}$ | $T$ in °C, $\theta = 1.04$ | Temperature correction |
| 6 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{\text{MLSS settleability (mL/L)} \cdot 1000}{\text{MLSS (mg/L)}}$ | mL/g | Sludge settleability |
| 7 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | d | Mean Cell Residence Time |
| 8 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X}$ | d⁻¹ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio |
| 9 | $C_{s,T} = C_{s,20} \cdot 1.024^{(20-T)}$ | mg/L | DO saturation correction |
| 10 | $\text{OTE} = \dfrac{\text{OTR}_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{O}_2 \text{ input rate})}$ | dimensionless | Aerator efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the backbone of **blower selection**, **diffuser specification**, and **energy audits** in municipal STPs. Modern WWTPs (e.g., the 2024 KTU reference plant at Alappuzha) use real-time **OUR probes** to dynamically modulate blower output, saving up to **30 %** of aeration energy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD) for a Generic Substrate

Consider the complete oxidation reaction of an organic compound $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$:

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4} - \tfrac{z}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 1 — Atom Balance on Carbon:**
Atoms of C in substrate = $x$ → must produce $x$ moles $\text{CO}_2$. ✔

**Step 2 — Atom Balance on Hydrogen:**
H atoms on left = $y$ → produce $\frac{y}{2}$ moles $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. ✔

**Step 3 — Atom Balance on Oxygen:**
Right side O atoms = $2x + \frac{y}{2}$.
Left side O atoms = $z + 2 \nu$, where $\nu$ is the moles of $\text{O}_2$ required.
Setting them equal:
$$
2x + \frac{y}{2} = z + 2\nu
$$
Solving for $\nu$:
$$
\nu = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}
$$
Hence the ThOD (in g $\text{O}_2$/g substrate):
$$
\boxed{\;\text{ThOD} = \frac{\left(x + \tfrac{y}{4} - \tfrac{z}{2}\right) \times 32}{12x + y + 16z}\;}
$$

> **Verification for glucose** ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$, $x=6$, $y=12$, $z=6$):
> $$\text{ThOD} = \frac{(6 + 3 - 3) \times 32}{72 + 12 + 96} = \frac{6 \times 32}{180} = \frac{192}{180} = 1.067 \text{ g/g} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nitrification Oxygen Demand = 4.57 g O₂/g N

**Step 1 — Write the half-reaction of nitrification (coupled):**
$$
\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to gram basis:**
- 1 mole of $\text{NH}_4^+$-N = 14 g of N
- 2 moles of $\text{O}_2$ = $2 \times 32 = 64$ g of $\text{O}_2$

**Step 3 — Per gram of N oxidized:**
$$
\frac{64 \text{ g O}_2}{14 \text{ g N}} = \mathbf{4.57 \; g\,O_2/g\,N}
$$

**Step 4 — Include cell synthesis correction (Lawrence & McCarty, 1970):**
Nitrifiers also assimilate a small fraction of N into biomass, but the synthesis oxygen demand is *negative* (frees oxygen). Net correction yields **4.33 – 4.57 g O₂/g N** depending on sludge age. KTU accepts **4.57** as the standard value.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Eckenfelder Oxygen Equation

Starting from a steady-state **mass balance on substrate** around a CSTR (Completely Stirred Tank Reactor):
$$
Q S_0 = Q S_e + \dfrac{\mu_{\max} S_e}{K_s + S_e} \cdot \frac{V \cdot X}{Y}
$$
Solving for $V$ and the new biomass produced per day $P_x$:
$$
P_x = Y Q (S_0 - S_e) - k_d V X
$$
where $Y$ = true yield, $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient.

**Oxygen balance (carbonaceous part):**
- Oxygen for substrate oxidation = substrate removed *minus* oxygen locked in cells.
- Empirical coefficient $a$ absorbs this:
$$
a = (1 - Y_{\text{obs}}) \cdot 1.42
$$
where $Y_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ is the *observed* yield.

- Oxygen for endogenous decay:
$$
b = k_d \cdot 1.42
$$

**Final form:**
$$
\boxed{\;\text{O}_2 \;(\text{kg O}_2/\text{d}) = 1.42 \, Q \, Y_{\text{obs}} (S_0 - S_e) + 1.42 \, k_d \, V \, X\;}
$$
or equivalently the Eckenfelder form:
$$
\text{O}_2 = a \, Q \, (S_0 - S_e) + b \, V \, X
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A municipal WWTP treats $Q = 50{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ with influent BOD $S_0 = 250 \; \text{mg/L}$, effluent BOD $S_e = 20 \; \text{mg/L}$, MLVSS $X = 2500 \; \text{mg/L}$, SRT $\theta_c = 10$ d, aeration tank volume $V = 7{,}500 \; \text{m}^3$. Coefficients: $a = 0.5$, $b = 0.07$ d⁻¹. Influent $\text{NH}_3$-N = 30 mg/L, 90 % nitrified. Temperature = 25 °C. Find the **total oxygen requirement** and the **SOTR** required if $\alpha = 0.85$, $\beta = 0.95$, $C_L = 2.0$ mg/L.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Carbonaceous oxygen demand:**
$$
\text{O}_2^{(C)} = 0.5 \times 50{,}000 \times (250-20)\times 10^{-3} + 0.07 \times 7{,}500 \times 2500 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
= 0.5 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.230 + 0.07 \times 7{,}500 \times 2.5
$$
$$
= 5{,}750 + 1{,}312.5 = \mathbf{7{,}062.5 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

**Step 2 — Nitrification oxygen demand:**
$$
\text{O}_2^{(N)} = 4.57 \times 50{,}000 \times 30 \times 0.90 \times 10^{-3} = 4.57 \times 1{,}350 = \mathbf{6{,}169.5 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total daily oxygen demand:**
$$
\text{TOD} = 7{,}062.5 + 6{,}169.5 = \mathbf{13{,}232 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

**Step 4 — Temperature-corrected DO saturation:**
$$
C_{s,T} = 9.17 \times 1.024^{(20-25)} = 9.17 \times 0.886 = \mathbf{8.13 \; \text{mg/L}}
$$

Wait — the temperature is 25 °C > 20 °C, so:
$$
C_{s,T} = 9.17 \times 1.024^{(20-25)} = 9.17 \times 1.024^{-5} = 9.17 / 1.126 = \mathbf{8.13 \; \text{mg/L}}
$$

**Step 5 — SOTR required (field demand → standard conditions):**
$$
\text{SOTR} = \text{AOTR} \times \frac{C_{s,20}}{\alpha(\beta C_{s,20} - C_L)} \times 1.024^{(20-T)}
$$
$$
= 13{,}232 \times \frac{9.17}{0.85(0.95 \times 9.17 - 2.0)} \times 1.024^{-5}
$$
$$
= 13{,}232 \times \frac{9.17}{0.85 \times 6.71} \times 0.886
$$
$$
= 13{,}232 \times \frac{9.17}{5.704} \times 0.886
$$
$$
= 13{,}232 \times 1.608 \times 0.886 = \mathbf{18{,}854 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

**Step 6 — Blower power sizing (for completeness):**
Assuming a fine-bubble diffuser with **SOTE = 30 %** at field conditions:
$$
\text{Air flow} = \frac{18{,}854}{0.30 \times 0.232 \times 1.429 \times 1000} \approx \mathbf{190 \; Nm}^3/\text{min} \text{ (air)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Insight for Students:** The field demand is **7,062.5 kg/d** for carbonaceous + **6,169.5 kg/d** for nitrification. The *carbonaceous* fraction dominates slightly because of the high BOD removal. The SOTR (18,854 kg/d) is ~43 % greater than the field demand, showing the dramatic influence of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ on blower sizing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Oxygen Demand at Different F/M Ratios

| F/M (d⁻¹) | Mode | Carbonaceous $\text{O}_2$ | Nitrification $\text{O}_2$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0.05 – 0.15** | Extended Aeration | Low per unit V | High (long SRT supports nitrifiers) | High power per m³ treated |
| **0.20 – 0.40** | Conventional ASP | Moderate | Partial (need separate stage) | Most common |
| **0.40 – 1.50** | High-Rate ASP | High | Suppressed (washout of nitrifiers) | Compact, low SRT |
| **> 1.5** | Ultra-High-Rate | Very High | None | Poor effluent quality |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Oxygen Mass-Transfer Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Blower Air Supply] --> B1[Fine-Bubble Diffuser Grid]
    B1 --> C1[Liquid-Gas Interface]
    C1 --> D1[Volumetric OTR via Kla]
    D1 --> E1[Oxygen Sinks in Tank]
    E1 --> F1[Carbonaceous Oxidation of BOD]
    E1 --> G1[Nitrification of NH3]
    E1 --> H1[Endogenous Decay of VSS]
    F1 --> I1[Degraded CO2 and H2O]
    G1 --> J1[NO3 minus Production]
    H1 --> K1[Sludge Mass Reduction]
    L1[DO Probe Feedback] --> M1[PLC Controller]
    M1 --> A1
    N1[Influent BOD and NH3] --> E1
```

## 4.2 Oxygen Demand Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Total Oxygen Demand TOD] --> R2[Carbonaceous Demand]
    R1 --> R3[Nitrification Demand]
    R1 --> R4[Endogenous Decay Demand]
    R2 --> S1[a Q times S0 minus Se]
    R2 --> S2[Eckenfelder Synthesis Fraction]
    R3 --> T1[4.57 g O2 per g N Oxidized]
    R3 --> T2[Sludge Age greater than 5d for Nitrifiers]
    R4 --> U1[b V X at 20 deg C]
    R4 --> U2[1.04 Temperature Coefficient]
```

## 4.3 Aeration Control Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Setpoint DO 2 mg per L] --> B2[Comparator]
    C2[Actual DO from Probe] --> B2
    B2 --> D2[PID Controller]
    D2 --> E2[VFD to Blower]
    E2 --> F2[Air Flow to Tank]
    F2 --> G2[Change in Actual DO]
    G2 --> C2
```

## 4.4 Aerobic Process Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[Raw Sewage Influent] --> P1[Primary Clarifier]
    P1 --> P2[Activated Sludge Aeration Tank]
    P2 --> P3[Secondary Clarifier]
    P2 --> A3[Diffused Air from Blower Room]
    A3 --> P2
    P3 --> P4[Treated Effluent Out]
    P3 --> P5[Return Activated Sludge RAS to P2]
    P3 --> P6[Waste Activated Sludge WAS to Sludge Thickener]
    P2 --> M2[DO Probe 2 mg per L]
    M2 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three Mermaid diagrams show, respectively: (1) the **physical oxygen transfer pathway** from blower to biomass, (2) the **decomposition of total oxygen demand** into its three academic sub-components, and (3) the **closed-loop control** that KTU 2024 highlights as the modern energy-efficient aeration philosophy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Distinguish between Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD) and Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD). Why does BOD₅ never equal ThOD in a 5-day test?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ThOD | BOD₅ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Stoichiometric O₂ for **complete** chemical oxidation to $\text{CO}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | O₂ consumed by **microorganisms** in 5 days at 20 °C |
| Time | Instantaneous (theoretical) | 5 days (incomplete) |
| Value for glucose | **1.067 g O₂/g** | **0.65 – 0.75 g O₂/g** |
| Includes nitrification? | No (only carbonaceous) | Negligible (nitrifiers grow slowly) |

BOD₅ < ThOD because: (i) only 60 – 70 % of the biodegradable organic matter is oxidized in 5 days (the remainder is slow-degrading), (ii) ~10 – 15 % of substrate is **assimilated into cell mass** (not oxidized), and (iii) the test does not reach the carbonaceous plateau (≈ 20 d) within 5 days.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the stoichiometric oxygen demand for complete nitrification of ammonia to nitrate. Justify the value 4.57 g O₂/g N with a balanced reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\text{NH}_4^+ + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_3^- + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- Moles of $\text{O}_2$ = 2 → Mass = $2 \times 32 = 64$ g
- Moles of N = 1 → Mass = 14 g
- Demand = $64/14 = \mathbf{4.57 \; g\,O_2/g\,N}$

This value assumes the **net oxygen demand after deducting** the oxygen credit for cell synthesis. The autotrophic yield is ~0.15 g VSS/g N oxidized, and oxidation of that biomass further consumes ~0.20 g O₂/g N, leaving a net 4.33 – 4.57 g O₂/g N. KTU accepts 4.57 as standard.

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern (ESE Module 2):** Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **one full question** (either Q.A or Q.B).

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** A completely mixed activated sludge plant treats a flow of $30{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ with an influent BOD of **300 mg/L** and an effluent BOD of **20 mg/L**. The aeration tank volume is $4{,}000 \; \text{m}^3$ and the MLVSS is $2{,}500 \; \text{mg/L}$. Using the Eckenfelder equation with $a = 0.50$ and $b = 0.08 \; \text{d}^{-1}$ at 20 °C, calculate the **total carbonaceous oxygen requirement** in kg/d. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the wastewater additionally contains **30 mg/L NH₃-N** and 95 % is nitrified, calculate the **nitrification oxygen demand** and the **combined total oxygen demand**. Also, if the plant operates at **28 °C** with $\alpha = 0.80$, $\beta = 0.95$, $C_L = 2 \; \text{mg/L}$, and the aeration system has an **SOTE of 25 %**, estimate the **SOTR (kg O₂/d)** and the **required airflow rate (Nm³/min)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Carbonaceous Oxygen Demand:**

Given:
- $Q = 30{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 - S_e = 300 - 20 = 280 \; \text{mg/L} = 0.280 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $V = 4{,}000 \; \text{m}^3$
- $X = 2{,}500 \; \text{mg/L} = 2.5 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $a = 0.50$, $b = 0.08 \; \text{d}^{-1}$

Eckenfelder equation:
$$
\text{O}_2^{(C)} = a Q (S_0 - S_e) + b V X
$$

Substrate-removal term:
$$
0.50 \times 30{,}000 \times 0.280 = 0.50 \times 8{,}400 = 4{,}200 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

Endogenous term:
$$
0.08 \times 4{,}000 \times 2.5 = 0.08 \times 10{,}000 = 800 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{O}_2^{(C)} = 4{,}200 + 800 = \mathbf{5{,}000 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Nitrification + Total + SOTR + Air Flow:**

Nitrification oxygen demand:
$$
\text{O}_2^{(N)} = 4.57 \times Q \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}] \times f_{\text{nit}}
$$
$$
= 4.57 \times 30{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95
$$
$$
= 4.57 \times 30{,}000 \times 0.0285 = \mathbf{3{,}907.4 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

Total oxygen demand (field):
$$
\text{AOTR}_{\text{field}} = 5{,}000 + 3{,}907.4 = \mathbf{8{,}907.4 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

Temperature correction to standard 20 °C:
$$
C_{s,28} = 9.17 \times 1.024^{(20-28)} = 9.17 \times 1.024^{-8} = 9.17 / 1.210 = 7.58 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

But for SOTR calculation we need the **clean water saturation** at 20 °C and the operating DO:
$$
C_{s,20} = 9.17 \; \text{mg/L}, \quad C_L = 2.0 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

$$
\text{SOTR} = \text{AOTR} \times \frac{C_{s,20}}{\alpha(\beta C_{s,20} - C_L)} \times 1.024^{(20-T)}
$$

$$
= 8{,}907.4 \times \frac{9.17}{0.80(0.95 \times 9.17 - 2.0)} \times 1.024^{-8}
$$

$$
= 8{,}907.4 \times \frac{9.17}{0.80 \times 6.71} \times 0.826
$$

$$
= 8{,}907.4 \times \frac{9.17}{5.368} \times 0.826
$$

$$
= 8{,}907.4 \times 1.708 \times 0.826 = \mathbf{12{,}567 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

Air flow rate:
- Air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass; air density = 1.293 kg/m³; pure O₂ = 1.429 kg/m³
- Mass of O₂ per Nm³ of air = $0.232 \times 1.293 = 0.300 \; \text{kg/Nm}^3$ (at 0 °C, 1 atm)

With SOTE = 25 %:
$$
\text{Air} = \frac{12{,}567}{0.25 \times 0.300} = \frac{12{,}567}{0.075} = 167{,}560 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{d}
$$

$$
= \frac{167{,}560}{1440} = \mathbf{116.4 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **Eckenfelder oxygen requirement equation** starting from a mass balance on substrate and biomass in a CSTR. Clearly state the meaning and typical range of the empirical coefficients $a$ and $b$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A high-rate activated sludge unit treats $Q = 10{,}000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ with $S_0 = 400$ mg/L, $S_e = 30$ mg/L, $X = 4{,}000$ mg/L, $V = 1{,}500 \; \text{m}^3$, $T = 22 \; °\text{C}$, $\theta_c = 4$ d. Using $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06 \; \text{d}^{-1}$, calculate: (i) the **observed yield**, (ii) the **oxygen used for synthesis**, and (iii) the **endogenous oxygen demand**, and hence the **total oxygen requirement**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Eckenfelder Equation:**

*(See Section 3.3 above for the full derivation.)*

In summary:

1. **Substrate mass balance** on a CSTR at steady state:
   $$Q S_0 = Q S_e + \dfrac{\mu X V}{Y}$$
2. **Biomass balance** with endogenous decay:
   $$P_x = Y Q (S_0 - S_e) - k_d X V$$
3. **Observed yield** (fraction of substrate truly oxidized):
   $$Y_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$
4. **Oxygen for synthesis** = (1.42) × mass of substrate oxidized:
   $$\text{O}_2^{\text{syn}} = 1.42 \, Y_{\text{obs}} \, Q \, (S_0 - S_e)$$
5. **Oxygen for endogenous respiration** = (1.42) × mass decayed:
   $$\text{O}_2^{\text{end}} = 1.42 \, k_d \, X \, V$$
6. **Combine** into the **Eckenfelder form**:
   $$\text{O}_2 = a \, Q \, (S_0 - S_e) + b \, X \, V$$
   where $a = 1.42 Y_{\text{obs}} \approx 0.4 - 0.6$ and $b = 1.42 k_d \approx 0.05 - 0.10 \; \text{d}^{-1}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

(i) **Observed yield:**
$$
Y_{\text{obs}} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 4} = \frac{0.6}{1.24} = \mathbf{0.484}
$$

(ii) **Oxygen for synthesis:**
$$
\text{O}_2^{\text{syn}} = 1.42 \times Y_{\text{obs}} \times Q \times (S_0 - S_e)
$$
$$
= 1.42 \times 0.484 \times 10{,}000 \times (400-30) \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
= 1.42 \times 0.484 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.370
$$
$$
= 1.42 \times 1{,}790.8 = \mathbf{2{,}543 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

(iii) **Endogenous oxygen demand:**
$$
\text{O}_2^{\text{end}} = 1.42 \times k_d \times X \times V
$$
$$
= 1.42 \times 0.06 \times 4{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \times 1{,}500
$$
$$
= 1.42 \times 0.06 \times 6{,}000 = \mathbf{510.7 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

**Total oxygen demand:**
$$
\text{TOD} = 2{,}543 + 510.7 = \mathbf{3{,}053.7 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

At $T = 22$ °C (close to 20 °C, correction factor ≈ 1.08), the field demand is approximately:
$$
\text{AOTR} \approx 3{,}054 \times 1.08 \approx \mathbf{3{,}298 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg/L → kg/m³** by multiplying by $10^{-3}$. A common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing $a$ and $a'$** in the McKinney vs. Eckenfelder forms. KTU explicitly tests this in 2-mark sub-parts.
> 3. **Using 4.57 g O₂/g N incorrectly** for partial nitrification. The 0.95 or 0.90 efficiency factor **must be multiplied**, not the stoichiometric constant.
> 4. **Wrong sign in temperature correction.** When $T > 20$ °C, $C_{s,T} < C_{s,20}$ (warmer water holds less O₂). This is a classic trick question.
> 5. **Not distinguishing AOTR from SOTR.** Field design is in AOTR; blower selection is in SOTR. KTU will specifically ask the SOTR if the question includes $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
> 6. **Ignoring the $1.42$ factor** (oxygen demand per g of cell mass). This represents the O₂ needed to oxidize 1 g of $\text{C}_5\text{H}_7\text{NO}_2$ to $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and $\text{NH}_3$.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **ThOD** is *theoretical* (stoichiometric), **BOD** is *biological* (5-day), **COD** is *chemical* (2–3 h).
- ✅ **BOD₅ ≈ 0.65 – 0.75 × ThOD** for typical municipal sewage; **BOD₅ ≈ 0.50 – 0.65 × COD**.
- ✅ **Eckenfelder form** (most tested): $\text{O}_2 = a Q (S_0 - S_e) + b V X$.
- ✅ **Coefficient ranges at 20 °C:** $a = 0.40 - 0.60$, $b = 0.04 - 0.10 \; \text{d}^{-1}$.
- ✅ **1.42 g O₂/g VSS** is the conversion factor for complete cell oxidation.
- ✅ **Nitrification O₂ = 4.57 g O₂/g NH₃-N oxidized** (memorize this number).
- ✅ **SRT > 5 d** is required to sustain nitrifiers at 20 °C; > 10 d at 10 °C.
- ✅ **AOTR = field demand**, **SOTR = standard (20 °C, clean water) demand**.
- ✅ **Conversion uses $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $1.024^{(T-20)}$** with $C_{s,20} = 9.17$ mg/L.
- ✅ **Typical operating DO** = 1.5 – 2.0 mg/L; critical DO = 0.5 – 1.0 mg/L.
- ✅ **OUR** (Oxygen Uptake Rate) = TOD / (V × 24) in mg/L·h; used in respirometry.
- ✅ **Temperature correction** of $b$: $b_T = b_{20} \cdot 1.04^{(T-20)}$.
- ✅ **Energy audit rule of thumb:** Aeration consumes **50 – 60 %** of total plant electrical energy; optimizing $\text{O}_2$ delivery is the single biggest cost-saver.
- ✅ **DO control loop:** Probe → PLC → VFD → Blower → Diffuser → Tank. Modern WWTPs use **ammonia-NO₃ based aeration control** (ABAC) to save 20 – 30 % energy.
- ✅ **Common KTU trick:** When the question gives "influent TKN" instead of "influent NH₃-N", you must first apply an **alkalinity and biodegradable-TKN split** before computing nitrification demand.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Sludge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Production of Sludge # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sludge** is the semi-solid residual mass generated as an inevitable by-product during the physical, chemical, and biological unit operations of a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP). It is composed of the organic and inorganic solids originally present in the influent wastewater, augmented by the **microbial biomass (biomass solids)** synthesized during biological treatment, plus any chemical precipitates formed during tertiary or advanced treatment. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (OECHT725 – Advanced Wastewater Treatment, Module 2), the term *Production of Sludge* specifically refers to the **quantitative estimation, mass-balance accounting, and origin classification** of the solid residues produced across each stage of a treatment train. Sludge is *not waste* in the engineering sense — it is a *secondary resource stream* that must be quantified before it can be thickened, stabilized, dewatered, and ultimately disposed of or reused. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim importance):** > Sludge is a mixture of solids and liquids, where the solid fraction ranges typically from **0.25% to 12%** by weight, depending on the unit operation from which it is withdrawn (primary, secondary, or tertiary). ## 1.2 Classification of Sludge by Origin | Source Unit Operation | Sludge Type | Key Characteristic | |---|---|---| | Primary Sedimentation Tank | **Primary Sludge (PS)** | Grey, slimy, high organic content (≈ **60–80% VS/TS**), high putrescibility | | Secondary Clarifier (after ASP) | **Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)** / Secondary Sludge | Brown, flocculent, high microbial content | | Trickling Filter / RBC | **Biological Sludge / Humus Sludge** | Dark brown, well-stabilized partially | | Chemical Precipitation (Tertiary) | **Chemical / Tertiary Sludge** | Inorganic-rich, low VS content, voluminous | | Membrane Bioreactor (MBR) | **Concentrated Biological Sludge** | Very high MLSS (8,000–15,000 mg/L) | | DAF unit | **Float Sludge** | Light, high moisture | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Filter" Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Sieve Story** > > Imagine you pour a bowl of vegetable soup through a fine kitchen sieve. > - The **clear broth that passes through** is the *treated effluent* (clean water). > - The **wet fibrous residue trapped on the sieve** is exactly what *sludge* is — a wet, semi-solid mass containing everything that was too big or too heavy to remain dissolved in the liquid phase. > > Now extend this analogy to a municipal WWTP: > - The **influent sewage** is the soup (containing human waste, food particles, oils, grit, microbes, dissolved salts). > - The **sieves of various mesh sizes** are the screens, grit chambers, primary clarifiers, aeration tanks, and secondary clarifiers. > - The **wet residue collected on the sieves** is sludge. > > Just as a chef would never throw the strained pulp without measuring it (because it represents nutrients and mass), a wastewater engineer must **measure, mass-balance, and account for the sludge** before designing its handling train. ## 1.4 Why Sludge Production Matters in Advanced Treatment 1. **Design basis for sludge handling units** — thickeners, digesters, dewatering systems are sized directly from the **daily mass of sludge** produced. 2. **Cost driver** — sludge handling constitutes **50–60% of the total operating cost** of a municipal WWTP. 3. **Environmental liability** — improper sludge disposal leads to secondary pollution (pathogens, heavy metals, N & P leaching). 4. **Energy and resource recovery** — modern plants treat sludge as a *resource* (biogas, struvite, biosolids for agriculture). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Highlight:** > Specific sludge production is typically expressed in **kg dry solids (DS) per m³ of wastewater treated**, or equivalently in **kg DS per kg BOD removed**. The design value for an activated sludge plant ranges from **0.3 to 0.8 kg DS/kg BOD₅ removed**, depending on sludge age and influent characteristics. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sludge Mass Balance — The Governing Philosophy The **Law of Conservation of Mass** is the philosophical and mathematical backbone of every sludge production calculation. For any control volume drawn around a treatment unit: $$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$ At **steady state** (which is the standard design assumption for KTU problems), Accumulation = 0, giving: $$\text{Inflow} + \text{Generation} = \text{Outflow} + \text{Consumption}$$ Applied to solids in an aeration tank, this yields the celebrated **biomass balance equation** that drives all sludge production calculations. ## 2.2 The Two Pillars of Sludge Production ### Pillar 1 — Sludge from Primary Clarification (Physical) Primary sludge arises from the **gravitational settling** of suspended solids in the influent. Its mass is purely a function of the **influent TSS** and the **removal efficiency (ηₚ)** of the primary clarifier: $$P_{X,\text{primary}} = Q \cdot \text{TSS}_0 \cdot \eta_P$$ - $Q$ = average daily flow, **m³/d** - $\text{TSS}_0$ = influent total suspended solids, **kg/m³** (or g/m³) - $\eta_P$ = primary clarifier TSS removal efficiency, typically **0.50 to 0.70** (50–70%) ### Pillar 2 — Sludge from Biological Treatment (Activated Sludge) This is the **theoretically rich** part. In a Conventional Activated Sludge Process (ASP), sludge is produced as a result of two competing biological phenomena: 1. **Biomass synthesis (growth)** — microbes convert a fraction of the substrate into new cells. 2. **Endogenous decay** — microbes consume their own cellular mass for maintenance energy. The **net observed yield** ($Y_{obs}$) captures this balance: $$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$ where: - $Y$ = true yield coefficient, **kg VSS/kg BOD₅** (typically 0.4–0.6 for domestic wastewater) - $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient, **d⁻¹** (typically 0.04–0.10 d⁻¹ at 20 °C) - $\theta_c$ = mean cell residence time / sludge age / SRT, **days** The **biological sludge production** is then: $$P_{X,\text{bio}} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$$ or, expanded: $$P_{X,\text{bio}} = \dfrac{Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. These equations appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on sludge production.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Range / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{X,\text{primary}} = Q \cdot \text{TSS}_0 \cdot \eta_P$ | Primary sludge (dry solids) | $\eta_P$ = 0.5–0.7 | | 2 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | 0.2–0.5 kg/kg | | 3 | $P_{X,\text{bio}} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ | Biological sludge (dry solids) | kg DS/d | | 4 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{P_{X,\text{bio}}}$ | Sludge age (SRT) | 3–30 days | | 5 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | 0.05–0.5 d⁻¹ | | 6 | $V_{\text{sludge}} = \dfrac{P_{X,\text{bio}}}{X \cdot \rho_s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - w}$ | Sludge volume (with moisture $w$) | m³/d | | 7 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{\text{MLSS settled volume (mL/L)} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS (mg/L)}}$ | Sludge Volume Index | 50–150 mL/g (good) | | 8 | $P_{X,\text{total}} = P_{X,\text{primary}} + P_{X,\text{bio}} + P_{X,\text{chem}}$ | Total sludge mass (overall plant) | kg DS/d | | 9 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d}$ | SRT–growth rate relationship | days | | 10 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{\theta (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$ | Steady-state MLSS in aeration tank | mg/L | **Notation conventions used in KTU solutions:** - $S_0$ = influent soluble BOD₅, **mg/L** - $S$ = effluent soluble BOD₅, **mg/L** - $X$ = mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS), **mg/L** - $V$ = aeration tank volume, **m³** - $\theta$ = hydraulic retention time, **d** = $V/Q$ - $w$ = moisture content of sludge (fraction), typically **0.94 to 0.99** ## 2.4 Physical Significance of Key Parameters - **$Y$ (True Yield)** — represents the *theoretical maximum* biomass that could be produced per kg substrate consumed if there were *no* decay. It is determined in laboratory chemostat studies. - **$k_d$ (Decay Coefficient)** — represents the rate at which biomass is *lost* due to endogenous respiration, predation, lysis, and death. Higher $k_d$ → less net sludge. - **$\theta_c$ (Sludge Age / SRT)** — the *master design variable* in biological treatment. Increasing $\theta_c$: - Decreases $Y_{obs}$ (less net sludge) ✓ - Increases MLSS $X$ (more biomass in reactor) ✓ - Improves nitrification ✓ - Requires larger aeration tank ✓ > [!TIP] > **Engineering utility:** A plant designer can control *sludge production* simply by manipulating $\theta_c$. Doubling $\theta_c$ reduces $Y_{obs}$ by roughly 30–50%, which directly reduces downstream digester and dewatering loads. ## 2.5 Total Sludge Production — The Plant-Level Mass Balance The **total dry solids (DS)** produced per day by an entire WWTP is the algebraic sum of contributions from each unit: $$P_{X,\text{total}} = P_{X,\text{primary}} + P_{X,\text{bio}} + P_{X,\text{chem}}$$ where $P_{X,\text{chem}}$ from chemical precipitation (e.g., alum, FeCl₃, lime) is: $$P_{X,\text{chem}} = Q \cdot [\text{Chemical dose}] \cdot [\text{Stoichiometric factor}]$$ For phosphorus precipitation with alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O), the stoichiometric factor is ≈ **1.0 kg alum/kg P removed**, generating an additional **≈ 5.4 kg DS/kg P** as chemical sludge. ## 2.6 The Effect of Sludge Age on Sludge Volume vs. Mass — A Subtle Distinction > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** A higher $\theta_c$ *reduces sludge mass* (kg DS/d) but *increases total sludge volume* (m³/d) because the mixed liquor is more dilute in the wasted stream relative to thickened sludge. Always check whether the question asks for *mass* or *volume*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Biological Sludge Production Equation This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. We will derive the equation $P_{X,\text{bio}} = \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ from first principles using a steady-state mass balance on a Completely Mixed Activated Sludge (CMAS) reactor followed by a secondary clarifier. ### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume Draw a control boundary around the **aeration tank** of volume $V$ (m³). Let: - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, m³/d - $X$ = biomass concentration in the tank (= MLVSS), mg/L - $X_e$ = biomass concentration in the effluent leaving the secondary clarifier - $S_0$, $S$ = substrate (BOD₅) concentrations in influent and effluent, mg/L - $Y$ = true yield coefficient - $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient, d⁻¹ - $\mu$ = specific growth rate, d⁻¹ ### Step 2 — Mass Balance on Biomass (Steady State) $$ \begin{aligned} V \frac{dX}{dt} &= Q X_0 - Q X_e + V (r_g - r_d) \\[4pt] 0 &= 0 - Q X_e + V \left( \mu X - k_d X \right) \quad \text{(influent is biomass-free, } X_0 \approx 0) \\[4pt] Q X_e &= V X \left( \mu - k_d \right) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Express Sludge Wasted ($P_X$) The **total biomass leaving the system per day** is the sum of biomass in the effluent plus the **wasted sludge** withdrawn deliberately from the return line. The *net sludge production* is the wasted portion $P_X$: $$P_X = V X \left( \mu - k_d \right)$$ ### Step 4 — Introduce the Sludge Age $\theta_c$ By definition, $\theta_c$ is the average time a biomass particle spends in the system: $$\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{P_X} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d$$ ### Step 5 — Substituting Back $$ \begin{aligned} P_X &= V X \left( \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d - k_d \right) = \dfrac{V X}{\theta_c} \end{aligned} $$ This shows $P_X$ can be computed from the operating MLSS and SRT alone, **without substrate data**.
