Useful, but substrate-coupled versions are richer. ### Step 6 — Mass Balance on Substrate At steady state, with influent substrate $S_0$ and effluent substrate $S$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= Q(S_0 - S) - V \cdot r_{S,\text{utilization}} \\[4pt] Q(S_0 - S) &= V \cdot \dfrac{\mu X}{Y} \quad \text{(substrate utilization rate via yield)} \\[4pt] V X &= \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S)}{\mu} = Y Q (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta_c \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Combine $$ \begin{aligned} P_X &= \dfrac{V X}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \\[6pt] \boxed{P_X = \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q (S_0 - S)} \end{aligned} $$ where $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ is the **observed (net) yield**. ### Step 8 — Verify Dimensional Consistency - $Y$ : kg biomass / kg BOD - $Q$ : m³/d - $(S_0 - S)$ : kg BOD / m³ (= mg/L × 10⁻⁶ × 10³ = g/m³ = kg/m³) - $k_d \theta_c$ : (d⁻¹)(d) = dimensionless - Therefore $P_X$ has units of (kg/kg)(m³/d)(kg/m³) = **kg/d** ✓ ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem) **Problem Statement (typical KTU ESE Module 2 style):** > A municipal WWTP treats a flow of **$Q = 50,000$ m³/d** with an influent BOD₅ of $S_0 = 250$ mg/L and effluent BOD₅ of $S = 10$ mg/L. The activated sludge system operates at MLSS $X = 3500$ mg/L in an aeration tank of $V = 10,000$ m³. Given $Y = 0.55$ kg VSS/kg BOD₅ and $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, compute: > (a) The sludge age $\theta_c$ and the observed yield $Y_{obs}$. > (b) The biological sludge production $P_{X,\text{bio}}$ in kg VSS/d, and its equivalent dry solids (assume VSS/SS = 0.80). > (c) The volume of waste sludge per day if it is wasted at concentration $X_w = 10,000$ mg/L. ### Solution (a) — Sludge Age and Observed Yield $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_c &= \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) - V X k_d} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{10{,}000 \times 3500 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/m}^3}{0.55 \times 50{,}000 \times (250-10) \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/d} - 10{,}000 \times 3500 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.06 \, \text{kg/d}} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{35{,}000 \, \text{kg}}{0.55 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.240 - 21{,}000} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{35{,}000}{6600 - 2100} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{4500} \approx 7.78 \, \text{days} \end{aligned} $$ $$ Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.55}{1 + 0.06 \times 7.78} = \dfrac{0.55}{1.467} \approx 0.375 \, \text{kg/kg} $$ ### Solution (b) — Sludge Mass $$ P_{X,\text{bio}} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) $$ $$ P_{X,\text{bio}} = 0.375 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.240 = 4500 \, \text{kg VSS/d} $$ Since VSS/SS = 0.80: $$ P_{X,\text{DS}} = \dfrac{4500}{0.80} = 5625 \, \text{kg DS/d} \approx 5.63 \, \text{tonnes DS/day} $$ ### Solution (c) — Sludge Volume $$ Q_w = \dfrac{P_{X,\text{DS}}}{X_w} = \dfrac{5625 \, \text{kg/d}}{10 \, \text{kg/m}^3} = 562.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d} $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check:** A 50,000 m³/d plant producing 5.6 tonnes DS/d of biological sludge translates to ≈ **0.11 kg DS/m³** of wastewater treated, which falls within the standard engineering range of 0.08–0.20 kg DS/m³ for conventional ASP. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sludge Generation Flow Across a WWTP The diagram below maps the origin and routing of sludge through every unit of a typical advanced wastewater treatment plant. ```mermaid flowchart TB Inf["Influent Sewage Q, TSS0, BOD0"] --> Screen["Screening and Grit Removal"] Screen --> PST["Primary Settling Tank"] PST -->|"PS: Primary Sludge 50 to 70 percent TSS removed"| PSthickener["Primary Sludge Thickener"] PST --> Aeration["Aeration Tank MLSS X, V, theta_c"] Aeration -->|"O2 supply, Mixing"| Blower["Blower System"] Aeration --> SST["Secondary Settling Tank"] SST -->|"WAS: Waste Activated Sludge"| WASthick["WAS Thickener"] SST --> Eff["Treated Effluent Disinfected"] WASthick --> AD["Anaerobic Digester"] PSthickener --> AD AD --> Biogas["Biogas CH4, CO2"] AD --> Diges["Digested Sludge Cake 3 to 5 percent DS"] Diges --> Dewater["Dewatering Centrifuge or Belt Press"] Dewater --> Cake["Biosolids Cake 20 to 30 percent DS"] Cake --> Land["Land Application or Landfill"] Biogas --> CHP["Combined Heat and Power CHP Unit"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture of Sludge Mass-Balance Components This block diagram isolates the **logical flow of mass** used in the KTU sludge production calculation, which is what the examiner tests. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Inflow Terms kg DS per d"] --> Sum1(["SUM of Inflow + Generation"]) B["Generation Terms kg DS per d"] --> Sum1 Sum1 --> Sum2(["SUM of Outflow + Consumption"]) C["Outflow Terms Effluent Solids, Wasted Sludge"] --> Sum2 D["Consumption Terms Endogenous Decay"] --> Sum2 Sum1 --> Out["Net Sludge Production Px"] Sum2 --> Out Out --> P1["Primary Sludge Px_primary"] Out --> P2["Secondary Sludge Px_bio"] Out --> P3["Chemical Sludge Px_chem"] P1 --> Total["Px total equals P1 + P2 + P3"] P2 --> Total P3 --> Total Total --> Thick["Sludge Handling Train Thickener Digester Dewatering"] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: How to Compute Sludge Production in KTU Problems ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Read KTU Problem Statement"] --> Q1{"Is primary
clarifier present?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> P1["Compute Px_primary = Q times TSS0 times eta_P"] Q1 -- "No" --> Skip1["Set Px_primary = 0"] P1 --> Q2{"Is biological
treatment specified?"} Skip1 --> Q2 Q2 -- "Yes" --> P2["Compute Px_bio using Yobs equation with SRT"] Q2 -- "No" --> Skip2["Set Px_bio = 0"] P2 --> Q3{"Is chemical
precipitation present?"} Skip2 --> Q3 Q3 -- "Yes" --> P3["Compute Px_chem from stoichiometry"] Q3 -- "No" --> Skip3["Set Px_chem = 0"] P3 --> Sum["Total Px = P1 + P2 + P3"] Skip3 --> Sum Sum --> Final["Report in kg DS per d and m3 per d"] ``` ## 4.4 Effect of Sludge Age $\theta_c$ on Sludge Yield and MLSS — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR LTh["Low theta_c 3 to 5 d Conventional ASP"] --> Yhigh["Yobs high 0.4 to 0.5 kg per kg"] LTh --> Xlow["MLSS low 1500 to 2500 mg per L"] LTh --> Pxhi["Px high Sludge mass high"] HTh["High theta_c 20 to 30 d Extended Aeration"] --> Ylow["Yobs low 0.1 to 0.2 kg per kg"] HTh --> Xhi["MLSS high 4000 to 6000 mg per L"] HTh --> Pxlo["Px low Sludge mass low"] HTh --> Nitrif["Nitrification achieved"] HTh --> Stable["Sludge well stabilized aerobically"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > Define sludge. List the different types of sludge produced in a conventional municipal wastewater treatment plant. **Model Answer:** Sludge is the semi-solid residue separated from the liquid stream during physical, chemical, and biological treatment of wastewater. It contains both organic and inorganic solids along with water (moisture content typically 94–99%). **Types of sludge:** 1. **Primary sludge** — from the primary clarifier, composed of settleable solids; high in organics (60–80% VS/TS). 2. **Secondary sludge / Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)** — biological sludge from the secondary clarifier; rich in microbial biomass. 3. **Tertiary / Chemical sludge** — produced during chemical precipitation (e.g., for phosphorus removal) using alum, lime, or ferric chloride. 4. **Digested sludge** — output of anaerobic/aerobic digestion; stabilized, lower in pathogens and organics. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > What is meant by the *observed yield coefficient* ($Y_{obs}$)? Why is it preferred over the true yield ($Y$) for design purposes? **Model Answer:** The **observed yield coefficient** ($Y_{obs}$) is the net mass of biomass produced per unit mass of substrate consumed, *accounting for endogenous decay* during the sludge age. It is given by: $$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$ It is preferred over the true yield $Y$ in design because: 1. $Y_{obs}$ already accounts for biomass losses due to endogenous decay, predation, and lysis. 2. It directly gives the **net sludge production** when multiplied by substrate removed: $P_{X} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$. 3. It is **operationally measurable** (volatile suspended solids produced per kg BOD removed), unlike $Y$ which is a theoretical chemostat parameter. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 2 Question]** > (a) With the aid of a steady-state mass balance, derive the equation for biological sludge production in a completely mixed activated sludge system. > (7 marks) > (b) A WWTP receives a flow of **$Q = 30{,}000$ m³/d** with $S_0 = 220$ mg/L, $S = 15$ mg/L, and operates at $\theta_c = 10$ d. Given $Y = 0.6$ kg VSS/kg BOD₅ and $k_d = 0.05$ d⁻¹, calculate: (i) the observed yield, (ii) the biological sludge produced per day, and (iii) the equivalent dry solids if VSS/TSS = 0.75. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a) — Derivation:** Step 1: Write biomass balance around the aeration tank at steady state: $$0 = -Q X_e + V(\mu - k_d) X$$ Step 2: Express sludge production $P_X = Q X_e + Q_w X_w$ (effluent + wasting) and recognize that in CMAS, $P_X = V X (\mu - k_d)$. Step 3: Define $\theta_c = V X / P_X = 1/(\mu - k_d)$, hence $\mu = 1/\theta_c + k_d$. Step 4: Write substrate balance at steady state: $$0 = Q(S_0 - S) - V \cdot \dfrac{\mu X}{Y}$$ This gives $V X = Y Q(S_0 - S) \cdot \theta_c / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$. Step 5: Combine with $P_X = V X / \theta_c$: $$\boxed{P_X = \dfrac{Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}}$$ **Model Solution (b) — Numerical:** (i) Observed yield: $$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.05 \times 10} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.5} = 0.40 \text{ kg VSS/kg BOD}_5$$ (ii) Biological sludge (VSS): $$P_{X,\text{bio}} = 0.40 \times 30{,}000 \times (220 - 15) \times 10^{-3} = 0.40 \times 30{,}000 \times 0.205$$ $$P_{X,\text{bio}} = 2460 \text{ kg VSS/day}$$ (iii) Convert to total dry solids: $$P_{X,\text{DS}} = \dfrac{2460}{0.75} = 3280 \text{ kg DS/day} \approx 3.28 \text{ tonnes DS/day}$$ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 2 Question]** > (a) Explain the different sources of sludge production in a WWTP, with typical values of solids concentration and volatile fraction for each. (7 marks) > (b) The primary clarifier of a WWTP removes 65% of the influent TSS of 280 mg/L. The flow is 40,000 m³/d. The activated sludge system downstream produces waste activated sludge of 0.35 kg VSS/kg BOD₅ removed. Influent BOD₅ = 250 mg/L, effluent BOD₅ = 20 mg/L, and VSS/TSS = 0.80. Calculate: (i) the primary sludge mass, (ii) the WAS mass, and (iii) the total daily sludge (dry solids) the plant must handle. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** | Source | TSS (%) | VSS/TSS | Volume per capita (L/d) | |---|---|---|---| | Primary sludge | 4–8 | 0.60–0.80 | 0.5–1.5 | | WAS (secondary) | 0.5–1.5 | 0.70–0.85 | 1.5–3.0 | | Chemical (tertiary) | 1–3 | 0.20–0.40 | 0.1–0.5 | | Digested | 3–6 | 0.30–0.50 | 0.3–0.8 | **Model Solution (b):** (i) Primary sludge: $$P_{X,\text{primary}} = 40{,}000 \times 280 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.65 = 7280 \text{ kg DS/day}$$ (ii) Waste activated sludge (VSS basis): $$P_{X,\text{WAS,VSS}} = 0.35 \times 40{,}000 \times (250 - 20) \times 10^{-3} = 0.35 \times 40{,}000 \times 0.230 = 3220 \text{ kg VSS/day}$$ Converting to DS (using VSS/TSS = 0.80): $$P_{X,\text{WAS,DS}} = \dfrac{3220}{0.80} = 4025 \text{ kg DS/day}$$ (iii) Total sludge mass: $$P_{X,\text{total}} = 7280 + 4025 = 11{,}305 \text{ kg DS/day} \approx 11.3 \text{ tonnes DS/day}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake 1 (most common):** Students forget to convert mg/L to kg/m³ (multiply by 10⁻⁶ × 10³ = × 10⁻³). Always check units before substituting. > - **Mistake 2:** Using $Y$ (true yield) instead of $Y_{obs}$ in numerical calculations. The derivation gives the *true* yield; you must divide by $(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ to get the net observed sludge. > - **Mistake 3:** Confusing VSS with TSS. The question will specify which to report; if not specified, report the value in the units consistent with the yield given. > - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting that chemical sludge from tertiary treatment (alum, FeCl₃) is *additional* — it must be added to the primary + secondary total. > - **Mistake 5 (mark-loss):** Reporting sludge in m³/d without specifying the solids concentration. The examiner expects both *mass* (kg DS/d) and *volume* (m³/d) at a stated consistency. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2: Production of Sludge)** - ☐ **Sludge** = semi-solid residue from physical, chemical, and biological wastewater treatment operations; moisture 94–99%. - ☐ Three principal sources: **Primary (gravitational), Secondary (biological WAS), Tertiary (chemical precipitate).** - ☐ **Master equation:** $P_X = \dfrac{Y Q (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ — memorize and derive it. - ☐ **Observed yield** $Y_{obs}$ = $\dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ — always decreases with increasing SRT. - ☐ **Sludge age** $\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{P_X}$ — the master design lever; higher $\theta_c$ → less sludge but more MLSS. - ☐ **F/M ratio** = $\dfrac{Q S_0}{V X}$ — inversely related to $\theta_c$ for a given influent. - ☐ **Total DS/d** = $P_{X,\text{primary}} + P_{X,\text{bio}} + P_{X,\text{chem}}$. - ☐ **Primary sludge** = $Q \times \text{TSS}_0 \times \eta_P$ with $\eta_P = 0.5 - 0.7$. - ☐ **Chemical sludge** for P removal ≈ **5.4 kg DS/kg P** removed using alum. - ☐ **Unit conversion rule:** $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3$. - ☐ **SVI** (Sludge Volume Index) — settleability indicator; good range **50–150 mL/g**. - ☐ **Sludge handling train** = Thickening → Stabilization (digestion) → Dewatering → Disposal/Reuse. - ☐ **Higher $\theta_c$ = less mass, more volume (in wasted stream)** — a subtle point KTU questions love to test. - ☐ **Extended aeration** (high $\theta_c$ = 20–30 d) minimizes sludge; **conventional ASP** (low $\theta_c$ = 3–7 d) maximizes it. - ☐ **Energy reality:** Sludge handling = **50–60% of total WWTP operating cost.** - ☐ **Resource view:** Modern plants recover **biogas, struvite, and biosolids** from sludge — align with KTU 2024 NEP-2020 sustainability focus. - ☐ **Order-of-magnitude check:** A 50,000 m³/d municipal plant produces ≈ **5–10 tonnes DS/d** of total sludge — useful for sanity-checking answers.

topics.title: Conventional Biological Process: Activated Sludge Process (ASP) - Development of Material Balance Equations
Trickling Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trickling Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Trickling Filter (TF)** is a **fixed-film biological wastewater treatment reactor** in which pre-settled wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse, inert, porous media (rock, slag, or synthetic plastic). A **microbial slime layer (biofilm)** grows on the media surface, and as the wastewater trickles downward by gravity over the slime, organic matter is **aerobically oxidized** by the attached biomass. The treated effluent is collected by an underdrain system and conveyed to a secondary clarifier for sludge separation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> "A trickling filter is an aerobic, attached-growth, packed-bed biological treatment unit where dissolved and suspended organic pollutants are removed by a self-regulating microbial film supported on the surface of a stationary medium through which wastewater is passed."

## 1.2 Key Engineering Parameters (Mandatory Bold Metrics)

- **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** = **Q / A** (m³/m²·day)
- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** = **Q · S₀ / V** (kg BOD / m³·day)
- **BOD Removal Efficiency (E)** typically **65 – 95 %** depending on filter class.
- **Recirculation Ratio (R)** = Qr / Q (dimensionless).
- **Standard media size** = **25 – 75 mm** for rock; **specific surface area** of plastic media up to **100 – 200 m²/m³**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vertical Forest" of Microbes

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a **multi-story vertical garden** in a shopping mall where every floor (each stone or plastic piece in the filter) is coated with a thin layer of moss. Pass a gentle mist of dirty water from the top (sprinkler = rotary distributor); the moss feeds on the mist and the droplets that fall emerge cleaner. The sloughed moss drops to the floor below and is swept away — this sloughed "moss" is your **humus sludge**.

In a trickling filter, the "mall floor" is the **underdrain**, the "moss" is the **biofilm**, the "sprinkler" is the **rotary distributor**, and the "mist" is the **sprayed wastewater**. The whole structure is a **passive, low-energy** aerobic digester.

## 1.4 Place of Trickling Filters in KTU 2024 Module-2 Framework

Trickling Filters belong to the **Attached-Growth / Fixed-Film** category of secondary biological treatment and are positioned in KTU's Module 2 alongside:

1. **Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC)** — partially submerged discs.
2. **Biological Aerated Filters (BAF)** — submerged packed-bed.
3. **Moving Bed Biofilm Reactors (MBBR)** — free-floating carriers.

TFs are the **oldest and most proven** (since late 19th century) fixed-film technology, prized for their **energy efficiency, operational simplicity, and resilience to shock loads**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — The "6-Stage Trickling Filter Cascade"

A trickling filter performs BOD removal in a tightly choreographed sequence. Each stage is **physically superimposed** on the filter media itself:

**Stage 1 → Distribution (Rotary Distributor Action)**
- The **distributor arm** rotates at **0.5 – 2 rpm**, driven by the **hydraulic head** (reaction torque of jets).
- Spreads wastewater evenly → ensures uniform biofilm wetting.
- Continuous dosing prevents drying / ponding.

**Stage 2 → Slime Growth (Biofilm Colonization)**
- Bacteria (Zoogloea, Pseudomonas, Achromobacter), protozoa, fungi, and higher organisms attach to media.
- Biofilm matures in **2 – 4 weeks** into a **stratified ecosystem**: aerobic outer → anoxic inner → anaerobic core near the stone.
- Thickness ≈ **0.5 – 3 mm**; beyond this, mass-transfer resistance becomes limiting → triggers sloughing.

**Stage 3 → Mass Transfer & Adsorption**
- **Diffusion of soluble organics** from bulk liquid into the biofilm's **diffusion boundary layer**.
- Adsorption onto the **extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)** matrix.
- This is the **rate-limiting step** under high-load conditions.

**Stage 4 → Aerobic Oxidation**
- **Oxygen** diffuses counter-currently (from air in voids) into the biofilm.
- Microbes metabolize BOD per:
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells}$$
- Active respiration occurs in the **aerobic zone** (top ~200 µm of biofilm).

**Stage 5 → Sloughing & Renewed Activity**
- As biofilm thickens, anaerobic digestion of inner core releases gas bubbles → **sloughing**.
- Sloughed biomass falls → becomes **humus sludge** in clarifier.
- New biofilm re-grows → process is **self-regulating**.

**Stage 6 → Underdrain Collection**
- Treated effluent collected at base → passes through **effluent channel** → secondary clarifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of TF design:** Trickling filters decouple **residence time** from **flow rate** by using a stationary medium — meaning **biological solids retention time (SRT)** can be very high (10–50 days) while **hydraulic retention time (HRT)** is just minutes. This is the opposite of activated sludge where SRT is artificially controlled.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **KTU board-exam standards** — memorize the variable definitions, not just the symbols, because examiners award partial credit for correct identification of terms.

| # | Quantity / Equation | Mathematical Expression | Units | Validity / Comments |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Loading Rate | $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m³/m²·d | A = plan area of filter |
| 2 | Organic Loading Rate | $OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | V = filter volume |
| 3 | Recirculation Factor (NRC) | $F = \dfrac{1+R}{(1+0.1R)^2}$ | dimensionless | For standard & high rate |
| 4 | NRC Efficiency Equation | $E_1 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.0561\sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$ | % | W = BOD load (kg/d), V = filter volume (m³) |
| 5 | Rankine's Equation | $E = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443\sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$ | % | Alternative to NRC |
| 6 | Eckenfelder (Plug-Flow) Model | $\dfrac{S}{S_0} = e^{-k \cdot t}$ | dimensionless | First-order, k = 0.05–0.10 /hr |
| 7 | Velz Equation | $\dfrac{S}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right)$ | dimensionless | K = rate constant, D = depth, n ≈ 0.5 |
| 8 | Germain Equation | $\dfrac{S}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-K \cdot D \cdot \theta^{n-1}\right)$ | dimensionless | Most rigorous KTU model |
| 9 | Filter Volume | $V = \dfrac{W \cdot 100}{OLR}$ | m³ | Direct sizing formula |
| 10 | Cross-sectional Area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{HLR}$ | m² | Given design HLR |
| 11 | Depth | $D = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | m | Typically 1.8–3.0 m |
| 12 | Wetting Rate (Recirc. bonus) | $WR = HLR \cdot (1+R)$ | m³/m²·d | Must exceed **15 m³/m²·d** to keep biofilm moist |
| 13 | Sludge Production | $Y = \dfrac{0.6 \cdot (S_0 - S)}{f}$ | kg VSS/kg BOD | f = COD/VSS ≈ 1.42 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe symbol rule (KTU 2024 PDF compliance):** Absolute value bars in the NRC/Rankine equations are written using $\sqrt{\;\cdot\;}$ and NOT the bare `|` character to preserve Markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Trickling filters are deployed in:
- **Municipal STPs** for cities with populations 5,000 – 500,000 (especially where energy is expensive).
- **Industrial wastewater** (food, pulp & paper, pharmaceutical) with **high-strength organic load**.
- **Rural & decentralized** plants due to **simple O&M, no aeration blower required**.
- **Upgrading existing plants** as **roughing filters** to reduce load on downstream activated sludge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate (KTU 2024):** Every step is shown explicitly. No "similarly we get…" shortcuts. Examiners specifically test step-by-step flow.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Eckenfelder Plug-Flow Biofilm Model

**Starting principle:** BOD removal by an aerobic biofilm follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the residual soluble substrate concentration $S$ (after assuming attached biomass $X$ is approximately steady-state over short intervals).

**Step 1 — Rate equation for differential slice of media:**

The rate of substrate depletion across a vertical differential slice of depth $dz$ is proportional to the cross-sectional area, the local biomass, and the substrate concentration:

$$\frac{dS}{dz} = -K \cdot \frac{S}{Q}$$

**Step 2 — Re-arrange variables to separate them:**

$$dS = -K \cdot \frac{S}{Q} \, dz$$

$$\frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{K}{Q} \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Apply integration limits:** At $z = 0$ (top), $S = S_0$ (influent). At $z = D$ (bottom), $S = S_e$ (effluent):

$$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -\frac{K}{Q} \int_{0}^{D} dz$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration:**

$$\left[ \ln S \right]_{S_0}^{S_e} = -\frac{K \cdot D}{Q}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = -\frac{K \cdot D}{Q}$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the design form:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K \cdot D}{Q}\right)}$$

This is the **Velz equation**. It tells us **BOD removal efficiency increases exponentially with depth** and decreases as the hydraulic loading $Q$ rises — exactly matching practical experience.

**Step 6 — Convert to KTU-friendly efficiency form:**

$$E = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \left(1 - e^{-KD/Q}\right) \times 100 \;\%$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — National Research Council (NRC) Efficiency Equation

The NRC equation was empirically derived from operating data of **standard-rate filters** in the 1940s. Although empirical, it can be **re-stated in a continuous mass-balance form**.

**Step 1 — Define the loading parameter:**

Define the **BOD loading parameter** $L = \dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}$, where:
- $W$ = total BOD load applied (kg/d),
- $V$ = filter volume (m³),
- $F$ = recirculation factor (dimensionless).

**Step 2 — Empirical relation between $L$ and efficiency $E$:**

The NRC study fitted > 100 plants and arrived at the empirical regression:

$$\frac{E}{100} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{L}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $E$:**

$$\boxed{E_1 = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}} \;\%}$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of the recirculation factor $F$:**

For two-stage trickling filters with recirculation, the **Recirculation Factor** accounts for the dilution effect of the recycle stream on the apparent effluent:

$$F = \frac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 \, R)^2}$$

where $R = Q_r / Q$ is the recirculation ratio.

**Derivation rationale:** The numerator $(1+R)$ reflects the total flow applied, while the denominator $(1+0.1R)^2$ represents empirical correction for the fact that not all of the recycled water is biologically equivalent to fresh feed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rankine's Equation

**Step 1 — Rankine proposed an alternative empirical form** with a stronger denominator coefficient:

$$\frac{E}{100} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{L}}$$

**Step 2 — Therefore:**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}} \;\%}$$

**Comparison with NRC:** Rankine consistently predicts **higher efficiency** for the same $L$, because its denominator constant ($0.443$) is larger than NRC's ($0.0561$) only after rescaling — Rankine was fitted to **British and tropical data**, NRC to **North American data**. KTU students must **identify which is requested** in a numerical.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A municipal STP receives $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/d with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD. Design a high-rate trickling filter with OLR = $0.6$ kg/m³·d and $R = 1.5$. Estimate efficiency using the NRC equation.

**Step 1 — Total BOD load:**

$$W = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{1000} = \frac{5000 \times 250}{1000} = 1250 \;\text{kg BOD/d}$$

**Step 2 — Required filter volume (from OLR):**

$$V = \frac{W}{OLR} = \frac{1250}{0.6} = 2083.33 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Recirculation factor:**

$$F = \frac{1 + 1.5}{(1 + 0.1 \times 1.5)^2} = \frac{2.5}{(1.15)^2} = \frac{2.5}{1.3225} = 1.890$$

**Step 4 — Loading parameter $L$:**

$$L = \frac{W}{V \cdot F} = \frac{1250}{2083.33 \times 1.890} = \frac{1250}{3937.5} = 0.3174$$

**Step 5 — Apply NRC equation:**

$$E_1 = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{0.3174}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.3174} = 0.5634$$

$$E_1 = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \times 0.5634} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0316} = \frac{100}{1.0316}$$

$$\boxed{E_1 = 96.94 \;\%}$$

**Step 6 — Effluent BOD:**

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot (1 - 0.9694) = 250 \times 0.0306 = 7.65 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 7 — Verification via Rankine:**

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{0.3174}} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \times 0.5634} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.2496} = \frac{100}{1.2496}$$

$$E = 80.03 \;\%$$

(Significantly lower than NRC, confirming that NRC fits **warmer-climate high-rate filters** better.)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Condition | Recommended Model | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Standard rate filter, $OLR < 0.2$ kg/m³·d, $R = 0$ | **NRC Equation** | Empirical fit to standard rate data |
| High rate filter, $R > 1$, plastic media | **Rankine or Eckenfelder** | Accounts for non-trivial recirculation |
| Deep filter (> 2 m), variable $Q$ | **Velz Equation** | Continuous depth-resolved model |
| Single-stage, plug-flow assumption valid | **Eckenfelder** | Simpler first-order kinetic form |
| Two-stage filter, different media | **Germain** | Allows depth- and time-resolved analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Components of a Trickling Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Well] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[Feed Well]
    C --> D[Pumping Station]
    D --> E[Rotary Distributor Assembly]
    E --> F[Filter Media Bed with Biofilm]
    F --> G[Underdrain System with Vents]
    G --> H[Effluent Channel]
    H --> I[Secondary Clarifier Humus Removal]
    I --> J[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    I --> K[Sludge Return to Primary]

    subgraph BiofilmZone
      F1[Aerobic Outer Layer] 
      F2[Anoxic Middle Layer]
      F3[Anaerobic Inner Core]
      F1 --> F2 --> F3
    end

    subgraph VentilationAir
      L1[Draught by Wind]
      L2[Forced Fan Optional]
      L1 --> F
      L2 --> F
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The diagram is structured as a top-down processing pipeline (left side, A → J). The two sub-graphs (right side) display **interior biofilm stratification** and the **dual oxygen supply paths**. This format replaces a physical sectional drawing while preserving every KTU-relevant functional interaction.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Operational Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Influent Flow Q] --> MIX1{Mixing with Recycle?}
    QR[Recycle Flow Q sub R] --> MIX1
    MIX1 -->|Yes| DIST[Combined Flow to Distributor]
    MIX1 -->|No| DIST

    DIST --> SPIN[Rotary Distributor]
    SPIN --> MEDIA[Filter Media Biofilm]
    MEDIA --> UD[Underdrain]
    UD --> SPLIT{Split Flow?}
    SPLIT -->|Effluent Path| EFF[To Secondary Clarifier]
    SPLIT -->|Recycle Path| REC[Pump to Recycle]
    REC --> QR
    EFF --> OUT[Treated Effluent]

    MEDIA -.->|Sloughed Sludge| SLG[Sludge Zone in Clarifier]
    SLG --> SC[Sludge Collection]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always identify the **recirculation loop** (the dashed arrows) in any TF schematic — examiners award marks for clearly labeling $Q$, $Q_r$, and $R$.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Classification of Trickling Filters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TF[TRICKLING FILTERS] --> SR[Standard Rate]
    TF --> HR[High Rate]
    TF --> RF[Roughing Filter]
    TF --> TS[Two Stage]

    SR --> SR1[OLR less than 0.2 kg per m3 per d]
    SR --> SR2[R equals 0 typically]
    SR --> SR3[Recirculation via dosing tank]

    HR --> HR1[OLR 0.2 to 1.0 kg per m3 per d]
    HR --> HR2[R between 1 and 2]
    HR --> HR3[Plastic media preferred]

    RF --> RF1[OLR greater than 1.5 kg per m3 per d]
    RF --> RF2[Used as pretreatment ahead of ASP]

    TS --> TS1[Two filters in series]
    TS --> TS2[Each designed with Rankine or NRC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on diagram labels:** All node identifiers (e.g., `SR1`, `HR2`) are alphanumeric only — no reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph` as standalone IDs) are used, complying with the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**
> *"Define a trickling filter. Distinguish it from an activated sludge process using any two relevant criteria."*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

A trickling filter is an **attached-growth aerobic biological treatment unit** in which wastewater is passed over a stationary bed of coarse, porous media (rock, slag, plastic) supporting a microbial slime (biofilm); organic matter is removed as the biofilm oxidizes it under ambient aeration, and sloughed biomass is captured in a downstream clarifier.

**Distinctions from Activated Sludge:**

| Criterion | Trickling Filter | Activated Sludge |
|---|---|---|
| Microbial state | Attached (biofilm) on fixed media | Suspended flocs in mixed liquor |
| Energy | Low (no aeration blower needed) | High (blowers supply ~1.5 kWh/m³) |
| Sludge | Sloughed humus, easy to settle | Pin floc, bulking issues possible |

*(Award full 3 marks for: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each distinction.)*

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Bloom Level: Understand / Apply)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**
> *"Explain the role of the recirculation factor $F$ in the NRC trickling filter design equation. Why is $F < 2$ even when $R > 3$?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

The recirculation factor $F$ **corrects the NRC equation for the diluting effect of returning clarified effluent to the filter head**. Although $R$ increases linearly with recycle flow, $F$ saturates because recycled water has lower soluble BOD and thus a **diminished capacity to dilute the influent organics**. Mathematically, $F = (1+R)/(1+0.1R)^2$ approaches an asymptotic ceiling of $25$ as $R \to \infty$, but in practice $F$ rarely exceeds **2.0** because higher $R$ yields no proportional biological benefit.

*(Award 3 marks: 1 for role, 1 for the equation, 1 for the asymptotic explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Essay Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Set A]**
> *"(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a rotary distributor in a trickling filter.*
> *(b) Design a single-stage high-rate trickling filter for a town with $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, OLR = $0.6$ kg/m³·d, and recirculation ratio $R = 1.0$. Estimate the BOD removal efficiency using the NRC equation."*

---

#### Part (a) — Rotary Distributor (7 marks)

**Working:**
The rotary distributor consists of:
1. A **central well** (column) into which feed enters.
2. Two or four **horizontal arms** mounted on a central pivot.
3. **Nozzles (orifices)** along the arms (typically $10$–$15$ mm diameter).
4. **Deflector plates** at the orifices for jet direction control.

**Operating principle:**
- Feed enters the central column and fills the horizontal arms.
- Water **shoots out of the orifices** at high velocity; the **reaction force** (Newton's third law) generates a torque that rotates the assembly.
- Rotation speed: **0.5 – 2 rpm**.
- As the arm rotates, the **jet impact on the media** spreads wastewater uniformly over the entire bed area.

**Function served:**
- **Hydraulic head** (typically $0.6$–$1.0$ m above the media) ensures continuous distribution.
- Wetting rate maintained $> 15$ m³/m²·d even with intermittent dosing.
- Continuous motion prevents **filter fly** breeding.

**Sketch components expected in answer:**

- Feed inlet pipe,
- Central column,
- Pivoting arms,
- Orifices with deflectors,
- Media surface below,
- Driving torque arrow.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for components, 2 for working, 2 for jet torque principle, 1 for sketch clarity.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total BOD load $W$:**

$$W = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{1000} = \frac{20000 \times 300}{1000} = 6000 \;\text{kg BOD/d}$$

**Step 2 — Required filter volume $V$ (from OLR):**

$$V = \frac{W}{OLR} = \frac{6000}{0.6} = 10000 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Recirculation factor $F$ (with $R = 1.0$):**

$$F = \frac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1R)^2} = \frac{1 + 1.0}{(1 + 0.1 \times 1)^2} = \frac{2.0}{(1.1)^2} = \frac{2.0}{1.21}$$

$$F = 1.653$$

**Step 4 — Loading parameter $L$:**

$$L = \frac{W}{V \cdot F} = \frac{6000}{10000 \times 1.653} = \frac{6000}{16530} = 0.363$$

**Step 5 — Apply NRC equation:**

$$E_1 = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{L}} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \sqrt{0.363}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.363} = 0.6025$$

$$E_1 = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0561 \times 0.6025} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0338} = \frac{100}{1.0338}$$

$$\boxed{E_1 = 96.73 \;\%}$$

**Step 6 — Effluent BOD concentration:**

$$S_e = S_0 \cdot (1 - 0.9673) = 300 \times 0.0327 = 9.81 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 7 — Filter dimensions (assuming standard $D = 2.5$ m):**

$$A = \frac{V}{D} = \frac{10000}{2.5} = 4000 \;\text{m}^2$$

If circular: $D_{filter} = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 4000}{3.1416}} = 71.4 \;\text{m}$ (use two filters of ~50 m diameter each).

*(Award 7 marks: 1 each for $W$, $V$, $F$, $L$, $E_1$, $S_e$, and dimensions.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**
> *"(a) Compare the NRC, Rankine, Eckenfelder, Velz, and Germain equations used for trickling filter design with their applicability ranges.*
> *(b) A two-stage trickling filter plant is to be designed for $Q = 30{,}000$ m³/d with $S_0 = 400$ mg/L BOD. Design load $W_1 = W_2 = 1.5 \, W$ per stage (i.e., equal loads). Each stage has $F = 1.5$ and $V_1 = V_2 = 5000$ m³. Calculate the overall BOD removal efficiency using the Rankine equation."*

---

#### Part (a) — Comparison of Design Equations (7 marks)

| # | Model | Mathematical Form | Best-Use Case | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NRC** | $E = 100/[1+0.0561\sqrt{W/(VF)}]$ | Standard-rate filters, USA, no recycle | Old STP retrofits |
| 2 | **Rankine** | $E = 100/[1+0.443\sqrt{W/(VF)}]$ | British / tropical high-rate filters | Most KTU problems |
| 3 | **Eckenfelder** | $S/S_0 = e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order plug flow, plastic media | Modular design |
| 4 | **Velz** | $S/S_0 = \exp(-K \cdot D/Q^n)$ | Depth-resolved continuous model | Deep filters > 2 m |
| 5 | **Germain** | $S/S_0 = \exp(-K \cdot D \cdot \theta^{n-1})$ | Two-stage, multi-phenomena model | Advanced design |

**Limitations summary:**
- NRC and Rankine are **empirical**, restricted to **steady-state plug flow**.
- Eckenfelder assumes **fixed biomass**, which fails under high OLR.
- Velz is sensitive to $n$ value.
- Germain is the most accurate but requires more **kinetic parameters**.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark each per row in the table + 2 marks for limitations summary.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Two-Stage Rankine Design (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $W_1 = W_2 = 1.5 \cdot W$ implies the total load is split equally, but the statement convention here is that **each stage receives an equal share** of the total $W$.
- Total $W = Q \cdot S_0 / 1000 = 30000 \times 400 / 1000 = 12000$ kg BOD/d.
- Per stage: $W_i = 12000 / 2 = 6000$ kg/d (after equal distribution).
- $V_1 = V_2 = 5000$ m³, $F = 1.5$ for each stage.

**Step 1 — Per-stage loading parameter:**

$$L_i = \frac{W_i}{V_i \cdot F} = \frac{6000}{5000 \times 1.5} = \frac{6000}{7500} = 0.80$$

**Step 2 — Per-stage Rankine efficiency:**

$$\sqrt{0.80} = 0.8944$$

$$E_i = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \times 0.8944} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.3963} = \frac{100}{1.3963}$$

$$E_i = 71.62 \;\% \text{ per stage}$$

**Step 3 — Effluent from Stage 1:**

$$S_1 = S_0 \cdot (1 - 0.7162) = 400 \times 0.2838 = 113.5 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Effluent from Stage 2 (using $S_1$ as new input to Stage 2):**

$$S_2 = S_1 \cdot (1 - 0.7162) = 113.5 \times 0.2838 = 32.21 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Overall plant efficiency:**

$$\boxed{E_{overall} = \frac{S_0 - S_2}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{400 - 32.21}{400} \times 100 = 91.95 \;\%}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative combined-load approach (verification):**

Using total $W = 12000$ kg/d, $V_{total} = 10000$ m³, $F = 1.5$:

$$L_{total} = \frac{12000}{10000 \times 1.5} = 0.80$$

$$E_{combined} = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{0.80}} = 71.62 \;\%$$

This shows **two-stage series** far exceeds a **single-stage combined** equivalent (91.95 % vs 71.62 %).

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for per-stage efficiency, 2 for sequential effluent calculation, 2 for combined efficiency, 1 for comparison observation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–4 marks per answer:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $W$**: Many students use $W$ in grams/day instead of kg/day. The NRC coefficient $0.0561$ is calibrated for **$W$ in kg/d**. Using g/d yields a **~1000× error**.
> 2. **Forgetting the recirculation factor $F$**: A bare NRC application without $F$ loses **2 marks** even if the math is correct.
> 3. **Confusing $F$ definitions between NRC and Rankine**: Both equations use $F$, but the empirical formula for $F$ itself differs from textbook to textbook; always state $F = (1+R)/(1+0.1R)^2$.
> 4. **Neglecting secondary clarifier removal**: Trickling filter effluent still contains sloughed solids; the actual plant effluent BOD is **lower** than $S_e$ from the equation alone, but the question asks about the filter, not the plant.
> 5. **Plotting Rankine vs NRC as if interchangeable**: They are **not** — pick the right one based on geography and OLR.
> 6. **Two-stage problem confusion**: Students often sum efficiencies directly ($71.6 + 71.6 = 143.2\%$) — incorrect. Apply $S_1 \to S_2$ sequentially.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as a last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ Trickling Filter = **fixed-film aerobic packed-bed reactor**; biofilm develops on stationary media.
- ✅ Six operational stages: **Distribution → Slime Growth → Mass Transfer → Aerobic Oxidation → Sloughing → Collection**.
- ✅ Media: **rock (25–75 mm)**, **plastic (high surface area)**, **slag (older plants)**.
- ✅ Key loadings: **HLR (m³/m²·d)** and **OLR (kg BOD/m³·d)**.
- ✅ Four types: **Standard Rate, High Rate, Roughing, Two-Stage**.
- ✅ **NRC** for standard-rate, **Rankine** for high-rate, **Velz** for depth-resolved, **Eckenfelder** for first-order, **Germain** for advanced multi-parameter design.
- ✅ Recirculation factor $F = (1+R)/(1+0.1R)^2$ — saturates as $R \to \infty$.
- ✅ Standard design **depth** = 1.8 – 3.0 m; **rotary distributor speed** = 0.5 – 2 rpm.
- ✅ **BOD removal efficiency** for well-designed HR-TF = **80 – 95 %**.
- ✅ **Effluent BOD** from filter = 10 – 40 mg/L (typical).
- ✅ **Ventilation** is critical — wind-induced draught plus thermal differentials drive airflow.
- ✅ **Wetting rate** must exceed **15 m³/m²·d** to keep biofilm moist.
- ✅ **Sloughing** is the natural biofilm self-regulator; sloughed biomass = **humus sludge** handled in secondary clarifier.
- ✅ Advantages: **low energy, simple O&M, sludge stability, resistant to shock loads**.
- ✅ Disadvantages: **footprint large, odor at high load, fly/psychoda nuisance, requires secondary clarification**.
- ✅ Always **convert $W$ to kg/day** before applying NRC/Rankine.
- ✅ For **two-stage filters**, apply efficiency **sequentially** on residual substrate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor** is a high-rate, suspended-growth **anaerobic** wastewater treatment system in which wastewater is introduced at the bottom of the reactor and flows upward through a dense, granular, self-immobilized **sludge blanket**, where anaerobic microorganisms convert organic pollutants into **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** and treated effluent. The gas–liquid–solid (three-phase) separator at the top retains the biomass and collects the biogas. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Mud Pot" Imagine a tall kitchen pot half-filled with thick, bubbling mud (the **sludge blanket**) sitting on a slow fire. As you pour dirty water in from the bottom, the water pushes gently upward through the mud. Tiny microbes living in the mud "eat" the dirt and release bubbles of methane-rich gas — exactly like a slow cooker of *Avial* bubbling away. The bubbles lift some particles upward, but a special cap (the **three-phase separator**) at the top traps the mud and lets only the cleaned water and gas escape. The pot never needs a stirrer, never needs air, and the mud keeps working for months! ### Key Design Parameters at a Glance | Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Upflow velocity | **0.5 – 1.5 m/h** | Avoids sludge washout | | HRT | **4 – 12 h** | Residence time for organics | | OLR | **5 – 15 kg COD/m³·d** | Organic loading rate | | Temperature | **30 – 37 °C (mesophilic)** | Optimal for methanogens | | COD removal | **60 – 95 %** | High-strength wastewater | | Biogas yield | **0.30 – 0.50 m³/kg COD removed** | Energy recovery | > [!NOTE] > UASB reactors were pioneered by **Dr. Gatze Lettinga (Wageningen University, Netherlands, 1970s)** and are classified as **high-rate anaerobic systems** because they retain a very high concentration of active biomass (50,000 – 100,000 mg/L VSS) without any carrier media. --- ## 1.2 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > The **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** is an **attached-growth (biofilm)** aerobic biological treatment process in which a series of closely spaced, slowly rotating circular discs — partially immersed in a flowing wastewater stream — provides a large surface area for the growth of a **microbial slime (biofilm)**. As the discs rotate, the biofilm is alternately submerged (absorbing organics) and exposed to air (absorbing oxygen), enabling BOD/COD removal and **simultaneous nitrification** in later stages. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Slow-Spinning Salad Washer" Think of a giant salad spinner mounted on a horizontal shaft over a long tank of dirty water. Hundreds of round discs are stacked on the shaft like vinyl records. The shaft turns *very slowly* (about **1–2 revolutions per minute**). As each disc dips into the water, a slimy green film of bacteria sticks to it and "eats" the dirt. As the disc emerges and spins into the air, the slime drinks in oxygen. By the time the disc comes around again, it's hungry for more food. The wastewater passes under the discs from one end to the other, getting cleaner and cleaner as it travels through multiple "stages." ### Key Design Parameters at a Glance | Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Rotational speed | **1 – 2 rpm** | Driven by shaft torque | | Disc diameter | **2 – 4 m** | Effective wetted surface | | Disc spacing | **20 – 40 mm** | Prevents disc collisions | | Disc submergence | **30 – 40 %** | Air/water exposure balance | | Hydraulic loading | **0.05 – 0.15 m³/m²·d** | Liquid contact rate | | Organic loading | **5 – 20 g BOD/m²·d** | Carbonaceous removal | | Stages | **3 – 4 (typically)** | Sequencing for BOD + nitrification | | BOD removal | **85 – 95 %** | Single-stage efficiency | > [!NOTE] > RBC systems were developed in the **1960s** in Germany (Fuchs) and the USA, and became popular due to **low energy consumption**, **simplicity of operation**, and excellent performance for **small-to-medium communities (up to ~50,000 PE)**. --- ## 1.3 UASB vs RBC — Comparative Snapshot > [!TIP] > A frequently asked KTU question contrasts these two technologies. Use this comparative snapshot during revision. | Feature | UASB Reactor | Rotating Biological Contactor | |---|---|---| | Process type | **Anaerobic / Suspended growth** | **Aerobic / Attached growth** | | Oxygen requirement | **None (sealed reactor)** | **Mechanical rotation (no blower)** | | Biomass retention | Granular sludge blanket | Biofilm on discs | | Energy yield | Produces biogas (CH₄) | Energy consumer (motor) | | Footprint | Very compact | Moderate | | Effluent quality | Needs post-treatment | Often meets discharge norms | | Typical wastewater | High-strength industrial | Low-to-medium strength domestic | | Temperature sensitivity | Sensitive (mesophilic) | Less sensitive | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 UASB Reactor — Operational Theory The UASB reactor operates on the principle of **upflow anaerobic digestion** combined with **internal gas–liquid–solid separation**. Four distinct zones exist vertically within the reactor: ### Logical Step-by-Step Operation 1. **Influent Distribution Zone (Bottom Sludge Bed)** - Wastewater is uniformly distributed through **multiple inlet ports** at the reactor base. - Uniform distribution is critical — non-uniform flow causes **channeling** and **dead zones**. 2. **Sludge Blanket Zone (Digestion Zone)** - Dense bed of **granular anaerobic sludge** (typically 1–3 mm pellets). - Microbial communities here include: - **Acidogens** — ferment organics to VFAs (volatile fatty acids). - **Acetogens** — convert VFAs to acetate, H₂, CO₂. - **Methanogens** — produce CH₄ from acetate and H₂/CO₂. 3. **Sludge Blanket / Clarifier Interface (Transition Zone)** - Fluidized zone where biogas bubbles rise and create gentle mixing. - Solids separation begins here. 4. **Three-Phase Separator (GLS Device — Top Zone)** - **Gas collector hoods** capture rising biogas. - **Deflectors** bend solids back into the reactor. - **Effluent weirs** collect clarified treated water. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **three-phase separator** is the *heart* of the UASB reactor. Without it, biomass would wash out and the system would fail. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**. ### Engineering Significance of UASB - **High biomass concentration** (50,000 – 100,000 mg/L VSS) is retained by **granulation**, not by media or membranes. - Biogas produced (60–70 % CH₄) is a **renewable energy source** with calorific value ≈ **20–25 MJ/m³**. - Ideal for **high-strength industrial wastewater** with COD > 1000 mg/L (dairy, brewery, distillery, sugarcane, pulp & paper, slaughterhouse). - **No aeration tank** and **no mechanical mixing** → very low power input. --- ## 2.2 RBC — Operational Theory The RBC operates as a **hybrid biofilm system** combining sorption, biological oxidation, and physical aeration in one unit. ### Logical Step-by-Step Operation 1. **Biofilm Development** - As discs rotate in wastewater, microorganisms attach and multiply, forming a **biofilm layer (0.1 – 3 mm thick)**. - Biofilm comprises bacteria, protozoa, fungi, and sometimes nitrifiers. 2. **Submerged Half-Cycle (Intake Phase)** - As a disc slice passes through wastewater, organics and dissolved oxygen are **absorbed** into the biofilm. - Soluble BOD diffuses into the biofilm matrix. 3. **Emergence Half-Cycle (Aeration Phase)** - Disc emerges into the air. Oxygen is absorbed into the moist biofilm. - This step is unique: it provides oxygen **passively**, without blowers. - Excess water drains off, exposing new biofilm surface to oxygen. 4. **Shearing and Renewal** - Rotation-induced shear keeps biofilm thickness in equilibrium — sloughed biomass exits with effluent. - **Net growth rate = sloughing rate** maintains steady biofilm. ### Multi-Stage Configuration - **First stage** → Highest organic load; removes bulk carbonaceous BOD. - **Intermediate stages** → Polishing of BOD. - **Final stage** → **Nitrification** (low organic, high DO, ammonia → nitrate). - Each stage typically contains 20–100 discs on a common shaft. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** RBC stages go **"C → C → C → N"** (Carbonaceous → Nitrification). The first disc you see is for carbon; the last is for nitrogen. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` (LaTeX) instead of `|` in all table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | hours (h) | $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $Q$ = flow (m³/h) | | 2 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg COD/m³·d | $S_0$ = influent substrate (kg/m³) | | 3 | $v_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Upflow velocity in UASB | m/h | $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²) | | 4 | $E = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | $S_e$ = effluent substrate | | 5 | $\eta_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S_e) - 1.42 \cdot X_{wasted}}{Q \cdot S_0}$ | Methane yield coefficient | — | Modified Buswell equation | | 6 | $G_{CH_4} = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Y_{CH_4}$ | Daily methane production | m³ CH₄/d | $Y_{CH_4}$ ≈ **0.40 m³ CH₄/kg COD** | | 7 | $A_{disc} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2} \cdot N$ | Total disc surface (RBC) | m² | $D$ = diameter, $N$ = disc count (half-submerged) | | 8 | $OLR_{RBC} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A_{disc}}$ | RBC organic loading | g BOD/m²·d | Per unit disc area | | 9 | $U = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Peripheral tip speed | m/s | $N_{rpm}$ = rotations/minute | | 10 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{M}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | UASB uses VSS $M$ | | 11 | $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot k - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT for methanogens | days | Critical at low temperatures | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **UASB → Biogas Engineering:** India’s SATAT scheme and industrial ETP designs use these formulas to size reactors and estimate **energy recovery potential** from distillery/dairy wastewater. - **RBC → Decentralized STPs:** Used in Kerala's coastal Panchayats and small townships where energy reliability is poor — RBC consumes **~30–40 % less electricity** than activated sludge. --- ## 2.4 Critical Design Considerations ### For UASB - **Granulation** takes 2–6 months — startup is slow. - **Slug dosing** of Ca²⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺ promotes granulation. - Avoid **sulphate-rich wastewater** (>500 mg/L) — causes **sulphide toxicity** and SRB competition. - **Temperature shock** of >2 °C/day reduces methanogenic activity by 30–50 %. ### For RBC - **Excessive rotation (>5 rpm)** causes biofilm erosion and shaft fatigue. - **Under-rotation (<0.5 rpm)** causes anaerobic conditions in biofilm. - **Disc materials:** Polyethylene / PVC / Polystyrene — must resist UV and biofilm acids. - **Shaft bearings** must be protected from spray — shaft is often enclosed. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Methane Gas Production from a UASB Reactor > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation question**. Master every step. ### Step 1 — Stoichiometric Foundation (Buswell Equation) The complete anaerobic conversion of an organic substrate $C_n H_a O_b$ (per mole) is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right) H_2O &\longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 \\ &+ \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right) CO_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic of the conversion:** The coefficients balance (C, H, O atoms) on both sides. Water is consumed or produced depending on the degree of oxidation of the substrate. ### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Methane Yield For a generic carbohydrate substrate expressed as COD, the **theoretical methane yield** is derived from the equivalence that **1 mole of COD consumed produces 1 mole of O₂ equivalents**, which in turn yields **½ mole CH₄** (since CH₄ + 2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O). Therefore: $$ \boxed{Y_{CH_4,\,th} = \dfrac{22.4\,L\,CH_4}{2 \times 32\,g\,COD} = 0.35\,L\,CH_4/g\,COD_{removed}} $$ At **STP** (0 °C, 1 atm), $Y_{CH_4,th}$ = **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD**. At 25 °C, this value becomes ≈ **0.40 m³ CH₄/kg COD** (using the ideal gas correction factor 298/273). ### Step 3 — Practical Methane Yield Account for **biomass synthesis** — a fraction of substrate is converted to new cells rather than methane. Let $Y_{obs}$ = observed biomass yield (kg VSS/kg COD), typically **0.05–0.10** for methanogenic granules. $$ \boxed{Y_{CH_4,\,practical} = Y_{CH_4,\,th} - 1.42 \cdot Y_{obs}} $$ The factor **1.42** comes from the COD equivalent of biomass: $C_5H_7NO_2$ has COD ≈ 1.42 g COD/g VSS. ### Step 4 — Daily Methane Generation Rate For a UASB reactor with influent flow $Q$ (m³/d) and COD removal $(S_0 - S_e)$ (kg COD/m³): $$ G_{CH_4} = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Y_{CH_4,\,practical} $$ **Substituting unit-consistent values:** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{CH_4} &= Q \,[\text{m}^3/\text{d}] \times (S_0 - S_e) \,[\text{kg COD/m}^3] \times Y_{CH_4} \,[\text{m}^3\,CH_4/\text{kg COD}] \\[4pt] &= \text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{d} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A UASB reactor receives $Q$ = **500 m³/d** of distillery wastewater with influent COD $S_0$ = **40,000 mg/L** and effluent COD $S_e$ = **4,000 mg/L**. Assume $Y_{obs}$ = 0.08 kg VSS/kg COD and STP correction applies. **Solution:** Step (a) — Compute COD removed: $$ (S_0 - S_e) = (40{,}000 - 4{,}000)\,\text{mg/L} = 36{,}000\,\text{mg/L} = 36\,\text{kg/m}^3 $$ Step (b) — Apply practical methane yield: $$ Y_{CH_4} = 0.40 - (1.42 \times 0.08) = 0.40 - 0.1136 = 0.2864\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg COD} $$ Step (c) — Daily methane production: $$ G_{CH_4} = 500 \times 36 \times 0.2864 = 5155.2\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{d} $$ Step (d) — Energy content (calorific value of CH₄ ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³**): $$ \text{Energy} = 5155.2 \times 35.8 = 184{,}556\,\text{MJ/d} \approx 51.3\,\text{kWh/d of thermal energy} $$ > [!TIP] > KTU examiners **love** such numerical derivations. Show all unit cancellations — that's where students lose marks! --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Disc Surface Area for RBC ### Step 1 — Effective Wetted Area per Disc A disc of diameter $D$ (m) rotates in wastewater with submergence ratio $\alpha$ (≈ **0.40**). Only the submerged half receives wastewater contact, but both halves get aeration. **Convention:** Effective wetted area for BOD removal = $\alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}$ per disc. ### Step 2 — Total Surface Area for N Discs $$ A_{total} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2} $$ ### Step 3 — Required Disc Count for Given Load For a required BOD removal load $L_B$ (g BOD/d) at design organic loading rate $U$ (g BOD/m²·d): $$ N = \dfrac{L_B}{U \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_B}{U \cdot \alpha \cdot \pi \cdot D^2} $$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Verification **Given:** $L_B$ = **200 kg BOD/d**, $D$ = **3 m**, $U$ = **15 g BOD/m²·d**, $\alpha$ = **0.40**. $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{2 \times (200{,}000\,\text{g/d})}{15\,\text{g/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{d} \times 0.40 \times \pi \times (3)^2} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{400{,}000}{15 \times 0.40 \times 3.1416 \times 9} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{400{,}000}{169.65} \approx 2358\,\text{discs} \end{aligned} $$ These 2358 discs would be distributed across **4 stages** → **~590 discs/stage** along parallel shafts. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Decision Criteria for UASB vs RBC > [!NOTE] > This matrix is essential for KTU 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions. | Decision Parameter | Choose UASB if… | Choose RBC if… | |---|---|---| | Influent COD | **> 2000 mg/L** (high strength) | **< 1000 mg/L** (low-medium strength) | | Energy goal | **Energy recovery desired** (biogas) | **Low energy input desired** (no aeration) | | Land availability | **Severely limited** (high rate) | **Moderate availability** | | Climate | **Warm climate (≥ 20 °C year-round)** | **Any climate** | | Operator skill | **Skilled** (anaerobic microbiology) | **Less skilled** (simpler operation) | | Effluent target | **Post-treatment will follow** (anaerobic effluent needs polishing) | **Direct discharge to surface water** | | Wastewater type | Industrial (brewery, dairy, distillery) | Domestic sewage, small townships | | Capital cost (per m³) | Higher for small systems | Lower for small communities | | O&M cost | Lower long-term (offset by biogas) | Slightly higher (motor + bearings) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UASB Reactor — Internal Architecture & Flow Path The following block-level flow diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a UASB reactor, including all internal zones, flow streams, and phase-separation components. (This representation replaces a complex 3-D drawing that cannot be rendered in Mermaid.) ```mermaid flowchart TD InfluentNode["INFLUENT WASTEWATER
High Strength COD = 40,000 mg/L"]:::inlet Distributor["INFLOW DISTRIBUTOR
Multiple Nozzles
Uniform Upflow"]:::unit SludgeBed["SLUDGE BED ZONE
Granular Sludge 60 to 100 kg VSS per m3
Acidogens Acetogens Methanogens"]:::digest Blanket["SLUDGE BLANKET ZONE
Fluidized Granules
Biogas Bubble Mixing"]:::digest Separator["THREE PHASE GLS SEPARATOR
Gas Collector Hood
Sludge Deflector Plates"]:::sep GasPipe["BIOGAS COLLECTION HEADER
CH4 = 60 to 70 percent
CO2 = 30 to 40 percent
H2S Trace"]:::gas EffluentWeir["EFFLUENT WEIR LAUNDER
Treated Water Outlet
COD Less Than 4000 mg/L"]:::outlet EffluentNode["POST TREATMENT UNIT
Polishing Aerobic Filter"]:::outlet Recycle["EFFLUENT RECYCLE PUMP
During Startup Only"]:::aux InfluentNode --> Distributor Distributor --> SludgeBed SludgeBed --> Blanket Blanket --> Separator Separator --> GasPipe Separator --> EffluentWeir EffluentWeir --> EffluentNode Recycle -.-> Distributor classDef inlet fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef unit fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef digest fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 classDef sep fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000000 classDef gas fill:#ffffb3,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 classDef outlet fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 classDef aux fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The upward arrows follow the **upflow principle**. The recycle line is dashed because it operates **only during startup** to promote granulation. --- ## 4.2 Rotating Biological Contactor — Multi-Stage Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR InfluentRBC["RAW SEWAGE INLET
BOD = 250 mg/L
NH3 = 30 mg/L"]:::inlet Stage1["STAGE 1 CARBONACEOUS
40 to 50 Discs
BOD Load 20 g per m2 per d
Bulk BOD Removal"]:::stage1 Stage2["STAGE 2 CARBONACEOUS
40 to 50 Discs
BOD Load 10 g per m2 per d
Polishing BOD"]:::stage2 Stage3["STAGE 3 TRANSITION
40 to 50 Discs
BOD Load 5 g per m2 per d
Low C N Ratio"]:::stage3 Stage4["STAGE 4 NITRIFICATION
40 to 50 Discs
BOD Load less than 3 g per m2 per d
NH3 to NO3 Conversion"]:::stage4 Clarifier["FINAL CLARIFIER
Sloughed Biofilm Removal"]:::unit TreatedOut["TREATED EFFLUENT
BOD less than 20 mg/L
NH3 less than 5 mg/L"]:::outlet Sludge["WASTE BIOFILM SLUDGE"]:::aux InfluentRBC --> Stage1 Stage1 --> Stage2 Stage2 --> Stage3 Stage3 --> Stage4 Stage4 --> Clarifier Clarifier --> TreatedOut Clarifier --> Sludge classDef inlet fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef stage1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 classDef stage2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef stage3 fill:#ffffb3,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 classDef stage4 fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 classDef unit fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 classDef outlet fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 classDef aux fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Side-View Block Architecture — A Single RBC Disc's Half-Rotation ```mermaid flowchart TD AirHalf["AIR PHASE OF ROTATION
Disc Surface Exposed to Atmosphere
Oxygen Absorbed into Moist Biofilm
CO2 Released to Air"]:::air Drainage["DRAINAGE OF EXCESS WATER
Slime Layer Refreshed
Sloughing of Excess Biomass"]:::air SubmergedHalf["SUBMERGED PHASE OF ROTATION
Disc Wetted in Wastewater
Organics and Oxygen Diffuse into Biofilm
BOD Uptake by Aerobic Bacteria"]:::water AirHalf --> Drainage Drainage --> SubmergedHalf SubmergedHalf --> AirHalf classDef air fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef water fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 ``` > [!TIP] > Memorize the **"AIR → DRAINAGE → WATER → AIR"** cycle for any descriptive question on RBC operation. --- ## 4.4 Process Block Diagram — UASB and RBC Integration Options ```mermaid flowchart LR RawWW["RAW WASTEWATER"]:::inlet UASBopt["UASB REACTOR
Anaerobic
COD Removal 80 percent"]:::anae PostPol["POST TREATMENT
Aerobic Trickling Filter
Or Activated Sludge"]:::aero UASBout["DISCHARGE OR REUSE"]:::outlet RawDom["DOMESTIC SEWAGE"]:::inlet RBCopt["RBC SYSTEM
4 STAGES
Aerobic Biofilm"]:::aero RBCcl["FINAL CLARIFIER"]:::unit RBCout["DISCHARGE OR REUSE"]:::outlet RawWW --> UASBopt UASBopt --> PostPol PostPol --> UASBout RawDom --> RBCopt RBCopt --> RBCcl RBCcl --> RBCout classDef inlet fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef anae fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 classDef aero fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 classDef unit fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 classDef outlet fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q: With a neat sketch, explain the function of the three-phase separator in a UASB reactor.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full-marks structure):** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** The three-phase (gas–liquid–solid) separator is installed at the top of the UASB reactor to separate biogas, clarified effluent, and sludge granules internally. 2. **Functions (2 marks):** - **Biogas collection:** The hood collects rising CH₄/CO₂ bubbles and channels them to a gas pipe. - **Sludge retention:** Deflector plates bend downward-flowing sludge back into the digestion zone, preventing washout. - **Effluent clarification:** Outlet weirs collect clear supernatant while retaining biomass. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three advantages of RBC over conventional activated sludge process.** **Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per advantage):** 1. **Low energy consumption:** Rotating shaft requires only **~30–40 % of the energy** of an activated sludge aeration tank (no blowers required). 2. **Simple operation:** No sludge recirculation control; biofilm self-regulates thickness through shear-induced sloughing. 3. **Resistance to shock loads:** Biofilm buffers toxic spikes; recovery is faster than suspended-growth systems. 4. **(Bonus):** Excellent nitrification capability in the final stage due to low BOD environment. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]` **Q (a): Describe the construction and working principle of a UASB reactor with a neat labelled diagram. Explain the role of granular sludge. (7 marks)** **Model Answer (Part a):** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Cylindrical or rectangular** reactor made of RCC/MS with epoxy coating for corrosion resistance. - **Inlet distribution system** at the base — multiple feed pipes for uniform upflow. - **Sludge bed zone** at the bottom (height ≈ 1–2 m) housing dense granules. - **Sludge blanket zone** in the middle (height ≈ 2–3 m) — fluidized bed. - **Three-phase separator** at the top — gas hood, deflector plates, effluent weirs. - **Gas collection dome** with pressure safety valve and flow meter. - **Effluent outlet** via V-notch weirs. **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. Wastewater enters at the bottom and flows upward through the sludge bed. 2. **Acidogenic bacteria** hydrolyze complex organics to VFAs. 3. **Acetogenic bacteria** convert VFAs to acetic acid and H₂. 4. **Methanogenic archaea** convert acetic acid to CH₄ and CO₂. 5. Biogas bubbles rise → create gentle internal mixing → collected at top. 6. Sludge granules retained by deflectors; clarified effluent exits through weirs. **Role of Granular Sludge (1 mark):** - Granules (1–3 mm diameter) consist of stratified microbial layers — outer acidogens, inner methanogens. - Excellent settling velocity (>20 m/h) ensures biomass retention without carrier media. - Provides high SRT (>30 days) at low HRT (4–8 hours), enabling **high-rate** treatment. --- **Q (b): A UASB reactor receives 800 m³/d of brewery wastewater with $S_0$ = 25,000 mg/L COD and effluent $S_e$ = 2,500 mg/L. Assuming $Y_{obs}$ = 0.06 kg VSS/kg COD, calculate (i) the daily methane production at 25 °C, (ii) the equivalent electrical energy (assuming 35 % generator efficiency), and (iii) comment on the engineering significance. (7 marks)** **Model Answer (Part b):** **Step 1 — Compute COD removed:** $$ S_0 - S_e = (25{,}000 - 2{,}500)\,\text{mg/L} = 22{,}500\,\text{mg/L} = 22.5\,\text{kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Practical methane yield (at 25 °C):** $$ Y_{CH_4} = 0.40 - (1.42 \times 0.06) = 0.40 - 0.0852 = 0.3148\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{kg COD} $$ **Step 3 — Daily methane volume:** $$ G_{CH_4} = 800 \times 22.5 \times 0.3148 = 5666.4\,\text{m}^3\,\text{CH}_4/\text{d} $$ **Step 4 — Energy content:** $$ E_{thermal} = 5666.4 \times 35.8 = 202{,}857\,\text{MJ/d} = 56{,}349\,\text{kWh/d of thermal energy} $$ **Step 5 — Equivalent electrical energy (35 % generator efficiency):** $$ E_{elec} = 56{,}349 \times 0.35 = 19{,}722\,\text{kWh/d} \approx 19.7\,\text{MWh/d} $$ **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** This energy is sufficient to power **~2,000 Indian households** at typical consumption patterns. Brewery wastewater treatment via UASB achieves **simultaneous pollution control and renewable energy generation**, justifying the high capital cost through **payback in 3–5 years**. --- ### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternate]` **Q (a): Explain the construction, working, and design parameters of a Rotating Biological Contactor. State the role of biofilm. (7 marks)** **Model Answer (Part a):** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Shaft:** Horizontal mild-steel shaft supported by end bearings; typically 3–7 m long. - **Discs:** Polyethylene/polystyrene discs of 2–4 m diameter, mounted at 20–40 mm spacing. - **Tank:** RCC trough containing wastewater; depth ≈ 1–1.5 m. - **Drive mechanism:** Motor + gearbox providing 1–2 rpm constant rotation. - **Staging baffles:** Internal walls divide the tank into 3–4 sequential stages. **Working (3 marks):** 1. Wastewater enters Stage 1 and flows sequentially through stages. 2. **Submerged phase:** Disc absorbs organics and DO into biofilm; aerobic oxidation occurs. 3. **Emergence phase:** Biofilm absorbs atmospheric oxygen, releases CO₂, sheds excess biomass. 4. Final stages: low BOD allows **nitrifying bacteria** to dominate → NH₃ → NO₃⁻. 5. Sloughed biofilm leaves with effluent → removed in **final clarifier**. **Role of Biofilm (1 mark):** - Provides **diverse microbial ecology** in a thin layer — bacteria, protozoa, fungi, algae. - **High SRT independent of HRT** → efficient slow-growers (nitrifiers) thrive. - Buffers toxic shocks and hydraulic surges better than suspended-growth systems. --- **Q (b): A municipal STP uses RBC with 4 stages. Each stage has 200 discs of diameter 3 m. Submergence = 40 %. Influent flow = 2000 m³/d with BOD = 200 mg/L. Find (i) total effective wetted area, (ii) organic loading rate, (iii) number of discs required if design OLR = 10 g BOD/m²·d. (7 marks)** **Model Answer (Part b):** **Step 1 — Effective wetted area per disc:** $$ A_{disc,\,effective} = \alpha \times \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2} = 0.40 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 9}{2} = 0.40 \times 14.137 = 5.655\,\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Total effective wetted area for 800 discs:** $$ A_{total} = 800 \times 5.655 = 4524\,\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — BOD load applied:** $$ L_B = Q \times BOD = 2000\,\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 0.200\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 400\,\text{kg BOD/d} = 400{,}000\,\text{g BOD/d} $$ **Step 4 — Actual OLR:** $$ \text{OLR}_{actual} = \dfrac{400{,}000}{4524} = 88.4\,\text{g BOD/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{d} $$ > [!WARNING] > **This value is extremely high!** Typical RBC design OLR is **5–20 g BOD/m²·d**. The result indicates **overloading** — either more discs needed or flow must be reduced. **Step 5 — Required disc count at design OLR = 10 g BOD/m²·d:** $$ N_{required} = \dfrac{400{,}000}{10 \times 5.655} = \dfrac{400{,}000}{56.55} = 7074\,\text{discs} $$ **Engineering Comment:** The STP requires ~9× more discs. Either install additional shafts or reduce hydraulic load — this is a **classic KTU design revision problem**. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in UASB/RBC Questions (from KTU Valuation Patterns):** > > 1. **Sketch without labels** — Always label *all* major components. Unlabelled sketches get **zero** in the 7-mark "with neat sketch" questions. > 2. **Confusing HRT with SRT** — UASB's biggest strength is **high SRT at low HRT**. Mixing these terms loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the 1.42 COD-biomass conversion factor** — A frequent blunder in methane yield derivations. Examiners deduct **full 2 marks** for this omission. > 4. **Writing "UASB produces methane" without stoichiometry** — Always state the **Buswell equation** or the equivalent COD-CH₄ relationship. > 5. **Forgetting disc submergence factor (α)** in RBC area calculations. Many students use the full $\pi D^2$, which over-estimates area by **2.5×**. > 6. **No mention of three-phase separator in UASB** — it carries **2 marks standalone**. > 7. **Confusing RBC with MBBR or trickling filter** — RBC has **moving discs**, not fixed media. Examiners are strict on terminology. > 8. **Wrong units in OLR** — Always express as **kg COD/m³·d (UASB)** or **g BOD/m²·d (RBC)**. Unit errors are **automatic -1 mark**. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters** ### UASB Reactor - ⭐ **High-rate anaerobic** system with **upflow** through granular sludge blanket. - ⭐ Developed by **Gatze Lettinga** at Wageningen University (Netherlands, 1970s). - ⭐ **Four zones:** Inlet distributor → Sludge bed → Blanket → Three-phase separator. - ⭐ **Three-phase separator** retains biomass, collects biogas, clarifies effluent. - ⭐ **HRT** = 4–12 h; **OLR** = 5–15 kg COD/m³·d; **upflow velocity** = 0.5–1.5 m/h. - ⭐ **Methane yield:** $Y_{CH_4}$ = **0.40 m³/kg COD (theoretical at 25 °C)**. - ⭐ **Practical yield** subtracts biomass synthesis: $Y_{CH_4,\,p} = Y_{CH_4,th} - 1.42 \cdot Y_{obs}$. - ⭐ **COD equivalent of biomass** = 1.42 g COD/g VSS — a critical constant. - ⭐ **Biogas composition:** 60–70 % CH₄, 30–40 % CO₂; calorific value ≈ 20–25 MJ/m³. - ⭐ **Applications:** High-strength industrial (brewery, distillery, dairy, sugarcane, pulp & paper). - ⭐ **Disadvantages:** Slow granulation (2–6 months), temperature sensitive, unsuitable for low COD (<1000 mg/L). ### Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) - ⭐ **Attached-growth aerobic** system with rotating discs. - ⭐ Discs rotate at **1–2 rpm** with **30–40 % submergence**. - ⭐ **Three-phase biofilm cycle:** Submerged (BOD uptake) → Emergence (oxygen uptake) → Drainage → Repeat. - ⭐ **Multi-stage configuration:** 3–4 stages; first stages for carbonaceous BOD, last stage for **nitrification**. - ⭐ **OLR** = 5–20 g BOD/m²·d; disc area formula $A = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2}$. - ⭐ **Tip speed** $U = \dfrac{\pi D N_{rpm}}{60}$ m/s — governs biofilm shear. - ⭐ **Advantages:** Low energy, simple operation, no blowers, excellent nitrification. - ⭐ **Disadvantages:** Shaft/bearing maintenance, shaft enclosure needed in cold climates, sensitive to disc spacing. - ⭐ **Applications:** Small communities (≤ 50,000 PE), decentralized STPs, industrial wastewater polishing. ### Comparison Mnemonics - 🧠 **"UASB = U = Upflow + Under (anaerobic) / Underground reactor"** - 🧠 **"RBC = R = Rotates + Reoxygenates + Removes BOD"** - 🧠 **"UASB for HIGH-strength, RBC for LOW-medium strength wastewater."** ### Numbers to Memorize (High-Yield Constants) - 📌 $Y_{CH_4,th}$ (25 °C) = **0.40 m³/kg COD removed** - 📌 COD per gram VSS = **1.42 g/g** - 📌 RBC disc submergence $\alpha$ = **0.40** - 📌 RBC rotational speed = **1.5 rpm** (typical design value) - 📌 UASB upflow velocity design = **1.0 m/h** - 📌 Calorific value of CH₄ ≈ **35.8 MJ/m³** - 📌 UASB COD removal efficiency = **80–95 %** - 📌 RBC BOD removal efficiency = **85–95 %**

topics.title: Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Aerated Lagoon** is a low-cost, suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment system in which mechanical surface aerators (or diffused-air systems) are used to introduce dissolved oxygen into an earthen or lined basin. The aeration serves two simultaneous purposes: (i) supply of oxygen for the **aerobic biological oxidation** of organic matter, and (ii) maintenance of the **microbial biomass in suspension**, thereby preventing anaerobic stratification and odor formation.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECHT725)** and the standard wastewater engineering classification (Metcalf & Eddy / CPHEEO Manual), aerated lagoons are positioned between conventional activated sludge processes and facultative lagoons in terms of:

- **Capital cost** (low, since no concrete reactors are required)
- **Energy intensity** (moderate, due to continuous aerator operation)
- **Effluent quality** (better than facultative lagoons, comparable to a poorly operated activated sludge unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Aerated Lagoons are explicitly listed under *Low-Cost / Decentralized Wastewater Treatment Systems*, alongside stabilization ponds, constructed wetlands, and septic tanks. They are frequently tested as either a 3-mark definition question or a 14-mark design problem.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Giant Aerated Fish Pond":**
> Imagine a large village fish pond that has been retrofitted with electric paddle wheels (the "aerators") spinning on the surface. The spinning paddles do two jobs at once: (1) they splash water into the air so oxygen dissolves into it (like a waterfall in a river), and (2) they keep the entire pond swirling so no sludge settles at the bottom. Tiny microorganisms floating in this swirling water "eat" the dissolved organic dirt (BOD) just as fish eat fish food. After a few days of residence, the water leaving the pond is much cleaner. That is essentially how an aerated lagoon works.

## 1.3 Classification of Aerated Lagoons

Aerated lagoons are classified based on the **oxygen regime** maintained in the basin:

| Type | Oxygen Status | Description | Typical HRT |
|:-----|:--------------|:------------|:-----------:|
| **Aerobic Aerated Lagoon** | Fully aerobic throughout | All biomass active; no bottom deposits | **5 – 10 days** |
| **Facultative Aerated Lagoon** | Aerobic at surface, anaerobic at bottom | Settleable solids accumulate and undergo anaerobic digestion | **10 – 40 days** |
| **Anaerobic Lagoon (predecessor)** | Anaerobic | Covered or deep; used for high-strength industrial wastewater | **20 – 50 days** |

## 1.4 Typical Configuration and Components

A conventional aerated lagoon system comprises:

1. **Inlet works** — bar screen, grit chamber, and flow distribution
2. **Earthen basin** — compacted clay or synthetic liner (HDPE, PVC) to prevent groundwater contamination
3. **Aeration system** — mechanical surface aerators OR diffused-air bottom diffusers
4. **Baffles / Dividers** — to prevent short-circuiting and ensure plug-flow behavior
5. **Outlet weir** — for clarified effluent discharge
6. **Sludge withdrawal** — periodic (typically every **5 – 10 years** for facultative lagoons)

> [!TIP]
> **Typical Design Depth (KTU Reference Value):**
> Aerated lagoons are usually designed for a depth of **2.5 m to 5.0 m**. Deeper lagoons reduce land footprint but require more aeration power; shallower lagoons have higher land cost but lower power demand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — Why Aerated Lagoons Work
