Aerated lagoons rely on the same **aerobic suspended-growth kinetics** as the activated sludge process, but with a key difference: **no sludge recirculation** is practiced. The biomass remains in the lagoon only for the duration equal to the **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**. This leads to:

- **Lower MLVSS** (Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids) compared to activated sludge
- **Lower treatment efficiency per unit volume**, which is why lagoons are large
- **Lower capital and operating cost**, since no clarifier or recycle system is needed

### 2.1.1 Microbial Ecology

The biomass in an aerated lagoon consists of:
- **Bacteria** — primary decomposers of organic carbon and ammonia
- **Protozoa** — consume free-swimming bacteria, polishing the effluent
- **Algae** — minimal (because lagoon is deep and turbid, light penetration is low)
- **Nitrifying bacteria** — if HRT is sufficient (**> 10 days** at warm temperatures)

## 2.2 Process Design Parameters (KTU Reference Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|:----------|:------:|:-------------:|:-----:|
| Hydraulic Retention Time | $\theta$ or $t$ | **5 – 30** | days |
| Depth | $H$ | **2.5 – 5.0** | m |
| BOD Loading | $L$ | **0.0025 – 0.005** | kg BOD/m³·d |
| F/M Ratio | F/M | **0.05 – 0.3** | kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d |
| MLVSS | $X$ | **100 – 300** | mg/L |
| Surface Aerator Power | $P$ | **5 – 15** | W/m³ |
| Effluent BOD | $S_e$ | **20 – 60** | mg/L |
| Effluent SS | $SS_e$ | **30 – 150** | mg/L |
| Temperature Coefficient | $\theta_T$ | **1.05 – 1.08** | dimensionless |
| Oxygen Transfer Efficiency | OTE | **1.2 – 2.4** | kg O₂/kWh |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for Aerated Lagoon design (KTU Module 2):**

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) |
| 2 | $V = Q \times \theta$ | Required lagoon volume |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{V}{H}$ | Required surface area at depth $H$ |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Volumetric BOD loading rate |
| 5 | $\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot \theta}$ | First-order BOD removal (Eckenfelder) |
| 6 | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta_T^{(T - 20)}$ | Temperature correction of $k$ |
| 7 | $O_2 = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X \cdot \theta$ | Oxygen demand (activated-sludge analogy) |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{O_2}{\text{OTE} \cdot \eta}$ | Aerator power requirement |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD removal efficiency (in %) |
| 10 | $\text{F/M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of aerated lagoons as "slow-motion activated sludge." Same kinetics, same oxygen demand formula, but $\theta$ is **10× to 30× larger**, and **recycle ratio $R = 0$**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aerated lagoons are extensively deployed in:

- **Small municipalities** (population **< 50,000**) in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Karnataka
- **Pulp & paper, distillery, and dairy industries** for high-strength wastewater pretreatment
- **Aquaculture and food processing** units
- **Coastal tourist resorts** where land is available but capital is limited

The major engineering trade-off is between **land area** and **aeration energy**. Kerala's tropical climate is favorable because the higher temperature accelerates biological reaction rates (i.e., larger $k$ values), reducing required HRT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)

**Definition:** HRT is the average time a fluid parcel spends inside the lagoon.

**Given:**
- $V$ = Volume of the lagoon in m³
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate of wastewater in m³/day

**Step 1 — Control Volume Assumption**
We treat the lagoon as a **completely mixed flow reactor (CMFR)** with no short-circuiting.

**Step 2 — Mass Balance Concept**
The volume of liquid entering the lagoon per day is $Q$. If the lagoon holds $V$ m³ of liquid, then on average, each m³ of liquid is replaced every $V/Q$ days.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Expression**

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q}$$

**Step 4 — Dimensional Check**

$$\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^3/\text{day}} = \text{day} \quad \checkmark$$

This is the foundational equation used in every subsequent design calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BOD Removal Using First-Order Kinetics (Eckenfelder Model)

**Statement:** BOD removal in an aerated lagoon follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the residual substrate concentration.

**Assumptions:**
- Completely mixed flow regime
- Constant temperature
- No sludge accumulation (or steady-state)
- No inhibition

**Step 1 — Reaction Rate Equation**

$$-\frac{dS}{dt} = k \cdot S$$

where:
- $S$ = residual BOD at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (day⁻¹)

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables**

$$\frac{dS}{S} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3 — Integration from $S_0$ to $S_e$ over time $\theta$**

$$\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = -k \int_{0}^{\theta} dt$$

**Step 4 — Result After Integration**

$$\ln\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = -k \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Exponential Form (Solving for $S_e/S_0$)**

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k \cdot \theta}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent Linearized Form (used in KTU problems)**

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot \theta}$$

This is the **Eckenfelder equation** for plug-flow approximation, which is algebraically equivalent for small $k\theta$.

**Step 7 — Temperature Correction (Streeter-Phelps Law)**

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta_T^{(T - 20)}$$

where:
- $k_T$ = rate constant at temperature $T$ (°C)
- $k_{20}$ = rate constant at 20 °C (typical value: **0.25 – 0.35 day⁻¹** for lagoons)
- $\theta_T$ = temperature coefficient (**1.05 – 1.08**)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Area from HRT and Depth

**Given:**
- Design flow $Q$ (m³/day)
- Required HRT $\theta$ (days)
- Selected lagoon depth $H$ (m)

**Step 1 — Compute the Volume Needed**

$$V = Q \times \theta$$

**Step 2 — Relate Volume to Area and Depth**

$$V = A \times H$$

where $A$ = surface area in m²

**Step 3 — Solve for Area**

$$A = \frac{V}{H}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hectares (Engineering Practice)**

$$A_{\text{ha}} = \frac{A}{10{,}000}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Oxygen Demand and Aerator Power

**Statement:** The total oxygen required by an aerated lagoon is the sum of oxygen for **carbonaceous BOD oxidation** plus **endogenous respiration**.

**Step 1 — Empirical Oxygen Demand Formula (Activated Sludge Analogy)**

$$O_2 = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X \cdot \theta$$

where:
- $O_2$ = oxygen demand rate (kg O₂/day)
- $a$ = synthesis coefficient (typically **0.40 – 0.60 kg O₂/kg BOD removed**)
- $S_r$ = BOD removed per day = $Q(S_0 - S_e)$ (kg/day)
- $b$ = endogenous coefficient (typically **0.05 – 0.15 day⁻¹**)
- $X$ = MLVSS concentration (mg/L)
- $\theta$ = HRT (days)

**Step 2 — Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate (SOTR)**

The aerator must deliver this $O_2$ demand, accounting for field conditions:

$$\text{SOTR} = \frac{O_2}{\text{OTE} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}}$$

where:
- OTE = Oxygen Transfer Efficiency in field conditions (kg O₂/kWh)
- $\eta_{\text{motor}}$ = motor efficiency (typically **0.90 – 0.95**)

**Step 3 — Aerator Power Calculation**

$$P_{\text{aerator}} = \frac{O_2 \, (\text{kg/day})}{24 \times \text{OTE} \, (\text{kg O}_2/\text{kWh}) \times \eta_{\text{motor}}}$$

Result is in **kW**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Design an aerated lagoon for a small community with the following data:
> - Average flow, $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/day
> - Influent BOD, $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
> - Effluent BOD requirement, $S_e = 30$ mg/L
> - Lagoon temperature, $T = 25$ °C
> - Selected depth, $H = 3.0$ m
> - First-order rate constant at 20 °C, $k_{20} = 0.30$ day⁻¹
> - Temperature coefficient, $\theta_T = 1.06$
> - MLVSS, $X = 150$ mg/L
> - Synthesis coefficient, $a = 0.50$ kg O₂/kg BOD
> - Endogenous coefficient, $b = 0.10$ day⁻¹
> - Aerator OTE = **1.8 kg O₂/kWh**
> - Motor efficiency = **0.92**

### Solution

**Step 1 — Temperature-corrected rate constant**

$$k_{25} = 0.30 \times (1.06)^{(25 - 20)} = 0.30 \times (1.06)^5$$

$$(1.06)^5 = 1.3382$$

$$k_{25} = 0.30 \times 1.3382 = 0.4015 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Required HRT from First-Order Equation**

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta}$$

$$\frac{30}{250} = 0.12 = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4015 \cdot \theta}$$

$$1 + 0.4015\theta = \frac{1}{0.12} = 8.333$$

$$0.4015\theta = 8.333 - 1 = 7.333$$

$$\theta = \frac{7.333}{0.4015} = 18.27 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Lagoon Volume**

$$V = Q \times \theta = 5{,}000 \times 18.27 = 91{,}350 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Surface Area**

$$A = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{91{,}350}{3.0} = 30{,}450 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Area in Hectares**

$$A_{\text{ha}} = \frac{30{,}450}{10{,}000} = 3.045 \text{ ha}$$

**Step 6 — BOD Removed per Day**

$$S_r = Q \times (S_0 - S_e) = 5{,}000 \times (250 - 30) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$S_r = 5{,}000 \times 0.220 = 1{,}100 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**Step 7 — Total Oxygen Demand**

Convert $X$ to kg/m³:

$$X = 150 \text{ mg/L} = 0.150 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$O_2 = (0.50)(1{,}100) + (0.10)(0.150)(91{,}350)$$

$$O_2 = 550 + 0.015 \times 91{,}350$$

$$O_2 = 550 + 1{,}370.25 = 1{,}920.25 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 8 — Aerator Power**

$$P = \frac{1{,}920.25}{24 \times 1.8 \times 0.92}$$

$$P = \frac{1{,}920.25}{39.744} = 48.31 \text{ kW}$$

### Final Design Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|------:|
| HRT ($\theta$) | **18.3 days** |
| Volume ($V$) | **91,350 m³** |
| Depth ($H$) | **3.0 m** |
| Area ($A$) | **30,450 m² ≈ 3.05 ha** |
| Oxygen demand | **1,920 kg O₂/day** |
| Aerator power | **≈ 48.3 kW** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define an aerated lagoon. Mention its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **aerated lagoon** is a low-cost, suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment system in which mechanical surface aerators or diffused-air systems are used to supply oxygen and keep the microbial biomass in suspension inside an earthen or lined basin. The main components are: **(1) Inlet works** (bar screen and grit chamber), **(2) Earthen basin with liner**, **(3) Aeration system** (surface aerators or diffusers), **(4) Baffles** to prevent short-circuiting, **(5) Outlet weir**, and **(6) Sludge withdrawal pipeline**.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between facultative pond and aerated lagoon.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Facultative Pond | Aerated Lagoon |
|:----------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Oxygen source | **Algal photosynthesis** | **Mechanical aerators** |
| Depth | **1.0 – 2.0 m** | **2.5 – 5.0 m** |
| HRT | **20 – 180 days** | **5 – 30 days** |
| Land requirement | **Very high** | **Moderate** |
| Power consumption | **None** | **Moderate (5–15 W/m³)** |
| Effluent BOD | **20 – 50 mg/L** | **20 – 60 mg/L** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle, types, and design parameters of aerated lagoons. Discuss the advantages and limitations of aerated lagoon systems.

**(b)** A small township generates an average wastewater flow of **4,500 m³/day** with influent BOD of **220 mg/L**. Design an aerated lagoon to produce an effluent with BOD not exceeding **25 mg/L**. Assume lagoon depth **H = 3.0 m**, rate constant at 25 °C is **k = 0.40 day⁻¹**, OTE of aerator **= 1.6 kg O₂/kWh**, motor efficiency **= 0.90**, synthesis coefficient **a = 0.48**, endogenous coefficient **b = 0.08 day⁻¹**, and MLVSS **X = 130 mg/L**. State all assumptions.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

**Principle:** Aerated lagoons operate on the **aerobic suspended-growth principle**, where dissolved oxygen supplied by mechanical aerators supports bacterial oxidation of organic matter (BOD). The aerators simultaneously keep the biomass in suspension and prevent anaerobic conditions.

**Types (2 marks):**
1. **Aerobic Aerated Lagoon** — fully aerobic, depth 2.5–4 m, HRT 5–10 days
2. **Facultative Aerated Lagoon** — aerobic surface layer with anaerobic bottom, depth 3–5 m, HRT 10–40 days
3. **Anaerobic Lagoon (covered or deep)** — depth > 5 m, used for high-strength industrial wastewater

**Design Parameters (2 marks):** HRT, depth, BOD loading rate, F/M ratio, MLVSS, aerator OTE, temperature coefficient, and oxygen demand — all as listed in the formula sheet of Section 2.

**Advantages:**
- Low capital cost (no concrete tanks or clarifiers)
- Simple operation and maintenance
- Tolerates shock loads and toxic inflows
- Suitable for small communities
- No sludge recirculation required

**Limitations:**
- Large land area required
- Continuous power supply needed
- Effluent quality is moderate; may require polishing
- Possible odor and mosquito nuisance if under-aerated
- Suspended solids in effluent are relatively high

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Required HRT (Eckenfelder Equation)**

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta}$$

$$\frac{25}{220} = 0.1136 = \frac{1}{1 + 0.40\theta}$$

$$1 + 0.40\theta = \frac{1}{0.1136} = 8.803$$

$$0.40\theta = 7.803$$

$$\theta = 19.51 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Lagoon Volume**

$$V = Q \times \theta = 4{,}500 \times 19.51 = 87{,}795 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Surface Area**

$$A = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{87{,}795}{3.0} = 29{,}265 \text{ m}^2 = 2.93 \text{ ha}$$

**Step 4 — BOD Removed**

$$S_r = Q(S_0 - S_e) = 4{,}500 \times (220 - 25) \times 10^{-3} = 877.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Oxygen Demand**

Convert $X$: 130 mg/L = 0.130 kg/m³

$$O_2 = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X \cdot V$$

$$O_2 = (0.48)(877.5) + (0.08)(0.130)(87{,}795)$$

$$O_2 = 421.2 + 913.07 = 1{,}334.27 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{day}$$

**Step 6 — Aerator Power**

$$P = \frac{O_2}{24 \times \text{OTE} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}} = \frac{1{,}334.27}{24 \times 1.6 \times 0.90}$$

$$P = \frac{1{,}334.27}{34.56} = 38.61 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Design Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|------:|
| HRT | **19.5 days** |
| Volume | **87,795 m³** |
| Depth | **3.0 m** |
| Surface area | **2.93 ha** |
| Oxygen demand | **1,334 kg O₂/day** |
| Aerator power | **≈ 38.6 kW** |

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an aerated lagoon. Compare it with an activated sludge process.

**(b)** An aerated lagoon of volume **80,000 m³** treats a wastewater flow of **4,000 m³/day** with influent BOD of **300 mg/L**. If the first-order rate constant at the operating temperature is **k = 0.35 day⁻¹**, determine: (i) HRT, (ii) effluent BOD, and (iii) BOD removal efficiency. Also compute the surface area if depth is **3.5 m**.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An aerated lagoon consists of an earthen basin lined with clay or HDPE/PVC synthetic liner to prevent seepage into groundwater. Mechanical surface aerators (commonly **2–6 units** of floating or fixed-bracket type) are mounted along the lagoon length. Baffles or curtain walls are placed at 90° to the flow to convert mixed flow to plug flow. An outlet weir discharges treated effluent into a polishing pond or directly into a receiving water body. Periodic sludge removal is done once every 5–10 years.

**Working (2 marks):**
Wastewater enters the lagoon and is continuously aerated. Aerators supply **oxygen** for aerobic bacteria, which oxidize BOD into CO₂ and H₂O. The turbulence keeps biomass suspended and ensures uniform contact. After the HRT period, clarified effluent exits via the outlet weir.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Aerated Lagoon | Activated Sludge |
|:--------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| HRT | **5–30 days** | **4–8 hours** |
| Reactor | **Earthen basin** | **Concrete tank** |
| Clarifier | **Not required** | **Required** |
| Sludge recycle | **None** | **Yes (R = 0.25–0.75)** |
| MLVSS | **100–300 mg/L** | **1500–4000 mg/L** |
| Effluent quality | **Moderate** | **High** |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — HRT**

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{80{,}000}{4{,}000} = 20 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Effluent BOD (Eckenfelder)**

$$\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta} = \frac{1}{1 + (0.35)(20)} = \frac{1}{1 + 7} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

$$S_e = 0.125 \times 300 = 37.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — BOD Removal Efficiency**

$$E = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{300 - 37.5}{300} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 4 — Surface Area**

$$A = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{80{,}000}{3.5} = 22{,}857 \text{ m}^2 = 2.29 \text{ ha}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg/L to kg/m³** in the oxygen demand formula — a single unit error loses **2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Using the wrong BOD model** — Many students incorrectly use the **Streeter-Phelps dissolved-oxygen sag equation** instead of the **Eckenfelder first-order model**. Aerated lagoons use Eckenfelder, not Streeter-Phelps.
> 3. **Omitting temperature correction** — At $k$ values, you must use $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta_T^{(T-20)}$ if temperature is not 20 °C. Skipping this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Concluding that $S_e$ from first-order model can be zero** — Mathematically, $S_e \to 0$ requires $\theta \to \infty$, which is impossible in practice. Always state design assumptions.
> 5. **No mention of lining** in construction questions — KTU expects the answer to mention **clay or HDPE liner** for groundwater protection; missing this loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- [ ] **Definition** — Aerated lagoon = earthen basin + mechanical aeration + suspended-growth aerobic process
- [ ] **Three types** — Aerobic, Facultative, Anaerobic (deep lagoon)
- [ ] **Typical depth** — **2.5 to 5.0 m**
- [ ] **Typical HRT** — **5 to 30 days** (much longer than activated sludge)
- [ ] **BOD model** — Eckenfelder first-order: $S_e/S_0 = 1/(1 + k\theta)$
- [ ] **Temperature correction** — $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta_T^{(T-20)}$
- [ ] **Oxygen demand** — $O_2 = a \cdot S_r + b \cdot X \cdot V$
- [ ] **Aerator power** — $P = O_2 / (24 \times \text{OTE} \times \eta_{\text{motor}})$
- [ ] **MLVSS** — Low, **100–300 mg/L** (no recycle)
- [ ] **Effluent BOD** — Moderate, **20–60 mg/L**; polishing usually needed
- [ ] **F/M ratio** — **0.05 – 0.3 kg BOD/kg MLVSS·d**
- [ ] **Lagoon area unit** — Always convert m² to hectares: divide by 10,000
- [ ] **No recycle, no clarifier** — these are the two cost-saving features
- [ ] **Sludge removal** — Once every 5–10 years (facultative) — major O&M advantage
- [ ] **KTU-favorite keywords** — "low-cost," "decentralized," "small communities," "tropical climate advantage," "mechanical aeration"

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **stabilization pond** is a shallow, earthen basin designed to treat wastewater through the symbiotic interaction of **bacteria and algae** under natural solar energy, with negligible mechanical aeration. Per the KTU 2024 OECHT725 syllabus, stabilization ponds are classified as **natural / low-energy biological treatment systems** in which organic matter is stabilized by a combination of **aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic microbiological activity**, governed by depth, detention time, and solar radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Highlight**
> *“Stabilization ponds are engineered impoundments that utilize photosynthesis-driven oxygenation and gravity settling to achieve carbonaceous BOD removal, pathogen reduction, and partial nutrient removal without continuous external energy input.”*

The three principal categories recognised in the KTU 2024 scheme are:

| Pond Type | Dominant Microbiology | Typical Depth | Role in Treatment Train |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerobic (Maturation) Pond** | Strict aerobes + algae | 0.3 – 0.45 m | Polishing / pathogen kill |
| **Facultative Pond** | Aerobic surface + anaerobic bottom | 1.0 – 2.5 m | Primary BOD removal |
| **Anaerobic Pond** | Strict anaerobes | 2.5 – 5.0 m | High-strength BOD reduction |

A fourth, modern variant — the **High-Rate Algal Pond (HRAP)** — is also part of the Module 2 syllabus for advanced wastewater engineering.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Aquarium"**
>
> Imagine a giant, sunlit outdoor swimming pool filled with **green water**. The green colour comes from **algae** (microscopic plants). The pool receives dirty water (sewage) from a pipe. Inside the pool:
>
> 1. **Sun** falls on the surface → **algae** grow → release **oxygen (O₂)** via photosynthesis.
> 2. **Bacteria** living in the same water use that oxygen to **eat the sewage (BOD)**, just like fish eat food.
> 3. Bacteria exhale **CO₂**, which algae use to grow more.
> 4. The cycle repeats: **Algae ↔ Bacteria ↔ Sunlight → Clean water**.
>
> The deeper the pool, the less sunlight reaches the bottom, so the bottom turns into an **anaerobic** zone (no oxygen) where different bacteria break down sludge. This is exactly the **facultative pond** — a two-storey building: **top floor aerobic, basement anaerobic**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are **must-know** for KTU 2024 valuation:

- **Oxygen yield from algae:** ≈ **1.5 g O₂ produced per 1.0 g algae synthesised**
- **Photosynthetic O₂ stoichiometry:** 32 g O₂ per 180 g glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)
- **BOD-to-biomass yield (Y):** ≈ **0.65 g algae per g BOD removed**
- **Solar constant at earth’s surface:** ≈ **1000 W/m²** (peak)
- **Typical land requirement:** ≈ **1 hectare per 100 m³/day of municipal sewage**
- **Pathogen die-off rate (Coliform, k₂):** **0.7 – 1.5 /day** at 20 °C
- **BOD removal efficiency (overall pond system):** **75 – 95 %**
- **Faecal coliform removal:** **90 – 99.9 % (1 – 4 log units)**

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Algae–Bacteria Symbiosis — The Heart of Pond Engineering

The pond is a **closed biological loop**. Let us break down the **“Why” and “How”** step-by-step:

### Step 1 — Organic Loading Enters the Pond
- Raw or settled wastewater enters the facultative pond carrying **dissolved BOD** (typically **150 – 350 mg/L** for domestic sewage).
- Suspended solids settle in the upper anaerobic layer, forming the **bottom sludge blanket**.

### Step 2 — Bacterial Oxidation (Top Aerobic Zone)
- Aerobic heterotrophic bacteria (**Zoogloea, Pseudomonas, Flavobacterium**) consume the dissolved BOD.
- The stoichiometric demand: **C₅H₇NO₂ + 5 O₂ → 5 CO₂ + 2 H₂O + NH₃ + energy (bacterial biomass)**.
- Bacteria need **≈ 1.5 kg O₂ per kg BOD oxidised** (theoretical, real = 1.0 – 1.3 due to synthesis).

### Step 3 — Photosynthetic Oxygen Supply (Light Penetration Zone)
- **Euglena, Chlorella, Scenedesmus** (algae) use sunlight (PAR, 400–700 nm) to perform:
  $$\text{6 CO}_2 + \text{6 H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\;h\nu\;} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + \text{6 O}_2$$
- Net **gross oxygen production rate**:
  $$\text{GOP} = 0.25 \times I_s \times f \quad \left[\text{kg O}_2/\text{ha·day}\right]$$
  where $I_s$ = incident solar radiation (cal/cm²·day) and $f$ = photosynthetic efficiency (0.04 – 0.06).

### Step 4 — Anaerobic Digestion (Bottom Zone, depth > 1 m)
- The sludge blanket is decomposed by **methanogenic archaea**:
  $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow \text{3 CH}_4 + \text{3 CO}_2$$
- Methane may bubble up and escape — a small but useful energy byproduct.

### Step 5 — Recycling of CO₂
- CO₂ released by bacteria is the **carbon substrate for algae** → completes the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Bacteria and algae are **metabolic mirrors** — what one exhales, the other needs. The pond is therefore a *self-sustaining* ecosystem *provided* sunlight is adequate (typically ≥ 4 hr bright sunshine/day).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

The following table consolidates **every equation** the KTU examiner can reasonably ask in the 14-mark question on Stabilization Ponds. All units are SI / KTU-preferred.

| # | Formula / Design Criterion | Symbols & Units | Application / Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Areal (Surface) BOD Loading** $\lambda_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{A}$ | $\lambda_s$ in kg BOD/ha·day; $Q$ in m³/d; $S_i$ in mg/L; $A$ in ha | Facultative pond design |
| 2 | **Volumetric BOD Loading** $\lambda_v = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{V}$ | $\lambda_v$ in kg BOD/m³·day | Anaerobic pond sizing |
| 3 | **Hydraulic Detention Time** $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q}$ | $t$ in days; $H$ = pond depth (m) | All pond types |
| 4 | **Complete-Mix BOD Effluent** $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_1 t}$ | $k_1$ = first-order BOD rate constant (1/day) | Aerobic & facultative ponds |
| 5 | **Marais–Shaw Temperature Correction** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | $\theta \approx 1.05$ (facultative), $1.085$ (anaerobic) | Temperature-adjusted kinetics |
| 6 | **Plug-Flow BOD Effluent** $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = e^{-k_1 t}$ | — | Approximation for long narrow ponds |
| 7 | **Wehner–Wilhelm (Dispersed Flow)** $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{4a\,e^{1/(2d)}}{(1+a)^2 e^{a/(2d)} - (1-a)^2 e^{-a/(2d)}}$ | $a = \sqrt{1+4 k_1 t d}$; $d$ = dispersion number | Real ponds (best fit) |
| 8 | **Faecal Coliform (Chick’s Law)** $N_e = N_i \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$ | $k_2$ = coliform die-off constant (0.7 – 1.5 /d) | Pathogen reduction |
| 9 | **Algal Oxygen Production** $\text{O}_2 = 1.5 \times \text{Algae produced (g/m²/d)}$ | Empirical coefficient **1.5** | Oxygen balance check |
| 10 | **Net Oxygen Available** $\text{O}_{2,\text{net}} = \text{GOP} - \text{SOD} - \text{Respiration}$ | GOP = gross O₂ production; SOD = sediment oxygen demand | Aerobic-anaerobic balance |
| 11 | **BOD-to-Algae Yield** $X_a = Y \cdot (S_i - S_e)$ | $Y \approx 0.65$ g algae / g BOD removed | Algal biomass estimation |
| 12 | **Land Area** $A = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{\lambda_s}$ | $A$ in ha | Pond footprint design |
| 13 | **Weir Overflow Rate** $\text{WOR} = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | $L_w$ = weir length (m) | Effluent outlet design |
| 14 | **Pond Geometry Ratio** $L:B = 2:1$ to $3:1$ | Prevents short-circuiting | Layout |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner’s Tip:** Marks are routinely lost when students write $\lambda_s$ and $\lambda_v$ without units, or use $S_i$ in mg/L together with $Q$ in m³/d **without converting** to kg. **Always convert:** $Q \cdot S_i$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times \text{mg/L} = \text{kg/d} \times 10^{-3}$ — multiply by $10^{-3}$ to get kg.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stabilization ponds are the **backbone of decentralised wastewater management** in:

- **Tropical developing regions** (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka) — where year-round high solar irradiance (5–6 kWh/m²/day) makes algae-based treatment economical.
- **Small towns (< 50,000 population)** where O&M budgets cannot afford activated sludge plants.
- **Industrial applications** — especially in treating **distillery spent wash, sugar-mill effluent, dairy wastewater**, and **palm oil mill effluent (POME)**, where anaerobic ponds alone can achieve 60–80 % BOD reduction.
- **Aquaculture integration** — HRAPs are used to grow **Spirulina, Chlorella**, and **Scenedesmus** for feed, nutraceuticals, and biofertilisers — converting a treatment plant into a *resource recovery facility*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry example:** The *Bairabi Wastewater Treatment Plant* and several *Suchitwa Mission* projects in Kerala use **3-stage pond systems** (anaerobic → facultative → maturation) for towns < 20,000 PE, achieving BOD < 30 mg/L and FC < 1000 MPN/100 mL at a fraction of activated-sludge CAPEX.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Complete-Mix First-Order BOD Kinetics in a Pond

**Starting point:** A well-mixed pond with volumetric flow $Q$ (m³/day), volume $V$ (m³), influent BOD $S_i$ (mg/L), effluent BOD $S_e$ (mg/L), and first-order rate constant $k_1$ (1/day).

**Step 1 — Mass-balance on BOD across the pond:**

Mass in − Mass out − Mass consumed by reaction = Mass accumulated.

For **steady state** (accumulation = 0):

$$Q \cdot S_i - Q \cdot S_e - r \cdot V = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rate expression for biological reaction:**

For a first-order reaction, the rate of BOD consumption is proportional to the **remaining BOD concentration** (assumed to equal effluent concentration under complete-mix):

$$r = k_1 \cdot S_e \quad \text{(mg/L/day)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the mass balance:**

$$Q \cdot S_i - Q \cdot S_e = k_1 \cdot S_e \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $S_e$:**

$$Q \cdot S_i = Q \cdot S_e + k_1 \cdot S_e \cdot V$$

$$Q \cdot S_i = S_e \left( Q + k_1 \cdot V \right)$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $Q$:**

$$S_i = S_e \left( 1 + \dfrac{k_1 \cdot V}{Q} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Recognise detention time $t = V / Q$:**

$$S_i = S_e \left( 1 + k_1 \cdot t \right)$$

**Step 7 — Final compact result:**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_1 \, t}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $S_e/S_i$ is the **fraction of BOD remaining** after treatment. Higher $k_1$ (faster biology) and higher $t$ (longer detention) → lower remaining fraction → cleaner effluent.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Correction of the Rate Constant (Marais–Shaw, 1961)

The standard reference temperature is **20 °C**. The rate constant at any other temperature $T$ (°C) is:

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$$

**Step 1 — Reference values for different pond types (from KTU accepted literature):**

- Facultative pond: $k_{20} = 0.30 \;\text{day}^{-1}$, $\theta = 1.05$
- Anaerobic pond: $k_{20} = 0.60 \;\text{day}^{-1}$, $\theta = 1.085$

**Step 2 — Worked example (Facultative pond, T = 30 °C):**

$$k_{30} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^{(30-20)} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^{10}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power:**

$$(1.05)^{10} = 1.6289$$

**Step 4 — Final value:**

$$k_{30} = 0.30 \times 1.6289 = 0.4887 \;\text{day}^{-1} \approx 0.49 \;\text{day}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **10 °C rise** nearly **doubles** the BOD removal rate — this is why **Kerala’s tropical climate (avg 28–32 °C)** makes stabilization ponds perform 50–60 % better than the same pond in temperate regions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Marais–Shaw Sizing Equation for a Facultative Pond

**Goal:** Determine the **required pond area $A$** (ha) given influent BOD $S_i$ and target effluent BOD $S_e$.

**Step 1 — Start from complete-mix BOD equation:**

$$S_e = \dfrac{S_i}{1 + k_T t}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for detention time $t$:**

$$S_e (1 + k_T t) = S_i$$

$$1 + k_T t = \dfrac{S_i}{S_e}$$

$$k_T t = \dfrac{S_i}{S_e} - 1 = \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e}$$

$$t = \dfrac{1}{k_T} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of the pond:**

$$V = Q \cdot t = \dfrac{Q}{k_T} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e}$$

**Step 4 — Area (using $V = A \cdot H$, where $H$ is the operating depth):**

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{Q}{k_T \cdot H} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e}$$

**Step 5 — Convert $Q$ from m³/day and $H$ from m so that $A$ ends in ha ($10^4$ m²):**

$$A = \dfrac{Q \cdot (S_i - S_e)}{k_T \cdot H \cdot S_e \cdot 10^4} \quad \text{(ha)}$$

**Step 6 — Final design-friendly form:**

$$\boxed{\;A = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{k_T \cdot H \cdot S_e \cdot 10^4 / \left(1 - \dfrac{S_e}{S_i}\right)}\;}$$

For KTU board problems, students may simply use the **standard surface loading form:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{\lambda_s}$$

with $\lambda_s = 110 - 225$ kg BOD/ha·day (tropical facultative pond) as a cross-check.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wehner–Wilhelm Equation (Dispersed Flow)

For **real ponds** that lie between plug flow ($d = 0$) and complete mix ($d \to \infty$), use the Wehner–Wilhelm equation:

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{4 a \, e^{\frac{1}{2d}}}{\left(1+a\right)^2 e^{\frac{a}{2d}} - \left(1-a\right)^2 e^{-\frac{a}{2d}}}$$

where:

$$a = \sqrt{1 + 4 \, k_1 \, t \, d}$$

$$d = \dfrac{E}{u \cdot L} = \text{dispersion number}$$

**Step 1 — Limiting case 1: $d \to 0$ (plug flow).**
Using the small-argument expansion $e^x \to 1 + x$, the equation reduces to:

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 2 — Limiting case 2: $d \to \infty$ (complete mix).**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $e^{1/(2d)}$ and letting $d \to \infty$:

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_1 t}$$

**Step 3 — Typical $d$ values for stabilization ponds:**

| Pond Geometry | Approximate $d$ |
|---|---|
| Long, narrow, multi-inlet | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| Square, single inlet–outlet | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Circular, central feed | 4.0 – 8.0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Treating a facultative pond as a perfect plug flow reactor. Real ponds are **dispersed** — use $d \approx 0.5 - 1.0$ for design unless the pond is unusually long and narrow (L:B > 5:1).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pond-Type Comparison

| Parameter | Aerobic Pond | Facultative Pond | Anaerobic Pond | HRAP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Depth (m) | 0.3 – 0.45 | 1.0 – 2.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 | 0.3 – 0.45 |
| Detention time (d) | 3 – 5 | 5 – 30 (tropical: 7–15) | 20 – 50 | 2 – 6 |
| $\lambda_s$ (kg BOD/ha·d) | 67 – 135 | 110 – 225 (tropics) | 220 – 560 | 150 – 250 |
| $k_1$ at 20 °C (1/d) | 0.5 – 0.8 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.6 – 0.7 | 0.5 – 0.7 |
| BOD removal (%) | 60 – 80 | 75 – 90 | 50 – 70 | 80 – 95 |
| Coliform removal (log) | 1 – 2 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.5 | 1 – 2 |
| Odour potential | None | Mild | Strong (H₂S) | None |
| Typical use | Polishing | Secondary | Pre-treatment / high-strength | Resource recovery |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A facultative stabilization pond is to be designed for a community of 25,000 people. Assume per-capita sewage flow = 150 Lpcd, BOD₅ = 250 mg/L, target effluent BOD = 30 mg/L, operating temperature = 28 °C, depth = 1.5 m. Use Marais–Shaw kinetics. Find **(a)** detention time, **(b)** pond area, **(c)** total volume.

**Step 1 — Calculate Q:**
$$Q = 25000 \times 0.150 = 3750 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $k_T$ at 28 °C (facultative):**
$$k_{28} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^{(28-20)} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^8$$

$(1.05)^8 = 1.4775$

$$k_{28} = 0.30 \times 1.4775 = 0.4433 \;\text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Detention time:**
$$t = \dfrac{1}{k_T} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e} = \dfrac{1}{0.4433} \times \dfrac{250 - 30}{30} = 2.2557 \times 7.333$$

$$t = 16.54 \;\text{days}$$

**Step 4 — Volume:**
$$V = Q \cdot t = 3750 \times 16.54 = 62{,}025 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Area:**
$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{62{,}025}{1.5} = 41{,}350 \;\text{m}^2 = 4.135 \;\text{ha}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check via surface loading:**
$$\lambda_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{A} = \dfrac{3750 \times 0.250}{4.135} = \dfrac{937.5}{4.135} = 226.7 \;\text{kg BOD/ha·day}$$

> Within tropical range (110 – 225), marginally on the high side — pond is **just feasible**; in practice designer would round up to 4.5 ha.

**Step 7 — Cross-check with Marais–Shaw Sizing Equation:**

Using $A = \dfrac{Q}{k_T \cdot H} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e}$:
$$A = \dfrac{3750}{0.4433 \times 1.5} \times 7.333 = \dfrac{3750}{0.665} \times 7.333 = 5640 \times 7.333 = 41{,}358 \;\text{m}^2 = 4.136 \;\text{ha} \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Both methods (loading rate & kinetic) converge to **≈ 4.14 ha** — confirming the design is mathematically consistent.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Architecture of Stabilization Ponds

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STABILIZATION PONDS] --> B[Aerobic Ponds]
    A --> C[Facultative Ponds]
    A --> D[Anaerobic Ponds]
    A --> E[High Rate Algal Ponds]
    B --> B1[Maturation Type 0.3 to 0.45 m]
    B --> B2[Shallow Photosynthetic]
    C --> C1[Standard Type 1.0 to 2.5 m]
    C --> C2[Aerobic Surface Zone]
    C --> C3[Anaerobic Bottom Zone]
    D --> D1[Deep Type 2.5 to 5.0 m]
    D --> D2[Methanogenic Activity]
    D --> D3[High Strength Influent]
    E --> E1[Depth 0.3 to 0.45 m]
    E --> E2[Continuous Mixing]
    E --> E3[Resource Recovery]
```

## 4.2 Algae–Bacteria Symbiosis Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INF[Influent Wastewater BOD 250 mg per L] --> BACT[Step 1 Bacterial Oxidation Top Aerobic Zone]
    SUN[Step 4 Sunlight PAR 400 to 700 nm] --> ALG[Step 2 Algal Photosynthesis Light Penetrable Zone]
    BACT --> GAS[Step 5 CO2 Released]
    GAS --> ALG
    ALG --> O2[Step 3 Oxygen Released O2]
    O2 --> BACT
    BACT --> EF1[Treated Effluent Upper Outlet]
    ALG --> EF1
    INF --> SET[Step 6 Sludge Settles to Bottom]
    SET --> ANA[Step 7 Anaerobic Digestion CH4 and CO2]
    ANA --> METH[Methane Bubbles to Surface]
    ANA --> H2S[Trace H2S Managed]
    METH --> EF1
```

## 4.3 Treatment Train Block Diagram (3-Stage Pond Cascade)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INF[Raw Sewage Inlet] --> INLET[Inlet Works Screening and Grit Removal]
    INLET --> ANA[Stage 1 Anaerobic Pond Depth 3 to 4 m t 20 to 50 d]
    ANA --> FAC[Stage 2 Facultative Pond Depth 1.5 m t 7 to 15 d]
    FAC --> MAT[Stage 3 Maturation Pond Depth 0.45 m t 3 to 5 d]
    MAT --> DIS[Disinfection Optional Chlorine or UV]
    DIS --> EF[Final Effluent Discharge to Water Body or Reuse]
    ANA --> GAS1[Biogas Collection CH4 Optional]
    FAC --> O2P[Algal Oxygenation Natural]
    MAT --> PATH[Pathogen Dieoff UV and pH]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of an Algal–Bacterial Oxygen Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    SR[Step A Solar Radiation hits Pond Surface] --> AP[Step B Algae fix CO2 and produce O2]
    AP --> OB[Step C O2 Dissolved in Water]
    OB --> BX[Step D Aerobic Bacteria oxidise BOD]
    BX --> CO2[Step E Bacteria release CO2]
    CO2 --> AP
    BX --> REM[Step F BOD removed as CO2 and Water and Biomass]
    AP --> HB[Step G Algal Biomass harveted or lost in effluent]
    OB --> SED[Step H Sediment Oxygen Demand SOD consumes some O2]
    SED --> OB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in the exam:** In KTU 2024 ESE, **schematic flow** (arrows, labelled blocks) carries **2–3 marks** even within a numerical answer. Always include a simple block diagram before plugging numbers into a formula — it shows the examiner that you understood the *process flow*, not just memorised an equation.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic stabilization ponds with respect to depth, dominant microbial activity, and typical BOD loading.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-targeted):**

| Pond Type | Depth (m) | Dominant Microbes | BOD Loading (kg/ha·d) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerobic | 0.3 – 0.45 | Aerobic bacteria + algae | 67 – 135 |
| Facultative | 1.0 – 2.5 | Aerobic (top) + Anaerobic (bottom) | 110 – 225 (tropical) |
| Anaerobic | 2.5 – 5.0 | Anaerobic (methanogens) | 220 – 560 |

> Marking key: **1 mark for each row of the comparison table.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. Explain the role of algae in stabilization ponds. Why is depth limited to 0.3 – 0.45 m in high-rate algal ponds?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — key points):**

1. **(1 mark)** Algae perform photosynthesis, producing **O₂** used by aerobic bacteria to oxidise BOD: $6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2$.
2. **(1 mark)** Algae consume **CO₂** released by bacterial respiration, completing the **algae–bacteria symbiosis**; they also raise **pH** (> 9 in HRAPs), stripping ammonia.
3. **(1 mark)** Depth is limited to 0.3 – 0.45 m because **light penetration** (Secchi depth) must reach the **entire pond bottom** to drive photosynthesis uniformly — deeper ponds become facultative/anaerobic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Facultative Pond Design**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper, Module 2]**

> **Q. A facultative stabilization pond is proposed for a residential colony of 30,000 people. The per-capita wastewater generation is 135 Lpcd with BOD₅ of 220 mg/L. Design a single-cell facultative pond assuming:**
> - Target effluent BOD = 30 mg/L
> - Operating depth H = 1.5 m
> - Design temperature T = 27 °C
> - Use Marais–Shaw kinetics for facultative pond ($k_{20} = 0.30 /d$, $\theta = 1.05$)
>
> **Determine:**
> **(a)** **(7 marks)** The hydraulic detention time, pond volume, and surface area in hectares.
> **(b)** **(7 marks)** The required areal BOD loading in kg/ha·day and check whether it falls within the tropical design range. State TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of stabilization ponds compared to activated-sludge systems.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Average daily flow:**

$$Q = 30{,}000 \times 0.135 = 4050 \;\text{m}^3/\text{day} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature-corrected rate constant:**

$$k_{27} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^{(27-20)} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^7$$

$(1.05)^7 = 1.4071$

$$k_{27} = 0.30 \times 1.4071 = 0.4221 \;\text{day}^{-1} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Detention time from complete-mix equation:**

$$t = \dfrac{1}{k_{27}} \cdot \dfrac{S_i - S_e}{S_e} = \dfrac{1}{0.4221} \times \dfrac{220 - 30}{30}$$

$$t = 2.3691 \times 6.3333 = 15.00 \;\text{days} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Pond volume:**

$$V = Q \cdot t = 4050 \times 15.00 = 60{,}750 \;\text{m}^3 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Surface area:**

$$A = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{60{,}750}{1.5} = 40{,}500 \;\text{m}^2 = 4.05 \;\text{ha} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Areal BOD loading:**

$$\lambda_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_i}{A} = \dfrac{4050 \times 0.220}{4.05} = \dfrac{891.0}{4.05} = 220.0 \;\text{kg BOD/ha·day} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Design range check:**

> Tropical facultative pond design range: **110 – 225 kg BOD/ha·day**.
> Computed: 220 kg BOD/ha·day → **Within the acceptable upper limit (just at the edge)**. The design is feasible, but a **safety factor of 10 %** is recommended → final area **≈ 4.5 ha**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Advantages (2 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Low capital and operating cost** — no aerators, no skilled operators, no external energy; ideal for small towns.
2. **Effective pathogen removal** — 90–99.9 % coliform reduction through pH, sunlight, and long detention; ideal for tropical climates like Kerala.

**Step 4 — Disadvantage (1 mark):**

1. **Large land area required** — ≈ 1 ha per 100 m³/d of sewage; difficult to provide in urban/metropolitan areas.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (High-Rate Algal Pond + Wehner–Wilhelm)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary, Module 2]**

> **Q. A stabilisation pond system consists of an anaerobic pond followed by a facultative pond. Wastewater flow is 2000 m³/day with BOD₅ = 400 mg/L. The anaerobic pond removes 60 % BOD at 25 °C. The facultative pond (depth 1.2 m, length 80 m, width 40 m) operates at T = 22 °C. Use:**
> - Anaerobic kinetics: $k_{20} = 0.60 /d$, $\theta = 1.085$
> - Facultative kinetics: $k_{20} = 0.30 /d$, $\theta = 1.05$
> - Dispersion number for the facultative pond: $d = 1.0$
>
> **Determine:**
> **(a)** **(7 marks)** BOD concentration entering the facultative pond and the Wehner–Wilhelm effluent BOD.
> **(b)** **(7 marks)** Detention time, % BOD removal across the facultative pond, and a labelled schematic of the system.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Effluent BOD from anaerobic pond (60 % removal):**

$$S_{\text{after anaer}} = 400 \times (1 - 0.60) = 160 \;\text{mg/L} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

This is $S_i$ (influent) for the facultative pond.

**Step 2 — Facultative pond volume:**

$$V = L \times B \times H = 80 \times 40 \times 1.2 = 3840 \;\text{m}^3 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Detention time:**

$$t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{3840}{2000} = 1.92 \;\text{days} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Rate constant at 22 °C (facultative):**

$$k_{22} = 0.30 \times (1.05)^{(22-20)} = 0.30 \times 1.1025 = 0.3308 \;\text{day}^{-1} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Wehner–Wilhelm numerator parameter $a$:**

$$a = \sqrt{1 + 4 \, k_{22} \, t \, d} = \sqrt{1 + 4 \times 0.3308 \times 1.92 \times 1.0}$$

$$a = \sqrt{1 + 2.540} = \sqrt{3.540} = 1.8816 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $S_e / S_i$:**

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{4 a \, e^{1/(2d)}}{(1+a)^2 e^{a/(2d)} - (1-a)^2 e^{-a/(2d)}}$$

With $d = 1.0$: $1/(2d) = 0.5$, $a/(2d) = 0.9408$.

- $e^{0.5} = 1.6487$
- $e^{0.9408} = 2.5623$
- $e^{-0.9408} = 0.3903$

**Numerator:** $4 \times 1.8816 \times 1.6487 = 12.408$

**Denominator:**
- $(1 + 1.8816)^2 = (2.8816)^2 = 8.3036$
- $8.3036 \times 2.5623 = 21.276$
- $(1 - 1.8816)^2 = (-0.8816)^2 = 0.7772$
- $0.7772 \times 0.3903 = 0.3034$

Denominator $= 21.276 - 0.3034 = 20.973$

$$\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{12.408}{20.973} = 0.5916 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 7 — Effluent BOD:**

$$S_e = 0.5916 \times 160 = 94.66 \;\text{mg/L} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — % BOD removal across facultative pond:**

$$\% \text{ removal} = \left( 1 - 0.5916 \right) \times 100 = 40.84 \;\text{%} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Overall system removal (anaerobic + facultative):**

$$S_{\text{final}} = 94.66 \;\text{mg/L} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$\% \text{ overall} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{94.66}{400} \right) \times 100 = 76.34 \;\text{%} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Labelled schematic (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    INF[Influent 2000 m3 per d BOD 400 mg per L] --> AN[Anaerobic Pond Depth 3 to 4 m t 20 to 50 d]
    AN --> FA[Facultative Pond 80 m by 40 m by 1.2 m t 1.92 d]
    FA --> EF[Effluent BOD 94.7 mg per L]
    AN --> GAS[Biogas Recovery]
    FA --> O2X[Natural Algal Oxygenation]
```

**Marking key for schematic:** **1 mark** for each of the two ponds + **1 mark** for the labelled flow values.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Most students write $Q \cdot S_i$ without converting mg/L to kg/m³ (multiply by $10^{-3}$). You will lose **1 mark instantly** for every numerical answer where the unit is wrong.
> 2. **Decay constant confusion:** Some students use $k = 0.30$ (the 20 °C value) without applying the **Marais–Shaw temperature correction**. At 27 °C this underestimates pond performance by 30–40 %. Always state the corrected $k_T$ explicitly.
> 3. **HRAP depth mistake:** Several students confuse HRAP (0.3–0.45 m) with facultative (1.0–2.5 m). The depth dictates the *microbiology*, not the BOD loading.
> 4. **Forgetting $10^4$ in ha conversion:** A pond of 40,500 m² is **4.05 ha**, not 40,500 ha. Examiners specifically watch for this.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Stabilization Ponds**

- **Definition:** Shallow earthen impoundments using algae-bacteria symbiosis for wastewater treatment.
- **Four types:** Aerobic (maturation), facultative, anaerobic, HRAP.
- **Algae–Bacteria loop:** Algae → O₂ → Bacteria → CO₂ → Algae.
- **Depth ranges:** 0.3–0.45 m (aerobic/HRAP), 1.0–2.5 m (facultative), 2.5–5.0 m (anaerobic).
- **Detention times:** 3–5 d (aerobic), 7–30 d (facultative), 20–50 d (anaerobic).
- **Areal loading (tropical, kg BOD/ha·d):** 110–225 (facultative), 220–560 (anaerobic).
- **Marais–Shaw kinetics:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta = 1.05$ (facultative) and $\theta = 1.085$ (anaerobic).
- **Complete-mix BOD equation:** $S_e/S_i = 1/(1 + k_1 t)$.
- **Wehner–Wilhelm equation** (for real ponds with dispersion $d$): $\dfrac{S_e}{S_i} = \dfrac{4a e^{1/(2d)}}{(1+a)^2 e^{a/(2d)} - (1-a)^2 e^{-a/(2d)}}$.
- **Chick’s Law** for coliform: $N_e = N_i \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$, with $k_2 = 0.7$ to $1.5 /d$.
- **Algal O₂ yield:** ≈ **1.5 g O₂ per g algae**; **0.65 g algae per g BOD removed**.
- **Land area rule of thumb:** ≈ **1 ha per 100 m³/day** of domestic sewage.
- **Geometric ratio:** $L:B = 2:1$ to $3:1$ to minimise short-circuiting.
- **Oxygen sources in ponds:** Photosynthesis (dominant) + surface reaeration; losses: BOD oxidation + SOD + algal respiration.
- **Temperature effect:** Pond performance **doubles per 10 °C** rise — tropical Kerala conditions are ideal.
- **HRAP key facts:** depth 0.3–0.45 m, continuous mixing (paddle wheel), pH > 9 strips ammonia, biomass can be harvested for Spirulina/fertiliser.
- **Pathogen removal:** Combined effect of pH (> 9 in daytime), UV (sunlight), long detention, and predation → **90–99.9 % coliform kill**.
- **Advantages:** Low CAPEX/OPEX, no skilled operators, near-zero energy, excellent pathogen removal, suitable for small towns.
- **Disadvantages:** Large land area, mosquito breeding if poorly maintained, seasonal performance variation, algal-bloom carry-over into effluent.
- **Kerala context:** *Suchitwa Mission* and *KSUDP* deploy pond systems for towns < 50,000 PE; the **tropical $k$ values** in the KTU data book **differ from temperate** values by 30–50 %.
- **Examiner loves:** (i) the **Marais–Shaw correction**, (ii) labelled **schematic diagram**, (iii) units with every numerical step, (iv) **complete-mix vs. Wehner–Wilhelm** comparison.
- **Examiner dislikes:** Wrong units, unlabelled diagrams, use of $k_{20}$ at higher T without correction, and calling a facultative pond “plug flow” without justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation Ditches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oxidation Ditches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Oxidation Ditches

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Oxidation Ditch (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> An **Oxidation Ditch** is a modified variant of the conventional **activated sludge process** in which the mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS) are circulated in an endless, closed-loop (oval, racetrack, or circular) channel at a controlled horizontal velocity of **0.25 to 0.35 m/s**. Oxygen transfer and mixing are simultaneously achieved by mechanical surface aerators — most commonly the **brush-type Kessener rotor** or the **Mammoth rotor** — mounted transverse to the flow direction.

The system functions as an **extended aeration** biological reactor with typical **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** between **20 and 30 hours** (often 24 h) and **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT)** of **20 to 40 days**, enabling simultaneous **carbonaceous BOD removal, nitrification, and partial denitrification** within the same loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> Oxidation ditches are classified under *Suspended Growth Aerobic Treatment Systems* and are evaluated based on **(i) BOD removal efficiency (85–95 %)**, **(ii) effluent NH₃-N concentration (< 5 mg/L)**, **(iii) F/M ratio (0.05–0.1 d⁻¹)**, and **(iv) energy consumption per kg BOD removed (1.0–2.0 kWh/kg BOD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Endless Walking Track"

Imagine a **shallow, oval-shaped walking track** filled with grey water. People (microorganisms) are constantly jogging in a never-ending circle at a steady pace. On one straight side of the track, large ceiling fans (**rotors**) spin, blowing air into the water and keeping everyone well-fed with oxygen. As the joggers pass through the oxygen-rich zone, they gobble up the dirt (organic BOD) in the water. The cleaned water exits the track, and a small fraction of the joggers are sent to a recovery room (**secondary clarifier**), while the rest keep jogging back into the loop.

This is exactly how an oxidation ditch works: **continuous circulation + continuous aeration + continuous biological oxidation**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Ditch Depth (H) | 1.0 – 3.0 | m |
| Channel Width (W) | 2.0 – 6.0 | m |
| Horizontal Velocity (v) | 0.25 – 0.35 | m/s |
| MLSS (X) | 3,000 – 5,000 | mg/L |
| F/M Ratio | 0.05 – 0.10 | d⁻¹ |
| HRT (θ) | 20 – 30 | h |
| SRT (θc) | 20 – 40 | d |
| BOD Removal Efficiency | 85 – 95 | % |
| Effluent NH₃-N | < 5 | mg/L |
| O₂ Transfer Efficiency | 1.0 – 2.0 | kg O₂/kWh |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Philosophy & Working Principle

The oxidation ditch is essentially a **completely-mixed, continuous-flow activated sludge reactor** with three distinguishing features:

1. **Loop geometry** — eliminates dead zones and ensures uniform dissolved oxygen (DO ≥ 1.5 mg/L on average; 2 mg/L on the rotor side).
2. **Mechanical surface aeration** — transfers oxygen while simultaneously imparting momentum to the flow, achieving both aeration and circulation in a single piece of equipment.
3. **Long SRT** — enables autotrophic nitrifying bacteria (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) to thrive, hence complete nitrification.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1 — Influent Mixing:** Screened and degritted wastewater enters the ditch, where it is instantly dispersed into the bulk mixed liquor by the rotor-induced turbulence.
- **Step 2 — Aerobic Reaction:** As the mixed liquor circulates past the rotor, dissolved oxygen is absorbed (transfer efficiency **E ≈ 1.5–2.5 kg O₂/kWh** at standard conditions). Microorganisms oxidize organic carbon (BOD) to CO₂ + H₂O + new cells.
- **Step 3 — Nitrification:** At low F/M (0.05–0.1 d⁻¹) and SRT > 20 d, autotrophs convert ammonia to nitrate:
  
  $$NH_4^+ + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow NO_3^- + 2\,H^+ + H_2O$$

- **Step 4 — Clarification:** The MLSS overflows into a secondary clarifier where sludge settles; clarified effluent is discharged.
- **Step 5 — Sludge Return & Wasting:** Settled sludge is returned (typically **75–100 % of Q**), and a small fraction is wasted to maintain the target SRT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the loop shape?**
> The closed loop creates a **plug-flow-like axial gradient** of DO and substrate even within a fully-mixed reactor, which gives partial anoxic zones for denitrification — a built-in tertiary benefit.

## 2.3 Classification of Oxidation Ditch Systems

| Type | Aerator | Typical Use | Unique Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pasveer Ditch** | Horizontal brush (Kessener) | Small communities (≤ 50,000 PE) | Original design; single rotor |
| **Orbal System** | Disc aerators (multi-channel) | Medium plants | Three concentric channels in series; operates in true plug flow |
| **Carrousel Ditch** | Vertical-shaft slow aerators | Large plants | Deep ditches (up to 5 m); uses low-speed surface aerators |
| **Vertical Loop Reactor** | Draft tube + aerator | Compact sites | Uses a vertical cylindrical tank with internal circulation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the master reference for all KTU numerical problems on Oxidation Ditches.** All vertical bars / absolute values are written as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | HRT; V = ditch volume, Q = flow | h or d |
| 2 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{V\,X}$ | Food to Microorganism ratio | d⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V\,X}{Q_w\,X_w + (Q-Q_w)\,X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time | d |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta\,X}$ | Specific substrate utilization rate | d⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\,U - k_d$ | Fundamental design equation | d⁻¹ |
| 6 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d\,\theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | mg VSS/mg BOD |
| 7 | $R_0 = \dfrac{Q\,(S_0 - S)}{V} - 1.42\,k_d\,X$ | Oxygen utilization rate | mg/L·d |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{R_0 \cdot V}{1000 \cdot E}$ | Rotor power (E = OTE) | kW |
| 9 | $\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD removal efficiency | % |
| 10 | $N_{NH} = 4.57\,Q\,(N_0 - N_e) - 2.86\,Q\,(N_0 - N_e - NO_{3,e})$ | Nitrification oxygen demand | kg O₂/d |
| 11 | $v = \dfrac{Q_{channel}}{A_{cross}}$ | Horizontal flow velocity | m/s |
| 12 | $A_{oxygen} = \dfrac{P \cdot E}{24 \cdot 1000}$ | Actual oxygen transferred | kg O₂/h |

**Typical Kinetic Constants for Oxidation Ditch Design (20 °C):**

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Y (yield coeff.) | 0.4 – 0.6 | mg VSS/mg BOD |
| kd (endogenous decay) | 0.04 – 0.08 | d⁻¹ |
| Ks (half-velocity) | 60 – 120 | mg/L BOD |
| µm (max specific growth) | 3.0 – 5.0 | d⁻¹ |
| K_N (nitrification half-sat.) | 0.5 – 1.0 | mg/L NH₃-N |
| µ_nitrif. (nitrifier µ) | 0.3 – 0.7 | d⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Small townships & decentralized plants** — preferred for **population equivalents (PE) up to 50,000** due to operational simplicity.
- **Industrial wastewater** — used for **food processing, dairies, distilleries** where high BOD and biodegradable organics are present.
- **Nitrogen-sensitive catchments** — used as a **single-step carbon + nitrogen removal** unit.
- **Power-sensitive regions** — paired with **variable-frequency drives (VFDs)** on rotors to control DO and save up to **30 % energy**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU:** "**O-DITCH = O**xygen + **D**etention + **I**nfinite loop + **T**otal nitrification + **C**irculation + **H**igh HRT."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ditch Volume from F/M Criterion

**Given:** Influent flow Q (m³/d), influent BOD S₀ (mg/L), MLSS X (mg/L), target F/M ratio.

**Starting Equation:**

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{V\,X}$$

**Step 1 — Isolate V (Ditch Volume):**

$$V \cdot \dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{X}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiplication gives:**

$$V = \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$$

**Step 3 — Verify units (dimensional check):**

$$V = \dfrac{[\text{m}^3/\text{d}] \cdot [\text{mg/L}]}{[\text{mg/L}] \cdot [\text{d}^{-1}]} = \text{m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute HRT for verification:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soluble Effluent BOD Using Monod Kinetics

The Monod equation relates specific growth rate µ to limiting substrate S:

$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 1 — Substrate balance around the reactor at steady state:**

$$Q\,S_0 = Q\,S + \dfrac{\mu\,X}{Y}\,V$$

**Step 2 — Substitute µ from Monod equation:**

$$Q\,S_0 = Q\,S + \dfrac{\mu_m\,S}{Y\,(K_s + S)}\,X\,V$$

**Step 3 — Use the specific substrate utilization rate U:**

$$U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta\,X} = \dfrac{\mu}{Y}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back, leading to the classic design form:**

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot U - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d}$$

**Step 5 — Combining with U-expression yields:**

$$S = \dfrac{K_s\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}{\theta_c\,(\mu_m - k_d) - 1}$$

This is the **effluent BOD** predicted for the oxidation ditch.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oxygen Requirement for Combined Carbon + Nitrification

The total oxygen demand in an oxidation ditch has two components:

**(a) Carbonaceous Oxygen Demand:**

$$R_C = a'\,(S_0 - S)\,Q + b'\,X\,V$$

where a' ≈ 0.5 (oxygen per unit BOD oxidized) and b' ≈ 0.15 d⁻¹ (endogenous oxygen).

**(b) Nitrification Oxygen Demand:**

$$R_N = 4.57\,Q\,(N_0 - N_e) - 2.86\,Q\,(NO_{3,\,formed} - NO_{3,\,e})$$

**Step 1 — Total oxygen demand:**

$$R_{total} = R_C + R_N$$

**Step 2 — Convert to standard conditions using α and β factors:**

$$R_{field} = \dfrac{R_{total}}{\alpha \cdot F \cdot (\beta\,C_{s,T} - C_L)}$$

**Step 3 — Required air / rotor power:**

$$P = \dfrac{R_{field}}{E_{aerator}}$$

**Numerical Example (Worked Out):**

Suppose Q = 5000 m³/d, S₀ = 250 mg/L, S = 20 mg/L, X = 4000 mg/L, F/M = 0.08 d⁻¹, V to be designed.

**Step 1 — Ditch Volume:**

$$V = \dfrac{5000 \times 250}{4000 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{1{,}250{,}000}{320} = 3{,}906.25 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Ditch dimensions (assuming depth H = 2.5 m, width W = 4 m):**

$$L = \dfrac{V}{H \cdot W} = \dfrac{3906.25}{2.5 \times 4} = 390.6 \text{ m of channel centerline}$$

**Step 3 — Check HRT:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{3906.25}{5000} = 0.781\,\text{d} = 18.75\,\text{h} \quad \text{(acceptably close to 20 h)}$$

**Step 4 — Carbonaceous oxygen demand (using a' = 0.5, b' = 0.15):**

$$R_C = 0.5 \times (250-20) \times 5000 + 0.15 \times 4000 \times 3906.25$$

$$R_C = 0.5 \times 230 \times 5000 + 0.15 \times 4000 \times 3906.25$$

$$R_C = 575{,}000 + 2{,}343{,}750 = 2{,}918{,}750 \text{ mg/d} \approx 2.92 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d per 1000 m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Total O₂ = 2.92 × 3.906 = 11.4 kg O₂/d per 1000 m³ → total ≈ 44.5 kg O₂/d**

**Step 6 — Rotor power (E = 1.8 kg O₂/kWh):**

$$P = \dfrac{44.5}{1.8} = 24.7 \text{ kW continuous rotor power}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-verification step:** Energy per kg BOD removed = (24.7 × 24) / (5000 × 0.23) = 592.8 / 1150 ≈ **0.52 kWh/kg BOD** — well within the standard 1–2 kWh/kg range for well-designed ditches.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) of an Oxidation Ditch Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InfA[Influent Wastewater] --> BarA[Bar Screen]
    BarA --> GritA[Grit Chamber]
    GritA --> DitchA
    subgraph DitchSystem[Oxidation Ditch Loop]
        DitchA[Ditch Channel Inlet] --> RotorA[Rotor Aerator]
        RotorA --> DitchB[Channel Curve 1]
        DitchB --> DitchC[Channel Straight 2]
        DitchC --> DitchD[Channel Curve 2]
        DitchD --> DitchOut[Ditch Outlet]
    end
    DitchOut --> ClariA[Secondary Clarifier]
    ClariA --> EffA[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    ClariA --> RASp[RAS Pumping]
    RASp --> DitchA
    ClariA --> WASp[WAS Wasting Line]
    WASp --> SludgeA[Sludge Handling Unit]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Air1[Atmospheric Air] --> Rotor1[Brush Rotor / Disc Aerator]
    Rotor1 --> ML1[Mixed Liquor MLSS]
    Inf1[Raw Wastewater Q, S0] --> ML1
    ML1 --> Bio1[Aerobic Bioreaction Zone DO greater than 1.5 mg per L]
    ML1 --> Bio2[Anoxic Zone for Denitrification at Channel Bends]
    Bio1 --> Out1[Effluent S, NH3-N less than 5 mg per L]
    Bio2 --> Out1
    ML1 --> Clari1[Secondary Clarifier]
    Clari1 --> Ras1[Return Activated Sludge R equal to 0.75 Q to 1.0 Q]
    Ras1 --> ML1
    Clari1 --> Was1[Waste Activated Sludge to Maintain SRT 20 to 40 d]
```

## 4.3 Rotor Aerator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Motor1[Electric Motor VFD Controlled] --> Shaft1[Drive Shaft]
    Shaft1 --> Brush1[Brush / Disc Assembly]
    Brush1 --> Spray1[Water Droplet Spray Plume]
    Spray1 --> AirContact[Air Water Interface]
    AirContact --> O2Transfer[Oxygen Transfer E 1.5 to 2.5 kg O2 per kWh]
    O2Transfer --> Velocity1[Imparts Horizontal Velocity 0.25 to 0.35 m per s]
    Velocity1 --> Mix1[Complete Mixing of Mixed Liquor]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Conventional ASP vs. Oxidation Ditch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Conventional Activated Sludge] -->|HRT 4 to 8 h| C2[Compact Tank]
    C2 -->|SRT 5 to 15 d| C3[Partial Nitrification]
    C3 -->|F/M 0.2 to 0.5 per d| C4[High Sludge Yield]
    
    O1[Oxidation Ditch] -->|HRT 20 to 30 h| O2[Oval Channel]
    O2 -->|SRT 20 to 40 d| O3[Full Nitrification]
    O3 -->|F/M 0.05 to 0.1 per d| O4[Low Sludge Yield]
    O4 -->|Lower energy per kg BOD| O5[Energy Efficient Long SRT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams for KTU?** Examiners consistently award marks for **labelled, sequential flow sketches** showing the rotor, clarifier, RAS, and WAS — always include the **loop direction arrow** in your answer sheet to gain the visual comprehension marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define an Oxidation Ditch. List any four of its characteristic design parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** An oxidation ditch is a modified activated sludge process in which mixed liquor is circulated continuously in an oval-shaped channel at 0.25–0.35 m/s using mechanical surface aerators (rotors/discs), achieving combined **BOD removal and nitrification**.

**Characteristic Design Parameters (4 × ½ = 2 Marks):**
1. HRT = 20–30 hours
2. SRT = 20–40 days
3. MLSS = 3,000–5,000 mg/L
4. F/M = 0.05–0.10 d⁻¹
5. Ditch depth = 1.0–3.0 m
6. DO ≥ 1.5 mg/L

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Compare the Kessener brush rotor and the Mammoth rotor used in oxidation ditches.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Kessener Brush | Mammoth Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Diameter | 0.35 – 0.50 m | 0.7 – 1.0 m |
| Speed | Faster (75 rpm) | Slower (40–75 rpm) |
| Immersion | Partial | Deeper immersion |
| O₂ transfer | Lower (1.2 kg O₂/kWh) | Higher (2.0–2.5 kg O₂/kWh) |
| Application | Pasveer (small ditches) | Carrousel (large ditches) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an oxidation ditch with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the rotor in oxygen transfer and mixing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an oxidation ditch for the following data: Flow = 5,000 m³/d, Influent BOD = 250 mg/L, Effluent BOD = 25 mg/L, MLSS = 4,000 mg/L, F/M ratio = 0.08 d⁻¹. Assume ditch depth = 2.5 m and width = 4 m. Calculate **(i)** Ditch volume, **(ii)** Channel centerline length, **(iii)** HRT, **(iv)** Required rotor power (E = 1.8 kg O₂/kWh). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a):

1. **Working Principle (3 Marks):** The oxidation ditch operates as a completely-mixed, continuous-flow loop. Wastewater enters the oval channel, is instantly mixed with the bulk MLSS, and circulates past the rotor for oxygenation.
2. **Rotor Role (2 Marks):** The rotor performs **two simultaneous functions** — **(i) Oxygen transfer** through high-velocity droplet ejection at the air-water interface, and **(ii) Momentum transfer** by pushing water forward to maintain 0.25–0.35 m/s velocity.
3. **Sketch (2 Marks):** Show oval channel, rotor position, clarifier, RAS line, WAS line, with arrows for flow direction.

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**(i) Ditch Volume:**

$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{5000 \times 250}{4000 \times 0.08} = 3{,}906.25 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Channel Length:**

$$L = \dfrac{V}{H \times W} = \dfrac{3906.25}{2.5 \times 4} = 390.6 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) HRT:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{3906.25}{5000} = 0.781 \text{ d} = 18.75 \text{ h}$$

**(iv) Oxygen Demand (carbonaceous only):**

$$R_C = a'\,(S_0 - S)\,Q + b'\,X\,V = 0.5 \times 225 \times 5000 + 0.15 \times 4000 \times 3906.25$$

$$R_C = 562{,}500 + 2{,}343{,}750 = 2{,}906{,}250 \text{ mg/d} = 2.906 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

**Rotor Power:**

$$P = \dfrac{R_C}{E} = \dfrac{2.906 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/d}}{1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/kWh}} = 1.61 \text{ kW (per 1000 m}^3\text{)}$$

For full ditch: **P = 1.61 × 3.91 ≈ 6.3 kW** continuous (plus a 1.5× safety factor → install **10 kW** rotor).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between Orbal, Carrousel, and Pasveer oxidation ditch systems with respect to aerator type, geometry, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An oxidation ditch treats 8,000 m³/d of wastewater with influent BOD 300 mg/L. MLSS is maintained at 4,500 mg/L. If the F/M ratio is 0.07 d⁻¹, calculate the volume, HRT, and effluent BOD using Monod kinetics (assume Ks = 80 mg/L, µm = 4.0 d⁻¹, Y = 0.5, kd = 0.06 d⁻¹). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a):

| Feature | Pasveer | Orbal | Carrousel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerator | Kessener brush | Disc aerators | Vertical-shaft slow aerators |
| Geometry | Single oval channel | Three concentric channels | Deep racetrack (up to 5 m) |
| Flow regime | CSTR | Plug-flow in series | CSTR with deep mixing |
| Application | Small plants ≤ 50,000 PE | Medium 50k–200k PE | Large > 100,000 PE |
| Nitrification | Yes | Yes, multi-stage | Yes |
| Energy efficiency | Moderate | High | High |

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**(i) Volume:**

$$V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{8000 \times 300}{4500 \times 0.07} = 7{,}619 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) HRT:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{7619}{8000} = 0.952 \text{ d} = 22.86 \text{ h}$$

**(iii) SRT (θc):**

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d$$

Where $U = F/M \times \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0}$. Iterating, with S small relative to S₀:

$$\theta_c \approx \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot (F/M) - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{0.5 \times 0.07 - 0.06} = \dfrac{1}{-0.025}$$

This is **negative**, which means F/M × Y < kd, so the system operates in **endogenous respiration regime** — exactly as expected for oxidation ditches (low net yield). Taking the typical SRT = 25 days:

**(iv) Effluent BOD via Monod:**

$$S = \dfrac{K_s\,(1 + k_d\,\theta_c)}{\theta_c\,(\mu_m - k_d) - 1} = \dfrac{80 \times (1 + 0.06 \times 25)}{25 \times (4.0 - 0.06) - 1}$$

$$S = \dfrac{80 \times 2.5}{25 \times 3.94 - 1} = \dfrac{200}{97.5} = 2.05 \text{ mg/L} \quad \text{(BOD)}$$

This confirms **> 99 % BOD removal** — characteristic of a well-designed oxidation ditch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (3–5 marks lost on a 14-mark question):**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Q in m³/d must be paired with V in m³ (not litres). MLSS is mg/L = g/m³.
> 2. **Mixing F/M with SRT** — F/M is a **kinetic indicator**; SRT (θc) is the **operational control parameter** set by sludge wasting.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark theory parts** — KTU examiners mandate a **labelled diagram** (oval channel + rotor + clarifier + RAS/WAS lines) for at least 1.5–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring nitrification oxygen demand** — 4.57 kg O₂ per kg NH₃-N oxidized is a **mandatory addition** when designing for nitrogen removal.
> 5. **Wrong horizontal velocity** — less than 0.25 m/s causes **sludge settling**; greater than 0.4 m/s causes **shear floc breakup**. Always state this band in design answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oxidation Ditches**

- [x] **Definition:** Modified activated sludge with oval loop + rotor aeration.
- [x] **Typical HRT:** 20–30 h; **SRT:** 20–40 d; **F/M:** 0.05–0.1 d⁻¹.
- [x] **MLSS:** 3,000–5,000 mg/L; **Depth:** 1–3 m.
- [x] **Horizontal velocity:** 0.25–0.35 m/s (mandatory to prevent settling).
- [x] **DO:** ≥ 1.5 mg/L average; ≥ 2 mg/L at rotor zone.
- [x] **Three classic systems:** Pasveer, Orbal, Carrousel.
- [x] **Volume equation:** V = Q·S₀ / (X·F/M).
- [x] **BOD removal efficiency:** 85–95 %; Effluent BOD often < 25 mg/L.
- [x] **Nitrification is intrinsic** — autotrophs survive due to long SRT.
- [x] **Sludge yield is low** — Yobs ≈ 0.2–0.3 (extended aeration regime).
- [x] **Energy:** 1.0–2.0 kWh per kg BOD removed; use VFD for DO control.
- [x] **4.57 kg O₂** needed per kg NH₃-N oxidized.
- [x] **Rotor choice:** Kessener (small) vs. Mammoth/Carrousel (large).
- [x] **Always draw:** Loop + rotor + clarifier + RAS + WAS + flow arrow.
- [x] **Formula you MUST memorize for KTU 2024:**
  $$V = \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} \quad ; \quad \theta_c = \dfrac{1}{Y\,U - k_d} \quad ; \quad P = \dfrac{R_{total}}{E_{aerator}}$$

> **Final Note:** Oxidation ditches are the **"default answer"** in KTU viva voce and short questions for *small-to-medium* wastewater treatment plants. Master the **numerical design procedure** (volume → length → HRT → oxygen → power), and you will secure **≥ 90 % marks** consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ion Exchange** is a **reversible stoichiometric chemical process** in which ions present in a solution are transferred to an **insoluble solid matrix** (the ion exchange resin) and replaced by an equivalent amount of other ions of like charge from the resin, without any permanent alteration of the resin structure. In the context of **tertiary (advanced) wastewater treatment**, ion exchange is employed as a **polishing step** after primary and secondary treatment to remove **dissolved ionic species** that remain in the effluent — including residual hardness ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), heavy metals (Cd²⁺, Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cr⁶⁺), nitrate (NO₃⁻), sulfate (SO₄²⁻), fluoride (F⁻), ammonium (NH₄⁺), and dissolved silica. The process operates in accordance with the **Law of Conservation of Charge** and is governed by mass-action equilibria. The functional solid is called an **ion exchange resin** — typically a **synthetic polymeric matrix** (polystyrene cross-linked with divinylbenzene, DVB) carrying fixed ionic functional groups (e.g., –SO₃⁻, –COO⁻, –N⁺(CH₃)₃) and **mobile counter-ions** that exchange with ions in the surrounding aqueous phase. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnet Swap" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a long corridor lined with thousands of **magnetised pegs**, each holding a small **steel ball**. The pegs represent the **fixed functional groups** on the resin, and the balls are the **exchangeable counter-ions** (say, Na⁺ or H⁺). When wastewater (carrying unwanted ions like Ca²⁺ or Cl⁻) flows past, the more "attractive" (i.e., higher-selectivity) ions in solution **kick out** the balls on the pegs and **take their place**. The displaced balls flow out with the treated water. Once all the pegs are full of the captured ions, the resin is "spent" — but we can **reverse the process** by passing a concentrated regenerant solution (like brine or acid), which restores the original balls back onto the pegs, ready for another cycle. ### 1.3 Classification of Ion Exchange Operations | Operation Type | Ions Removed | Typical Resin Form | Common Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Water Softening** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | Na⁺-form cation resin | Boiler feed, cooling water | | **Demineralization** | All cations & anions | H⁺-form + OH⁻-form | High-purity industrial water | | **Dealkalization** | HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻ | H⁺-form weak-acid cation | Boiler makeup | | **Heavy Metal Removal** | Cd²⁺, Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cr³⁺ | Na⁺ or H⁺-form cation | Industrial effluent polishing | | **Nitrate Removal** | NO₃⁻ | Cl⁻-form anion | Drinking water, agricultural runoff | | **Desalination (partial)** | Total dissolved solids | Mixed-bed resins | Brackish water pretreatment | ### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Specific Capacity:** **0.5 – 2.0 meq/mL** of resin (wet) or **3 – 6 meq/g** (dry) - **Service Flow Rate:** **8 – 40 BV/h** (Bed Volumes per hour) - **Backwash Rate:** **7 – 15 m³/(m²·h)** - **Regenerant Dose:** **80 – 300 g NaCl/L resin** (softening); **50 – 150 g HCl/L** (cation regeneration) - **Resin Life:** **3 – 8 years** under proper operation - **Theoretical Exchange Constant (K):** Dimensionless, typically **1.5 – 15** for common cation pairs > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **cation exchange resins** (which carry fixed **negative** functional groups and exchange **positive** ions) and **anion exchange resins** (which carry fixed **positive** groups and exchange **negative** ions). Confusing the two is a guaranteed mark-deduction trap. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Stoichiometric Exchange Reactions The general reversible reaction for ion exchange between ions A and B of valences $a$ and $b$ on a resin (R) is: $$\frac{a}{b}\,B^{b+}_{(aq)} + A^{a+}R \rightleftharpoons \frac{a}{b}\,B^{b+}R + A^{a+}_{(aq)}$$ #### 2.1.1 Cation Exchange (Softening) Reaction $$Ca^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2\,Na^{+}R \longrightarrow Ca^{2+}R_{2} + 2\,Na^{+}_{(aq)}$$ - **Resin functional group:** Sulfonic acid (–SO₃⁻Na⁺) on polystyrene-DVB matrix - **Exchanged species:** Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ displaced from water onto resin; Na⁺ released into solution - **Regenerant:** Concentrated **NaCl brine (5–10% w/v)** #### 2.1.2 Anion Exchange Reaction $$Cl^{-}_{(aq)} + R\text{-}N^{+}(CH_{3})_{3}\,OH^{-} \longrightarrow R\text{-}N^{+}(CH_{3})_{3}\,Cl^{-} + OH^{-}_{(aq)}$$ - **Resin functional group:** Quaternary ammonium (Type I strong-base or Type II) - **Exchanged species:** Cl⁻, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻ captured; OH⁻ released - **Regenerant:** **NaOH (4–6% w/v)** #### 2.1.3 Demineralization (Two-Column System) **Step 1 — Strong Acid Cation (SAC) column:** $$Ca^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2\,H^{+}R \longrightarrow Ca^{2+}R_{2} + 2\,H^{+}_{(aq)}$$ **Step 2 — Strong Base Anion (SBA) column:** $$H^{+}_{(aq)} + Cl^{-}_{(aq)} + R\text{-}OH \longrightarrow R\text{-}Cl + H_{2}O$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Net effect of two-column demineralization:** The effluent becomes **de-ionised water** (resistivity > 1 MΩ·cm). The CO₂ formed from bicarbonate breakdown in the SAC column is often removed by a **degassifier** before the SBA column to reduce anion load. ### 2.2 The Selectivity Coefficient ($K_{A/B}$) The selectivity coefficient quantifies the **preference** of a resin for one ion over another. For the exchange reaction between monovalent ions A and B: $$K_{A/B} = \frac{[\bar{A}][B]}{[\bar{B}][A]}$$ For ions of unequal valences (generalised form): $$K_{A^{a+}/B^{b+}} = \frac{[\bar{A}]^{b}[B]^{a}}{[\bar{B}]^{a}[A]^{b}}$$ where: - $[\bar{A}],\,[\bar{B}]$ = concentrations of A, B on the resin (eq/L of resin) - $[A],\,[B]$ = concentrations of A, B in solution (eq/L) #### Selectivity Trends (Empirical — Schulze-Hardy Rule Analogue) > [!NOTE] > **For cation exchange resins, selectivity generally INCREASES with:** > 1. **Higher ionic charge** (Al³⁺ > Ca²⁺ > Na⁺) > 2. **Larger (less hydrated) ionic radius** for ions of same charge (e.g., for alkali metals: Cs⁺ > Rb⁺ > K⁺ > Na⁺ > Li⁺) > 3. **Higher polarisability** of the ion ### 2.3 Operating Capacity & Breakthrough The **operating (working) capacity** $Q_{op}$ (eq/L) is the amount of ions a resin actually exchanges between successive regenerations under field conditions. It is always **less than the total capacity** $Q_{tot}$ due to incomplete regeneration and leakage: $$Q_{op} = \eta \cdot Q_{tot}$$ where $\eta$ = **utilization factor** = 0.60 – 0.90. The **service run length** $T$ (hours) between regenerations is: $$T = \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_{resin}}{C_{in} \cdot Q_{service}}$$ where: - $V_{resin}$ = volume of resin in the column (L) - $C_{in}$ = influent ionic concentration (eq/L) - $Q_{service}$ = service flow rate (L/h) ### 2.4 Regenerant Quantity $$m_{reg} = \frac{Q_{op} \cdot V_{resin} \cdot E_{w}}{C_{reg}}$$ where: - $E_w$ = **equivalent weight of regenerant** (g/eq); NaCl = 58.5, HCl = 36.5, H₂SO₄ = 49, NaOH = 40 - $C_{reg}$ = regenerant concentration expressed as equivalents per gram of solution (eq/g) ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for KTU 2024 Board exams on this topic.
