topics.title: Reverse Osmosis Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reverse Osmosis Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a pressurized feed solution is forced through a semi-permeable membrane that is permeable to the solvent (typically water) but rejects dissolved solutes (ions, salts, organics, and microorganisms). The applied hydraulic pressure must exceed the natural **osmotic pressure** of the feed solution to reverse the natural osmotic flow, hence the term "Reverse" Osmosis.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OECHT725 – Advanced Wastewater Treatment), Reverse Osmosis is positioned as a **tertiary / advanced polishing step** used to remove dissolved solids, refractory organics, heavy metals, and micropollutants from secondary or tertiary treated effluents — producing a permeate suitable for industrial reuse, boiler feed, or near-potable applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *Reverse Osmosis is a membrane process in which a pressure greater than the osmotic pressure of the solution is applied on the concentrate side, causing the solvent molecules to flow from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Think of Reverse Osmosis like a **sieve with a security guard standing on the wrong side of the fence**.

- **Normal Osmosis:** Imagine a sealed container split by a very fine mesh. On one side, you have clean water; on the other, salty wastewater. Naturally, the clean water *wants* to sneak through the mesh toward the salty side (because the salty side is "thirstier" — it has less free water). This is **osmosis** — water flows from low to high concentration to try to balance things out.
- **Reverse Osmosis:** Now imagine pushing the salty water with a giant hydraulic piston (a high-pressure pump) hard enough that the pressure overcomes the "thirsty pull" of the salt. The clean water is then **forced** backward through the mesh, leaving the salts, bacteria, and organics trapped on the high-pressure side. The mesh (membrane) is so fine that even single ions like sodium (Na⁺) and chloride (Cl⁻) are rejected.

### 1.3 The Three Defining Physical Metrics

Any RO problem on the KTU exam will revolve around three numbers:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (RO) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | ΔP | **10 – 80 bar** |
| Permeate Flux | Jw | **10 – 60 L/m²·h** |
| Salt Rejection | Rj | **95 – 99.8 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 OECHT725 syllabus, Module 3 introduces RO as part of *Advanced Membrane Processes for Wastewater Reclamation*. Expect a direct 3-mark definition question and a derivation-based 7-mark question on osmotic pressure and solvent flux in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The RO phenomenon is built upon three nested physical layers. Mastering these in order is essential for KTU board exam answers.

### Layer 1: Osmotic Pressure (The Driving "Resistance")
Every dissolved solute exerts a thermodynamic "pull" on water. This pull is quantified as the **osmotic pressure (π)**. The higher the solute concentration, the higher the osmotic pressure. The RO system must first overcome this resistance before any net permeate flow occurs.

### Layer 2: Net Driving Pressure (The Net "Push")
If the applied hydraulic pressure **ΔP** on the feed side is **less than π**, water flows toward the concentrate (normal osmosis). If **ΔP = π**, there is equilibrium (no net flow). If **ΔP > π**, water flows from the concentrate side toward the permeate side — this is the **Reverse Osmosis regime**.

> **Net Driving Pressure (NDP):**  $\text{NDP} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi$

### Layer 3: Membrane Transport (The "Filter")
The semi-permeable membrane exhibits two distinct transport mechanisms:
- **Solution-Diffusion Model** — the most widely accepted model for RO, where water dissolves into the membrane, diffuses through it, and desorbs on the permeate side.
- **Preferential Sorption-Capillary Flow Model** — used for explaining solute rejection behavior at the membrane surface.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Reverse Osmosis Performance

The following are **high-yield KTU points** — examiners frequently test these in Part A (3-mark) questions:

- **Applied Pressure (ΔP):** Higher pressure → higher flux, but only up to a thermodynamic limit.
- **Feed Concentration:** Higher TDS → higher osmotic pressure → reduced net driving force.
- **Temperature:** Higher temperature → lower viscosity → higher flux (typically flux increases ~3 % per °C).
- **Membrane Properties:** Pore size, surface charge, hydrophilicity, and thickness govern selectivity.
- **Concentration Polarization:** Accumulation of rejected solutes at the membrane surface, which increases local osmotic pressure and reduces flux.
- **Fouling & Scaling:** Deposition of suspended solids, colloids, and sparingly soluble salts (e.g., CaCO₃, CaSO₄) on the membrane surface.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is exam-ready.** All equations use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` delimiters (NOT the vertical pipe) to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Van't Hoff Osmotic Pressure | $\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T$ | $i$ = van't Hoff factor (dimensionless); $M$ = molarity (mol/L); $R$ = **0.0821 L·atm/mol·K**; $T$ = absolute temperature (K) |
| 2 | Osmotic Pressure (SI, dilute) | $\pi = C \cdot R \cdot T$ | $C$ = molar concentration (mol/m³); $R$ = **8.314 J/mol·K**; result in Pascals (Pa) |
| 3 | Solution-Diffusion Flux (Water) | $J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $A$ = water permeability constant (m/s·Pa); $J_w$ in m/s or L/m²·h |
| 4 | Solution-Diffusion Flux (Solute) | $J_s = B \cdot (C_f - C_p)$ | $B$ = solute permeability (m/s); $C_f, C_p$ = feed & permeate concentrations (mol/m³) |
| 5 | Salt Rejection (Observed) | $R_j = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} \times 100$ | All concentrations in mg/L or ppm; dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | Water Recovery Rate | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | $Q_p$ = permeate flow (m³/h); $Q_f$ = feed flow (m³/h) |
| 7 | Concentration Polarization Factor | $\beta = \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}$ | $C_m$ = wall concentration; $C_b$ = bulk concentration |
| 8 | Langelier Saturation Index | $\text{LSI} = pH_a - pH_s$ | $pH_a$ = actual pH; $pH_s$ = saturation pH; LSI $\lt 0$ → no scaling risk |
| 9 | Stiff-Davis Stability Index | $\text{SDSI} = pH_a - pH_s$ | Used for high-TDS waters (TDS $\gt 10{,}000$ mg/L) |
| 10 | Net Driving Pressure | $\text{NDP} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi - \Delta P_{cp}$ | $\Delta P_{cp}$ = osmotic pressure rise due to concentration polarization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Wastewater Reuse:** Recovery of plating rinse water, textile dye effluents, and pharmaceutical streams.
- **Seawater Desalination:** Conversion of seawater (TDS ≈ **35,000 mg/L**) to potable water using SWRO plants operating at **55 – 80 bar**.
- **Brackish Water Treatment:** BWRO plants operating at **10 – 25 bar** for inland water supply.
- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) Systems:** RO serves as the primary concentration step before thermal/crystallizer systems in industries such as power plants and refineries.
- **Tertiary Polishing in Municipal STPs:** Removal of trace organics, endocrine disruptors, and PFAS compounds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Derivations of osmotic pressure and the solution-diffusion flux equation are **frequently asked 7-mark questions** in the ESE. Memorize the logical steps, not just the final equation.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Van't Hoff Equation for Osmotic Pressure (π)

### Starting Premise
Consider a semi-permeable membrane separating pure solvent (Side 1) from a dilute solution (Side 2). At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solvent on both sides must be equal.

### Step 1: Chemical Potential of Pure Solvent
$$\mu_{1,\text{pure}}(T,P) = \mu_{1}^{\circ}(T) + V_{1} \cdot P$$

where $V_{1}$ is the molar volume of the solvent and $\mu_{1}^{\circ}$ is the standard chemical potential.

### Step 2: Chemical Potential of Solvent in Solution
The chemical potential of a solvent in an ideal dilute solution is reduced by an entropic term involving the mole fraction $x_1$:
$$\mu_{1,\text{soln}}(T,P+\pi) = \mu_{1}^{\circ}(T) + R T \ln x_{1} + V_{1} \cdot (P + \pi)$$

### Step 3: Apply Equilibrium Condition ($\mu_{1,\text{pure}} = \mu_{1,\text{soln}}$)
At equilibrium, the higher pressure $P + \pi$ on the solution side exactly compensates for the entropic reduction:
$$\mu_{1}^{\circ} + V_{1} P = \mu_{1}^{\circ} + R T \ln x_{1} + V_{1}(P + \pi)$$

### Step 4: Simplify
Cancel $\mu_{1}^{\circ}$ and $V_1 P$ from both sides:
$$0 = R T \ln x_{1} + V_{1} \cdot \pi$$

### Step 5: Solve for π
Rearranging:
$$\pi = -\dfrac{R T \ln x_{1}}{V_{1}}$$

### Step 6: Apply the Dilute Solution Approximation
For a dilute solution, $\ln x_{1} \approx -(1 - x_{1}) \approx -x_{2}$ (where $x_2$ is the solute mole fraction), and $x_{2} \approx n_{2}/n_{1}$. Combining with $V_1 \approx V_{\text{soln}}/n_1$:
$$\pi \approx \dfrac{R T \cdot n_{2}}{V_{\text{soln}}}$$

### Step 7: Final Van't Hoff Form
Defining molarity $M = n_2 / V_{\text{soln}}$ and including the van't Hoff dissociation factor $i$:
$$\boxed{\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This equation tells us that osmotic pressure is a **colligative property** — it depends only on the *number* of dissolved particles, not their chemical identity. This is why a 1 M NaCl solution ($\pi \approx 2 \cdot RT$) exerts roughly twice the osmotic pressure of a 1 M glucose solution ($\pi \approx RT$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solution-Diffusion Model for Water Flux ($J_w$)

### Starting Premise
Water dissolves into the dense, non-porous membrane at the high-pressure feed side, diffuses through the membrane thickness $\delta x$, and desorbs on the low-pressure permeate side.

### Step 1: Fick's First Law of Diffusion
The diffusive molar flux of water through the membrane is:
$$J_w = -D_w \cdot \dfrac{dC_w}{dx}$$

where $D_w$ is the diffusion coefficient of water in the membrane and $C_w(x)$ is the local water concentration.

### Step 2: Steady-State Integration Across Membrane
At steady state, $J_w$ is constant through the membrane. Integrating from $x = 0$ (feed side) to $x = \delta x$ (permeate side):
$$J_w \cdot \delta x = -D_w \cdot [C_{w,p} - C_{w,f}]$$

### Step 3: Express Concentrations via Solubility (Henry-Type Law)
The water concentration at each face is proportional to the local partial pressure / chemical potential. Combining with the chemical potential gradient and the pressure-volume work:
$$C_{w,f} = C_{w}^{\circ} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{V_w \cdot (P_f - P^{\circ})}{RT}\right) \quad \text{(feed side)}$$
$$C_{w,p} = C_{w}^{\circ} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{V_w \cdot (P_p - P^{\circ})}{RT}\right) \quad \text{(permeate side)}$$

### Step 4: Subtract the Two Faces
$$C_{w,f} - C_{w,p} = \dfrac{D_w \cdot C_{w}^{\circ} \cdot V_w}{R T} \cdot (P_f - P_p)$$

### Step 5: Define Water Permeability Constant
Group all membrane-and-water-property constants into a single lumped parameter:
$$A = \dfrac{D_w \cdot C_w^{\circ} \cdot V_w}{R T \cdot \delta x}$$

This constant $A$ has units of m/s·Pa and is a **pure membrane property**.

### Step 6: Final Flux Equation (Including Osmotic Pressure)
The chemical potential must account for the osmotic pressure difference. The true net driving force is the **applied pressure difference minus the osmotic pressure difference**:
$$J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$

$$\boxed{J_w = A \cdot \left[(P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p)\right]}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the elegance — the water flux is **linearly proportional** to the net driving pressure. This is why RO engineers plot $J_w$ versus $(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ to extract the membrane constant $A$ experimentally.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 2000 mg/L NaCl solution (assumed 90 % ionized, $T = 298$ K) is to be desalinated by RO. The applied pressure is 15 bar and the permeate is essentially salt-free. Calculate: (a) the osmotic pressure of the feed, (b) the net driving pressure, and (c) the water flux given $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s·Pa.

### (a) Osmotic Pressure of the Feed

**Step 1 — Convert mg/L to molarity (M):**
$$\text{Molecular weight of NaCl} = 58.44 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$M = \dfrac{2000 \text{ mg/L}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g}} = 0.0342 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Van't Hoff equation with $i = 1.9$ (90 % ionization):**
$$\pi_f = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T = 1.9 \times 0.0342 \times 0.0821 \times 298$$

$$\boxed{\pi_f \approx 1.59 \text{ atm} \approx 1.61 \text{ bar}}$$

### (b) Net Driving Pressure

**Step 1 — Convert feed pressure to consistent units:**
$$\Delta P = 15 \text{ bar} - 0 \text{ bar} = 15 \text{ bar}$$
$$\Delta \pi = 1.61 \text{ bar} - 0 \text{ bar} = 1.61 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Compute NDP:**
$$\boxed{\text{NDP} = 15 - 1.61 = 13.39 \text{ bar} \approx 1.339 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}}$$

### (c) Water Flux

**Step 1 — Apply Solution-Diffusion equation:**
$$J_w = A \cdot \text{NDP} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.339 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{J_w = 3.35 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \approx 120.5 \text{ L/m}^{2}\text{·h}}$$

> This is a realistic brackish-water RO flux value, confirming the engineering validity of the calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RO Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete functional architecture of a Reverse Osmosis system, including the feed conditioning stage, the high-pressure pumping train, the membrane module, and the concentrate & permeate handling. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Wastewater Feed Tank] --> A2[Pre Treatment - Multimedia Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Pre Treatment - Activated Carbon Filter]
    A3 --> A4[Pre Treatment - Cartridge Filter 5 Micron]
    A4 --> A5[Booster Pump - Low Pressure]
    A5 --> B1[High Pressure Pump - Multistage Centrifugal]
    B1 --> C1[RO Membrane Module - Spiral Wound]
    C1 --> C2[Pressure Vessel Array - Series Parallel]
    C2 --> D1[Permeate Stream - Low TDS Product]
    C2 --> D2[Concentrate Stream - Reject]
    C2 --> D3[Energy Recovery Device - Turbine or PX]
    D3 --> B1
    D1 --> E1[Post Treatment - UV Disinfection]
    E1 --> E2[Permeate Storage Tank - Reuse Ready]
    D2 --> F1[Concentrate Management - ZLD or Disposal]
    B1 --> G1[Pressure Gauges and Flow Meters]
    G1 --> H1[PLC Control Panel with CIP Skid]
    H1 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for the KTU Examiner:**
> - **Node A1–A4:** Pre-treatment is **non-negotiable** in RO. SDI (Silt Density Index) of feed must be $\lt 3$ to prevent membrane fouling.
> - **Node B1:** The high-pressure pump typically delivers **10 – 80 bar** depending on feed salinity.
> - **Node C1–C2:** The membrane module is the heart of the system. Spiral-wound modules dominate ~80 % of the industrial market.
> - **Node D3:** Energy Recovery Devices (ERDs) reduce specific energy consumption from ~6 kWh/m³ down to ~2 – 3 kWh/m³ in seawater RO.
> - **Node H1:** Clean-In-Place (CIP) systems are critical for long-term membrane flux restoration.

## 4.2 Schematic — Osmotic vs. Reverse Osmosis States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Side 1 - Pure Solvent\nLow Solute Concentration] --- M1[Semi Permeable Membrane]
    M1 --- S2[Side 2 - Concentrated Solution\nHigh Solute Concentration]
    S2 --> AR1[Normal Osmosis\nApplied Pressure less than Osmotic\nSolvent flows toward S2]
    S2 --> AR2[Reverse Osmosis\nApplied Pressure greater than Osmotic\nSolvent flows toward S1]
    AR1 -.-> S1
    AR2 -.-> S1
```

> **Read this diagram as:** When the applied pressure on Side 2 is **less than the osmotic pressure**, solvent moves from S1 → S2 (normal osmosis, the natural flow). When the applied pressure is **greater than the osmotic pressure**, the flow is reversed (S2 → S1), producing purified solvent on Side 1 — this is the Reverse Osmosis regime.

## 4.3 Cross-Flow Velocity vs. Dead-End Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP1[RO Operating Modes] --> OP2[Cross Flow Mode\nTangential Flow over Membrane\nContinuous Sweep reduces Fouling\nIndustry Standard]
    OP1 --> OP3[Dead End Mode\nAll Feed Perpendicular to Membrane\nHigh Recovery but High Fouling\nUsed in Batch Lab Scale]
```

> **Engineering Note:** Industrial RO almost universally operates in **cross-flow mode** because the tangential flow continuously scours rejected salts off the membrane surface, reducing concentration polarization and extending membrane life.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Reverse Osmosis. State any two factors affecting RO membrane performance.**

> **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
> Reverse Osmosis is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which a hydraulic pressure greater than the natural osmotic pressure of the feed solution is applied across a semi-permeable membrane, forcing the solvent (water) to flow from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration.
> **Two factors affecting RO performance:** (1) Applied hydraulic pressure — higher pressure increases water flux up to the membrane's compaction limit; (2) Feed water temperature — higher temperature reduces viscosity and increases flux by approximately 3 % per °C. *(Other acceptable factors: feed TDS/concentration, membrane fouling, concentration polarization, pH.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is osmotic pressure? Write the Van't Hoff equation and explain each term.**

> **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
> Osmotic pressure ($\pi$) is the minimum pressure that must be applied to the solution side of a semi-permeable membrane to prevent the inward flow of pure solvent across the membrane. It is a colligative property proportional to the number of dissolved solute particles.
> **Van't Hoff Equation:** $\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T$
> where $i$ = van't Hoff factor (number of ions per molecule on dissociation), $M$ = molar concentration of solute (mol/L), $R$ = universal gas constant (**0.0821 L·atm/mol·K**), $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure starting from the equality of chemical potential of solvent across a semi-permeable membrane at equilibrium. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A brackish water RO plant treats 500 m³/day of feed with TDS 3000 mg/L as NaCl. The applied pressure is 18 bar, permeate is assumed salt-free, temperature is 25 °C, and the membrane water permeability constant $A$ is $3.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m/s·Pa. Calculate: (i) osmotic pressure of feed in bar, (ii) net driving pressure, (iii) water flux in L/m²·h. Take van't Hoff factor $i = 1.85$. (7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Derivation of Van't Hoff Equation

**Step 1:** Consider a semi-permeable membrane separating pure solvent (Side 1) from a solution of molarity $M$ (Side 2).

**Step 2:** Write the chemical potential of pure solvent at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$:
$$\mu_1^{\text{pure}} = \mu_1^{\circ}(T) + V_1 \cdot P$$

**Step 3:** Write the chemical potential of solvent in the solution at the same temperature but at pressure $P + \pi$:
$$\mu_1^{\text{soln}} = \mu_1^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln x_1 + V_1 \cdot (P + \pi)$$

**Step 4:** At equilibrium, the chemical potentials must be equal:
$$\mu_1^{\circ} + V_1 P = \mu_1^{\circ} + RT \ln x_1 + V_1(P + \pi)$$

**Step 5:** Cancel common terms:
$$0 = RT \ln x_1 + V_1 \pi$$

**Step 6:** For a dilute solution, $\ln x_1 \approx -x_2 \approx -n_2/n_1$ and $V_1 \cdot n_1 = V_{\text{soln}}$:
$$\pi = \dfrac{n_2 \cdot R T}{V_{\text{soln}}}$$

**Step 7:** Replace with molarity $M = n_2/V_{\text{soln}}$ and the dissociation factor $i$:
$$\boxed{\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T}$$

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Problem

**(i) Osmotic Pressure of Feed:**

**Step 1 — Convert TDS to molarity:**
$$M = \dfrac{3000 \text{ mg/L}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000} = 0.0513 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Van't Hoff equation:**
$$\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T = 1.85 \times 0.0513 \times 0.0821 \times 298$$
$$\boxed{\pi \approx 2.32 \text{ atm} \approx 2.35 \text{ bar}}$$

**(ii) Net Driving Pressure:**
$$\text{NDP} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi = 18 - 2.35 = 15.65 \text{ bar}$$
$$\boxed{\text{NDP} = 15.65 \text{ bar} = 1.565 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}$$

**(iii) Water Flux:**
$$J_w = A \cdot \text{NDP} = 3.0 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.565 \times 10^6 = 4.695 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

Convert to L/m²·h:
$$J_w = 4.695 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^{3} \times 3600 \text{ s/h}$$
$$\boxed{J_w \approx 169 \text{ L/m}^{2}\text{·h}}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Reverse Osmosis plant for wastewater treatment. List the major components and state the function of each. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Explain the Solution-Diffusion model for transport through an RO membrane. Derive the expression for water flux $J_w$ in terms of the net driving pressure. (7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — RO Plant Description

**Working Principle:** Wastewater feed is pre-treated to remove suspended solids, free chlorine, and SDI to acceptable limits. It is then pressurized by a high-pressure pump to a value exceeding the osmotic pressure of the feed. The pressurized feed is passed over a semi-permeable membrane in a cross-flow configuration. Two streams exit: a **permeate** (purified water) of low TDS, and a **concentrate/reject** of high TDS. The permeate is disinfected and stored; the concentrate is sent to disposal or further treatment (e.g., ZLD).

**Major Components & Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feed Tank & Pre-Treatment** | Stores feed and removes particulates, organics, and chlorine that can damage the membrane |
| 2 | **Cartridge Filter (5 µm)** | Final particulate barrier protecting the high-pressure pump and membrane |
| 3 | **High-Pressure Pump** | Provides the driving pressure (10 – 80 bar) to overcome osmotic pressure |
| 4 | **RO Membrane Module (Spiral Wound)** | Selectively allows water to pass while rejecting salts and organics |
| 5 | **Pressure Vessels & Array** | House the membranes in series-parallel to achieve desired recovery and flux |
| 6 | **Energy Recovery Device (ERD)** | Recovers hydraulic energy from the concentrate stream (saves 30 – 60 % energy) |
| 7 | **Permeate Collection & Disinfection** | Gathers purified water and applies UV/chlorine for microbial safety |
| 8 | **Clean-In-Place (CIP) System** | Periodic chemical cleaning to restore membrane flux |
| 9 | **Instrumentation & Control (PLC)** | Monitors pressure, flow, conductivity, pH, and temperature continuously |

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Solution-Diffusion Model

**Conceptual Basis:** The membrane is treated as a **dense, non-porous** film. Water and solutes first dissolve into the membrane on the high-pressure (feed) side, then diffuse through the membrane under a chemical potential gradient, and finally desorb on the low-pressure (permeate) side.

**Step 1:** Apply Fick's First Law of Diffusion to the water concentration gradient inside the membrane:
$$J_w = -D_w \cdot \dfrac{dC_w}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, $J_w$ is constant. Integrate across the membrane thickness $\delta x$:
$$J_w \cdot \delta x = D_w \cdot (C_{w,f} - C_{w,p})$$

**Step 3:** Express the water concentration at each face using a Henry-type linear sorption:
$$C_{w,f} - C_{w,p} = \dfrac{C_w^{\circ} \cdot V_w}{R T} \cdot (P_f - P_p)$$

**Step 4:** Group the membrane properties into a constant $A = \dfrac{D_w \cdot C_w^{\circ} \cdot V_w}{R T \cdot \delta x}$:
$$J_w = A \cdot (P_f - P_p)$$

**Step 5:** Incorporate the osmotic pressure difference across the membrane to obtain the true net driving force:
$$\boxed{J_w = A \cdot \left[(P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p)\right]}$$

> **Conclusion:** Water flux is directly proportional to the **net driving pressure** (NDP). The membrane constant $A$ is determined experimentally by plotting $J_w$ vs. NDP — the slope of the line gives $A$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the osmotic pressure term** in the flux equation (using $\Delta P$ instead of $\Delta P - \Delta \pi$) — costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not specifying the van't Hoff factor $i$** when computing osmotic pressure of ionic solutions like NaCl — examiners expect students to write $i = 1.85$ or $i = 2$ for fully ionized salts.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** in the numerical problem — students often mix atm, bar, and Pa without conversion. Always convert the final NDP to **Pascals** when using SI units of $A$.
> 4. **Skipping the chemical potential step** in the osmotic pressure derivation — KTU examiners allot **at least 3 marks** specifically for the equilibrium condition $\mu_1^{\text{pure}} = \mu_1^{\text{soln}}$.
> 5. **Drawing a vague "membrane diagram"** in the RO plant question — present a **labelled block diagram** with arrows showing feed, permeate, and concentrate flows.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Reverse Osmosis = Pressure-Driven** membrane process that **reverses** the natural osmotic flow by applying $\Delta P > \pi$.
- ✅ The **Van't Hoff equation** $\pi = i M R T$ is the foundation — know the constants $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/mol·K (CGS) and $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K (SI).
- ✅ The **Solution-Diffusion model** gives $J_w = A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$; the slope of $J_w$ vs. NDP plot = $A$.
- ✅ **Salt Rejection** $R_j = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} \times 100$; **Recovery** $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Typical operating pressures:** SWRO ≈ 55 – 80 bar; BWRO ≈ 10 – 25 bar.
- ✅ **Concentration polarization** reduces effective NDP — managed by **cross-flow velocity** and **membrane spacer design**.
- ✅ **Pre-treatment is critical** — feed SDI must be $\lt 3$ to prevent irreversible membrane fouling.
- ✅ **Energy Recovery Devices (ERDs)** are mandatory in large-scale SWRO for energy efficiency.
- ✅ RO is the **final polishing step** in a multi-barrier wastewater reclamation train — preceded by coagulation, filtration, and often UF/MF.
- ✅ **Real-world flag values** to remember: 2000 mg/L NaCl ≈ 1.6 bar osmotic pressure; seawater (35,000 mg/L) ≈ 25 – 30 bar osmotic pressure.
- ✅ In the **ESE**, always present a labelled block diagram for the 7-mark plant-description question.
- ✅ Always end numerical answers with a **dimensional check and a sanity check** ("is this flux realistic?").
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) inside markdown tables when writing LaTeX to prevent rendering errors.

---

topics.title: Membrane Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Membrane Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Membrane Material** is defined as the selective barrier substance (polymeric, ceramic, metallic, or composite) fabricated into a thin, semi-permeable layer that governs the separation mechanism—based on size exclusion, solution-diffusion, or charge repulsion—in advanced wastewater treatment systems such as Microfiltration (MF), Ultrafiltration (UF), Nanofiltration (NF), and Reverse Osmosis (RO).

The performance of any membrane separation process is **fundamentally governed by the chemistry, morphology, and surface energetics** of the material from which it is fabricated. Material selection dictates critical operational parameters such as **hydrophilicity**, **chemical resistance**, **fouling propensity**, **mechanical strength**, and **selectivity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **sieve used in a kitchen** to separate flour from bran:
- The **mesh size** of the sieve (analogous to membrane pore size) decides what passes through.
- The **material** of the sieve (plastic, metal, cloth) decides how long it lasts, how it reacts to water, and whether it gets clogged easily.

Similarly, a **membrane material** is the "fabric" of the sieve used to clean wastewater. Just as a metal sieve is more durable but expensive, different membrane materials offer trade-offs between **cost, durability, hydrophilicity, and separation efficiency**. For instance, a **hydrophilic (water-loving) material** repels oil and resists fouling much like a clean glass surface sheds water droplets easily, whereas a **hydrophobic material** attracts oils and clogs rapidly.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material Performance Metrics (Board-Favorite Vocabulary):**
> - **Mean Pore Size (d):** Measured in **µm** for MF, **nm** for UF/NF.
> - **Porosity (ε):** Ratio of void volume to total membrane volume (dimensionless, typically **0.3 – 0.7**).
> - **Hydrophilicity:** Quantified by **Water Contact Angle (θ)**; θ < 90° = hydrophilic, θ > 90° = hydrophobic.
> - **Membrane Flux (J):** Measured in **L/m²·h (LMH)**.
> - **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** Expressed in **Daltons (Da)** for UF membranes.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (High-Level Hook)

Membrane materials are broadly classified into four families:

| Family | Example | Driving Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymeric** | PES, PVDF, PSf, CA, PA, PAN | Low cost, versatile, dominant in MBR/UF |
| **Ceramic** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂, SiC | High strength, chemical resistance, high cost |
| **Metallic** | Pd, Ag, Stainless Steel | Thermal stability, niche catalytic use |
| **Composite / Mixed Matrix** | TFN (Thin Film Nanocomposite) | Hybrid performance enhancement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymeric Membrane Materials (Most Exam-Relevant)

Polymeric membranes dominate over **85% of the commercial wastewater treatment market** because they are inexpensive, easy to manufacture via **phase inversion**, and tunable. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following six polymers:

### 2.1.1 Polyethersulfone (PES)
- **Structure:** Aromatic backbone with sulfone and ether linkages.
- **Advantages:** Excellent thermal stability (up to 180 °C), high mechanical strength, good chemical resistance to acids and oxidants.
- **Drawbacks:** **Inherently hydrophobic**, hence highly prone to **fouling** by organic matter. Requires **hydrophilization** (e.g., PVP blending, PEG grafting, plasma treatment).
- **Application:** MBR (Membrane Bioreactor), UF for protein separation.

### 2.1.2 Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)
- **Structure:** Repeating unit of –(CH₂–CF₂)ₙ–.
- **Advantages:** Outstanding chemical resistance (especially to **chlorine, ozone, and strong oxidants**), mechanical robustness, UV stable.
- **Drawbacks:** Hydrophobic (water contact angle ~120°), requires modification.
- **Application:** UF, MBR, pretreatment in RO plants.

### 2.1.3 Polysulfone (PSf) & Polyphenylsulfone (PPSU)
- **Advantages:** High glass transition temperature (Tₘ ~ 190 °C), mechanically rigid, FDA-approved.
- **Drawbacks:** Sensitive to strong polar solvents.
- **Application:** Hemodialysis (medical grade), industrial UF.

### 2.1.4 Cellulose Acetate (CA)
- **Structure:** Acetylated cellulose; degree of substitution controls properties.
- **Advantages:** **Naturally hydrophilic** (low fouling), cheap, biodegradable.
- **Drawbacks:** Poor chemical resistance, narrow pH operating range (pH 3–7), susceptible to **biological attack (biodegradation)**.
- **Application:** RO desalination (legacy), UF, early MBR.

### 2.1.5 Polyamide (PA) / Thin-Film Composite (TFC)
- **Mechanism:** Formed by **interfacial polymerization** of *m*-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on a porous support.
- **Advantages:** Excellent selectivity, high rejection (>99% for NaCl in RO), dense structure.
- **Drawbacks:** **Chlorine sensitive** (amide bond cleavage), susceptible to biofouling.
- **Application:** NF and RO desalination—**the global industry standard.**

### 2.1.6 Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)
- **Advantages:** Good hydrophilicity, solvent resistance, stable up to 120 °C.
- **Application:** UF for oil/water separation, water treatment.

## 2.2 Ceramic Membrane Materials

Ceramic membranes address the limitations of polymers in **harsh environments**.

- **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** Most common; stable, high hardness.
- **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** Excellent pH tolerance (0–14), used in the active top layer of composite ceramics.
- **Titania (TiO₂):** Photocatalytic self-cleaning property under UV—**emerging anti-fouling application**.
- **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** Extreme thermal conductivity, used in high-temperature wastewater (e.g., produced water in oil & gas).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why:** Ceramic membranes have **3–10× longer lifespan** than polymeric ones but cost **5–20× more**. They are economically justified in industrial wastewater with aggressive chemical/thermal loads.

## 2.3 Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs) & TFN

A modern hybrid approach:
- **Mixed Matrix Membrane (MMM):** Embedding inorganic fillers (zeolites, GO, MOFs, CNTs) in a polymer matrix combines **mechanical robustness of filler + processability of polymer**.
- **Thin Film Nanocomposite (TFN):** Nanoparticles embedded in the PA layer of TFC membranes; improves permeability-selectivity trade-off.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1: Key Equations Governing Membrane Materials Performance**

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Application / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Darcy's Law (Permeation Flux)** | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | Driving pressure across viscous resistance | LMH or m³/m²·s |
| **Hagen-Poiseuille (Cylindrical Pore)** | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot \Delta P}{32 \mu L \tau}$ | Flux through idealized straight pores | m³/m²·s |
| **Kozeny-Carman (Tortuous Pore)** | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{\,3} \cdot \Delta P}{K \mu S^{2} (1-\varepsilon)^{2} L \tau}$ | Flux through porous, tortuous membrane | m³/m²·s |
| **Porosity** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$ | Void fraction of membrane | dimensionless |
| **Tortuosity** | $\tau = \left(\dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{straight}}\right)^{2}$ | Path deviation in porous medium | dimensionless |
| **Membrane Resistance** | $R_m = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu J}$ | Hydraulic resistance in series | m⁻¹ |
| **Rejection Coefficient** | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | Solute removal efficiency | % |
| **Water Contact Angle (Young)** | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ | Hydrophilicity / wettability | degrees |
| **Surface Free Energy (Owens-Wendt)** | $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^{d} + \gamma_S^{p}$ | Dispersive + polar components | mN/m |
| **Solution-Diffusion (RO/NF)** | $J_w = \dfrac{P_w}{\ell}\left(\Delta P - \Delta \pi\right)$ | Solvent flux in dense membranes | m/s |

> **Where:**
> $J$ = flux; $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa); $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s); $R_m$ = membrane resistance; $d_p$ = pore diameter (m); $L$ = membrane thickness; $\tau$ = tortuosity; $K$ = Kozeny constant (~5); $S$ = specific surface area; $C_p$, $C_f$ = permeate / feed concentrations; $P_w$ = water permeability; $\ell$ = dense layer thickness; $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Membrane Material Selection

- **MBR (Municipal Sewage):** PES or PVDF flat-sheet/hollow-fiber; hydrophilic-modified grades preferred.
- **Industrial Oily Wastewater:** PAN, PVDF, or ceramic (ZrO₂) UF.
- **Seawater Desalination:** Polyamide TFC RO membranes.
- **Landfill Leachate:** NF/RO with PA or ceramic membranes due to extreme pH/chlorine.
- **Pharma & Food:** PES UF, often with PVP or PEG as hydrophilic pore-former.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen-Poiseuille Flux Through a Cylindrical Membrane Pore

This derivation **links pore geometry to flux** and is a frequently tested concept in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Starting Equation (Navier–Stokes for steady, laminar flow in a cylindrical tube):**

$$ \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \mu \bar{v}}{r^{2}} $$

Here, $\bar{v}$ is the average velocity through a single pore of radius $r$, and $L$ is the pore length (effective membrane thickness).

**Step 2 — Solve for the Average Velocity:**

$$ \bar{v} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{8 \mu L} $$

**Step 3 — Convert Volumetric Flow per Pore to Membrane Flux (J):**

Flux $J$ = volumetric flow rate per unit membrane area. The volumetric flow through **one pore** is $Q_{pore} = \bar{v} \cdot (\pi r^{2})$. The number of pores per unit membrane area is:

$$ n_p = \frac{N_p}{A_m} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A_m \cdot \tau^{-1}}{(\pi r^{2}) \cdot A_m} \cdot \frac{1}{L} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\pi r^{2} L \tau} $$

**Step 4 — Total Flux (multiplication of single-pore flow × pore density):**

$$ J = n_p \cdot Q_{pore} = \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\pi r^{2} L \tau}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{8 \mu L}\right)(\pi r^{2}) $$

**Step 5 — Simplification:** Cancelling $\pi r^{2}$:

$$ J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{8 \mu L^{2} \tau} $$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of pore diameter $d_p = 2r$:**

$$ r^{2} = \frac{d_p^{\,2}}{4} \Rightarrow J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot \Delta P}{32 \mu L^{2} \tau} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Flux scales with **$d_p^{\,2}$** — doubling the pore size gives a **4× increase in flux**. This is why **UF (10 nm) gives far higher flux than NF (1 nm)** at the same pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Membrane Resistance Using Darcy's Law

**Step 1 — Darcy's Law for Permeation:**

$$ J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} $$

**Step 2 — Membrane resistance for porous membranes (Hagen-Poiseuille form):**

Substituting the result of Derivation 1, the membrane resistance becomes:

$$ R_m = \frac{32 L^{2} \tau}{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^{\,2}} $$

**Step 3 — Series Resistance Model for Fouled Membranes (Resistance-in-Series, RIS):**

In a real MBR/UF operation, total resistance is the sum of contributions:

$$ R_{total} = R_m + R_{c} + R_{p} + R_{cp} $$

Where:
- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance
- $R_c$ = cake layer resistance
- $R_p$ = pore plugging resistance
- $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance

**Step 4 — Practical use:** When flux declines during operation, we plot $\dfrac{1}{J}$ vs. $\Delta P$ — the slope gives $\dfrac{\mu \cdot R_{total}}{\Delta P}$ and hence $R_{total}$. This **separates fouling from membrane degradation**, a popular KTU question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrophilicity from Contact Angle (Young's Equation)

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic equilibrium of a droplet at the three-phase line:**

$$ \gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos \theta $$

Where $\gamma_{SG}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LG}$ are the interfacial free energies of solid-gas, solid-liquid, and liquid-gas interfaces, and $\theta$ is the water contact angle.

**Step 2 — Owens-Wendt Decomposition (solid surface free energy):**

$$ \gamma_S = \gamma_S^{d} + \gamma_S^{p} $$

Where superscripts $d$ and $p$ denote **dispersive (London) and polar (acid-base)** components.

**Step 3 — Work of Adhesion ($W_a$):**

$$ W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos \theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^{d}\gamma_{LV}^{d}} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^{p}\gamma_{LV}^{p}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A lower $\theta$ (more hydrophilic) corresponds to a higher $W_a$ between water and membrane, meaning **water spreads more easily**, displacing foulants and reducing fouling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1: Material Selection Trade-Off Matrix (Examination Reference)**

| Selection Criterion | Best Polymeric Choice | Best Ceramic Choice | KTU Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrophilicity (low θ)** | CA, PAN, PA-TFC | TiO₂ (after UV) | CA biodegrades—avoid biological WW |
| **Chemical Resistance (oxidants)** | PVDF | ZrO₂, SiC | PVDF best for chlorinated WW |
| **pH Range** | PVDF (2–12), PES (1–13) | ZrO₂ (0–14) | Ceramic wins in extreme pH |
| **Thermal Stability** | PES (180 °C), PPSU | All ceramics (>500 °C) | Ceramic dominant in hot WW |
| **Cost (per m²)** | **Lowest** | 5–20× polymeric | Polymer is industry default |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Moderate | Excellent | Ceramic for high TMP |
| **Anti-Fouling** | Modified PAN/PA | TiO₂ photocatalysis | Surface modification key |
| **Biocompatibility** | PES, CA | All ceramics | PES dominates MBR |
| **Lifespan** | 3–7 years | 10–20 years | TCO favors ceramic in industry |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any three polymeric membrane materials used in wastewater treatment and state one key property of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Polyethersulfone (PES):** Excellent thermal stability (up to 180 °C) and high mechanical strength; widely used in MBR/UF. (1 mark)
2. **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF):** Outstanding chemical resistance, especially to **chlorine and strong oxidants**; suited for RO pretreatment. (1 mark)
3. **Cellulose Acetate (CA):** Naturally hydrophilic with low fouling tendency, but narrow pH operating range (pH 3–7). (1 mark)

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: What is the difference between hydrophilic and hydrophobic membranes? Why is hydrophilicity preferred in wastewater treatment?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Hydrophilic membranes** have a water contact angle $\theta < 90°$ and attract water; **hydrophobic membranes** have $\theta > 90°$ and repel water. (1 mark)
- Hydrophilic surfaces reduce **organic and biological fouling** because water molecules form a hydration layer that **prevents foulant adhesion**. (1 mark)
- Example: Cellulose Acetate (CA) is hydrophilic; PVDF is hydrophobic unless surface-modified with PVP/PEG. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Classify membrane materials used in advanced wastewater treatment. Discuss in detail the properties, advantages, and limitations of any two polymeric membrane materials commonly used in MBR systems.**

**Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Membrane Materials (7 Marks)**

Membrane materials are classified into four major categories:

**1. Polymeric Membranes (1.5 marks):** Most widely used (~85% of market) due to low cost and processability.
- Examples: PES, PVDF, PSf, CA, PA, PAN.

**2. Ceramic Membranes (1.5 marks):** Inorganic oxides/carbides used in harsh chemical/thermal environments.
- Examples: Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂, SiC.

**3. Metallic Membranes (1 mark):** Niche application (Pd for hydrogen separation, SS for microfiltration).
- High thermal stability, but expensive.

**4. Composite / Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs, TFNs) (2 marks):** Hybrid of organic and inorganic phases.
- TFN: nanoparticles (zeolites, GO, MOFs) embedded in polyamide active layer.
- MMM: filler dispersed throughout polymer matrix.
- Combine mechanical strength of inorganics with processability of polymers.

**1 mark** reserved for neat tabulation or schematic.

#### **Part (b) — Polymeric Materials for MBR (7 Marks)**

**(i) Polyethersulfone (PES) — 3.5 marks**

- **Properties:** Aromatic backbone, Tₘ ~ 230 °C, glass transition ~ 220 °C, contact angle ~ 80–90°, MWCO range 1–500 kDa.
- **Advantages:** Excellent thermal/chemical resistance; high mechanical strength; good pH tolerance (1–13); resistant to oxidants (Cl₂ up to 50 ppm).
- **Limitations:** **Hydrophobic → fouling-prone**; requires hydrophilic modification (PVP blending, plasma treatment, PEG grafting).
- **Application in MBR:** Hollow-fiber and flat-sheet configurations; used for solid-liquid separation in mixed liquor with MLSS 8,000–12,000 mg/L.

**(ii) Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) — 3.5 marks**

- **Properties:** Semi-crystalline fluoropolymer, Tₘ ~ 170 °C, contact angle ~ 120° (hydrophobic), MWCO 50–500 kDa.
- **Advantages:** **Best chemical resistance** (Cl₂, O₃, strong acids); UV resistant; mechanically strong; long operational life (5–7 years).
- **Limitations:** Inherently hydrophobic → requires **hydrophilic modification** for MBR use; more expensive than PES.
- **Application in MBR:** Hollow-fiber UF modules in submerged MBRs; preferred for industrial effluents with high TDS and oxidant residues.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Explain the role of membrane materials in determining separation performance. With the help of Darcy's law and Hagen-Poiseuille equation, derive the relationship between flux, pore size, porosity, and tortuosity of a porous membrane.**

**Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Role of Membrane Material in Separation Performance (7 Marks)**

**1. Material Defines Pore Architecture (2 marks):**
- Pore size distribution, porosity (ε), and tortuosity (τ) are dictated by the **fabrication route** (phase inversion, sintering, interfacial polymerization).
- Example: Phase-inverted PES produces asymmetric pores (skin layer 0.1–1 µm; sublayer 50–200 µm).

**2. Material Controls Hydrophilicity / Surface Energy (2 marks):**
- A hydrophilic surface (CA, modified PVDF) yields low water contact angle, high work of adhesion → **reduced fouling** and stable flux.
- Hydrophobic surfaces (virgin PVDF, PSf) attract oil and protein foulants → rapid flux decline.

**3. Material Governs Chemical and Thermal Stability (2 marks):**
- Polyamide TFC: high selectivity but **chlorine sensitive**; PVDF: chlorine tolerant.
- Ceramic (ZrO₂): extreme pH and temperature tolerance.

**4. Material Determines Selectivity Mechanism (1 mark):**
- Microporous → size exclusion (MF/UF); Dense → solution-diffusion (RO/NF); Charged → Donnan exclusion (NF).

#### **Part (b) — Derivation of Flux Equation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Darcy's Law (1 mark):**

$$ J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} $$

**Step 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille for Single Cylindrical Pore (2 marks):**

$$ \bar{v} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^{2}}{8 \mu L} $$

**Step 3 — Express Membrane Flux in terms of Porosity and Tortuosity (2.5 marks):**

$$ J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot \Delta P}{32 \mu L^{2} \tau} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- $J \propto d_p^{\,2}$ — flux increases with **square of pore diameter**.
- $J \propto \varepsilon / \tau$ — higher porosity and lower tortuosity improve flux.
- $J \propto 1/L^{2}$ — thinner membranes give higher flux; drives research into TFC and TFN designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students write Hagen-Poiseuille as $J \propto d_p^{4}$ (mixing it up with Kozeny-Carman). Correct is **$d_p^{\,2}$** for cylindrical pores.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that **tortuosity appears as a squared factor** in the denominator ($\tau$, not $\tau^{2}$). Many students write $\tau^{2}$ incorrectly.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to convert $r$ to $d_p$ — examiners expect the final form in **diameter**.
> 4. **Mark Loss Zone:** Not stating **assumptions** (laminar flow, cylindrical straight pores, no slip at wall) — lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Unit Consistency:** Always quote $J$ in LMH in numerical problems; use SI (m/s) only in derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Module 3: Membrane Materials**

- **Four families of membrane materials:** Polymeric, Ceramic, Metallic, Composite (MMM/TFN).
- **Most-used polymers:** PES, PVDF, PSf, CA, PA, PAN — know **1 property + 1 application** of each.
- **PA-TFC** is the **industry standard for RO/NF**; formed by **interfacial polymerization** of MPD + TMC.
- **CA is naturally hydrophilic** but **biodegrades and pH-limited** (3–7).
- **PVDF is the most chemically resistant polymer** — ideal for chlorinated/oxidant-rich wastewater.
- **Ceramic (ZrO₂, SiC) for harsh** pH/temperature; cost is 5–20× polymeric.
- **Hydrophilicity is quantified by water contact angle θ**; θ < 90° = hydrophilic.
- **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ — relates surface energies to contact angle.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille flux:** $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot d_p^{\,2} \cdot \Delta P}{32 \mu L^{2} \tau}$ — flux scales as **$d_p^{\,2}$**.
- **Porosity (ε)** is the void fraction; typical polymeric UF: **0.3 – 0.7**.
- **Tortuosity (τ)** is the path deviation; ideal straight pores: τ = 1; typical UF: τ = 1.5 – 2.5.
- **Kozeny-Carman equation** applies to **irregular tortuous pores** (more realistic for phase-inversion membranes).
- **Darcy's Law:** $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ — basis of all flux calculations.
- **Rejection (R)** $= 1 - C_p/C_f$ — expressed as a percentage.
- **Resistance-in-Series Model:** $R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p + R_{cp}$ — quantifies **fouling contribution**.
- **Solution-Diffusion Model** governs dense RO/NF membranes: $J_w = \dfrac{P_w}{\ell}\left(\Delta P - \Delta \pi\right)$.
- **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM):** polymer + inorganic filler (zeolite, GO, MOF, CNT) for permeability-selectivity trade-off improvement.
- **Thin-Film Nanocomposite (TFN):** nanoparticles in the PA active layer — **emerging frontier**.
- **Surface modification of polymers** (PVP, PEG, plasma, grafting) is **essential** to make hydrophobic polymers usable in MBR.
- **Ceramic TiO₂** offers **photocatalytic self-cleaning** under UV — emerging anti-fouling strategy.
- **SiC membranes** are used in **high-temperature produced water** (oil & gas industry).
- **Chlorine attacks polyamide** — pretreatment of feedwater is mandatory for RO/NF systems.
- **Material cost vs. TCO (Total Cost of Ownership):** Ceramic costs more initially but lasts 10–20 years vs. polymer 3–7 years.
- **Engineering trade-off triangle:** **Permeability ↔ Selectivity ↔ Stability** — improving one often degrades another.
- **Module configuration affects material choice:** Hollow-fiber (PES/PVDF), Flat-sheet (PES/PSf), Tubular (Ceramic), Spiral-wound (PA-TFC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate and Frame Membrane Module (Plate-and-Frame Configuration)** is one of the earliest and most fundamental geometric arrangements of membrane elements used in pressure-driven membrane separation processes (Microfiltration, Ultrafiltration, Nanofiltration, and Reverse Osmosis). In this configuration, flat-sheet membranes are stacked alternately with spacers or permeate carriers between rigid supporting plates, forming a parallel array of membrane "leaf" elements. The feed water is forced across the membrane surface under pressure, and the permeate (treated water) is collected through a central manifold, while the concentrate (retentate) is discharged.

The Plate and Frame module is essentially the **membrane-process analogue of a plate heat exchanger**, where the role of the heat-transfer surface is replaced by a semi-permeable membrane and the role of the heat carrier is replaced by pressurized feed water. The module derives its name from its mechanical analogy to the **filter press**, a classical unit operation in chemical engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Design and evaluate advanced membrane-based wastewater treatment systems"* and aligns with **Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level: Understand (L2) and Apply (L3)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Press" Mental Model

Imagine you are making a layered sandwich for a large gathering:

- The **bread slices** represent the **rigid support plates** (they provide mechanical strength).
- The **cheese slices** represent the **flat-sheet membranes** (they do the actual "selective filtering").
- The **lettuce or cucumber layers** between them represent the **feed spacer / permeate carrier** (they create flow channels).
- You stack many such sandwiches together, then **press them firmly with a hydraulic jack** (the clamping frame).
- Now you pour a **dirty liquid** (wastewater) into the top of this stacked press.
- The liquid is squeezed through every cheese layer (membrane), and the **clean liquid drips out** through central collection tubes, while the **dirty residue stays behind**.

This is precisely how a Plate and Frame membrane module works. The "press" arrangement provides a **high membrane area per unit volume** while ensuring that every membrane sheet is mechanically supported and operates under uniform hydraulic pressure.

## 1.3 Position of Plate and Frame in the Membrane Module Family

Membrane modules are broadly classified into **four canonical geometries** based on KTU 2024 syllabus:

| S.No. | Module Type | Membrane Geometry | Typical Application |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Plate and Frame** | Flat sheet | MF, UF, ED, small RO plants |
| 2 | **Spiral Wound** | Flat sheet (wound) | RO, NF (most common) |
| 3 | **Tubular** | Tubular | High-fouling industrial feeds |
| 4 | **Hollow Fiber** | Capillary | UF, RO (high packing density) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Plate and Frame is **historically the oldest** configuration (developed alongside the original reverse osmosis work of Loeb and Sourirajan in the late 1950s–early 1960s). It remains in use today primarily for **small-scale installations**, **laboratory separations**, and **electrodialysis (ED)** applications.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (with bold standard values)

- **Packing density (membrane area per module volume):** typically **100 – 400 m²/m³** (relatively low compared to spiral wound ~600–1000 m²/m³).
- **Operating pressure range:** **1 – 70 bar** depending on the membrane type (MF/UF at lower end, RO at higher end).
- **Feed channel height:** typically **0.5 – 5 mm**, defined by the spacer thickness.
- **Cross-flow velocity:** typically **0.5 – 3 m/s**.
- **Membrane sheet dimensions:** standard industrial sizes are typically **0.2 m × 0.2 m** up to **1.0 m × 1.0 m**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Constructional Anatomy of a Plate and Frame Module

The Plate and Frame module is composed of **repeating unit cells**, each consisting of:

1. **Two rigid support plates** — typically fabricated from **stainless steel (SS-304 / SS-316), fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP), or polypropylene**. The plates serve two functions:
   - Mechanical support for the thin (≈ 100–300 µm) membrane sheet.
   - Channels for permeate collection.

2. **Flat-sheet membrane(s)** — laid on top of each support plate. Two membranes (with their feed sides facing outward and permeate sides against the plate) form one "membrane sandwich" with the permeate carrier in between.

3. **Feed-side spacer** — a porous mesh (typically **polypropylene or polyester**, 0.5–5 mm thick) placed between two adjacent membrane sandwiches. This spacer:
   - Promotes **turbulent flow** at the membrane surface (reduces concentration polarization).
   - Acts as the **feed flow channel**.
   - Provides mechanical cushioning between membranes.

4. **Permeate carrier** — a porous, highly permeable layer (felt or grooved plate) placed between the back of the membrane and the support plate. It conveys the permeate to the central permeate collection manifold.

5. **End plates and hydraulic press / tie rods** — the entire stack is clamped together using either **bolted tie rods** or **hydraulic rams** to provide the sealing pressure (typically **5–50 kN/m²** gasket load).

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four-Step Operating Cycle of a Plate-and-Frame Module:**

- **Step 1 — Pressurized Feed Introduction:** Raw wastewater enters the module through an inlet manifold and is distributed uniformly across the feed-side spacers of every membrane cell via inlet/outlet distribution holes machined into the plates.

- **Step 2 — Cross-flow Filtration:** Feed flows **tangentially** along the membrane surface. Under the applied transmembrane pressure (TMP), solvent (water) and species smaller than the membrane pore size permeate through the membrane, while larger species (suspended solids, colloids, macromolecules, ions above the rejection limit) are retained.

- **Step 3 — Permeate Collection:** The permeate enters the permeate carrier, flows radially inward (or along grooved channels), and is gathered in the **central permeate manifold** that runs through the centre of every plate.

- **Step 4 — Concentrate Discharge:** The feed stream, now progressively concentrated in rejected species, exits the module as **retentate/concentrate** through an outlet manifold and is either recycled (for higher recovery) or sent to further treatment/disposal.

## 2.3 Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) — The Core Driving Force

The **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** is the net hydraulic pressure differential that drives permeate through the membrane. For a Plate and Frame module operating in cross-flow mode:

$$
TMP = \frac{P_{feed,in} + P_{feed,out}}{2} - P_{permeate}
$$

Where:

- $P_{feed,in}$ = inlet feed pressure (bar or Pa)
- $P_{feed,out}$ = outlet feed pressure
- $P_{permeate}$ = permeate-side pressure (typically ≈ atmospheric or back-pressured)

**Why "average feed pressure"?** Because the feed loses pressure along the flow path due to friction, the pressure acting on the membrane varies from inlet to outlet. The arithmetic mean is the standard engineering approximation for the membrane-averaged driving force.

## 2.4 Permeate Flux Equation (Darcy's Law for Membranes)

$$
J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m}
$$

Where:

- $J$ = permeate flux (m³/m²·s or LMH)
- $TMP$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane hydraulic resistance (m⁻¹)

For **fouled / polarized** operation, total resistance is the sum of clean membrane resistance plus fouling and cake layer resistance:

$$
R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c + R_{cp}
$$

Where:

- $R_m$ = clean membrane resistance
- $R_f$ = irreversible fouling resistance (pore blocking, adsorption)
- $R_c$ = cake layer resistance (deposited solids)
- $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization boundary layer resistance

Therefore, the **operating flux** becomes:

$$
J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_c + R_{cp})}
$$

## 2.5 Recovery and Rejection — Module-Level Performance Indicators

**Recovery (Y):**

$$
Y(\%) = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100
$$

Where $Q_p$ = permeate flow rate, $Q_f$ = feed flow rate.

**Rejection (R):**

$$
R(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100
$$

Where $C_p$ = permeate concentration, $C_f$ = feed concentration.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary Table for Plate and Frame Module Calculations:**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Range/Value |
|-------|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Transmembrane Pressure | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in}+P_{out}}{2} - P_p$ | Pa or bar | **1 – 70 bar** |
| 2 | Permeate Flux (Clean) | $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_m}$ | m³/(m²·s) or LMH | **10 – 1000 LMH** |
| 3 | Operating Flux (Fouled) | $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_{total}}$ | m³/(m²·s) or LMH | **5 – 200 LMH** |
| 4 | Recovery | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | % | **50 – 95 %** |
| 5 | Rejection | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | % | **90 – 99.9 %** |
| 6 | Membrane Area Required | $A_m = \dfrac{Q_p}{J}$ | m² | **Site-specific** |
| 7 | Cross-flow Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q_f}{N \cdot W \cdot h_{sp}}$ | m/s | **0.5 – 3 m/s** |
| 8 | Pressure Drop (Channel) | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_h} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | **0.1 – 2 bar** |
| 9 | Packing Density | $\rho_A = \dfrac{A_m}{V_{module}}$ | m²/m³ | **100 – 400 m²/m³** |
| 10 | Volumetric Conversion | $1 \; LMH = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s) = 2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ | m/s | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **LMH** stands for **Liters per Square Meter per Hour** — the most commonly used flux unit in membrane engineering. Conversion: **1 LMH = 2.778 × 10⁻⁷ m³/(m²·s) = 2.778 × 10⁻⁴ m/h**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Plate and Frame configuration is the **module of choice** in the following practical scenarios:

- **Electrodialysis (ED) and Electrodialysis Reversal (EDR)** — almost universally uses plate-and-frame geometry because flat ion-exchange membranes must be separated by thin spacer compartments to enable ion transport under an electric field.
- **Small-to-medium scale RO/NF units** for niche applications such as pharmaceutical, dairy, and maple syrup concentration.
- **Pilot plants and laboratory testing** of new membranes — the open architecture allows easy membrane replacement and inspection.
- **High-viscosity feed streams** (e.g., fruit juices, polymer solutions) where the open feed channel of a plate module handles viscous fluids better than narrow-bore hollow fibers.
- **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** configurations historically used plate-and-frame UF modules submerged in the activated sludge tank.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Number of Plates and Membrane Area

**Problem Setup:** A Plate and Frame UF module is built using square support plates of side $L$ (m). Each membrane sandwich consists of two flat-sheet membranes of area $L \times L$, and the plates are stacked $N$ deep.

### Step 1: Express the membrane area per sandwich
Each sandwich contains 2 membrane sheets, each of area $L^2$:

$$
A_{sandwich} = 2 \times L^2
$$

### Step 2: Account for the central permeate tube (unusable membrane area)
A circular region of diameter $d_t$ at the centre of each sheet is occupied by the permeate collection tube and contributes no useful membrane area:

$$
A_{tube} = \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4}
$$

### Step 3: Effective membrane area per sandwich
$$
A_{eff,sandwich} = 2L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4}
$$

### Step 4: Total membrane area for $N$ sandwiches
$$
A_m = N \left( 2L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Packing density
If the total module volume (including end plates and frame) is $V_{mod}$:

$$
\rho_A = \frac{A_m}{V_{mod}} = \frac{N \left( 2L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4} \right)}{V_{mod}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation reveals **why the central permeate tube reduces packing density** — engineers minimize $d_t$ (often 20–50 mm) to maximize the useful membrane area fraction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-Flow Velocity and Reynolds Number in the Feed Channel

**Setup:** A Plate and Frame module has $N$ parallel feed channels, each of width $W$, height $h_{sp}$ (spacer thickness), and length $L_c$. Feed flow rate is $Q_f$.

### Step 1: Total flow cross-sectional area available
$$
A_{cs} = N \times W \times h_{sp}
$$

### Step 2: Average cross-flow velocity
$$
v = \frac{Q_f}{A_{cs}} = \frac{Q_f}{N \cdot W \cdot h_{sp}}
$$

### Step 3: Hydraulic diameter of rectangular channel
For a rectangular channel with $W \gg h_{sp}$ (typical in plate modules):

$$
D_h = \frac{4 \cdot A_{cs,P}}{P_w} = \frac{4 \cdot W \cdot h_{sp}}{2(W + h_{sp})} \approx 2 h_{sp} \quad (\text{when } W \gg h_{sp})
$$

### Step 4: Reynolds number
$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{\rho v \cdot 2 h_{sp}}{\mu}
$$

For spacer-filled channels, $Re > 200 – 400$ typically indicates turbulent flow (with spacer-induced turbulence). Laminar flow ($Re < 200$) is undesirable because it promotes concentration polarization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Spacers in the feed channel of Plate and Frame modules act as **static mixers**, increasing the effective Reynolds number and disrupting the concentration polarization boundary layer. This is why spacer geometry (mesh size, filament angle) is a critical design parameter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Along the Feed Channel

The pressure drop along a feed channel of length $L_c$ is given by the Darcy–Weisbach equation:

### Step 1: General Darcy–Weisbach form
$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L_c}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

### Step 2: Friction factor $f$ (Darcy) for spacer-filled channels
For spacer-filled rectangular channels, an empirical correlation often used (from Schock and Miquel, 1987):

$$
f = A \cdot Re^{B}
$$

Where typical spacer constants are $A \approx 6.23$ and $B \approx -0.3$ for a 31-mil diamond spacer.

### Step 3: Substitution yields the working equation
$$
\Delta P = \frac{A \cdot Re^{B} \cdot L_c}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This pressure drop is **subtracted from the inlet pressure** to find $P_{out}$, which then feeds back into the TMP calculation. In multi-pass systems, $P_{out}$ of one pass becomes $P_{in}$ of the next.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Membrane Area Required for a Given Plant Capacity

**Problem:** Design a Plate and Frame RO plant to treat $Q_{pump}$ (m³/h) of brackish feed to produce $Q_p$ (m³/h) of permeate. The membrane flux under design TMP is $J$ (LMH). Membrane sheet dimensions are $L \times L$. How many sheets are needed?

### Step 1: Convert flux to SI units
$$
J_{SI} = \frac{J_{LMH} \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

### Step 2: Total membrane area required
$$
A_m = \frac{Q_p}{J}
$$

### Step 3: Effective area per sheet (after subtracting permeate tube area)
$$
A_{sheet} = L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4}
$$

### Step 4: Number of sheets
$$
N_{sheet} = \frac{A_m}{A_{sheet}} = \frac{Q_p}{J \left( L^2 - \dfrac{\pi d_t^2}{4} \right)}
$$

### Step 5: Number of sandwiches (each = 2 sheets)
$$
N_{sandwich} = \frac{N_{sheet}}{2}
$$

### Step 6: Number of modules (if each module holds $n$ sandwiches)
$$
N_{mod} = \frac{N_{sandwich}}{n}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plate and Frame vs. Other Modules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table — Often Asked in KTU 14-mark Questions:**

| Parameter | Plate and Frame | Spiral Wound | Tubular | Hollow Fiber |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|---------|--------------|
| Packing Density (m²/m³) | **100 – 400** | **600 – 1000** | **< 100** | **5000 – 15000** |
| Membrane Replacement | **Easy** (open) | Replace whole element | Easy | Replace whole element |
| Capital Cost | **High** | **Low** | **Very High** | **Low** |
| Fouling Tolerance | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | Poor |
| Suspended Solids Tolerance | Moderate | Poor (feed spacer) | **Excellent** | Very Poor |
| Operating Pressure (bar) | 1 – 70 | 5 – 80 | 1 – 10 | 1 – 30 |
| Typical Applications | ED, MBR, lab | RO, NF industrial | Industrial wastewater | UF, RO water reuse |
| Energy Consumption | Moderate-High | **Low** | High | Low-Moderate |
| Suitability for Viscous Feeds | **Good** | Moderate | **Excellent** | Poor |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark Style)

**Question:** A Plate and Frame UF module treats dairy wastewater. Each module has **40 sandwiches**, with membrane sheets of size **0.4 m × 0.4 m**. The permeate collection tube diameter is **30 mm**. The clean water flux at TMP = 2 bar is **120 LMH**, and after 30 days of operation, the flux declines to **75 LMH** due to fouling.

**Calculate:**
- (a) The total membrane area in one module.
- (b) The intrinsic membrane resistance $R_m$ at clean water conditions (assuming $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 25°C).
- (c) The total operating resistance after fouling.
- (d) Comment on the nature of the fouling (reversible vs. irreversible) if simple backwash restores flux to 100 LMH.

### Solution:

#### Part (a): Total Membrane Area

Effective area per sheet:

$$
A_{sheet} = L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4} = (0.4)^2 - \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{sheet} = 0.16 - \frac{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 0.16 - 7.069 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
A_{sheet} \approx 0.1593 \; m^2
$$

Number of sheets in 40 sandwiches = $40 \times 2 = 80$ sheets.

Total membrane area:

$$
A_m = 80 \times 0.1593 \approx 12.74 \; m^2
$$

#### Part (b): Intrinsic Membrane Resistance

Convert flux to SI units:

$$
J = 120 \; LMH = \frac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

Using Darcy's law:

$$
R_m = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
R_m = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{3.333 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

#### Part (c): Total Operating Resistance

Convert fouled flux:

$$
J_{fouled} = 75 \; LMH = \frac{75 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 2.083 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

Assuming TMP unchanged at 2 bar:

$$
R_{total} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.083 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
R_{total} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2.083 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 9.6 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

#### Part (d): Fouling Nature

Fouling resistance after 30 days:

$$
R_f = R_{total} - R_m = 9.6 \times 10^{12} - 6.0 \times 10^{12} = 3.6 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

Resistance after backwash (J = 100 LMH):

$$
J_{bw} = 100 \; LMH = 2.778 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

$$
R_{after,bw} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.778 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

Irreversible fouling resistance:

$$
R_{irrev} = R_{after,bw} - R_m = 7.2 \times 10^{12} - 6.0 \times 10^{12} = 1.2 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

Reversible (cake) fouling resistance:

$$
R_{rev} = R_{total} - R_{after,bw} = 9.6 \times 10^{12} - 7.2 \times 10^{12} = 2.4 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Since backwash restored flux from 75 → 100 LMH, the majority of the fouling (≈ 67%, $R_{rev}/R_f$) was **reversible cake-layer fouling**. The remaining 33% is **irreversible fouling** due to pore plugging and adsorption. This indicates that the fouling is **mostly removable by physical cleaning** (good) but a permanent loss of membrane performance has occurred.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Plate and Frame membrane module. List any four advantages of this configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Plate and Frame membrane module is a membrane filtration device in which flat-sheet membranes are stacked alternately with feed spacers and permeate carriers between rigid support plates, forming a parallel array of membrane cells. Feed flows tangentially across the membrane surface under pressure; permeate is collected through a central manifold while concentrate exits separately.

**Four Advantages:**
- (i) **Easy membrane replacement** — individual sheets can be accessed, removed, and replaced without discarding the entire module.
- (ii) **Tolerant to high-viscosity feeds** — open feed channel geometry handles viscous fluids better than spiral or hollow fiber.
- (iii) **Uniform flow distribution** — feed distribution manifold ensures equal flow across all membrane sheets.
- (iv) **Mechanical robustness** — rigid plate backing prevents membrane rupture at high TMP.
- (v) **Easy cleaning** — open architecture permits in-situ chemical cleaning and visual inspection.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare Plate and Frame module with Spiral Wound module with respect to (i) packing density, (ii) membrane replacement, and (iii) susceptibility to fouling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Plate and Frame | Spiral Wound |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Packing Density | Low (100–400 m²/m³) | High (600–1000 m²/m³) |
| Membrane Replacement | Easy (individual sheets) | Replace entire element |
| Fouling Susceptibility | Moderate | Moderate (but spacer clogging in narrow channels is common) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Draw a neat labelled schematic of a Plate and Frame membrane module and explain its **construction and working principle** with the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate and Frame UF module has **25 sandwiches**, each sandwich using **0.5 m × 0.5 m** square membrane sheets with a central permeate tube of **40 mm diameter**. Calculate: **(i)** Total membrane area in the module, **(ii)** Packing density if the overall module volume is **0.04 m³**, and **(iii)** Number of such modules required to treat **50 m³/day** of wastewater at a design flux of **80 LMH**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Schematic:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Feed Inlet"] --> B["Inlet Manifold with Distribution Holes"]
    B --> C["Stacked Membrane Sandwiches"]
    C --> D["Outlet Manifold"]
    D --> E["Concentrate Outlet"]
    
    F["Central Permeate Collection Tube"] -.-> C
    F --> G["Permeate Outlet"]
    
    H["Hydraulic Clamp / Tie Rods"] -.-> C
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513
    style B fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style D fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
```

**Construction Components (3 marks):**

- **Support Plates:** Rigid plates (stainless steel or FRP) that provide mechanical strength to thin membranes.
- **Flat-Sheet Membranes:** Active separation layer (e.g., PES, PVDF), typically 100–300 µm thick.
- **Feed Spacers:** Porous mesh between adjacent membrane sheets — creates flow channel and induces turbulence.
- **Permeate Carriers:** Porous felt or grooved plate behind each membrane — conveys permeate to central tube.
- **Central Permeate Tube:** Collects permeate from all sheets.
- **End Plates & Tie Rods / Hydraulic Press:** Clamp the entire stack with sufficient force for leak-tight sealing.
- **Gaskets:** EPDM or Viton seals prevent bypass of feed to permeate.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Wastewater enters through the feed manifold and is distributed into all feed-side spacer channels in parallel.
- **Step 2:** Feed flows **tangentially** along the membrane surface under applied transmembrane pressure (TMP).
- **Step 3:** Permeate (water + species below cut-off) passes through the membrane, enters the permeate carrier, and is conveyed to the central tube.
- **Step 4:** Rejected species remain in the feed stream and exit as **concentrate/retentate** through the outlet manifold.
- **Step 5:** The whole stack is held under compression by tie rods or hydraulic ram to maintain gasket integrity.

**Process Optimization Note:** Operating at appropriate cross-flow velocity (0.5–3 m/s) minimizes concentration polarization.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Number of sandwiches, $N = 25$
- Sheet dimensions: $L \times L = 0.5 \times 0.5$ m
- Permeate tube diameter: $d_t = 40 \; mm = 0.040 \; m$
- Module volume: $V_{mod} = 0.04 \; m^3$
- Daily wastewater: $Q_{daily} = 50 \; m^3/day$
- Design flux: $J = 80 \; LMH$

**(i) Total Membrane Area (3 marks):**

Effective area per sheet (after subtracting central tube area):

$$
A_{sheet} = L^2 - \frac{\pi d_t^2}{4} = (0.5)^2 - \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{sheet} = 0.25 - \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 0.25 - 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
A_{sheet} \approx 0.2487 \; m^2 \text{ per sheet}
$$

Number of sheets = $2 \times 25 = 50$ sheets.

Total membrane area:

$$
A_m = 50 \times 0.2487 = 12.44 \; m^2
$$

**(ii) Packing Density (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_A = \frac{A_m}{V_{mod}} = \frac{12.44}{0.04} = 311 \; m^2/m^3
$$

This falls within the expected Plate and Frame range (100–400 m²/m³). ✓

**(iii) Number of Modules Required (2 marks):**

Convert flux to m³/day per m²:

$$
J_{daily} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3} \times 24}{1} = 1.92 \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot day)
$$

Membrane area required for 50 m³/day:

$$
A_{required} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{J_{daily}} = \frac{50}{1.92} \approx 26.04 \; m^2
$$

Number of modules:

$$
N_{mod} = \frac{A_{required}}{A_m} = \frac{26.04}{12.44} \approx 2.09
$$

Since partial modules are not possible, **round up to 3 modules** (with design safety margin).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Causing Mark Deductions:**
> - **Forgetting to subtract the central permeate tube area** in $A_{sheet}$ → loses 1 mark.
> - **Mixing up sandwiches vs. sheets** (1 sandwich = 2 sheets) → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Forgetting to convert LMH to m³/(m²·day)** before dividing flow rate → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Not rounding up partial module counts** → loses 0.5 mark (engineering judgment question).
> - **Using $V_{mod}$ as the spacer-channel volume** instead of total module volume → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Wrong SI conversion of tube diameter** (mm vs. m) → loses 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a comparative table, **discuss the relative merits and demerits** of Plate and Frame, Spiral Wound, Tubular, and Hollow Fiber membrane modules. Discuss why Plate and Frame remains the preferred choice for **Electrodialysis (ED)** applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate and Frame RO module operates at a feed inlet pressure of **30 bar**, feed outlet pressure of **28 bar**, and permeate pressure of **1 bar**. The intrinsic membrane resistance is $R_m = 2 \times 10^{13} \; m^{-1}$, viscosity is $0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and after one week of operation, the flux is **80%** of the clean water flux at the same TMP.
