Calculate: **(i)** The clean water permeate flux in LMH, **(ii)** The total operating resistance after one week, **(iii)** The fouling resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Discussion (7 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Plate and Frame | Spiral Wound | Tubular | Hollow Fiber |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|---------|--------------|
| Packing Density (m²/m³) | 100–400 | 600–1000 | <100 | 5000–15000 |
| Cost per m² | High | Low | Very High | Low |
| Membrane Replacement | Easy (individual sheets) | Replace whole element | Easy | Replace whole element |
| Fouling Tolerance | Moderate | Moderate (narrow channels) | Excellent (large bore) | Poor (narrow lumen) |
| Suspended Solids Tolerance | Good | Poor | Excellent | Very Poor |
| Operating Pressure | 1–70 bar | 5–80 bar | 1–10 bar | 1–30 bar |
| Typical Energy Use | Moderate | Low | High | Low–Moderate |
| Best Application | ED, MBR, Lab RO | Industrial RO/NF | Industrial wastewater | Water reuse, UF |

**Merits of Plate and Frame (1 mark):**
- Easy membrane access, replacement, and cleaning.
- Mechanically robust — supports high TMP.
- Tolerant of high-viscosity and high-solids feed.
- Uniform flow distribution via distribution manifold.

**Demerits (1 mark):**
- Low packing density → larger footprint.
- High capital cost per unit membrane area.
- Labour-intensive assembly and maintenance.

**Why Plate and Frame for Electrodialysis (1 mark):**
- ED requires **flat ion-exchange membranes** separated by thin spacer compartments to enable ion transport under electric field.
- Plate and Frame geometry allows **precise control of compartment thickness (typically 0.5–2 mm)**, which is critical for minimizing electrical resistance.
- Open architecture permits **easy removal of scale deposits** from membrane surfaces (a major ED operational issue).
- Stack design allows **many cell pairs in series** (hundreds of membrane sheets) to achieve required demineralization in a single pass.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $P_{in} = 30 \; bar = 30 \times 10^5 \; Pa$
- $P_{out} = 28 \; bar = 28 \times 10^5 \; Pa$
- $P_p = 1 \; bar = 1 \times 10^5 \; Pa$
- $R_m = 2 \times 10^{13} \; m^{-1}$
- $\mu = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$
- After 1 week: $J_{fouled} = 0.80 \times J_{clean}$

**(i) Clean Water Permeate Flux (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Calculate TMP:

$$
TMP = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p = \frac{30 + 28}{2} - 1
$$

$$
TMP = 29 - 1 = 28 \; bar = 28 \times 10^5 \; Pa
$$

Step 2 — Apply Darcy's law:

$$
J_{clean} = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m} = \frac{28 \times 10^5}{0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{13}}
$$

$$
J_{clean} = \frac{28 \times 10^5}{1.8 \times 10^{10}} = 1.556 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

Step 3 — Convert to LMH:

$$
J_{clean,LMH} = \frac{1.556 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000}{1} = 560 \; LMH
$$

**(ii) Total Operating Resistance after One Week (2 marks):**

Fouled flux:

$$
J_{fouled} = 0.80 \times 560 = 448 \; LMH = \frac{448 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 1.244 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/(m^2 \cdot s)
$$

Total resistance:

$$
R_{total} = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot J_{fouled}} = \frac{28 \times 10^5}{0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.244 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
R_{total} = \frac{28 \times 10^5}{1.120 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.5 \times 10^{13} \; m^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Fouling Resistance (2 marks):**

$$
R_{total} = R_m + R_f \Rightarrow R_f = R_{total} - R_m
$$

$$
R_f = 2.5 \times 10^{13} - 2.0 \times 10^{13} = 0.5 \times 10^{13} = 5.0 \times 10^{12} \; m^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation:** The fouling resistance is 25% of the clean membrane resistance, indicating **moderate fouling** has occurred in one week. For RO systems, this level typically warrants chemical cleaning (CIP) to restore performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Causing Mark Deductions:**
> - **Not averaging feed pressures** in TMP calculation (using only $P_{in}$) → loses 1 mark.
> - **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa** → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Incorrect LMH conversion** (factor of 1000 missing) → loses 1 mark.
> - **Subtracting $R_m$ from $R_{total}$ in wrong order** → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Not stating the engineering interpretation** of the fouling level → loses 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate and Frame Membrane Module:**

**Core Concept:**
- Plate and Frame is the **oldest** membrane module geometry, structurally analogous to a **plate heat exchanger** or **filter press**.
- Flat-sheet membranes are stacked between rigid support plates separated by feed spacers.
- Feed flows **tangentially** (cross-flow); permeate is collected via a **central manifold tube**.

**Construction Components (must remember all 7):**
- ✓ Support plates (mechanical strength)
- ✓ Flat-sheet membranes (selective layer)
- ✓ Feed spacer (turbulence promoter)
- ✓ Permeate carrier (collection pathway)
- ✓ Central permeate tube
- ✓ End plates and tie rods / hydraulic press
- ✓ Gaskets (sealing)

**Key Operating Parameters:**
- **TMP** = $\dfrac{P_{in}+P_{out}}{2} - P_p$
- **Flux** = $\dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_{total}}$ where $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c + R_{cp}$
- **Recovery** = $\dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$
- **Rejection** = $\left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$
- **Packing Density:** 100–400 m²/m³ (lowest among major modules except tubular)

**Comparative Facts (commonly asked):**
- ✓ Plate and Frame is **preferred for ED** due to flat ion-exchange membrane geometry.
- ✓ Spiral Wound has **highest packing density among flat-sheet** configurations.
- ✓ Tubular has **best fouling tolerance** but very low packing density.
- ✓ Hollow Fiber has **highest packing density overall** (5000–15000 m²/m³).
- ✓ Plate and Frame offers **easiest membrane replacement**.

**Numerical Must-Knows:**
- 1 LMH = $2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/(m²·s)
- 1 sandwich = 2 membrane sheets
- Effective area per sheet = $L^2 - \dfrac{\pi d_t^2}{4}$
- Packing density = $\dfrac{A_m}{V_{mod}}$
- Flux and TMP are linearly related (Darcy's law for clean membrane).

**Engineering Judgment Points:**
- Operating at higher cross-flow velocity reduces concentration polarization but increases pumping cost.
- Plate and Frame modules are favoured when **feed variability is high** and **membrane replacement frequency** is significant.
- For high-solids or viscous industrial wastewater, Plate and Frame or Tubular are preferred over Spiral Wound or Hollow Fiber.
- **Fouling reversibility** determines cleaning strategy: reversible (backwash) vs. irreversible (CIP with chemicals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks to **"compare"** Plate and Frame with another module, always structure your answer using a **comparison table** followed by a **short prose paragraph** explaining the practical implication. This format scores consistently higher than narrative-only or table-only answers. Always close with an **engineering recommendation** or **application-specific choice** to demonstrate applied understanding — this is heavily rewarded in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tubular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tubular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment**, the term **Tubular** refers to **Tubular Membrane Modules** — a specific geometric configuration of pressure-driven membrane filtration systems in which the selective membrane layer is cast on the **inner surface of a rigid cylindrical tube (or a bundle of tubes)** housed inside a pressure vessel. The feed wastewater is pumped axially through the bore of the tube at high velocity, while the purified permeate (water + dissolved solutes smaller than the membrane cut-off) permeates radially outward through the membrane wall and is collected in the shell-side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tubular membrane modules** are one of the four principal membrane module geometries, alongside **Hollow Fiber**, **Spiral Wound**, and **Plate & Frame**. They are the **most robust** geometry and are specifically preferred for feed streams containing **high Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**, **oils & greases**, or **viscous industrial effluents**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Slide Tube Filter":**
> Imagine a long, hollow, water-park slide tube made of a special fabric that only lets water droplets pass through its walls, but blocks sand, hair, and dirt. When dirty water rushes through the inside of this tube under high pressure, clean water "sweats" out through the tube walls and is collected in a tray underneath, while the concentrated sludge keeps racing forward. The constant high-speed flow inside scours the tube wall, preventing dirt from accumulating. **That is exactly how a Tubular Membrane works.**

### 1.3 Physical & Operational Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Tube inner diameter | **10 – 25** | mm |
| Tube length | **1.0 – 6.0** | m |
| Number of tubes per module | **4 – 200** | tubes |
| Cross-flow velocity | **1 – 6** | m/s |
| Operating transmembrane pressure | **0.5 – 10** | bar |
| Membrane material | **Ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂)** or **Polymeric (PES, PVDF)** | — |
| MWCO / pore size | **1 kDa – 0.2 µm** | Dalton / µm |
| Module recovery (typical) | **70 – 95** | % |
| pH tolerance (ceramic) | **0 – 14** | — |

### 1.4 Salient Features

- **Open channel geometry** → no plugging by fibrous or particulate matter.
- **Mechanical cleaning possible** → sponge balls, brushes, or high-pressure water jets can be physically passed through the lumen.
- **High cross-flow velocity** → suppresses concentration polarization and cake formation.
- **High energy footprint** → larger feed pumps required to sustain turbulent cross-flow.
- **Low packing density** → large footprint per unit membrane area compared to hollow fiber modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Feed Introduction:** Pre-screened wastewater enters the **bore (inside)** of the tubular membrane through a high-pressure feed pump.
2. **Axial Cross-Flow:** The fluid travels in a **turbulent regime** (Reynolds Number often > 10,000) along the tube length. This tangential shearing action continuously sweeps rejected solids downstream.
3. **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) Driving Force:** A pressure differential across the membrane wall is maintained. Solvent (water) and species smaller than the membrane pore-size are forced **radially outward** through the membrane into the shell side.
4. **Permeate Collection:** Cleaned water is drawn off through the permeate outlet manifold.
5. **Retentate/Concentrate Discharge:** The concentrated reject stream exits at the far end of the tube at elevated pressure (for recycling) or atmospheric pressure (single-pass mode).
6. **Backwash & Cleaning (CIP):** Periodic reversal of flow and chemical cleaning restore flux lost to membrane fouling.

### 2.2 Membrane Module Geometric Comparison (Why Tubular?)

| Module Type | Packing Density (m²/m³) | TSS Tolerance | Cleanability | Capital Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tubular | **Low (~100–300)** | **Very High (>1000 mg/L)** | **Excellent** | High |
| Hollow Fiber | Very High (~1000–10000) | Low | Poor | Low |
| Spiral Wound | High (~600–1000) | Low–Medium | Moderate | Medium |
| Plate & Frame | Low–Medium | High | Good | High |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **core analytical toolkit** for any numerical on Tubular membrane design in the KTU ESE.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Variable Meaning | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{Q_{p}}{A_{m}}$ | Membrane Flux | m³/m²·s or LMH | Permeate productivity |
| 2 | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{p}$ | Transmembrane Pressure | Pa or bar | Driving force |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_{tot}}$ | Darcy's Resistance Model | m³/m²·s | Flux–Resistance linkage |
| 4 | $R_{tot} = R_{m} + R_{cp} + R_{f}$ | Resistance-in-Series | m⁻¹ | Fouling quantification |
| 5 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}}$ | Recovery / Yield | % | Mass-balance recovery |
| 7 | $R_{obs} = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}\right) \times 100$ | Observed Rejection | % | Separation efficiency |
| 8 | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{C_{w}}{C_{b}}$ | Concentration Polarization factor | — | Wall vs bulk solute |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{4 Q_{f}}{\pi \, d^{2} \, N_{t}}$ | Cross-flow velocity in tubes | m/s | Tubular hydraulics |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{f} = \dfrac{32 \, \rho \, v \, L \, f}{d^{2}}$ | Friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa | Pumping energy |

**Notation:** $Q_{p}$ = permeate flow, $A_{m}$ = membrane area, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $R_{m}$ = intrinsic membrane resistance, $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance, $R_{f}$ = fouling resistance, $d$ = tube inner diameter, $N_{t}$ = number of parallel tubes.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Tubular membrane modules are deployed as the **workhorse** of:

- **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** treating high-strength industrial effluents (textile, tannery, food & dairy, oily wastewater).
- **Pretreatment to Reverse Osmosis** in water reclamation plants to reduce silt density index (SDI).
- **Landfill leachate treatment** — extremely high TDS and refractory organics.
- **Produced-water treatment** in oil & gas upstream operations.

The ability to **mechanically clean** and tolerate feed with **>1% suspended solids** is the decisive advantage over hollow-fiber geometries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cross-Flow Velocity in a Tubular Module

We want an explicit expression for the cross-flow velocity **v** inside the tubes given the feed volumetric flow rate $Q_f$, the tube inner diameter $d$, and the number of parallel tubes $N_t$.

**Step 1 — Continuity principle:**
The total feed flow must be split equally among $N_t$ parallel tubes.

$$
q_{tube} = \dfrac{Q_{f}}{N_{t}}
$$

*where $q_{tube}$ is the volumetric flow rate per tube (m³/s).*

**Step 2 — Area of a single tube cross-section:**

$$
A_{tube} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity = Flow / Area:**
Divide the per-tube flow by the per-tube area to obtain the axial velocity.

$$
v = \dfrac{q_{tube}}{A_{tube}} = \dfrac{Q_{f} \, / \, N_{t}}{\pi \, d^{2} \, / \, 4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = \dfrac{4 \, Q_{f}}{\pi \, d^{2} \, N_{t}}\,}
$$

> This is the **fundamental tubular hydraulic equation** — examiners expect students to derive this without skipping steps.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds Number for Tubular Flow

The Reynolds number determines whether the cross-flow is laminar (Re < 2300) or turbulent (Re > 4000). Substituting the velocity from above:

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d}{\mu}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, d}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{4 \, Q_{f}}{\pi \, d^{2} \, N_{t}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \, \rho \, Q_{f}}{\pi \, \mu \, d \, N_{t}}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement:**
A tubular UF module contains **$N_t$ = 40** parallel tubes, each of inner diameter **d = 12.5 mm**. The feed flow rate is **$Q_f$ = 8 m³/h** of secondary-treated wastewater at **25 °C** ($\rho$ = 997 kg/m³, $\mu$ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s). 

Determine: (a) the cross-flow velocity, (b) the Reynolds number, and (c) state the flow regime.

**Given Data:**

$$
Q_f = 8 \; \tfrac{m^3}{h} = \dfrac{8}{3600} = 2.222 \times 10^{-3} \; \tfrac{m^3}{s}
$$

$$
d = 12.5 \; mm = 0.0125 \; m
$$

$$
N_t = 40,\; \rho = 997 \; \tfrac{kg}{m^3},\; \mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s
$$

**Step (a) — Velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{4 \, Q_f}{\pi \, d^2 \, N_t} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.222 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.0125)^2 \times 40}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{8.888 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.4527 \; \tfrac{m}{s}
$$

**Result:** $v \approx 0.453$ m/s — well within the **1–6 m/s** design window.

**Step (b) — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, d}{\mu} = \dfrac{997 \times 0.4527 \times 0.0125}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{5.642}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 6340
$$

**Result:** Re ≈ 6,340.

**Step (c) — Flow Regime:**

Since **Re > 4000**, the flow is **fully turbulent**, providing excellent wall shear and self-cleaning of the membrane surface — a key design justification for tubular geometry in this application.

---

### 3.4 Resistance-in-Series Fouling Balance

For a tubular module operated for time *t*, total resistance is decomposed:

$$
R_{tot}(t) = R_{m} + R_{cp}(t) + R_{f}(t)
$$

Where:
- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (constant, determined by clean-water test)
- $R_{cp}(t)$ = concentration-polarization resistance (reversible, removable by hydraulic flushing)
- $R_f(t)$ = fouling resistance (irreversible, removed only by chemical CIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE answers, **always decompose $R_{tot}$** into these three terms explicitly. Examiners penalize vague single-resistance answers.

**Instantaneous flux:**

$$
J(t) = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, [R_m + R_{cp}(t) + R_{f}(t)]}
$$

As $R_{cp}$ and $R_f$ grow with time, **$J$ decays** → this justifies periodic backwash and CIP cycles in plant operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tubular Membrane Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Wastewater Influent] --> B[Pre-Screening 1 mm]
    B --> C[Feed Surge Tank]
    C --> D[High-Pressure Feed Pump]
    D --> E[Tubular Membrane Module]
    E --> F[Permeate Collection Tank]
    F --> G[Disinfection / Polishing]
    G --> H[Reuse or Discharge]
    E --> I[Retentate Back-Pressure Valve]
    I --> J[Concentrate Recycle Loop]
    J --> C
    E --> K[CIP Cleaning Skid]
    K --> E
```

**Caption:** The flow architecture shows feed entering the tubular bundle, permeate being drawn off radially, retentate either recycled to the feed tank for higher recovery or discharged, and a CIP (Clean-In-Place) skid periodically isolated for chemical cleaning.

---

### 4.2 Process Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEED[Feed Stream]
        F1[Pre-treated Effluent]
        F2[Quality Check: TSS, Turbidity, SDI]
    end

    subgraph TUB[Tubular Module Internals]
        T1[Tube Bundle 10-25 mm Diameter]
        T2[Cross-Flow Channel inside Tube]
        T3[Membrane Layer on Inner Wall]
        T4[Support Housing Outer Shell]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Streams]
        O1[Permeate: Clean Water]
        O2[Retentate: Concentrate Sludge]
    end

    F1 --> F2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T3 --> O1
    T2 --> O2
```

**Caption:** Internal hierarchy of a single tubular membrane element — feed enters the tube, permeate passes radially outward through the membrane layer into the shell, retentate continues axially along the tube.

---

### 4.3 Membrane Fouling vs Time Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t = 0 hours] --> B[Clean Membrane: R equals Rm only]
    B --> C[t = 1 hour: Concentration Polarization forms]
    C --> D[t = 10 hours: Cake Layer builds Rcp grows]
    D --> E[t = 50 hours: Fouling Rf develops]
    E --> F[Critical Flux Threshold reached]
    F --> G[Trigger CIP Cycle]
    G --> H[Chemical Clean: Rm restored]
```

**Caption:** Operational time-line of fouling build-up in a tubular module showing when chemical intervention is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a tubular membrane module and list any two feed streams for which it is the preferred configuration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A tubular membrane module is a pressure-driven filtration device in which the membrane is cast on the inner surface of a cylindrical tube (typically 10–25 mm bore). Feed flows axially through the tube lumen under turbulent cross-flow, permeate passes radially outward, and retentate exits at the tube end.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — operating principle, 1 mark — any two feed streams.)*

**Two preferred feed streams:**
1. High-strength industrial effluent with TSS > 500 mg/L (e.g., tannery, textile wastewater).
2. Oily wastewater / produced water from oil & gas operations.
3. Landfill leachate with high refractory organics and TDS.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between cross-flow and dead-end filtration as applicable to tubular membrane operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Dead-End | Cross-Flow (Tubular) |
|---|---|---|
| Feed flow direction | Perpendicular to membrane | Tangential to membrane |
| Cake build-up | Continuous and rapid | Continuously swept away |
| Flux stability | Decays sharply | Stable at steady state |
| Suited for | Low-solids feeds | High-solids feeds |

*(1 mark — feed flow concept, 1 mark — cake behaviour, 1 mark — comparison table or summary.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a tubular membrane module. List four feed water characteristics that favour selection of tubular geometry over hollow-fibre or spiral-wound modules. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A tubular UF plant treats **5 ML/day** of secondary effluent using a module of **$N_t$ = 80 tubes**, each tube **6.0 m long** with **inner diameter d = 19 mm**. The average transmembrane pressure is **TMP = 1.8 bar**, water viscosity at 25 °C is **$\mu$ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, and the measured clean-membrane resistance is **$R_m$ = 1.2 × 10¹² m⁻¹**. Compute:
(i) the total membrane surface area, (ii) the clean-water flux at the start of operation, and (iii) the daily permeate volume. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- A tubular module consists of a **pressure vessel (shell)** containing a bundle of **4–200 parallel cylindrical tubes**.
- Each tube is composed of a **porous support layer** (typically ceramic or sintered polymer) with a thin selective membrane coated on the inner surface.
- Feed water enters via a high-pressure pump into the **inlet header**, distributes among the tubes, and flows axially.
- Permeate passes radially through the membrane wall into the shell-side manifold.
- Retentate exits through the outlet header, partially recycled, partially discharged.
- A back-pressure valve controls the operating pressure.

**Four characteristics favouring tubular geometry:**
1. **High Total Suspended Solids (TSS > 500 mg/L)** — open channels prevent plugging.
2. **Viscous or oily feed streams** — high cross-flow velocity maintains turbulence.
3. **Aggressive chemical cleaning required** — ceramic tubular membranes tolerate pH 0–14 and oxidizers.
4. **Frequent mechanical cleaning needed** — sponge balls and brushes can be passed through the lumen.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$Q_f$ = 5 × 10⁶ L/day = 5.0 × 10³ m³/day, $N_t$ = 80, $L$ = 6.0 m, $d$ = 0.019 m, TMP = 1.8 × 10⁵ Pa, $\mu$ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, $R_m$ = 1.2 × 10¹² m⁻¹.

**(i) Total membrane area:**

$$
A_m = \pi \, d \, L \, N_t = \pi \times 0.019 \times 6.0 \times 80
$$

$$
A_m = 28.65 \; m^2 \quad (\text{approx.})
$$

**(ii) Clean-water flux:**

$$
J_0 = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_m} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^5}{0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{12}}
$$

$$
J_0 = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^5}{1.068 \times 10^{9}} = 1.686 \times 10^{-4} \; \tfrac{m^3}{m^2 \cdot s}
$$

Converting to LMH:

$$
J_0 = 1.686 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 607 \; \tfrac{L}{m^2 \cdot h} \;(\text{LMH})
$$

**(iii) Daily permeate volume (clean case):**

$$
Q_{p} = J_0 \times A_m \times 24 \times 3600 = 1.686 \times 10^{-4} \times 28.65 \times 86400
$$

$$
Q_p = 417.3 \; m^3/day
$$

*(In practice, due to fouling, $J_{actual} \approx 0.5 J_0$, so the real output is ~200 m³/day, far below the 5000 m³/day feed. Multiple modules in parallel are required for full-scale operation.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for cross-flow velocity in a tubular membrane module. *(3 marks)*
**(b)** Define the **Resistance-in-Series** model. Explain how **membrane fouling** alters the flux and discuss two control strategies. *(7 marks)*
**(c)** Compute the Reynolds number for a tubular module with $N_t$ = 60 tubes, $d$ = 15 mm, $Q_f$ = 12 m³/h, operating at 25 °C ($\rho$ = 997 kg/m³, $\mu$ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s). Comment on the flow regime. *(4 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a)** Already derived in Section 3.1 — the answer is $v = \dfrac{4 Q_f}{\pi \, d^2 \, N_t}$. *(3 marks)*

**(b) Resistance-in-Series Model (7 marks):**

The total resistance to permeate flow through a fouled membrane is the sum of three resistances acting **in series**:

$$
R_{tot} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f
$$

- **$R_m$** — intrinsic clean membrane resistance; determined by membrane pore-size, porosity and thickness.
- **$R_{cp}$** — concentration polarization layer resistance; formed by solute accumulation at the membrane wall; **reversible** by hydraulic flushing.
- **$R_f$** — fouling resistance from irreversible adsorption, pore-blocking, biofilm; **only removable by chemical CIP**.

As $R_{tot}$ increases, the clean-water flux given by $J = \text{TMP} / (\mu R_{tot})$ **decreases monotonically with operating time**.

**Two control strategies:**
1. **Periodic backwashing** — reverse flow every 15–60 min to dislodge the concentration polarization layer.
2. **Chemical Clean-In-Place (CIP)** — alkaline + acidic + oxidant (e.g., NaOH + HNO₃ + NaOCl) wash every 1–6 months to remove irreversible fouling.
3. **Air-scouring** — coarse-bubble aeration beneath the tube bundle to enhance shear.
4. **Pre-treatment optimization** — finer screening or coagulation to reduce foulant load.

**(c) Numerical (4 marks):**

Convert: $Q_f = 12 / 3600 = 3.333 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $d = 0.015$ m, $N_t = 60$.

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \, \rho \, Q_f}{\pi \, \mu \, d \, N_t}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \times 997 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.015 \times 60}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \dfrac{13.293}{2.516 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 5283
$$

**Comment:** Since **Re ≈ 5283 > 4000**, the flow regime is **turbulent**, ensuring effective wall shear and low concentration polarization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — examiners commonly deduct 2 marks for mixing m³/h with m³/s or forgetting to convert mm to m. Always show explicit conversion steps.
> 2. **Skipping the resistance decomposition** — writing only $J = \text{TMP}/(\mu R)$ without separating $R_m$, $R_{cp}$, $R_f$ loses 2 marks in fouling questions.
> 3. **Wrong cross-flow velocity formula** — confusing $v = Q_f / (\pi d^2 N_t / 4)$ with $v = 4 Q_f / A_{module}$ leads to incorrect answers.
> 4. **Forgetting to state flow regime** after computing Re — examiners expect the explicit comment "turbulent / laminar / transitional".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Tubular membrane** = membrane on inner wall of a cylindrical tube; feed flows axially, permeate radially outward.
- ✅ **Tube diameter:** 10–25 mm; **Length:** 1–6 m; **Cross-flow velocity:** 1–6 m/s.
- ✅ **Preferred for:** high TSS, oily, viscous, or chemically aggressive feeds.
- ✅ **Key velocity formula:** $v = \dfrac{4 Q_f}{\pi d^2 N_t}$ — must be derived cleanly.
- ✅ **Key Reynolds formula:** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \rho Q_f}{\pi \mu d N_t}$.
- ✅ **Flux law:** $J = \text{TMP} / (\mu R_{tot})$; with $R_{tot} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$.
- ✅ **Flux unit conversion:** 1 m/s = 3.6 × 10⁶ LMH (m³/m²·s → L/m²·h).
- ✅ **Turbulent flow (Re > 4000)** is essential to control fouling in tubular modules.
- ✅ **Cleaning:** backwash (reversible fouling) + CIP (irreversible fouling).
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** low packing density, high energy demand, large footprint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hollow Fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hollow Fibre # 🔬 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hollow Fibre (HF) membranes** are a specific geometric configuration of polymeric (or ceramic) separation membranes in which the selective barrier is formed as an extremely fine, self-supporting tubular filament with an outer diameter typically ranging from **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** and an internal (lumen) diameter of approximately **0.2 mm to 1.0 mm**. Thousands of these capillary-like fibres are bundled together and potted at both ends inside a pressure vessel to form a compact **Hollow Fibre Module**, which serves as the fundamental operating unit in advanced wastewater treatment processes such as **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)**, **Ultrafiltration (UF)**, and **Microfiltration (MF)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Class Note):** > *Hollow fibre membranes are capillary membranes in which the feed water flows either through the lumen (inside-out) or across the outside of the fibres (outside-in). They offer the highest packing density (surface area per unit volume) among all membrane configurations and are extensively used in submerged MBR systems for municipal and industrial wastewater treatment.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview Imagine a **bundle of very thin drinking straws** glued together at both ends, with the open ends facing into two collection headers (one at the top, one at the bottom). Now imagine each straw is made of a special material with **microscopic pores** that act like a coffee filter at nano/micro scale. When dirty wastewater is pushed: - **Through the inside of the straws** (lumen) → clean water seeps out through the fibre walls → solids stay inside (called **Inside-Out Mode**). - **OR across the outside of the straws** → clean water seeps **into the lumen** and is sucked out from the open ends (called **Outside-In Mode** — used in submerged MBRs). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Aquarium Filter:** > Think of a fish tank filter. Water passes through a sponge-like tube that traps fish waste but lets clean water flow. A hollow fibre does the same thing — but at the molecular level — trapping bacteria, suspended solids, and even viruses depending on the pore size, while letting treated water pass through the membrane wall. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU 2024) | Parameter | Standard Range / Value | |---|---| | **Outer Diameter (OD)** | **0.5 – 2.0 mm** | | **Inner Diameter (ID)** | **0.2 – 1.0 mm** | | **Packing Density** | **> 10,000 m²/m³** (highest among all module types) | | **Typical Pore Size (UF)** | **0.01 – 0.1 µm** | | **Typical Pore Size (MF)** | **0.1 – 10 µm** | | **Operating TMP** | **0.1 – 0.5 bar (MBR)** to **1 – 3 bar (UF)** | | **Membrane Materials** | PVDF, PES, PS, PP, PTFE, Polyacrylonitrile | | **Typical Flux (MBR)** | **15 – 25 L/m²·h** | > [!TIP] > **Why "Hollow Fibre" and not "Hollow Tube"?** Because the wall thickness is so thin that the fibre is genuinely flexible and self-supporting like a hair — the term "fibre" is reserved for sub-millimetre geometries with high flexibility. --- # 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Hollow Fibre A single hollow fibre is a **composite tubular structure** with three concentric layers (in advanced designs): 1. **Inner Skin (Selective Layer):** The ultra-thin dense or microporous layer (typically **0.1 – 1 µm thick**) that performs the actual separation. Pore size here defines whether the fibre acts as MF, UF, or NF. 2. **Support Layer (Substructure):** A thicker, more open, sponge-like or finger-like porous polymer matrix that provides **mechanical strength** to withstand the operating transmembrane pressure. 3. **Outer Skin (Optional):** In some integrally-skinned asymmetric membranes, a second skin may exist — but in most HF membranes for MBR/UF, only the inner skin is selective. --- ## 2.2 Operational Modes — Inside-Out vs Outside-In The selection of flow direction is one of the most exam-relevant topics in this module. ### 2.2.1 Inside-Out Mode (Luminal Feed) - Feed is pressurized **into the lumen** of the fibre. - Permeate is collected from the **outside shell** of the module. - **Operates in DEAD-END or CROSS-FLOW** depending on design. - Higher flux per unit area achievable. - **Limitation:** Suspended solids can clog the lumen → high fouling for high-solids wastewater. ### 2.2.2 Outside-In Mode (Shell-Side Feed) — *Standard for MBR* - Fibre is immersed directly in the activated sludge mixed liquor. - A **vacuum (suction)** is applied to the lumen, drawing clean water **inward** through the membrane wall. - Air-scour bubbles are continuously released from below the module to scour the fibre surface. - **Critical for KTU:** This is the configuration used in **submerged MBRs** (e.g., Kubota, Zenon/ZeeWeed type systems). > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** > Outside-in configuration is **preferred for high MLSS (8000 – 12000 mg/L) wastewater** because the larger shell-side cross-sectional area avoids lumen clogging. Air-scouring is essential to control cake layer formation. --- ## 2.3 Hollow Fibre vs Other Membrane Configurations (Comparative Table) | Feature | Hollow Fibre | Tubular | Spiral Wound | Plate & Frame | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Packing Density (m²/m³)** | **> 10,000 (Highest)** | ~300 | ~600 – 1000 | ~200 – 400 | | **Turbulence Tolerance** | Low – Moderate | **High** | Moderate | Moderate | | **Fouling Resistance** | Moderate (with air-scour) | Excellent | Moderate | Good | | **Cleaning Ease** | Backwash possible | **Easiest** | Difficult | Moderate | | **Capital Cost** | **Lowest** | Highest | Low | High | | **Suspended Solids Tolerance** | Moderate (outside-in) | **Highest** | Lowest | Moderate | | **KTU-Standard Application** | **MBR, UF, RO pretreatment** | High-strength industrial | Brackish water RO, NF | RO/NF niche | > [!TIP] > **Why does hollow fibre dominate MBRs?** Because MBRs replace the secondary clarifier of a conventional activated sludge plant — they handle the same MLSS but produce solids-free effluent. Hollow fibre's **high packing density** dramatically reduces the **footprint** of the treatment plant, a key advantage in space-constrained urban KTU-board exam case studies (e.g., Singapore NEWater, Tokyo Sewage). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **direct derivations of Darcy's Law applied to porous membranes** and are examinable in KTU ESE numerical problems. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | Membrane flux = permeate flow per unit membrane area | L/m²·h | | 2 | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}}$ | Darcy's Law for membrane filtration (the **master equation**) | m/s | | 3 | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_f + R_c$ | Resistance-in-series model | m⁻¹ | | 4 | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ | Transmembrane pressure (cross-flow/inside-out) | Pa or bar | | 5 | $P_{perm} \approx 0$ (suction mode) | For outside-in submerged mode, $\text{TMP} \approx \text{vacuum suction}$ | Pa | | 6 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric recovery ratio | % | | 7 | $\text{Rec}\% = \dfrac{V_{permeate}}{V_{feed}} \times 100$ | Mass/volume-based recovery | % | | 8 | $\text{Re}_{fibre} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_i}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number inside the lumen (laminar if < 2100) | dimensionless | | 9 | $\text{Fouling Index (SFI)} = -\dfrac{1}{J_0}\dfrac{dJ}{dt}$ | Slope-based fouling indicator | h⁻¹ | | 10 | $\text{Critical Flux} = J_{crit}$ | Flux below which no fouling occurs (KTU-favorite) | L/m²·h | > **Notation Glossary:** > - $J$ = Permeate flux, $Q_p$ = Permeate flow rate, $A_m$ = Membrane area > - $\text{TMP}$ = Transmembrane pressure, $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of permeate > - $R_m$ = Intrinsic membrane resistance, $R_f$ = Fouling resistance, $R_c$ = Cake layer resistance > - $P_{in}, P_{out}$ = Inlet & outlet pressures on the feed side, $P_{perm}$ = Permeate side pressure > - $d_i$ = Inner diameter of fibre, $v$ = Cross-flow velocity --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Hollow fibre membranes are the **workhorse configuration** of the global MBR market — used in: - **Kubota (Japan)** — Flat sheet MBR (often compared with HF) - **Veolia/ZeeWeed (Canada)** — Reinforced HF submerged MBR - **Mitsubishi (Japan)** — Sterapore HF UF for drinking water - **Singapore PUB NEWater** — HF UF pretreatment before RO for water reuse - **Industrial wastewater reclamation** — Petrochemical, textile, pharmaceutical > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **design thinking**, not just theory. In board exams, when asked to *recommend* a membrane configuration for a given wastewater, justify your answer using packing density, fouling tolerance, and cleanability — not just by quoting definitions. --- # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance-in-Series Model from Darcy's Law > **Starting Premise:** Permeate flow through a porous membrane can be modeled as flow through a packed bed of capillaries (Kozeny-Carman analogy) — the volumetric flow rate is proportional to the pressure drop and inversely proportional to the viscous resistance. ### Step 1 — State Darcy's Law for Porous Media The volumetric flux $J$ (volume per unit area per unit time) through a porous medium is: $$J = \frac{1}{A_m}\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R}$$ where: - $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the porous medium (Pa) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the permeating fluid (Pa·s) - $R$ = hydraulic resistance of the porous medium (m⁻¹) ### Step 2 — Apply the Concept to a Membrane For a membrane, the **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** is the driving force $\Delta P$: $$\Delta P = \text{TMP} = P_{feed} - P_{permeate}$$ Hence the flux equation becomes: $$J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$ where $R_m$ is the **intrinsic (clean) membrane resistance** — a constant property of the new membrane. ### Step 3 — Account for Fouling In actual operation, the flux is reduced because foulants (suspended solids, colloids, organics, biofilm) accumulate on/within the membrane. The total resistance is no longer $R_m$ alone but the **sum of multiple resistances** acting in series: $$R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f + R_p$$ where: - $R_m$ = Clean membrane resistance (constant) - $R_c$ = Cake layer resistance (deposited solids on the surface) - $R_f$ = Fouling resistance (pore plugging, adsorption) - $R_p$ = Pore-blocking resistance (often grouped with $R_f$) For KTU 2024 exam purposes, the simplified 3-term model is used: $$R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Back into Darcy's Law $$\boxed{J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_c + R_f)}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** > - As $R_c$ and $R_f$ grow with time, $J$ decreases at constant TMP (this is the **flux decline** observed in MBRs). > - To restore $J$, the operator either (a) increases TMP (consumes more energy), (b) performs chemical cleaning (removes $R_c, R_f$), or (c) applies air-scouring (controls $R_c$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Transmembrane Pressure in Cross-Flow Inside-Out Mode ### Step 1 — Recognize the Pressure Profile In cross-flow inside-out hollow fibre operation, the feed enters the lumen at pressure $P_{in}$, exits at $P_{out}$, and the permeate is collected outside at $P_{perm}$. The pressure inside the fibre **decreases linearly** with axial position (for laminar flow). ### Step 2 — Define Local TMP At any axial position $x$: $$\text{TMP}(x) = P(x) - P_{perm}$$ ### Step 3 — Average TMP Across the Fibre Length $$\text{TMP}_{avg} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L \text{TMP}(x)\,dx = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Form (KTU-Standard Expression) $$\boxed{\text{TMP} = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}}$$ > **Numerical Insight (for KTU problems):** If $P_{in} = 2.5$ bar, $P_{out} = 2.0$ bar, $P_{perm} = 0.2$ bar: $$\text{TMP} = \frac{2.5 + 2.0}{2} - 0.2 = 2.25 - 0.2 = 2.05 \text{ bar}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Question (Modelled on KTU ESE Pattern):** > A hollow fibre MBR treats domestic wastewater. The membrane area is 200 m², the TMP is maintained at 0.30 bar, the dynamic viscosity of permeate at the operating temperature is $0.0009$ Pa·s, the clean membrane resistance is $R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹, and the cake layer resistance is $R_c = 4.8 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹. Neglect $R_f$ for this calculation. > > **(a)** Calculate the steady-state permeate flux. > **(b)** If the operator wishes to double the flux by chemical cleaning (removing the cake layer), what TMP will be required at the same viscosity? ### Solution (Part a) **Step 1 — Convert TMP to Pascals:** $$\text{TMP} = 0.30 \text{ bar} = 0.30 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute total resistance:** $$R_{tot} = R_m + R_c = 1.2 \times 10^{12} + 4.8 \times 10^{12} = 6.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Darcy's Law:** $$J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^4}{0.0009 \times 6.0 \times 10^{12}}$$ $$J = \frac{3.0 \times 10^4}{5.4 \times 10^9} = 5.56 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to L/m²·h (KTU-preferred unit):** $$J = 5.56 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 20.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$$ $$\boxed{J = 20.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}}$$ ### Solution (Part b) After cleaning, $R_c \to 0$, so $R_{tot} = R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹. To double the flux (i.e., $J_{new} = 2 \times 20.0 = 40.0$ L/m²·h), convert to m/s: $$J_{new} = 40.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h} = 1.111 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$ Solve for the new TMP: $$\text{TMP}_{new} = J_{new} \times \mu \times R_m$$ $$\text{TMP}_{new} = 1.111 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.0009 \times 1.2 \times 10^{12}$$ $$\text{TMP}_{new} = 1.2 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 0.12 \text{ bar}$$ $$\boxed{\text{TMP}_{new} = 0.12 \text{ bar}}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **removing the cake layer allows a 60% reduction in TMP** (from 0.30 bar to 0.12 bar) for the same flux — this is the energy-saving rationale behind **clean-in-place (CIP)** protocols in KTU-board-exam design problems. --- ## 3.4 Reynolds Number for Hollow Fibre Lumen Flow **Step 1 — Define equivalent diameter:** For a circular lumen, $d_e = d_i$ (inner diameter). **Step 2 — Apply Reynolds number formula:** $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_i}{\mu}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute typical MBR values:** $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $d_i = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s. $$\text{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.001} = 250$$ Since $\text{Re} = 250 < 2100$, flow in HF lumen is **laminar** — this is the KTU-expected answer for backwash hydraulics. --- # 🏗️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Hollow Fibre MBR Functional Architecture (Outside-In Mode) ```mermaid flowchart TB InletA["Influent Wastewater
Screening and Grit Removal"] BioA["Equalisation Tank
Flow and Load Buffering"] BioB["Anoxic Zone
Denitrification NO3 to N2"] BioC["Aerobic Zone
BOD Removal and Nitrification"] Air1["Aeration Blower
DO Supply 2 mg per L"] MBR1["Hollow Fibre Membrane Module
Submerged in Mixed Liquor"] Air2["Air Scour Diffuser
Continuous Bubbling Below Module"] Perm1["Permeate Pump
Suction 0.1 to 0.4 bar Vacuum"] Perm2["Permeate Collection
Treated Effluent"] Sludge1["Waste Activated Sludge
WAS Stream"] CIP1["Clean In Place System
Periodic Chemical Cleaning"] InletA --> BioA BioA --> BioB BioB --> BioC BioC --> MBR1 Air1 --> BioC Air2 --> MBR1 MBR1 -->|"Vacuum Suction"| Perm1 Perm1 --> Perm2 BioC -->|"Sludge Wasting"| Sludge1 MBR1 -->|"Periodic CIP"| CIP1 CIP1 -->|"Return to Aeration"| BioC ``` > **Read the diagram as:** Wastewater enters → flows through biological zones → reaches the **hollow fibre module submerged in mixed liquor** → vacuum draws clean water through the fibre wall → air scour bubbles continuously clean the fibre surface → periodic chemical cleaning restores permeability. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Resistance-in-Series Fouling Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR Feed["Feed Water
Mixed Liquor"] --> F1["1 Cake Layer
Rc - Reversible
Air Scour Removes"] F1 --> F2["2 Fouling Layer
Rf - Semi Reversible
CIP Removes"] F2 --> F3["3 Clean Membrane
Rm - Constant
Intrinsic Property"] F3 --> Perm["Clean Permeate
Final Effluent"] F1 -.->|"Air Scour"| F1 F2 -.->|"Chemical CIP"| F2 ``` > **Interpretation:** The three resistance layers act **in series** — the total resistance $R_{tot}$ is the sum. Air-scour targets the **outermost** reversible layer; chemical CIP targets the middle layer; the **inner** clean membrane resistance is permanent. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Inside-Out vs Outside-In Mode Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InOut["Inside Out Mode Cross Flow"] IOFeed["Pressurized Feed"] --> IOLumen["Inside Lumen of Fibre"] IOLumen -->|"Pressure Drop"| IOPerm["Permeate Exits Through Wall"] IOPerm --> IOShell["Collected in Shell Side"] end subgraph OutIn["Outside In Mode Submerged MBR"] OIFeed["Mixed Liquor Surrounds Fibres"] --> OIShell["Outside Surface of Fibre"] OIShell -->|"Vacuum Applied"| OILumen["Permeate Drawn into Lumen"] OILumen --> OIPerm["Collected at Fibre Open End"] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Visual Cue:** In KTU exam answers, you may be asked to *sketch* a hollow fibre module. Always label **(a) feed direction, (b) permeate direction, (c) air-scour inlet, (d) TMP gauge location**. These four labels earn full process-diagram marks. --- ## 4.4 Module-Level Component Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB PottingA["Top Epoxy Potting
Seals Fibre Ends"] Fibres["Hollow Fibre Bundle
5000 to 50000 Fibres"] Vessel["Pressure Vessel
FRP or Stainless Steel"] PermOut["Permeate Port
Top Header"] FeedIn["Feed Inlet Port
Side or Bottom"] RetentateOut["Retentate Outlet
Concentrate Discharge"] AirIn["Air Scour Inlet
Bottom Manifold"] PottingB["Bottom Epoxy Potting
Seals Fibre Ends"] FeedIn --> Vessel AirIn --> Vessel Vessel --> Fibres Fibres --> PottingA PottingA --> PermOut Fibres --> PottingB PottingB --> RetentateOut ``` --- # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]* **Define a Hollow Fibre membrane. Why is it the most preferred configuration for Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)?** **Model Answer (3 marks — distribution indicated):** A **Hollow Fibre membrane** is a self-supporting capillary tube with an outer diameter of **0.5 – 2.0 mm** and a selective inner (or outer) skin, bundled together (typically 5,000 to 50,000 fibres) inside a module to perform **microfiltration, ultrafiltration, or nanofiltration** of wastewater. (1 mark) It is the **most preferred configuration for MBRs** because of two key reasons: (2 marks) 1. **Highest Packing Density (> 10,000 m²/m³)** — drastically reduces the membrane footprint, which is critical when retrofitting MBRs into space-constrained urban wastewater plants. 2. **Compatibility with Submerged (Outside-In) Operation** — the fibres can be directly immersed in activated sludge, with vacuum-driven permeate suction and air-scouring for fouling control. This eliminates the need for high-pressure cross-flow pumping and reduces energy consumption to **0.3 – 0.6 kWh/m³** of treated water. --- ### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]* **Distinguish between inside-out and outside-in flow configurations of hollow fibre membranes. State one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Inside-Out Mode | Outside-In Mode | |---|---|---| | Feed Location | Inside the lumen | Outside the fibres (shell side) | | Permeate Collection | Outside the shell | Inside the lumen | | Driving Force | Pressure (0.5 – 3 bar) | Vacuum (0.1 – 0.5 bar) | | Best For | **Pre-treatment / UF of low-solids water** | **Submerged MBR (high MLSS)** | **Applications:** (1 mark each, total 2) - **Inside-Out:** Used in pressurized UF plants for **drinking water treatment** (e.g., surface water clarification, RO pretreatment). - **Outside-In:** Used in **submerged MBRs** for municipal sewage treatment (Kubota/ZeeWeed-type systems). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU Pattern Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Students answer **one full question** from the choice of two. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q.A.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]* > **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a **submerged hollow fibre MBR** used for municipal wastewater treatment. Discuss the role of **air-scouring** and **backwash** in maintaining membrane permeability. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A hollow fibre UF module has a membrane area of $50 \text{ m}^2$. During operation, the inlet pressure is 1.8 bar, outlet pressure is 1.5 bar, and permeate pressure is 0.2 bar. The dynamic viscosity of permeate is $0.001$ Pa·s. The clean membrane resistance is $1.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$, and the operating cake layer resistance is $4.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$. Calculate: > (i) The transmembrane pressure (TMP) in bar. > (ii) The steady-state permeate flux in L/m²·h. > (iii) The permeate flow rate in m³/h. **(7 marks)** #### 📘 Model Solution — Q.A (a) **Construction (3 marks):** A submerged hollow fibre MBR consists of the following: - **Hollow fibre bundle:** Thousands of PVDF or PES capillary fibres (OD ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 mm, ID ≈ 0.5 – 0.8 mm) potted at both ends with epoxy resin. - **Module frame:** The bundle is fixed in a stainless-steel or PVC frame and suspended vertically in the aeration tank. - **Permeate header:** A suction pipe at the top of the module draws permeate out of the fibre lumens. - **Air-scour manifold:** A perforated pipe at the base of the module releases coarse bubbles that rise along the fibres, providing shear that controls cake formation. - **Mixed liquor:** The fibres remain immersed in activated sludge (MLSS = 8,000 – 12,000 mg/L). **Working Principle (2 marks):** A vacuum pump applies suction (typically 0.1 – 0.5 bar) to the lumen. Clean water is drawn **from outside to inside** through the microporous membrane wall, leaving suspended solids, biomass, and macromolecules behind. The clarified permeate is collected at the top header. **Role of Air-Scouring and Backwash (2 marks):** - **Air-scouring** induces cross-flow shear along the fibre surface, disrupting the **reversible cake layer** ($R_c$). It is continuous in MBRs and is the **primary energy-consuming operation** (~ 60% of MBR energy budget). - **Backwash** reverses the permeate flow periodically (every 10 – 30 min) to dislodge foulants from inside the pores. It addresses **semi-reversible fouling** ($R_f$) and is supplemented by **chemically enhanced backwash (CEB)** using NaOCl or citric acid. #### 📘 Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Given:** $A_m = 50 \text{ m}^2$, $P_{in} = 1.8$ bar, $P_{out} = 1.5$ bar, $P_{perm} = 0.2$ bar, $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s, $R_m = 1.5 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹, $R_c = 4.5 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹. **Step (i) — Calculate TMP:** $$\text{TMP} = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm} = \frac{1.8 + 1.5}{2} - 0.2 = 1.65 - 0.2 = 1.45 \text{ bar}$$ Convert to Pa: $\text{TMP} = 1.45 \times 10^5 = 1.45 \times 10^5$ Pa **Step (ii) — Calculate Flux:** $$R_{tot} = R_m + R_c = 1.5 \times 10^{12} + 4.5 \times 10^{12} = 6.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$ $$J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}} = \frac{1.45 \times 10^5}{0.001 \times 6.0 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{1.45 \times 10^5}{6.0 \times 10^9}$$ $$J = 2.417 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$ Convert to L/m²·h: $$J = 2.417 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 87.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$$ $$\boxed{J = 87.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}}$$ **Step (iii) — Permeate Flow Rate:** $$Q_p = J \times A_m = 87.0 \text{ L/m}^2\cdot\text{h} \times 50 \text{ m}^2 = 4350 \text{ L/h} = 4.35 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$\boxed{Q_p = 4.35 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$ --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **Q.B.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]* > **(a)** Explain the **Resistance-in-Series model** for hollow fibre membrane fouling. How does it help the operator decide between increasing TMP and performing chemical cleaning? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A hollow fibre module operates with the following parameters: lumen inner diameter = 0.6 mm, feed cross-flow velocity = 0.4 m/s, water density = 1000 kg/m³, dynamic viscosity = 0.001 Pa·s. Compute the **Reynolds number** in the lumen and comment on the flow regime. Also, state **two design modifications** the operator can implement to enhance turbulence. **(7 marks)** #### 📘 Model Solution — Q.B (a) **Concept of the Model (3 marks):** The Resistance-in-Series model is the engineering adaptation of **Darcy's Law** for porous media, applied to a fouled membrane. It states that the total hydraulic resistance offered to permeate flow is the **sum of three serial resistances**: $$R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$$ - $R_m$ — Clean membrane resistance (constant, intrinsic to the material). - $R_c$ — Cake layer resistance (deposited biomass/solids on the surface; **reversible** by air-scour or backwash). - $R_f$ — Internal fouling resistance (pore plugging, adsorption; **partially reversible** by chemical cleaning). **Linkage to Flux Equation:** $$J = \frac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}}$$ **Operator Decision Logic (4 marks):** | Condition | Action | Justification | |---|---|---| | $J$ drops, $R_c$ dominates (TMP can still be raised) | Air-scour / backwash | Cake is reversible; mechanical removal is cheaper | | $J$ drops even at higher TMP, $R_f$ dominates | **Chemical cleaning (CIP)** | Pore-blocked foulants need NaOCl/citric acid | | $R_m$ is unchanged but $J$ low even after CIP | Membrane replacement | End of useful life (~ 5 – 10 years) | > **Cost basis:** Increasing TMP to compensate for $R_c$ costs **~ 5× more energy per m³ of permeate** than removing $R_c$ by air-scour. Hence, **cleaning is always preferred** if it is technically feasible. #### 📘 Model Solution — Q.B (b) **Given:** $d_i = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.001$ Pa·s. **Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds Number:** $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_i}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.4 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{0.001}$$ $$\text{Re} = \frac{0.24}{0.001} = 240$$ **Step 2 — Comment on Flow Regime (3 marks):** Since $\text{Re} = 240 < 2100$, the flow inside the hollow fibre lumen is **laminar**. (1 mark) > **Consequence:** Laminar flow in the lumen means **low shear at the membrane wall** → high propensity for **concentration polarization and cake formation** on the inner surface. (2 marks) **Step 3 — Design Modifications to Enhance Turbulence (3 marks — 1.5 each):** 1. **Use Smaller Inner Diameter Fibres:** Reducing $d_i$ from 0.6 mm to 0.3 mm increases Re to 480 (still laminar but with higher wall shear). Additionally, increasing the **number of fibres** maintains the total membrane area. 2. **Pulsed / Intermittent Cross-Flow or Backwash:** Periodically reversing the flow direction or imposing a pulsed feed velocity induces **transient turbulent bursts** that disrupt the laminar boundary layer. 3. *(Bonus 1 mark)* **Insert Static Mixers / Baffles inside the lumen** to break the laminar flow. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing TMP formula direction:** Many students write $\text{TMP} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ (this is the **feed-side pressure drop**, NOT the transmembrane pressure). The correct KTU-accepted form is $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** if this is confused. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Flux must be reported in **L/m²·h** (KTU standard) — not m/s. Always show the conversion: $J(\text{m/s}) \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = J(\text{L/m}^2\cdot\text{h})$. > > 3. **Mixing up cake vs fouling resistance:** $R_c$ is **outside** the membrane (reversible); $R_f$ is **inside the pores** (irreversible). Examiners expect this distinction in any 7-mark descriptive question. > > 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark theory questions:** A neat, labelled hollow fibre module diagram (even a hand-drawn schematic) is **mandatory** for full marks. Label feed, permeate, air-scour, and TMP gauge. > > 5. **In numericals, writing TMP in bar but substituting in Pa·s:** Keep all units in **SI** for $\mu$ and **Pa** for TMP. If TMP is in bar, convert to Pa first: $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3, Hollow Fibre:** - ✅ **Definition:** Hollow Fibre = capillary membrane with OD 0.5 – 2.0 mm; **packing density > 10,000 m²/m³** (the highest among all configurations). - ✅ **Two Flow Modes:** **Inside-Out** (pressurized, used in UF/RO pretreatment) and **Outside-In** (vacuum, used in **submerged MBRs**). - ✅ **Master Equation:** $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}}$, with $R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$. - ✅ **TMP Formula:** $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ (cross-flow) or $\text{TMP} \approx$ suction vacuum (submerged). - ✅ **Typical Flux (MBR):** **15 – 25 L/m²·h** at TMP **0.1 – 0.5 bar**. - ✅ **Reynolds Number in Lumen:** Almost always **laminar** (Re < 2100) — hence air-scouring is essential. - ✅ **Fouling Control Hierarchy:** Air-scour (continuous) → Backwash (periodic) → CEB (with NaOCl) → CIP (offline, with chemicals) → Membrane replacement. - ✅ **Critical Flux Concept:** Operate below $J_{crit}$ to achieve **sustainable, fouling-free** filtration — KTU favourite for design questions. - ✅ **Materials:** PVDF (most common for MBR due to chemical resistance), PES, PS, PP, PTFE. - ✅ **Energy Benchmark:** Submerged MBR = **0.3 – 0.6 kWh/m³** treated water (vs. 1.0 – 1.5 for conventional activated sludge + tertiary). - ✅ **Module Anatomy:** Two epoxy pottings, fibre bundle, pressure vessel, permeate header, feed inlet, air-scour manifold — **must be labelled in exam diagrams**. > **Final Exam-Day Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says *"Discuss a membrane configuration for wastewater treatment"* — start your answer with **packing density comparison** (HF vs tubular vs spiral), then justify with **fouling tolerance** and **cleanability**. This is the **3-step KTU answer template** for 7-mark descriptive questions on hollow fibres. ---

topics.title: Spiral Wound Membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spiral Wound Membranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Spiral Wound Membrane (SWM)** module is a compact, high-surface-area pressure-driven membrane configuration in which a flat-sheet membrane envelope — composed of two membrane leaves glued on three sides around a porous permeate carrier (permeate spacer) — is wrapped helically around a central perforated permeate collection tube. A **feed/brine spacer** is inserted between adjacent membrane envelopes to create a turbulent feed channel, while the entire assembly is sealed externally within a pressure vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Spiral wound elements are the *industry workhorse* for nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO) in advanced wastewater treatment and water reclamation. Students must know the geometric construction, flow paths, and design equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolled Enchilada" Model

Imagine a flat **enchilada** made of:
- Two thin, semi-permeable tortilla sheets (the membranes) facing each other.
- A porous cheese-filling layer between them (the permeate spacer).
- The three open ends sealed with paste, except one end which is glued to a central drinking straw (the perforated tube).

Roll this enchilada tightly around the straw, then place it inside a rigid plastic cylindrical pipe. When salty, dirty water is **pushed lengthwise** through the pipe (axially), clean water "sweats" sideways through the membrane leaves, spirals inward through the porous cheese layer, and exits via the central straw. The remaining concentrate exits the other end of the pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway:** *Feed flows axially along the outside of the rolled envelope; permeate flows spirally inward and is collected by the central tube.*

### 1.3 Key Components & Standard Metrics

| Component | Standard Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane leaf | Flat polyamide/TFC sheet | Selective barrier (≈ **0.1–0.2 mm** thick) |
| Permeate spacer | Tri-axial polyester (Vexar) | Carries permeate to central tube |
| Feed spacer | Diamond/prowel net (≈ **0.7–1.1 mm** thick) | Promotes turbulence, supports membrane |
| Permeate tube | Perforated PVC/PP, OD ≈ **25–28 mm** | Collects & discharges permeate |
| Pressure vessel | FRP/stainless steel | Withstands **10–80 bar** operating pressure |
| Anti-telescoping device (ATD) | Noryl/PP end cap | Prevents element movement at high ΔP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard commercial element:** 8-inch (≈ **203 mm**) diameter × 40-inch (≈ **1016 mm**) length, providing ≈ **37–41 m²** of active membrane area. "8×40" is the universal KTU-cited reference geometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Membrane Casting:** A thin polyamide rejection layer (≈ 100–200 nm) is deposited on a microporous polysulfone support on a non-woven polyester backing.
2. **Envelope Assembly:** Two membrane leaves are placed with their active (shiny) sides facing **outward** (toward the feed), separated by the permeate spacer. Three edges are sealed with polyurethane or epoxy adhesive; the fourth open edge is glued to the perforated central tube.
3. **Spacer Insertion:** A feed-channel spacer net is glued on top of the membrane envelope so the next envelope, when laid down, has a defined feed gap.
4. **Winding:** The assembled "leaf" is wound helically around the central tube. Number of wraps typically **10–30** depending on spacer thickness.
5. **Sealing & Capping:** The outer surface is wrapped with fiberglass-reinforced tape (tape wrap) and shrinkable polyethylene/ polypropylene outer shell. ATDs are fitted at both ends.
6. **Loading:** The element is inserted into a **FRP pressure vessel** with end-cones, O-rings, and thrust rings.

### 2.2 Flow Path Analysis — The "Why" Behind the Geometry

- **Feed flow** enters the vessel end-cap, flows **axially** through the feed spacer channels in a cross-flow manner (Re ≈ **100–500**, deliberately turbulent via spacer-induced eddies).
- **Permeate flow** moves **perpendicular** to the membrane (trans-membrane), then **spirally** through the permeate spacer, finally **radially inward** into the central tube perforations.
- **Concentrate (brine)** exits the opposite end at elevated pressure (typically **65–75%** of feed pressure due to friction losses through spacers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The geometry creates a **high surface-area-to-volume ratio** of ≈ **800–1000 m²/m³**, an order of magnitude higher than tubular modules, which is why SWM dominates industrial RO/NF.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are exhaustively used in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems on membrane design.

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membrane Flux | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | $\text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ (LMH) | $Q_p$ = permeate flow; $A_m$ = membrane area |
| 2 | Transmembrane Pressure | $\Delta P_{tm} = \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p$ | bar / kPa | $P_p$ ≈ permeate pressure (≈ 0) |
| 3 | Osmotic Pressure Correction | $\Delta\pi = \pi_{f,in} - \pi_p$ | bar | $\pi_p$ ≈ 0 for low-salt permeate |
| 4 | Net Driving Pressure (NDP) | $NDP = \Delta P_{tm} - \Delta\pi$ | bar | Effective driving force |
| 5 | Recovery Ratio (Y) | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_f}$ | fraction / % | $Q_c$ = concentrate flow |
| 6 | Salt Passage | $SP = \dfrac{C_p}{C_{f,avg}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates membrane selectivity |
| 7 | Rejection (R) | $R = 1 - SP = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_{f,avg}}$ | fraction | Companion of SP |
| 8 | Solute Mass Balance | $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_c C_c$ | mg/L (or ppm) | Used to find concentrate concentration |
| 9 | Concentration Polarization Modulus | $\beta = \dfrac{C_w}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | – | $C_w$ = wall conc.; $C_b$ = bulk; $k$ = mass-transfer coeff. |
| 10 | Permeate Spacer Length (spiral angle) | $L_s = \dfrac{\pi D N}{\cos\theta}$ | m | $D$ = mean dia.; $N$ = leaves; $\theta$ = wrap angle |
| 11 | Active Area (typical 8×40) | $A_m \approx 35\text{–}41$ | m² | Manufacturer-specific (e.g., DOW Filmtec) |
| 12 | Specific Flux | $J_{sp} = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P_{tm}}$ | LMH/bar | Used for normalization |
| 13 | Permeability (A-value) | $A = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P_{tm} - \Delta\pi}$ | $\text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1} \cdot \text{bar}^{-1}$ | Membrane constant for RO |
| 14 | Element Pressure Drop (ΔP_vessel) | $\Delta P_{vessel} = f \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{D_h \cdot 2}$ | bar | Feed spacer loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical pitfall:** In SWM, $\Delta P_{tm}$ is the **average TMP** across the length of the element, not the inlet TMP. Forgetting the $P_{f,in} + P_{f,out} \over 2$ term is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in KTU ESE.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal wastewater reuse** (NEWater-style reclamation).
- **Industrial RO** for cooling-tower blowdown recovery.
- **Brackish water & seawater desalination** for potable supply.
- **Landfill leachate treatment** as a polishing step post-biologic/UF.
- **Pharmaceutical & food** concentration/dewatering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Membrane Area from Flux & Permeate Demand

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Style):** A wastewater reclamation RO plant must deliver $Q_p = 240 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ using 8×40 spiral-wound elements, each providing $A_m = 37 \ \text{m}^2$. Operating flux $J = 18 \ \text{LMH}$. Determine: (a) total membrane area required; (b) number of elements; (c) total feed flow if recovery $Y = 75\%$.

**Step 1 — Convert permeate demand to compatible units.**

$$
Q_p = 240 \ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}} \times \frac{1000 \ \text{L}}{1 \ \text{m}^3} \times \frac{1 \ \text{day}}{24 \ \text{h}} = 10000 \ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the flux equation $J = Q_p / A_m$ to find required area.**

$$
A_m = \frac{Q_p}{J} = \frac{10000 \ \text{L/h}}{18 \ \text{L/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}} = 555.56 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Determine number of elements (assuming 6 elements per pressure vessel).**

$$
N_{vessels} = \frac{555.56}{6 \times 37} = \frac{555.56}{222} \approx 2.50
$$

Rounded up → **3 pressure vessels** holding 6 elements each = 18 elements = **666 m²** of installed area (oversized by ≈ 20% safety).

**Step 4 — Find feed flow from recovery.**

$$
Q_f = \frac{Q_p}{Y} = \frac{240}{0.75} = 320 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Concentration Polarization Modulus (β)

**Step 1 — Start from film-theory mass balance at membrane wall.**

The convective solute flux to the wall must equal the sum of back-diffusive flux + permeate solute flux:

$$
J \cdot C + D \frac{dC}{dy} = J \cdot C_p
$$

where $y$ is the coordinate normal to the membrane.

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$
D \frac{dC}{dy} = J (C_p - C)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from bulk ($y = 0$, $C = C_b$) to wall ($y = \delta$, $C = C_w$).**

$$
\int_{C_b}^{C_w} \frac{dC}{C_p - C} = \frac{J}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral. Let $u = C_p - C$, $du = -dC$.**

$$
-\ln(C_p - C)\Big|_{C_b}^{C_w} = \frac{J \delta}{D} = \frac{J}{k}
$$

(where $k = D/\delta$ is the mass-transfer coefficient).

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_w$ in terms of $C_b$.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_p - C_b}{C_p - C_w}\right) = \frac{J}{k}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange and apply the definition $\beta = C_w / C_b$.**

For nearly complete rejection, $C_p \approx 0$ and $C_p - C_b \approx -C_b$:

$$
\beta = \frac{C_w}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Higher flux $J$ → higher $\beta$ → higher $C_w$ → increased scaling/fouling risk. SWM designers therefore operate at **$J$ = 15–25 LMH** to keep $\beta$ < 1.2.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance to Find Concentrate Concentration

For a single-stage SWM system with full mixing assumption:

$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_c C_c
$$

With $Q_c = Q_f - Q_p$ and $C_p$ known (from rejection):

$$
C_c = \frac{Q_f C_f - Q_p C_p}{Q_c} = \frac{C_f - Y \cdot C_p}{1 - Y}
$$

**Example:** $C_f = 2000$ mg/L TDS, $Y = 0.75$, $R = 0.99$ → $C_p = 20$ mg/L.

$$
C_c = \frac{2000 - (0.75)(20)}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1985}{0.25} = 7940 \ \text{mg/L}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Numerical Solving Checklist

| Quantity | Given? | Find Using | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_f$ | Yes | $Q_f = Q_p / Y$ | Recovery relation |
| $C_c$ | Mixed feed | $C_c = (C_f - Y C_p)/(1-Y)$ | Single-stage mixing |
| $\Delta P_{tm}$ | Pressures | $(P_{in} + P_{out})/2 - P_p$ | Use mean TMP |
| $N_{elements}$ | $A_m$, $J$, $Q_p$ | $N = Q_p / (J \cdot A_{m,\text{element}})$ | Round up |
| $\beta$ | $J$, $k$ | $\beta = \exp(J/k)$ | Check for fouling |
| $A$-value | $J$, NDP | $A = J / NDP$ | Membrane constant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Spiral Wound Element — Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Inlet\nHigh Pressure Pump\n10-80 bar] --> B[FRP Pressure Vessel\nAxial flow path]
    B --> C[Feed Spacer Net\nDiamond / Prowel\nTurbulence promoter]
    C --> D[Membrane Leaf 1\nPolyamide TFC\nRejection Layer]
    D --> E[Permeate Spacer\nPorous Tri-axial\nSpiral flow inward]
    E --> F[Central Perforated Tube\nProduct Collection]
    F --> G[Permeate Outlet\nLow Pressure\nClean Water]
    C --> H[Membrane Leaf 2\nPolyamide TFC\nRejection Layer]
    H --> I[Outer Wrap\nFRP Tape + Shrink Shell]
    I --> J[Concentrate Outlet\n65-75 percent of inlet P\nBrine discharge]
    K[Anti Telescoping Device ATD] --> B
    L[Brine Seal O Ring] --> B
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Single Membrane Envelope (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Feed Channel 1\nBulk Flow Axial] --> M1[Membrane Leaf 1\nActive Side Out]
    M1 --> S1[Permeate Spacer\nTrilobal Vexar]
    S1 --> M2[Membrane Leaf 2\nActive Side Out]
    M2 --> P2[Feed Channel 2\nBulk Flow Axial]
    S1 --> T[Perforated Central Tube\nPermeate Collection]
    F1[Concentrate Exits\nEnd Cap] --- P1
    F1 --- P2
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Full SWM RO Skid (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Raw Feed\nPre treated UF Effluent] --> P[High Pressure Pump\nMultistage Centrifugal]
    P --> V1[Cartridge Filter 5 micron]
    V1 --> R1[RO Vessel 1\n6 x 8x40 Elements\nStage 1]
    R1 --> R2[RO Vessel 2\n4 x 8x40 Elements\nStage 2]
    R1 --> C1[Concentrate 1\n75 percent of feed P]
    R2 --> C2[Concentrate 2\nFinal Brine]
    R2 --> PERM[Permeate\nTDS less than 50 mg per L\nReclaimed Water]
    C1 --> ERD[Energy Recovery Device\nPX or Turbocharger]
    ERD --> P
    PERM --> UV[UV Disinfection\nPost Treatment]
    UV --> OUT[Reuse Distribution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Stage 2 concentrate is often recycled to Stage 1 feed to lift overall system recovery to **85–90%** — a common KTU design-economics question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List the main components of a spiral wound membrane element and state the function of each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Membrane leaves (2 sheets per envelope):** Provide the semi-permeable barrier; selective separation of solutes from water. *(1 mark)*
2. **Permeate carrier/spacer:** A porous tricot or Vexar fabric sandwiched between the two membrane leaves that channels the permeate spirally to the central tube. *(1 mark)*
3. **Feed spacer:** A diamond/prowel net placed between adjacent envelopes to maintain a defined feed channel, induce turbulence, and support the membrane against pressure. *(1 mark)*

*(Acceptable mention: central perforated permeate tube — additional credit.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define 'recovery ratio' for a spiral wound RO system and state a typical value for seawater desalination."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** The recovery ratio $Y$ is the fraction of feed water that is converted into permeate (product water). It is expressed as $Y = Q_p / Q_f \times 100\%$. *(2 marks)*
- **Typical value:** For seawater desalination using SWM RO, single-pass recovery is **35–45%** due to osmotic-pressure limitations and scaling risk; for brackish water it is **70–85%**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a spiral wound membrane (SWM) element** used in reverse osmosis treatment of wastewater. Label all major components and indicate the direction of feed, permeate, and concentrate flows. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A municipal wastewater reuse RO plant must produce **$Q_p = 180 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$** of permeate. Design specifications: operating flux $J = 20 \ \text{LMH}$, recovery ratio $Y = 0.80$, element area $A_m = 37 \ \text{m}^2$, 6 elements per pressure vessel, and feed TDS $C_f = 1500 \ \text{mg/L}$. Membrane salt rejection $R = 0.985$. Determine:
- (i) Total membrane area required *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Number of pressure vessels required *(2 marks)*
- (iii) Concentrate TDS, $C_c$ *(3 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working — 7 marks**

A spiral wound RO element is constructed by:

1. **Envelope formation (1 mark):** Two flat-sheet polyamide TFC membrane leaves are placed with their active (rejecting) sides facing **outward**. A porous permeate spacer is sandwiched between them. Three edges are glued with polyurethane adhesive; the fourth open edge is glued to a perforated central PVC permeate tube.

2. **Spacer and winding (1 mark):** A diamond-net feed spacer is glued to the outside of the envelope. The envelope is then helically wound around the central tube. An outer FRP tape wrap provides mechanical strength; ATDs at both ends prevent telescoping.

3. **Pressure vessel loading (1 mark):** The element is loaded into an FRP pressure vessel; multiple elements (typically 6) are placed in series within one vessel. Brine seals prevent bypass.

4. **Working principle (3 marks):**
   - High-pressure feed enters the vessel end-cap and flows **axially** through the feed spacer channels in a cross-flow pattern.
   - Under the applied pressure (typically **10–25 bar** for brackish water RO), water permeates through the membrane *perpendicular* to the surface.
   - Permeate is collected in the porous carrier and flows **spirally inward** to the central perforated tube, exiting at low pressure.
   - The retained salts and concentrate exit the other end at ≈ 70% of inlet pressure, where the energy may be recovered via an ERD.
   - Cross-flow keeps the membrane surface swept, reducing (but not eliminating) fouling and concentration polarization.

5. **Sketch credit (1 mark):** Labelled diagram showing feed in → feed spacer → membrane → permeate spacer → central tube → permeate out; concentrate out opposite end.

**(b) Numerical Design — 7 marks**

**Given:** $Q_p = 180 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $J = 20 \ \text{LMH}$, $Y = 0.80$, $A_m = 37 \ \text{m}^2$, elements per vessel = 6, $C_f = 1500 \ \text{mg/L}$, $R = 0.985$.

**Step (i) — Total membrane area:**

$$
Q_p = 180 \ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}} \times \frac{1000 \ \text{L}}{1 \ \text{m}^3} \times \frac{1}{24 \ \text{day/h}} = 7500 \ \text{L/h}
$$

$$
A_{total} = \frac{Q_p}{J} = \frac{7500}{20} = 375 \ \text{m}^2 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step (ii) — Number of pressure vessels:**

$$
N_{elements} = \frac{375}{37} = 10.14 \Rightarrow 11 \ \text{elements (round up)}
$$

$$
N_{vessels} = \frac{11}{6} = 1.83 \Rightarrow \textbf{2 pressure vessels} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

(2 vessels × 6 elements = 12 elements installed, providing 444 m²; oversized by ≈ 18%.)

**Step (iii) — Concentrate TDS:**

First, permeate TDS:

$$
C_p = C_f (1 - R) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.985) = 1500 \times 0.015 = 22.5 \ \text{mg/L}
$$

Then, using mass balance:

$$
C_c = \frac{C_f - Y \cdot C_p}{1 - Y} = \frac{1500 - (0.80)(22.5)}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1500 - 18}{0.20} = \frac{1482}{0.20}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_c = 7410 \ \text{mg/L}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **concentration polarization** in spiral wound RO membranes. Derive the film-theory expression for the polarization modulus $\beta = C_w / C_b$ and discuss its practical consequences on system design. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An SWM RO unit is operated at flux $J = 22 \ \text{LMH}$ and cross-flow velocity $v = 0.15 \ \text{m/s}$ in the feed spacer channel. The mass-transfer coefficient for the spacer is given by the Sherwood correlation:

$$
Sh = 0.065 \, Re^{0.875} \, Sc^{0.25}
$$

The diffusivity of NaCl $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, spacer hydraulic diameter $D_h = 0.8 \ \text{mm} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$, and Schmidt number $Sc = 666$. Determine:
- (i) Reynolds number $Re$ *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Sherwood number $Sh$ *(2 marks)*
- (iii) Mass-transfer coefficient $k$ *(1 mark)*
- (iv) Polarization modulus $\beta$ and wall concentration if bulk $C_b = 2000$ mg/L *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concentration Polarization — Theory & Derivation — 7 marks**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Concentration polarization is the accumulation of rejected solutes at the membrane surface, forming a thin boundary layer of elevated concentration $C_w$ compared to the bulk $C_b$.

2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** Under applied pressure, water (and a small amount of salt) is forced through the membrane. Salt is preferentially rejected, so it accumulates at the surface.
