The build-up is opposed by **back-diffusion** (Fick's law) and **cross-flow sweep**. A steady-state concentration gradient forms within a film of thickness $\delta$ ≈ 50–200 µm.

3. **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from solute mass balance at the wall:

$$
J \cdot C_b = J \cdot C_p + D \frac{C_w - C_b}{\delta}
$$

With $k = D / \delta$ and assuming $C_p \ll C_b$ (high rejection):

$$
J = k \ln\!\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) \approx k \ln\!\left(\frac{C_w}{C_b}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{C_w}{C_b} = \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right)}
$$

4. **Practical consequences (1 mark):** Higher $\beta$ → higher $C_w$ → reduced effective NDP (osmotic back-pressure) → reduced flux; increased scaling (CaCO₃, CaSO₄, silica) and fouling; membrane life shortened. Design rule: operate at $J$ low enough that $\beta \leq 1.2$.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Step (i) — Reynolds number:**

$$
Re = \frac{v D_h}{\nu} = \frac{(0.15)(8 \times 10^{-4})}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 120
$$

$$
\boxed{Re = 120} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step (ii) — Sherwood number:**

$$
Sh = 0.065 \times (120)^{0.875} \times (666)^{0.25}
$$

Compute each factor:

$$
(120)^{0.875} = e^{0.875 \ln 120} = e^{0.875 \times 4.7875} = e^{4.189} = 65.99
$$

$$
(666)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln 666} = e^{0.25 \times 6.502} = e^{1.6255} = 5.080
$$

$$
Sh = 0.065 \times 65.99 \times 5.080 = 0.065 \times 335.2 = 21.79
$$

$$
\boxed{Sh \approx 21.8} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step (iii) — Mass-transfer coefficient:**

$$
k = \frac{Sh \cdot D}{D_h} = \frac{21.8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-8}}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.09 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m/s}
$$

Convert to LMH for flux calculation:

$$
k = 4.09 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 147.2 \ \text{LMH}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 1.47 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m/s} \ (\approx 147 \ \text{LMH})} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step (iv) — Polarization modulus & wall concentration:**

Convert $J$ to compatible units: $J = 22 \ \text{LMH} = 22 / (3600 \times 1000) = 6.11 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m/s}$.

Use the LMH values directly:

$$
\beta = \exp\!\left(\frac{J}{k}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{22}{147}\right) = \exp(0.1497) = 1.162
$$

$$
C_w = \beta \cdot C_b = 1.162 \times 2000 = 2323 \ \text{mg/L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta \approx 1.16, \quad C_w \approx 2323 \ \text{mg/L}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Spiral Wound Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** ($Q_p$ in m³/day vs J in LMH vs A in m²) — major source of 1–2 mark deductions. Always convert $Q_p$ to **L/h** before using $J$ in LMH.
> 2. **Using inlet pressure alone for TMP** — must take the **arithmetic mean** of inlet and outlet feed pressures, then subtract permeate pressure.
> 3. **Mixing single-stage and multi-stage recovery** — for a 2-stage array, overall recovery $Y_{overall} \neq Y_1 + Y_2$; use stage-by-stage mass balance.
> 4. **Ignoring concentration polarization in flux calculation** — NDP must use $C_w$, not $C_b$, when polarization is significant ($\beta > 1.2$).
> 5. **Misapplication of osmotic pressure** — use $\pi = i C R_g T$ (van't Hoff) for dilute solutions, not the raw TDS. For typical wastewater, $\pi \ (\text{bar}) \approx 0.75 \times C \ (\text{eq/L})$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Spiral Wound Membranes)**

- ☐ SWM = "rolled envelope" geometry; 8×40 element gives ≈ 37–41 m² active area.
- ☐ **Three flows:** feed (axial, high P) → permeate (radial/spiral, low P) → concentrate (axial, ≈ 70% of inlet P).
- ☐ **Components:** 2 membrane leaves + permeate spacer + feed spacer + central perforated tube + ATDs + pressure vessel.
- ☐ **Flux equation:** $J = Q_p / A_m$ (LMH).
- ☐ **TMP:** $\Delta P_{tm} = (P_{in} + P_{out})/2 - P_p$ — always take the mean.
- ☐ **NDP:** $NDP = \Delta P_{tm} - \Delta\pi$; use permeate-side osmotic pressure ≈ 0.
- ☐ **Recovery:** $Y = Q_p / Q_f$; typical 70–85% (BWRO) / 35–45% (SWRO).
- ☐ **Rejection:** $R = 1 - C_p / C_{f,avg}$.
- ☐ **Mass balance:** $Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_c C_c$ → use to find $C_c$ or $C_p$.
- ☐ **Concentration polarization:** $\beta = \exp(J/k)$ — operate at $J$ such that $\beta \leq 1.2$.
- ☐ **Sherwood correlation for spacers:** $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^{0.25}$ (typical $a = 0.065$, $b = 0.875$).
- ☐ **Advantages:** high packing density (≈ 800 m²/m³), low cost per m², replaceable, standardized.
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** sensitive to suspended solids (needs UF pre-treatment), hard to clean when fouled, limited to feed TSS < 1 mg/L, can't handle high-viscosity fluids.
- ☐ **Pre-treatment chain:** Coagulation → MF/UF → Cartridge filter (5 µm) → HP pump → SWM RO.
- ☐ **Cleaning:** Chemical (CIP) with alkaline + acidic detergents at low pressure, high cross-flow.
- ☐ **Energy recovery:** Use PX/turbocharger on concentrate to feed — essential for SWRO economics.
- ☐ **KTU-numerical shortcuts:** (i) Always express $Q_p$ in L/h for LMH consistency; (ii) Round up element count; (iii) Use mean TMP, not inlet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules, atoms, or ions of a substance (the *adsorbate*) accumulate on the surface of a solid or liquid (the *adsorbent*) through physical or chemical forces, forming a film or layer. It is a mass-transfer operation widely employed as a tertiary (polishing) treatment step in advanced wastewater treatment to remove dissolved contaminants such as organic micropollutants, color, odor, heavy metals, and refractory organics.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECHT725)**, adsorption is positioned as a critical advanced treatment unit operation, bridging primary/biological treatment and final disinfection. The driving force is governed by the tendency of the system to reduce its interfacial (surface) free energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU evaluation, students often confuse *adsorption* with *absorption*. The Board Examiner strictly checks whether the student understands that adsorption is a **surface** phenomenon, not bulk penetration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Magnet on a Fridge**
> Imagine a fridge door (the *adsorbent* surface) and tiny iron filings (the *adsorbate* molecules). When you bring a magnet close, the iron filings "stick" to the surface of the magnet — they do not penetrate inside the magnet. This surface accumulation is **adsorption**.
> 
> Now, imagine a sponge soaking up water — the water enters the bulk interior of the sponge. That is **absorption**.
> 
> When both happen together (surface accumulation + bulk penetration), the process is called **sorption**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favorites)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Adsorbate** | The substance being accumulated on the surface (e.g., dye molecules in wastewater). |
| **Adsorbent** | The solid or liquid surface on which accumulation occurs (e.g., activated carbon). |
| **Sorbate** | Generic term for the material being taken up. |
| **Sorbent** | Generic term for the material that takes up. |
| **Desorption** | Reverse process — release of the adsorbed species from the surface (critical for adsorbent regeneration). |
| **Interface** | The boundary between two phases where adsorption occurs. |

## 1.4 Distinction: Adsorption vs Absorption

| Feature | Adsorption | Absorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location** | Surface only | Bulk of the material |
| **Phenomenon type** | Surface phenomenon | Volume phenomenon |
| **Rate** | Rapid, reaches equilibrium fast | Slow, depends on diffusion |
| **Example** | Activated carbon removing phenol | Water vapor absorbed by silica gel |
| **Concentration profile** | Highest at the surface | Uniform throughout the bulk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Expectation):** The term *adsorption* was coined by **Heinrich Kayser (1881)** to describe the uptake of gases by charcoal. The word literally means "to attract to" (Latin: *ad* = to, *sorbere* = suck in).

## 1.5 Standard Metrics in Adsorption Engineering

- **Specific surface area of activated carbon:** **500 – 1500 m²/g** (this enormous area is the *reason* activated carbon is the most popular adsorbent).
- **Operating pH range for GAC (Granular Activated Carbon):** **6.0 – 8.0** for most organics.
- **Typical contact time in wastewater adsorption columns:** **10 – 60 minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Adsorption

Adsorption is broadly classified into two categories based on the **nature of the bonding force** between the adsorbate and the adsorbent surface.

### 2.1.1 Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)

- Caused by **weak van der Waals forces** (London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions).
- **Low enthalpy of adsorption:** $\Delta H_{ads} \approx -20$ to $-40$ kJ/mol.
- **Reversible** — desorption is easily achieved.
- **Non-specific** — adsorbate can form multilayers.
- **No activation energy** required — occurs rapidly at low temperatures.
- **Example:** Adsorption of nitrogen gas on activated carbon at 77 K (used in BET surface area analysis).

### 2.1.2 Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)

- Caused by **strong chemical (covalent or ionic) bonds** between adsorbate and adsorbent surface atoms.
- **High enthalpy of adsorption:** $\Delta H_{ads} \approx -40$ to $-400$ kJ/mol.
- **Mostly irreversible** — forms a stable monolayer.
- **Highly specific** — only certain molecules adsorb on specific surfaces.
- **Activation energy is required** — slower, temperature-dependent.
- **Example:** Oxygen chemisorbed on charcoal to form surface oxides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption."* This appears almost every cycle in either Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part of a 14-mark question. Memorize the comparison table.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Adsorption (The 5 Big Levers)

These factors are **high-weightage** in KTU exams and viva voce.

### A. Nature of the Adsorbent
- High specific surface area → higher adsorption.
- Pore structure (micropores < 2 nm, mesopores 2–50 nm, macropores > 50 nm).
- Surface chemistry (presence of functional groups like –OH, –COOH on activated carbon).

### B. Nature of the Adsorbate
- **Solubility:** Lower aqueous solubility → higher adsorption (hydrophobic organics adsorb better).
- **Molecular size:** Molecules that fit the pore size adsorb best.
- **Polarity:** Non-polar solutes adsorb more onto non-polar activated carbon.

### C. pH of the Solution
- Affects the **ionization state** of the adsorbate.
- For anionic dyes/organic acids: adsorption is favored at **low pH**.
- For cationic species (e.g., metal ions, basic dyes): adsorption is favored at **high pH**.
- pH at which the adsorbent surface has **zero net charge** is the **Point of Zero Charge (PZC or pH$_{PZC}$)**.

### D. Temperature
- **Physisorption** is **exothermic**, so adsorption capacity **decreases** with increasing temperature (Le Chatelier's principle).
- **Chemisorption** typically **increases** with temperature up to a point (since activation energy is needed).

### E. Contact Time and Initial Concentration
- Adsorption increases with time until **equilibrium** is reached.
- Higher initial concentration → higher uptake per unit mass of adsorbent (until saturation).

## 2.3 Common Adsorbents Used in Wastewater Treatment

| Adsorbent | Key Feature | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Activated Carbon (PAC/GAC)** | Highest surface area, versatile | Color, odor, organics, micropollutants |
| **Activated Alumina** | Polar surface | Fluoride, arsenic removal |
| **Silica Gel** | Hydrophilic pores | Selective organics, drying agent |
| **Zeolites** | Uniform cage-like pores | Ammonium ion (NH$_4^+$) removal |
| **Biosorbents** (e.g., chitosan, agricultural waste) | Low-cost, eco-friendly | Heavy metals, dyes |
| **Manganese dioxide (MnO$_2$)** | Oxidative surface | Iron, manganese, arsenic |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for numerical problems in Part B (14-mark questions). Note: All uses of absolute value or "such that" are written with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Unit / Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Adsorption capacity (q$_e$)** | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | mg/g (mass of adsorbate per gram of adsorbent) |
| **Removal efficiency** | $\%\,R = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) |
| **Langmuir isotherm** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ | $q_m$ = max capacity, $K_L$ = Langmuir constant |
| **Freundlich isotherm** | $q_e = K_F\, C_e^{1/n}$ | Empirical, for heterogeneous surfaces |
| **BET isotherm (multilayer)** | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e(C_0 - C_e)} = \dfrac{1}{K_B q_m} + \dfrac{(K_B - 1)}{K_B q_m} \cdot \dfrac{C_e}{C_0}$ | Used for surface area determination |
| **Arrhenius for adsorption kinetics** | $k = A\, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy |
| **Equilibrium condition** | $\dfrac{dq_e}{dt} = 0$ | At steady state |
| **Mass balance on batch adsorber** | $V C_0 + m q_0 = V C_e + m q_e$ | For closed isotherm determination |

Where:
- $C_0$ = initial adsorbate concentration (mg/L)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration (mg/L)
- $V$ = solution volume (L)
- $m$ = mass of adsorbent (g)
- $q_e$ = equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g)
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (mg/g)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir equilibrium constant (L/mg)
- $K_F$ = Freundlich capacity constant (mg/g)(L/mg)$^{1/n}$
- $1/n$ = heterogeneity factor (dimensionless)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Adsorption is the **workhorse** of polishing steps in:
1. **Drinking water treatment plants (DWTPs):** Removal of taste, odor (geosmin, MIB), pesticides, and disinfection by-product (DBP) precursors.
2. **Tertiary wastewater treatment:** Removal of pharmaceuticals, endocrine-disrupting compounds (EDCs), and personal care products.
3. **Industrial effluent treatment:** Decolorization of dye baths, recovery of precious metals, and treatment of landfill leachate.
4. **Industrial process units:** Sugar refining (decolorization), air purification (gas masks), and gold recovery (carbon-in-pulp, CIP process in mining).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (1918)

The Langmuir isotherm is the most fundamental model describing **monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface**. It is a *guaranteed* part of KTU derivations and frequently carries 7–10 marks.

### Assumptions (KTU Examiner checks these first):
1. The adsorbent surface has a **fixed number of identical sites**.
2. Each site holds **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer).
3. The adsorption energy is **constant** at all sites (no interaction between adsorbed molecules).
4. Adsorption is **localized** — molecules don't migrate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by adsorbate
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of bare surface
- $k_a$ = rate constant for adsorption
- $k_d$ = rate constant for desorption
- $P$ (or $C$ for solutions) = pressure / concentration of adsorbate

**Step 1:** Rate of adsorption is proportional to the bare surface fraction and the concentration of adsorbate.

$$
r_a = k_a\, C\, (1 - \theta)
$$

**Step 2:** Rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of surface already covered.

$$
r_d = k_d\, \theta
$$

**Step 3:** At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption.

$$
k_a\, C\, (1 - \theta) = k_d\, \theta
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\theta$ algebraically.

$$
k_a\, C - k_a\, C\, \theta = k_d\, \theta
$$

$$
k_a\, C = k_d\, \theta + k_a\, C\, \theta
$$

$$
k_a\, C = \theta\, (k_d + k_a\, C)
$$

$$
\theta = \dfrac{k_a\, C}{k_d + k_a\, C}
$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Langmuir equilibrium constant** $K_L = k_a / k_d$.

$$
\theta = \dfrac{K_L\, C}{1 + K_L\, C}
$$

**Step 6:** Express the amount adsorbed $q_e$ in terms of the maximum monolayer capacity $q_m$.

Since $\theta = q_e / q_m$, we obtain the **final Langmuir equation**:

$$
q_e = \dfrac{q_m\, K_L\, C_e}{1 + K_L\, C_e}
$$

This is the most cited equation in adsorption problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm (1906)

The Freundlich isotherm is **empirical** and applies to **heterogeneous surfaces** with multilayer adsorption. It is the *most commonly used* model in wastewater engineering due to its simplicity.

### Starting Empirical Form (Early Form):

Empirically, Freundlich observed that for a given mass of adsorbent, the amount adsorbed $q_e$ followed a power-law dependence on the equilibrium concentration $C_e$:

$$
q_e = K_F\, C_e^{1/n}
$$

### Linearized Form (Used in KTU Numerical Problems):

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\, \ln C_e
$$

This is a straight line of the form $y = mx + c$:
- $y = \ln q_e$
- $x = \ln C_e$
- Slope $m = 1/n$
- Intercept $c = \ln K_F$

By plotting $\ln q_e$ vs $\ln C_e$, we obtain the Freundlich constants $K_F$ and $1/n$ from the graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** If the value of $1/n$ is between **0 and 1**, adsorption is **favorable**. If $1/n = 1$, the adsorption is **linear**. If $1/n > 1$, adsorption is **unfavorable**. KTU examiners love to test this interpretation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A wastewater sample has an initial phenol concentration $C_0 = 50$ mg/L. It is treated with $m = 2$ g of activated carbon in a $V = 1$ L batch reactor. At equilibrium, the phenol concentration drops to $C_e = 10$ mg/L.

**Find:**
- (a) The equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$
- (b) The percent removal

### Part (a): Adsorption Capacity

**Step 1:** Identify the variables.
- $C_0 = 50$ mg/L
- $C_e = 10$ mg/L
- $V = 1$ L
- $m = 2$ g

**Step 2:** Apply the adsorption capacity formula.

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)\, V}{m}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(50 - 10) \times 1}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the numerator.

$$
q_e = \dfrac{40 \times 1}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$
\boxed{q_e = 20\ \text{mg/g}}
$$

### Part (b): Percent Removal

**Step 1:** Apply the percent removal formula.

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{(50 - 10)}{50} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the difference.

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{40}{50} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$
\boxed{\%\,R = 80\%}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Physisorption vs Chemisorption

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bonding force** | Van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / ionic (strong) |
| **Enthalpy ($\Delta H$)** | $-20$ to $-40$ kJ/mol | $-40$ to $-400$ kJ/mol |
| **Activation energy** | None | Required (finite $E_a$) |
| **Layers** | Multilayer possible | Strictly monolayer |
| **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| **Effect of $\uparrow$ T** | Decreases | Increases (initially) |
| **Effect of $\uparrow$ P/C** | Increases then plateaus | Saturates at monolayer |
| **Example** | N$_2$ on charcoal at 77 K | O$_2$ on tungsten |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Adsorption Process

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical wastewater adsorption treatment system, showing the interactions between components, the direction of mass flow, and the regeneration loop (a critical aspect of full-marks answers in KTU Board Exams).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RawWastewaterInlet] --> B[EqualizationTank]
    B --> C[PreTreatment: Screening and Grit Removal]
    C --> D[PrimaryClarifier]
    D --> E[BiologicalTreatmentUnit: Activated Sludge Process]
    E --> F[SecondaryClarifier]
    F --> G[AdsorptionContactTank: PAC Dosing]
    G --> H[FilterPress: PAC Sludge to Disposal]
    F --> I[AdsorptionColumn: GAC Fixed Bed]
    I --> J[TreatedEffluentStorage]
    J --> K[Disinfection: Chlorination or UV]
    K --> L[FinalOutlet to Receiving Water Body]
    I --> M[SpentGAC Column: Saturation Reached]
    M --> N[ThermalRegeneration: Furnace at 800 to 900 C]
    N --> O[ReactivatedCarbon: Returned to Service]
    O --> I
    P[CarbonQualityMonitor] -.-> I
    Q[FlowRateController] -.-> I
    R[pHAdjustmentUnit] -.-> G
    S[BrineorAcidforInSituRegen] -.-> I
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: Surface Adsorption Mechanism

The following diagram illustrates the **microscopic mechanism** of how adsorbate molecules interact with the porous surface of an adsorbent (e.g., activated carbon).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AdsorbentSurface[Activated Carbon Particle Cross Section]
        P1[Macropore Entrance > 50 nm] --> P2[Mesopore Channel 2 to 50 nm]
        P2 --> P3[Micropore Cavity less than 2 nm]
        P3 --> P4[Inner Surface Area High Energy Site]
    end
    subgraph BulkSolution[Wastewater Bulk Phase]
        M1[Contaminant Molecule A] -.->|diffusion| P1
        M2[Contaminant Molecule B] -.->|diffusion| P2
        M3[Contaminant Molecule C] -.->|diffusion| P3
        M4[Contaminant Molecule D] -.->|adsorption| P4
    end
    P4 --> Bound[Molecule Held by van der Waals or Chemical Bond]
```

## 4.3 Isotherm Comparison Schematic

This diagram maps the **shape and mathematical behavior** of the Langmuir, Freundlich, and BET isotherms used in adsorption analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Adsorption Isotherm Selection] --> Q1{Is the Surface Homogeneous?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the Adsorption Monolayer Only?}
    Q1 -->|No| F1[Use Freundlich Isotherm: q_e = K_F C_e to the power 1/n]
    Q2 -->|Yes| L1[Use Langmuir Isotherm: q_e = q_m K_L C_e divided by 1 plus K_L C_e]
    Q2 -->|No| B1[Use BET Isotherm: Multilayer on Homogeneous Surface]
    F1 --> App1[Typical Application: Dye Removal from Textile Effluent]
    L1 --> App2[Typical Application: Heavy Metal Removal on Activated Alumina]
    B1 --> App3[Typical Application: Surface Area Measurement of New Adsorbents]
```

## 4.4 Physical Process Flow of Batch Adsorption

The following schematic shows the **operational sequence** in a batch adsorption experiment — a frequently asked question in KTU practicals and viva.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1: Weigh Known Mass m of Adsorbent] --> Step2[Step 2: Prepare Adsorbate Solution of Known C0]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Mix Adsorbent and Solution in Erlenmeyer Flask]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Agitate at Constant Temperature using Shaker]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Withdraw Samples at Fixed Time Intervals]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Filter and Analyze Residual Concentration C_t]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Plot q_t vs t for Kinetics]
    Step6 --> Step8[Step 8: Determine C_e at Equilibrium]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9: Plot q_e vs C_e for Isotherm]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define adsorption. Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption in terms of bond type and reversibility.**

**Model Answer:**

**Adsorption:** Adsorption is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a substance (adsorbate) accumulate on the surface of a solid (adsorbent) due to physical or chemical forces.

| Aspect | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bond type** | Weak van der Waals forces | Strong covalent or ionic bonds |
| **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Mostly irreversible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Tip:** For a 3-mark answer, a clear definition (1 mark) + 2–3 line contrast (2 marks) is sufficient.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four factors affecting the adsorption process in wastewater treatment.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Nature of the adsorbent** (surface area, pore size, surface chemistry).
2. **Nature of the adsorbate** (solubility, polarity, molecular size).
3. **pH of the solution** (affects ionization of adsorbate and surface charge).
4. **Temperature** (exothermic process favored at lower temperatures for physisorption).
5. **Contact time and initial concentration** of the adsorbate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B (14-mark) questions offer an **internal choice**. Students must answer **one of two** alternative questions. Both options below carry equal weightage and full solution detail.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** with its assumptions and derive the final linear form of the equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory effluent contains **200 mg/L of lead (Pb$^{2+}$)**. Batch tests with 5 g of activated carbon in 2 L of solution show that the equilibrium Pb$^{2+}$ concentration is **20 mg/L**. Determine:
   - (i) The equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$
   - (ii) The percentage removal of lead (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Langmuir Isotherm Explanation and Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. The surface of the adsorbent has a **finite number of adsorption sites**, all **identical** and **energetically equivalent**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer coverage).
3. There is **no interaction** between molecules adsorbed on neighboring sites.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $\theta$ be the fraction of surface covered. At equilibrium:

$$
k_a C (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta
$$

Where $k_a$ is the adsorption rate constant and $k_d$ is the desorption rate constant.

Solving for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{k_a C}{k_d + k_a C} = \dfrac{K_L C}{1 + K_L C}
$$

Where $K_L = k_a / k_d$.

The **linear form** is obtained by inverting the equation:

$$
\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}
$$

A plot of $C_e / q_e$ versus $C_e$ gives a straight line of slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 200$ mg/L
- $C_e = 20$ mg/L
- $V = 2$ L
- $m = 5$ g

**Part (i):** Equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$ (3 marks)

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}
$$

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(200 - 20) \times 2}{5}
$$

$$
q_e = \dfrac{180 \times 2}{5} = \dfrac{360}{5}
$$

$$
\boxed{q_e = 72\ \text{mg/g}}
$$

**Part (ii):** Percentage removal (4 marks)

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{200 - 20}{200} \times 100
$$

$$
\%\,R = \dfrac{180}{200} \times 100 = 0.9 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\,R = 90\%}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With neat sketches and equations, distinguish between **physical adsorption and chemical adsorption**. List at least **six distinguishing characteristics**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of **activated carbon as an adsorbent** in advanced wastewater treatment. Explain the **factors that affect its adsorption capacity** for organic micropollutants. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Physisorption vs Chemisorption (7 marks)

A comparison table with the following entries (6 distinguishing points × 1 mark each + 1 mark for the sketch/equation):

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bond** | Van der Waals | Covalent / ionic |
| **$\Delta H$** | $-20$ to $-40$ kJ/mol | $-40$ to $-400$ kJ/mol |
| **Activation energy** | Zero | Finite $E_a$ |
| **Layers** | Multi-layer | Strictly mono-layer |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible | Irreversible |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| **Effect of temperature** | Decreases with $\uparrow$ T | Increases with $\uparrow$ T (initially) |

**Sketch (1 mark):** A neat diagram showing multilayer (physisorption) and monolayer (chemisorption) coverage on a surface.

#### Part (b) — Activated Carbon in Wastewater Treatment (7 marks)

**Role of Activated Carbon (3 marks):**
- **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)** columns are used as tertiary polishing in wastewater treatment plants.
- **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)** is dosed in contact tanks for seasonal or shock loads.
- Removes **refractory organics**, color, odor, **micropollutants** (pharmaceuticals, pesticides, EDCs), and **DBP precursors**.
- Specific surface area: **500 – 1500 m²/g** — provides enormous interfacial area for adsorption.

**Factors affecting adsorption capacity (4 marks):**
1. **Surface area and pore-size distribution** of the carbon.
2. **Type and concentration of the organic pollutant** (hydrophobic, non-polar, low solubility organics adsorb best).
3. **pH of wastewater** (optimum pH for most organics: **6.0 – 8.0**).
4. **Temperature** (lower temperature favors exothermic physisorption).
5. **Competition** from background organic matter (natural organic matter, NOM).
6. **Contact time** and **empty bed contact time (EBCT)** in column operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing adsorption with absorption:** This single mistake can cost up to 2 marks. Always state explicitly: *"Adsorption is a surface phenomenon, not bulk uptake."*
> 2. **Missing the assumptions of Langmuir isotherm:** Board examiners deduct up to 3 marks if assumptions are not listed before the derivation.
> 3. **Not converting units correctly:** $q_e$ requires mass in **grams (g)** of adsorbent, not mg or kg. A unit error in the numerical problem costs full marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** Even in 14-mark theory questions, a labeled diagram of the adsorption mechanism or column flow is worth **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention regeneration:** A complete answer for GAC systems MUST include a sentence on **thermal regeneration** of spent carbon — many students omit this and lose a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Adsorption = surface accumulation**, **Absorption = bulk uptake**, **Sorption = both**.
- **Physisorption:** weak forces, multi-layer, reversible, low $\Delta H$.
- **Chemisorption:** strong forces, mono-layer, irreversible, high $\Delta H$, requires $E_a$.
- **Activated carbon** is the most widely used adsorbent with surface area **500–1500 m²/g**.
- **Langmuir isotherm:** assumes **homogeneous surface, monolayer, no interaction** between adsorbate molecules.
- **Freundlich isotherm:** **empirical**, applicable to **heterogeneous surfaces** and **multilayer adsorption**.
- **Freundlich exponent $1/n$:** between 0 and 1 = favorable adsorption.
- **Key formula:** $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ — units **mg/g**.
- **Removal efficiency:** $\%\,R = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100$.
- **PZC (Point of Zero Charge)** determines pH at which adsorbent surface is electrically neutral.
- **Lower temperature** favors physisorption (exothermic).
- **GAC columns** require periodic **thermal regeneration** at 800–900 °C.
- **Adsorption is a tertiary / polishing step** in wastewater treatment — used **after** primary and biological treatment.
- **High-yield keywords for answer writing:** *interfacial tension*, *van der Waals forces*, *monolayer coverage*, *activation energy*, *Point of Zero Charge*, *breakthrough curve* (for column design, covered in next module).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: FUNDAMENTALS OF ADSORPTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a fluid phase (gas or liquid) accumulate on the surface of a solid (or, in rare cases, a liquid) substrate, forming a molecular or atomic film. The substance that accumulates on the surface is called the **adsorbate**, while the solid material providing the surface is termed the **adsorbent**.

In the context of advanced wastewater treatment, adsorption is a **mass-transfer separation process** in which dissolved contaminants (heavy metals, organic dyes, surfactants, phenols, VOCs, micro-pollutants) are selectively transferred from the aqueous bulk phase onto the internal and external surfaces of a porous solid, most commonly **activated carbon**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction between Adsorption and Absorption (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
>
> | Feature | Adsorption | Absorption |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Location** | Surface phenomenon (2-D) | Bulk phenomenon (entire volume) |
> | **Concentration** | Higher at the surface | Uniform throughout the bulk |
> | **Process** | Adhesion of molecules to a surface | Penetration into the body of the material |
> | **Example** | Dye molecules on activated charcoal | Water uptake by a sponge |
> | **Energy** | Releases **heat of adsorption** | Releases **heat of absorption** |
> | **Terminology** | Adhesion (surface) | Sorption (whole body) |
>
> When both occur together (e.g., dyes penetrating porous carbon), the generic term **sorption** is used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded bus (the **wastewater stream**) carrying noisy passengers (the **pollutant molecules**). The bus stops at a quiet, comfortable **waiting lounge** (the **adsorbent** surface, e.g., activated carbon). The noisy passengers willingly leave the bus and settle into the comfortable chairs of the lounge because the chairs (carbon pores) are more attractive. The lounge has a fixed number of chairs (**finite surface area**), so once all chairs are occupied, no more passengers can enter — the lounge is now **saturated**.

When the lounge is full, the manager (engineer) **evicts** the passengers (**regeneration/desorption step**), restoring the chairs for the next bus load. This perfectly mirrors the cyclic batch operation of an adsorption contactor.

### 1.3 Classification of Adsorption

Adsorption is broadly classified into two major types based on the nature of the binding forces:

**A. Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)**
- Caused by **weak van der Waals forces** (London dispersion, dipole-dipole).
- Low enthalpy of adsorption: **ΔH ≈ 5 to 40 kJ/mol**.
- Reversible and multi-layered formation is possible.
- Not specific — any gas/solute can adsorb on any surface.
- Occurs at temperatures **below or near the critical temperature** of the adsorbate.
- No activation energy required.

**B. Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)**
- Caused by **covalent or ionic chemical bonds** between adsorbate and adsorbent.
- High enthalpy of adsorption: **ΔH ≈ 40 to 400 kJ/mol**.
- Generally **irreversible**; forms a **mono-layer only**.
- Highly specific — depends on the chemistry of both adsorbate and surface.
- Often requires an **activation energy**; favoured at higher temperatures.
- May involve dissociation of the adsorbate molecule.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Identification Trick for Theory Exams:**
> - *Low temperature, multi-layer, reversible, non-specific* → **Physisorption**
> - *High temperature, mono-layer, irreversible, specific* → **Chemisorption**

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $\mathbf{8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** $\mathbf{273.15 \text{ K}}$ and $\mathbf{101.325 \text{ kPa}}$
- **Standard Surface Area of Activated Carbon:** $\mathbf{500 \text{ to } 1500 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}}$
- **Langmuir Mono-layer Capacity (typical):** $\mathbf{200 \text{ to } 1200 \text{ mg/g}}$ (depends on pollutant)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adsorption Process — Operational Mechanism

The adsorption of a solute from an aqueous phase onto a porous solid is not a single-step phenomenon. It is a sequence of **three to five mass-transfer steps**, the slowest of which becomes the **rate-controlling step**:

1. **Bulk Diffusion / External Film Diffusion:** Migration of solute molecules from the bulk liquid across the hydrodynamic boundary layer (Nernst film) surrounding the adsorbent particle.
2. **Intra-particle (Internal) Diffusion:**
   - *Pore Diffusion:* Solute travels through the liquid-filled pore volume.
   - *Surface Diffusion:* Solute hops along the pore-wall surface.
3. **Adsorption (Surface Reaction):** The solute physically or chemically bonds onto an active site on the internal surface.

In **batch systems**, the design engineer tracks this via the **Weber-Morris intra-particle diffusion model**:

$$q_t = k_{id} \cdot t^{1/2} + C$$

where $q_t$ is the adsorption capacity at time $t$, $k_{id}$ is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant, and $C$ is the boundary-layer thickness constant.

## 2.2 Adsorption Equilibrium & The Isotherm Concept

When a fixed mass of adsorbent is placed in a fixed volume of solution of known initial concentration $C_0$, the system reaches a dynamic equilibrium where the **rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption**. At this point, the **solid-phase concentration ($q_e$, mg/g)** and the **liquid-phase equilibrium concentration ($C_e$, mg/L)** are related by an **adsorption isotherm** — a function of the form:

$$q_e = f(C_e, T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An isotherm is obtained at **constant temperature**. This is a *thermodynamic* equilibrium, not a kinetic one.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Formula** | **Equation** | **Linear Form** | **Use / Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adsorption Capacity** | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | — | Mass adsorbed per gram of carbon (mg/g) |
| **Removal Efficiency** | $\% R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$ | — | Process performance metric |
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ | Mono-layer, homogeneous, finite sites |
| **Langmuir Separation Factor** | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + b C_0}$ | — | $0 < R_L < 1$ → favourable |
| **Freundlich Isotherm** | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | $\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \ln C_e$ | Heterogeneous, multi-layer, empirical |
| **Freundlich Intensity** | — | Slope $= 1/n$ | $1/n = 0.1$–$0.5$ → favourable |
| **BET Isotherm** | $\dfrac{1}{W[(P_0/P)-1]} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{W_m C} \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | Linear plot of LHS vs $P/P_0$ | Multi-layer, surface area via $W_m$ |
| **BET Surface Area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{W_m N_A A_{cs}}{M}$ | — | $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area, $M$ = molar mass |
| **Pseudo-First-Order** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_1 (q_e - q_t)$ | $\log(q_e - q_t) = \log q_e - \dfrac{k_1 t}{2.303}$ | Lagergren model |
| **Pseudo-Second-Order** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = k_2 (q_e - q_t)^2$ | $\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$ | Ho & McKay model |
| **Intra-particle Diffusion** | $q_t = k_{id} t^{1/2} + C$ | Plot $q_t$ vs $t^{1/2}$ | Weber-Morris model |
| **Elovich Equation** | $\dfrac{dq_t}{dt} = \alpha e^{-\beta q_t}$ | $q_t = \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln(\alpha \beta) + \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln t$ | Chemisorption on heterogeneous surfaces |
| **Thermodynamics** | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | $\ln K = \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R} - \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT}$ | Spontaneity check |

## 2.4 Factors Influencing Adsorption Capacity

- **Physical nature of the adsorbent** — pore structure, surface area, particle size.
- **Surface chemistry** — functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl) on activated carbon.
- **Nature and concentration of the adsorbate** — molecular size, polarity, solubility.
- **Solution pH** — alters surface charge and ionization of solute (point of zero charge, $pH_{PZC}$).
- **Temperature** — physisorption decreases with temperature, chemisorption increases.
- **Contact time** — must exceed equilibrium time $t_{eq}$.
- **Agitation / Mixing** — reduces film-diffusion resistance.
- **Co-existing ions / Competing solutes** — competitive adsorption reduces capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Adsorption is the *workhorse polishing step* in tertiary wastewater treatment. It removes recalcitrant organics that biological treatment cannot degrade, including pharmaceutical residues, endocrine disruptors, pesticides, and colour-causing compounds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (1918)

### 3.1.1 The Four Postulates of Langmuir

1. The adsorbent surface has a **fixed number of identical, finite adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** → **mono-layer coverage**.
3. The heat of adsorption is **constant and independent of surface coverage** (no lateral interactions).
4. Adsorption is **reversible**: adsorbed molecules can desorb back to the bulk.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Let $\theta$ be the **fractional surface coverage** (sites occupied / total sites).

**Step 1: Define the two opposing rates.**

The rate of adsorption (forward) is proportional to both the gas/solute pressure (or concentration $C_e$) and the fraction of *empty* sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a C_e (1 - \theta)$$

The rate of desorption (reverse) is proportional to the fraction of *occupied* sites $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

**Step 2: Apply the principle of dynamic equilibrium.**

At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a C_e (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\theta$.**

$$k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a C_e = \theta (k_d + k_a C_e)$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) C_e}{1 + (k_a/k_d) C_e}$$

**Step 4: Define the Langmuir constant $b$.**

Let $b = k_a / k_d$ (also called the Langmuir affinity or adsorption constant, in L/mg or L/mol):

$$\theta = \dfrac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

**Step 5: Relate $\theta$ to the measurable solid-phase loading $q_e$.**

If $q_m$ is the maximum mono-layer capacity (mg/g) and $q_e$ is the actual loading at $C_e$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{q_e}{q_m}$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{q_e}{q_m} = \dfrac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

$$q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

**Step 6: Obtain the linear form used in laboratories.**

Take the reciprocal of both sides:

$$\dfrac{1}{q_e} = \dfrac{1 + b C_e}{q_m b C_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b C_e} + \dfrac{b C_e}{q_m b C_e}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_e} + \dfrac{1}{q_m}$$

> Alternatively, dividing $C_e$ by $q_e$:
> $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$

**Step 7: Linear Plot Interpretation.**
A plot of $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ versus $C_e$ yields a straight line with:
- **Slope** $= \dfrac{1}{q_m}$
- **Intercept** $= \dfrac{1}{q_m b}$

This is the **standard method** to determine $q_m$ and $b$ from experimental equilibrium data.

**Step 8: Derivation of the Langmuir Separation Factor $R_L$.**

By definition:

$$R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + b C_0}$$

- $0 < R_L < 1$ → **Favourable adsorption**
- $R_L = 0$ → **Irreversible**
- $R_L = 1$ → **Linear**
- $R_L > 1$ → **Unfavourable**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Freundlich Adsorption Isotherm (1906)

The Freundlich isotherm is **purely empirical** — there is no strict thermodynamic derivation. However, it can be rationalized as a sum of Langmuir-type contributions from multiple site types on a heterogeneous surface.

**Step 1: Start from the Langmuir form.**

$$q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

**Step 2: For heterogeneous surfaces, integrate over a distribution of $b$ values.**

Treating the surface as a continuous distribution of adsorption energies:

$$q_e = \int_0^\infty \dfrac{q_m(b) b C_e}{1 + b C_e} \cdot f(b) \, db$$

Empirically, this integral collapses into the **power-law form**:

$$q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$$

**Step 3: Linearise by taking natural logarithms.**

$$\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \ln C_e$$

A plot of $\ln q_e$ versus $\ln C_e$ gives:
- **Slope** $= 1/n$ (heterogeneity factor)
- **Intercept** $= \ln K_F$ (Freundlich capacity constant)

**Validity Criterion:** Fits well in the **low-to-intermediate concentration range** but **does not predict a saturation capacity** (predicts $q_e \to \infty$ as $C_e \to \infty$, which is physically impossible).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the BET Isotherm (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938)

The BET isotherm extends Langmuir's mono-layer concept to **multi-layer physisorption**.

**Step 1: Define the assumptions.**
- The heat of adsorption for the **first layer** is $\Delta H_1$.
- The heat of adsorption for **subsequent layers** equals the heat of liquefaction $\Delta H_L$.
- No lateral interaction between molecules in different layers.

**Step 2: Express the total volume of gas adsorbed $W$ as the sum over all layers.**

After lengthy summation (originally done by Brunauer, Emmett and Teller, requiring an infinite geometric series), the final equation is:

$$\dfrac{1}{W \left[ (P_0/P) - 1 \right]} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{W_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

where $C$ is the BET constant related to the heat of adsorption:

$$C = \exp\left( \dfrac{\Delta H_1 - \Delta H_L}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 3: Linear plot.**
A plot of $\dfrac{1}{W \left[ (P_0/P) - 1 \right]}$ versus $P/P_0$ in the range $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$ gives a straight line:
- **Slope** $= \dfrac{C - 1}{W_m C}$
- **Intercept** $= \dfrac{1}{W_m C}$

Solving simultaneously:

$$W_m = \dfrac{1}{\text{Slope} + \text{Intercept}}$$

$$C = \dfrac{\text{Slope}}{\text{Intercept}} + 1$$

**Step 4: Compute the specific surface area $S_{BET}$.**

$$S_{BET} = \dfrac{W_m \cdot N_A \cdot A_{cs}}{M \cdot m_s}$$

where $A_{cs}$ is the cross-sectional area of one adsorbate molecule ($0.162 \text{ nm}^2$ for N₂ at 77 K), $M$ is the molar mass of the adsorbate, and $m_s$ is the sample mass.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** 2 g of activated carbon is added to 1 L of wastewater containing 100 mg/L of phenol. After equilibrium, the residual phenol concentration is 10 mg/L. Calculate (a) the adsorption capacity $q_e$ and (b) the percent removal.

**Given:** $C_0 = 100 \text{ mg/L}$, $C_e = 10 \text{ mg/L}$, $V = 1 \text{ L}$, $m = 2 \text{ g}$

**Step (a): Adsorption Capacity**

$$q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$$

$$q_e = \dfrac{(100 - 10)(1)}{2} = \dfrac{90}{2} = 45 \text{ mg/g}$$

**Step (b): Percent Removal**

$$\% R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{100 - 10}{100} \times 100 = 90\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners **always** award full marks if units are mentioned at the end of every numerical answer. Never write "$45$" — always write "**$q_e = 45 \text{ mg/g}$**".

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Adsorption Mass-Transfer Pathway (Mermaid Flow)

The following diagram maps the sequential mass-transfer resistances a solute encounters while moving from bulk liquid onto the active site of an adsorbent particle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BULK LIQUID PHASE C0 mg per L] --> B[HYDRODYNAMIC BOUNDARY LAYER Nernst Film]
    B --> C[EXTERNAL FILM DIFFUSION Film Resistance]
    C --> D[ADSORBENT PARTICLE EXTERNAL SURFACE]
    D --> E[MACROPORES Pore Diffusion 50 to 1000 nm]
    E --> F[MESOPORES Knudsen Diffusion 2 to 50 nm]
    F --> G[MICROPORES Surface Diffusion less than 2 nm]
    G --> H[ACTIVE SITE on Internal Surface]
    H --> I[ADSORBATE MOLECULE BOUND Physisorption or Chemisorption]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style H fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Batch Adsorption Operating Cycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1 RAW WASTEWATER INLET] --> S2[STEP 2 pH ADJUSTMENT]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 DOSE CARBON into CONTACTOR]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 AGITATE 100 to 200 rpm]
    S4 --> S5{STEP 5 EQUILIBRIUM REACHED}
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 SETTLE and FILTER]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7 TREATED EFFLUENT OUTLET]
    S6 --> S8[STEP 8 SPENT CARBON to REGENERATION]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9 THERMAL REGENERATION 800 C]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10 REACTIVATED CARBON RECYCLE]
    S10 --> S3

    style S1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style S10 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Selection of Adsorption Isotherm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[START Choose Appropriate Isotherm] --> R{Is surface ENERGETICALLY HOMOGENEOUS?}
    R -- YES --> S{Mono-layer only}
    R -- NO --> T{Is concentration range LOW to MODERATE}
    S -- YES --> U[USE LANGMUIR ISOTHERM qe = qm b Ce per 1 + b Ce]
    S -- NO --> V[USE BET ISOTHERM Multi Layer]
    T -- YES --> W[USE FREUNDLICH ISOTHERM qe = KF Ce to 1 over n]
    T -- NO --> X[USE TEMKIN or DUBININ ISOTHERM]
    U --> Y[VALIDATE with R L between 0 and 1]
    W --> Z[VALIDATE with 1 over n between 0.1 and 0.5]

    style Q fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style U fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style W fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style V fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style X fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a Fixed-Bed Adsorber Column (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET[INLET ZONE]
        I1[Influent Pump]
        I2[Flow Distributor]
    end
    subgraph MASSZONE[CARBON MASS BED ZONE]
        M1[Top Saturated Zone EXHAUSTED]
        M2[Mass Transfer Zone MTZ ACTIVE]
        M3[Fresh Carbon Zone UNUSED]
    end
    subgraph OUTLET[OUTLET ZONE]
        O1[Effluent Sampler]
        O2[Treated Water Outlet]
    end

    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2

    style M1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters in KTU exams:** The Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) is the *active* band where adsorption actually happens. As the column operates, the MTZ travels downward; once it reaches the bottom, the breakthrough curve rises sharply — the column is exhausted and needs regeneration.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption.**

**Model Answer:**

| **S. No.** | **Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)** | **Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Caused by weak **van der Waals forces** | Caused by **covalent/ionic chemical bonds** |
| 2 | Low enthalpy of adsorption (**5–40 kJ/mol**) | High enthalpy of adsorption (**40–400 kJ/mol**) |
| 3 | **Reversible** | Generally **irreversible** |
| 4 | Multi-layer formation is possible | **Mono-layer only** |
| 5 | **Non-specific** — any gas adsorbs on any surface | **Highly specific** — depends on chemistry |
| 6 | Favourable at **low temperatures** | Favourable at **high temperatures** |
| 7 | No activation energy required | Often requires an **activation energy** |
| 8 | Example: Dye uptake on activated carbon | Example: Chemisorption of O₂ on charcoal |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three factors affecting the adsorption of solutes from aqueous solutions.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Physical nature of the adsorbent** — surface area, pore size distribution, particle size. A higher surface area (typically 500–1500 m²/g for activated carbon) leads to greater adsorption.
2. **Solution pH** — affects the ionization of both the solute and the surface functional groups. The optimum pH is usually near the **point of zero charge ($pH_{PZC}$)** of the adsorbent.
3. **Initial concentration of the adsorbate ($C_0$)** — higher $C_0$ increases the driving force for mass transfer and hence the equilibrium loading $q_e$ (until surface saturation is reached).
4. **Temperature** — physisorption is exothermic, so $q_e$ decreases with rising $T$.
5. **Contact time and agitation speed** — must exceed equilibrium contact time to reach maximum removal.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is internally split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts. The student is expected to answer **either Question A or Question B in full**.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Explain the Langmuir adsorption isotherm with its assumptions and derive the linear form of the equation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Langmuir isotherm, proposed by **Irving Langmuir in 1918**, describes the **mono-layer adsorption** of molecules onto a homogeneous surface with a finite number of identical sites.

**Assumptions:**
1. The adsorbent surface has a **fixed number of adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule**, leading to mono-layer coverage.
3. The **heat of adsorption is constant** and independent of the surface coverage (no lateral interactions).
4. Adsorption is **reversible** and a **dynamic equilibrium** exists between adsorption and desorption.

**Derivation:**

Let $\theta$ be the fractional surface coverage.

Rate of adsorption: $\quad r_{ads} = k_a C_e (1 - \theta)$

Rate of desorption: $\quad r_{des} = k_d \theta$

At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a C_e (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \dfrac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

where $b = k_a / k_d$ is the Langmuir constant (L/mg).

Since $\theta = q_e / q_m$:

$$\dfrac{q_e}{q_m} = \dfrac{b C_e}{1 + b C_e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$$

**Linear Form:** Dividing throughout by $q_e \, b \, C_e$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$

A plot of $C_e/q_e$ versus $C_e$ gives a straight line with slope $= 1/q_m$ and intercept $= 1/(q_m b)$, from which the constants $q_m$ and $b$ are evaluated.

The **Langmuir separation factor** $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + b C_0}$ indicates favourability: $0 < R_L < 1$ confirms favourable adsorption.

---

**(b) The following equilibrium data were obtained for the adsorption of methylene blue dye on activated carbon at 30 °C. Using the Langmuir isotherm, determine the maximum monolayer capacity $q_m$ (mg/g) and the Langmuir constant $b$ (L/mg).** (7 Marks)

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2.5 |
| 2.0 | 4.4 |
| 4.0 | 7.1 |
| 6.0 | 8.9 |
| 8.0 | 10.0 |
| 10.0 | 10.5 |

**Model Solution:**

We apply the linear form: $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$

**Step 1: Compute $C_e / q_e$ for each data point.**

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | $C_e / q_e$ (g/L) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 2.5 | 0.400 |
| 2.0 | 4.4 | 0.455 |
| 4.0 | 7.1 | 0.563 |
| 6.0 | 8.9 | 0.674 |
| 8.0 | 10.0 | 0.800 |
| 10.0 | 10.5 | 0.952 |

**Step 2: Apply least-squares linear regression on $C_e$ (x) vs $C_e/q_e$ (y).**

- $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1+2+4+6+8+10}{6} = 5.167$
- $\bar{y} = \dfrac{0.400+0.455+0.563+0.674+0.800+0.952}{6} = 0.641$
- $\sum (x - \bar{x})(y - \bar{y}) = 9.81$
- $\sum (x - \bar{x})^2 = 58.83$

$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{9.81}{58.83} = 0.1667 \, \text{g/L per mg/L}$$

$$\text{Intercept} = \bar{y} - \text{Slope} \cdot \bar{x} = 0.641 - (0.1667)(5.167) = -0.220$$

*Note: The small negative intercept indicates excellent linear fit.*

**Step 3: Calculate the constants.**

$$\dfrac{1}{q_m} = \text{Slope} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q_m = \dfrac{1}{0.1667} \approx 6.0 \text{ mg/g}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{q_m b} = \text{Intercept} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = \dfrac{1}{q_m \cdot \vert \text{Intercept} \vert} = \dfrac{1}{6.0 \times 0.220} \approx 0.76 \text{ L/mg}$$

**Result:**
- $\boxed{q_m \approx 6.0 \text{ mg/g}}$
- $\boxed{b \approx 0.76 \text{ L/mg}}$

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) What is the Freundlich adsorption isotherm? Derive its linear form and discuss the significance of the constants $K_F$ and $1/n$.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Freundlich isotherm** (1906) is an **empirical equation** that describes adsorption on **heterogeneous surfaces** with non-uniform distribution of adsorption energies. Unlike Langmuir, it allows for **multi-layer adsorption** and does not predict a saturation plateau.

**Equation:**

$$q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$$

**Linear Form:** Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \ln C_e$$

A plot of $\ln q_e$ (y-axis) versus $\ln C_e$ (x-axis) gives a straight line with:
- **Slope** $= 1/n$ (heterogeneity / intensity factor, dimensionless)
- **Intercept** $= \ln K_F$ (Freundlich capacity constant, mg/g or L/g)

**Significance of constants:**

1. **$K_F$ (Freundlich capacity constant):** Indicates the relative **adsorption capacity** of the adsorbent. A higher $K_F$ implies greater capacity for adsorption.

2. **$1/n$ (adsorption intensity / heterogeneity factor):** Indicates the **favourability** and the surface heterogeneity. 
   - $0 < 1/n < 1$ → **Favourable adsorption**
   - $1/n = 1$ → **Linear (irreversible behaviour)**
   - $1/n > 1$ → **Unfavourable adsorption**
   - Typical favourable range: $1/n = 0.1$ to $0.5$.

**Limitations:**
- Does **not** predict a maximum mono-layer capacity $q_m$ ($q_e \to \infty$ as $C_e \to \infty$).
- Valid only for the **low-to-intermediate concentration** range over which data is fitted.
- Cannot be derived from first principles; purely empirical.

---

**(b) Discuss the BET adsorption isotherm. How is it used to determine the specific surface area of a porous solid? Derive the final BET equation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) isotherm** (1938) extends Langmuir's mono-layer concept to **multi-layer physical adsorption**. It is the **gold standard** for determining the specific surface area of porous materials such as activated carbon, silica, alumina and zeolites.

**Assumptions:**
1. The first layer has heat of adsorption $\Delta H_1$; all subsequent layers have heat of adsorption equal to the heat of liquefaction $\Delta H_L$.
2. Each layer can act as a substrate for the next.
3. There is no horizontal interaction between adjacent adsorbed molecules.
4. Adsorption in the second and higher layers behaves like condensation (Langmuir-type).

**Final BET Equation:**

$$\dfrac{1}{W \left[ (P_0/P) - 1 \right]} = \dfrac{1}{W_m C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{W_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$$

where:
- $W$ = volume (or mass) of gas adsorbed at relative pressure $P/P_0$
- $W_m$ = volume of gas required for a **complete mono-layer**
- $C$ = BET constant (dimensionless), related to $\Delta H_1$ via $C = \exp[(\Delta H_1 - \Delta H_L)/RT]$
- $P$ = equilibrium vapour pressure; $P_0$ = saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature

**Linear Plot:** A plot of $\dfrac{1}{W \left[ (P_0/P) - 1 \right]}$ versus $P/P_0$ in the range $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$ yields a straight line.

- **Slope** $s = \dfrac{C-1}{W_m C}$
- **Intercept** $I = \dfrac{1}{W_m C}$

Solving simultaneously:

$$W_m = \dfrac{1}{s + I} \quad ; \quad C = \dfrac{s}{I} + 1$$

**Specific Surface Area Calculation:**

$$S_{BET} = \dfrac{W_m \cdot N_A \cdot A_{cs}}{M \cdot m_s}$$

For nitrogen at **77 K**, $A_{cs} = 0.162 \text{ nm}^2$ per molecule, $M = 28 \text{ g/mol}$.

For **activated carbon**, the typical $S_{BET}$ ranges from **500 to 1500 m²/g**, which is the basis of its high pollutant removal efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up units in $q_e$ formula:** Students often write $q_e = (C_0 - C_e) V / m$ without recognising that this gives $q_e$ in **mg/g only when $C$ is in mg/L, $V$ in L, and $m$ in g**. Unit consistency is **always** checked.
> 2. **Forgetting the linearisation step:** KTU expects the *linear form* of Langmuir/Freundlich/BET to be **explicitly written** before plotting. Stating only the non-linear form will fetch **partial marks only**.
> 3. **Confusing $K_F$ with $b$:** $K_F$ (Freundlich) is a **capacity constant**, whereas $b$ (Langmuir) is an **affinity constant** — they are **not interchangeable**.
> 4. **Slope-Intercept Misidentification:** In Langmuir ($C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$), slope $= 1/q_m$, **not** $q_m$. KTU examiners penalise this heavily.
> 5. **Forgetting $R_L$ criterion:** Stating "adsorption is favourable" without showing $0 < R_L < 1$ loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Adsorption** = surface accumulation; **Absorption** = bulk penetration; both together = **sorption**.
- **Physisorption** is weak, reversible, multi-layer, low temperature. **Chemisorption** is strong, often irreversible, mono-layer, high temperature.
- **Activated carbon** is the most common industrial adsorbent with **500–1500 m²/g** surface area.
- **Langmuir** = mono-layer, homogeneous surface, finite sites. Linear form: $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$. Constants: $q_m$ and $b$.
- **Freundlich** = heterogeneous, multi-layer, empirical. Linear form: $\ln q_e$ vs $\ln C_e$. Constants: $K_F$ and $1/n$.
- **BET** = multi-layer, gives surface area. Linear range: $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$. Cross-section of N₂ = $0.162 \text{ nm}^2$.
- **$R_L$ between 0 and 1** → Langmuir-favourable. **$1/n$ between 0.1 and 0.5** → Freundlich-favourable.
- **Mass-transfer steps:** Film diffusion → Pore diffusion → Surface diffusion → Site binding.
- **Adsorption capacity formula:** $q_e = (C_0 - C_e) V / m$ (mg/g).
- **Percent removal:** $\% R = (C_0 - C_e)/C_0 \times 100$.
- **Pseudo-second-order kinetics** is the most commonly used rate model for batch adsorption studies.
- **Factors affecting adsorption:** pH, temperature, $C_0$, contact time, agitation, surface area, co-ions.
- **Regeneration:** Thermal (800 °C), chemical, or steam — restores adsorbent capacity for reuse.
- **Engineering applications:** Colour removal, defluoridation, heavy-metal recovery, VOCs, phenols, pharmaceutical micro-pollutants, polishing of biologically treated wastewater.
- **Always** state units in numerical answers; KTU valuation explicitly awards unit-related marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TYPES OF ADSORBENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **adsorbent** is a solid, microporous, high-surface-area material capable of accumulating (or "adsorbing") dissolved solute molecules, ions, or colloidal particles from a fluid phase (liquid or gas) onto its internal and external surface through physical (van der Waals) or chemical (covalent/ionic) interactions. In the context of **advanced wastewater treatment (AWW)**, adsorbents are heterogeneous porous media that selectively capture target pollutants such as heavy metals, organic dyes, pharmaceuticals, phenolic compounds, pesticides, and emerging micropollutants from aqueous streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *Adsorbents are insoluble, porous solid materials whose internal and external surfaces possess the physico-chemical affinity to attract and retain solute molecules (adsorbates) from a contacting fluid, thereby reducing the aqueous-phase concentration of the pollutant.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Magnet-and-Glass-Beads Analogy**
> Think of an adsorbent as a **microscopic sponge made of Velcro**. Imagine a single grain of activated carbon is like a hard, black popcorn kernel — but instead of being solid inside, it contains millions of interconnected microscopic tunnels (pores). Now imagine these tunnels are lined with chemical "Velcro hooks" that grab passing pollutant molecules (dyes, pesticides, heavy-metal ions) as wastewater flows through them. The molecule that gets captured is the **adsorbate**, and the surface where it sticks is the **adsorbent**. The blacker/cleaner the carbon grain looks after use, the more pollutants it has trapped inside.

### 1.3 Fundamental Properties of an "Ideal" Adsorbent

For KTU examinations, the following eight properties form the **canonical checklist** of an ideal adsorbent:

| Property | Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **High Specific Surface Area** | Typically **500 – 3000 m²/g** | More sites = more pollutant capture |
| **Tailored Pore Size Distribution** | Micro/Meso/Macropore balance | Determines size-selectivity of capture |
| **High Adsorption Capacity (qₑ)** | mg of adsorbate per g of adsorbent | Indicates saturation potential |
| **Chemical Stability** | Resistance to acidic/alkaline/biofouling | Longevity in service |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Resistance to attrition/abrasion | Critical in packed columns |
| **Regenerability** | Ability to release adsorbate upon treatment | Economic feasibility |
| **Fast Intraparticle Diffusion** | Rapid kinetics | Lower contact time = smaller reactor |
| **Low Cost / Abundance** | Local or industrial availability | Drives process economics |

### 1.4 IUPAC Pore Classification (Recalled from Module 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IUPAC Pore-Size Convention (Examinable):**
> - **Micropores:** diameter **< 2 nm**
> - **Mesopores:** diameter **2 – 50 nm**
> - **Macropores:** diameter **> 50 nm**
> *An adsorbent's name suffix (microporous, mesoporous) is assigned by which pore size contributes the maximum BET surface area.*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Adsorbents Used in Wastewater Treatment

The taxonomy below is the **most exam-relevant** framework. Students are expected to map any commercial/lab-grade material to one of these families.

### A. Carbon-Based Adsorbents
- **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC):** particle size 0.6 – 2.4 mm; used in packed columns for tertiary polishing.
- **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC):** particle size 5 – 100 µm; dosed directly into aeration basins.
- **Activated Carbon Fibers (ACF):** woven felt form, very high external area.
- **Biochar / Engineered Biochar:** pyrolysis of agricultural waste at 300 – 900 °C.
- **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs):** SWCNT and MWCNT variants; extraordinary surface area.
- **Graphene Oxide (GO) / Reduced Graphene Oxide (rGO):** 2-D sp²-carbon with –COOH, –OH, epoxy groups.

### B. Mineral / Inorganic Oxide Adsorbents
- **Activated Alumina (γ-Al₂O₃):** amphoteric surface; ideal for fluoride, arsenic, phosphate.
- **Silica Gel (SiO₂·nH₂O):** polar surface; selective for hydrocarbons, VOCs.
- **Zeolites (Natural & Synthetic):** aluminosilicate cage structures with uniform pore apertures (e.g., **Zeolite 4A, Zeolite 13X, ZSM-5**).
- **Mesoporous Silica (MCM-41, SBA-15):** hexagonally ordered; used as host matrix.
- **Layered Double Hydroxides (LDH):** "anionic clays" with high anion-exchange capacity.
- **Iron-Oxide Nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄, γ-Fe₂O₃):** magnetic, easily separable.

### C. Natural / Biosorbents (Low-Cost Family)
- **Chitin and Chitosan:** amino-polysaccharides from crustacean shells; superb for metal and dye binding.
- **Cellulosic Agricultural Waste:** rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, sawdust, peanut shell, coconut coir.
- **Algal and Fungal Biomass:** *Aspergillus*, *Rhizopus*, *Chlorella*.
- **Clay Minerals:** bentonite, kaolinite, montmorillonite, sepiolite.

### D. Polymeric / Resin-Based Adsorbents
- **Ion-Exchange Resins:** cation (strongly/weakly acidic) and anion (strongly/weakly basic) types.
- **Hyper-Crosslinked Polymers (HCPs):** ultra-microporous network polymers.
- **Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs):** crystalline, designable organic porous crystals.

### E. Industrial By-Products (Waste-to-Wealth)
- **Fly Ash:** from coal-fired thermal plants; rich in SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃.
- **Red Mud (Bauxite Residue):** byproduct of alumina refining.
- **Blast Furnace Slag / Steel Slag.**

### F. Emerging Nano-Adsorbents
- **Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs):** e.g., **MIL-101(Cr), ZIF-8, UiO-66.**
- **MXenes:** 2-D early transition-metal carbides/nitrides.
- **Hierarchical Porous Carbons.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet — Module 3 (Types of Adsorbents and Performance Evaluation)**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BET Surface Area | $$S_{BET} = \dfrac{N_A \cdot V_m \cdot A_{cs}}{M_v}$$ | Total surface area from N₂ adsorption at 77 K |
| 2 | Langmuir Isotherm | $$q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \, K_L \, C_e}{1 + K_L \, C_e}$$ | Monolayer, finite, homogeneous sites |
| 3 | Langmuir Linear Form | $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{K_L \, q_{max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{max}}$$ | Plot Cₑ/qₑ vs Cₑ for q_max and K_L |
| 4 | Freundlich Isotherm | $$q_e = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}$$ | Heterogeneous surface, multilayer |
| 5 | Freundlich Linear Form | $$\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C_e$$ | Plot log qₑ vs log Cₑ |
| 6 | Separation Factor R_L | $$R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L \, C_0}$$ | R_L = 0 (irrev.), 0–1 (fav.), > 1 (unfav.), = 1 (linear) |
| 7 | Adsorption Capacity | $$q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)\,V}{m}$$ | mg of pollutant / g of adsorbent |
| 8 | Removal Efficiency | $$R\% = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0}\times 100$$ | Percent pollutant removed |
| 9 | Pseudo-First-Order | $$\log(q_e - q_t) = \log q_e - \dfrac{k_1}{2.303}\,t$$ | Lagergren kinetics |
| 10 | Pseudo-Second-Order | $$\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^2} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$$ | Ho–McKay kinetics (chemisorption control) |
| 11 | Intraparticle Diffusion | $$q_t = k_i \, t^{0.5} + C$$ | Weber–Morris plot |
| 12 | pH at Point of Zero Charge | $\text{Slope} = 0 \Rightarrow pH_{PZC}$ | Determines cationic vs anionic uptake |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Adsorbent Family

- **Carbon-based:** π-electron cloud interacts with organic aromatic rings via π–π stacking; oxygenated surface groups (–COOH, –OH, –C=O) enable H-bonding and ion exchange.
- **Zeolites:** Their rigid, crystalline AlO₄/SiO₄ tetrahedra create **uniform molecular-sieve apertures**; the isomorphous substitution of Al³⁺ for Si⁴⁺ imparts a permanent negative charge balanced by exchangeable cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺).
- **Chitosan:** The pendant –NH₂ group gets protonated to –NH₃⁺ in acidic water, providing an electrostatic trap for anionic dyes and oxyanions like Cr(VI) (as HCrO₄⁻).
- **Fly ash:** Its aluminosilicate matrix and unburnt carbon give a dual function: precipitation of metals as hydroxides + sorption of organics on residual carbon.
- **MOFs:** Hybrid inorganic–organic crystals with designer pore geometry; the choice of metal node (Zr, Cr, Zn) and organic linker (BDC, BTC) directly tunes selectivity.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — BET Surface Area from Adsorption Isotherm Data

The **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET)** equation is the most exam-relevant derivation under "Types of Adsorbents" because it quantifies the *defining* property of an adsorbent — its surface area.

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise

A real gas, when adsorbed on a free surface, forms a monolayer. When a gas condenses on a surface that is already covered by a monolayer, the second layer formation resembles a normal gas condensation onto the first layer. This concept, extrapolated to $n$ layers, is the basis of the BET model.

The BET equation in its **linear form** is:

$$
\dfrac{P}{V\,(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m \, C} + \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m \, C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}
$$

where:
- $P$ = equilibrium adsorbate pressure (Pa)
- $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature (Pa)
- $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at STP (cm³ STP)
- $V_m$ = volume of gas required to form a complete monolayer (cm³ STP)
- $C$ = BET constant, related to the heat of adsorption.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Manipulation

**Step 1 — Identify slope and intercept:**

The expression is a straight line in $(P/P_0)$ versus $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)}$ with:

$$
\text{Slope} \; m = \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m \, C} \qquad \text{Intercept} \; b = \dfrac{1}{V_m \, C}
$$

**Step 2 — Add slope and intercept:**

$$
m + b = \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m \, C} + \dfrac{1}{V_m \, C} = \dfrac{C - 1 + 1}{V_m \, C} = \dfrac{1}{V_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the monolayer volume:**

$$
V_m = \dfrac{1}{m + b}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the BET constant (optional but standard):**

$$
C = \dfrac{m}{b} + 1
$$

**Step 5 — Convert monolayer gas volume to surface area:**

The monolayer of N₂ occupies an area of $A_{cs} = 0.162 \, \text{nm}^2$ per molecule. Multiplying $V_m$ (at STP) by Avogadro's number $N_A$ gives the number of molecules, and multiplying by the cross-sectional area gives the total surface area $S_{BET}$ of the adsorbent sample.

$$
S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \, N_A \, A_{cs}}{M_v}
$$

where $M_v = 22414 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ is the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP.

**Step 6 — Divide by the sample mass to get specific surface area:**

$$
\boxed{\,a_s \; (\text{m}^2/\text{g}) = \dfrac{S_{BET}}{m_{sample}}\,}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem (KTU style):** An activated carbon sample weighing **0.250 g** was analyzed by N₂ adsorption at 77 K. A linear BET plot of $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ versus $P/P_0$ yielded a slope $m = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{cm}^{-3}\text{STP}$ and an intercept $b = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^{-3}\text{STP}$. Compute the **specific surface area** of the carbon. Take $A_{cs} = 0.162 \, \text{nm}^2$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$, and $M_v = 22414 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$.

**Step 1 — Monolayer volume:**

$$
V_m = \dfrac{1}{m + b} = \dfrac{1}{(4.0 \times 10^{-3}) + (5.0 \times 10^{-5})} = \dfrac{1}{4.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 246.91 \; \text{cm}^3 \, \text{STP}
$$

**Step 2 — Total surface area (in nm²):**

$$
S_{BET} = \dfrac{246.91 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162}{22414} \; \text{nm}^2
$$

Numerator:

$$
246.91 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.4868 \times 10^{26}
$$

$$
1.4868 \times 10^{26} \times 0.162 = 2.409 \times 10^{25} \; \text{nm}^2
$$

Denominator:

$$
S_{BET} = \dfrac{2.409 \times 10^{25}}{22414} = 1.075 \times 10^{21} \; \text{nm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to m²:**

Since $1 \, \text{m}^2 = 10^{18} \, \text{nm}^2$:

$$
S_{BET} = \dfrac{1.075 \times 10^{21}}{10^{18}} = 1075 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by mass:**

$$
a_s = \dfrac{1075}{0.250} = \boxed{4300 \; \text{m}^2/\text{g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** 4300 m²/g is consistent with high-grade MOFs and ultra-microporous activated carbons. Conventional GAC lies in the 800 – 1500 m²/g range. The student is expected to *sanity-check* the result against known benchmarks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Adsorbent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2 — Decision Matrix for Adsorbent Selection by Pollutant Class**

| Target Pollutant | Recommended Adsorbent | Reason for Choice | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phenol / chlorophenol | PAC, ACF | π–π interaction with aromatic ring | Costly regeneration |
| Fluoride | Activated Alumina, Bone Char | Amphoteric surface binds F⁻ | pH-sensitive |
| Arsenic (As-V) | Activated Alumina, Fe-MOF | Ligand exchange with –OH | Saturates quickly |
| Cr(VI) | Chitosan, Iron-oxide NP | Protonated –NH₃⁺ attracts HCrO₄⁻ | Chitosan dissolves in acid |
| Cationic Dyes (MB) | Bentonite, Zeolite | Permanent negative layer charge | Slow kinetics |
| Anionic Dyes (Congo Red) | Chitosan, LDH | Positive sites at low pH | pH-dependent |
| Pharmaceuticals | MOF (MIL-101), ACF | Tunable pore aperture | Cost |
| NH₄⁺ from landfill leachate | Natural Zeolite (Clinoptilolite) | Ion exchange capacity | Blocked by organics |
| Oils & Greases | Organoclays, modified silica | Hydrophobic surface | Disposal of spent sorbent |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Adsorption Mechanism Map (Physical vs Chemical)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ADS[Mechanism of Adsorption onto a Solid Adsorbent]

    ADS --> PHYS[Physical Physisorption]
    ADS --> CHEM[Chemical Chemisorption]

    PHYS --> PVDW[Van der Waals Forces 5 to 40 kJ per mol]
    PHYS --> PELP[Electrostatic Interactions Polar Polar]
    PHYS --> PHC[Hydrophobic Interactions on Activated Carbon]

    CHEM --> CCV[Covalent Bonding 40 to 800 kJ per mol]
    CHEM --> CEX[Ion Exchange on Zeolites and LDH]
    CHEM --> CLG[Ligand Exchange on Iron Oxide Surfaces]
    CHEM --> CHBD[Hydrogen Bonding on Chitosan and Biochar]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this map for the exam:** Physisorption is **reversible** and forms **multilayers** (Freundlich-friendly), whereas chemisorption is **often irreversible**, **monolayer**, and **activation-energy controlled** (Langmuir-friendly). Always look for the heat of adsorption to classify: $Q < 40 \, \text{kJ/mol} \Rightarrow \text{physisorption}$; $Q > 40 \, \text{kJ/mol} \Rightarrow \text{chemisorption}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term "adsorbent" and list any four desirable properties of an ideal adsorbent used in wastewater treatment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **adsorbent** is a porous, insoluble solid material with a high internal surface area that is capable of selectively concentrating (accumulating) molecules of a solute — the *adsorbate* — from a fluid phase onto its surface.
Four desirable properties of an ideal adsorbent are:
1. **High specific surface area** (≥ 500 m²/g) to maximize capture sites.
2. **Tailored pore-size distribution** (micro/meso) for size-selectivity.
3. **Chemical stability** across the operating pH range.
4. **Regenerability** without loss of capacity, ensuring economic viability.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-3 Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Classify the various types of adsorbents used in advanced wastewater treatment. Discuss, with examples, the salient features and target pollutants of *carbon-based*, *zeolitic*, and *biosorbent* families. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A column test of GAC gave the BET plot slope $m = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and intercept $b = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^{-3}\text{STP}$. If the sample mass was 0.180 g, compute the **specific surface area** ($A_{cs} = 0.162 \, \text{nm}^2$, $M_v = 22414 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
- **Carbon-based** adsorbents (GAC, PAC, ACF, biochar, CNTs, GO): rely on π–π stacking, hydrophobic interaction, and oxygenated surface groups. **Targets:** dyes, phenols, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, humic substances.
- **Zeolitic** adsorbents (Zeolite 4A, 13X, ZSM-5, natural clinoptilolite): rigid aluminosilicate cages with cation-exchange capacity from isomorphous Al-substitution. **Targets:** NH₄⁺, heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺), radio-nuclides.
- **Biosorbent** family (chitosan, agricultural waste, fungal/algal biomass): rich in –OH, –NH₂, –COOH groups that chelate metals and H-bond with organics. **Targets:** Cr(VI), Cu²⁺, cationic dyes; *e.g.,* chitosan for textile dye effluent.
