**Model Solution for (b):**
Step 1: $V_m = \dfrac{1}{m + b} = \dfrac{1}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) + (2.0 \times 10^{-5})} = \dfrac{1}{1.52 \times 10^{-3}} = 657.89 \; \text{cm}^3$

Step 2:
$$
S_{BET} = \dfrac{657.89 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162}{22414} \; \text{nm}^2
$$

Numerator: $657.89 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.162 = 6.418 \times 10^{25} \, \text{nm}^2$

Step 3: $S_{BET} = \dfrac{6.418 \times 10^{25}}{22414} = 2.864 \times 10^{21} \, \text{nm}^2$

Step 4: Convert to m² ($1 \, \text{m}^2 = 10^{18} \, \text{nm}^2$): $S_{BET} = 2864 \, \text{m}^2$

Step 5: Specific surface area:
$$
a_s = \dfrac{2864}{0.180} = \boxed{15911 \; \text{m}^2/\text{g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of ~ 15 900 m²/g is unrealistic for GAC (which is typically ≤ 1500 m²/g). This signals that the slope value used in the question would represent a hypothetical ultra-high-area material such as a **MOF** like **MIL-101(Cr)**. Examiners expect students to comment on plausibility as a concluding remark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **QUESTION B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the IUPAC classification of pores. Describe, with a neat schematic of surface chemistry, how an **activated alumina** surface captures fluoride ions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 1.0 L wastewater sample containing 50 mg/L of aniline is treated with 0.5 g of activated carbon in a batch reactor. The equilibrium concentration of aniline after 24 h is **8.0 mg/L**. Compute the **(i)** equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$ and **(ii)** percent removal $R\%$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
**IUPAC pore classification (as recalled in §1.4):** micropores (< 2 nm), mesopores (2 – 50 nm), macropores (> 50 nm).
**Mechanism on activated alumina:** Activated alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) surface is densely covered with amphoteric –OH groups. At mildly acidic pH (5.5 – 6.5, below pH_PZC ≈ 8.5), the surface becomes protonated to –OH₂⁺. The positively charged surface is repelled by cations but attracts anions like F⁻ by electrostatic attraction. Additionally, the surface –OH groups engage in **ligand (anion) exchange** with F⁻ to form inner-sphere Al–F complexes, providing a strong, selective bond.

**Model Solution for (b):**
**(i) Equilibrium adsorption capacity:**
$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \, V}{m} = \dfrac{(50 - 8.0) \times 1.0}{0.5} = \dfrac{42}{0.5} = \boxed{84 \; \text{mg/g}}
$$

**(ii) Percent removal:**
$$
R\% = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{50 - 8.0}{50} \times 100 = \dfrac{42}{50} \times 100 = \boxed{84\%}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 (Types of Adsorbents):**
> 1. **Units slippage:** Students write $V_m$ in litres instead of cm³ STP. Since $M_v = 22414 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$, unit-mismatch will erase **2 of 7** marks on a numerical.
> 2. **Cross-sectional area confusion:** Using $A_{cs} = 0.195 \, \text{nm}^2$ (Kr BET) instead of $0.162 \, \text{nm}^2$ (N₂ BET) gives a ~ 20% error — examiners will not award full credit.
> 3. **GAC vs PAC confusion:** Students write "PAC is used in packed columns" — *this is incorrect*; PAC is dosed in slurry form, GAC is used in columns. This costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to divide by mass in BET:** A frequent omission — the question asks for *specific* surface area (m²/g) and not *total* surface area (m²).
> 5. **Mixing up $q_{max}$ and $q_e$:** $q_{max}$ is the *Langmuir monolayer capacity*; $q_e$ is the *operational capacity at a chosen Cₑ*. Writing one for the other leads to a 1-mark cut.
> 6. **No mention of pH_PZC in mechanism answers:** For "Why does activated alumina remove F⁻ at pH 6.5?", the *expected* reason is the amphoteric surface and pH_PZC argument. Skip this, and you lose 2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Module 3: Types of Adsorbents**
> - An **adsorbent** is a porous, high-surface-area solid that captures pollutants from a fluid via surface forces.
> - The **defining metric** of an adsorbent is its **specific surface area** (m²/g), measured by the **BET method** using **N₂ at 77 K**.
> - **IUPAC pore sizes:** **micro** < 2 nm, **meso** 2–50 nm, **macro** > 50 nm.
> - **Six canonical families:** Carbon-based, Inorganic/Mineral, Natural/Biosorbent, Polymeric Resin, Industrial By-Product, Emerging Nano (MOFs/COFs/MXenes).
> - **GAC** is granular and used in packed columns; **PAC** is powdered and dosed in slurries.
> - **Activated alumina** removes **fluoride, As(V), phosphate** via amphoteric –OH exchange; effective below pH_PZC (≈ 8.5).
> - **Zeolites** remove **NH₄⁺ and divalent metals** via **cation exchange** at the isomorphous Al-substituted sites.
> - **Chitosan** (–NH₂ → –NH₃⁺ at low pH) is exceptional for **Cr(VI)** and **anionic dyes**.
> - **Fly ash** is a low-cost industrial by-product with a macroporous aluminosilicate matrix.
> - **MOFs** (MIL-101, UiO-66) achieve **3000 – 4500 m²/g** but are still costly; primarily in R&D.
> - **Physisorption (Q < 40 kJ/mol) → multilayer → Freundlich-friendly → reversible.**
> - **Chemisorption (Q > 40 kJ/mol) → monolayer → Langmuir-friendly → often irreversible.**
> - The **Langmuir linear plot** gives $q_{max}$ and $K_L$; the **Freundlich linear plot** gives $K_F$ and $1/n$ (with $1/n < 1$ indicating favourable adsorption).
> - The **separation factor** $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$ is the **single best quick-check** for favourability of adsorption.
> - Always perform a **sanity check** of computed surface area against known benchmarks (GAC ≈ 1000 m²/g; MOF ≈ 4000 m²/g).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activated Carbon Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activated Carbon Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Activated Carbon Adsorption** is a mass-transfer separation process in which dissolved, colloidal, or gaseous contaminants present in wastewater are accumulated and held onto the internal porous surface of **Activated Carbon (AC)** through surface phenomena arising from intermolecular forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, or chemical bonding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Adsorption is the adhesion of atoms, ions, or molecules from a gas, liquid, or dissolved solid to a surface. Activated carbon is a highly porous, microcrystalline, non-graphitic form of carbon with a vast internal surface area (typically **500–1500 m²/g**) used as an adsorbent for removing refractory organics, color, odor, and toxic micropollutants from wastewater.*

### Adsorption vs. Absorption — A Critical Distinction

| Term | Mechanism | Engineering Implication |
|------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Adsorption** | Molecules accumulate on a 2-D *surface* | Depends on surface area of solid |
| **Absorption** | Molecules penetrate into the 3-D bulk of a liquid | Depends on volume & solubility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Activated carbon operates by **adsorption**. The huge internal surface area inside its pores provides enormous 2-D area for molecules to "stick."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Molecular Sponge

Think of activated carbon as a **microscopic, hollowed-out sponge built at the molecular level**:

- Imagine a hard, black charcoal briquette. Now imagine that inside each grain are **millions of interconnected tunnels and caves**, so small that you would need a billion laid end-to-end to span one meter.
- Pollutant molecules dissolved in wastewater (think of them as wandering marbles) drift through these tunnels and become **trapped on the cave walls**.
- A single gram of activated carbon has roughly the surface area of **half a football field** packed into a pinch of powder. The "marbles" (organic molecules) have nowhere to hide — they will eventually collide with and adhere to the wall.

## 1.3 Types of Activated Carbon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU classification is based on **physical form** and the **application mode**:

1. **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)**
   - Particle size: **< 0.18 mm (75–325 mesh)**
   - Applied as a slurry, dosed directly into the wastewater stream.
   - Single-use; usually not regenerated because recovery from mixed liquor is uneconomical.

2. **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)**
   - Particle size: **0.5–2.5 mm**
   - Used in **packed-bed contactors / columns**.
   - Regenerable (thermal or chemical); favored for continuous industrial operations.

3. **Extruded / Pelletized AC**
   - Cylindrical pellets (1–5 mm diameter) used in gas-phase and special liquid applications.

## 1.4 Raw Materials for AC Production

- **Bituminous coal**, **lignite**, **anthracite**
- **Coconut shell** (high micropore volume → preferred for water treatment)
- **Wood, peat, nutshell, bamboo, petroleum pitch**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why coconut shell?** It produces AC with the highest proportion of **micropores (< 2 nm)** — perfect for capturing small organic molecules in water.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range (Water-Grade AC) |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **BET Surface Area** | **500 – 1500 m²/g** |
| **Iodine Number** | 600 – 1200 mg/g |
| **Methylene Blue Number** | 100 – 300 mg/g |
| **Apparent Density** | 0.35 – 0.55 g/cm³ |
| **Ash Content** | < 8 % (premium grade < 3 %) |
| **Hardness Number** | > 92 % (coconut shell) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pore Structure of Activated Carbon (Hierarchical)

The performance of AC is dictated by its **pore-size distribution (PSD)** following the **IUPAC classification**:

| Pore Class | Diameter Range | Function |
|------------|----------------|----------|
| **Micropores** | < 2 nm | Capture small molecules (e.g., phenol, pesticides) — provide **> 95 %** of total surface area |
| **Mesopores** | 2 – 50 nm | Transport channels; adsorption of medium-sized dyes and humic substances |
| **Macropores** | > 50 nm | Bulk conduits; allow rapid diffusion into interior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most water-treatment applications depend on **micropores**, while decolorization of dye effluent relies heavily on **mesopores**.

## 2.2 Types of Adsorption Forces

1. **Physisorption (Physical Adsorption)**
   - Forces: weak **van der Waals** (London dispersion).
   - Energy: **5–40 kJ/mol** (low).
   - Reversible, multilayer possible, fast equilibrium.
   - Dominant in water-treatment AC.

2. **Chemisorption**
   - Forces: covalent/ionic bonding.
   - Energy: **40–400 kJ/mol** (high).
   - Often irreversible, monolayer only.
   - Relevant for catalytic carbon and some metal-ion removals.

## 2.3 The Two Operational Modes

- **PAC (Batch / Slurry mode):** carbon is added to a basin, mixed, settled/discarded.
- **GAC (Fixed-bed mode):** wastewater flows through a packed column; the carbon becomes progressively exhausted → forms a **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** that travels down the bed.

## 2.4 Adsorption Equilibrium — The Isotherm Concept

An **adsorption isotherm** describes the equilibrium partitioning of a solute between the liquid and solid phases at constant temperature:

$$q_e = f(C_e)$$

where:
- $q_e$ = mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of carbon (mg/g).
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration of solute remaining in solution (mg/L).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question from this module.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables, Units & Significance |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Freundlich Isotherm** (empirical, heterogeneous surface) | $$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$ | $K_F$ (mg/g)(L/mg)¹/ⁿ ; $1/n$ = intensity (0 < 1/n < 1). $n$ between **2–10** → good adsorption. |
| 2 | Freundlich Linearized | $$\log q_e = \log K_F + \tfrac{1}{n}\log C_e$$ | Plot $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$ → slope $= 1/n$, intercept $= \log K_F$. |
| 3 | **Langmuir Isotherm** (monolayer on uniform sites) | $$q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$ | $q_m$ = max. monolayer capacity (mg/g); $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg). |
| 4 | Langmuir Linearized | $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$ | Plot $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$ → slope $= 1/q_m$, intercept $= 1/(q_m K_L)$. |
| 5 | Langmuir Separation Factor | $$R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$$ | $R_L > 1$ unfavorable; $= 1$ linear; $0 < R_L < 1$ favorable; $= 0$ irreversible. |
| 6 | **BET Isotherm** (multilayer) | $$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e(C_s - C_e)} = \dfrac{1}{K_B q_m} + \dfrac{K_B - 1}{K_B q_m}\cdot\dfrac{C_e}{C_s}$$ | $C_s$ = saturation solubility of solute; $K_B$ = BET constant. |
| 7 | Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics | $$\log(q_e - q_t) = \log q_e - \dfrac{k_1 t}{2.303}$$ | $k_1$ = rate constant (1/min). |
| 8 | Pseudo-Second-Order Kinetics | $$\dfrac{t}{q_t} = \dfrac{1}{k_2 q_e^{2}} + \dfrac{t}{q_e}$$ | $k_2$ = rate constant (g/mg·min). Generally fits water-treatment data better. |
| 9 | **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)** | $$\tau = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q}$$ | $V_{bed}$ = bed volume (m³); $Q$ = flow (m³/h); $H$ = bed depth (m). Typical: 5–30 min. |
| 10 | Mass-Balance Carbon Dosage | $$\text{Dose} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e}$$ | Required mass of AC (mg/L) to drop concentration from $C_0$ to $C_e$. |
| 11 | Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR) | $$HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$$ | Units m³/(m²·h) or m/h. Typical GAC: 5–25 m/h. |
| 12 | Spent Carbon Mass | $$m_{AC} = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t \cdot \eta$$ | $\eta$ = working capacity (kg adsorbate / kg AC); typical 0.05–0.30. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tertiary polishing** of secondary effluent → meet BOD < 5 mg/L and COD < 20 mg/L.
- **Removal of priority pollutants**: pesticides (atrazine, lindane), phenols, chlorinated hydrocarbons, PAHs.
- **Decolorization** in textile, pulp & paper, and dye-stuff effluents.
- **Removal of taste & odor** compounds (geosmin, 2-MIB) from drinking-water treatment plants.
- **Trace pharmaceutical removal** in advanced water-reuse schemes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

### 3.1.1 Postulates (Langmuir, 1918)

1. The carbon surface contains a **fixed number of adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one molecule** (monolayer coverage).
3. All sites are **energetically equivalent** (homogeneous surface).
4. There is **no lateral interaction** between adsorbed molecules.

### 3.1.2 Kinetic Derivation

Let $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered. Then:

- **Uncovered** fraction = $1 - \theta$
- Concentration of solute in bulk $\propto P$ (gas) or $C_e$ (liquid).

**Rate of adsorption** $\propto$ (uncovered sites) × (solute concentration)

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

**Rate of desorption** $\propto$ (covered sites)

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

At **equilibrium**, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) P}{1 + (k_a/k_d) P} = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}$$

where $K = k_a / k_d$ is the **equilibrium constant** (also called $K_L$ in liquid phase).

### 3.1.3 Conversion to Adsorption Form

Since $\theta = q_e / q_m$:

$$\dfrac{q_e}{q_m} = \dfrac{K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

$$\boxed{q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}}$$

This is the **Langmuir isotherm** in its liquid-phase form.

### 3.1.4 Linearization for Experimental Determination

$$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1 + K_L C_e}{q_m K_L C_e} \cdot C_e = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{1}{q_m} C_e}$$

A plot of $C_e/q_e$ (Y-axis) vs $C_e$ (X-axis) yields slope $= 1/q_m$ and intercept $= 1/(q_m K_L)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical Basis)

The Freundlich equation is **not derived from first principles**; it is an empirical extension that fits heterogeneous-surface data.

### 3.2.1 Starting Energy Assumption

For a heterogeneous surface, the heat of adsorption varies with surface coverage as:

$$q(x) = q_m \cdot c \cdot x^{c - 1}, \quad 0 < c < 1$$

Integrating across all site energies gives the integral form. The mathematical result reduces to:

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

### 3.2.2 Linearization

Taking logarithms:

$$\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$$

Plot $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$ → slope $= 1/n$, intercept $= \log K_F$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnitude interpretation**:
> - $n$ between **2 – 10** → favorable adsorption.
> - $n < 1$ → poor adsorption; $n > 10$ → irreversible.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Problem

### **Problem Statement**

An industrial effluent contains **$C_0 = 100$ mg/L** of a refractory phenolic compound. Jar-test isotherm experiments at $25^{\circ}$C gave:

$$K_F = 25 \;(\text{mg/g})(\text{L/mg})^{1/n}, \quad \dfrac{1}{n} = 0.40$$

**Determine:**

(a) The equilibrium solid-phase loading $q_e$ if PAC is added to bring the residual concentration to **$C_e = 10$ mg/L**.

(b) The required **carbon dose (mg/L)** to achieve this target.

(c) The new carbon dose if the target is tightened to **$C_e = 2$ mg/L**.

### **Step 1 — Calculate $q_e$ at $C_e = 10$ mg/L**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} = 25 \times (10)^{0.40}$$

$$(10)^{0.40} = 10^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 10} = e^{0.4 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.9210} = 2.512$$

$$q_e = 25 \times 2.512 = 62.80 \;\text{mg/g}$$

### **Step 2 — Carbon Dose for $C_e = 10$ mg/L**

Mass of pollutant removed per litre of wastewater:

$$\Delta C = C_0 - C_e = 100 - 10 = 90 \;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\text{Dose} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{q_e} = \dfrac{90}{62.80} = 1.433 \;\text{g/L} = 1433 \;\text{mg/L}$$

### **Step 3 — Carbon Dose for $C_e = 2$ mg/L**

$$q_e' = 25 \times (2)^{0.40}$$

$$(2)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 2} = e^{0.4 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.2773} = 1.3195$$

$$q_e' = 25 \times 1.3195 = 32.99 \;\text{mg/g}$$

$$\Delta C' = 100 - 2 = 98 \;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\text{Dose}' = \dfrac{98}{32.99} = 2.971 \;\text{g/L} = 2971 \;\text{mg/L}$$

### **Result Summary**

| Target $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | Carbon Dose (mg/L) |
|----------------------|--------------|--------------------|
| 10 | 62.80 | **1433** |
| 2 | 32.99 | **2971** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Tightening the discharge limit from 10 → 2 mg/L more than **doubles** the carbon dose. This non-linear relationship is the key economic argument for setting realistic effluent standards.

---

## 3.4 Determination of Langmuir Parameters from Experimental Data

### **Method (Linear Regression)**

| Run | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) | $C_e / q_e$ (g/L) |
|-----|--------------|--------------|--------------------|
| 1 | 5 | 22.5 | 0.222 |
| 2 | 10 | 38.0 | 0.263 |
| 3 | 20 | 55.0 | 0.364 |
| 4 | 40 | 68.0 | 0.588 |

Plot $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$:

- **Slope** = $1/q_m$ = 0.0108 g/mg → $q_m$ = **92.6 mg/g**.
- **Intercept** = $1/(q_m K_L)$ = 0.187 → $K_L$ = **0.0579 L/mg**.

**Conclusion:** This carbon has a monolayer capacity of ~93 mg/g and a moderate binding affinity ($K_L \approx 0.058$ L/mg).

### **Separation Factor Check** (initial concentration $C_0 = 50$ mg/L)

$$R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0579 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{3.895} = 0.257$$

Since $0 < R_L < 1$, adsorption is **favorable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — PAC Batch Application in Wastewater Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Equalization Basin]
    B --> C[Rapid Mix Chamber]
    D[PAC Slurry Preparation Tank] --> C
    C --> E[Flocculation Basin]
    E --> F[Sedimentation Clarifier]
    F --> G[Filtration Unit]
    G --> H[Polished Effluent Discharge]
    F --> I[Spent PAC Sludge]
    I --> J[Sludge Dewatering]
    J --> K[Landfill Disposal]
```

> *Block-Level Functional Flow:* Influent enters equalization → receives PAC dose in rapid mix → floc growth → clarification removes loaded PAC → final polish → disposal. Spent sludge is sent to landfill (PAC is generally non-regenerated in municipal plants).

## 4.2 GAC Fixed-Bed Contactor Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-treated Effluent Inlet] --> B[Distribution Header / Inlet Diffuser]
    B --> C[GAC Bed Main Adsorption Zone]
    C --> D[Support Gravel Layer]
    D --> E[Underdrain / Backwash System]
    E --> F[Treated Effluent to Disinfection]
    G[Backwash Water Storage] --> E
    E --> H[Backwash Waste Recycle]
    C --> I[Mass Transfer Zone MTZ Travelling Downstream]
    I --> J[Spent Carbon Outlet to Regeneration]
    K[Regeneration Furnace] --> J
    K --> L[Reactivated Carbon Recycle]
    L --> C
```

> *Notes:* The **Mass-Transfer Zone (MTZ)** travels downward as the upper layers become saturated. When the MTZ reaches the bottom, **breakthrough** occurs and the bed must be regenerated.

## 4.3 Breakthrough Curve Concept — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Concentration Co] --> B[Fixed GAC Bed with Time]
    B --> C[Effluent Concentration vs Time Profile]
    C --> D[Initial Period Co at Zero]
    C --> E[Rising Sigmoid MTZ Travel]
    C --> F[Breakpoint Cb at Tb]
    C --> G[Exhaustion Point C approx Co at Tex]
    C --> H[Regeneration Trigger]
```

> *Engineering meaning:* $T_b$ = time to **breakpoint** (operational life); $T_{ex}$ = time to **exhaustion**. Working capacity is computed between $T_b$ and $T_{ex}$. Typical design rule: $T_b \approx 0.5 \, T_{ex}$.

## 4.4 PAC vs GAC — Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Adsorption System] --> B{Flow Volume}
    B -- Small / Intermittent --> C{Pollutant Type}
    B -- Large / Continuous --> D{Target Removal Efficiency}
    C -- Variable / Shock Loads --> E[PAC System Slurry Mode]
    C -- Constant Composition --> F[GAC Packed Bed]
    D -- Moderate 50 to 70 percent --> E
    D -- High > 90 percent --> G{GAC Regeneration Feasible}
    G -- Yes --> F
    G -- No --> E
```

## 4.5 Pore-Size Function vs Target Pollutant (Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pollutant Molecule Size] --> B{Molecular Diameter}
    B -- Less than 2 nm --> C[Microporous AC Coconut Shell Bituminous Coal]
    B -- 2 to 50 nm --> D[Mesoporous AC Wood Based Lignite]
    B -- Greater than 50 nm --> E[Macroporous AC Petroleum Pitch Based]
    C --> F[Phenol Chlorinated Pesticides Humics]
    D --> G[Dyes Tannins Intermediate organics]
    E --> H[Large Colloidal Color Bodies]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define activated carbon adsorption. Distinguish between adsorption and absorption. List the two principal physical forms of activated carbon used in wastewater treatment.*

**Model Answer:**

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a solute accumulate and adhere to the surface of a solid (the *adsorbent*) due to physical (van der Waals) or chemical forces, without penetrating its bulk. In contrast, **absorption** involves the penetration of molecules into the bulk of a liquid or solid, with no surface accumulation.

| Feature | Adsorption | Absorption |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Location | Surface only | Throughout the bulk |
| Effect | Concentration at interface | Equal concentration throughout |

The two principal forms used in wastewater treatment are:
1. **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)** — particle size < 0.18 mm; added as slurry.
2. **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)** — particle size 0.5–2.5 mm; used in packed-bed columns.

*(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*State the Freundlich adsorption isotherm. Explain the physical significance of the constants $K_F$ and $n$. Under what condition is Freundlich adsorption considered "favorable"?*

**Model Answer:**

The Freundlich adsorption isotherm is given by:

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

or in linear form:

$$\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_e$$

**Physical significance:**
- **$K_F$** — capacity constant, indicates the adsorbent's relative capacity for the solute; higher $K_F$ = higher capacity.
- **$n$** — intensity constant, indicates the favorability and heterogeneity of the surface. A larger $n$ implies stronger adsorption and surface heterogeneity.

**Favorable condition:** Adsorption is considered favorable when the slope $1/n$ lies between **0.1 and 0.5**, i.e., $n$ is between **2 and 10**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Describe, with the aid of a flowchart, the manufacturing of activated carbon by **(i)** physical activation and **(ii)** chemical activation methods. Compare their advantages and limitations.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** from kinetic considerations. Discuss the significance of the **separation factor $R_L$** in evaluating adsorption favorability.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a)**

**Activated carbon** is produced in two principal ways:

**(i) Physical (Thermal) Activation — Two-stage process:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Coal Coconut Shell Wood] --> B[Stage 1 Carbonization 400 to 800 C in Absence of Air]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Activation 800 to 1000 C with Steam or CO2]
    C --> D[Activated Carbon]
```

- **Carbonization**: The raw material is heated to 400–800 °C in the absence of air, driving off volatiles and tars, leaving a char with rudimentary pore structure.
- **Activation**: The char is heated to 800–1000 °C in the presence of a mild oxidizing gas (steam, CO₂, or flue gas). The reaction selectively burns off disordered carbon, creating and widening pores.

Key gasification reactions:
- $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$
- $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$

**(ii) Chemical Activation — Single-stage process:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Lignocellulosic Biomass] --> B[Impregnation with ZnCl2 or H3PO4 or KOH]
    B --> C[Heat Treatment 400 to 700 C in Inert Atmosphere]
    C --> D[Wash to Remove Chemical Agent]
    D --> E[Activated Carbon]
```

- The raw material is first **impregnated** with a dehydrating chemical (ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH, or K₂CO₃).
- It is then heated at **400–700 °C** in an inert atmosphere.
- Finally, the chemical agent is washed out, leaving a porous carbon skeleton.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Physical Activation | Chemical Activation |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Temperature | High (800–1000 °C) | Lower (400–700 °C) |
| Time | Long (24–72 h) | Short (a few hours) |
| Yield | Lower (~25 %) | Higher (~50 %) |
| Surface area | Up to 1500 m²/g | Up to 3000 m²/g |
| Equipment | Simple furnace | Corrosion-resistant reactor |
| Pollution | Minimal gas emission | Chemical waste liquor |
| Best for | Coconut shell, coal | Wood, agricultural waste |

*(7 marks: 4 marks each for process description + 1 mark for comparison table; adjustment allowed.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b)**

**Derivation (kinetic basis):**

Consider a fixed number of identical adsorption sites on the carbon surface. Let $\theta$ be the fraction of sites occupied by adsorbed molecules. The fraction of vacant sites is $(1-\theta)$.

At equilibrium, the **rate of adsorption** equals the **rate of desorption**:

$$\underbrace{k_a C_e (1 - \theta)}_{\text{Rate of adsorption}} \;=\; \underbrace{k_d \theta}_{\text{Rate of desorption}}$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$\theta(k_d + k_a C_e) = k_a C_e$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d)C_e}{1 + (k_a/k_d)C_e} = \dfrac{K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

Since $q_e = q_m \theta$:

$$\boxed{q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}}$$

**Linear form:**

$$\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m K_L} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$$

Plotting $C_e/q_e$ versus $C_e$ gives a straight line; slope $= 1/q_m$, intercept $= 1/(q_m K_L)$.

**Significance of Separation Factor $R_L$:**

$$R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$$

| $R_L$ Value | Type of Isotherm |
|-------------|------------------|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavorable |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | **Favorable** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible |

A value of $R_L$ in the range **0 < R_L < 1** confirms that the Langmuir isotherm describes a favorable adsorption system — typical of successful AC treatment.

*(7 marks: 4 marks derivation + 2 marks linear form + 1 mark significance of $R_L$.)*

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

**(a)** Describe the design of a **granular activated carbon (GAC) contactor** for tertiary wastewater treatment. Sketch and explain the **breakthrough curve** indicating the key operational parameters.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial effluent contains **150 mg/L** of a refractory COD component. Freundlich isotherm parameters obtained from lab studies are:

$$K_F = 30 \;(\text{mg/g})(\text{L/mg})^{1/n}, \quad \dfrac{1}{n} = 0.45$$

Determine:
   (i) The carbon loading $q_e$ (mg/g) at equilibrium residual **$C_e = 15$ mg/L**.
   (ii) The required **carbon dosage (mg/L)** to achieve this residual.
   (iii) The **percentage removal** of the target pollutant.
   (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a)**

**GAC Contactor Design — Step-by-Step**

A GAC contactor is essentially a vertical pressure or gravity column packed with carbon granules, through which pre-treated effluent flows **downward** (preferred) for maximum contact time.

**Key Design Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Formula |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **EBCT** (Empty Bed Contact Time) | 5 – 30 min | $\tau = V_{bed} / Q = A \cdot H / Q$ |
| **HLR** (Hydraulic Loading Rate) | 5 – 25 m³/(m²·h) | $HLR = Q / A$ |
| **Bed Depth (H)** | 1.5 – 6 m | — |
| **Backwash Expansion** | 20 – 40 % | — |
| **Carbon Usage Rate (CUR)** | 0.05 – 0.30 kg/kg | $m_{AC}/m_{pollutant}$ |
| **Service Time** | Weeks to months | $t = m_{AC} \cdot q_{working} / (Q \cdot C_0)$ |

**Design Procedure:**

1. **Pilot column test** to obtain breakthrough curve.
2. Determine **working capacity** from the breakthrough area:
$$q_w = \dfrac{Q}{m_{AC}}\int_0^{T_b}(C_0 - C_e)\,dt$$
3. Calculate **carbon mass required** for design service time:
$$m_{AC} = \dfrac{Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t_{design}}{q_w}$$
4. Calculate **bed volume and cross-section** based on EBCT and HLR.
5. Specify **backwash regime**, **inlet distributor**, and **underdrain**.

**Breakthrough Curve Explanation:**

A plot of effluent concentration $C_{eff}$ (Y-axis) versus time $t$ (X-axis) yields the characteristic **S-shaped breakthrough curve** with three zones:

- **Zone I (Initial):** $C_{eff} \approx 0$ — the upper carbon layers are still adsorbing all incoming pollutant.
- **Zone II (Breakthrough):** The **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** has reached the bed exit. Effluent concentration rises steeply from $C_b$ (breakpoint limit, e.g., 0.05 $C_0$) to $C_e \approx C_0$.
- **Zone III (Exhaustion):** The entire bed is saturated; $C_{eff} \approx C_0$.

**Key Operational Parameters:**

- $T_b$ — **Breakthrough time** (time to reach $C_b$)
- $T_{ex}$ — **Exhaustion time** (time to reach $C_0$)
- **Working capacity** = $\int_0^{T_b}(C_0 - C)\,dt \cdot Q / m_{AC}$
- Design rule: operate the bed up to **$T_b$**, then regenerate.

*(7 marks: 3 marks design parameters + 2 marks design procedure + 2 marks breakthrough curve explanation.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b)**

**Given:**
$C_0 = 150$ mg/L, $C_e = 15$ mg/L, $K_F = 30 \;(\text{mg/g})(\text{L/mg})^{1/n}$, $1/n = 0.45$.

**(i) Carbon loading $q_e$:**

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} = 30 \times (15)^{0.45}$$

Compute $(15)^{0.45}$:

$$(15)^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \ln 15} = e^{0.45 \times 2.7081} = e^{1.2186} = 3.382$$

$$q_e = 30 \times 3.382 = 101.46 \;\text{mg/g}$$

$$\boxed{q_e \approx 101.5 \;\text{mg/g}}$$

**(ii) Carbon dosage:**

Mass of pollutant removed per litre of wastewater:

$$\Delta C = C_0 - C_e = 150 - 15 = 135 \;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\text{Dose} = \dfrac{\Delta C}{q_e} = \dfrac{135}{101.46} = 1.331 \;\text{g/L} = 1331 \;\text{mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Carbon dosage} \approx 1331 \;\text{mg/L}}$$

**(iii) Percentage removal:**

$$\% \text{ removal} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{135}{150} \times 100 = 90 \;\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Removal efficiency} = 90 \;\%}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $q_e$ | **101.5 mg/g** |
| Carbon dosage | **1331 mg/L** |
| Removal efficiency | **90 %** |

*(7 marks: 2 marks part i + 3 marks part ii + 2 marks part iii.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Units mismatch:** Always keep $q_e$ in **mg/g** and dose in **mg/L** consistently. A common error is dividing by 1000 incorrectly.
> 2. **Power-rule evaluation:** $(15)^{0.45}$ must be computed via logarithms — partial marks are lost when students write the answer as simply "30 × 15^0.45" without computing.
> 3. **Confusing $K_F$ and $K_L$:** $K_F$ is **Freundlich**; $K_L$ is **Langmuir**. Mixing these in a derivation is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Breakthrough curve:** Many students describe only the curve shape but **fail to define $T_b$ and $T_{ex}$ explicitly** — marks for "operational parameters" are forfeited.
> 5. **EBCT formula:** Do NOT confuse EBCT ($V/Q$) with HLR ($Q/A$). Examiners frequently swap these as a test of understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adsorption ≠ Absorption.** Adsorption is a surface phenomenon; activated carbon works because of its vast internal surface area.
- A single gram of premium activated carbon exposes **500–1500 m²/g** of surface — equivalent to half a football field.
- **PAC** is dosed as a slurry and discarded after use; **GAC** is used in packed beds and is **regenerable**.
- **Coconut-shell carbon** dominates water treatment because it has the highest **micropore (< 2 nm)** content.
- **Physical activation** uses steam/CO₂ at 800–1000 °C; **chemical activation** uses ZnCl₂/H₃PO₄ at 400–700 °C.
- **Langmuir isotherm** assumes monolayer coverage on a homogeneous surface; **Freundlich** is empirical and fits heterogeneous surfaces.
- A Langmuir plot of $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$ gives slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$.
- A Freundlich plot of $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$ gives slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$.
- $n$ between **2–10** = favorable Freundlich adsorption.
- The **separation factor $R_L$** between 0 and 1 indicates favorable Langmuir adsorption.
- **EBCT** is bed volume divided by flow rate; it controls contact time and removal efficiency.
- The **breakthrough curve** is the central operational plot for GAC; design service time ends at the **breakpoint**, not at exhaustion.
- **Working capacity** (kg adsorbate/kg AC) is the key design parameter for sizing a GAC contactor.
- **Pseudo-second-order kinetics** generally fits AC adsorption data better than first-order.
- Carbon dose is computed by **mass balance**: $\text{Dose} = (C_0 - C_e)/q_e$.
- Tightening effluent limits **non-linearly** increases carbon dose — consider this when setting discharge standards.
- Always compute dimensionless $R_L$ and report $n$ explicitly when justifying "favorable" adsorption.
- **Regeneration**: Thermal regeneration at 800–900 °C restores 90–95 % of original capacity after 3–5 cycles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Granular Carbon Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Granular Carbon Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) Adsorption** is a physico-chemical mass-transfer separation process in which dissolved contaminants present in wastewater are concentrated and held on the internal and external surfaces of carbonaceous granular particles (size range **0.1 mm to 2.4 mm**) by van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and chemisorptive bonding. GAC possesses a highly developed internal pore structure with specific surface areas typically ranging from **500 m²/g to 1500 m²/g**, making it one of the most effective polishing steps in advanced wastewater treatment for removing refractory organics, micropollutants, colour, odour, and toxic substances not amenable to biological oxidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GAC is positioned as a *tertiary/polishing* unit process downstream of biological treatment (ASP, SBR, MBR) and is treated as a *mass-transfer limited* unit operation governed by adsorption isotherms and contactor hydraulics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition)

Imagine a **sponge made of carbon** — but instead of soaking up water in large holes, it has billions of tiny invisible pores (like a microscopic honeycomb). When wastewater flows past this sponge, dissolved impurities (paint, medicines, pesticides, dyes) "stick" to the walls of these tiny pores, leaving cleaner water behind. Eventually, the sponge (GAC) gets full and must be "cleaned" — this is called **regeneration**.

Think of GAC as a **"magnetic parking lot" for molecules**:
- Each molecule that arrives tries to find an empty "parking spot" on the carbon surface.
- Once all spots are full, no more pollutants can be removed — the carbon is *exhausted*.
- The richer (more polluted) the wastewater, the faster the carbon gets full.

> [!NOTE]
> **Activated** in the name means the carbon was deliberately processed (usually with steam or chemicals at high temperature, ~800–1000 °C) to create the vast internal pore network that gives it the adsorptive power.

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics

| Metric | Standard Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | **0.1 – 2.4 mm** | Larger than PAC, suitable for packed beds |
| Surface area | **500 – 1500 m²/g** | Indicates adsorption capacity |
| Iodine number | **600 – 1100 mg/g** | Measures micropore capacity |
| Apparent density | **0.4 – 0.5 g/cm³** | Used in bed volume calculations |
| Bed porosity (ε) | **0.35 – 0.45** | Critical for hydraulic design |
| EBCT | **5 – 30 minutes** | Empty Bed Contact Time |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Structure of Activated Carbon

The structure of GAC is a hierarchically porous network classified by IUPAC:

- **Micropores:** diameter < **2 nm** — contribute ~**95 %** of total surface area; responsible for adsorption of small molecules (e.g., phenols, chlorinated solvents).
- **Mesopores:** diameter **2 – 50 nm** — provide transport pathways; adsorb larger dye molecules.
- **Macropores:** diameter > **50 nm** — act as feeder channels transporting pollutants from bulk liquid to interior micropores.

> [!TIP]
> **Why hierarchical?** Micropores do the *storage*; macropores do the *delivery*. Without macropores, pollutants could never reach the deep micropores — this is the **pore-diffusion resistance** concept.

## 2.2 Adsorption Isotherms (Equilibrium Models)

Adsorption isotherms describe the **equilibrium relationship** between the mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of carbon (*q_e*) and the residual concentration in solution (*C_e*) at a constant temperature.

### A. Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical, Multi-Layer, Heterogeneous Surfaces)

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

where:
- *q_e* = mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of carbon (mg/g)
- *C_e* = equilibrium concentration in solution (mg/L)
- *K_F* = Freundlich capacity constant ((mg/g)·(L/mg)^(1/n))
- *1/n* = intensity constant (dimensionless); typically **0.1 < 1/n < 1.0**

**Linearized form** (for graphical determination):
$$\log q_e = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C_e$$

### B. Langmuir Isotherm (Theoretical, Mono-Layer, Homogeneous Surfaces)

$$q_e = \frac{q_{max} \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$$

where:
- *q_max* = maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (mg/g)
- *K_L* = Langmuir constant related to binding energy (L/mg)

### C. BET Isotherm (Multi-Layer)

$$\frac{C_e}{(C_s - C_e)} = \frac{1}{K_B \cdot q_{max}} + \frac{(K_B - 1)}{K_B \cdot q_{max}} \cdot \frac{C_e}{C_s}$$

where *C_s* = saturation concentration and *K_B* = BET constant.

## 2.3 Contactors and Reactor Configurations

| Configuration | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Down-flow packed bed** | Most common; gravity flow; bed depth 1.8–3.6 m | Municipal polishing |
| **Up-flow expanded bed** | Bed expanded 10–50 % by upflow velocity | Avoid clogging with TSS |
| **Moving bed (PACT/CARBOFLUID)** | Carbon slurry + biofilm in aeration tank | Combined adsorption + biology |
| **Pressure contactor** | Closed vessel, 3–10 bar | Industrial wastewater |
| **Series contactors (2 or 3)** | Lead–Lag configuration | Extend run length |

## 2.4 Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)

$$EBCT = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{A_{bed} \cdot Z}{Q}$$

where *V_bed* = carbon volume, *Q* = flow rate, *A_bed* = cross-sectional area, *Z* = bed depth. Typical EBCT: **5–30 min**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | Freundlich capacity (mg/g) |
| 2 | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$ | Langmuir monolayer (mg/g) |
| 3 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{m}$ | Experimental capacity from batch test (mg/g) |
| 4 | $EBCT = \dfrac{A \cdot Z}{Q}$ | Contact time (min) |
| 5 | $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Hydraulic loading rate (m³/m²·h) |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{N_b \cdot Z}{U_0} - \dfrac{1}{K \cdot U_0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)$ | BDST model — service time |
| 7 | $CUR = \dfrac{m_{carbon}}{V_{treated}}$ | Carbon usage rate (kg/m³) |
| 8 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{carbon}}{V_{bed}}$ | Bulk density of GAC (kg/m³) |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot U_0 \cdot d_p}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for backwash design |
| 10 | $LUB = \dfrac{(d_{p,90})}{d_{p,10}}$ | Lazenby uniformity coefficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition (Langmuir):** When *C_e* → ∞, *q_e* → *q_max*. This is the **asymptotic capacity** used to size GAC.
> **Boundary condition (Freundlich):** The exponent **1/n** indicates favourability — values between **0.1 and 0.5** indicate *highly favourable* adsorption; **1/n = 1** indicates *linear* (infinitely dilute) isotherm.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Removes antibiotics, hormones, endocrine disruptors.
- **Tannery effluents**: Removes colour and refractory COD.
- **Landfill leachate**: GAC + ozone combined treatment.
- **Drinking water**: Removes geosmin, MIB (odour compounds), DBPs.
- **Petrochemical**: Removes BTEX, PAHs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bed Depth Service Time (BDST) Equation

The BDST model correlates **service time** (*t*) to **bed depth** (*Z*) at a given breakthrough concentration. It is derived from a simplified mass balance on a fixed-bed GAC contactor.

**Step 1 — Write a differential mass balance** on a slice of carbon bed of thickness *dZ* at pseudo-steady state:

$$U_0 \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial Z} + \rho_b \cdot \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume local equilibrium** and use a linearized adsorption driving force (surface diffusion controlling). The Bohart-Adams approximation gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right) = \ln(e^{K \cdot N_b \cdot Z / U_0} - 1) - K \cdot C_0 \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — For moderate service times** the term $e^{K N_b Z/U_0} \gg 1$, so the inner logarithm is approximated as $\frac{K N_b Z}{U_0}$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right) = \frac{K \cdot N_b \cdot Z}{U_0} - K \cdot C_0 \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the service time t** by rearranging the algebraic expression:

$$\begin{aligned}
K \cdot C_0 \cdot t &= \frac{K \cdot N_b \cdot Z}{U_0} - \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right) \\
t &= \frac{N_b \cdot Z}{U_0 \cdot C_0} - \frac{1}{K \cdot C_0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form** (BDST):

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{N_b \cdot Z}{U_0} - \frac{1}{K \cdot U_0} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)\,}$$

where:
- *t* = service time until breakthrough (h)
- *N_b* = adsorption capacity of bed (kg solute/m³ carbon)
- *Z* = bed depth (m)
- *U_0* = superficial velocity (m/h)
- *K* = rate constant (m³/kg·h)
- *C_0, C_e* = inlet and effluent concentrations (kg/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A plot of *t* vs *Z* yields a straight line. The slope = *N_b / U_0*; the intercept on the time axis = $-\frac{1}{K U_0}\ln(\frac{C_0}{C_e}-1)$. This linearity is what makes BDST a favourite for KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Linearized Freundlich Isotherm

Starting from the empirical form and applying log₁₀ to both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_e &= K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} \\
\log_{10} q_e &= \log_{10}\!\left(K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}\right) \\
\log_{10} q_e &= \log_{10} K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log_{10} C_e
\end{aligned}$$

Comparing with the straight-line equation $y = m x + c$:

- $y = \log_{10} q_e$
- $x = \log_{10} C_e$
- $m = \dfrac{1}{n}$ (slope)
- $c = \log_{10} K_F$ (intercept)

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical tip for KTU:** Always convert $C_e$ and $q_e$ to the same units used in the isotherm. Slope < 1 indicates *favourable* adsorption; slope > 1 indicates *unfavourable*; slope = 1 → linear.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Carbon Usage Rate (CUR) Calculation

**Problem:** A GAC contactor treats **10,000 m³/d** of secondary effluent. The carbon adsorbs **8.5 mg/g** of TOC. The GAC in the contactor is **5000 kg** and the breakthrough occurs after **45 days** of continuous service. Calculate the Carbon Usage Rate.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total TOC adsorbed} &= q_e \times m_{carbon} = 8.5 \,\text{mg/g} \times 5{,}000{,}000 \,\text{g} \\
&= 4.25 \times 10^7 \,\text{mg} = 42.5 \,\text{kg TOC} \\
\text{Volume treated} &= 10{,}000 \,\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 45 \,\text{d} = 450{,}000 \,\text{m}^3 \\
CUR &= \frac{m_{carbon}}{V_{treated}} = \frac{5000 \,\text{kg}}{450{,}000 \,\text{m}^3} \\
\boxed{CUR} &= 0.0111 \,\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAC Contactor System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Secondary Clarifier Effluent] --> B[Influent Sump and Flow Equalization]
    B --> C[Pressure Screen and Strainers]
    C --> D[GAC Contactor Vessel Number 1 Lead]
    D --> E[GAC Contactor Vessel Number 2 Lag]
    E --> F[Polished Effluent to Disinfection]
    F --> G[Chlorination or UV Unit]
    G --> H[Final Treated Effluent Outlet]

    D --> I[Backwash Pump and Line]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Backwash Water Settling Tank]

    K[Fresh GAC Storage Silo] --> L[Carbon Feed Hopper]
    L --> D
    L --> E

    M[Exhausted GAC Storage] --> N[Thermal Regeneration Furnace]
    N --> O[Quench and Dewatering Unit]
    O --> P[Regenerated GAC Storage]
    P --> L

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style N fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style P fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Description of Block Flow:** Wastewater enters the *Lead* GAC vessel where the bulk of the adsorbable load is removed; the partially spent *Lag* vessel acts as a polishing safeguard. When the lead vessel reaches breakthrough, it is taken offline for backwash and regeneration; the lag vessel becomes the new lead, and a freshly regenerated vessel is brought online as the new lag — this is the classic **Lead–Lag Operation**.

## 4.2 Adsorption Process — Pore-Level Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Liquid Phase] -->|Film Diffusion| B[External Film Boundary Layer]
    B -->|Pore Diffusion| C[Macropore Network]
    C -->|Surface Diffusion| D[Mesopore and Micropore Surface]
    D -->|Physical Adsorption| E[Adsorbed Molecule Held by van der Waals Forces]
    E --> F[Equilibrium State qe vs Ce]
    F --> G[Desorption after Carbon Regeneration]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#e7ffe1
    style G fill:#ffe1c2
```

**Step-wise mass transfer resistances (in series):**
1. **Film diffusion** — resistance of the liquid film surrounding the GAC particle.
2. **Pore diffusion** — resistance inside the macropore/mesopore network.
3. **Surface diffusion** — hopping of molecules along the pore walls.
4. **Adsorption** — final capture at the active site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In most GAC systems treating wastewater, **film diffusion is rate-limiting** for large molecules (dyes, humic substances), while **intraparticle pore diffusion** dominates for small molecules (chlorinated solvents, phenols).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC) and Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) with respect to particle size, mode of application, and regeneration feasibility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | PAC | GAC |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | < **0.074 mm** (US Sieve No. 200) | **0.1 – 2.4 mm** |
| Mode of application | Dosed directly into reactor (contact basin), then settled/filtered | Used in **fixed/expanded beds** as a contactor |
| Regeneration | **Not feasible** — used once, then disposed | **Easily regenerated** thermally or chemically |
| Contact time | Minutes; slurry contact | Hours to days; column contact |
| Typical dose | 5–50 mg/L | Determined by EBCT (5–30 min) |

*(1 mark each for correct comparison; full 3 marks for crisp tabular answer.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and explain the significance of the constant *q_max*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Langmuir isotherm is given by:

$$q_e = \frac{q_{max} \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$$

**Significance of *q_max*:**
- It represents the **maximum monolayer adsorption capacity** of the carbon (mg of solute per g of GAC).
- It is the **theoretical upper limit** of adsorption — the value that *q_e* asymptotically approaches as *C_e* becomes very large.
- It is used for **sizing GAC contactors** and comparing different adsorbents. A higher *q_max* indicates a more effective carbon.
- (1 mark for equation, 2 marks for significance — *Examiner note: students often confuse q_max with equilibrium loading; mention "monolayer" explicitly for full credit.*)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bed Depth Service Time (BDST)** equation for a GAC fixed-bed contactor. State clearly the assumptions and define all variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater with initial TOC concentration **C₀ = 50 mg/L** is treated in a GAC column. Pilot data:
- Bed depth Z₁ = 0.5 m → service time t₁ = 380 h
- Bed depth Z₂ = 1.0 m → service time t₂ = 870 h
- Superficial velocity U₀ = 5 m/h
- Breakthrough ratio C₀/C_e = 100

Determine the **adsorption capacity N_b** and the **rate constant K** using the BDST model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1:** State the governing differential mass balance on a slice of GAC bed at pseudo-steady state:

$$U_0 \frac{\partial C}{\partial Z} + \rho_b \frac{\partial q}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Bohart–Adams assumption of a local linear driving force, and for service times such that $e^{K N_b Z/U_0} \gg 1$, simplify:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right) = \frac{K N_b Z}{U_0} - K C_0 t$$

**Step 3:** Solve for t:

$$t = \frac{N_b Z}{U_0 C_0} - \frac{1}{K C_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4:** For the special case where *C₀* is absorbed into *N_b* (so *N_b* is expressed in kg solute / m³ carbon), the compact BDST form is:

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{N_b Z}{U_0} - \frac{1}{K U_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)\,}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Isothermal, single-component adsorption.
- Constant physical properties of carbon and fluid.
- Film and surface diffusion are combined into a single rate constant *K*.
- No longitudinal dispersion.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1:** Write BDST in the linear form $t = a Z - b$, where:

$$a = \frac{N_b}{U_0}, \qquad b = \frac{1}{K U_0} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2:** Use the two (Z, t) data points to find slope and intercept:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{slope } a &= \frac{t_2 - t_1}{Z_2 - Z_1} = \frac{870 - 380}{1.0 - 0.5} = \frac{490}{0.5} = 980 \,\text{h/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Back-calculate the intercept *b* using the first point:

$$b = a Z_1 - t_1 = 980 \times 0.5 - 380 = 490 - 380 = 110 \,\text{h}$$

**Step 4:** Compute *N_b*:

$$N_b = a \times U_0 = 980 \times 5 = 4900 \,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Compute *K*:

$$K = \frac{\ln(C_0/C_e - 1)}{b \cdot U_0} = \frac{\ln(100 - 1)}{110 \times 5} = \frac{\ln(99)}{550} = \frac{4.595}{550} \approx 0.00835 \,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}\cdot\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** *N_b* ≈ **4900 kg/m³**, *K* ≈ **0.00835 m³/kg·h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to compute the **intercept** and mistakenly report *N_b* = slope × *U₀* without confirming units. Also, *C₀/C_e* in the BDST equation is **dimensionless**; substituting *C₀/C_e = 100* is correct only when the ratio is taken on concentration (not the value 100 itself). Use $\ln(C_0/C_e - 1)$ carefully — a common error is $\ln((C_0 - C_e)/C_e)$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **working principle of a down-flow GAC contactor** in series (lead-lag) configuration. Explain how breakthrough curves are used to determine when carbon must be regenerated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **thermal regeneration process** of exhausted GAC. List the four stages, the typical furnace temperature, and the carbon loss per regeneration cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

A **down-flow GAC contactor in lead–lag series** consists of two (or more) cylindrical vessels packed with GAC. Wastewater enters the *Lead* vessel first, where the most easily adsorbable fraction of pollutants is removed. The effluent from the lead vessel becomes the influent to the *Lag* vessel, which provides a polishing margin of safety.

**Breakthrough curve:** A breakthrough curve is a plot of **effluent concentration (C_e)** versus **service time (t)** at the outlet of a fixed-bed contactor. The shape is sigmoid-like (S-curve). Three operational zones are identified:
- **Saturation zone** at the inlet end of the bed.
- **Mass transfer zone (MTZ)** — the active adsorbing region moving down the bed.
- **Fresh carbon zone** at the outlet — still capable of adsorbing.

When the MTZ reaches the bed exit, the effluent concentration rises sharply (**breakpoint**), e.g., at 5 % or 10 % of the inlet concentration. At this moment, the carbon in the lead vessel is **exhausted** and is taken offline for regeneration. The lag vessel is promoted to lead, and a freshly regenerated vessel is brought in as the new lag.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

Thermal regeneration is the most widely used method for restoring the adsorptive capacity of exhausted GAC. It consists of **four stages**:

| Stage | Temperature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Drying** | 100 – 150 °C | Removes moisture from carbon pores |
| 2. **Pyrolysis / Carbonization** | 300 – 700 °C | Volatilizes and carbonizes adsorbed organics |
| 3. **Activation (Steam or CO₂)** | 800 – 950 °C | Re-develops micropore network via controlled gasification |
| 4. **Cool-down** | Quench to < 100 °C | Prevents oxidation, prepares for re-use |

- **Typical furnace temperature:** **800 – 950 °C**
- **Carbon loss per cycle:** **5 – 10 %** (mostly due to burn-off in the activation stage)
- **Energy consumption:** ~ **3,000 – 4,000 kcal/kg of carbon**
- The regenerated carbon loses capacity progressively with each cycle; typically replaced after **5 – 10 cycles**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Q5.2-B(b):** Many students confuse **pyrolysis (carbonization)** with **activation**. Pyrolysis only *removes* the adsorbate; activation *re-creates* the pore structure. Without the activation stage, regenerated carbon would have very low surface area. Also, students frequently report activation temperature as 400 °C — this is **incorrect**; the correct range is 800–950 °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **GAC** is a polishing step used **after** biological treatment — not a stand-alone primary treatment.
- ⭐ **GAC particle size = 0.1 – 2.4 mm**; PAC < 0.074 mm — PAC cannot be regenerated, GAC can.
- ⭐ **Surface area = 500 – 1500 m²/g**; mostly from **micropores (< 2 nm)**.
- ⭐ **Freundlich isotherm** $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ — best fit for **heterogeneous** GAC surfaces; 1/n < 1 indicates *favourable* adsorption.
- ⭐ **Langmuir isotherm** — assumes **monolayer** adsorption; *q_max* is the asymptotic capacity.
- ⭐ **EBCT = A·Z / Q** is the design contact time; typical = **5 – 30 minutes**.
- ⭐ **BDST equation** $t = \dfrac{N_b Z}{U_0} - \dfrac{1}{K U_0} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)$ — plot of *t* vs *Z* is linear.
- ⭐ **Lead–Lag configuration** is standard for continuous operation; lead vessel is regenerated on breakthrough.
- ⭐ **Breakthrough curve** shape: sigmoid; **MTZ** moves down the bed with time.
- ⭐ **Thermal regeneration** has 4 stages: Drying → Pyrolysis → Activation (800–950 °C) → Cool-down. Carbon loss = 5–10 %/cycle.
- ⭐ **Common KTU numericals:** CUR calculation, EBCT calculation, BDST parameter determination from pilot data, Freundlich constant determination from batch isotherm data.
- ⭐ **Mass transfer resistances (in series):** Film → Pore → Surface → Adsorption site.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy for KTU:**
> 1. Always write the **isotherm equation symbolically** first, then substitute values — examiners reward stepwise working.
> 2. For BDST problems, draw the **t vs Z plot** in your answer booklet (even a rough one) — it shows the slope and intercept clearly.
> 3. In regeneration questions, **always mention all four stages** with their temperature ranges; missing the activation stage costs 2 marks.
> 4. Always specify **units** for *N_b* (kg/m³), *K* (m³/kg·h), and *t* (h) — unit errors are the most common marks-deducting mistake in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT)**, also termed **Tertiary Treatment**, refers to the additional treatment processes applied to secondary effluent (biologically treated wastewater) to achieve a level of purification that goes beyond the capability of conventional primary and secondary (biological) treatment. According to the KTU 2024 scheme and standard environmental engineering texts, AWT is employed when the effluent quality must meet stringent standards for the removal of specific residual contaminants such as **suspended solids, dissolved organic matter (refractory/non-biodegradable), nutrients (Nitrogen and Phosphorus), pathogenic microorganisms, heavy metals, dissolved inorganic salts, and emerging micropollutants (pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors)**.

Mathematically, the goal of any AWT process can be expressed as achieving a target residual concentration $C_e$ such that:

$$C_e \leq C_{standard}$$

where $C_{standard}$ is the regulatory limit prescribed by agencies such as the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **EPA Act, 1986** for discharge into inland surface waters, land, or for specific reuse applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses on the *tertiary polishing* stage of wastewater treatment. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **Conventional Secondary Treatment** (which removes bulk organics via BOD/COD reduction) and **Advanced/Tertiary Treatment** (which removes specific residual constituents that secondary treatment cannot eliminate).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of wastewater treatment as a **three-stage laundry process**:

1. **Primary Treatment** = Rinsing off the large, visible dirt (screening, grit removal, sedimentation of settleable solids).
2. **Secondary Treatment** = Using detergent and agitation to dissolve the oily and organic stains (biological oxidation of dissolved organics via activated sludge, trickling filters, or biofilm reactors).
3. **Advanced/Tertiary Treatment** = The *fabric softener, starch, and final perfume* stage — it doesn't clean further so much as it **polishes, conditions, and removes the traces** that secondary treatment couldn't (residual nitrogen, phosphorus, micropollutants, pathogens, color, and odor).

In simpler words: **Secondary treatment makes the water "clean enough not to cause oxygen depletion in the river". Advanced treatment makes the water "clean enough to drink, irrigate food crops, or industrial reuse".**

## 1.3 The Need for Advanced Treatment

Conventional secondary treatment removes only **50%–90%** of suspended solids and biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and is largely ineffective at removing:

- **Nutrients** like ammonia, nitrate, and phosphate (which cause **eutrophication** in receiving water bodies).
- **Refractory organics** such as surfactants, phenols, and chlorinated compounds.
- **Dissolved inorganic solids** (total dissolved solids, TDS).
- **Pathogens** to near-zero levels (for unrestricted reuse).
- **Heavy metals** and trace organics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Water Quality Metrics Used in AWT Design:**
> - **BOD₅** ≤ **10 mg/L** (tertiary effluent)
> - **COD** ≤ **50 mg/L**
> - **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** ≤ **10 mg/L**
> - **Total Nitrogen (TN)** ≤ **10 mg/L**
> - **Total Phosphorus (TP)** ≤ **2 mg/L**
> - **Fecal Coliform** ≤ **200 MPN/100 mL**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Advanced Wastewater Treatment

The principal objectives of AWT, as aligned with KTU Module 4 outcomes, are:

1. **Removal of Suspended Solids** — to reduce turbidity and SS to very low levels (< 5–10 mg/L).
2. **Removal of Residual BOD/COD** — polishing off the refractory organics that escape biological oxidation.
3. **Nutrient Removal (Nitrogen & Phosphorus)** — to prevent eutrophication and algal blooms in receiving waters.
4. **Removal of Refractory Organics** — non-biodegradable trace organics via oxidation, adsorption, or membrane separation.
5. **Removal of Dissolved Inorganic Ions** — desalination via reverse osmosis or ion exchange.
6. **Disinfection** — elimination of pathogens for safe reuse.
7. **Removal of Heavy Metals** — by precipitation, adsorption, ion exchange, or membrane processes.

## 2.2 Classification of Advanced Treatment Processes

Advanced treatment processes are broadly classified into three major categories:

- **Physical/Mechanical Polishing:** Micro-screening, sand filtration, membrane filtration (MF, UF, NF, RO), adsorption on activated carbon.
- **Chemical Polishing:** Chemical precipitation (for phosphorus and metals), advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) such as Ozonation, UV/H₂O₂, Fenton's oxidation, ion exchange.
- **Biological Polishing (Tertiary Biological):** Nitrification–denitrification for nitrogen removal, enhanced biological phosphorus removal (EBPR), rotating biological contactors (RBC) for polishing.

## 2.3 The Step 'Why' Behind AWT — Logic Breakdown

- **Why is secondary effluent not enough?** — Because secondary treatment converts organics to biomass (sludge) but releases soluble nutrients back into the water. Ammonia is nitrified to nitrate but nitrate is still a pollutant.
- **Why must nitrogen be removed?** — Nitrate causes methemoglobinemia ("blue-baby syndrome") in infants and fuels eutrophication.
- **Why must phosphorus be removed?** — Phosphorus is the **limiting nutrient** in most freshwater systems; even 1 mg/L can trigger algal blooms.
- **Why must TDS be reduced?** — High salinity damages soil structure (for irrigation), harms aquatic life, and corrodes pipes.
- **Why disinfect?** — Pathogens like *E. coli*, *Giardia*, and viruses must be reduced below reuse/recreational standards.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **high-yield formulas** required for solving problems on AWT and its associated design calculations.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Universal performance indicator |
| 2 | **Log Removal Value (LRV)** | $LRV = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Disinfection & membrane performance |
| 3 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | h or d | Reactor sizing |
| 4 | **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | day$^{-1}$ | Activated sludge / biofilm kinetics |
| 5 | **Specific Substrate Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X}$ | day$^{-1}$ | Biological kinetics |
| 6 | **Observed Yield Coefficient** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | mg VSS/mg BOD | Sludge production |
| 7 | **Mean Cell Residence Time** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | d | Sludge age (SRT) |
| 8 | **Mass Balance (Steady State)** | $Q_{in} C_{in} = Q_{out} C_{out} + r_A \cdot V$ | mg/d | Reactor analysis |
| 9 | **Chlorine Dose (Disinfection)** | $C_{T} \cdot t^{n} = k$ (Watson–Law model) | — | CT concept for disinfection |
| 10 | **Membrane Flux** | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | L/m²·h (LMH) | Membrane process design |
| 11 | **Rejection Coefficient** | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | dimensionless | RO / NF membrane performance |
| 12 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m b C_e}{1 + b C_e}$ | mg/g | Activated carbon adsorption |
| 13 | **Pore Diffusion Resistances** | $\dfrac{1}{k_{obs}} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{k_s}$ | s/m | Mass-transfer kinetics |
| 14 | **Effluent Standard Compliance** | $C_{out} \leq C_{standard}$ | mg/L | Regulatory check |

> [!NOTE]
> In every formula above, $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ represent influent and effluent concentrations respectively, $Q$ is volumetric flow, $V$ is reactor volume, $S_0$ and $S$ are substrate concentrations, $X$ is biomass concentration, $\theta_c$ is the sludge age, $C_T$ is disinfectant concentration, and $t$ is contact time.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Advanced wastewater treatment is the **backbone of water reuse and zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD)** strategies adopted globally. It enables:

- **Industrial reuse** — Boiler feed water, cooling tower make-up water.
- **Agricultural irrigation** — Particularly in arid regions like Rajasthan and parts of Kerala's dry belt.
- **Groundwater recharge** — Direct injection of polished effluent into aquifers.
- **Indirect potable reuse** — Discharge into reservoirs upstream of drinking water intakes.
- **Compliance with stringent discharge norms** — CPCB's **MINAS (Minimum National Standards)** under the EPA, 1986.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Generalized Mass Balance for a Steady-State Reactor

**Statement:** For any well-mixed reactor operating at steady state, the conservation of mass states that the *accumulation* equals *input − output ± generation/consumption*.

**Step 1 — Write the general accumulation equation:**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation}$$

**Step 2 — At steady-state, accumulation = 0:**

$$0 = Q_{in} C_{in} - Q_{out} C_{out} + r_A \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for output concentration:**

$$Q_{out} C_{out} = Q_{in} C_{in} + r_A \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a time-based form by dividing throughout by $Q_{out}$:**

$$C_{out} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{Q_{out}} C_{in} + \dfrac{r_A \cdot V}{Q_{out}}$$

**Step 5 — For a CSTR with no reaction ($r_A = 0$) and equal flow ($Q_{in} = Q_{out}$), this reduces to:**

$$C_{out} = C_{in}$$

This is the *tracer-test principle* that confirms a CSTR's hydraulic behavior.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of Removal Efficiency Formula

**Statement:** Show that removal efficiency is bounded between 0 and 100%, and establish its physical meaning.

**Step 1 — Define removal efficiency as the *fractional reduction* in concentration:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Mass removed}}{\text{Mass input}} = \dfrac{Q (C_{in} - C_{out})}{Q \cdot C_{in}}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $Q$ (assuming steady flow):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage form:**

$$\eta = \left(\dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Boundary checks:**
- If $C_{out} = 0$ (perfect removal): $\eta = 100\%$ ✓
- If $C_{out} = C_{in}$ (no removal): $\eta = 0\%$ ✓
- If $C_{out} > C_{in}$ (negative removal, e.g., due to leaching): $\eta < 0\%$ (physically interpreted as production of the substance).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Log Removal Value (LRV) — Disinfection Kinetics

**Statement:** Derive the LRV from Chick–Watson disinfection kinetics.

**Step 1 — Chick's Law (single-parameter disinfection model):**

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot C_T \cdot N$$

**Step 2 — Watson's modification recognizes that disinfection effectiveness is non-linear in time:**

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot C_T \cdot N^{n} \cdot t^{m}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the contact time $T$:**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = k \cdot C_T^{n} \cdot T^{m}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the LHS is the Log Removal Value (LRV):**

$$LRV = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) = k \cdot C_T^{n} \cdot T^{m}$$

This is the **CT concept** widely used by the **US EPA** and CPCB for disinfectant dose design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Membrane Flux Decline under Fouling

**Statement:** Derive the resistance-in-series model for membrane flux.

**Step 1 — Darcy's law for membrane flux:**

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance is the sum of intrinsic membrane resistance and fouling layers:**

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c$$

where $R_m$ = membrane resistance, $R_f$ = fouling resistance (pore blocking), $R_c$ = cake layer resistance.

**Step 3 — Substituting back:**

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)}$$

**Step 4 — At initial clean-membrane condition ($R_f = R_c = 0$):**

$$J_0 = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio of fouled to clean flux gives the degree of fouling:**

$$\dfrac{J}{J_0} = \dfrac{R_m}{R_m + R_f + R_c}$$

This derivation is **fundamental for RO, NF, UF, and MF design** in AWT plants.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Nitrogen Mass Balance for Nitrification–Denitrification

**Statement:** Show that the stoichiometric oxygen demand for complete nitrification is 4.57 g O₂/g NH₃-N oxidized.

**Step 1 — The overall nitrification reaction:**

$$\text{NH}_4^{+} + 2 \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^{-} + 2 \text{H}^{+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate molar masses:**
- $\text{NH}_4^{+}$-N: 14 g/mol
- $\text{O}_2$: 32 g/mol

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric ratio:**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ per N} = \dfrac{2 \times 32}{14} = 4.571 \text{ g O}_2/\text{g N}$$

**Step 4 — For denitrification, the heterotrophic reduction reaction:**

$$\text{NO}_3^{-} + \text{COD} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric COD demand for denitrification:**

$$\text{COD per N} = \dfrac{2.86 \text{ g COD}}{1 \text{ g NO}_3\text{-N}}$$

This pair of stoichiometric factors (**4.57 for nitrification, 2.86 for denitrification**) is **extensively tested** in KTU university exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Advanced Wastewater Treatment Train

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a complete advanced wastewater treatment plant, indicating where each AWT unit operation slots into the conventional treatment train.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Influent"] --> B["Primary Clarifier"]
    B --> C["Secondary Biological Reactor"]
    C --> D["Secondary Clarifier"]
    D --> E["Tertiary Effluent Pool"]
    E --> F1["Tertiary Microscreening Stage"]
    E --> F2["Tertiary Sand Filtration Stage"]
    E --> F3["Tertiary Activated Carbon Adsorber"]
    F1 --> G["Membrane Module MF UF NF RO"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Advanced Oxidation Process AOP"]
    H --> I["Disinfection Unit UV Chlorine Ozone"]
    I --> J["Polished Effluent for Reuse"]
    E --> K["Biological Nutrient Removal N P"]
    K --> J
    J --> L["Sludge Handling and Dewatering"]
    C --> L
    D --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Functional Grouping of AWT Processes (Classification Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Advanced Wastewater Treatment"]
    P0 --> A1["Physical Polishing"]
    P0 --> A2["Chemical Polishing"]
    P0 --> A3["Biological Polishing"]
    P0 --> A4["Disinfection"]

    A1 --> B1["Microstrainer"]
    A1 --> B2["Sand Filtration"]
    A1 --> B3["Membrane MF UF NF RO"]
    A1 --> B4["Activated Carbon"]

    A2 --> C1["Chemical Precipitation P Metals"]
    A2 --> C2["Advanced Oxidation"]
    A2 --> C3["Ion Exchange"]

    A3 --> D1["Nitrification"]
    A3 --> D2["Denitrification"]
    A3 --> D3["Enhanced Biological P Removal"]

    A4 --> E1["Chlorination"]
    A4 --> E2["UV Irradiation"]
    A4 --> E3["Ozonation"]
```

## 4.3 Process Selection Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Influent Quality Analysis"] --> Q2{"Target Pollutant?"}
    Q2 -->|Suspended Solids| R1["Filtration or Membrane MF UF"]
    Q2 -->|Dissolved Organics| R2["Activated Carbon or AOP"]
    Q2 -->|Nitrogen| R3["Nitrification Denitrification"]
    Q2 -->|Phosphorus| R4["Chemical Precipitation or EBPR"]
    Q2 -->|TDS Salinity| R5["Reverse Osmosis"]
    Q2 -->|Pathogens| R6["Disinfection UV Cl2 Ozone"]
    Q2 -->|Heavy Metals| R7["Precipitation or Ion Exchange"]
    R1 --> S1["Polishing and Discharge"]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
    R5 --> S1
    R6 --> S1
    R7 --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT). List any four contaminants that conventional secondary treatment fails to remove.**

**Model Answer:**
Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT) refers to the additional treatment processes applied to secondary effluent to remove residual contaminants that are not effectively eliminated by primary and secondary (biological) treatment stages. It aims to produce a polished effluent suitable for reuse or discharge into sensitive water bodies.

Four contaminants that conventional secondary treatment **fails to remove adequately**:
1. **Dissolved nutrients** — Nitrogen (ammonia, nitrate) and Phosphorus.
2. **Refractory / non-biodegradable organics** — Surfactants, phenols, humic substances.
3. **Dissolved inorganic salts** — Total Dissolved Solids (TDS).
4. **Pathogenic microorganisms** — Viruses, protozoan cysts, fecal coliforms.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Secondary Treatment and Tertiary Treatment in wastewater management.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Secondary Treatment | Tertiary (Advanced) Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Bulk removal of biodegradable organics (BOD) | Polishing for specific residual contaminants |
| Typical BOD removal | 80–95% | Up to 99% (final polishing) |
| Process type | Biological (Activated sludge, TF, RBC) | Physical, chemical, biological polishing |
| Removes N & P? | No | Yes (specifically targeted) |
| Removes TDS? | No | Yes (via RO) |
| Removes pathogens? | Partially | Comprehensively (via disinfection) |
| Effluent reuse potential | Limited (irrigation of non-food crops) | High (potable, industrial, recreational) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various **objectives of advanced wastewater treatment**. Explain with neat sketches how **nutrient removal (Nitrogen and Phosphorus)** is achieved in a typical AWT plant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A secondary clarifier effluent has an ammonia-nitrogen concentration of **40 mg/L**. The discharge standard requires $\leq$ 10 mg/L. Calculate the **percentage removal efficiency** required. If the plant uses a nitrification–denitrification train with an HRT of **8 hours** and flow rate of **5,000 m³/d**, determine the **reactor volume** required.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The objectives of AWT include:
1. **Residual suspended solids removal** through microstrainers, sand filters, or membranes (MF/UF) — reduces SS to $\leq$ 5–10 mg/L.
2. **Residual organic removal** through activated carbon adsorption or advanced oxidation — targets refractory COD.
3. **Nutrient removal** (N and P) through biological nitrification–denitrification and chemical/biological precipitation.
4. **Pathogen destruction** through chlorination, UV, or ozonation.
5. **Dissolved salt removal** through reverse osmosis or electrodialysis.
6. **Heavy metal removal** through chemical precipitation, ion exchange, or adsorption.

**Nitrogen removal** is achieved by a two-step biological process:
- **Nitrification** (aerobic): $\text{NH}_4^{+} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^{-} \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^{-}$ by *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*.
- **Denitrification** (anoxic): $\text{NO}_3^{-} \rightarrow \text{N}_2$ by heterotrophic denitrifiers using methanol or waste-COD as electron donor.

**Phosphorus removal** is achieved by:
- **Chemical precipitation** using alum, ferric chloride, or lime:
$$\text{Al}^{3+} + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow$$
- **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)** using *Acinetobacter* in alternating anaerobic–aerobic zones.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate required removal efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{40 - 10}{40} \times 100 = 75\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the reactor volume from HRT:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} \implies V = Q \cdot \theta$$

$$V = 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times \left(\dfrac{8}{24}\right) \text{ d} = 1666.67 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Express result:**

Required reactor volume ≈ **1,667 m³** (or 1.67 ML).

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a flow diagram the **classification of advanced wastewater treatment processes**. Briefly describe any two physical and two chemical AWT processes.
