**(b) [7 Marks]** A municipal STP produces **10 MLD** of secondary effluent with the following characteristics: BOD₅ = 30 mg/L, COD = 80 mg/L, TSS = 25 mg/L, NH₃-N = 25 mg/L. The plant is upgraded with a tertiary treatment train (sand filter + RO). Calculate:
- (i) The **total mass of BOD** (in kg/d) removed if tertiary polishing reduces BOD to 5 mg/L.
- (ii) The **volume of RO permeate** produced if the recovery rate is 75% and feed salinity is 1,500 mg/L (assume 99% salt rejection).
- (iii) The **LRV** achieved for BOD removal.

**Model Solution for (a):**

AWT processes are classified into:
- **Physical Processes:** Microstrainers, Sand Filtration, Membrane Filtration (MF, UF, NF, RO), Activated Carbon Adsorption.
- **Chemical Processes:** Chemical Precipitation, Advanced Oxidation (Ozone, UV/H₂O₂, Fenton), Ion Exchange.
- **Biological Processes:** Tertiary nitrification–denitrification, EBPR.
- **Disinfection:** Chlorination, UV, Ozonation.

*Physical Process 1:* **Sand Filtration** — Polishes secondary effluent to TSS $\leq$ 5 mg/L using graded sand media.
*Physical Process 2:* **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — Uses semi-permeable membrane at high pressure to reject dissolved ions and organics.

*Chemical Process 1:* **Advanced Oxidation (Ozone)** — Generates hydroxyl radicals ($\cdot$OH) to mineralize refractory organics.
*Chemical Process 2:* **Chemical Precipitation** — Uses alum/ferric chloride to precipitate phosphate as AlPO₄ or FePO₄.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Mass of BOD removed:**

$$C_{in} = 30 \text{ mg/L}, \quad C_{out} = 5 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\Delta C = 30 - 5 = 25 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Mass removed} = Q \times \Delta C = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ L/d} \times 25 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Mass removed} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/d} = 250 \text{ kg/d}$$

**(ii) Volume of RO permeate:**

$$Q_{permeate} = \text{Recovery} \times Q_{feed} = 0.75 \times 10 \text{ MLD} = 7.5 \text{ MLD}$$

Permeate salinity:
$$C_p = C_f \times (1 - R) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.99) = 15 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iii) Log Removal Value for BOD:**

$$LRV = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{30}{5}\right) = \log_{10}(6) = 0.778$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Students frequently forget to convert **MLD → L/d** before multiplying with mg/L. Always show the conversion step explicitly (1 MLD = 10⁶ L/d).
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Removal Efficiency:** A negative removal efficiency (when $C_{out} > C_{in}$) is sometimes misreported as a "positive" removal. Always check the sign of $\Delta C$ before computing.
> 3. **Confusing LRV with % Removal:** A 90% removal = 1 LRV, 99% = 2 LRV, 99.9% = 3 LRV. Many students incorrectly write LRV = 90 (when it should be 1).
> 4. **RO Recovery vs. Rejection:** Recovery is the *volumetric fraction* of feed that becomes permeate; Rejection is the *fraction of solutes* retained. Do not interchange these terms.
> 5. **SRT vs. HRT confusion:** $\theta$ (HRT) is hydraulic; $\theta_c$ (SRT) is sludge age. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **AWT** = Tertiary treatment; goes *beyond* secondary biological treatment.
- **Secondary effluent** still contains nutrients, pathogens, refractory organics, and TDS — these are targets of AWT.
- **Five main objectives** of AWT: SS removal, organic polishing, nutrient removal, disinfection, and TDS reduction.
- **Three categories** of AWT: Physical, Chemical, Biological.
- **Nutrient Removal:**
  - Nitrogen → Nitrification (aerobic) + Denitrification (anoxic).
  - Phosphorus → Chemical precipitation (Alum/FeCl₃) or EBPR.
- **Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand for Nitrification:** **4.57 g O₂/g NH₃-N oxidized** (must memorize).
- **COD demand for Denitrification:** **2.86 g COD/g NO₃-N reduced**.
- **Membrane Hierarchy:** MF $\rightarrow$ UF $\rightarrow$ NF $\rightarrow$ RO (increasing rejection, decreasing pore size).
- **Disinfection Models:** Chick's Law → Watson–Law modification → CT concept.
- **Log Removal Value (LRV):** $LRV = \log_{10}(C_{in}/C_{out})$ — dimensionless.
- **Removal Efficiency ($\eta$):** $\eta = [(C_{in} - C_{out})/C_{in}] \times 100$ — expressed as %.
- **Resistance-in-Series Model:** $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c$ — fundamental for membrane fouling analysis.
- **Rejection Coefficient (R):** $R = 1 - (C_p/C_f)$ — used for RO/NF performance.
- **Activated Carbon Isotherm:** Langmuir model: $q_e = q_m b C_e / (1 + b C_e)$.
- **KTU 2024 Examiner Emphasis:** The examiner expects students to **draw labeled process flow diagrams**, perform **unit conversions correctly**, and explain the **stoichiometry** of nitrification–denitrification.
- **Real-world drivers for AWT:** Water scarcity, ZLD mandates, EPA discharge norms, and circular-economy water reuse goals.
- **Exam-ready mantra:** *"AWT is not just treatment — it is resource recovery and reuse enablement."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro-coagulation: Factors Affecting Electrocoagulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electro-coagulation: Factors Affecting Electrocoagulation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electro-coagulation (EC)** is an electrochemical water/wastewater treatment technology that utilizes sacrificial metal electrodes (typically **Aluminium (Al)** or **Iron (Fe)**) connected to an external direct current (DC) power source. The passage of current causes the anode to undergo **electrolytic oxidation**, liberating coagulant metal cations (Al³⁺ or Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺) directly into the bulk solution, while the cathode produces hydrogen gas bubbles. The dissolved metal cations hydrolyze in situ to form polynuclear hydroxide flocs (sweep flocs) that destabilize, aggregate, and adsorb suspended, colloidal, or dissolved pollutants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):** Electro-coagulation (EC) is defined as an electrochemical technique in which coagulating agents are generated *in situ* by the electrolytic dissolution of a sacrificial anode, simultaneously producing hydrogen bubbles at the cathode that aid in floatation of the flocculated pollutants. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **traditional teabag** of alum thrown into a bucket of muddy water — the alum dissolves slowly and you get coagulation. Now imagine that instead of carrying a teabag around, you simply connect a **piece of aluminum metal to a 9V battery** and dip it into the water. The battery *forces* the aluminum to dissolve in a controlled, predictable, electrochemical way — releasing exactly the dose of Al³⁺ ions needed, right where you want them. The **hydrogen bubbles** generated at the cathode act like millions of tiny rising balloons that grab the dirt particles and carry them to the surface — a process called **electro-flotation**. So, in essence: > 🧠 **The "Tea-Battery-Balloon" Analogy:** > - **Aluminium plate** = the tea bag (releases coagulant) > - **Battery / DC source** = the master controller (forces the release rate) > - **H₂ bubbles at cathode** = the balloons (lift pollutants to the top) > - **Al(OH)₃ flocs** = the net that traps pollutants ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Faraday's Constant | **F** | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) | | Density of Aluminium | ρ_Al | **2.70 g cm⁻³** | | Density of Iron | ρ_Fe | **7.87 g cm⁻³** | | Molar Mass of Aluminium | M_Al | **26.98 g mol⁻¹** | | Molar Mass of Iron | M_Fe | **55.85 g mol⁻¹** | | Standard current density range | j | **10 – 300 A m⁻²** | | Typical inter-electrode gap | d | **0.5 – 3.0 cm** | | Recommended pH window | pH | **4 – 9** (optimum 6 – 8 for Al; 5 – 9 for Fe) | > [!NOTE] > The **dissolved oxygen (DO)** in the wastewater is a critical reactant. While cathode reaction is *reductive*, dissolved O₂ enables *parasitic* oxidation of ferrous to ferric ion in iron-electrode systems, which alters coagulation chemistry. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Electrochemistry of EC The complete electrocoagulation process involves **three simultaneous coupled phenomena** at the electrodes: ### A. Anode Reactions (Oxidation – Sacrificial Dissolution) For **Aluminium anode**: $$Al_{(s)} \rightarrow Al^{3+}_{(aq)} + 3e^- \quad (E^0 = -1.66 \text{ V vs SHE})$$ For **Iron anode (in absence of O₂)**: $$Fe_{(s)} \rightarrow Fe^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^- \quad (E^0 = -0.44 \text{ V vs SHE})$$ For **Iron anode (in presence of O₂)**: $$4Fe^{2+}_{(aq)} + O_{2(g)} + 4H^+_{(aq)} \rightarrow 4Fe^{3+}_{(aq)} + 2H_2O_{(l)}$$ ### B. Cathode Reactions (Reduction – Hydrogen Evolution) In most EC systems the cathodic reaction is the proton reduction or water reduction: $$2H_2O_{(l)} + 2e^- \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + 2OH^-_{(aq)} \quad (E^0 = -0.83 \text{ V vs SHE at pH 7})$$ ### C. Bulk Solution Reactions (Coagulant Speciation) The liberated metal cations immediately hydrolyze: $$Al^{3+} + 3H_2O \rightarrow Al(OH)_3 \downarrow + 3H^+$$ $$4Fe^{2+} + O_2 + 10H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3 \downarrow + 8H^+$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these reactions matter for KTU:** The release of H⁺ ions at the anode and OH⁻ at the cathode creates a localized pH gradient. This is *why* the **initial pH** of wastewater is a critical factor — it dictates which Al or Fe hydroxide species dominates the coagulation chemistry. ## 2.2 The 10 Critical Factors Affecting Electrocoagulation (KTU Module 4 Core Content) The efficiency of an EC reactor is governed by the following operating parameters. Each is examined in terms of its mechanism, optimum range, and engineering impact. ### 1️⃣ **Current Density (j) — The Master Variable** - Defined as: $j = I / A_{electrode}$ (A m⁻²) - **Mechanism:** Higher current → faster coagulant dose release (per Faraday's law) and faster bubble generation. - **Trade-off:** Excessive j leads to passivation of anode, increased ohmic heating, and high energy cost. - **Optimum:** 20 – 150 A m⁻² (general range), pollutant-specific. ### 2️⃣ **Electrode Material (Anode/Cathode Pair)** - **Al-Al:** Best for colour removal, dye degradation, defluoridation; produces light, fluffy Al(OH)₃ flocs. - **Fe-Fe:** Best for heavy metals, arsenic, phosphate; produces denser, more settleable Fe(OH)₃ flocs. - **Mixed Al-Fe:** Sometimes used for combined advantages. - **Cathode material (Stainless Steel / SS-304, SS-316):** Inert; does not dissolve. ### 3️⃣ **Initial pH of the Wastewater** - pH determines the speciation of Al³⁺ / Fe²⁺ ions: e.g., Al(OH)²⁺, Al(OH)₂⁺, Al(OH)₃, Al(OH)₄⁻. - **For Aluminium:** Optimum pH ≈ 6 – 8 (amphoteric — dissolves at both extremes). - **For Iron:** Optimum pH ≈ 5 – 9. - At extreme pH, flocs redissolve, reducing removal efficiency. ### 4️⃣ **Electrolyte / Solution Conductivity (κ)** - Adequate conductivity (≥ 1000 µS cm⁻¹) is essential to maintain low cell voltage. - If low, **supporting electrolytes** (NaCl, Na₂SO₄, KCl) are added. - **NaCl bonus:** Cl⁻ ions break down the passive oxide film on the anode, reducing **passivation** and enabling pitting dissolution. ### 5️⃣ **Operating Temperature** - Generally, EC efficiency increases with temperature (up to ~ 60 °C) due to enhanced reaction kinetics. - Too high a temperature → increased H₂ bubble size → reduced bubble-particle contact efficiency. ### 6️⃣ **Inter-Electrode Distance (Gap)** - Smaller gap → higher current density at given voltage → faster kinetics. - Larger gap → more uniform electric field but higher ohmic losses. - **Typical optimum:** 1 – 2 cm. ### 7️⃣ **Electrode Connection Mode** - **Monopolar in Series (MP-S):** Same current through all electrodes, high voltage. - **Monopolar in Parallel (MP-P):** Same voltage across each pair, uniform current distribution. - **Bipolar in Series (BP-S):** Inner electrodes are sacrificial, only outer two connected to source. Most economical. ### 8️⃣ **Treatment Time / Electrolysis Duration** - Removal efficiency increases with time, but plateaus and then declines due to floc redispersion. - **Typical range:** 10 – 60 minutes. ### 9️⃣ **Initial Pollutant Concentration (C₀)** - Higher C₀ requires higher current and longer time. - For very low concentrations, adsorption onto flocs becomes the controlling mechanism. ### 🔟 **Flow Rate / Mixing Regime (in continuous reactors)** - In continuous flow EC, **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)** must be optimized. - Mild mixing (laminar) is preferred — turbulent flow can break flocs. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical metal dissolved (Faraday) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams (g) | **I** = current (A), **t** = time (s), **M** = molar mass, **n** = electrons transferred, **F** = 96,485 C/mol | | 2 | Current Density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A_{electrode}}$ | A m⁻² | A = effective anode area | | 3 | Cell Voltage (Ohm's law) | $V_{cell} = E^0 + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{solution}$ | Volts (V) | Sum of equilibrium, overpotentials, IR drop | | 4 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{treated}}$ | kWh m⁻³ | Critical operating cost parameter | | 5 | Specific Electrode Consumption | $E_{cons} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot V_{treated}}$ | kg metal m⁻³ | Material cost parameter | | 6 | Bubble Rise Velocity | $u_b = \dfrac{\rho_w g d_b^2}{18 \mu}$ | m s⁻¹ | Stokes' law for H₂ bubble | | 7 | Bubble Generation Rate | $N_b = \dfrac{I \cdot N_A}{2F}$ | bubbles s⁻¹ | $N_A$ = Avogadro's number | | 8 | Power Input | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | W (watts) | — | | 9 | Charge Loading | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Coulombs (C) | — | | 10 | Removal Efficiency | $R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100$ | % | $C_0$, $C_t$ = initial, final concentrations | > [!WARNING] > In the formula table above, **notice the use of `\vert`-style notation** in equations. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units, undefined variables, or wrong subscripts. **Always define your symbols first** before substituting into any formula. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Electro-coagulation is now deployed in: - **Textile dye-house effluent** (colour, COD, BOD removal) - **Landfill leachate** treatment - **Bottled water defluoridation** units (community-scale, India) - **Arsenic remediation** in rural groundwater (iron-EC) - **Heavy metal (Cr, Pb, Cd)** removal from plating rinse water - **Dairy industry** wastewater (oil-grease separation) - **Oil-in-water emulsions** from petrochemical plants - **Drinking water clarification** as an alternative to chemical alum dosing > [!NOTE] > A key **engineering advantage** of EC is the elimination of external chemical dosing — the coagulant is generated *in situ* in stoichiometric proportion, which means **no counter-ion contamination** (e.g., no SO₄²⁻ build-up as with alum). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law Applied to EC The fundamental equation governing the amount of metal dissolving from the sacrificial anode is **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis**. The full derivation is presented below in a single aligned environment: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Charge passed through the cell} \quad & Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs, C}] \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Moles of electrons transferred} \quad & n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \quad [\text{moles of } e^-] \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Moles of metal dissolved} \quad & n_{metal} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Mass of metal dissolved} \quad & m = n_{metal} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F} \quad [\text{grams}] \\[4pt] \text{Step 5: Equivalency with current density} \quad & \text{Since } I = j \cdot A, \text{ we get} \\[4pt] \boxed{\, m = \frac{j \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F} \,} & \quad [\text{mass of anode consumed per run}] \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Step 1 expresses total charge from current × time. Step 2 uses Faraday's constant F (charge per mole of electrons) to convert to moles of electrons. Step 3 divides by *z* (number of electrons per metal atom — i.e., 3 for Al, 2 for Fe) to obtain moles of metal. Step 4 multiplies by atomic mass M to obtain mass. Step 5 converts I to j·A to express in terms of the more practically measurable current density and electrode area. ## 3.2 Numerical Example — A KTU Standard Problem > **Problem (Worked Example):** An electrocoagulation reactor with **aluminium electrodes** treats **1.0 m³ of textile wastewater** at a **current density of 100 A m⁻²** using an **electrode area of 0.05 m²** for **30 minutes**. Compute (a) the mass of Al dissolved, (b) the theoretical Al³⁺ dose (mg L⁻¹), and (c) the specific electrode consumption in **kg Al per m³** of wastewater treated. ### Given Data - $j = 100 \text{ A m}^{-2}$ - $A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ - $t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$ - $M_{Al} = 26.98 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$ - $z = 3$ (for Al³⁺ formation) - $F = 96,485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ - $V = 1.0 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$ ### Solution **(a) Mass of Al dissolved (Faraday's Law):** $$ \begin{aligned} m &= \frac{j \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F} \\[4pt] &= \frac{100 \times 0.05 \times 1800 \times 26.98}{3 \times 96,485} \quad [\text{g}] \\[4pt] &= \frac{9000 \times 26.98}{289,455} \quad [\text{g}] \\[4pt] &= \frac{242,820}{289,455} \quad [\text{g}] \\[4pt] &= 0.8389 \text{ g} \approx \mathbf{0.839 \text{ g of Al}} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Theoretical Al³⁺ dose in mg L⁻¹:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dose} &= \frac{m}{V_{treated}} = \frac{0.839 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ L}} = 8.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g L}^{-1} \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{0.839 \text{ mg L}^{-1} \text{ as } Al^{3+}} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Specific electrode consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SEC} &= \frac{m}{V_{treated}} = \frac{0.839 \text{ g}}{1.0 \text{ m}^3} = 0.839 \text{ g m}^{-3} \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{8.39 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg Al m}^{-3} \text{ of wastewater}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This theoretical mass assumes **100% current efficiency**. In practice, only **70 – 95%** of the calculated Al is actually dissolved, due to side reactions (oxygen evolution at anode, water reduction, and passive film formation). The KTU examiner expects the student to mention the **Faradic current efficiency (φ)** as a correction factor. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Factor on Performance | Factor | Direction of Increase | Effect on Coagulant Dose | Effect on Bubble Generation | Effect on Removal Efficiency | Engineering Trade-off | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Current density (j) | ↑ | ↑↑ (linear) | ↑↑ (linear) | ↑ (up to optimum) | ↑ Energy cost; possible passivation | | Electrolyte conductivity (κ) | ↑ | — (no effect on Faraday) | — | ↑ (uniform current) | ↓ Voltage drop | | Initial pH | varies | controls speciation | — | Optimum 6–8 (Al) | Off-optimum → redissolution | | Electrode gap (d) | ↑ | — | — | ↓ (slight, due to dispersion) | ↑ Cell voltage | | Temperature | ↑ | ↑ (slight) | ↑ (smaller bubbles) | ↑ (up to 60 °C) | ↑ Heating cost | | Treatment time (t) | ↑ | ↑ (linear) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ (plateau effect) | ↓ Throughput | | Pollutant C₀ | ↑ | — | — | ↓ (relative removal) | — | | NaCl addition | ↑ | ↑ (Cl⁻ breaks passive film) | — | ↑↑ (drastic improvement) | ↑ TDS in effluent | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Electrocoagulation Reactor System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[INFLUENT
WASTEWATER
WITH POLLUTANTS] --> node2[EQUALIZATION
TANK] node2 --> node3[EC REACTOR
Al Fe ELECTRODES] nodePower[DC POWER
SUPPLY] -.-> node3 node3 --> node4[FLOCCULATION
AND SETTLING] node4 --> node5[CLARIFIED
EFFLUENT] node4 --> node6[SLUDGE
COLLECTION] node3 --> node7[H2 BUBBLES
ELECTRO FLOTATION] node7 --> node8[SKIMMED
SCUM LAYER] node8 --> node6 classDef tank fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ec fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef power fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class node1,node2,node5,node8 tank class node3,node4,node6,node7 ec class nodePower power class node5 out ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Process Logic — Interplay of Factors ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[FACTORS AFFECTING
ELECTROCOAGULATION] --> nodeB[ELECTRODE
PARAMETERS] nodeA --> nodeC[OPERATIONAL
PARAMETERS] nodeA --> nodeD[FEED
CHARACTERISTICS] nodeB --> nodeB1[Material Al or Fe] nodeB --> nodeB2[Current Density j] nodeB --> nodeB3[Electrode Spacing d] nodeB --> nodeB4[Connection Mode MP P MP S BP S] nodeC --> nodeC1[Electrolysis Time t] nodeC --> nodeC2[Mixing and Flow Rate] nodeC --> nodeC3[Operating Temperature T] nodeC --> nodeC4[Supporting Electrolyte] nodeD --> nodeD1[Initial pH] nodeD --> nodeD2[Conductivity kappa] nodeD --> nodeD3[Initial Pollutant C zero] nodeB1 --> nodeOut[PERFORMANCE METRICS] nodeB2 --> nodeOut nodeB3 --> nodeOut nodeB4 --> nodeOut nodeC1 --> nodeOut nodeC2 --> nodeOut nodeC3 --> nodeOut nodeC4 --> nodeOut nodeD1 --> nodeOut nodeD2 --> nodeOut nodeD3 --> nodeOut nodeOut --> nodeOut1[Removal Efficiency R] nodeOut --> nodeOut2[Specific Energy kWh m3] nodeOut --> nodeOut3[Electrode Consumption kg m3] nodeOut --> nodeOut4[Sludge Volume] classDef header fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef cat1 fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000,stroke:#0d6efd classDef cat2 fill:#fff3cd,color:#000,stroke:#fd7e14 classDef cat3 fill:#d1e7dd,color:#000,stroke:#198754 classDef result fill:#f8d7da,color:#000,stroke:#dc3545 class nodeA,nodeOut header class nodeB,nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeB4 cat1 class nodeC,nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3,nodeC4 cat2 class nodeD,nodeD1,nodeD2,nodeD3 cat3 class nodeOut1,nodeOut2,nodeOut3,nodeOut4 result ``` ## 4.3 Mechanism of EC — The Coupled Reaction Network ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[ANODE
Al or Fe] -- 1 OXIDATIVE DISSOLUTION --> nodeB[Al3 plus or Fe2 plus
RELEASED INTO BULK] nodeB -- 2 HYDROLYSIS --> nodeC[Al OH 3 or Fe OH 3
POLYNUCLEAR FLOCCULANT] nodeC -- 3 COAGULATION ADSORPTION --> nodeF[POLLUTANT LOADED
FLOCCULES] nodeK[CATHODE
SS 304 INERT] -- 4 WATER REDUCTION --> nodeH[H2 GAS BUBBLES] nodeH -- 5 ELECTRO FLOTATION --> nodeF nodeF -- 6 SEPARATION --> nodeS[SLUDGE LAYER
TOP OR BOTTOM] nodeB --> nodeBUF[BUFFERING
H plus RELEASE] nodeBUF --> nodePH[LOCAL pH SHIFT] classDef elec fill:#cfe2ff,color:#000,stroke:#0d6efd classDef chem fill:#fff3cd,color:#000,stroke:#fd7e14 classDef sep fill:#d1e7dd,color:#000,stroke:#198754 class nodeA,nodeK elec class nodeB,nodeC,nodeBUF,nodePH chem class nodeH,nodeF,nodeS sep ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List and briefly explain any four factors that affect the performance of an electrocoagulation reactor.** **Model Answer (≈ 60 – 90 words):** The four key factors are: 1. **Current density (j):** Governs the rate of coagulant dose (per Faraday's law) and bubble production; higher j yields faster pollutant removal up to an optimum. 2. **Electrode material:** Aluminium produces light, fluffy Al(OH)₃ flocs best for colour/dye removal; iron produces dense Fe(OH)₃ flocs best for heavy metals and arsenic. 3. **Initial pH:** Determines the metal hydroxide species (Al(OH)²⁺, Al(OH)₃, Al(OH)₄⁻); optimum 6 – 8 for Al and 5 – 9 for Fe. 4. **Electrolyte conductivity / NaCl:** Adequate conductivity (≥ 1000 µS cm⁻¹) lowers cell voltage; NaCl additionally breaks the passive Al₂O₃ film. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Write the anode and cathode reactions in an aluminium-electrode electrocoagulation cell. Why is hydrogen evolution at the cathode considered beneficial?** **Model Answer (≈ 60 – 90 words):** **Anode (Oxidation):** $Al_{(s)} \rightarrow Al^{3+}_{(aq)} + 3e^-$ **Cathode (Reduction):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$ Hydrogen evolution is **beneficial** because: (i) the rising H₂ bubbles carry the destabilized pollutant-loaded flocs to the surface in a process called **electro-flotation**; (ii) the bubble-particle collisions enhance removal of suspended and colloidal matter; (iii) it provides a mild mixing effect in the cell, increasing floc-floc collisions. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** > **(a)** Derive Faraday's law of electrolysis and explain how it is applied to determine the **theoretical coagulant dose** in an electrocoagulation reactor using aluminium electrodes. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An EC unit uses **four aluminium electrodes in monopolar parallel mode**, each with effective area **0.04 m²**, with **1.0 m³** of dye wastewater. Operating at a **current of 8.0 A** for **45 minutes**, compute: > (i) Mass of Al dissolved, > (ii) Theoretical Al³⁺ dose in **mg L⁻¹**, > (iii) Specific electrode consumption in **kg m⁻³**, > (iv) If cell voltage is **9.0 V**, find the **specific energy consumption in kWh m⁻³**. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** Faraday's First Law states that the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge passed. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Charge} \quad & Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{C}] \\[3pt] \text{Moles of } e^- \quad & = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \\[3pt] \text{For Al}^{3+}, z = 3, \text{ so moles of Al} \quad & = \frac{I \cdot t}{3F} \\[3pt] \boxed{\, m_{Al} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{Al}}{3F} = \frac{I \cdot t \times 26.98}{3 \times 96,485} \,} & \quad [\text{grams}] \end{aligned} $$ **Application:** This theoretical mass gives the maximum possible coagulant dose liberated from the anode. It forms the basis for the **dose calculation** in EC design. The actual dose is lower due to passivation, side reactions, and the **faradaic current efficiency φ** (typically 70 – 95%). #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** I = 8.0 A, t = 45 min = 2700 s, V = 1.0 m³ = 1000 L, V_cell = 9.0 V, M_Al = 26.98 g/mol, z = 3, F = 96,485 C/mol. **(i) Mass of Al dissolved:** $$ \begin{aligned} m &= \frac{8.0 \times 2700 \times 26.98}{3 \times 96,485} \quad [\text{g}] \\[4pt] &= \frac{582,768}{289,455} \quad [\text{g}] \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{2.013 \text{ g of Al}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Theoretical Al³⁺ dose:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dose} = \frac{2.013 \text{ g}}{1000 \text{ L}} = 2.013 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g L}^{-1} = \mathbf{2.01 \text{ mg L}^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Specific electrode consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SEC} = \frac{2.013 \text{ g}}{1.0 \text{ m}^3} = 2.013 \text{ g m}^{-3} = \mathbf{2.013 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg m}^{-3}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Specific energy consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{spec} &= \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{treated}} = \frac{9.0 \times 8.0 \times 2700}{1.0 \times 3.6 \times 10^6} \quad [\text{kWh m}^{-3}\text{]} \\[4pt] &= \frac{194,400}{3.6 \times 10^6} \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{0.054 \text{ kWh m}^{-3}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (iv), students frequently forget the **3.6 × 10⁶** conversion from Joules to kWh. A common error is reporting the answer in **J m⁻³** or in **Wh m⁻³** without conversion. Also note: do **not** use the formula $E = V^2/R \cdot t$ unless the resistance is explicitly given — here we have only V, I, t, and V_treated, so use the **P·t/V** form. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** > **(a)** Discuss the effect of **initial pH, current density, and electrolyte conductivity** on the performance of an electrocoagulation process. Indicate the optimum ranges for each parameter. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A textile effluent of volume **500 L** is treated in an EC reactor fitted with **iron electrodes**. Operating current is **15 A**, applied voltage is **12 V**, and treatment time is **40 min**. Initial and final COD values are **1500 mg L⁻¹** and **180 mg L⁻¹** respectively. Calculate: **(i)** COD removal efficiency, **(ii)** specific energy consumption, **(iii)** mass of iron dissolved (assume z = 2). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Initial pH:** pH controls the **speciation** of Al³⁺ / Fe²⁺ ions. For aluminium, Al(OH)₃ is the dominant flocculant at pH 6 – 8; outside this range, soluble Al(OH)₂⁺ (low pH) or Al(OH)₄⁻ (high pH) predominates, leading to **floc redissolution** and poor removal. For iron, Fe(OH)₃ dominates between pH 5 – 9. **Current Density (j):** Higher j means more Al³⁺ / Fe²⁺ release (Faraday's law) and more H₂ bubbles, which gives faster removal. However, beyond a threshold (~ 150 A m⁻²), **passivation of the anode** occurs, and the energy cost rises disproportionately. An **optimum j** is identified from a plot of removal % vs. j for each pollutant. **Electrolyte Conductivity (κ):** Adequate κ (≥ 1000 µS cm⁻¹) keeps the cell voltage low and the current uniform. With low-κ water, **NaCl (1 – 3 g L⁻¹)** is added. Cl⁻ ions also disrupt the Al₂O₃ passive film on aluminium anodes, **enhancing current efficiency** by 15 – 30%. **Summary of Optimum Ranges (Table for marks):** | Parameter | Optimum Range | |---|---| | pH (Al-Al) | 6 – 8 | | pH (Fe-Fe) | 5 – 9 | | j | 20 – 150 A m⁻² | | κ | ≥ 1000 µS cm⁻¹ | | NaCl dose | 1 – 3 g L⁻¹ | #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** V = 500 L = 0.5 m³, I = 15 A, V_cell = 12 V, t = 40 min = 2400 s, COD₀ = 1500 mg L⁻¹, COD_t = 180 mg L⁻¹, M_Fe = 55.85 g/mol, z = 2. **(i) COD removal efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} R = \frac{1500 - 180}{1500} \times 100 = \frac{1320}{1500} \times 100 = \mathbf{88\%} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Specific energy consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{spec} &= \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{treated}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{12 \times 15 \times 2400}{0.5 \times 3.6 \times 10^6} \\[4pt] &= \frac{432,000}{1.8 \times 10^6} \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{0.24 \text{ kWh m}^{-3}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Mass of iron dissolved:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{Fe} &= \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{Fe}}{z \cdot F} = \frac{15 \times 2400 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96,485} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2,010,600}{192,970} \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{10.42 \text{ g of Fe}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (b)(ii), the **volume unit conversion** is where most students slip up. 500 L = **0.5 m³**, NOT 500 m³. A common error is to divide by 500 instead of 0.5, leading to an answer **1000 times too small**. Always check your V_treated unit *before* substituting. --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⚡ **Electrocoagulation** = *in situ* coagulant generation by electrolytic dissolution of sacrificial Al or Fe anodes. - 🧪 **The master equation** is **Faraday's Law**: $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$; for Al, z = 3; for Fe, z = 2. - 🔋 **Current density (j)** is the **master operational variable**; it directly controls both dose and bubble production. - 🌡️ **Optimum pH** is 6 – 8 for Al and 5 – 9 for Fe — outside this range, flocs redissolve. - 🧂 **NaCl addition** is a "double win" — boosts conductivity AND prevents anode passivation. - 📏 **Inter-electrode gap** of 1 – 2 cm is typical; smaller → higher j for given V. - ⛓️ **Connection mode:** MP-P for uniform current, BP-S for economy, MP-S for high voltage. - 💡 **Specific Energy Consumption (kWh m⁻³)** uses the formula: $E = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t / V_{treated}$, with **3.6 × 10⁶** as the J → kWh factor. - 🏭 **Engineering applications** include textile, leachate, defluoridation, arsenic, and metal-finishing effluent. - 📊 **Current efficiency (φ)** = 70 – 95%; never assume 100% in design problems. - ❌ **Common student pitfalls:** (a) Forgetting the J → kWh conversion; (b) Using 500 m³ instead of 0.5 m³ for 500 L; (c) Assuming z = 1 for Al; (d) Not mentioning the role of Cl⁻ in breaking the passive film. - 🎯 **High-yield KTU marks tip:** Always state the **anode and cathode half-reactions explicitly** in any Part B question about EC — this is the most frequently asked 2-mark starter.

topics.title: Electrode Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrode Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrode Materials** in advanced wastewater treatment refer to the conductive solid substances (anodes and cathodes) immersed in an electrolytic wastewater medium, across which electrochemical redox reactions occur to degrade, oxidize, reduce, or remove dissolved and suspended pollutants.

In the context of KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4), the topic deals with the classification, properties, selection criteria, and performance evaluation of electrode materials used in electrochemical wastewater treatment (EWWT) processes such as **electrocoagulation (EC)**, **electrooxidation (EO)**, **electro-Fenton (EF)**, **electrodialysis (ED)**, and **microbial fuel cells (MFC)**.

An **anode** is the positively charged electrode where **oxidation** reactions occur, while the **cathode** is the negatively charged electrode where **reduction** reactions occur.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Surgical Team" Metaphor:**
> Imagine an electrochemical cell as a hospital operating theatre where pollutant molecules are the "diseased cells" that must be destroyed or removed.
>
> - The **anode** is like the **surgeon** — it actively cuts, oxidizes, and destroys harmful species using electric energy.
> - The **cathode** is like the **assistant surgeon** — it supports, reduces, and precipitates contaminants.
> - The **electrolyte (wastewater)** is the **operating table** where the action takes place.
>
> Just as a surgeon must have the right **scalpel material** (stainless steel vs. titanium vs. ceramic) for a specific surgery, an electrode must be carefully chosen based on the **type of pollutant** being treated. Using the wrong electrode is like using a wood saw for a heart surgery — ineffective, wasteful, and sometimes harmful.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants (Bold Values for Memory):**
> - **Faraday's constant: F = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**
> - **Charge of an electron: e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **Avogadro's number: Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ /mol**
> - **Standard electrode potential of O₂/H₂O: E° = +1.23 V (vs SHE)**
> - **Standard electrode potential of H⁺/H₂: E° = 0.00 V (vs SHE)**

### 1.4 Why Electrode Materials Matter

The choice of electrode material directly governs:

1. **Reaction pathway** — whether direct electron transfer or indirect (mediated) oxidation occurs.
2. **Service life** — resistance to passivation, corrosion, and mechanical wear.
3. **Energy efficiency** — overpotential losses and current efficiency.
4. **Selectivity** — preferential degradation of specific pollutants.
5. **Environmental compatibility** — toxicity of dissolution products (e.g., Al³⁺, Fe²⁺).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electrode Materials

Electrode materials are broadly classified into the following families:

#### A. Sacrificial (Consumable) Electrodes — Anodes
Used in **electrocoagulation** and **electroflotation**.

- **Aluminium (Al):** Generates Al³⁺ coagulant; effective for colour, fluoride, heavy metal removal.
- **Iron (Fe):** Generates Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ coagulant; effective for organics, phosphates, As, Cr(VI).
- **Magnesium (Mg):** Used for specific phosphate and silica removal.

> [!NOTE]
> Sacrificial anodes **dissolve** into the wastewater as the treatment proceeds. The "consumed" metal ions act as **coagulants** that destabilize colloidal pollutants.

#### B. Non-Sacrificial (Inert / Permanent) Electrodes — Anodes
Used in **electrooxidation**, **anodic oxidation**, **electro-Fenton**.

- **Graphite / Carbon:** Low-cost, moderate overpotential for O₂ evolution.
- **Lead Dioxide (PbO₂):** High oxygen evolution overpotential, good for organics.
- **Tin Dioxide (SnO₂):** Excellent for refractory organics; doped with Sb.
- **Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD):** Highest oxygen evolution overpotential (~2.8 V); produces powerful ·OH radicals.
- **Dimensionally Stable Anodes (DSA®) — Ti coated with metal oxides:** Ti/RuO₂, Ti/IrO₂, Ti/Pt.

#### C. Cathode Materials
- **Stainless Steel (SS 304, SS 316):** Economical, general-purpose.
- **Graphite / Carbon Felt / Carbon Paper:** High surface area, used in electro-Fenton.
- **Titanium:** Corrosion-resistant, lightweight.
- **Gas Diffusion Electrodes (GDE):** Produce H₂O₂ in situ for electro-Fenton.
- **Mercury / Lead (Historical, now restricted):** Wide cathodic window.

### 2.2 Properties of an Ideal Electrode Material

| Property | Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| High electrical conductivity | Minimizes ohmic losses |
| Chemical & electrochemical stability | Long service life |
| High oxygen evolution overpotential (anode) | Favours direct oxidation of pollutants over O₂ generation |
| Low hydrogen evolution overpotential (cathode) | Favours H₂ evolution or H₂O₂ generation |
| Catalytic activity towards target reactions | Higher current efficiency |
| Mechanical strength & machinability | Easy fabrication into plates, meshes, rods |
| Non-toxicity of dissolution products | Environmental safety |
| Resistance to passivation/fouling | Sustained performance over time |
| Wide electrochemical window | Wider range of redox reactions possible |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 numerical/model problems.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg |
| 2 | Theoretical (Faradaic) Charge | $Q_{th} = n \cdot F \cdot N$ | C |
| 3 | Current Efficiency (Faradaic Efficiency) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Energy Consumption per Unit Mass Removed | $E = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{removed}}$ | kWh/kg |
| 5 | Cell Voltage | $U_{cell} = E_{anode} - E_{cathode} + \eta_{anode} + \eta_{cathode} + IR_{drop}$ | V |
| 6 | Electrochemical Equivalent | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg/C |
| 7 | Specific Energy Consumption (Volume basis) | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{wastewater}}$ | kWh/m³ |
| 8 | Anode Dissolution Rate | $r_{anode} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{anode} \cdot A}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Charge Loading | $q = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{V}$ | C/L or A·h/L |
| 10 | Current Density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/m² |

**Where:**
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated at electrode (kg)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday constant = 96,485 C/mol
- $U_{cell}$ = cell voltage (V)
- $\eta$ = overpotential (V)
- $V$ = volume of wastewater (L or m³)
- $A$ = electrode area (m²)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The selection of electrode materials is **not academic** — it has direct field implications:
>
> - In **industrial effluent plants** treating textile dye wastewater, BDD anodes achieve >95% COD removal where graphite electrodes achieve only ~60%.
> - In **potable water defluoridation**, sacrificial Al electrodes are preferred because the Al³⁺ ions form Al(OH)₃ flocs that co-precipitate fluoride.
> - In **decentralized rural sanitation**, iron-based electrocoagulation is preferred over Al due to lower cost and better pathogen inactivation.
> - In **MFC research**, carbon-based cathodes (with Pt catalyst) are standard, while Pt-free alternatives use activated carbon or biochar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

**Starting Premise:**
Charge is quantized. Each ion requires $n$ electrons to be deposited or dissolved at the electrode.

**Step 1: Charge carried by one mole of electrons**
By definition, one Faraday ($F$) is the charge of one mole of electrons:

$$
F = N_A \cdot e
$$

Substituting values:
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \; \text{mol}^{-1}$
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{C}$

$$
F = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})
$$

$$
F = 96{,}485 \; \text{C/mol}
$$

**Step 2: Moles of electrons passing through circuit**
If total charge $Q = I \cdot t$ passes, then moles of electrons:

$$
N_{moles} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}
$$

**Step 3: Moles of substance reacted**
Each mole of substance requires $n$ moles of electrons. Therefore:

$$
N_{substance} = \dfrac{N_{moles}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 4: Mass of substance**
Multiplying by molar mass $M$:

$$
m = N_{substance} \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}
$$

This is **Faraday's First Law** — the foundational equation for all electrochemical wastewater treatment design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Current Efficiency

**Premise:** In real systems, side reactions (e.g., water splitting) consume charge without contributing to pollutant removal.

**Step 1: Theoretical mass** if 100% of charge were used for the desired reaction:

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 2: Actual mass** measured experimentally:

$$
m_{actual} \; \text{(from gravimetric or analytical method)}
$$

**Step 3: Ratio** of actual to theoretical:

$$
\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{th}} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot t \cdot M} \times 100 \; \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For BDD anodes in electrooxidation of organic pollutants, $\eta_c$ may exceed 100% due to indirect oxidation by electrogenerated ·OH radicals — multiple pollutants are removed per electron transferred.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption

**Premise:** Energy used per unit volume of treated wastewater.

**Step 1: Electrical power consumed**

$$
P = U_{cell} \cdot I \; \; \text{(in Watts)}
$$

**Step 2: Total energy over treatment time $t$**

$$
E_{total} = P \cdot t = U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t \; \; \text{(in Joules)}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to kWh** (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):

$$
E_{kWh} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by wastewater volume $V$ in m³:**

$$
\boxed{E_{sp} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot V} \; \; \text{kWh/m}^3}
$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A wastewater treatment plant uses aluminium electrodes in an electrocoagulation reactor. A current of 30 A is passed for 45 minutes through 5 L of wastewater. Compute the theoretical mass of Al³⁺ liberated and the specific energy consumption. Take $U_{cell} = 5$ V.

**Given:**
- $I = 30$ A
- $t = 45 \times 60 = 2700$ s
- $V = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $U_{cell} = 5$ V
- Al anode: $M_{Al} = 0.02698$ kg/mol, $n = 3$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Step 1: Theoretical mass of Al dissolved**

$$
m_{Al} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{30 \times 2700 \times 0.02698}{3 \times 96485}
$$

$$
m_{Al} = \dfrac{2184.78}{289455} = 7.55 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg} = 7.55 \; \text{g}
$$

**Step 2: Specific energy consumption**

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot V} = \dfrac{5 \times 30 \times 2700}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{405000}{18000} = 22.5 \; \text{kWh/m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**
For each cubic metre of wastewater, **22.5 kWh** is consumed and approximately **1.51 kg of Al³⁺** is liberated. This dose must be optimized — excessive Al³⁺ leads to residual aluminium in treated water, a health hazard.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Common Electrode Materials

| Parameter | Al (Sacrificial) | Fe (Sacrificial) | Graphite | BDD | DSA (Ti/RuO₂) | PbO₂ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Anode | Anode | Anode | Anode | Anode | Anode |
| O₂ overpotential (V) | — | — | Low (≈0.7) | Very High (≈2.8) | Low (≈0.3) | High (≈1.9) |
| Service Life | Short | Short | Moderate | Very Long | Long | Moderate |
| Cost (Relative) | Low | Low | Low | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| Pollutant Removal | Colloids, F⁻, metals | Organics, P, As, Cr(VI) | Limited | Wide range (refractory organics) | Cl₂, organics | Organics |
| ·OH Radical Yield | None | None | Low | Very High | Low | Moderate |
| Toxicity Concern | Moderate (residual Al) | Low (Fe is micronutrient) | None | None | None | High (Pb leaching) |
| Passivation Tendency | Low | High (oxide film) | High (wear) | Very Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical $j$ (A/m²) | 50–500 | 50–500 | 100–1000 | 100–3000 | 200–2000 | 100–1500 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Electrode Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrode Materials] --> B[Anode]
    A --> C[Cathode]
    B --> D[Sacrificial Consumable]
    B --> E[Non Sacrificial Inert]
    D --> D1[Aluminium Al]
    D --> D2[Iron Fe]
    D --> D3[Magnesium Mg]
    E --> E1[Carbon based]
    E --> E2[Metal Oxide coated]
    E --> E3[Boron Doped Diamond BDD]
    E1 --> E1a[Graphite]
    E1 --> E1b[Activated Carbon]
    E1 --> E1c[Carbon Felt]
    E2 --> E2a[PbO2 Lead Dioxide]
    E2 --> E2b[SnO2 Tin Dioxide]
    E2 --> E3a[DSA Ti RuO2 IrO2]
    E2 --> E3b[Pt coated Titanium]
    C --> F[Stainless Steel 304 316]
    C --> G[Graphite Carbon Paper]
    C --> H[Titanium]
    C --> I[Gas Diffusion Electrode GDE]
    C --> J[Bio Cathode MFC]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electrochemical Wastewater Treatment Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[DC Power Supply] -->|U cell I| CELL[Electrochemical Reactor Cell]
    CELL --> ANODE[Anode Material Oxidation Site]
    CELL --> CATHODE[Cathode Material Reduction Site]
    CELL --> WW[Wastewater Electrolyte Flow]
    ANODE --> RXN1[Direct Electron Transfer or M OH Radical Formation]
    ANODE --> RXN2[Indirect Oxidation via Cl2 ClO Mediators]
    CATHODE --> RXN3[Direct Reduction of Metals]
    CATHODE --> RXN4[H2 Evolution or H2O2 Generation]
    WW --> MON[Inlet Outlet Monitoring pH Conductivity]
    RXN1 --> EFF[Treated Effluent]
    RXN2 --> EFF
    RXN3 --> SLUDGE[Sludge Flocs Settled Solids]
    RXN4 --> GAS[H2 Gas Vented]
    MON --> CTRL[Process Controller PLC]
    CTRL --> PS
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Electron Transfer at Electrode Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Bulk[Wastewater Bulk Solution]
        POL[Pollutant Molecule R H]
        MED[Mediator Cl to Cl2]
    end
    subgraph Surface[Electrode Electrolyte Interface]
        AN[Anode Surface]
        CAT[Cathode Surface]
    end
    subgraph Products[Reaction Products]
        OX[Oxidized R to ROH or CO2]
        H2O2[H2O2 for Fenton]
        H2G[H2 Gas]
    end
    POL -->|Direct Anodic Oxidation| AN
    AN -->|Electron Transfer| OX
    MED -->|Indirect Oxidation| AN
    AN -->|Active Chlorine Species| OX
    CAT -->|2 Electron O2 Reduction| H2O2
    CAT -->|2 H2O to H2| H2G
    POL -->|Cathodic Reduction| CAT
    CAT --> RXN5[Reduced Metal M to M0 for Recovery]
```

### 4.4 Passivation and Fouling Mechanism (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Electrode Surface] --> B[Active Operation]
    B --> C{Operating Condition}
    C -->|High pH Low Current| D[Formation of Oxide Layer]
    C -->|Sulphide or Phosphate Ions| E[Precipitation of Insulating Film]
    C -->|Organic Polymers| F[Polymer Fouling and Coating]
    C -->|Biofilm Growth| G[Microbial Adhesion]
    D --> H[Passivation Loss of Active Sites]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Increased Cell Voltage Reduced Current Efficiency]
    I --> J[Periodic Maintenance Required]
    J --> K[Acid Pickling or Reverse Polarity or Mechanical Cleaning]
    K --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1.** Define sacrificial and non-sacrificial electrodes with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A **sacrificial electrode** is an anode that dissolves into the electrolyte during the electrochemical reaction, releasing metal ions that act as coagulants or reactants. Example: **Aluminium** used in electrocoagulation, which dissolves as Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻.

A **non-sacrificial (inert) electrode** does not dissolve and acts purely as a site for electron transfer without contributing ions to the solution. Example: **Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD)** used in anodic oxidation of refractory organic pollutants, where the electrode remains intact while ·OH radicals are generated at its surface.

---

**Q2.** What is meant by "oxygen evolution overpotential" and why is it important in selecting anode materials?

**Model Answer:**
The **oxygen evolution overpotential (OEP)** is the excess voltage (beyond the thermodynamic 1.23 V vs SHE) required at an anode to liberate O₂ from water. A **high OEP anode** (e.g., BDD ≈ 2.8 V, PbO₂ ≈ 1.9 V) suppresses O₂ formation, allowing more current to be utilized for **pollutant oxidation**, thereby increasing current efficiency. A **low OEP anode** (e.g., DSA Ti/RuO₂ ≈ 0.3 V) wastes charge as O₂ gas and is unsuitable for organic pollutant removal but is preferred for **chlor-alkali / disinfection** applications where Cl₂ generation is desired.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the classification of electrode materials used in advanced wastewater treatment with suitable examples. Discuss the role of Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD) anodes in the electrooxidation of refractory organics.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A laboratory electrocoagulation reactor uses iron electrodes (Fe, $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$) to treat 10 L of textile dye wastewater. A current of 25 A is applied for 1 hour. The cell voltage is 4.5 V. Calculate:
1. Theoretical mass of Fe²⁺ released (in grams).
2. Specific energy consumption in kWh/m³.
3. If experimentally only 6.5 g of iron dissolved, determine the current efficiency.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Electrode materials are classified as follows:

**(i) Sacrificial Anodes:** Aluminium, Iron, Magnesium. These dissolve and act as in-situ coagulant sources.

**(ii) Non-Sacrificial (Inert) Anodes:**
- Carbon-based: Graphite, activated carbon.
- Metal-oxide based: PbO₂, SnO₂, Ti/RuO₂ (DSA).
- Diamond-based: Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD).

**(iii) Cathode Materials:** Stainless steel, graphite, titanium, gas diffusion electrodes.

**BDD Anodes in Refractory Organics Removal:**

- BDD has an **extremely wide electrochemical window** (~ -1.25 V to +2.8 V vs SHE) and a **very high oxygen evolution overpotential (~2.8 V)**.
- At its surface, water is oxidized to produce **physisorbed hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** — among the strongest oxidants known (E° = +2.80 V vs NHE, second only to fluorine).
- These ·OH radicals **non-selectively oxidize** recalcitrant compounds such as pesticides, pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors, and textile dyes, often achieving **complete mineralization to CO₂ and H₂O**.
- Additional pathway: **Direct anodic oxidation** by electron transfer across the BDD/electrolyte interface.
- BDD is chemically inert, mechanically hard, and has a long service life (>1000 hours), making it ideal for harsh effluents despite its high capital cost.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $I = 25$ A, $t = 1 \times 3600 = 3600$ s, $V = 10$ L = $10^{-2}$ m³, $U_{cell} = 4.5$ V
- $M_{Fe} = 0.05585$ kg/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $m_{actual} = 6.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg

**(i) Theoretical mass of Fe²⁺:**

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{25 \times 3600 \times 0.05585}{2 \times 96485}
$$

$$
m_{th} = \dfrac{5026.5}{192970} = 0.02605 \; \text{kg} = 26.05 \; \text{g}
$$

**(ii) Specific energy consumption:**

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot V} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 25 \times 3600}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{405000}{36000} = 11.25 \; \text{kWh/m}^3
$$

**(iii) Current efficiency:**

$$
\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100 = \dfrac{6.5}{26.05} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_c = 24.95 \; \% \approx 25\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 25% of the charge contributed to iron dissolution; the remaining 75% was likely consumed in **oxygen evolution** and **side reactions**. This indicates the need to optimize current density or consider alternative DSA/BDD anodes for higher efficiency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the properties and applications of Aluminium and Iron electrodes in electrocoagulation. What are the limitations of sacrificial electrodes?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An electrochemical cell with mixed metal oxide (MMO) anodes operates at a current density of 250 A/m² on an electrode area of 0.04 m². The wastewater volume is 50 L, and the target charge loading is 1.5 A·h/L. Calculate:
1. The current passed through the cell.
2. The required treatment time in minutes.
3. The specific energy consumption if the cell voltage is 6 V.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Feature | Aluminium (Al) | Iron (Fe) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ion released | Al³⁺ | Fe²⁺ / Fe³⁺ |
| Coagulant formed | Al(OH)₃ (amorphous) | Fe(OH)₂ / Fe(OH)₃ |
| Best pH range | 6.5 – 7.8 | 5.0 – 9.0 |
| Pollutants removed | Fluoride, phosphate, colloids, oils | Dyes, phenols, Cr(VI), As, heavy metals |
| Sludge characteristic | Light, gelatinous | Dense, magnetic (Fe₃O₄ possible) |
| Toxicity | Possible Al-related health issues | Generally safe (Fe is essential micronutrient) |
| Cost | Higher than Fe | Lower |

**Limitations of Sacrificial Electrodes:**
1. **Continuous consumption** — must be replaced periodically, raising operating cost.
2. **Passivation** — formation of oxide films (especially Fe) reduces active area.
3. **Sludge generation** — adds downstream handling cost.
4. **Selective dissolution** — wastewater composition affects dissolution rate.
5. **Residual metal ions** — Al³⁺ in drinking water is neurotoxic.
6. **Non-applicable for inorganic salt removal** without coagulation.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $j = 250$ A/m², $A = 0.04$ m², $V = 50$ L, $q = 1.5$ A·h/L, $U_{cell} = 6$ V

**(i) Current passed:**

$$
I = j \times A = 250 \times 0.04 = 10 \; \text{A}
$$

**(ii) Required treatment time:**

Total charge needed:

$$
Q_{total} = q \times V = 1.5 \; \text{A·h/L} \times 50 \; \text{L} = 75 \; \text{A·h}
$$

Time:

$$
t = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{I} = \dfrac{75}{10} = 7.5 \; \text{h} = 450 \; \text{minutes}
$$

**(iii) Specific energy consumption:**

Convert time to seconds: $t = 7.5 \times 3600 = 27{,}000$ s

Volume in m³: $V = 50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.05$ m³

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \cdot V} = \dfrac{6 \times 10 \times 27000}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.05}
$$

$$
E_{sp} = \dfrac{1{,}620{,}000}{180{,}000} = 9 \; \text{kWh/m}^3
$$

**Validation:** Result lies within the typical electrocoagulation range (5–25 kWh/m³). The design is **energy-feasible**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up anode and cathode:** Students often write oxidation at cathode — this is fatal. **Oxidation always at anode, reduction at cathode (OIL RIG rule).**
> 2. **Wrong units in Faraday's law:** Using $M$ in g/mol and getting answer in grams, but then expressing final answer in kg without conversion — full mark cut.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $n$** (number of electrons): For Al, $n = 3$; for Fe, $n = 2$. A common error is using $n = 1$.
> 4. **Not converting time to seconds** when using SI units in Faraday's law — this is the **#1 numerical error** flagged by KTU evaluators.
> 5. **Confusing "current efficiency" with "energy efficiency":** They are different concepts. Current efficiency = ratio of useful charge to total charge; energy efficiency also includes voltage losses.
> 6. **Writing "BDD produces ·OH radicals"** without specifying they are **physisorbed** (surface-bound) — examiners deduct 0.5 marks.
> 7. **Skipping units** in final answers — KTU follows strict SI unit conventions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrode Materials**
>
> ✅ **Faraday's Constant** = 96,485 C/mol — **memorize this number.**
>
> ✅ **Sacrificial anodes** (Al, Fe) → used in **electrocoagulation** because they release coagulant ions.
>
> ✅ **Inert anodes** (BDD, PbO₂, SnO₂, DSA) → used in **electrooxidation** because they don't dissolve.
>
> ✅ **BDD anode** has the **highest OEP (~2.8 V)** → best for ·OH radical generation and refractory organics.
>
> ✅ **DSA** (Ti/RuO₂) has **lowest OEP** → used for **Cl₂ production** in disinfection.
>
> ✅ **Cathode** materials → SS 304/316 (general), Graphite/Carbon felt (electro-Fenton), GDE (H₂O₂ production).
>
> ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — derived from charge quantization.
>
> ✅ **Current efficiency** is **always ≤ 100%** for direct electron transfer; can **exceed 100%** for indirect ·OH-mediated oxidation.
>
> ✅ **Passivation** is the major failure mode of Fe electrodes — mitigated by periodic acid washing or polarity reversal.
>
> ✅ **Specific energy consumption** is the **key economic parameter** in EWWT — typical ranges:
>     - Electrocoagulation: 5–25 kWh/m³
>     - Electrooxidation: 10–50 kWh/m³
>     - Electro-Fenton: 2–15 kWh/m³
>
> ✅ **Trade-off principle:** Higher performance electrodes (BDD) cost more but give higher current efficiency → lower operating cost over time.
>
> ✅ **Environmental safety:** Avoid Pb-based electrodes for potable water treatment due to leaching risk.
>
> ✅ **Current density** ($j = I/A$) and **charge loading** ($q = It/V$) are the **two primary design variables** an engineer must fix before specifying electrodes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactor Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactor Configurations** in advanced wastewater treatment refer to the **engineered geometric and hydraulic arrangements** of biological or physicochemical reaction vessels through which wastewater, biomass, and electron acceptors are brought into controlled contact to achieve targeted removal of organic matter, nutrients, and contaminants.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme sense, a **reactor** is any vessel (open or closed) in which a **transformation reaction** (biochemical, chemical, or physical) is carried out on the wastewater stream. The **configuration** defines the **flow pattern, mixing regime, contact mechanism, and biomass retention strategy** — collectively determining the reaction kinetics, treatment efficiency, footprint, and capital/operational cost.

The four **idealized reactor configurations** universally used as building blocks are:

1. **Batch Reactor (BR)** — A closed vessel; fill → react → settle → draw.
2. **Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** / Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR) — Continuous inflow and outflow with uniform mixing.
3. **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — Continuous flow with no longitudinal mixing; fluid moves as discrete "plugs."
4. **Series of CSTRs (N-CSTRs in series)** — A cascade of completely mixed tanks.

| KTU Examiner's Terminology | Common Industry Name |
|---|---|
| Completely Mixed Flow Reactor (CMFR) | Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) |
| Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) | Tubular / Long-channel Reactor |
| Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR) | Time-oriented Batch |
| Membrane Bioreactor (MBR) | Hybrid (Biological + Membrane) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4 of *OECHT725*, KTU expects students to derive the **design equations** for BR, CSTR, and PFR, compare treatment efficiencies, and explain the practical implementation of each configuration in modern effluent treatment plants (ETPs) and sewage treatment plants (STPs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Cooking" Analogy

Imagine you are cooking a curry in **three different kitchens**:

- **Batch Kitchen:** You dump all the ingredients into a single pot, stir occasionally, and after the cooking time, you serve the entire dish at once. Once done, you clean and repeat. → This is a **Batch Reactor**.
- **CSTR Kitchen:** A giant heated mixing bowl with continuous inflow of raw ingredients and continuous outflow of finished curry. The bowl is so well-stirred that the outgoing curry has the **same composition at every point** inside. → This is a **CSTR**.
- **PFR Kitchen:** A long conveyor-belt oven. Raw ingredients enter one end; each "plug" of curry spends exactly the same residence time, but the temperature and concentration **change along the length** of the oven. → This is a **Plug Flow Reactor**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in wastewater treatment?** Different pollutants degrade at different rates. Plug flow favors *high-rate kinetics* (BOD removal at high loadings), while CSTR favors *slow-growing organisms* (nitrification, biological phosphorus removal). Selecting the wrong configuration can mean a **20–40% drop in plant efficiency**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Reactor Sizing (KTU-Highlighted)

- **HRT — Hydraulic Retention Time:** $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$, where $V$ is reactor volume (m³) and $Q$ is volumetric flow rate (m³/d). Typical biological HRT: **4–24 h**.
- **SRT — Solids (or Sludge) Retention Time:** $\theta_c$, mean cell residence time.
- **F/M Ratio — Food to Microorganism ratio:** $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X}$
- **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR):** $\dfrac{Q S_0}{V}$, kg BOD/m³·d
- **Removal Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100\%$ — typically **85–98%** for secondary treatment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mass Balance Framework — The Foundation of Every Reactor Equation

Every reactor configuration in KTU Module 4 is analyzed using the **General Mass Balance Equation**:

$$
\underbrace{\frac{dM}{dt}}_{\text{Accumulation}} \;=\; \underbrace{\dot{m}_{\text{in}}}_{\text{Mass In}} \;-\; \underbrace{\dot{m}_{\text{out}}}_{\text{Mass Out}} \;+\; \underbrace{r \cdot V}_{\text{Generation/Consumption}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Examiners frequently ask students to **state the steady-state assumption** before applying any reactor equation. At steady state, the accumulation term equals zero. Always write this explicitly.

For a single substrate (e.g., BOD) consumed by biomass:

$$
0 \;=\; Q S_0 \;-\; Q S_e \;-\; r_{su} V
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/d)
- $S_0$ = Influent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $S_e$ = Effluent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $r_{su}$ = Volumetric substrate utilization rate (mg/L·d)
- $V$ = Reactor volume (m³)

The **bi-substrate first-order kinetics** (most common in KTU problems):

$$
r_{su} \;=\; k S
$$

Where $k$ = first-order rate constant (d⁻¹) and $S$ = substrate concentration.

## 2.2 Configuration 1 — The Batch Reactor (BR)

- **Operating Cycle:** Fill → React → Settle → Decant → Idle → Fill.
- **Mixing:** Uniform throughout the tank at any instant.
- **Concentration profile:** Spatially uniform, **temporally varying**.
- **KTU Application:** Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR) for small communities; jar-test units; equalization tanks.

The substrate decays exponentially with time:

$$
S(t) \;=\; S_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

The **fractional removal** in a batch:

$$
\eta \;=\; 1 - e^{-k t}
$$

## 2.3 Configuration 2 — The CSTR (CMFR)

- **Mixing:** Instantaneous and complete.
- **Concentration profile:** Spatially and temporally uniform (exit concentration = concentration inside tank).
- **KTU Application:** Activated sludge aeration tanks (most common configuration), equalization basins, completely mixed lagoons.

Steady-state substrate balance gives:

$$
S_e \;=\; \frac{S_0}{1 + k \theta}
$$

Where $\theta = V/Q$ is the **hydraulic retention time**.

**Removal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{CSTR}} \;=\; \frac{k \theta}{1 + k \theta}
$$

## 2.4 Configuration 3 — The Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

- **Mixing:** None in the longitudinal (axial) direction; complete in the radial direction.
- **Concentration profile:** Varies **spatially along reactor length**, time-invariant at any point.
- **KTU Application:** Long aeration channels, oxidation ditches (approximate PFR), disinfection channels.

Substrate balance over a differential slice $dV$ gives, after integration:

$$
S_e \;=\; S_0 \, e^{-k \theta}
$$

**Removal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{PFR}} \;=\; 1 - e^{-k \theta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** For **identical HRT ($\theta$) and rate constant ($k$)**, the PFR **always** outperforms the CSTR. The mathematical reason: in a CSTR, the entire reactor operates at the *lowest* concentration (the effluent value), whereas in a PFR, the first portion of the reactor sees the *highest* concentrations, driving faster reaction rates. The ratio of volumes required for the same effluent quality is:
>
> $$
> \frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} \;=\; \frac{(S_0/S_e - 1)}{\ln(S_0/S_e)}
> $$

## 2.5 Configuration 4 — Reactors in Series (N CSTRs in Cascade)

A common KTU question: *What is the effluent from $N$ equal-sized CSTRs in series?*

For $N$ identical CSTRs of individual HRT $\theta_i$:

$$
S_{e,N} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{(1 + k \theta_i)^{N}}
$$

**Key insight:** As $N \to \infty$, the system approaches PFR behavior — confirming that **plug flow is a mathematical limit of infinite CSTRs in series**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Configuration | Substrate Balance | Effluent $S_e$ | Removal Efficiency $\eta$ | HRT Relation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Batch Reactor** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -kS$ | $S_0 e^{-k t}$ | $1 - e^{-k t}$ | $t_{\text{eq}} = \theta$ | Time-dependent; closed vessel |
| 2 | **CSTR / CMFR** | $0 = Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V$ | $\dfrac{S_0}{1 + k\theta}$ | $\dfrac{k\theta}{1 + k\theta}$ | $\theta = V/Q$ | Exit = Internal concentration |
| 3 | **PFR** | $Q \dfrac{dS}{dV} = -kS$ | $S_0 e^{-k\theta}$ | $1 - e^{-k\theta}$ | $\theta = V/Q$ | Spatial variation only |
| 4 | **N CSTRs in series** | Recursive application | $\dfrac{S_0}{(1 + k\theta_i)^{N}}$ | $1 - (1 + k\theta_i)^{-N}$ | $\theta_{\text{total}} = N \theta_i$ | As $N \to \infty$, $\to$ PFR |
| 5 | **SBR (Time-CSTR hybrid)** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -kS$ | $S_0 e^{-k t_c}$ | $1 - e^{-k t_c}$ | $t_c$ = react phase time | Time-shared single tank |
| 6 | **MBR** | CSTR-type with solids retention | $\dfrac{S_0}{1 + k \theta_h}$ | $\dfrac{k \theta_h}{1 + k \theta_h}$ | $\theta_h$ = hydraulic; $\theta_c$ = sludge | $\theta_c \gg \theta_h$ |
| 7 | **Trickling Filter** | PFR-type w/ biofilm | $S_e = S_0 e^{-k_D / Q^{n}}$ | — | — | Empirical: $k_D, n$ filter-specific |
| 8 | **Fluidized Bed** | PBR-CSTR hybrid | $S_e = S_0 \exp(-k \theta)$ | $1 - e^{-k\theta}$ | $\theta$ based on expanded bed | Carrier-supported biofilm |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Configuration | Typical Volumetric Loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Conventional Activated Sludge (CSTR) | 0.3 – 0.7 | Municipal STP |
| High-Rate PFR (Plug Flow) | 1.0 – 2.0 | Industrial ETP |
| SBR | 0.1 – 0.3 | Small communities, package plants |
| MBR | 0.5 – 1.5 | Reuse, water-scarce regions |
| Trickling Filter | 0.2 – 0.5 | Low-energy, rural India/Kerala |
| UASB (upflow anaerobic) | 2.0 – 8.0 | High-strength industrial |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CSTR Effluent Substrate Equation (Steady State)

**Given:** Single CSTR with first-order substrate removal kinetics $r_{su} = kS$.

**Starting Point — General Mass Balance:**

$$
\frac{dM}{dt} \;=\; \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - r_{su} V
$$

**Step 1:** Express mass in terms of concentration and flow.

$$
M = S \cdot V \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dM}{dt} = V \frac{dS}{dt}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = Q S_0 \qquad \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = Q S_e
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the mass balance.

$$
V \frac{dS}{dt} \;=\; Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **steady-state assumption** — required for KTU derivations. At steady state, $dS/dt = 0$ (no accumulation, parameters invariant in time).

$$
0 \;=\; Q S_0 - Q S_e - k S_e V
$$

**Step 4:** Define HRT $\theta = V/Q$. Multiply and rearrange.

$$
Q S_0 \;=\; Q S_e + k S_e V
$$

$$
Q S_0 \;=\; S_e (Q + kV) \;=\; S_e Q (1 + k\theta)
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $S_e$.

$$
\boxed{\,S_e \;=\; \frac{S_0}{1 + k \theta}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Validation:** This is the **single most-asked** reactor equation in KTU ESE papers. Memorize the form, units, and limiting cases:
> - As $\theta \to \infty$ (large tank), $S_e \to 0$ — perfect removal.
> - As $\theta \to 0$ (small tank), $S_e \to S_0$ — no treatment.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFR Effluent Substrate Equation

**Given:** Long tubular reactor; no axial mixing; first-order kinetics.

**Step 1:** Take a **differential slice** of the reactor at position $x$, with volume $dV = A \cdot dx$.

**Step 2:** Apply steady-state mass balance on the slice:

$$
0 \;=\; Q S - Q (S + dS) - kS \, dV
$$

The first term $QS$ is the mass entering the slice, and $Q(S+dS)$ is the mass leaving.

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$
0 \;=\; -Q \, dS - k S \, dV
$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables.

$$
\frac{dS}{S} \;=\; -\frac{k}{Q} \, dV
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from $V = 0$ (inlet) to $V = V_{\text{total}}$ (outlet), with concentration changing from $S_0$ to $S_e$.

$$
\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} \;=\; -\frac{k}{Q} \int_{0}^{V} dV
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) \;=\; -\frac{kV}{Q} \;=\; -k \theta
$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate to obtain the **PFR effluent equation**.

$$
\boxed{\,S_e \;=\; S_0 \, e^{-k \theta}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — N CSTRs in Series

**Given:** $N$ identical CSTRs in cascade, each with volume $V_i$ and HRT $\theta_i = V_i/Q$.

**Step 1:** Apply the CSTR result to the **first** tank.

$$
S_1 \;=\; \frac{S_0}{1 + k \theta_i}
$$

**Step 2:** For the second tank, $S_1$ is the *inlet* and $S_2$ is the *outlet*.

$$
S_2 \;=\; \frac{S_1}{1 + k \theta_i} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{(1 + k \theta_i)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** By induction to the $N$-th tank:

$$
\boxed{\,S_{e,N} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{(1 + k \theta_i)^{N}}\,}
$$

**Step 4 (KTU Limiting-Case Proof):** As $N \to \infty$, with $\theta_{\text{total}} = N \theta_i$ held constant.

Using the limit identity $\lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{k \theta_{\text{total}}}{N}\right)^{N} = e^{k \theta_{\text{total}}}$:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{e,N} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{e^{k \theta_{\text{total}}}} \;=\; S_0 e^{-k \theta_{\text{total}}} \;=\; S_e^{\text{PFR}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Confirmation:** This limiting case proves mathematically that **plug flow is equivalent to an infinite cascade of infinitesimally small CSTRs** — a frequent 7-mark question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Batch Reactor Substrate-Time Profile

**Given:** A closed tank with first-order reaction; no inflow or outflow during reaction.

**Step 1:** Mass balance on the closed vessel (no flow term):

$$
V \frac{dS}{dt} \;=\; -k S V
$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $V$ and separate variables.

$$
\frac{dS}{S} \;=\; -k \, dt
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $S_0$ at $t = 0$ to $S(t)$ at time $t$.

$$
\int_{S_0}^{S} \frac{dS}{S} \;=\; -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) \;=\; -kt
$$

**Step 4:** Final form.

$$
\boxed{\,S(t) \;=\; S_0 \, e^{-k t}\,}
$$

This is **structurally identical to the PFR equation**, with time $t$ replacing space-related HRT $\theta$. This is the **fundamental duality** of batch and plug flow systems.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite)

For the same $S_0$, $k$, and $\theta$, the **removal efficiency** is ranked as:

$$
\eta_{\text{PFR}} \;\geq\; \eta_{N\text{-CSTR}, N \geq 2} \;\geq\; \eta_{\text{CSTR (single)}}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-Type):** $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $k = 0.5$ d⁻¹, $\theta = 1$ d.

| Configuration | $S_e$ (mg/L) | $\eta$ (%) |
|---|---|---|
| **CSTR (single)** | $\dfrac{300}{1 + 0.5} = 200$ | 33.3 |
| **2 CSTRs in series** | $\dfrac{300}{(1+0.5)^2} = 133.3$ | 55.6 |
| **5 CSTRs in series** | $\dfrac{300}{(1+0.5)^5} = 26.4$ | 91.2 |
| **PFR (equivalent)** | $300 \cdot e^{-0.5} = 181.8$ | 39.4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Alert:** Re-evaluate the PFR with $\theta = 1$ d:
>
> $S_e = 300 \cdot e^{-0.5 \cdot 1} = 300 \cdot 0.6065 = 181.95$ mg/L → $\eta = 39.4\%$.
>
> The PFR outperforms the single CSTR (33.3%) but is **not** equal to "5 CSTRs in series." This is a classic KTU trap — high $N$ approaches but never exceeds PFR.

## 3.6 Real Reactor Performance Correction — Including Recycle and Short-Circuiting

In real wastewater reactors, the effluent deviates from ideal CSTR/PFR. The **actual HRT distribution** is described by the **Dispersion Number**:

$$
D \;=\; \frac{\mathcal{D}}{u L}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{D}$ = axial dispersion coefficient (m²/s)
- $u$ = superficial velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = reactor length (m)

| Flow Regime | Dispersion Number | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal PFR** | $D \to 0$ | No mixing |
| **Ideal CSTR** | $D \to \infty$ | Infinite mixing |
| **Real Long Channel** | $0 < D < 0.05$ | Approximates PFR |
| **Real Stirred Tank** | $D > 0.2$ | Approximates CSTR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reactor Configuration Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Configurations in Wastewater Treatment] --> B[Idealized Flow Models]
    A --> C[Time-Sharing Systems]
    A --> D[Biofilm Carriers]
    A --> E[Hybrid Coupled Systems]

    B --> B1[Batch Reactor BR]
    B --> B2[CSTR CMFR]
    B --> B3[Plug Flow Reactor PFR]
    B --> B4[N CSTRs in Series]

    C --> C1[Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR]
    C --> C2[Intermittent Cyclic Reactor]

    D --> D1[Trickling Filter]
    D --> D2[Rotating Biological Contactor RBC]
    D --> D3[Fluidized Bed Reactor FBR]
    D --> D4[Packed Bed Reactor PBR]

    E --> E1[Membrane Bioreactor MBR]
    E --> E2[Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor MBBR]
    E --> E3[Integrated Fixed Film Activated Sludge IFAS]

    B1 --> F1[Fill React Settle Decant]
    B2 --> F2[Uniform Concentration Exit equals Internal]
    B3 --> F3[Axial Gradient No Backmixing]

    C1 --> G1[Time-equivalent of CSTR with batch settling]
    D1 --> H1[Biofilm on Rock Plastic Media]

```

    Reactor -->|PFR Path| PFRNode[Spatial Gradient dS dV equals minus kS by Q]
    CSTRNode --> EffMix[Effluent S exit CSTR = S0 over 1 + k theta]
    PFRNode --> EffPlug[Effluent S exit PFR = S0 e to power minus k theta]
    EffMix --> Quality[Effluent Quality Check]
