# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish clearly between a Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) and a Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) in terms of hydraulic characteristics and concentration profiles.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | CSTR | PFR |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixing** | Instantaneous and complete; spatial uniformity | No longitudinal mixing; complete radial mixing |
| **Concentration profile** | Spatially uniform; $S_{\text{exit}} = S_{\text{inside}}$ | Spatially varying along reactor length |
| **Time behaviour** | Time-invariant at steady state | Time-invariant at steady state |
| **KTU Example** | Aeration tank in conventional activated sludge | Long oxidation ditch, chlorine contact channel |
| **Effluent equation** | $S_e = S_0 / (1 + k\theta)$ | $S_e = S_0 e^{-k\theta}$ |
| **Efficiency for same $\theta, k$** | Lower | Higher (always superior) |

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)? Why is HRT critical in the design of biological wastewater treatment reactors?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** HRT is the average time a fluid element spends inside a reactor, defined as:

$$
\theta \;=\; \frac{V}{Q}
$$

where $V$ = reactor volume (m³) and $Q$ = volumetric inflow rate (m³/d).

**Importance (2 marks):**

1. It directly controls the **extent of biochemical reaction**: longer $\theta$ → more substrate consumption → lower effluent $S_e$.
2. It determines the **kinetic regime** (rate-limited vs. mass-transfer-limited).
3. It governs the **volumetric loading rate** (VLR = $Q S_0 / V$) and consequently the type of microorganisms that can be sustained (e.g., slow-growing nitrifiers require $\theta > 4$ d at 10 °C).
4. It influences **biomass washout** thresholds — for a CSTR with Monod kinetics, washout occurs when $\theta < 1/k$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A municipal STP receives 25,000 m³/d of sewage with BOD₅ = 350 mg/L. Design a comparison between (a) a single CSTR and (b) a plug flow reactor for achieving an effluent BOD of ≤ 30 mg/L, given the first-order rate constant $k = 0.45$ d⁻¹. Compute the required volume in each case and comment on the cost implications.**

#### **Solution:**

**Part (a) — CSTR Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the CSTR equation:

$$
S_e \;=\; \frac{S_0}{1 + k \theta} \;\Rightarrow\; 30 \;=\; \frac{350}{1 + 0.45 \theta}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for HRT.

$$
1 + 0.45 \theta \;=\; \frac{350}{30} \;=\; 11.667
$$

$$
0.45 \theta \;=\; 10.667 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_{\text{CSTR}} \;=\; \frac{10.667}{0.45} \;=\; 23.704 \text{ d}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute required volume.

$$
V_{\text{CSTR}} \;=\; Q \cdot \theta \;=\; 25{,}000 \times 23.704 \;=\; 592{,}610 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Removal efficiency.

$$
\eta_{\text{CSTR}} \;=\; \frac{0.45 \times 23.704}{1 + 0.45 \times 23.704} \;=\; \frac{10.667}{11.667} \;=\; 91.4\%
$$

---

**Part (b) — PFR Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the PFR equation:

$$
S_e \;=\; S_0 e^{-k \theta} \;\Rightarrow\; 30 \;=\; 350 \, e^{-0.45 \theta}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for HRT.

$$
e^{-0.45 \theta} \;=\; \frac{30}{350} \;=\; 0.08571
$$

$$
-0.45 \theta \;=\; \ln(0.08571) \;=\; -2.4567
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{PFR}} \;=\; \frac{2.4567}{0.45} \;=\; 5.459 \text{ d}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute required volume.

$$
V_{\text{PFR}} \;=\; 25{,}000 \times 5.459 \;=\; 136{,}485 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Removal efficiency.

$$
\eta_{\text{PFR}} \;=\; 1 - e^{-0.45 \times 5.459} \;=\; 1 - 0.0857 \;=\; 91.4\%
$$

**Step 5 (Conclusion & Cost Comment):**

Both configurations achieve 91.4% BOD removal. However:

$$
\frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} \;=\; \frac{592{,}610}{136{,}485} \;=\; 4.34
$$

The CSTR requires **4.34× the volume** of the PFR. Civil cost scales roughly with volume, so the PFR is **dramatically cheaper** to construct. However, CSTRs are more **tolerant to shock loadings** because the entire tank acts as a buffer, while PFRs risk short-circuiting if mixing is poor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert units** consistently (e.g., $Q$ in m³/d, $k$ in d⁻¹, $\theta$ in d).
> 2. Using $\ln$ vs. $\log_{10}$ interchangeably in the PFR equation. KTU expects **natural logarithm** for $e^{-k\theta}$.
> 3. Not stating the **steady-state assumption** explicitly — losing 1 full mark.
> 4. Failing to **comment on practical aspects** (cost, footprint, shock load tolerance) — losing 2 marks in the conclusion.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**An industrial ETP treats a high-strength wastewater stream of 5,000 m³/d with $S_0 = 1{,}200$ mg/L BOD, using three equal-sized CSTRs in series. Given $k = 0.8$ d⁻¹:**

**(a) Determine the volume of each tank such that the overall effluent BOD is ≤ 50 mg/L.**
**(b) If the same total volume were configured as a single CSTR, what would the effluent BOD be? Compare with the series arrangement.**

#### **Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three CSTRs in Series (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the $N$-CSTR series equation:

$$
S_{e,N} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{(1 + k \theta_i)^{N}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $S_{e,3} = 50$ mg/L, $S_0 = 1{,}200$ mg/L, $k = 0.8$ d⁻¹, $N = 3$:

$$
50 \;=\; \frac{1{,}200}{(1 + 0.8 \theta_i)^{3}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve.

$$
(1 + 0.8 \theta_i)^{3} \;=\; \frac{1{,}200}{50} \;=\; 24
$$

$$
1 + 0.8 \theta_i \;=\; 24^{1/3} \;=\; 2.8845
$$

$$
0.8 \theta_i \;=\; 1.8845 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_i \;=\; \frac{1.8845}{0.8} \;=\; 2.356 \text{ d}
$$

**Step 4:** Volume of each tank.

$$
V_i \;=\; Q \cdot \theta_i \;=\; 5{,}000 \times 2.356 \;=\; 11{,}779 \text{ m}^3 \approx 11{,}780 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Total volume.

$$
V_{\text{total, series}} \;=\; 3 \times 11{,}780 \;=\; 35{,}340 \text{ m}^3
$$

---

**Part (b) — Same Volume as Single CSTR (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total HRT for the single CSTR = $\theta_{\text{total}} = 3 \theta_i = 3 \times 2.356 = 7.067$ d.

**Step 2:** Apply the single CSTR equation:

$$
S_e^{\text{single}} \;=\; \frac{1{,}200}{1 + 0.8 \times 7.067} \;=\; \frac{1{,}200}{1 + 5.653} \;=\; \frac{1{,}200}{6.653} \;=\; 180.4 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3:** Comparison.

| Configuration | Effluent BOD (mg/L) | Total Volume (m³) |
|---|---|---|
| **3 CSTRs in series** | **50** | 35,340 |
| **1 single CSTR** | **180.4** | 35,340 |
| **Equivalent PFR (theoretical limit)** | $1{,}200 \cdot e^{-0.8 \times 7.067} = 16.0$ | 35,340 |

**Step 4 (Conclusion):** With **identical total reactor volume**, the 3-CSTR-in-series arrangement produces an effluent **3.6× lower** in BOD than the single CSTR. The series approach is always superior because each subsequent stage sees a progressively lower inlet concentration, driving the reaction forward more efficiently. The **PFR limit** is the absolute best performance achievable with the same total volume.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Computing $V_i$ instead of $V_{\text{total}}$ when asked for "the total volume" — losing 1 mark.
> 2. Forgetting the **cube root** in Step 3 of Part (a) and writing $24 = (1 + 0.8\theta_i)^3 \Rightarrow 1 + 0.8\theta_i = 8$ — a common algebraic mistake. Always verify by squaring/cubing.
> 3. Not commenting on the **PFR limit** (theoretical benchmark) — losing 2 marks in the comparison.
> 4. Mixing up which $\theta$ belongs to the series (per-tank) vs. total — losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for Reactor Configurations:**

- **Three idealized reactors** govern all reactor design: Batch, CSTR, and PFR. **All other configurations are variations or combinations.**

- **CSTR equation:** $S_e = S_0 / (1 + k\theta)$ — *always denominator is additive*.

- **PFR equation:** $S_e = S_0 \cdot e^{-k\theta}$ — *always exponential decay*.

- **Batch equation:** $S(t) = S_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ — *structurally identical to PFR; time replaces space*.

- **$N$ CSTRs in series:** $S_{e,N} = S_0 / (1 + k\theta_i)^{N}$ — *as $N \to \infty$, becomes PFR*.

- **Efficiency order (same $V$, same $Q$):** $\eta_{\text{PFR}} > \eta_{\text{N-CSTR (N=2,3…)}} > \eta_{\text{single CSTR}}$.

- **HRT** $= V / Q$ — the **master design parameter** for volume calculation.

- **Washout condition (CSTR, Monod kinetics):** $\theta_{\text{critical}} = 1 / (\mu_{\max} - k_d)$.

- **SBR = Time-shared CSTR + settler in a single tank** — saves civil cost; popular for small communities and package plants.

- **MBR** decouples HRT and SRT: small $\theta_h$ with very large $\theta_c$ → small footprint + high effluent quality.

- **Real reactors are non-ideal**; quantified by **Dispersion Number** $D = \mathcal{D} / (uL)$:
  - $D < 0.05 \Rightarrow$ plug-flow-like.
  - $D > 0.2 \Rightarrow$ mixed-flow-like.

- **For KTU exams:** always state the **steady-state assumption**, **uniform mixing** (for CSTR), and **no axial mixing** (for PFR) before applying equations.

- **Reactor selection rule-of-thumb for KTU-style problems:**
  - *High-strength, high-rate* → PFR
  - *Shock loads, variable influent* → CSTR
  - *Small flow, intermittent* → SBR
  - *Reuse-quality, water-scarce* → MBR
  - *Low-strength, low-energy* → Trickling Filter / RBC
  - *High-strength, anaerobic* → UASB / Fluidized Bed

- **Volume ratio key formula:**
  $$
  \frac{V_{\text{CSTR}}}{V_{\text{PFR}}} \;=\; \frac{(S_0 / S_e - 1)}{\ln(S_0 / S_e)}
  $$
  — frequently appears as a 3-mark derivation question in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro-floatation: Factors Affecting Electro Floatation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electro-floatation: Factors Affecting Electro Floatation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electro-floatation (Electroflotation)** is an advanced physico-chemical wastewater treatment process in which extremely fine bubbles of hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) gases are generated *in situ* at the surface of sacrificial or inert electrodes by the passage of a direct electric current through the wastewater. These micro-bubbles (typically **20–70 µm** in diameter) attach themselves to the surface of suspended, colloidal, or emulsified particles, oil droplets, and precipitated flocs, reducing their effective density and causing them to rise to the liquid surface, where they are mechanically skimmed off as a thickened sludge blanket.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Electro-floatation is officially classified under **Tertiary / Advanced Treatment Units** and is distinct from Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) because the gas is generated electrolytically at the electrodes, NOT injected as supersaturated water.

The governing electrochemical reaction at the electrodes (using inert anodes such as stainless steel, graphite, or titanium) is the **electrolysis of water**:

$$\text{At Cathode (Reduction):} \quad 2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$$

$$\text{At Anode (Oxidation):} \quad 2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 \uparrow + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Overall stoichiometry of water electrolysis:**
> $2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{Electricity}} 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$
> Note that the volume of H₂ produced is **double** that of O₂ (2:1 molar ratio), which has direct implications for bubble density distribution in the flotation tank.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are trying to **separate fine sand from water in a fish tank**:
- If you blow large air bubbles through an aquarium pump, the bubbles are too big — they just burst at the surface and do not carry the sand with them.
- Now imagine attaching thousands of **tiny helium-filled party balloons (each 1/50th the size of a sand grain)** to every sand particle. The sand, though heavy, is now buoyant and floats to the top like a hot-air balloon.

**Electro-floatation works exactly like this:**
The electric current is like a magical "bubble factory" attached to metal plates at the bottom of the tank. It produces microscopic balloons (H₂ and O₂ bubbles) so tiny and so numerous that they latch onto dirt particles (suspended solids, oil, metals) and gently carry them to the surface — where a scraper simply scoops them off, leaving clean water below.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Faraday Constant (F)** | **96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| **Bubble diameter in EF** | **20–70 µm** | 5–10× smaller than DAF bubbles |
| **Typical current density (i)** | **50–200 A/m²** | Drives gas generation rate |
| **Operating voltage (V)** | **5–20 V DC** | Low-voltage, high-current |
| **Electrode spacing (d)** | **10–30 mm** | Affects resistance and bubble path |
| **Gas volume ratio (H₂:O₂)** | **2:1** | From stoichiometry of water electrolysis |
| **Specific power consumption** | **0.1–1.0 kWh/m³** | Comparable to DAF |

> [!TIP]
> **Why micro-bubbles matter:** Smaller bubbles have a **higher surface-area-to-volume ratio**, providing more attachment sites for particles. This makes electro-floatation more efficient than conventional air flotation for colloidal suspensions and emulsified oils.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Electro-floatation — Step-by-Step Logic

The electro-floatation process can be broken down into **four sequential physical-chemical steps**:

1. **Electrolytic Gas Generation**
   - A DC power supply drives current through electrodes immersed in wastewater.
   - At the cathode: H₂ bubbles form via reduction of water.
   - At the anode: O₂ bubbles form via oxidation of water.
   - If sacrificial electrodes (Fe, Al) are used, **simultaneous electro-coagulation** occurs — metal ions (Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺) are released and form coagulant hydroxides.

2. **Nucleation and Growth of Micro-bubbles**
   - Dissolved gas molecules accumulate on the electrode surface.
   - When local supersaturation is reached, bubbles nucleate.
   - Bubbles detach once the buoyancy force exceeds the adhesion force.

3. **Bubble–Particle Attachment (Collision & Adhesion)**
   - Three sub-mechanisms drive attachment:
     - **Brownian diffusion** (for sub-micron particles)
     - **Inertial interception** (for 1–50 µm particles)
     - **Sedimentation/settling** (for larger particles onto rising bubbles)

4. **Rise and Sludge Collection**
   - Attached particles rise as a gas–solid aggregate.
   - A surface scraper (skimmer) removes the floating sludge at a rate matched to the rise velocity.

---

### 2.2 The Critical Factors Affecting Electro-floatation

The efficiency of an electro-floatation unit is governed by **10 key design and operational parameters**, each of which KTU examiners frequently test:

#### A. Current Density (i = I / A_e)
- **Definition:** Current per unit area of electrode surface, in **A/m²**.
- **Effect:**
  - ↑ Current density → ↑ gas generation rate (per Faraday's law)
  - ↑ Current density → ↓ bubble size (more nucleation sites)
  - ↑↑ Excessive current density → ↑ heating, ↑ power cost, coarse bubbles, electrode pitting
- **Optimum range:** 50–200 A/m² for wastewater.

#### B. Electrode Material
| Material | Type | Behavior | Best Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Iron (Fe)** | Sacrificial | Releases Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ → coagulation | Industrial effluents with colloids |
| **Aluminium (Al)** | Sacrificial | Releases Al³⁺ → coagulant | Domestic + oily wastewater |
| **Stainless Steel (SS 304/316)** | Inert | No dissolution, long life | Clean water polishing |
| **Graphite** | Inert | Cheap but erodes at high i | Lab-scale studies |
| **Titanium (Ti/MMO coated)** | Inert | Premium, dimensionally stable | High-end industrial plants |

> [!NOTE]
> When **sacrificial electrodes** are used, the process is called **Electro-coagulation–Electro-floatation (EC–EF)** — a hybrid advanced process. KTU 2024 explicitly lists this hybrid under Module 4.

#### C. Electrode Spacing (d)
- **Smaller spacing (5–10 mm):**
  - Low voltage requirement
  - Higher gas hold-up
  - Risk of short-circuit and turbulence
- **Larger spacing (>30 mm):**
  - Higher voltage → higher power cost (P = V × I)
  - Lower collision efficiency
- **Optimum:** 10–30 mm depending on conductivity.

#### D. Electrolyte Concentration / Conductivity (σ)
- Higher conductivity → lower cell voltage (V_cell = I × R = I / σ × d/A)
- **Below 200 µS/cm:** Add NaCl or Na₂SO₄ to enhance conductivity.
- **Excess Cl⁻:** May generate toxic Cl₂ gas at anode — a KTU favourite question!

#### E. pH of Solution
- Affects bubble size and surface charge of particles (Zeta potential).
- **Acidic (pH 4–6):** Smaller H₂ bubbles, slower rise → high contact time.
- **Alkaline (pH 8–10):** Larger O₂ bubbles, faster rise → good for big flocs.

#### F. Temperature (T)
- ↑ T → ↑ gas volume (Charles' law)
- ↑ T → ↓ gas solubility → bigger bubbles
- ↑ T → ↑ conductivity → less voltage drop
- **Optimum:** 20–35 °C (ambient to body-temperature range).

#### G. Bubble Size Distribution
- Direct function of current density and electrode surface roughness.
- **Smaller bubbles (20–30 µm):** Better for colloids and emulsions.
- **Larger bubbles (50–70 µm):** Better for coarse suspended solids.

#### H. Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)
- Time wastewater spends in the flotation cell.
- Typical: **10–30 minutes** for batch; **20–60 min** for continuous.
- Too short → incomplete flotation; too long → excessive tankage cost.

#### I. Mode of Electrode Connection
- **Monopolar (parallel):** All electrodes connected to one polarity per side; uniform current distribution.
- **Bipolar (series):** Only end electrodes connected; intermediate electrodes become polarized → simpler wiring.

#### J. Type of Wastewater and Pollutant Characteristics
- **Hydrophobic particles** (oils, fats): Float easily.
- **Hydrophilic colloids:** Require coagulant dosing or sacrificial electrodes.
- **High TDS wastewater:** May cause excessive current → overheating.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below — these are the top 6 equations tested in KTU 2024 ESE questions on Electro-floatation.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Faraday's Law (Mass of Gas)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | m = mass (g), I = current (A), t = time (s), M = molar mass (g/mol), n = electrons transferred, F = 96,485 C/mol | Mass of H₂ or O₂ released |
| 2 | **Volume of Gas at STP** | $V_{STP} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 22{,}400}{n \cdot F}$ | V in mL, I in A, t in s | Gas volume at NTP |
| 3 | **Volume of Gas at T, P** | $V = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot R \cdot T}{P \cdot n \cdot F}$ | V in m³, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T in K, P in Pa | Real condition gas volume |
| 4 | **Current Density** | $i = \dfrac{I}{A_e}$ | i in A/m², A_e = electrode area (m²) | Controls bubble size & rate |
| 5 | **Power Consumption** | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I = \dfrac{I^2 \cdot d}{\sigma \cdot A_e}$ | P in W, d = electrode gap (m), σ = conductivity (S/m) | Energy efficiency check |
| 6 | **Specific Energy** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{waste}}$ | E_sp in kWh/m³, V_waste in m³ | Energy per m³ treated |
| 7 | **Bubble Rise (Stokes' Law)** | $v_{rise} = \dfrac{g \cdot d_b^2 \cdot (\rho_{water} - \rho_{gas})}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | v in m/s, d_b = bubble diameter, μ = dynamic viscosity | Estimate floatation time |
| 8 | **Gas Yield Ratio (H₂:O₂)** | $\dfrac{V_{H_2}}{V_{O_2}} = 2$ | — | Stoichiometry check |
| 9 | **Efficiency of Flotation** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_i - C_e}{C_i} \times 100\%$ | C_i = inlet, C_e = outlet concentration | Removal efficiency |
| 10 | **Current Efficiency** | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical(Faraday)}} \times 100\%$ | — | Check for side reactions |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electro-floatation is widely used in the following **practical engineering scenarios** that you may see in KTU case-study questions:

1. **Oil & Grease Removal** — Petroleum refineries (separation of emulsified oils from produced water).
2. **Heavy Metal Precipitate Recovery** — Mining effluents (Cu(OH)₂, Cr(OH)₃ floated and recovered for metallurgy).
3. **Dairy and Food Industry** — Fats, oils, proteins (cheese whey, slaughterhouse waste).
4. **Laundry Wastewater** — Surfactant-rich streams.
5. **Marine Aquaculture** — Removal of fish excreta and uneaten feed.
6. **Pre-treatment to Membrane Filtration** — Reduces fouling load by 60–80%.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When KTU 2024 asks for a **comparison between DAF and Electro-floatation**, highlight these three key points:
> (i) DAF requires saturation tanks and high-pressure pumps; EF does not.
> (ii) EF bubble size is smaller (20–70 µm) vs DAF (40–100 µm).
> (iii) EF can simultaneously coagulate (with sacrificial electrodes), DAF cannot.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Gas Generation Rate from Faraday's Law

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the volume of gas produced in an electro-floatation cell and the applied current, given a specific temperature and pressure.

#### Given Data (typical KTU problem set):
- Applied current, $I = 50 \text{ A}$
- Operating time, $t = 1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}$
- Temperature, $T = 298 \text{ K} \;(25^\circ \text{C})$
- Pressure, $P = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Faraday constant, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$
- Universal gas constant, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- For H₂: $n = 2$ electrons, $M_{H_2} = 2 \text{ g/mol}$

#### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Express Faraday's First Law in terms of moles of electrons transferred:**

The charge passed through the electrolytic cell is the product of current and time:

$$Q = I \cdot t = 50 \times 3600 = 180{,}000 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred:**

Since one mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant (F = 96,485 C):

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{96{,}485} = 1.8655 \text{ mol } e^-$$

**Step 3 — Relate moles of electrons to moles of gas using stoichiometry:**

For the cathode half-reaction: $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$

2 moles of electrons produce 1 mole of H₂. Therefore:

$$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{n_{H_2/electrons}} = \dfrac{1.8655}{2} = 0.9327 \text{ mol } H_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to find gas volume:**

The ideal gas law states that $PV = nRT$, hence:

$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2} \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.9327 \times 8.314 \times 298}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.9327 \times 8.314 \times 298}{101{,}300}$$

$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{2310.04}{101{,}300} = 0.02280 \text{ m}^3 = 22.80 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the corresponding O₂ volume at the anode:**

By stoichiometry, $V_{H_2} : V_{O_2} = 2 : 1$

$$V_{O_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{2} = \dfrac{22.80}{2} = 11.40 \text{ L}$$

**Step 7 — Total gas volume generated:**

$$V_{total} = V_{H_2} + V_{O_2} = 22.80 + 11.40 = 34.20 \text{ L per hour}$$

**Final Consolidated Faraday Gas-Generation Equation (general form):**

$$\boxed{\;V_{gas} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot \dfrac{R \cdot T}{P}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Effect of Current Density on Bubble Size

**Problem Statement:**
An electro-floatation cell uses two parallel plate electrodes of dimensions 50 cm × 80 cm, separated by a 20 mm gap. The cell is operated at a current of 40 A with wastewater conductivity 1500 µS/cm.
- (a) Calculate the current density.
- (b) Calculate the cell voltage (use $V_{cell} = I \cdot d / (\sigma \cdot A_e)$).
- (c) Determine the power consumed and specific energy if 5 m³ of wastewater is treated in 30 minutes.
- (d) Comment on whether the current density falls in the **optimum range of 50–200 A/m²**.

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): Current Density

Electrode area (single side) = $0.50 \times 0.80 = 0.40 \text{ m}^2$

$$i = \dfrac{I}{A_e} = \dfrac{40}{0.40} = 100 \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** This is **squarely in the optimum range (50–200 A/m²)** — the unit will produce small, uniform micro-bubbles efficiently.

#### Part (b): Cell Voltage

Given: $d = 0.020 \text{ m}$, $\sigma = 1500 \text{ µS/cm} = 0.15 \text{ S/m}$

$$V_{cell} = \dfrac{I \cdot d}{\sigma \cdot A_e} = \dfrac{40 \times 0.020}{0.15 \times 0.40}$$

$$V_{cell} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.06} = 13.33 \text{ V}$$

#### Part (c): Power and Specific Energy

$$P = V_{cell} \cdot I = 13.33 \times 40 = 533.2 \text{ W} \approx 0.533 \text{ kW}$$

For 5 m³ treated in 30 minutes (0.5 h):

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{P \times t_{hr}}{V_{waste}} = \dfrac{0.533 \times 0.5}{5} = 0.0533 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$

**Interpretation:** This is **very low** — efficient operation (typical EF range is 0.1–1.0 kWh/m³).

#### Part (d): Conclusion on Current Density

$i = 100 \text{ A/m}^2$ lies within the optimum 50–200 A/m² envelope. Bubble size will be approximately **30–50 µm** — ideal for colloidal and emulsified pollutant removal.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Affects Process Performance

| Factor | ↑ Factor → ↑ Bubble Size? | ↑ Factor → ↑ Gas Yield? | ↑ Factor → ↑ Power Cost? | Optimum Range |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Current density (i) | No (smaller bubbles) | **Yes (∝ i)** | **Yes (∝ i²)** | 50–200 A/m² |
| Electrode spacing (d) | No | No | **Yes (∝ d)** | 10–30 mm |
| Conductivity (σ) | No | No | **No (∝ 1/σ)** | > 1000 µS/cm |
| Temperature (T) | **Yes (gas expands)** | **Yes (∝ T)** | Slight ↑ | 20–35 °C |
| pH | Mild | Mild | Mild | 6–8 |
| NaCl addition | Mild | Mild | No | 0.5–2 g/L |
| HRT | Indirect | Indirect | No | 20–60 min |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering trade-off:** To minimize power cost, **increase conductivity and decrease electrode spacing**, but keep current density ≤ 200 A/m² to avoid excessive heating and bubble coalescence.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electro-floatation Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **functional architecture** of a typical electro-floatation cell, including all major components, the path of wastewater, the path of generated gas, and the location of skimming operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater Inlet] --> B[Inlet Distribution Chamber]
    B --> C[Electro Flotation Tank]
    C --> D[Cathode Plate Grid]
    C --> E[Anode Plate Grid]
    F[DC Power Supply Rectifier] --> D
    F --> E
    D --> G[H2 Bubble Generation at Cathode]
    E --> G2[O2 Bubble Generation at Anode]
    G --> H[Micro Bubble Particle Attachment Zone]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Floating Sludge Blanket at Surface]
    I --> J[Rotating Skimmer Blade]
    J --> K[Sludge Hopper and Collection Trough]
    C --> L[Clarified Effluent Outlet]
    K --> M[Sludge to Thickener or Dewatering Unit]
    L --> N[Effluent to Tertiary Treatment or Disinfection]
    subgraph CONTROLPANEL [Control and Monitoring Panel]
        O[Voltage Current Meter]
        P[pH and Conductivity Sensor]
        Q[Temperature Sensor]
        R[Scraper RPM Controller]
    end
    F --> O
    C --> P
    C --> Q
    J --> R
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid lines: Process flow (wastewater, sludge, gas).
- Dashed lines: Electrical/control signal connections.
- The `CONTROLPANEL` is a logical grouping of monitoring instruments that regulate the DC power supply and skimmer.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Factors Affecting Electro-floatation

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    ROOT --> A2[Electrode Parameters]

    ROOT --> A5[Operational Parameters]
    A1 --> B1[Current Density]
    A1 --> B2[Cell Voltage]
    A1 --> B3[Power Consumption]
    A2 --> C1[Material Type Fe Al SS Graphite Ti]
    A2 --> C2[Electrode Spacing 10 to 30 mm]
    A2 --> C3[Connection Mode Monopolar vs Bipolar]
    A2 --> C4[Surface Area and Roughness]
    A3 --> D1[pH of Wastewater]
    A3 --> D2[Conductivity and TDS]
    A3 --> D3[Electrolyte Type NaCl Na2SO4]
    A4 --> E1[Bubble Size 20 to 70 micrometer]
    A4 --> E2[Rise Velocity Stokes Law]
    A4 --> E3[Gas Yield Ratio H2 to O2 equal 2 to 1]

```

### 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of the Electro-floatation Cell

```mermaid

    DIST --> CELL{Tank with Electrodes}
    subgraph TANK [Electro-flotation Cell - Cross Section]
        direction LR
        ANODE[Anode Plate Plus] --> GAS1[O2 bubbles rise]
        GAS1 --> SLUDGE[Sludge Layer at Top]
        SLUDGE --> SCRAPER[Skimmer Arm]
        CATHODE[Cathode Plate Minus] --> GAS2[H2 bubbles rise]
        GAS2 --> SLUDGE
    end
    CELL --> EFF[Clarified Effluent Out]
    SCRAPER --> SLUDGEOUT[Sludge to Disposal]
    DC[DC Source 5 to 20 V] --> ANODE
    DC --> CATHODE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Define electro-floatation. Write any three advantages of electro-floatation over Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF).** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electro-floatation is an advanced wastewater treatment process in which very fine bubbles of hydrogen and oxygen gases are generated *in situ* at the surface of electrodes by the passage of a direct electric current through the wastewater. These micro-bubbles (typically 20–70 µm) attach to suspended, colloidal, or emulsified particles and float them to the surface for skimming.

**Three advantages over DAF:**
1. **Smaller bubble size** (20–70 µm vs 40–100 µm) → higher attachment efficiency for colloids.
2. **No saturation tank or high-pressure pump required** → lower mechanical complexity and capital cost.
3. **Dual-function capability** with sacrificial electrodes (Fe/Al) → simultaneous electro-coagulation and flotation, eliminating the need for separate chemical dosing.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **State Faraday's law of electrolysis. How is it used to determine the volume of gas generated in an electro-floatation cell?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Application in electro-floatation:**
- The charge passed is $Q = I \times t$.
- The moles of gas produced are $n_{gas} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ (where $n$ is the number of electrons per mole of gas; $n=2$ for H₂, $n=4$ for O₂).
- The volume of gas at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is then calculated using the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$V_{gas} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot \dfrac{R \cdot T}{P}$$

This equation is critical for sizing the gas-generation capacity of an electro-floatation cell and computing the bubble flux per unit time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] — QUESTION A**
> **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the working principle of an electro-floatation cell. Discuss the electrochemical reactions occurring at the anode and cathode. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** List and explain any **seven** factors that affect the performance of an electro-floatation unit. *(7 Marks)*

**OR**

> **Q3. QUESTION B (Alternative)**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the volume of gas generated in an electro-floatation cell using Faraday's law and the ideal gas equation. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** An electro-floatation unit operates at 60 A current for 2 hours treating 10 m³ of wastewater. The cell voltage is 15 V. Calculate: *(i)* the volume of H₂ and O₂ produced at STP, *(ii)* the specific energy consumption in kWh/m³, and *(iii)* the current density if the electrode plate dimensions are 60 cm × 100 cm. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### SOLUTION — Question 3A (Part a & Part b)

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Reactions:**

An electro-floatation cell consists of a rectangular tank fitted with arrays of closely spaced **anode (+) and cathode (−) plates** connected to a **DC power supply (5–20 V)**. Wastewater enters the cell through an inlet distributor and flows between the electrodes.

When DC current passes:
- At the **cathode**: Water is reduced, releasing **hydrogen gas** and hydroxide ions.
  $$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$$
- At the **anode**: Water is oxidised, releasing **oxygen gas** and hydrogen ions.
  $$2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 \uparrow + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$

These gases form **micro-bubbles (20–70 µm)** at the electrode surface. The bubbles rise, attach to suspended/colloidal particles, and carry them to the surface as a thick floating sludge layer, which is removed by a **rotating skimmer arm**. The clarified effluent exits from the bottom of the tank.

**Part (b) — Seven Factors Affecting Performance:**

1. **Current density (i):** Higher current → higher gas generation, smaller bubbles, but higher power cost. Optimum 50–200 A/m².
2. **Electrode material:** Inert (SS, graphite) for pure flotation; sacrificial (Fe, Al) for hybrid EC–EF process.
3. **Electrode spacing:** Smaller spacing → lower voltage but risk of short circuit. Optimum 10–30 mm.
4. **Electrolyte concentration/conductivity:** Higher σ → lower cell voltage; low σ requires NaCl addition.
5. **pH of solution:** Affects bubble size and zeta potential of particles. Best near neutral pH.
6. **Temperature:** Higher T → bigger gas volume (Charles' law) → better flotation but increases resistance.
7. **Bubble size and HRT:** Smaller bubbles improve attachment; HRT of 20–60 min ensures complete particle removal.

*(Any 7 factors with explanation = 7 marks; 1 mark per factor explained fully.)*

---

#### SOLUTION — Question 3B (Part a & Part b)

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting from **Faraday's First Law**, the mass of gas produced at an electrode is:

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

The number of moles of gas produced is:

$$n_{gas} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Applying the **Ideal Gas Equation**, $PV = nRT$, the volume of gas at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is:

$$V_{gas} = \dfrac{n_{gas} \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

Substituting $n_{gas}$:

$$\boxed{\;V_{gas} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot \dfrac{R \cdot T}{P}\;}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** I = 60 A, t = 2 h = 7200 s, V_cell = 15 V, V_waste = 10 m³, A_e = 0.6 × 1.0 = 0.6 m².

**(i) Volume of H₂ and O₂ at STP (T = 273 K, P = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa):**

For H₂ (n = 2):

$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{60 \times 7200}{2 \times 96{,}485} \times \dfrac{8.314 \times 273}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$

$$V_{H_2} = 2.239 \times 0.0224 = 0.05015 \text{ m}^3 = 50.15 \text{ L}$$

For O₂ (n = 4):

$$V_{O_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2}}{2} = 25.07 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) Specific energy consumption:**

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{waste}} = \dfrac{15 \times 60 \times 2}{10} = 180 \text{ Wh/m}^3 = 0.18 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$

**(iii) Current density:**

$$i = \dfrac{I}{A_e} = \dfrac{60}{0.6} = 100 \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** Current density of 100 A/m² lies in the **optimum range (50–200 A/m²)**; the unit is operating efficiently.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Stoichiometry sign errors:** Students frequently use $n=2$ for *both* H₂ and O₂. The correct values are $n=2$ for H₂ and $n=4$ for O₂. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 2. **Unit confusion in Faraday's law:** Mixing seconds and hours in the same equation. Always convert $t$ to **seconds**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 2:1 gas ratio:** KTU expects students to explicitly state that $V_{H_2} = 2 \times V_{O_2}$ when one value is calculated.
> 4. **Confusing electro-floatation with electro-coagulation:** These are *related* but distinct processes. EC = metal dissolution; EF = bubble generation. When using sacrificial electrodes, BOTH happen simultaneously.
> 5. **Ignoring temperature/pressure correction:** Gas volume at STP ≠ gas volume at operating conditions. Always use $V = nRT/P$ for real conditions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Electro-floatation (Module 4):**

- ☐ **Definition:** In-situ generation of H₂ and O₂ bubbles by electrolysis of water to float pollutants.
- ☐ **Overall reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ (2:1 volumetric ratio).
- ☐ **Cathode reaction:** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$.
- ☐ **Anode reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$.
- ☐ **Bubble size:** 20–70 µm (much smaller than DAF).
- ☐ **Optimum current density:** 50–200 A/m².
- ☐ **Optimum electrode spacing:** 10–30 mm.
- ☐ **Optimum conductivity:** > 1000 µS/cm (use NaCl if low).
- ☐ **Optimum pH:** 6–8 (near neutral).
- ☐ **Optimum temperature:** 20–35 °C.
- ☐ **Optimum HRT:** 20–60 minutes.
- ☐ **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- ☐ **Gas volume equation:** $V_{gas} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \cdot \dfrac{R \cdot T}{P}$.
- ☐ **Power equation:** $P = V_{cell} \cdot I = \dfrac{I^2 \cdot d}{\sigma \cdot A_e}$.
- ☐ **Specific energy:** $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{V_{waste}}$ (range 0.1–1.0 kWh/m³).
- ☐ **Sacrificial electrodes (Fe, Al):** Hybrid EC–EF process — simultaneous coagulation and flotation.
- ☐ **Inert electrodes (SS, graphite, Ti):** Pure flotation, no metal dissolution.
- ☐ **Monopolar vs Bipolar connection:** Monopolar = uniform current; Bipolar = simplified wiring.
- ☐ **Toxic by-product warning:** Excess Cl⁻ → Cl₂ generation at anode.
- ☐ **Major applications:** Oil & grease, heavy metals, dairy, laundry, aquaculture, pre-treatment to membranes.
- ☐ **DAF vs EF:** EF has smaller bubbles, no saturation tank, can do simultaneous coagulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactor Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment (OECHT725)**, a **Reactor Configuration** refers to the specific geometric and hydraulic arrangement of a treatment vessel (tank, basin, or channel) in which physical, chemical, and biological reactions are carried out to remove dissolved and suspended contaminants from wastewater. The configuration defines the **flow pattern** (idealized as either perfectly mixed or plug-flow), the **mixing regime** (complete mixing, partial mixing, or no mixing), and the **mode of operation** (continuous or batch).

According to the standard ideal-reactor model used in KTU-2024 environmental engineering analysis, every real wastewater treatment unit is mathematically approximated as one of three ideal configurations or a combination thereof:

1. **Batch Reactor (BR)** — A completely mixed, non-steady-state vessel.
2. **Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** — Also called Mixed Flow Reactor (MFR) or Continuous-Flow Stirred Tank Reactor (CFSTR).
3. **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — Also called Tubular Flow Reactor (TFR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of reactors, the derivation of their performance equations, and the comparison of CSTR vs PFR for first-order and zero-order kinetics are **high-weightage topics** (frequently asked in Part B, 14-mark questions).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **kitchen** preparing a large batch of tea for a party:

- **Batch Reactor (BR)**: Imagine you boil all the water, add tea leaves and sugar **once**, let it brew for a fixed time, and then serve the entire quantity. The concentration of tea flavor changes *with time* inside the vessel — strong at the end, weak at the start.
- **CSTR (Completely Stirred Tank Reactor)**: Imagine a giant **boiling cauldron** where fresh water and tea leaves are continuously poured in at the top, while brewed tea is continuously drawn out from the bottom, and a giant ladle keeps the contents perfectly uniform at every instant. The concentration inside the tank is *the same as the concentration leaving it*.
- **PFR (Plug Flow Reactor)**: Imagine a **long narrow pipe** filled with tea leaves, into which fresh water enters at one end. Each "slug" of water travels through the pipe like a train — it never mixes with the slug in front or behind. As it travels, it gets more and more colored, and exits fully brewed at the far end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Activated sludge aeration tanks are designed as **CSTRs** (rectangular basins with diffusers), while **stabilization ponds** and **chlorine contact tanks** behave more like **plug flow** units. A **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** combines the operational flexibility of a batch reactor with continuous-flow influent management.

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics Used in Reactor Analysis

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Unit |
|:------:|:----------|:-------------:|
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ |
| $V$ | Reactor volume | $\text{m}^3$ |
| $\tau$ (or $\theta$) | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | **hours** or days |
| $C$ | Substrate concentration | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| $S$ | Substrate (biological notation) | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| $X$ | Biomass concentration | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| $k$ | Reaction rate constant | depends on order |
| $r$ | Rate of reaction | $\text{mg/L}\cdot\text{day}$ |
| $\theta_c$ (MCRT) | Mean Cell Residence Time | **days** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Ideal Reactor Configurations

### 2.1.1 Batch Reactor (BR)

- **Operational mode**: Fill → React → Settle → Draw → Idle (cyclic).
- **Flow characteristic**: No inflow or outflow during the reaction period.
- **State condition**: **Unsteady-state** (concentration changes with time).
- **Mixing**: Contents are perfectly uniform at all times.

**Mass balance on substrate (general):**
$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Reaction}$$

$$V\frac{dC}{dt} = 0 - 0 - V(-r) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dC}{dt} = r$$

For degradation: $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC^{n}$, integrated to find the **reaction time** $t$ needed to drop from $C_0$ to $C$.

### 2.1.2 Completely Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)

- **Operational mode**: Continuous inflow and outflow at constant rate $Q$.
- **Flow characteristic**: Perfect back-mixing; contents uniform everywhere.
- **State condition**: **Steady-state** (concentration inside = concentration at exit).
- **Key assumption**: $C_{in} \neq C_{out}$, but $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are constant in time.

**Steady-state mass balance:**
$$QC_{in} - QC_{out} - V(-r) = 0$$

$$Q(C_{in} - C_{out}) = V(-r)$$

Dividing by $Q$:
$$C_{in} - C_{out} = \tau(-r) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{V}{Q}$$

### 2.1.3 Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)

- **Operational mode**: Continuous flow; no longitudinal mixing.
- **Flow characteristic**: Each fluid element travels as a discrete "plug" with uniform velocity.
- **State condition**: **Steady-state spatially**; concentration varies *along the length* of the reactor.
- **Key assumption**: No axial dispersion; no radial gradients.

**Differential mass balance on an element of volume $dV$:**

$$Q\,C - Q(C + dC) - (-r)\,dV = 0$$

$$-Q\,dC = (-r)\,dV$$

Separating variables and integrating over the full reactor length:
$$Q\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{-r} \;=\; \int_{0}^{V} dV \;=\; V$$

Dividing by $Q$:
$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{-r}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **master reference** for solving reactor design problems in KTU ESE. Memorize the integrals for first-order and zero-order kinetics for both CSTR and PFR.

| Kinetics | Reactor | Performance Equation | Conversion Form |
|:--------:|:-------:|:---------------------|:----------------|
| Zero-order ($n=0$) | Batch | $C_0 - C = k\,t$ | $t = \dfrac{C_0 - C}{k}$ |
| Zero-order ($n=0$) | CSTR | $C_{in} - C_{out} = k\,\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{k}$ |
| Zero-order ($n=0$) | PFR | $C_{in} - C_{out} = k\,\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{k}$ |
| First-order ($n=1$) | Batch | $\dfrac{C}{C_0} = e^{-kt}$ | $t = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C}\right)$ |
| First-order ($n=1$) | CSTR | $\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1+k\tau}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} - 1\right)$ |
| First-order ($n=1$) | PFR | $\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = e^{-k\tau}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$ |
| Second-order ($n=2$) | CSTR | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{C_{out}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{in}}\right)$ | $C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + kC_{in}\tau}$ |
| Second-order ($n=2$) | PFR | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{C_{out}} - \dfrac{1}{C_{in}}\right)$ | — (same as CSTR) |
| Monod (Biological) | CSTR | $S = \dfrac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Yk - k_d\theta_c - 1}$ | **Activated sludge design** |

### 2.2.1 Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT)

The **Solids Retention Time** $\theta_c$ is the average time a biomass particle spends in the system. It is the **single most important** design and operating parameter in biological wastewater treatment.

$$\theta_c \;=\; \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w \;+\; Q_e X_e}$$

- $V$ = reactor volume ($\text{m}^3$)
- $X$ = biomass concentration in reactor ($\text{mg/L}$)
- $Q_w$ = wasted sludge flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{day}$)
- $X_w$ = biomass concentration in waste line ($\text{mg/L}$)
- $Q_e$ = effluent flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{day}$)
- $X_e$ = biomass concentration in effluent ($\text{mg/L}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a **conventional activated sludge plant**, $\theta_c$ typically ranges from **5 to 15 days** for carbonaceous BOD removal, **20–30 days** for nitrification, and is intentionally reduced to **< 3 days** to achieve **biological phosphorus removal** (PAOs washout). This single number drives the entire plant's biological behavior.

## 2.3 The CSTR-in-Series and PFR-in-Series Models

A long plug-flow reactor is mathematically equivalent to an infinite series of CSTRs. For $N$ CSTRs in series with equal volume $V/N$ each:

$$\tau_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{Q}$$

For first-order kinetics:
$$C_N \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + k\tau/N\right)^{N}}$$

As $N \to \infty$, the behavior **approaches that of a PFR**, recovering the PFR exponential decay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** It explains why designers sometimes split a long rectangular aeration tank into **3 to 4 baffled compartments** ("step-aeration" or "step-feed" configurations) — to combine plug-flow efficiency with the operational stability of CSTRs.

## 2.4 Comparison Summary: CSTR vs PFR

| Property | CSTR | PFR |
|:---------|:-----|:----|
| Spatial concentration profile | Uniform | Decreases along length |
| Time inside reactor | Exponentially distributed | Nearly uniform (all ≈ $\tau$) |
| Best for | Reactions where intermediates are desired; toxic/inhibitory substrates | Slow reactions; high conversion required |
| Volume required (1st order, same $C_{out}/C_{in}$) | **Larger** | **Smaller** |
| Susceptibility to short-circuiting | Low | High |
| Examples in wastewater treatment | Aeration tanks, equalization basins | Trickling filters, chlorine contact tanks, oxidation ditches (approx.) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CSTR Performance Equation (First-Order Kinetics)

**Starting assumption:** Substrate decay follows first-order kinetics, $-r = kC$.

**Steady-state mass balance on substrate in a CSTR:**

$$Q\,C_{in} \;-\; Q\,C_{out} \;-\; V(-r) \;=\; 0$$

**Substitute** the first-order rate law $-r = k\,C_{out}$ (note: the rate is evaluated at the *exit* concentration because the reactor is perfectly mixed):

$$Q\,C_{in} \;-\; Q\,C_{out} \;-\; V(k\,C_{out}) \;=\; 0$$

**Group the $C_{out}$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$Q\,C_{in} \;=\; Q\,C_{out} \;+\; V\,k\,C_{out}$$

$$Q\,C_{in} \;=\; C_{out}\,(Q + Vk)$$

**Divide both sides by $Q$:**

$$C_{in} \;=\; C_{out}\!\left(1 + \frac{Vk}{Q}\right)$$

**Recognize the hydraulic retention time** $\tau = V/Q$:

$$C_{in} \;=\; C_{out}\,(1 + k\tau)$$

**Solve for $C_{out}$:**

$$C_{out} \;=\; \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau} \quad\blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFR Performance Equation (First-Order Kinetics)

**Differential mass balance** on a thin slice of volume $dV$ at distance $x$ along the reactor:

$$Q\,C \;-\; Q(C + dC) \;-\; (-r)\,dV \;=\; 0$$

**Simplify:**
$$-Q\,dC \;=\; (-r)\,dV$$

**Substitute** the first-order rate law $-r = k\,C$:

$$-Q\,dC \;=\; k\,C\,dV$$

**Separate variables:**
$$\frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\frac{k}{Q}\,dV$$

**Integrate** from inlet (where $C = C_{in}$ at $V = 0$) to outlet (where $C = C_{out}$ at $V = V$):

$$\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\frac{k}{Q}\int_{0}^{V} dV$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{kV}{Q} \;=\; -k\tau$$

**Exponentiate both sides:**
$$\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} \;=\; e^{-k\tau} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{out} \;=\; C_{in}\,e^{-k\tau} \quad\blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalence of N-CSTR-in-Series to PFR

**Setup:** $N$ CSTRs in series, each of volume $V/N$. The exit of stage 1 is the inlet of stage 2.

**For stage 1 (first-order kinetics):**
$$C_1 \;=\; \frac{C_0}{1 + k\tau/N}$$

**For stage 2 (with $C_1$ as inlet):**
$$C_2 \;=\; \frac{C_1}{1 + k\tau/N} \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + k\tau/N\right)^{2}}$$

**By induction, for $N$ stages:**
$$C_N \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + k\tau/N\right)^{N}}$$

**Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{N\to\infty}\left(1 + \frac{k\tau}{N}\right)^{N} \;=\; e^{k\tau}$$

**Therefore:**
$$\lim_{N\to\infty} C_N \;=\; C_0\,e^{-k\tau}$$

which is identical to the PFR result. This proves that an infinite series of infinitesimally small CSTRs behaves as a PFR. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A wastewater stream with an influent BOD of $C_{in} = 500\;\text{mg/L}$ and flow rate $Q = 4000\;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ is to be treated to $C_{out} = 20\;\text{mg/L}$ using a CSTR followed by a PFR. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.35\;\text{day}^{-1}$. Calculate the total volume required. Compare this to a single CSTR alone and a single PFR alone.

### Step 1 — Volume of a Single CSTR Alone

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{0.35}\left(\frac{500}{20} - 1\right)$$

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{0.35}(25 - 1) = \frac{24}{0.35} = 68.57\;\text{days}$$

$$V_{CSTR} = \tau \cdot Q = 68.57 \times 4000 = 274{,}285.7\;\text{m}^3 \approx 274{,}286\;\text{m}^3$$

### Step 2 — Volume of a Single PFR Alone

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) = \frac{1}{0.35}\ln\!\left(\frac{500}{20}\right) = \frac{1}{0.35}\ln(25)$$

$$\ln(25) = 3.2189 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \tau_{PFR} = \frac{3.2189}{0.35} = 9.197\;\text{days}$$

$$V_{PFR} = 9.197 \times 4000 = 36{,}788\;\text{m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Volume of CSTR + PFR in Series

The PFR is fed the CSTR effluent, so the intermediate concentration is the CSTR outlet, $C_{CSTR,out}$.

**Strategy:** First, calculate the CSTR outlet. Then design the PFR to bring this down to 20 mg/L.

$$C_{CSTR,out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau_{CSTR,intermediate}}$$

Let the CSTR volume be such that the combined total meets the target. We can use the **additivity of reactor volumes in series for first-order kinetics:**

$$V_{total} = V_{CSTR} + V_{PFR}$$

For first-order kinetics in series:
$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{Q} + \frac{V_{PFR}}{Q} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} - 1\right) - \text{?}$$

A more direct approach: for a given **total** $\tau$, the optimal split between CSTR and PFR for first-order kinetics is found by setting the marginal cost equal — but for the KTU exam, the simpler procedure is:

**Trial split — say CSTR gives 50% removal:**

If CSTR gives $C_{CSTR,out} = 100\;\text{mg/L}$ (i.e., 80% removal):

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{0.35}\left(\frac{500}{100} - 1\right) = \frac{4}{0.35} = 11.43\;\text{days}$$

Then for the PFR to drop $100 \to 20\;\text{mg/L}$:

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{1}{0.35}\ln\!\left(\frac{100}{20}\right) = \frac{\ln 5}{0.35} = \frac{1.6094}{0.35} = 4.598\;\text{days}$$

**Total $\tau$** $= 11.43 + 4.598 = 16.03\;\text{days}$

**Total volume** $= 16.03 \times 4000 = 64{,}120\;\text{m}^3$

### Step 4 — Comparison

| Configuration | Total $\tau$ (days) | Total Volume ($\text{m}^3$) |
|:--------------|:------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Single CSTR | 68.57 | 274,286 |
| Single PFR | 9.20 | 36,788 |
| CSTR + PFR (this split) | 16.03 | 64,120 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The single PFR requires the **smallest volume**, but in practice, a small CSTR (equalization basin) is placed upstream to dampen shock loads — this is the engineering compromise that gives rise to the **CSTR + PFR hybrid** widely used in municipal sewage treatment.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Performance Equations at a Glance

| Configuration | Volume Required (1st order) | Volume Required (Zero order) |
|:--------------|:--------------------------:|:----------------------------:|
| Single CSTR | $V = \dfrac{Q(C_{in} - C_{out})}{kC_{out}}$ | $V = \dfrac{Q(C_{in} - C_{out})}{k}$ |
| Single PFR | $V = \dfrac{Q}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$ | $V = \dfrac{Q(C_{in} - C_{out})}{k}$ |
| $N$ CSTRs in series | $V = \dfrac{NQ}{k}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)^{1/N} - 1\right]$ | $V = \dfrac{NQ(C_{in}/N - C_{out,N})}{k}$ |
| 2 CSTRs + 1 PFR (hybrid) | Optimized split | Optimized split |

    G --> I["CSTR: Cin minus Cout = tau times r"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between CSTR and PFR in terms of mixing and concentration profile.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | CSTR | PFR |
|:---------|:-----|:----|
| Mixing | Perfect back-mixing, contents uniform | No axial mixing, fluid moves as discrete "plugs" |
| Concentration profile | Uniform in space (equal to outlet) | Decreases along reactor length |
| Time in reactor | Exponentially distributed | Nearly uniform for all fluid elements |
| Steady-state condition | Spatially and temporally uniform | Spatially varying, temporally constant |

> **Validity (1 mark):** Valid for fully developed flow. CSTRs approach ideal mixing only when $\text{Re}_{mix} \gg 10^4$ and residence-time distribution is verified.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT) and state its significance in activated sludge process design.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT)** — also called Solids Retention Time (SRT), denoted $\theta_c$ — is defined as the total mass of biomass in the reactor divided by the mass of biomass leaving the system per unit time:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V\,X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$$

It is significant because:

1. It **controls the microbial ecology** of the system (which species can grow).
2. For **nitrification**, $\theta_c \geq 20\;\text{days}$ is required at 10 °C.
3. For **biological phosphorus removal (EBPR)**, $\theta_c$ is intentionally kept low (~2–5 days) to wash out nitrifiers and favor PAOs.
4. It is **decoupled from HRT** in CSTR systems — a key engineering advantage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A wastewater with BOD concentration $C_{in} = 600\;\text{mg/L}$ flowing at $Q = 5000\;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ is to be treated in a CSTR. The first-order reaction rate constant is $k = 0.40\;\text{day}^{-1}$.**

**(a)** Derive the performance equation for a CSTR under first-order kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the volume of the reactor required to achieve an effluent BOD of $C_{out} = 30\;\text{mg/L}$. If the same effluent quality is required in a PFR, what is the volume needed? Compare the two. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the steady-state mass balance for a CSTR:

$$Q\,C_{in} - Q\,C_{out} - V(-r) = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rearrange to isolate the reaction term:

$$Q(C_{in} - C_{out}) = V(-r) \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_{in} - C_{out} = \frac{V}{Q}(-r) = \tau(-r)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For first-order kinetics, $-r = k\,C_{out}$ (rate is evaluated at the exit concentration due to perfect mixing):

$$C_{in} - C_{out} = \tau\,(k\,C_{out}) = k\tau\,C_{out}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $C_{out}$:

$$C_{in} = C_{out} + k\tau\,C_{out} = C_{out}(1 + k\tau)$$

$$\boxed{C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tau = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} - 1\right) \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the engineering significance — this is the fundamental design equation for a CSTR, valid only for steady-state and first-order (or pseudo-first-order) reactions.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the assumption: perfectly mixed, single-phase, constant density, isothermal, and that the rate is evaluated at $C_{out}$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note that for **Monod kinetics** in biological reactors, the equation takes the more complex form:

$$C_{out} = \frac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Yk - k_d\theta_c - 1}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For the CSTR, use the derived formula:

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{0.40}\left(\frac{600}{30} - 1\right)$$

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{0.40}(20 - 1) = \frac{19}{0.40} = 47.5\;\text{days}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the CSTR volume:

$$V_{CSTR} = \tau_{CSTR} \times Q = 47.5 \times 5000 = 237{,}500\;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For the PFR, the first-order performance equation is:

$$C_{out} = C_{in}\,e^{-k\tau} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \tau_{PFR} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $\tau_{PFR}$:

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{1}{0.40}\ln\!\left(\frac{600}{30}\right) = \frac{1}{0.40}\ln(20) = \frac{2.9957}{0.40} = 7.489\;\text{days}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the PFR volume:

$$V_{PFR} = \tau_{PFR} \times Q = 7.489 \times 5000 = 37{,}446\;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Calculate the volume ratio:

$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{237{,}500}{37{,}446} = 6.34$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Comment on the result: the PFR requires **6.34 times less volume** than the CSTR for the same effluent quality under first-order kinetics. This is because, in a CSTR, the entire reactor operates at the *lowest* concentration $C_{out}$ (lowest reaction rate), while in a PFR, the inlet portion operates at the *highest* rate. Hence PFR is **always more volume-efficient for first-order reactions**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**A wastewater treatment plant uses a CSTR followed by a PFR. The CSTR has volume $V_1 = 2000\;\text{m}^3$, the PFR has volume $V_2 = 4000\;\text{m}^3$, and the flow rate is $Q = 1000\;\text{m}^3/\text{day}$. The substrate follows first-order kinetics with $k = 0.25\;\text{day}^{-1}$.**

**(a)** Derive the design equation for a PFR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If $C_{in} = 400\;\text{mg/L}$, calculate the effluent concentration $C_{out}$ from the system and the overall removal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a thin slice of PFR volume $dV$ at distance $x$ from the inlet. Mass balance in steady state:

$$Q\,C - Q(C + dC) - (-r)\,dV = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Simplify:

$$-Q\,dC = (-r)\,dV \quad\Rightarrow\quad dV = -\frac{Q\,dC}{-r}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Separate variables and integrate over the full reactor:

$$\int_{0}^{V} dV = -Q\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{-r}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute $\tau = V/Q$:

$$\tau = \int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{-r} = \int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{-(-kC)} = \int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{kC}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Evaluate the integral:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k}\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{C} = \frac{1}{k}\bigl[\ln C\bigr]_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Rearrange to get the PFR design equation:

$$\boxed{C_{out} = C_{in}\,e^{-k\tau}} \quad\text{or}\quad \tau = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right) \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note: the key assumptions are no axial dispersion, uniform velocity profile, isothermal operation, and constant density. Mention that real reactors deviate from this ideal — actual PFRs are modeled with axial dispersion number $D/(\mu L)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the individual retention times:

$$\tau_1 = \frac{V_1}{Q} = \frac{2000}{1000} = 2.0\;\text{days (CSTR)}$$

$$\tau_2 = \frac{V_2}{Q} = \frac{4000}{1000} = 4.0\;\text{days (PFR)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the CSTR performance equation for stage 1:

$$C_1 = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau_1} = \frac{400}{1 + 0.25 \times 2.0} = \frac{400}{1.5} = 266.67\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the PFR performance equation for stage 2, with inlet $C_1 = 266.67\;\text{mg/L}$:

$$C_{out} = C_1\,e^{-k\tau_2} = 266.67 \times e^{-(0.25)(4.0)} = 266.67 \times e^{-1.0}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$:

$$C_{out} = 266.67 \times 0.3679 = 98.10\;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the overall removal efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{400 - 98.10}{400} \times 100\% = 75.47\%$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Check using the combined $\tau$ (since first-order, multiplication is valid):

$$\tau_{total} = 2.0 + 4.0 = 6.0\;\text{days}$$

Combined equivalent: $C_{out,combined} = 400 \times e^{-0.25 \times 6.0} \times \dfrac{1}{1+0.25\times 2.0}$ ... no, the proper check is sequential — which we already did. The result of $98.10\;\text{mg/L}$ is **correct**.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Discussion: a single PFR of $\tau = 6\;\text{days}$ would give $C_{out} = 400 e^{-1.5} = 89.26\;\text{mg/L}$, a slightly better effluent. The hybrid system adds operational flexibility (the CSTR equalizes flow) at a marginal efficiency cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply two separate formulas in series:** A very common error is to use $C_{out} = C_{in}\,e^{-k\tau_{total}}$ for the entire system. This is **wrong** when the system is CSTR + PFR, because the CSTR equation is algebraic, not exponential. Always apply **the appropriate equation at each stage**.
> 2. **Mixing up HRT and SRT:** HRT = $V/Q$ is the fluid residence time. SRT = $VX/(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$ is the biomass residence time. They are **equal only when there is no biomass recycle and no biomass in the effluent**.
> 3. **Units of $k$:** If $k$ is given in $\text{day}^{-1}$ and $\tau$ is in days, no conversion is needed. If $k$ is in $\text{hour}^{-1}$, you **must convert** $\tau$ to hours as well.
> 4. **Sign convention for $-r$:** In the KTU syllabus, $-r$ is the rate of *disappearance* of substrate (always $\geq 0$). The mass balance should always read $C_{in} - C_{out} = \tau(-r)$, not the other way around.
> 5. **For Monod kinetics, do NOT use the first-order CSTR equation.** Use the Monod form or the K-table design approach taught in Module 3.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reactor Configurations**

- **Three ideal configurations:** Batch (BR), CSTR, PFR. Every real reactor is modeled as one or a combination.
- **Mass balance skeleton:** $\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Reaction}$.
- **CSTR steady-state equation (1st order):** $C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}$.
- **PFR steady-state equation (1st order):** $C_{out} = C_{in}\,e^{-k\tau}$.
- **PFR is always more volume-efficient than CSTR for 1st-order reactions** when high conversion is required; for **zero-order** kinetics, both are equal.
- **HRT** $\tau = V/Q$ governs the **fluid** residence time. **SRT/MCRT** $\theta_c$ governs the **biomass** residence time.
- **N CSTRs in series** behave like a PFR as $N \to \infty$.
- **In series:** use the exit of stage $i$ as the inlet of stage $i+1$.
- **Monod equation** for biological CSTRs: $S = \dfrac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Yk - k_d \theta_c - 1}$.
- **High $\theta_c$** → nitrification favored; **low $\theta_c$** → biological phosphorus removal favored.
- **Real reactor deviations** are handled via the axial dispersion model, residence-time distribution (RTD) analysis, and tanks-in-series (TIS) model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro-oxidation: Electro Oxidation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electro-oxidation: Electro Oxidation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro-Oxidation (EO)** is an **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** used in wastewater treatment in which organic and inorganic pollutants are oxidized either *directly* at the surface of a high-oxygen-overvoltage anode or *indirectly* by electro-generated oxidizing species (such as hydroxyl radicals `·OH`, active chlorine `Cl₂/HOCl/OCl⁻`, hydrogen peroxide `H₂O₂`, peroxosulfates, ozone, or persulfates) produced during electrolysis of the aqueous medium.

The process is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and proceeds by applying a direct current (DC) between an inert/active anode and a suitable cathode immersed in the wastewater (which acts as the electrolyte). The overall objective is the mineralization of refractory, toxic, or non-biodegradable contaminants into **CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic ions**.

**Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Definition):**
- **Current Density (j)** = **A/m²** (typically 100–1000 A/m² for industrial EO)
- **Cell Voltage (U)** = **V** (typically 2.5–10 V)
- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol**
- **Specific Energy Consumption** = **kWh/m³ of treated wastewater**
- **Coulombic Efficiency / Current Efficiency (CE or φ)** = **dimensionless (%)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electric Toothbrush for Water"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a bathtub full of dirty, oily water that soap cannot clean. Now imagine dipping two metal plates into the water, connecting them to a battery, and switching it on. Tiny invisible "scrubbers" (oxidants like `·OH` radicals) appear on the surface of one plate and start tearing apart the dirt molecules into harmless carbon dioxide and water — **without any added chemicals**. This is Electro-Oxidation: using **electricity as a reagent** to destroy pollutants at the molecular level, atom by atom.

The anode acts as the *"scrubber factory,"* the cathode acts as the *"supporting worker,"* and the current flowing between them is the *"foreman"* that drives the entire workforce.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Electro-Oxidation

Electro-oxidation is broadly classified into **two mechanistic pathways**, both of which are examinable at KTU level:

| Pathway | Description | Dominant Where |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Anodic Oxidation (DAO)** | Pollutants adsorb onto the anode surface and transfer electrons directly | High-O₂-overvoltage anodes (BDD, PbO₂) |
| **Indirect Electro-Oxidation (IEO)** | Oxidants are *electrochemically generated in the bulk* and then react with pollutants | When electrolyte contains Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, PO₄³⁻ |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The examiner frequently asks students to **distinguish between direct and indirect electro-oxidation** with one supporting reaction each. Memorize this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Configuration & Reactor Types

The electro-oxidation cell (electrolytic cell) consists of:

1. **Anode** (positive electrode) — site of oxidation
2. **Cathode** (negative electrode) — site of reduction
3. **Electrolyte** — the wastewater itself, often supplemented with supporting salts
4. **DC Power Supply** — provides the driving potential
5. **Separator (optional)** — divided cell using ion-exchange membrane

**Common Reactor Geometries:**

- **Plate-and-Frame Cell** (most common, mono-polar or bi-polar electrodes)
- **Cylindrical Concentric Cell** (rod cathode inside cylindrical anode)
- **Fluidized Bed Cell** (granular bed anode for high surface area)
- **Filter-Press Cell** (industrial scale, Goupil/EMAX type)

---

## 2.2 Electrode Materials — The Heart of the Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **anode material dictates the oxidation mechanism and efficiency** — this is the single most important design choice in EO. KTU questions often ask: *"Why is Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD) preferred?"*

| Anode Material | Oxygen Overvoltage (V) | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pt (Platinum)** | Low (~0.3 V) | Indirect only | Reference electrode |
| **Graphite / Carbon** | Medium (~0.7 V) | Mixed | Dye removal |
| **DSA — Ti/SnO₂** | High (~1.0 V) | Direct + Indirect | Phenol degradation |
| **DSA — Ti/PbO₂** | High (~1.2 V) | Direct + Indirect | Landfill leachate |
| **BDD (Boron-Doped Diamond)** | Very High (~1.5–2.0 V) | Pure `·OH` direct | Pharmaceutical effluent |
| **Mixed Metal Oxides (MMO)** | High | Indirect (active Cl⁻) | Textile wastewater |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** The **Standard Potential** of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is **E° = +1.23 V vs SHE**, but on a BDD anode the actual OER occurs only at **~+2.3 V vs SHE**, giving a large electrochemical window for `·OH` radical generation without parasitic oxygen evolution.

---

## 2.3 Fundamental Electrode Reactions

### At the Anode (Oxidation):

**Reaction 1 — Water Discharge to Hydroxyl Radical:**
$$M + H_2O \longrightarrow M(\cdot OH) + H^+ + e^-$$

**Reaction 2 — Direct Anodic Oxidation (Pollutant at Active Site):**
$$M(\cdot OH) + R \longrightarrow M + CO_2 + H_2O + intermediates$$

**Reaction 3 — Active Chlorine Generation (Indirect Path):**
$$2Cl^- \longrightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^-$$
$$Cl_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$$
$$HOCl \longrightarrow H^+ + OCl^-$$

**Reaction 4 — Persulfate / Peroxodisulfate Generation:**
$$2SO_4^{2-} \longrightarrow S_2O_8^{2-} + 2e^-$$

**Reaction 5 — Hydrogen Peroxide (predominantly at cathode):**
$$O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2O_2$$

### At the Cathode (Reduction):

**Reaction 6 — Hydrogen Evolution:**
$$2H_2O + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$$

**Reaction 7 — Direct Reduction of Pollutants:**
$$R + ne^- \longrightarrow \text{Reduced products}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following equations appear in nearly every KTU Numerical on Electro-Oxidation.** Memorize the units and definitions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | m = mass of substance liberated; I = current; t = time; M = molar mass; n = electrons transferred; F = Faraday constant | g, A, s, g/mol, mol, C/mol | **Faraday's 1st Law** — Theoretical mass of oxidant generated |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{V_s}$ | E_sp = specific energy consumption; U = cell voltage; V_s = sample volume | kWh/m³ | **Energy cost of treatment** (KPI for design) |
| 3 | $\varphi = \dfrac{(COD_0 - COD_t) \cdot F \cdot V_s}{8 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$ | φ = current efficiency; COD = chemical oxygen demand; V = volume | %, mg/L, C/mol, L, A, s | **Coulombic / Current Efficiency** |
| 4 | $j = \dfrac{I}{A_e}$ | j = current density; A_e = effective electrode area | A/m² | **Operating current selection** |
| 5 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{EC_{removed}}{E_{sp}}$ | EC_removed = energy converted to chemical oxidation | dimensionless | **Overall EO efficiency** |
| 6 | $\text{MCE} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{4 \cdot 32 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$ | MCE = Mineralization Current Efficiency; 32 = molar mass of O₂; 4 = electrons per O₂ | % | **KTU-standard COD-based efficiency** |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{\ln(C_0 / C_t)}{t}$ | k = pseudo-first-order rate constant | min⁻¹ | **Kinetics of pollutant decay** |
| 8 | $\text{ICE} = \dfrac{(COD_0 - COD_t)}{8 \cdot F \cdot I \cdot t / (V_s)} \times 100$ | ICE = Instantaneous Current Efficiency | % | **Time-resolved efficiency** |
| 9 | $E^0 = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$ | Thermodynamic cell voltage | V | **Minimum theoretical voltage** |
| 10 | $\eta_{cell} = U_{applied} - E^0_{cell}$ | Cell overpotential | V | **Real driving voltage** |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM PITFALL:** In equation (6) for MCE, the constant **8** (or equivalently $4 \times 32 / 16$) comes from the fact that 1 mol of O₂ requires 4 mol of electrons (n = 4) and 1 mol of O₂ = 32 g. Students commonly use 32 in the numerator instead of denominator and lose 7 marks. **Commit to memory:** *MCE denominator always carries the '8' factor.*

---

## 2.5 Operating Parameters Affecting EO Performance

| Parameter | Effect on Performance | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Density (j)** | ↑ j → faster degradation but ↑ energy cost & ↓ CE | 100 – 1000 A/m² |
| **pH** | Acidic (pH 2–4) favors `·OH`; Alkaline (pH 8–10) favors `OCl⁻` | 2 – 10 |
| **Electrolyte (Cl⁻)** | ↑ Cl⁻ → ↑ active chlorine → faster degradation, may form chlorinated by-products | 0.5 – 5 g/L NaCl |
| **Temperature** | ↑ T → ↑ kinetics but ↓ `·OH` stability & ↑ corrosion | 20 – 60 °C |
| **Flow Rate / Stirring** | ↑ mass transfer → ↑ CE up to limiting current | 0.1 – 5 L/min |
| **Electrode Gap** | ↓ gap → ↓ ohmic drop → ↑ efficiency | 1 – 10 mm |
| **COD Initial** | ↑ COD → ↑ CE (more substrate available) | 100 – 50,000 mg/L |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electro-oxidation is now a **commercial reality** in the following domains:

- **Pharmaceutical Effluent** — removal of antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin)
- **Landfill Leachate** — polishing step after biological treatment
- **Textile Dye Wastewater** — decolorization of reactive azo dyes
- **Petrochemical Refineries** — phenol, cyanide, sulfide removal
- **Olive Mill Wastewater** — polyphenol degradation
- **Tannery Effluents** — chromium (Cr³⁺/Cr⁶⁺) removal
- **Drinking Water** — atrazine, pesticide removal

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** Real-world EO plants operate at **specific energy consumption values of 5–80 kWh/m³** depending on influent COD. A BDD-anode system typically consumes **2–5× more energy** than a DSA system but achieves **near-complete mineralization** for recalcitrant compounds.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law Applied to Electro-Oxidation (for Active Chlorine Generation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the theoretical mass of `Cl₂` produced at the anode when a current *I* flows for a time *t* through a chloride-containing wastewater.

**Starting Principle (Faraday's First Law):**
> The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

**Step 1 — Total Charge Passed:**

Charge is the product of current and time. Expressing units properly:

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs (A·s)}]$$

**Step 2 — Number of Moles of Electrons:**

Since 1 Faraday (F) = 96,485 C corresponds to 1 mole of electrons, the moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F} \quad [\text{moles of } e^-]$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric Coupling to Anode Reaction:**

The anode reaction for chlorine evolution is:

$$2Cl^- \longrightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^-$$

From stoichiometry, **2 moles of electrons produce 1 mole of Cl₂**. Therefore, the moles of chlorine produced are:

$$n_{Cl_2} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{2F}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Moles to Mass:**

The molar mass of Cl₂ is *M_{Cl₂}* = 2 × 35.5 = **71 g/mol**. Multiplying:

$$m_{Cl_2} = n_{Cl_2} \cdot M_{Cl_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{Cl_2}}{2F}$$

**Step 5 — General Faraday Expression for Electro-Oxidation:**

Generalizing for *n* electrons per molecule of target species (where n = 2 for Cl₂, n = 1 for Ag, n = 4 for O₂, etc.):

$$\boxed{m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

This is **Faraday's First Law**, the foundational equation for any KTU numerical on electro-oxidation.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style):**

> *An electro-oxidation cell operates at I = 10 A for t = 2 hours in wastewater containing NaCl. Calculate the theoretical mass of Cl₂ generated.*

**Given:**
- I = 10 A
- t = 2 h = 7200 s
- M_{Cl₂} = 71 g/mol
- n = 2
- F = 96,485 C/mol

**Substituting into the formula:**

$$m_{Cl_2} = \frac{10 \times 7200 \times 71}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{Cl_2} = \frac{5{,}112{,}000}{192{,}970} = 26.49 \text{ g}$$

> **Answer:** Theoretical Cl₂ produced ≈ **26.5 g**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mineralization Current Efficiency (MCE) from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the expression for Mineralization Current Efficiency used in KTU board exams.

**Step 1 — Define Mineralization:**

Complete oxidation of organics to CO₂ requires oxygen. For a generalized organic compound, the electron count is fixed:

$$C_xH_yO_z + \left(\frac{2x + y/2 - z}{2}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Define Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) Stoichiometry:**

The **theoretical oxygen demand per gram of COD** is **1 mol O₂ per 32 g COD** (since 1 mol O₂ = 32 g, and 1 mol O₂ = 4 mol e⁻).

**Step 3 — COD Removed During Electrolysis:**

$$\Delta COD = (COD_0 - COD_t) \cdot V_s \quad [\text{mg of O}_2 \text{ equivalent}]$$

**Step 4 — Moles of Oxygen Consumed for Mineralization:**

$$n_{O_2, \text{actual}} = \frac{\Delta COD}{32{,}000} \quad [\text{mol}]$$

(Using 32,000 because ΔCOD is in mg and 1 mol O₂ = 32,000 mg = 32 g)

**Step 5 — Total Moles of Electrons Passed:**

$$n_{e^-,\text{passed}} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 6 — Equate to COD Basis:**

Since 1 mol O₂ corresponds to 4 mol e⁻, the **theoretical electron requirement** for the observed COD removal is:

$$n_{e^-,\text{theoretical}} = 4 \cdot n_{O_2,\text{actual}} = \frac{4 \cdot \Delta COD}{32{,}000}$$

**Step 7 — Define MCE as Ratio:**

$$MCE = \frac{n_{e^-,\text{theoretical}}}{n_{e^-,\text{passed}}} \times 100$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$MCE = \frac{\frac{4 \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t) \cdot V_s}{32{,}000}}{\frac{I \cdot t}{F}} \times 100$$

$$MCE = \frac{4 \cdot F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{32{,}000 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$$

Using the identity $\frac{4 \cdot F}{32{,}000} = \frac{4 \times 96485}{32{,}000} = \frac{F}{8 \times 1000}$, this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{MCE = \frac{F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{8 \cdot 1000 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100}$$
