When COD is in g/L and V in L, the 1000 factor drops out, giving the **KTU-board standard form**:

$$MCE = \frac{F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{8 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **high MCE** means the electrons supplied are efficiently used for COD removal; a **low MCE** indicates wasted energy on side reactions like O₂ evolution or H₂ generation. Industrial EO systems aim for **MCE > 30%** for economic viability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (E_sp)

**Step 1 — Total Electrical Energy Input:**

$$E_{input} = U \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{Joules (J)}]$$

where U = applied cell voltage (V), I = current (A), t = time (s).

**Step 2 — Convert to kWh:**

Since 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J:

$$E_{input} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^6} \quad [\text{kWh}]$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to Treated Volume:**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{input}}{V_s} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot V_s} \quad [\text{kWh/m}^3]$$

$$\boxed{E_{sp} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times V_s} \quad [\text{kWh/m}^3, \text{when } V_s \text{ in L}]}$$

**Worked Numerical:**

> *In a 5 L wastewater sample, EO is conducted for 90 min at 15 A and cell voltage 8 V. Calculate the specific energy consumption.*

$$E_{sp} = \frac{8 \times 15 \times (90 \times 60)}{3.6 \times 5} = \frac{8 \times 15 \times 5400}{18} = \frac{648{,}000}{18}$$

$$E_{sp} = 36{,}000 \text{ kWh/m}^3 \quad \text{(value too high — likely a typo; standard values 1–100 kWh/m³)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Take 5 L as 0.005 m³:**
>
> $$E_{sp} = \frac{8 \times 15 \times 5400}{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.005} = \frac{648{,}000}{18{,}000} = 36 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$
>
> This is a realistic industrial value.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Direct vs. Indirect Electro-Oxidation

| Attribute | Direct Anodic Oxidation (DAO) | Indirect Electro-Oxidation (IEO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Where reaction occurs** | Anode surface (heterogeneous) | Bulk solution (homogeneous) |
| **Primary oxidant** | Physisorbed `·OH` radical on anode (`M(·OH)`) | Dissolved Cl₂, HOCl, OCl⁻, H₂O₂, S₂O₈²⁻ |
| **Best Anode** | BDD, PbO₂ | DSA, Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO) |
| **Electrolyte dependency** | None (works in pure water) | Requires Cl⁻ or SO₄²⁻ |
| **By-products** | Minimal chlorinated compounds | Possible chlorinated organics (AOX) |
| **Current efficiency** | Moderate to high | High when Cl⁻ is abundant |
| **pH sensitivity** | Acidic favorable | Both acidic and alkaline work |
| **Energy demand** | Higher (high OER overpotential) | Lower |
| **KTU Example Pollutants** | Phenol, aniline, dyes | Cyanide, ammonia, sulfide |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electro-Oxidation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Power Supply 0-30V 0-20A] --> B[Electrochemical Cell Reactor]
    B --> C[Anode Chamber: High O2 Overvoltage Anode BDD or PbO2]
    B --> D[Cathode Chamber: Stainless Steel 316 Cathode]
    E[Wastewater Influent COD pH Conductivity] --> F[Inlet Reservoir with Optional NaCl Na2SO4]
    F --> B
    C --> G[Anode Reactions: 2Cl minus to Cl2 plus 2e minus]
    C --> H[Anode Reactions: M plus H2O to M OH radical plus H plus e minus]
    D --> I[Cathode Reactions: 2H2O plus 2e minus to H2 plus 2OH minus]
    D --> J[Cathode Reactions: O2 plus 2H plus plus 2e minus to H2O2]
    G --> K[Bulk Oxidant Zone: HOCl, OCl minus, Cl2, H2O2]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Pollutant Oxidation: Organics plus Oxidants to CO2 plus H2O plus Inorganic Ions]
    L --> M[Effluent Stream Reduced COD and TOC]
    L --> N[Sludge Sparingly produced]
    B --> O[Off Gas Stream: H2 at Cathode, O2 and Cl2 at Anode]
    M --> P[Optional Post Treatment: Biological Polishing or Adsorption]
    P --> Q[Final Discharge to Environment or Reuse]
    M --> R[Monitoring Sensors: pH Meter, Conductivity, COD, TOC, UV Vis]
    R --> S[PLC SCADA Control Loop for Current Voltage Flow]
    S --> A
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mechanistic Pathways of Electro-Oxidation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Wastewater plus Electrolyte] --> A1[Apply DC Voltage]
    A1 --> P1[Direct Anodic Oxidation Pathway]
    A1 --> P2[Indirect Oxidation Pathway]
    P1 --> M1[Anode Surface Adsorption of Pollutant R]
    M1 --> M2[Electron Transfer: R to R oxidized plus e minus]
    M2 --> M3[Mineralization: CO2 plus H2O plus Ions]
    P2 --> N1[Anode Generates Active Species]
    N1 --> N2[Chloride to Cl2 to HOCl to OCl minus]
    N1 --> N3[Water to Hydroxyl Radical OH]
    N1 --> N4[Sulfate to Persulfate S2O8 2 minus]
    N2 --> O1[Bulk Reaction with Pollutant R]
    N3 --> O1
    N4 --> O1
    O1 --> M3
    M3 --> OUT[Treated Effluent plus Off Gases]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Industrial EO Plant

| Functional Block | Component | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Block 1: Influent Conditioning** | Equalization tank, pH adjustment, salt dosing | Stabilize feed for EO |
| **Block 2: Power Supply Unit** | Rectifier, transformer, control panel | Provide stable DC |
| **Block 3: Electrochemical Reactor** | Plate-and-frame or filter-press cell with BDD/MMO anodes | Site of oxidation |
| **Block 4: Recirculation System** | Pump, flow meter, heat exchanger | Maintain mass transfer and temperature |
| **Block 5: Off-Gas Management** | Scrubber, demister | Capture H₂, Cl₂, O₂ |
| **Block 6: Effluent Polishing** | Sand filter, activated carbon, biological BAF | Remove residual organics and AOX |
| **Block 7: Monitoring & Control** | pH, ORP, conductivity, COD, TOC sensors + PLC | Real-time optimization |
| **Block 8: Sludge Handling** | Lamella clarifier, sludge press | Manage sparingly produced solids |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

### **Q1. Define electro-oxidation. Differentiate between direct and indirect electro-oxidation.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Electro-oxidation is an advanced electrochemical wastewater treatment process in which pollutants are oxidized at the anode or by electro-generated oxidants such as `·OH`, active chlorine, or persulfates under the action of an applied DC current.

| Aspect | Direct Anodic Oxidation | Indirect Electro-Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Site | Anode surface | Bulk solution |
| Mechanism | Electron transfer from adsorbed pollutant to anode | Mediated by electro-generated oxidants |
| Anode type | BDD, PbO₂ | DSA, MMO |
| Example | Phenol oxidation at BDD | Ammonia oxidation by OCl⁻ |

---

### **Q2. State Faraday's first law of electrolysis and write its mathematical form.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Faraday's First Law states that the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is **directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge)** passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q = I \cdot t$$

Mathematical form:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where *m* = mass (g), *I* = current (A), *t* = time (s), *M* = molar mass, *n* = electrons transferred, *F* = 96,485 C/mol.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **[Question A — 14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Q.A (a)** Explain the mechanism of electro-oxidation with suitable anode and cathode reactions. Compare BDD and PbO₂ anodes. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Electro-Oxidation:**

Electro-oxidation proceeds via two pathways:

**1. Direct Anodic Oxidation (DAO):**

The pollutant (R) is adsorbed on the anode surface (M) and loses electrons directly:

$$M + H_2O \longrightarrow M(\cdot OH) + H^+ + e^-$$
$$M(\cdot OH) + R \longrightarrow M + \text{intermediates} \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + e^-$$

**2. Indirect Electro-Oxidation (IEO):**

Strong oxidants are generated in situ, then attack pollutants in bulk:

- Active chlorine pathway: $2Cl^- \to Cl_2 + 2e^-$, then $Cl_2 + H_2O \to HOCl + HCl$
- Persulfate pathway: $2SO_4^{2-} \to S_2O_8^{2-} + 2e^-$
- Cathodic H₂O₂: $O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2O_2$

**Cathode Reaction:**

$$2H_2O + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$$

**Comparison: BDD vs PbO₂ Anodes**

| Parameter | BDD | PbO₂ |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen overpotential | Very high (~2.3 V) | High (~1.9 V) |
| `·OH` radical production | Very strong, non-active anode | Moderate |
| Corrosion resistance | Excellent | Good (but Pb²⁺ leaching risk) |
| Cost | Very high | Moderate |
| Service life | >10,000 hours | ~2,000–5,000 hours |
| Best application | Pharmaceutical, endocrine disruptors | Landfill leachate, dyes |

> **Conclusion:** BDD is the "gold standard" anode for refractory organics; PbO₂ offers a cost-effective compromise.

---

#### **Q.A (b)** A wastewater sample of volume 2 L with initial COD of 2,500 mg/L is subjected to electro-oxidation for 3 hours at 8 A current and 10 V cell voltage. The final COD after treatment is 400 mg/L. Calculate: (i) Specific energy consumption, (ii) Mineralization current efficiency (MCE). `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- V_s = 2 L = 0.002 m³
- COD₀ = 2,500 mg/L = 2.5 g/L
- COD_t = 400 mg/L = 0.4 g/L
- t = 3 h = 10,800 s
- I = 8 A, U = 10 V
- F = 96,485 C/mol

**Part (i) — Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$E_{sp} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot V_s}$$

$$E_{sp} = \frac{10 \times 8 \times 10800}{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.002} = \frac{864{,}000}{7200} = 120 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Mineralization Current Efficiency:**

Using the standard KTU formula (COD in g/L, V in L, t in s):

$$MCE = \frac{F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{8 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$$

$$MCE = \frac{96485 \times 2 \times (2.5 - 0.4)}{8 \times 8 \times 10800} \times 100$$

$$MCE = \frac{96485 \times 2 \times 2.1}{691200} \times 100 = \frac{405{,}237}{691200} \times 100$$

$$MCE = 58.63\%$$

> **Result:** E_sp = **120 kWh/m³** and MCE = **58.63%**

---

### **[Question B — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Q.B (a)** Discuss the role of anode material, current density, pH, and supporting electrolyte in the electro-oxidation process. How do they affect the current efficiency? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Anode Material:**
- High-oxygen-overvoltage anodes (BDD, PbO₂) generate physisorbed `·OH` radicals → direct oxidation.
- Low-overvoltage anodes (Pt) favor oxygen evolution → low MCE.
- Choice of anode is the **single most important design parameter** determining pathway.

**2. Current Density (j):**
- ↑ j → faster degradation rate (more `·OH` produced per unit time).
- BUT ↑ j → ↑ side reactions (O₂, H₂ evolution) and ↑ I²R losses → MCE drops at very high j.
- **Optimum j** balances rate and efficiency (typically 200–500 A/m² for BDD).

**3. pH:**
- **Acidic pH (2–4):** Stabilizes `·OH` radicals, suppresses H₂O₂ decomposition, enhances direct oxidation.
- **Alkaline pH (8–10):** Favors OCl⁻ over HOCl; less effective for `·OH` pathway.
- **Neutral pH:** Compromise zone.

**4. Supporting Electrolyte:**
- **NaCl** (most common): Generates active chlorine → ↑ rate but may form AOX (chlorinated by-products).
- **Na₂SO₄**: Generates persulfate (S₂O₈²⁻), a powerful oxidant; no AOX.
- **NaNO₃**: Less effective but avoids AOX.
- ↑ electrolyte concentration → ↑ conductivity → ↓ U → ↓ energy cost.

**Summary on MCE:**

| Factor | Effect on MCE |
|---|---|
| BDD anode | ↑ MCE |
| Moderate j (200–500 A/m²) | ↑ MCE |
| Acidic pH | ↑ MCE for direct path |
| High Cl⁻ | ↑ MCE (but AOX risk) |
| High flow/turbulence | ↑ MCE (better mass transfer) |

---

#### **Q.B (b)** An electro-oxidation cell uses a BDD anode and stainless steel cathode, each of effective area 0.02 m². If 5 g of Cl₂ is theoretically required for chlorination-based oxidation of an effluent, calculate the time required at a current of 5 A. (M_{Cl₂} = 71 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol, n = 2) `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- m_{Cl₂} = 5 g
- I = 5 A
- M = 71 g/mol
- n = 2
- F = 96,485 C/mol

**Apply Faraday's First Law:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Solve for t:**

$$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M} = \frac{5 \times 2 \times 96485}{5 \times 71}$$

$$t = \frac{964{,}850}{355} = 2{,}717.6 \text{ s}$$

**Convert to hours:**

$$t = \frac{2717.6}{3600} = 0.755 \text{ h} = 45.3 \text{ min}$$

> **Result:** Time required ≈ **2,718 seconds** or **45.3 minutes**.

**Bonus — Current Density Check:**

$$j = \frac{I}{A_e} = \frac{5}{0.02} = 250 \text{ A/m}^2$$

This is well within the recommended BDD operating range (200–500 A/m²), confirming the design is feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **MCE formula confusion:** Students confuse the constant '8' with '32' (molar mass of O₂). Memorize: **8 in denominator** = (4 × 32)/16 simplification.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in E_sp:** If V is in **L**, divide by **3.6** (not 3.6×10⁶); if V is in **m³**, divide by 3.6×10⁶.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert hours → seconds** in Faraday's law. Always state the conversion explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing MCE with CE (current efficiency):** MCE specifically refers to COD-based mineralization; CE is a more general term.
> 5. **Naming the anode material but not explaining its oxygen overpotential** — half-marks lost.
> 6. **Stating reactions without balancing** — KTU deducts 1 mark for unbalanced equations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Bullet-Point Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Electro-oxidation = in-situ generation of oxidants at an anode under DC current for pollutant destruction.
- ✅ **Two pathways:** **Direct (DAO)** at anode surface using `·OH` on BDD/PbO₂; **Indirect (IEO)** via Cl₂/HOCl/S₂O₈²⁻ in bulk.
- ✅ **Best anode:** **BDD** (highest O₂ overpotential, strongest `·OH` production, longest life).
- ✅ **Standard Cathode:** Stainless steel 316 (cheap, H₂ evolution, optional H₂O₂ generation).
- ✅ **Faraday's 1st Law:** $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — fundamental for any KTU numerical.
- ✅ **Specific Energy (E_sp):** $E_{sp} = \frac{U \cdot I \cdot t}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot V_s}$ in kWh/m³ (V in m³).
- ✅ **MCE Formula:** $MCE = \frac{F \cdot V_s \cdot (COD_0 - COD_t)}{8 \cdot I \cdot t} \times 100$ — denominator carries '**8**'.
- ✅ **Current Efficiency** ↑ with: high Cl⁻, acidic pH, BDD anode, moderate j, good mass transfer.
- ✅ **Industrial range:** j = 100–1000 A/m²; U = 2.5–10 V; E_sp = 5–80 kWh/m³.
- ✅ **Applications:** Pharma, leachate, textile dyes, petrochemical, tannery Cr, pesticides.
- ✅ **By-products:** H₂ gas (cathode), O₂ + Cl₂ (anode) → off-gas scrubbing mandatory.
- ✅ **Post-treatment:** Often followed by **biological BAF** or activated carbon for polishing.
- ✅ **Key thermodynamic value:** E°(OER) = **+1.23 V vs SHE**; on BDD, OER onset = **+2.3 V vs SHE**.
- ✅ **Faraday Constant:** F = **96,485 C/mol** (always quote in KTU answers).
- ✅ **Chlorine chain to remember:** $2Cl^- \to Cl_2 \to HOCl \to OCl^-$ — all pH-dependent equilibria.
- ✅ **Distinguishing exam tip:** When asked to "compare with electrocoagulation," highlight that EO produces **no sludge**, while EC generates **Fe(OH)₃ / Al(OH)₃ flocs**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactor Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactor Configurations** refer to the geometric, hydraulic, and operational arrangement of vessels (reactors) in a wastewater treatment system, governing the contact pattern between the wastewater (substrate) and the active microbial mass (biomass/catalyst). The three fundamental ideal configurations are the **Batch Reactor**, **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)**, and **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)**, each producing a distinct residence time distribution (RTD) and substrate-removal efficiency.

In biological wastewater treatment, the reactor is the engineered vessel in which the biochemical conversion of dissolved/suspended pollutants takes place. The **configuration** dictates:
- The **flow regime** (mixing intensity and flow pattern).
- The **kinetic regime** (order of reaction effectively observed).
- The **biomass-substrate contact time**, quantified by the **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**, denoted $\theta$ in **hours (h)**.
- The **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / $\theta_c$)** in **days (d)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — HRT Equation:**
> $$\theta = \frac{V}{Q}$$
> where $V$ is reactor **volume in m³** and $Q$ is the **volumetric flow rate in m³/h**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Vessel" 🍲

Imagine you are cooking lentils to make them soft:

| Reactor Analogy | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Cooker (Batch)** | You add all lentils and water, seal it, and let it cook. The concentration of "raw lentils" decreases uniformly with time. No new lentils enter, none leave — until done. |
| **Open Stockpot with Constant Stirring (CSTR)** | You continuously add raw lentils and water at the top, and continuously draw off softened lentils+broth from the bottom. The pot is stirred vigorously, so the contents are perfectly homogeneous — every drop leaving the pot is identical in "softness." |
| **Long Horizontal Trough (PFR)** | Lentils enter one end, are pushed along a long narrow channel with no back-mixing, and exit the other end. Each lentil spends exactly the same time in the trough, but those near the inlet are still firm while those near the outlet are fully cooked. |

> [!TIP]
> The "softness" of lentils is the **substrate conversion (X)**. The different vessels represent three fundamentally different **contact-time histories** for each fluid element.

## 1.3 Classification of Reactor Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Ideal Hydraulic Models** (Levenspiel, foundational to KTU design problems):
> 1. **Batch Reactor (BR)** — No inflow/outflow during reaction; unsteady state.
> 2. **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR / CFSTR)** — Completely mixed; steady state; uniform concentration throughout.
> 3. **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR / Tubular Reactor)** — No axial mixing; concentration varies along the length.

Real-world advanced treatment systems use **hybrid configurations**:
- **Series of CSTRs** (n-CSTRs in series approximates PFR behavior).
- **Plug Flow with Recycle (PFR + Recycle)** for activated sludge.
- **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** — time-based, not space-based, sequencing of batch phases.
- **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** — CSTR/SBR coupled with membrane solid-liquid separation.
- **Anaerobic/Anoxic/Oxic (A²/O)** and **Modified Ludzack–Ettinger (MLE)** — compartmentalized nutrient removal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Mass Balance Framework

The design of every reactor configuration begins with the **Steady-State Material Balance** on the substrate (the polluting compound). For any control volume of reactor:

$$\{\text{Accumulation}\} = \{\text{In}\} - \{\text{Out}\} + \{\text{Generation}\} - \{\text{Consumption}\}$$

At steady state, **Accumulation = 0**, reducing this to:

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption} = 0$$

This single equation, when applied differently to each reactor geometry, yields the **distinct design equations** seen in every KTU problem.

## 2.2 Logic Steps — Why Each Reactor Behaves Differently

1. **Step 1 — Identify the flow regime:**
   - *Completely Mixed* (CSTR): Concentration inside = Concentration leaving.
   - *Plug Flow* (PFR): A fluid element travels as a "plug," never overtaking or lagging.
   - *Batch* (BR): A single charge reacts over time, no inflow/outflow.

2. **Step 2 — Write the differential mass balance** for an infinitesimal slice of reactor (dV):
   - CSTR: algebraic equation (no spatial gradient).
   - PFR: ordinary differential equation in $V$ or $z$.
   - BR: ordinary differential equation in $t$.

3. **Step 3 — Insert the kinetic rate law**, typically:
   - **Zero-order:** $-r = k$ (units: $\text{mg/L} \cdot \text{d}^{-1}$)
   - **First-order:** $-r = kC$ (units: $\text{d}^{-1}$)
   - **Monod (saturation):** $-r = \dfrac{kXC}{K_s + C}$

4. **Step 4 — Solve** the algebraic/differential equation for $C$ as a function of $V$ or $t$.

5. **Step 5 — Solve for Reactor Volume** required to achieve a target effluent concentration $C_e$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every equation a student must memorize for KTU Part A and Part B questions on Reactor Configurations. **Note:** Vertical bars are rendered as $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Reactor Type | Governing Equation | Conversion (Zero-order) | Conversion (First-order) | Required Volume (First-order) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Batch Reactor (BR)** | $V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = -rV$ | $C = C_0 - kt$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \exp(-k\theta)$ | $V = \dfrac{Q}{k}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_e}\right)\right]$ |
| 2 | **CSTR (Single, Ideal)** | $QC_0 - QC_e - rV = 0$ | $C_e = C_0 - k\theta$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1+k\theta}$ | $V = \dfrac{QC_0(C_0 - C_e)}{kC_e}$ |
| 3 | **PFR (Plug Flow)** | $Q\,dC = -r\,dV$ | $C_e = C_0 - k\theta$ | $\dfrac{C_e}{C_e^{\,0}} = \exp(-k\theta)$ | $V = \dfrac{Q}{k}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_e}\right)\right]$ |
| 4 | **n-CSTRs in Series** | Apply CSTR eqn $n$ times | — | $\dfrac{C_n}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{(1+k\theta/n)^n}$ | $V_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{nQ\,C_{n-1}(C_{n-1} - C_n)}{kC_n}$ |
| 5 | **PFR with Recycle** | $V = Q(1+R)\theta$ | — | $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left[-k\theta(1+R)\right]}{1+R\left(1 - \exp\!\left[-k\theta(1+R)\right]\right)}$ | — |
| 6 | **General Design Relation** | $V = \dfrac{Q(C_0 - C_e)}{-r}$ | — | — | — |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{d}$).
- $V$ = Reactor volume ($\text{m}^3$).
- $C_0$ = Influent substrate concentration ($\text{mg/L}$).
- $C_e$ = Effluent substrate concentration ($\text{mg/L}$).
- $k$ = Reaction rate constant (units depend on order).
- $\theta = V/Q$ = Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) in **d** or **h**.
- $R$ = Recycle ratio (dimensionless).
- $-r$ = Rate of substrate removal ($\text{mg/L} \cdot \text{d}^{-1}$).

## 2.4 Key Comparative Insights (Exam Gold)

> [!TIP]
> **For the SAME conversion, PFR always requires the SMALLEST volume; CSTR the LARGEST. Batch ≈ PFR** for first-order reactions.

- **Volume Efficiency Ratio (First-order, PFR vs CSTR):**
$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{(1 + k\theta)^2}{k\theta \cdot \ln(1 + k\theta)}$$

- **Limiting Behaviors:**
  - As $k\theta \to 0$ (very low conversion): $V_{CSTR} \approx V_{PFR}$.
  - As $k\theta \to \infty$ (high conversion): $V_{CSTR} \gg V_{PFR}$.

- **n-CSTRs in series approach PFR** as $n \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{k\theta}{n}\right)^{-n} = \exp(-k\theta) \equiv \text{PFR result}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CSTR** is favored for **equalization, nitrification (low F/M), and high-strength industrial wastewater** because of operational simplicity and resistance to shock loads.
- **PFR** is favored for **high-conversion BOD removal, primary treatment optimization, and chlorine contact basins** where plug-flow kinetics maximize disinfection.
- **SBR** is favored in **decentralized/package plants** and **industrial batch discharges** where flow is intermittent.
- **A²/O and MLE** are favored in **modern STPs for simultaneous BOD, N, and P removal**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. The derivations below are central to KTU Part B questions worth 7–14 marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Design Equation for an Ideal CSTR

**Step 1.** Consider a single completely-mixed tank of volume $V$ operating at steady state with volumetric inflow $Q$ and concentration $C_0$.

**Step 2.** Write the **substrate mass balance** over the control volume:

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption} = 0$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the volumetric fluxes (concentration × flow):

$$Q C_0 - Q C_e + 0 - r V = 0$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to isolate the rate term $r$ (the rate of substrate disappearance is conventionally written as $-r > 0$):

$$Q(C_0 - C_e) = -r V$$

**Step 5.** Divide both sides by $Q$:

$$C_0 - C_e = \frac{-r V}{Q} = -r \theta$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $V$ — the **General CSTR Design Equation**:

$$\boxed{\,V = \dfrac{Q(C_0 - C_e)}{-r}\,}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute **first-order kinetics** $-r = kC_e$ (note: in CSTR, the rate is evaluated at the effluent concentration since the contents are uniform):

$$V = \frac{Q(C_0 - C_e)}{kC_e}$$

**Step 8.** Divide numerator and denominator by $C_e$ to obtain the conversion form:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C_e}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Equation for an Ideal PFR

**Step 1.** Consider a tubular reactor of length $L$ and cross-section $A$. Take a differential slice of thickness $dz$ at position $z$, with volume $dV = A\,dz$.

**Step 2.** At steady state, the **axial mass balance** for the slice:

$$Q C \big\vert_{z} - Q (C + dC) - r\,dV = 0$$

**Step 3.** Expand and cancel:

$$-Q\,dC - r\,dV = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q\,dC = -r\,dV$$

**Step 4.** Separate variables and integrate from inlet ($z=0$, $C = C_0$) to outlet ($z=L$, $C = C_e$):

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_e} \frac{dC}{-r} = \int_0^V \frac{dV}{Q} = \frac{V}{Q}$$

This gives the **General PFR Design Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,\tau = \theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \int_{C_e}^{C_0} \frac{dC}{-r}\,}$$

**Step 5.** For **first-order kinetics** $-r = kC$:

$$\theta = \int_{C_e}^{C_0} \frac{dC}{kC} = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e}\right)$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $V$:

$$\boxed{\,V = \frac{Q}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e}\right)\,}$$

**Step 7.** Exponentiate to obtain the conversion form:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C_e}{C_0} = \exp(-k\theta)\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Design Equation for a Batch Reactor

**Step 1.** No inflow, no outflow. The mass balance reduces to **accumulation = rate of disappearance**:

$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = -r V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dC}{dt} = -r$$

**Step 2.** For **first-order kinetics**:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$$

**Step 3.** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k\,dt$$

**Step 4.** Integrate from $t=0$ to $t = t_b$ (batch time):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_e}{C_0}\right) = -k t_b$$

**Step 5.** Solve for the **batch reaction time**:

$$\boxed{\,t_b = \frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice $t_b$ for a Batch reactor is **mathematically identical** to $\theta$ for a PFR. Hence: **Batch ≡ PFR for first-order reactions.**

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Question Favorite)

| Property | Batch | CSTR | PFR | n-CSTR in Series |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating state | Unsteady | Steady | Steady | Steady |
| Effluent concentration | $C_0 e^{-k\theta}$ | $\dfrac{C_0}{1+k\theta}$ | $C_0 e^{-k\theta}$ | $\dfrac{C_0}{(1+k\theta/n)^n}$ |
| Volume for first-order (same $C_0, C_e, Q$) | Same as PFR | Largest | Smallest | Approaches PFR as $n \to \infty$ |
| Conversion uniformity | Uniform in time | Uniform in space | Varies along $z$ | Stepwise uniform |
| Shock-load resistance | Poor | Excellent | Poor | Good (improves with $n$) |
| Ease of operation | Labor intensive | Easy | Moderate | Moderate |
| Preferred in advanced treatment | SBR, MBR-SBR | Activated sludge, equalization | Chlorine contact, biofilters | Multistage nitrification/denitrification |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A wastewater with $C_0 = 500 \text{ mg/L}$ BOD is to be reduced to $C_e = 20 \text{ mg/L}$ in a single CSTR. The first-order rate constant $k = 0.4 \text{ d}^{-1}$ and flow rate $Q = 1000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$. Determine (a) the required HRT, (b) the reactor volume, and (c) the volume of a PFR for the same duty. Comment on the volume saved.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** HRT for CSTR from the conversion formula:
$$\theta_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right)$$
$$\theta_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{0.4}\left(\frac{500}{20} - 1\right) = 2.5 \times 24 = 60 \text{ d}$$

**Part (b):** Volume of CSTR:
$$V_{CSTR} = Q\theta_{CSTR} = 1000 \times 60 = 60{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Part (c):** Volume of PFR:
$$V_{PFR} = \frac{Q}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e}\right) = \frac{1000}{0.4}\ln(25) = 2500 \times 3.2189 \approx 8047 \text{ m}^3$$

**Comment:** $V_{CSTR}/V_{PFR} = 60000 / 8047 \approx 7.46$. The CSTR requires **7.46 times more volume** than the PFR for the same substrate removal — confirming the universal superiority of plug-flow kinetics for first-order reactions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams render the **functional flow architecture** of each reactor configuration, including concentration profiles, mixing patterns, and multi-stage nutrient-removal trains. All node IDs are alphanumeric and safe (no reserved keywords).

## 4.1 Schematic — Batch Reactor Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Storage Tank] -->|Charge Q| B[Batch Reactor V]
    B -->|React Time t_b| C[Effluent Storage]
    B --> D[Sludge Draw]
    subgraph BatchLoop["BATCH REACTOR CYCLE"]
        direction TB
        F1[Phase 1: FILL] --> F2[Phase 2: REACT]
        F2 --> F3[Phase 3: SETTLE]
        F3 --> F4[Phase 4: DECANT]
        F4 --> F5[Phase 5: IDLE]
        F5 --> F1
    end
    B -.-> BatchLoop
```

## 4.2 Schematic — CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inf[Q, C0] -->|Inflow| Tank((CSTR Tank\nVolume V\nStirrer))
    Tank -->|Outflow Q, Ce| Eff[Effluent]
    Tank -.->|Sludge Wasted| W[WAS]
    Tank -->|Recycle Qr| R[Return Sludge]
    R --> Tank
    style Tank fill:#e1f5ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **uniform concentration $C_e$** throughout the tank and in the outflow — the defining feature of an ideal CSTR.

## 4.3 Schematic — PFR (Plug Flow Reactor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Q, C0] -->|z=0| Z1[Slice dV1\nConcentration C1]
    Z1 -->|z=z1| Z2[Slice dV2\nConcentration C2]
    Z2 -->|z=z2| Z3[Slice dV3\nConcentration C3]
    Z3 -->|z=L| Out[Q, Ce]
    In -->|No back-mixing| Out
    style In fill:#ffe1e1
    style Out fill:#e1ffe1
    style Z1 fill:#fff4cc
    style Z2 fill:#fff4cc
    style Z3 fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> Color gradient from red (high $C$) to green (low $C$) depicts the **monotonically decreasing concentration profile** along the PFR length.

## 4.4 Schematic — n-CSTRs in Series Approximating PFR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0[Q, C0] --> R1((Reactor 1\nV1, C1))
    R1 --> R2((Reactor 2\nV2, C2))
    R2 --> R3((Reactor 3\nV3, C3))
    R3 --> R4((Reactor n\nVn, Ce))
    R4 --> Qe[Effluent]
    style R1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style R2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style R3 fill:#e1f5ff
    style R4 fill:#e1f5ff
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Modified Ludzack–Ettinger (MLE) Process for Nitrification–Denitrification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inf[Influent\nNH4+, BOD] --> Anoxic((Anoxic Zone\nDenitrification))
    Anoxic -->|Mixed Liquor| Aerobic((Aerobic Zone\nNitrification))
    Aerobic -->|Settle| Clr[Clarifier]
    Clr -->|Effluent| Dis[Disinfection]
    Clr -->|RAS Qr| RAS[Return Sludge]
    RAS --> Anoxic
    Aerobic -.->|Nitrate Recycle Qir| Anoxic
    style Anoxic fill:#fff0cc
    style Aerobic fill:#cce5ff
    style Clr fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This functional flow diagram shows the **internal recycle of nitrate** from the aerobic effluent to the anoxic zone — the signature of the MLE configuration. In the A²/O variant, a **pre-anoxic zone** is added to enhance biological phosphorus removal.

## 4.6 Schematic — PFR with Biomass Recycle (Activated Sludge Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inf[Q, C0] --> PFR((Plug Flow Aeration Tank\nVolume V))
    PFR --> Clar[Final Clarifier]
    Clar --> Eff[Effluent Ce]
    Clar -->|Underflow Qw| WAS[Waste Activated Sludge]
    Clar -->|Underflow Qr| RAS[Return Sludge]
    RAS --> PFR
    style PFR fill:#cce5ff
    style Clar fill:#e1ffe1
    style RAS fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> Recycle ratio $R = Q_r/Q$ is a key design parameter. Increasing $R$ raises MLSS in the reactor, reduces $\theta$ required, but lowers effective substrate concentration for kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a CSTR and a PFR with respect to (i) concentration profile, (ii) hydraulic retention time distribution, and (iii) volume required for first-order substrate removal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | CSTR | PFR |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Concentration profile** | Uniform throughout; $C$ at exit = $C$ inside | Varies along length; $C$ decreases from $C_0$ to $C_e$ |
| **(ii) HRT distribution** | Broad exponential distribution; all ages 0 to ∞ possible | Narrow distribution; ideally all elements have same residence time $\theta$ |
| **(iii) Volume (first-order)** | Largest: $V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{Q C_0 (C_0 - C_e)}{k C_e}$ | Smallest: $V_{PFR} = \dfrac{Q}{k}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C_e}\right)$ |

**Conclusion:** For the same $C_0, C_e, Q$ and first-order kinetics, $V_{CSTR} > V_{PFR}$ always. The ratio is $\dfrac{(1+k\theta)^2}{k\theta \ln(1+k\theta)} > 1$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "n-CSTRs in series"? Why is this configuration used in advanced wastewater treatment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **series of $n$ equal-volume CSTRs** is a flow configuration where wastewater passes sequentially through $n$ completely-mixed tanks before discharge. The overall conversion is:
$$\frac{C_n}{C_0} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{k\theta}{n}\right)^n}$$

As $n \to \infty$, the system **approaches ideal PFR behavior**, while retaining the operational advantages of CSTRs (tank-level mixing, easier control). In advanced treatment, this configuration is used for:
- **Staged nitrification–denitrification** (separate anoxic/aerobic zones).
- **Step-feed activated sludge** for shock-load buffering.
- **Multi-point phosphorus precipitation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each choice typically has sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Model solutions are exhaustive below.

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the steady-state design equation for an ideal CSTR treating a wastewater with first-order reaction kinetics. Start from the general mass balance and clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industry discharges $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$ of wastewater with BOD $= 800 \text{ mg/L}$. A CSTR aeration tank is to be designed to achieve an effluent BOD of $C_e = 40 \text{ mg/L}$. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.30 \text{ d}^{-1}$. Determine: **(i)** Hydraulic Retention Time, **(ii)** Tank volume, **(iii)** Percentage substrate removal efficiency, **(iv)** Compare the CSTR volume with a PFR volume for the same duty. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady-state operation (accumulation = 0).
> 2. Completely mixed tank (effluent concentration = tank concentration).
> 3. Single, well-defined influent concentration $C_0$.
> 4. First-order kinetics: $-r = kC_e$ (rate evaluated at $C_e$).
> 5. Constant density, constant $Q$, isothermal.

**Mass balance on substrate in CSTR:**

$$Q C_0 - Q C_e + r V = 0 \quad \text{(with r being the net rate)}$$

> Using the convention that $-r$ is the rate of substrate disappearance:

$$Q C_0 - Q C_e - (-r) V = 0$$

$$Q(C_0 - C_e) = -r V$$

> Substituting $-r = kC_e$:

$$Q(C_0 - C_e) = k C_e V$$

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{Q(C_0 - C_e)}{k C_e}}$$

> Alternative form using $\theta = V/Q$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k\theta}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) HRT for CSTR:**
$$\theta_{CSTR} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{0.30}\left(\frac{800}{40} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{0.30} \times 19 = 63.33 \text{ d}$$

**(ii) CSTR Volume:**
$$V_{CSTR} = Q\theta = 500 \times 63.33 = 31{,}666.67 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Removal Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100\% = \frac{800 - 40}{800} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

**(iv) PFR Volume for same duty:**
$$V_{PFR} = \frac{Q}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_e}\right) = \frac{500}{0.30}\ln(20) = 1666.67 \times 2.9957 = 4992.85 \text{ m}^3$$

**Volume ratio:**
$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{31{,}666.67}{4992.85} \approx 6.34$$

**Conclusion:** The CSTR requires **6.34 times the volume of a PFR** for the same 95% BOD removal. In practice, the CSTR is often preferred despite the larger volume because of superior operational stability, tolerance to shock loads, and ease of biomass–recycle integration in activated-sludge systems.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Modified Ludzack–Ettinger (MLE)** process with a neat functional flow diagram. Discuss the role of (i) the anoxic zone, (ii) the aerobic zone, and (iii) the internal recycle stream. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant uses **three identical CSTRs in series** to treat $Q = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$ of flow. The influent BOD is $C_0 = 600 \text{ mg/L}$ and the desired effluent BOD is $C_e = 30 \text{ mg/L}$. If the first-order rate constant is $k = 0.50 \text{ d}^{-1}$, determine: **(i)** The required total HRT, **(ii)** Volume of each tank, **(iii)** Concentration of BOD after the first and second reactors, **(iv)** Compare the result with a single CSTR of the same total volume. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MLE Process Explanation (7 marks):**

> The **Modified Ludzack–Ettinger (MLE)** process is a **single-sludge, pre-anoxic biological nutrient removal** configuration for **simultaneous BOD, nitrification, and denitrification**.

**Functional flow:** Influent → **Anoxic Zone** → **Aerobic Zone** → Clarifier → Disinfected Effluent.

**Roles:**

1. **(i) Anoxic Zone:** Receives raw influent (rich in BOD) and the **internal recycle of nitrate-rich mixed liquor** from the aerobic zone. Here, facultative heterotrophs use BOD as the carbon source to **reduce nitrate ($NO_3^-$) to nitrogen gas ($N_2$)** — this is **denitrification**. No free oxygen is present; $NO_3^-$ serves as the terminal electron acceptor.

2. **(ii) Aerobic Zone:** Nitrification occurs. Autotrophic bacteria (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) oxidize **ammonia ($NH_4^+$) → nitrite ($NO_2^-$) → nitrate ($NO_3^-$)** using dissolved oxygen. Most of the BOD removal is also completed here.

3. **(iii) Internal Recycle Stream:** Carries nitrate-laden mixed liquor from the aerobic effluent back to the anoxic zone. The **internal recycle ratio (IR)** typically ranges from **2:1 to 4:1** of the influent flow $Q$. Increasing IR improves nitrogen removal efficiency, but at the cost of higher pumping and aeration energy.

**Settling and Sludge Recycle:** The clarifier separates the mixed liquor; clarified effluent is disinfected; the **return activated sludge (RAS)** is recycled to the anoxic zone at a recycle ratio $R \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$, $C_0 = 600 \text{ mg/L}$, $C_e = C_3 = 30 \text{ mg/L}$, $k = 0.50 \text{ d}^{-1}$, $n = 3$.

> The general formula for $n$ CSTRs in series (first-order):

$$\frac{C_n}{C_0} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{k\theta_{\text{total}}}{n}\right)^n}$$

> Substituting $C_n/C_0 = 30/600 = 0.05$ and $n = 3$:

$$0.05 = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{0.5 \theta_{\text{total}}}{3}\right)^3}$$

**(i) Total HRT:**

$$\left(1 + \frac{0.5 \theta_{\text{total}}}{3}\right)^3 = 20$$

$$1 + \frac{0.5 \theta_{\text{total}}}{3} = 20^{1/3} = 2.7144$$

$$\frac{0.5 \theta_{\text{total}}}{3} = 1.7144$$

$$\theta_{\text{total}} = \frac{3 \times 1.7144}{0.5} = 10.287 \text{ d}$$

**(ii) Volume of each tank:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = Q\theta_{\text{total}} = 1500 \times 10.287 = 15{,}430.5 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{each}} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{n} = \frac{15{,}430.5}{3} = 5143.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Concentration after each reactor:**

$$\frac{C_1}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k\theta/n} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.7144} = \frac{1}{2.7144} = 0.3684$$

$$C_1 = 600 \times 0.3684 = 221.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{1}{2.7144} \Rightarrow C_2 = 221.0 \times 0.3684 = 81.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\frac{C_3}{C_2} = 0.3684 \Rightarrow C_3 = 81.4 \times 0.3684 = 30.0 \text{ mg/L} \;\checkmark$$

**(iv) Comparison with a single CSTR of the same total volume:**

$$\theta_{\text{single}} = \theta_{\text{total}} = 10.287 \text{ d}$$

$$\frac{C_e^{\text{single}}}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5 \times 10.287} = \frac{1}{6.1435} = 0.1628$$

$$C_e^{\text{single}} = 600 \times 0.1628 = 97.66 \text{ mg/L}$$

> A single CSTR of the **same total volume** can only achieve an effluent of **97.66 mg/L** — far higher than the target 30 mg/L. The 3-CSTR series achieves a much lower effluent (30 mg/L), demonstrating that **staging improves performance** for first-order reactions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing HRT ($\theta$) with MCRT ($\theta_c$):** HRT is hydraulic (water residence), MCRT is sludge age (solids residence). Mixing these in a 7-mark numerical will cost full marks.
> 2. **Wrong rate law substitution in CSTR:** Always use $-r$ evaluated at $C_e$ (the effluent concentration), NOT at $C_0$. This single error invalidates the entire derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $k$ for first-order has units of $\text{d}^{-1}$; the student must state this explicitly. Numerical answers without units attract deductions.
> 4. **Misapplying n-CSTR formula:** The exponent is $n$, and $k\theta$ is divided by $n$ (not multiplied). Verify by setting $n=1$ to recover the single-CSTR formula.
> 5. **PFR vs CSTR sign errors:** In the PFR derivation, integration limits must go from $C_0$ to $C_e$ correctly; reversing them gives a negative volume.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment" step:** KTU values the interpretive conclusion (e.g., "the CSTR requires 6× the volume") — never omit the discussion.
> 7. **Diagram omissions in MLE/A²O answers:** A flow diagram is **mandatory** for 7-mark questions on nutrient-removal configurations. A textual-only answer is penalized.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reactor Configurations**

- **Three Ideal Reactors:** Batch (BR), Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR), Plug Flow Reactor (PFR).
- **HRT formula:** $\theta = V/Q$, units: **hours or days**.
- **General design equation (any reactor):** $V = \dfrac{Q(C_0 - C_e)}{-r}$.
- **CSTR conversion (1st order):** $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k\theta}$.
- **PFR conversion (1st order):** $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \exp(-k\theta)$.
- **Batch conversion (1st order):** $\dfrac{C_e}{C_0} = \exp(-k t_b)$ — **identical to PFR**.
- **Volume efficiency:** $V_{CSTR} > V_{PFR}$ for any $k\theta > 0$ and 1st-order reactions.
- **n-CSTR formula:** $C_n/C_0 = (1 + k\theta/n)^{-n}$; limit $n \to \infty$ gives PFR.
- **PFR with recycle:** Use $-r = kC$ with recycle-modified $C_0$ and $Q_{\text{eff}} = Q(1+R)$.
- **Real-world reactors:** CSTR → activated sludge, equalization; PFR → chlorine contact, biofilters; SBR → package plants; MBR → membrane-coupled; MLE / A²O → nutrient removal.
- **Selection criteria:** Conversion target, influent variability (shock loads), land availability, capital vs. operating cost, and downstream process compatibility.
- **Common KTU trap:** For zero-order kinetics, $V_{CSTR} = V_{PFR}$ (both equal to $Q(C_0 - C_e)/k$); the volume advantage of PFR is **only** seen for first-order and higher-order reactions.
- **For Monod kinetics:** CSTR is generally more efficient at low substrate concentrations (e.g., polishing nitrification), while PFR handles high-concentration shocks better.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Oxidation Processes: Theory of Advanced Oxidation
Types of Oxidizing Agents
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Oxidizing Agents

# 🧪 Types of Oxidizing Agents in Advanced Wastewater Treatment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxidizing Agents (Oxidants) in Wastewater Treatment** are chemical species that accept electrons from a substrate (the reductant) during a redox reaction, thereby oxidizing the target pollutant. In the context of Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT), oxidizing agents are employed to transform recalcitrant organic compounds, inactivate pathogenic microorganisms, precipitate dissolved metals, and decolorize effluent streams that are resistant to conventional biological treatment.

In electrochemistry terms, an oxidizing agent is a species with a **high standard electrode potential ($E^\circ$)**, which is the thermodynamic driving force that governs the spontaneity of electron transfer. The stronger the oxidant, the higher its tendency to gain electrons and degrade pollutants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Electrode Potential ($E^\circ$):** A measure (in Volts, **V**) of the tendency of a chemical species to be reduced. Higher (more positive) $E^\circ$ values indicate stronger oxidizing agents.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an oxidizing agent as a **"garbage collector"** in the molecular world. Imagine a city (your wastewater) filled with trash (pollutants, pathogens, dyes, organics). The garbage truck (the oxidant) comes in, takes away the waste (gains electrons), and in doing so, cleans the city. The stronger the truck's engine (higher $E^\circ$), the more stubborn and toxic the waste it can handle.

A more accurate chemical analogy is **"molecular Pac-Man"** — oxidants like ozone ($O_3$), chlorine ($Cl_2$), and hydroxyl radicals ($\cdot OH$) "consume" electrons from pollutants, breaking them into smaller, less harmful, or even mineralized end-products ($CO_2$, $H_2O$, salts).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU-style problems, you will often be asked to *select* an oxidant for a given pollutant. The selection logic follows the **Oxidant Strength Hierarchy** and the **targeted functional group**. Always link the choice of oxidant to the mechanism of action (e.g., $O_3$ for phenols, $ClO_2$ for ammonia-resistant disinfection, $H_2O_2$/UV for AOPs).

---

### 1.3 Why Oxidizing Agents Are Crucial in Module 4

Module 4 of OECHT725 typically deals with **chemical oxidation processes** that go beyond primary/secondary treatment. Oxidants are the workhorses for:

- **Disinfection** (residual pathogen control)
- **Micropollutant removal** (pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors)
- **Color & odor removal** (textile and municipal effluents)
- **Refractory organics breakdown** (humic substances, surfactants)
- **Inorganics oxidation** ($Fe^{2+} \to Fe^{3+}$, $Mn^{2+} \to Mn^{4+}$, $S^{2-} \to SO_4^{2-}$)

---

### 1.4 The Master Classification of Oxidizing Agents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mandate:** The following taxonomy is the canonical classification structure expected in board answers.

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Halogen-based** | $Cl_2$, $ClO_2$, $NaOCl$, $Ca(OCl)_2$, Chloramines | Disinfection, color removal |
| **Oxygen-based** | $O_2$, $O_3$, $H_2O_2$ | Aerobic oxidation, AOPs, color removal |
| **Permanganate** | $KMnO_4$ | Iron/Manganese removal, taste & odor control |
| **Advanced Oxidation (Radical-based)** | $\cdot OH$, $O_2^{\cdot-}$, $SO_4^{\cdot-}$ via $H_2O_2/UV$, $O_3/UV$, $O_3/H_2O_2$, Fenton, Photo-Fenton | Refractory organics, micropollutants |
| **Heterogeneous / Catalytic** | $TiO_2/UV$, $Fe^0$ (ZVI) | Photocatalysis, in-situ chemical oxidation (ISCO) |
| **Miscellaneous** | $Br_2$, $I_2$, $BrCl$ | Specialized industrial applications |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Theory of Each Major Oxidizing Agent

#### 2.1.1 Chlorine ($Cl_2$) and Hypochlorite ($OCl^-$)

Chlorine is supplied as **liquid $Cl_2$**, **sodium hypochlorite ($NaOCl$)**, or **calcium hypochlorite ($Ca(OCl)_2$)**. When added to water, it forms **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)**, which is the actual disinfecting species.

The governing equilibrium reactions are:

$$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$$

$$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$$

**Why HOCl matters:** HOCl is **~80–100 times more effective** as a disinfectant than $OCl^-$ because the uncharged HOCl penetrates microbial cell walls more easily (cell walls are lipophilic). At pH > 7.5, the equilibrium shifts to the weaker $OCl^-$ form — this is the **"breakpoint chlorination"** concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breakpoint Chlorination:** The chlorine dose at which all ammonia has been oxidized to $N_2$ (and nitriles are minimized). Beyond this dose, **free available chlorine (FAC)** residual appears. KTU 2024 questions frequently test this concept.

**Advantages:** Low cost, established residual, broad-spectrum.
**Disadvantages:** Forms **trihalomethanes (THMs)** and **haloacetic acids (HAAs)** — DBPs (Disinfection By-Products) of serious public health concern.

---

#### 2.1.2 Chlorine Dioxide ($ClO_2$)

$ClO_2$ is a **selective oxidant** with $E^\circ = +0.95\ \text{V}$ (vs. SHE). It is generated on-site (cannot be compressed) via:

$$5 NaClO_2 + 4 HCl \to 4 ClO_2 + 5 NaCl + 2 H_2O$$

Or via the **sodium chlorite + chlorine** route:

$$2 NaClO_2 + Cl_2 \to 2 ClO_2 + 2 NaCl$$

> [!NOTE]
> $ClO_2$ does **not** form THMs because it does **not** react with humic substances to produce halogenated organics in the same way $Cl_2$ does. It is, however, **~5× more expensive** than $Cl_2$.

**Mechanism:** Direct electron transfer; remains as a dissolved gas (no hydrolysis to HOCl).

---

#### 2.1.3 Ozone ($O_3$)

Ozone is the **second strongest oxidant** after the hydroxyl radical, with $E^\circ = +2.07\ \text{V}$.

$$O_3 + H_2O \to 2 HO^{\cdot} + O_2 \quad \text{(indirect, radical pathway at high pH)}$$

$$O_3 + \text{Pollutant} \to \text{Oxidation products} \quad \text{(direct, electrophilic attack at low pH)}$$

**Two pathways:**

| **Pathway** | **Conditions** | **Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Molecular** | $pH < 7$ | Selective electrophilic attack on C=C, amines, sulfides |
| **Indirect Radical** | $pH > 7$ | Decomposition to $\cdot OH$ radicals; non-selective |

**Generation:** Corona discharge in dry air/$O_2$ (energy-intensive, ~10–18 kWh/kg $O_3$).

---

#### 2.1.4 Hydrogen Peroxide ($H_2O_2$)

A "green" oxidant (only by-product is water) with $E^\circ = +1.78\ \text{V}$ alone, but vastly enhanced when coupled with catalysts or UV (yielding the $\cdot OH$ radical with $E^\circ = +2.80\ \text{V}$).

$$H_2O_2 + h\nu \to 2 HO^{\cdot}$$

$$H_2O_2 + Fe^{2+} \to HO^{\cdot} + HO^- + Fe^{3+} \quad \text{(Fenton's reaction)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Fenton's Reagent:** A mixture of $H_2O_2$ and $Fe^{2+}$ at acidic pH (2.5–3.5) producing hydroxyl radicals. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** for 14-mark questions.

---

#### 2.1.5 Potassium Permanganate ($KMnO_4$)

Deep purple oxidant, $E^\circ = +1.51\ \text{V}$ (in acidic) or $+0.59\ \text{V}$ (in neutral).

$$MnO_4^- + 8H^+ + 5e^- \to Mn^{2+} + 4H_2O \quad (\text{acidic})$$

$$MnO_4^- + 2H_2O + 3e^- \to MnO_2 + 4OH^- \quad (\text{neutral/alkaline})$$

**Specialty:** Excellent for $Fe^{2+}$ and $Mn^{2+}$ oxidation in groundwater treatment, where it forms insoluble oxides that are then filtered.

---

#### 2.1.6 Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

AOPs are defined as **near-ambient temperature and pressure processes** that generate the **hydroxyl radical ($\cdot OH$)** as the primary oxidant. $\cdot OH$ has $E^\circ = +2.80\ \text{V}$ — second only to fluorine.

| **AOP System** | **Key Reaction** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| $O_3/UV$ | $O_3 + H_2O + h\nu \to 2 HO^{\cdot}$ | Micropollutants, pesticides |
| $O_3/H_2O_2$ (Peroxone) | $H_2O_2 \to HO_2^- + H^+$; $O_3 + HO_2^- \to \cdot OH$ | Refractory organics |
| $H_2O_2/UV$ | $H_2O_2 + h\nu \to 2 HO^{\cdot}$ | Trace organics, TOC |
| Fenton | $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + HO^- + HO^{\cdot}$ | Industrial wastewater |
| Photo-Fenton | Fenton + UV | Increased $\cdot OH$ yield, $Fe^{3+}$ photo-reduction |
| $TiO_2/UV$ | $TiO_2 + h\nu \to e^- + h^+$; $h^+ + H_2O \to HO^{\cdot}$ | Photocatalysis (heterogeneous) |
| Sonolysis | $H_2O \xrightarrow{)))} HO^{\cdot} + H^{\cdot}$ | Sludge disintegration |

---

### 2.2 The Oxidant Strength Hierarchy (Memorize This!)

$$F_2 > HO^{\cdot} > O_3 > H_2O_2 > MnO_4^- > ClO_2 > Cl_2 > Br_2 > I_2$$

Quantitatively (standard reduction potentials, V vs. SHE):

| **Oxidant** | **$E^\circ$ (V)** | **Relative Power** |
|---|---|---|
| $F_2$ | +2.87 | 1 (reference) |
| $\cdot OH$ | +2.80 | 0.98 |
| $O_3$ | +2.07 | 0.72 |
| $H_2O_2$ | +1.78 | 0.62 |
| $MnO_4^-$ (acidic) | +1.51 | 0.53 |
| $ClO_2$ | +0.95 | 0.33 |
| $Cl_2$ | +1.36 | 0.47 |
| $O_2$ | +1.23 | 0.43 |
| $Br_2$ | +1.07 | 0.37 |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This table is the **highest-priority revision block** for Module 4. Memorize all equations, the $E^\circ$ values, and the conditions.

| **Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Significance / Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Electrode Potential | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | Predicts thermodynamic feasibility of oxidation |
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Adjusts $E^\circ$ for non-standard conditions |
| Chlorine Hydrolysis | $Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$ | Defines HOCl availability |
| HOCl / $OCl^-$ Equilibrium | $pK_a = 7.5$ at 25 °C | HOCl dominates below pH 7.5 |
| Ozone Decomposition | $2O_3 \to 2O_2 + O^{\cdot}$ ; $O^{\cdot} + H_2O \to 2HO^{\cdot}$ | Initiates AOP chain |
| Fenton's Reaction | $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$ | Workhorse AOP |
| Photo-Fenton | $Fe(OH)^{2+} + h\nu \to Fe^{2+} + HO^{\cdot}$ | Regenerates $Fe^{2+}$ catalyst |
| Peroxone | $2O_3 + H_2O_2 \to 3O_2 + 2HO^{\cdot}$ | $O_3$–$H_2O_2$ synergy |
| Permanganate (neutral) | $MnO_4^- + 2H_2O + 3e^- \to MnO_2 + 4OH^-$ | $MnO_2$ sludge for filtration |
| Permanganate (acidic) | $MnO_4^- + 8H^+ + 5e^- \to Mn^{2+} + 4H_2O$ | $Mn^{2+}$ in solution |
| Disinfection (Chick-Watson) | $\ln(N/N_0) = -kC^n t$ | Dose × Contact time |
| CT Concept | $\text{CT} = C \times t$ | KTU standard: $C$ (mg/L), $t$ (min) |
| Ozone Mass Transfer | $E = \dfrac{[O_3]_{in} - [O_3]_{out}}{[O_3]_{in}}$ | Typical: 90–98% |
| Oxidant Demand | $\text{Dose} = \text{Demand} + \text{Residual}$ | Operational design equation |
| Fenton Stoichiometry | $Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2$ ratio $\approx 1:5$ to $1:10$ (molar) | Optimum for COD removal |

**Symbols:** $F$ = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol**; $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**; $T$ = absolute temperature (K); $n$ = electrons transferred; $Q$ = reaction quotient.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application** | **Best-Suited Oxidant** | **Why?** |
|---|---|---|
| Municipal disinfection | $Cl_2$ (large plants), $ClO_2$ (DBP concerns) | Cost, residual |
| Industrial effluent (refractory COD) | $O_3$, Fenton, $O_3/H_2O_2$ | Non-selective, complete mineralization |
| Iron/Manganese removal (groundwater) | $KMnO_4$ or $Cl_2$ | Forms insoluble oxides |
| Sludge conditioning | $H_2O_2$ + $Fe^{2+}$ (Fenton) | Cell lysis, dewaterability |
| Micropollutants (pharmaceuticals) | $O_3$, $O_3/H_2O_2$, UV/$H_2O_2$ | AOPs, $\cdot OH$ |
| Color removal (textile dye) | $O_3$ or $NaOCl$ | Chromophore cleavage |
| Boiler feed water (deaeration) | $O_2$ (mechanical) | Corrosion control |
| Tetrachloroethylene destruction | $KMnO_4$ (in-situ) | Permeable reactive barrier |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations

#### Derivation 1: Disinfection Kinetics (Chick–Watson Model)

The classic model assumes first-order inactivation with respect to viable organisms and an empirical power-law dependence on disinfectant concentration.

$$\text{Step 1 — Start with the general rate expression:}$$

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot C^n \cdot N$$

where $N$ = number of viable organisms at time $t$, $k$ = inactivation rate constant, $C$ = oxidant concentration, $n$ = empirical coefficient (often $\approx 1$ for chlorine).

$$\text{Step 2 — Separate variables:}$$

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot C^n \cdot dt$$

$$\text{Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$ (assuming constant $C$):}$$

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot C^n \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot C^n \cdot t$$

$$\text{Step 4 — Exponentiate to obtain survival ratio:}$$

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kC^n t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical **Chick–Watson equation** used universally to design disinfection contact tanks. KTU questions often ask: *"Compute the log reduction given $k$, $C$, and $t$."* The **log removal** is $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = \dfrac{kC^n t}{2.303}$.

---

#### Derivation 2: Linking Electrode Potential to Oxidant Feasibility (Nernst Equation)

For a generic half-reaction $aA + ne^- \rightleftharpoons bB$, the Nernst equation corrects $E^\circ$ for non-standard conditions:

$$\text{Step 1 — The Gibbs–Helmholtz relation:}$$

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

$$\text{Step 2 — Using $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:}$$

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

$$\text{Step 3 — Divide through by $-nF$:}$$

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

$$\text{Step 4 — At standard temperature $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$:}$$

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

**Real-world use:** Used in KTU problems to determine whether a given oxidant (e.g., $O_3$ with $E^\circ = +2.07\ \text{V}$) can oxidize a target species (e.g., $Mn^{2+} \to Mn^{4+}$, $E^\circ = +1.51\ \text{V}$). The cell potential is $\Delta E = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} > 0$.

**Worked example:** Will $O_3$ oxidize $Mn^{2+}$ at pH 7?

$$\Delta E^\circ = 2.07 - 1.51 = +0.56\ \text{V} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Yes, thermodynamically feasible}$$

---

#### Derivation 3: Optimum Fenton Dose Ratio for COD Removal

The Fenton process stoichiometry is:

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$$

$$\cdot OH + \text{Organics} \to \text{CO}_2 + H_2O + \text{intermediates}$$

**Mass-balance approach for design:**

$$\text{Step 1 — Define initial COD as $COD_0$ (mg/L), final as $COD_f$ (mg/L).}$$

$$\text{Step 2 — Hydrogen peroxide demand (theoretical, for complete mineralization):}$$

$$H_2O_2\ (\text{mg/L}) = 2.125 \times COD_0 \quad (\text{stoichiometric coefficient})$$

because $H_2O_2$ supplies 2 electrons per mole (molar mass 34 g/mol), and 1 g $O_2$ = 8 g COD equivalent.

$$\text{Step 3 — Practical dose:}$$

$$[H_2O_2]_{practical} = \alpha \times 2.125 \times COD_0$$

where $\alpha$ is an empirical factor (typically 1.0–1.5 for readily oxidizable COD).

$$\text{Step 4 — Optimum } Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2 \text{ molar ratio:}$$

Empirically $\approx 1:5$ to $1:10$. For KTU design problems, use the lower bound $1:5$ as the conservative start.

$$[Fe^{2+}]_{opt} = \frac{[H_2O_2]}{5 \times 34} \times 56 = \frac{[H_2O_2]}{5 \times 34} \times 56 \quad \text{(in mg/L of } Fe^{2+})$$

---

#### Derivation 4: CT (Concentration × Time) Concept for Disinfection Design

The US EPA's CT tables specify the product of residual disinfectant concentration (mg/L) and contact time (min) required for a given log inactivation.

$$\text{CT} = C_{residual} \times t_{contact}$$

**Example (KTU-style):** Required log inactivation of *E. coli* = 3-log, pH < 7, water temperature 20 °C. EPA table specifies CT = 1.5 mg·min/L for free chlorine. If the residual after the contact tank is $C = 0.5\ \text{mg/L}$, then:

$$t = \frac{CT}{C} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3\ \text{minutes}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection Decision Tree

| **Pollutant / Goal** | **Recommended Oxidant** | **Mechanism** | **Caveats** |
|---|---|---|---|
| E. coli / Total coliforms | $Cl_2$ or $NaOCl$ | HOCl oxidation of enzymes | DBP formation |
| Giardia / Virus (CT-strict) | $O_3$ or $ClO_2$ | Higher $E^\circ$ | $O_3$ cost |
| $Fe^{2+}$ → $Fe^{3+}$ | $Cl_2$ or $KMnO_4$ | Direct electron transfer | $KMnO_4$ → $MnO_2$ sludge |
| $Mn^{2+}$ → $Mn^{4+}$ | $KMnO_4$ (best) | $MnO_2$ precipitate | pH ≥ 7.5 |
| Phenol / Pesticide | $O_3$, $H_2O_2$/UV | Ring cleavage | Mineralization to $CO_2$ |
| Textile dye (azo, anthraquinone) | $O_3$, Fenton | Chromophore attack | Salt interference |
| Trace pharmaceuticals | AOPs ($O_3/H_2O_2$, UV/$H_2O_2$) | $\cdot OH$ radical | BDD/TiO₂ for hard cases |
| $H_2S$ / odor | $Cl_2$ or $H_2O_2$ | Sulfide oxidation | Residual $S$ species |
| Ammonia ($NH_3$) removal | **Breakpoint chlorination** | $NH_3$ → $N_2$ | High Cl₂ dose (8–10 mg/mg N) |
