## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Define an oxidizing agent in the context of advanced wastewater treatment. List any four commonly used oxidants with one application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

An **oxidizing agent** in AWT is a chemical species that accepts electrons from a pollutant (the reductant), thereby raising the oxidation state of the target contaminant. The strength of an oxidant is quantified by its **standard electrode potential ($E^\circ$)** — the higher the value, the more powerful the oxidant.

| **Oxidant** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| $Cl_2$ | Municipal disinfection (kills coliforms) |
| $O_3$ | Color removal from textile effluent |
| $H_2O_2$ | Component of Fenton reagent for COD reduction |
| $KMnO_4$ | Oxidation of soluble $Fe^{2+}$/$Mn^{2+}$ in groundwater |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Distinguish between breakpoint chlorination and marginal chlorination. Why is breakpoint chlorination preferred?"**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Marginal Chlorination** | **Breakpoint Chlorination** |
|---|---|---|
| Cl₂ dose | < Sum of chlorine demand | ≥ Sum of chlorine demand + ammonia destruction dose |
| Residual | Combined (chloramines) | Free available chlorine (HOCl/$OCl^-$) |
| Disinfection power | Weak | High |
| By-products | Chloramines (odor) | Mostly $N_2$ (if optimized) |
| Purpose | Maintain residual for distribution | Effective disinfection + ammonia removal |

**Breakpoint chlorination is preferred** because it yields **free available chlorine (FAC)** which is **far more potent (80–100×)** than combined chlorine (chloramines) and also **destroys ammonia**, eliminating nitrogenous oxygen demand in the distribution system.

---

### 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a) Classify oxidizing agents used in advanced wastewater treatment with suitable examples.** **(7 Marks)**
> **(b) Explain the Fenton oxidation process with its chemistry, operating conditions, and merits/demerits.** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Oxidizing Agents (7 marks):**

Oxidizing agents in AWT are classified based on their chemical nature and operating mechanism:

**1. Halogen-based oxidants** (Acts via HOCl/$OCl^-$ formation)
   - $Cl_2$ (gaseous/liquid)
   - $NaOCl$ (sodium hypochlorite)
   - $Ca(OCl)_2$ (calcium hypochlorite, bleaching powder)
   - $ClO_2$ (chlorine dioxide, generated on-site)
   - Chloramines ($NH_2Cl$, $NHCl_2$)

**2. Oxygen-based oxidants** (Direct or via radical generation)
   - $O_2$ (pure oxygen activated sludge)
   - $O_3$ (ozone, generated by corona discharge)
   - $H_2O_2$ (hydrogen peroxide, often activated)

**3. Permanganate-based**
   - $KMnO_4$ in acidic ($Mn^{2+}$ product) or neutral ($MnO_2$ product) media

**4. Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** — generate $\cdot OH$ radical
   - $O_3/UV$, $O_3/H_2O_2$ (Peroxone), $H_2O_2/UV$
   - Fenton ($Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2$) and Photo-Fenton
   - $TiO_2/UV$ (heterogeneous photocatalysis)

**5. Miscellaneous**
   - $Br_2$, $I_2$ (specialized), Zero-valent iron ($Fe^0$)

Selection criteria: $E^\circ$ value, target pollutant chemistry, pH, cost, by-product profile.

---

**(b) Fenton Oxidation Process (7 marks):**

**Chemistry:**
The Fenton reaction is a homogeneous catalytic process that generates hydroxyl radicals ($\cdot OH$) — one of the most powerful oxidants ($E^\circ = +2.80\ \text{V}$):

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$$

The $\cdot OH$ radical non-selectively attacks organic molecules:

$$\cdot OH + \text{RH (organic)} \to R^{\cdot} + H_2O \to \text{CO}_2 + H_2O$$

**Operating Conditions:**
- pH: **2.5–3.5** (critical; at pH > 4, $Fe^{3+}$ precipitates as $Fe(OH)_3$)
- $Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2$ molar ratio: **1:5 to 1:10** (optimum)
- $H_2O_2$ dose: $\approx 2.125 \times COD$ (mg/L) stoichiometrically
- Temperature: ambient (20–30 °C)
- Reaction time: 30–120 min

**Process Sequence:**
1. Acidification → pH 2.5–3.5
2. $Fe^{2+}$ addition (e.g., $FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O$)
3. $H_2O_2$ dosing with slow mixing
4. Reaction (Fenton tank)
5. Neutralization (pH 7–8) with NaOH/lime
6. Coagulation/Flocculation (Fe(OH)₃ flocs)
7. Sedimentation → Iron sludge + clarified effluent

**Merits:**
- High COD removal efficiency (60–90%)
- Cheap reagents
- Simple operation
- Effective for refractory/bio-toxic compounds
- Iron sludge can be settled out

**Demerits:**
- Strict pH control needed (acidification + neutralization cost)
- Sludge disposal issue (iron hydroxide)
- $H_2O_2$ self-decomposition at high doses
- Requires post-treatment for residual iron

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** — **Alternative choice**
> **(a) With the help of standard electrode potentials, justify why ozone is preferred over chlorine for advanced treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater.** **(7 Marks)**
> **(b) Describe the chemistry and operational advantages of chlorine dioxide as a wastewater disinfectant.** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Why Ozone over Chlorine for Pharmaceutical Wastewater (7 marks):**

**Standard Electrode Potentials:**
- $O_3$: $E^\circ = +2.07\ \text{V}$ (acidic solution, $O_3 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \to O_2 + H_2O$)
- $Cl_2$: $E^\circ = +1.36\ \text{V}$ (acidic solution, $Cl_2 + 2e^- \to 2Cl^-$)

**Justification:**

1. **Higher Oxidizing Power:** $O_3$ ($E^\circ = +2.07\ \text{V}$) is **~50% stronger** than $Cl_2$ ($E^\circ = +1.36\ \text{V}$), enabling it to break down **recalcitrant aromatic rings** in pharmaceutical molecules (e.g., diclofenac, carbamazepine) that $Cl_2$ cannot attack.

2. **DBP Avoidance:** $Cl_2$ reacts with humic substances and pharmaceuticals to form **trihalomethanes (THMs)** and **haloacetic acids (HAAs)** — many of which are themselves carcinogenic and toxic to aquatic life. $O_3$ produces **no halogenated DBPs**.

3. **Hydroxyl Radical Generation:** At pH > 7, $O_3$ decomposes via:
$$O_3 + OH^- \to \cdot O_2^- + HO_2^{\cdot}; \quad \cdot O_2^- + O_3 \to \cdot OH$$
The resulting $\cdot OH$ radical ($E^\circ = +2.80\ \text{V}$) attacks pharmaceuticals **non-selectively** at near-diffusion-controlled rates ($k \approx 10^9$ L/mol·s).

4. **No Persistent Residuals:** $O_3$ decomposes to $O_2$, avoiding long-term residual toxicity. However, this is also a drawback (no residual protection downstream).

5. **Thermodynamic Feasibility Check:** For a target species with $E^\circ_{\text{red}} = 1.0\ \text{V}$ (e.g., a substituted phenol), the cell potential is:
   - With $O_3$: $\Delta E = 2.07 - 1.0 = +1.07\ \text{V}$ (highly spontaneous)
   - With $Cl_2$: $\Delta E = 1.36 - 1.0 = +0.36\ \text{V}$ (less favorable)

**Conclusion:** $O_3$ provides higher driving force, broader spectrum, and safer by-product profile, justifying its selection for advanced pharmaceutical wastewater treatment.

---

**(b) Chlorine Dioxide Chemistry and Operational Advantages (7 marks):**

**Chemistry of $ClO_2$:**
$ClO_2$ is a **dissolved gas** (orange-yellow), generated on-site by:

$$2NaClO_2 + Cl_2 \to 2ClO_2 + 2NaCl$$

It is a **selective, one-electron oxidant**:
$$ClO_2 + e^- \to ClO_2^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.95\ \text{V})$$

**Key Reactions in Wastewater:**
- Phenol oxidation: $ClO_2$ cleaves aromatic rings via electrophilic substitution
- $Fe^{2+}$ oxidation: $5Fe^{2+} + ClO_2 + 4H^+ \to 5Fe^{3+} + Cl^- + 2H_2O$
- $Mn^{2+}$ oxidation: $2ClO_2 + 5Mn^{2+} + 6H_2O \to 5MnO_2(s) + 2Cl^- + 12H^+$
- Disinfection: inactivates *E. coli*, viruses, *Giardia* by damaging enzymes

**Operational Advantages:**

1. **No THM/HAAs Formation:** $ClO_2$ does **not** undergo hydrolysis to HOCl; therefore, no halogenation of organics → no THMs/HAAs. This is the **single biggest advantage** over $Cl_2$.

2. **Effective at High pH:** Disinfection efficiency is **less pH-dependent** than chlorine. HOCl/OCl⁻ equilibrium ($pK_a = 7.5$) does not apply to $ClO_2$.

3. **Strong Oxidant:** $E^\circ = +0.95\ \text{V}$ (but acts as a 1-electron oxidant, allowing selective attacks on phenols, sulfides, amines).

4. **Pre-Oxidation Capability:** Excellent for $Fe^{2+}$/$Mn^{2+}$ removal without forming chlorinated by-products.

5. **Long-Lasting Residual:** Provides residual disinfection throughout the distribution system (longer-lived than $O_3$).

**Disadvantages:**
- Must be generated on-site (volatile, explosive at concentrations > 10% in air)
- Forms chlorite ($ClO_2^-$) and chlorate ($ClO_3^-$) by-products (regulated by EPA)
- ~5× more expensive than $Cl_2$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Don't confuse $E^\circ$ of $Cl_2$ in acidic vs. basic medium.** $E^\circ(Cl_2/Cl^-) = +1.36\ \text{V}$ in acid, but $+1.36\ \text{V}$ is the standard; remember conditions in the problem statement.
>
> 2. **Don't write "ClO₂ is the strongest oxidant."** It is NOT. The hierarchy is $F_2 > \cdot OH > O_3 > H_2O_2 > KMnO_4 > ClO_2 > Cl_2$. Misordering costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Don't forget pH effects.** Always mention pH when discussing HOCl/OCl⁻ (pKa = 7.5), Fenton (pH 2.5–3.5), and ozone decomposition (pH > 7 promotes $\cdot OH$).
>
> 4. **Don't skip the by-products.** $Cl_2$ → THMs; $O_3$ → bromate (in Br⁻-containing water); $H_2O_2$ → water (clean); $KMnO_4$ → $MnO_2$ sludge. KTU examiners expect this!
>
> 5. **Don't write $H_2O_2 + Fe^{2+} \to Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$ without balancing.** Full balance is mandatory.
>
> 6. **Always state $E^\circ$ in Volts and reference SHE (Standard Hydrogen Electrode).**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Quick-Reference Checklist (Use for last-hour revision)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Oxidant = electron acceptor; strength ∝ $E^\circ$
- [ ] **Hierarchy:** $F_2 > \cdot OH (2.80) > O_3 (2.07) > H_2O_2 (1.78) > KMnO_4 (1.51) > Cl_2 (1.36) > ClO_2 (0.95)$
- [ ] **Chick–Watson:** $\ln(N/N_0) = -kC^n t$ — disinfection kinetics
- [ ] **CT concept:** $CT = C \times t$ — design parameter for contact tanks
- [ ] **HOCl vs $OCl^-$:** HOCl dominates at pH < 7.5; HOCl is **80–100× more effective**
- [ ] **Breakpoint chlorination:** Dose that destroys all $NH_3$ and yields free chlorine residual
- [ ] **$ClO_2$ advantage:** No THMs/HAAs (no HOCl intermediate)
- [ ] **$O_3$ pathways:** Direct (low pH) vs indirect $\cdot OH$ (high pH)
- [ ] **Fenton chemistry:** $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$ at pH 2.5–3.5
- [ ] **Fenton optimum:** $Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2$ molar ratio ≈ 1:5 to 1:10
- [ ] **Photo-Fenton:** UV regenerates $Fe^{2+}$ from $Fe^{3+}$ → enhances $\cdot OH$ yield
- [ ] **AOPs:** Generate $\cdot OH$ ($E^\circ = 2.80\ \text{V}$) — non-selective, second-order rate constant $\sim 10^9$ L/mol·s
- [ ] **$KMnO_4$ products:** Acidic → $Mn^{2+}$ (soluble); Neutral → $MnO_2$ (sludge)
- [ ] **Nernst equation:** $E = E^\circ - (0.0592/n) \log Q$ (at 25 °C)
- [ ] **$H_2O_2$ stoichiometry:** 2.125 mg $H_2O_2$ per mg COD (theoretical, for complete mineralization)
- [ ] **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- [ ] **DBPs of concern:** $Cl_2$ → THMs, HAAs; $O_3$ → bromate; $ClO_2$ → chlorite
- [ ] **Selection rule:** Match the oxidant's $E^\circ$ and chemistry to the target pollutant's functional group
- [ ] **Residual behavior:** $Cl_2$ ✓ (long); $ClO_2$ ✓ (moderate); $O_3$ ✗ (none); $H_2O_2$ ✗ (none)
- [ ] **Industrial vs municipal:** $O_3$/Fenton for industrial; $Cl_2$ for municipal (cost-driven)

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ozone Based and Non-Ozone Based Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ozone Based and Non-Ozone Based Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ozone-Based Processes

### Formal Definition
**Ozone-based processes** in advanced wastewater treatment (AWT) are chemical Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) that utilize ozone (O₃), either alone or in combination with hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), ultraviolet (UV) radiation, or catalysts, to generate highly reactive **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** for the destruction of refractory, non-biodegradable, and micropollutant organic compounds.

> [!NOTE]
> AOPs are defined as processes that generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in sufficient quantity to act as the primary oxidant for water purification. The •OH radical has a redox potential of **E° = 2.80 V**, second only to fluorine (2.87 V) and far superior to ozone (2.07 V) and chlorine (1.36 V).

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Pac-Man of Pollutants")
Imagine the hydroxyl radical (•OH) as a tiny, hyperactive "Pac-Man" that darts through wastewater at near-light speed, gobbling up stubborn organic pollutants (pesticides, pharmaceuticals, dyes) that normal bacteria cannot digest. **Ozone** is the "machine" that creates these Pac-Men. When ozone is bubbled into water, it doesn't just attack pollutants directly (the "direct pathway") — it also breaks apart to spawn millions of •OH Pac-Men (the "indirect pathway"). Adding helpers like H₂O₂ or UV light is like turning up the Pac-Man factory's production rate.

### The Three Main Ozone-Based AOP Configurations
1. **O₃ alone (Ozonation)** — Direct molecular attack + indirect radical pathway.
2. **O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone process)** — Hydrogen peroxide accelerates •OH generation.
3. **O₃/UV (Photo-ozonation)** — UV photolysis of O₃ produces extra H₂O₂ and radicals.

### Key Physical/Chemical Constants

| Constant | Value | Significance |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| Redox potential of •OH | **2.80 V** | Most practical aqueous oxidant |
| Redox potential of O₃ | **2.07 V** | Strong but selective |
| O₃ half-life in water | **minutes to hours** | pH-dependent |
| O₃ solubility in water at 20 °C | **12.07 mg/L** | Low → requires fine bubble diffusion |
| •OH radical half-life | **~10⁻⁹ s** | Extremely short-lived |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AOPs are considered "secondary AOPs" when applied after biological treatment for polishing effluents containing trace organics. They are critical for **tertiary treatment** of pharmaceutical, textile, and petrochemical wastewater.

---

## 1.2 Non-Ozone-Based Processes

### Formal Definition
**Non-ozone-based AOPs** are Advanced Oxidation Processes that do not use ozone, but instead rely on combinations of **H₂O₂, Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ (Fenton's reagent), UV light, TiO₂ photocatalysts, or elevated temperature/pressure** to generate hydroxyl radicals for organic pollutant degradation.

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Cooking" of Pollutants)
Think of non-ozone AOPs as different ways of "cooking" stubborn pollutants until they disintegrate:
- **Fenton (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂)** → "Stove-top cooking" at room temperature, iron acts as the catalyst chef.
- **UV/H₂O₂** → "Microwave cooking" — UV photons split H₂O₂ like a microwave splits water molecules.
- **Photo-Fenton (UV + Fe + H₂O₂)** → "Pressure-cooking" — combines heat (UV) and iron chef for faster results.
- **TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis** → "Solar cooking" — semiconductor surface captures sunlight to drive reactions.

### The Main Non-Ozone AOP Configurations
1. **Fenton Process (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂)** — Dark reaction, acidic pH (2.5–3.5).
2. **Photo-Fenton (UV-Vis/Fe²⁺/H₂O₂)** — UV enhances Fe³⁺ reduction to Fe²⁺.
3. **UV/H₂O₂** — Photolysis of hydrogen peroxide.
4. **TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis** — Heterogeneous semiconductor photocatalysis.
5. **Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)** — High T and P with dissolved O₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synergy principle:** AOPs are typically paired with biological treatment — AOP breaks the recalcitrant molecules into smaller biodegradable fragments, which the downstream biological step mineralizes into CO₂ + H₂O.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ozone Chemistry — The Foundation

### 2.1.1 Ozone Generation
Ozone is industrially produced by the **corona discharge method** (also called "silent electric discharge"). Dry air or pure O₂ is passed through a high-voltage discharge gap (5–25 kV), splitting O₂ molecules:

$$O_2 \xrightarrow{e^- + \text{electric field}} 2O^{\bullet}$$

$$O^{\bullet} + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

where **M** is a third-body collision partner (N₂ or O₂) that absorbs excess energy. Typical yield = **1–3% O₃ by weight in air**, **3–6% in pure O₂**.

### 2.1.2 Ozone Decomposition in Water (Initiator of •OH Formation)

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
$$O_3 + OH^- \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -}$$

**Step 2 — Radical chain propagation:**
$$O_3 + O_2^{\bullet -} \rightarrow O_3^{\bullet -} + O_2$$

$$O_3^{\bullet -} + H^+ \rightarrow HO_3^{\bullet}$$

$$HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + O_2$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**
$$OH^{\bullet} + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow H_2O_2$$

$$O_3 + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **pH > 8**, ozone decomposition to •OH accelerates dramatically — this is why alkaline ozonation is preferred for AOP applications.

### 2.1.3 Direct vs. Indirect Oxidation
- **Direct pathway:** Molecular O₃ attacks pollutants (selective, slower, pH-independent). Examples: amines, sulfides, phenols.
- **Indirect pathway:** •OH radicals attack pollutants (non-selective, very fast, pH-dependent). The •OH reacts with virtually any organic compound at near-diffusion-controlled rates (k ≈ 10⁸–10¹⁰ M⁻¹ s⁻¹).

---

## 2.2 O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone Process)

Hydrogen peroxide accelerates the decomposition of O₃ into •OH:

$$O_3 + H_2O_2 \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2$$

$$O_3 + HO_2^- \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -} + O_2$$

**Operational Advantages:**
- Enhances •OH yield **2–5×** vs. O₃ alone.
- Works effectively at pH 7–9 (no need for extreme alkalinity).
- Typical H₂O₂:O₃ molar ratio = **0.2–0.5**.
- Widely used for **TOC reduction** and **micropollutant destruction** in drinking water.

---

## 2.3 O₃/UV Process

UV photolysis of ozone generates H₂O₂ in situ, which then produces more •OH:

$$O_3 + H_2O \xrightarrow{h\nu} H_2O_2 + O_2$$

$$H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2OH^{\bullet}$$

**Key requirements:**
- Low-pressure mercury lamps (254 nm) or medium-pressure (200–300 nm).
- O₃ dose = **1–5 mg/L** for potable water.
- High capital cost → used mainly for trace contaminant polishing.

---

## 2.4 Fenton Process (Non-Ozone AOP)

### Core Fenton Reaction
$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^- + OH^{\bullet}$$

This is the famous **Haber's reaction** (Fenton's reagent, 1894). The reaction proceeds at optimum **pH = 2.5–3.5**.

### Catalytic Regeneration
The slow step in Fenton chemistry is Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ reduction. This limits continuous •OH production:

$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HO_2^{\bullet} + H^+$$

$$Fe^{2+} + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^- \text{ (termination)}$$

### Practical Recipe (the "Fenton Cake")
- [H₂O₂] : [Fe²⁺] molar ratio ≈ **5–25 : 1**
- Typical dose: **50–200 mg/L H₂O₂** + **5–20 mg/L Fe²⁺**
- Temperature: ambient (no heating needed)

---

## 2.5 Photo-Fenton Process

UV-Vis irradiation (λ < 580 nm) accelerates Fe³⁺ reduction:

$$Fe(OH)^{2+} \xrightarrow{h\nu} Fe^{2+} + OH^{\bullet}$$

Solar photo-Fenton uses sunlight, reducing electrical cost significantly.

---

## 2.6 UV/H₂O₂ Process

$$H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu, \, \lambda < 254 \, nm} 2OH^{\bullet}$$

Quantum yield Φ ≈ 0.5 mol/Einstein at 254 nm. Commercial LP Hg lamps emit at 254 nm, making this process very efficient.

---

## 2.7 TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis (Heterogeneous AOP)

$$TiO_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu, \, \lambda < 388 \, nm} e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$

$$h^+_{VB} + H_2O \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + H^+$$

$$O_2 + e^-_{CB} \rightarrow O_2^{\bullet -}$$

Used with **anatase TiO₂** (bandgap 3.2 eV). Suspended or immobilized (P25, Aeroxide) configurations exist.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize these for KTU ESE problems.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pseudo-first-order pollutant decay | $\ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k' t$ | k′ in min⁻¹ |
| Second-order •OH rate | $-\dfrac{d[C]}{dt} = k_{\bullet OH}[C][\bullet OH]$ | k ≈ 10⁸–10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹ |
| Ozone decay in water | $\dfrac{d[O_3]}{dt} = -k_d [O_3]^n$ | n = 1 (acidic), 2 (alkaline) |
| CT disinfection concept | $C^{1.19} T$ (for O₃) | C in mg/L, T in min |
| Fenton H₂O₂ dose | $[H_2O_2] = 2.25 \times [COD] - 0.625$ (Stoichiometric) | Empirical, in mg/L |
| Photo-Fenton photonic efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{moles pollutant degraded}}{\text{moles photons absorbed}} \times 100$ | % |
| TiO₂ bandgap energy | $E_g = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{max}}$ | λ in nm, Eg in eV |
| Peroxone H₂O₂ : O₃ ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{[H_2O_2]}{[O_3]}_{molar}$ | Typical: 0.2 – 0.5 |
| OH⁻ initiated ozone decomposition | $R_{OH} = 2 \cdot k_1 [O_3][OH^-]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| Fenton optimum pH | $pH_{opt} = 2.8 - 3.2$ | Dimensionless |
| Fenton Fe²⁺ recycle rate | $r_{Fe^{2+}} = k_{red}[Fe^{3+}][H_2O_2]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| Ozone Henry's constant (25 °C) | $H_{O_3} = 0.082 \text{ M atm}^{-1}$ | (low solubility) |
| Electrical efficiency of ozone gen. | $\eta_{O_3} = \dfrac{[O_3]_{produced}}{P \cdot t}$ | g/kWh, typical: 10–25 |
| Photo-Fenton quantum yield | $\Phi_{OH} = 0.5 - 1.0$ at λ = 254 nm | mol/Einstein |

> **Note on units:** For absolute values in derivations, use $\vert x \vert$ notation (Latex) — never the `|` symbol directly in markdown tables.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| O₃ alone | Drinking water, color removal in textile effluent |
| O₃/H₂O₂ | Pharmaceutical WW, pesticide degradation |
| O₃/UV | Trace organics polishing, cyanide destruction |
| Fenton | Textile, olive mill, pulp & paper WW (high COD) |
| Photo-Fenton | Solar detoxification plants (Spain: Plataforma Solar de Almería) |
| UV/H₂O₂ | TOC polishing, N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) removal |
| TiO₂/UV | Air purification, self-cleaning surfaces, emerging contaminants |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydroxyl Radical Steady-State Concentration

The pollutant destruction rate in an AOP is governed by the steady-state concentration of •OH radicals. Let us derive it.

### Step 1 — General Rate Equation
For a target pollutant P, attacked by •OH:

$$-\frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{P,\bullet OH} [P] [\bullet OH]_{ss}$$

where k_{P,•OH} is the second-order rate constant (M⁻¹ s⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Pseudo-First-Order Assumption
In a well-buffered AOP reactor, [•OH] reaches a quasi-steady state rapidly. Treating [•OH] as constant:

$$-\frac{d[P]}{dt} = k'_{apparent} [P]$$

where the **apparent pseudo-first-order rate constant** is:

$$k'_{apparent} = k_{P,\bullet OH} \cdot [\bullet OH]_{ss}$$

### Step 3 — Integrated Form (Batch Reactor)

$$\int_{[P]_0}^{[P]} \frac{d[P]}{[P]} = -k' \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{[P]}{[P]_0}\right) = -k' t$$

### Step 4 — Solving for Half-Life
Setting [P] = [P]₀/2:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k' \, t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k'} = \frac{0.693}{k'_{apparent}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 90% destruction (1 log removal) requires $t = 2.303/k'$. A 99% removal (2 log) requires $t = 4.606/k'$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State [•OH] from Ozone Decomposition

In alkaline ozonation, the key initiation step is:

$$O_3 + OH^- \xrightarrow{k_1, \, k_1 = 70 \, M^{-1} s^{-1}} HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -}$$

Subsequent radical chain reactions produce •OH at a yield α ≈ 0.5 mol •OH per mol O₃ decomposed. At steady state, the •OH formation rate equals its consumption rate:

$$R_{f,OH} = R_{c,OH}$$

$$\alpha \cdot k_1 [O_3][OH^-] = k_s [\bullet OH]_{ss} [S]_{tot}$$

where [S]_tot = total scavenger concentration (carbonates, NOM, etc.), and k_s is the global scavenging rate constant (≈ 5 × 10⁸ M⁻¹ s⁻¹).

Solving for [•OH]_ss:

$$[\bullet OH]_{ss} = \frac{\alpha \cdot k_1 [O_3][OH^-]}{k_s [S]_{tot}}$$

### Step 5 — Substituting Numerical Values
Take [O₃] = 2 mg/L = 4.17 × 10⁻⁵ M, [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁴ M (pH 10), [S]_tot = 10⁻³ M, α = 0.5:

$$[\bullet OH]_{ss} = \frac{0.5 \times 70 \times (4.17 \times 10^{-5}) \times (10^{-4})}{(5 \times 10^8)(10^{-3})}$$

$$[\bullet OH]_{ss} = \frac{0.5 \times 70 \times 4.17 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$[\bullet OH]_{ss} = \frac{1.46 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{5}} = 2.92 \times 10^{-13} \, M$$

This extremely low concentration (~pM) is enough to drive the degradation due to the extraordinarily high rate constants of •OH reactions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric H₂O₂ Dose in Fenton Process

The theoretical H₂O₂ requirement for complete mineralization of a generic organic compound C_nH_mO_pN_q is derived from the electron-balance principle.

### Step 6 — Half-Reactions
**Oxidation (organic carbon → CO₂):** Each carbon atom requires 2 electrons to go from oxidation state 0 to +4 (in CO₂):

$$C \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^- \quad \text{(per mole of C)}$$

Wait — this is incorrect framing. The correct approach is:

For organic compound **C_a H_b O_c N_d** (C in 0 state), complete combustion requires:

$$C_aH_bO_cN_d + (2a + b/2 - c)O_2 \rightarrow aCO_2 + (b/2)H_2O + (d/2)N_2$$

In Fenton's chemistry, the •OH radical is the oxidant. The electron balance per •OH is **1 electron**. Therefore:

$$H_2O_2 \rightarrow O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \text{ (per mole of H}_2\text{O}_2)$$

**Stoichiometric ratio:**
$$\frac{[H_2O_2]}{[COD]} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \, \text{(mass ratio, since O}_2\text{ = 32, H}_2\text{O}_2\text{ = 34)} \rightarrow \frac{34}{32 \times 8} = 0.1325$$

The **practical dose** is **1.5–2.5× stoichiometric** due to side reactions (H₂O₂ decomposition, scavenging, incomplete mineralization).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Photon Flux in UV/H₂O₂ Reactor

The local volumetric rate of photon absorption (LVRPA) follows the Beer-Lambert law:

$$E(x) = E_0 \cdot 10^{-A x}$$

where A = solution absorbance (cm⁻¹), E₀ = incident fluence rate (mW/cm²).

For a collimated beam reactor with depth L:

$$\text{LVRPA} = \int_0^L E(x) \mu_a \, dx$$

For a thin layer approximation:

$$\text{LVRPA} \approx E_0 \mu_a$$

where μ_a = absorption coefficient of H₂O₂ at 254 nm = 19.6 M⁻¹ cm⁻¹.

### Step 7 — Photolysis Rate of H₂O₂
$$R_{H_2O_2} = \Phi_{H_2O_2} \cdot \text{LVRPA} = 0.5 \cdot E_0 \cdot \mu_a [H_2O_2]$$

And •OH generation rate:
$$R_{OH^{\bullet}} = 2 \cdot R_{H_2O_2} = 2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot E_0 \cdot \mu_a [H_2O_2]$$

$$R_{OH^{\bullet}} = \Phi \cdot E_0 \cdot \mu_a [H_2O_2]$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix (Analytical)

| Parameter | O₃ | O₃/H₂O₂ | O₃/UV | Fenton | Photo-Fenton | UV/H₂O₂ | TiO₂/UV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optimum pH | 7–9 | 7–8.5 | 7–8 | 2.5–3.5 | 2.5–3.5 | 6–8 | 5–7 |
| •OH yield | Low-Med | High | High | Med | High | High | Med |
| Operating cost | Med | Med | High | Low | Med | Med-High | High |
| Sludge production | None | None | None | High (Fe sludge) | High | None | None (TiO₂ recyclable) |
| Capital cost | Med | Med | High | Low | Med | High | High |
| Solar feasible? | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | Partial | **Yes** |
| Best for | Color, phenols | Micropollutants | Trace organics | High COD | Refractory + bio combo | TOC polishing | Emerging contaminants |
| Scale of deployment | Large | Med-Large | Pilot | Full | Pilot-Full | Large | Pilot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for AOPs (KTU):** **"Our Ozone Operates Uniquely Fast, Generating Hydroxyls"** → **O-OO-UV-Fenton-GH** (Oral/Ozonation, O₃/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, Fenton, Generate Hydroxyl).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ozone Generation & Injection System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air/Oxygen Feed] --> B[Compressor and Dryer]
    B --> C[Corona Discharge Ozone Generator]
    C --> D[Ozone Gas Outlet]
    D --> E[Dissolution Chamber with Venturi Injector]
    E --> F[Contact Tank Reactor]
    F --> G[Off-gas Ozone Destructor]
    G --> H[Thermal or UV Destruction Unit]
    F --> I[Treated Effluent to Polishing]
    C -.High voltage AC 5-25 kV.-> C
    E -.Fine bubble diffuser.-> E
```

## 4.2 Peroxone (O₃/H₂O₂) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Pre-ozonation Contact Tank]
    C[Ozone Generator] --> B
    D[Hydrogen Peroxide Storage Tank] --> E[Peroxide Dosing Pump]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Reaction Chamber 1: Direct Oxidation]
    F --> G[Reaction Chamber 2: OH Radical Zone]
    G --> H[Quench Tank: Residual H2O2 Removal]
    H --> I[Biological Post-Treatment]
    I --> J[Effluent Discharge]
    B -.pH 7-9.-> B
    G -.H2O2:O3 = 0.3.-> G
```

## 4.3 Fenton Process Reactor Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wastewater] --> B[Equalization and pH Adjustment Tank pH 3]
    B --> C[Fenton Reactor FeSO4 and H2O2 Addition]
    C --> D[Coagulation-Flocculation Zone]
    D --> E[Lamella Clarifier]
    E --> F[Iron Sludge to Dewatering]
    E --> G[Clarified Effluent to Neutralization Tank]
    G --> H[pH Adjustment to 7 with NaOH]
    H --> I[Sand Filter and Activated Carbon Polishing]
    I --> J[Final Effluent]
    C -.Fe2+ 5-20 mg/L.-> C
    C -.H2O2 50-200 mg/L.-> C
```

## 4.4 Photo-Fenton Solar Reactor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wastewater Inlet] --> B[Acidification pH 2.8]
    B --> C[Fe and H2O2 Mixing Tank]
    C --> D[Compound Parabolic Collector CPC Reactor]
    D -.Solar UV-Vis.-> D
    D --> E[Settling Tank]
    E --> F[Iron Recovery via Ion Exchange]
    F --> G[Neutralization Basin]
    G --> H[Biological Polishing]
    H --> I[Discharge]
    D -.Residence time 30-90 min.-> D
```

## 4.5 UV/H₂O₂ Reactor Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wastewater Inlet] --> B[Peristaltic H2O2 Dosing Pump]
    B --> C[Static Mixer for H2O2 Distribution]
    C --> D[UV Reactor Chamber]
    D --> E[LP Mercury Lamp Array 254 nm]
    E --> F[Quartz Sleeve Housing]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Reactor Outlet to Sampling]
    G --> H[Biological Post-Treatment]
    D -.Lamp intensity 30 mW/cm2.-> D
    D -.H2O2 5-20 mg/L.-> D
```

## 4.6 Master Comparison Architecture: All AOPs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Advanced Oxidation Processes AWT]
    Root --> Obase[Ozone Based AOPs]
    Root --> Nbase[Non-Ozone Based AOPs]
    Obase --> O1[O3 Alone]
    Obase --> O2[O3 plus H2O2 Peroxone]
    Obase --> O3[O3 plus UV Photo-ozonation]
    Nbase --> N1[Fenton Fe2 plus H2O2]
    Nbase --> N2[Photo-Fenton UV-Vis Fe H2O2]
    Nbase --> N3[UV plus H2O2]
    Nbase --> N4[TiO2 plus UV Photocatalysis]
    O1 --> Radicals[Generate OH radicals and Direct O3 attack]
    O2 --> Radicals
    O3 --> Radicals
    N1 --> Radicals
    N2 --> Radicals
    N3 --> Radicals
    N4 --> Radicals
    Radicals --> Out[Mineralization to CO2 plus H2O]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question)]**
**What are Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)? Why is the hydroxyl radical (•OH) considered the most powerful oxidant in water treatment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** AOPs are defined as a set of chemical treatment procedures designed to remove organic (and sometimes inorganic) materials in water and wastewater by oxidation through reactions with hydroxyl radicals (•OH).
- **Mechanism (1 mark):** These processes generate •OH in situ via reactions involving ozone, hydrogen peroxide, UV light, Fenton's reagent, or photocatalysts.
- **Oxidation power (1 mark):** •OH has a standard reduction potential of **E° = 2.80 V** (vs. SHE), which is higher than O₃ (2.07 V), H₂O₂ (1.78 V), Cl₂ (1.36 V), and O₂ (1.23 V). It reacts non-selectively with nearly all organic compounds at near-diffusion-controlled rates (k ≈ 10⁸–10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹), leading to complete mineralization to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**
**Briefly explain the Peroxone process. Write the key reaction involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Peroxone process is a combination of ozone (O₃) and hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) used in advanced wastewater treatment to enhance the generation of hydroxyl radicals beyond that achieved by ozonation alone.
- **Key reaction (1 mark):**
$$O_3 + H_2O_2 \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2$$
- **Advantages (1 mark):** Higher •OH yield (2–5× over O₃ alone), works at near-neutral pH (7–8.5), no sludge formation, very effective for micropollutants and refractory organics. Typical H₂O₂:O₃ molar ratio = 0.2–0.5.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each module typically features one 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 (Model Question)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of relevant reactions, explain the Fenton process for advanced wastewater treatment. Discuss the role of pH, Fe²⁺/H₂O₂ ratio, and sludge generation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast Fenton and Photo-Fenton processes. Discuss the role of solar photo-Fenton in industrial wastewater treatment.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fenton Process:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The Fenton process uses a mixture of ferrous ions (Fe²⁺) and hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) in acidic conditions to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH), discovered by H.J.H. Fenton in 1894.

2. **Core reaction (1 mark):**
$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^- + OH^{\bullet}$$

3. **Mechanism and side reactions (2 marks):**
   - Initiation (above reaction generates •OH).
   - Propagation:
$$Fe^{2+} + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^- \quad (\text{scavenging, } k = 4.3 \times 10^8 \, M^{-1} s^{-1})$$
$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HO_2^{\bullet} + H^+ \quad (\text{Fenton-like, slow})$$
   - Termination:
$$OH^{\bullet} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + HO_2^{\bullet}$$
$$2 OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow H_2O_2$$

4. **Role of pH (1 mark):** Optimum pH is **2.5–3.5**.
   - Below pH 2.5: H₂O₂ becomes stable (peroxonium ion formation: H₃O₂⁺), reducing •OH yield.
   - Above pH 4: Fe³⁺ precipitates as Fe(OH)₃, losing catalytic activity and producing excessive sludge.

5. **Fe²⁺/H₂O₂ ratio and sludge (1 mark):** Optimal molar ratio = 1:5 to 1:25. Stoichiometric H₂O₂ dose for COD = (COD/8) × (34/32). Higher doses give better COD removal but excess Fe²⁺ scavenges •OH. Iron sludge (Fe(OH)₃) is a major drawback (~1–3 kg dry sludge/kg COD removed).

6. **Engineering utility (1 mark):** Widely used for textile dye, olive mill, pharmaceutical, and pulp-paper wastewater as a pre- or main-treatment step.

**Part (b) — Fenton vs. Photo-Fenton:**

| Feature | Fenton | Photo-Fenton |
|---|---|---|
| pH range | 2.5–3.5 | 2.5–4.0 |
| Light source | None (dark) | UV-Vis (λ < 580 nm) |
| Fe²⁺ regeneration | Slow (thermal) | **Fast** (photolytic) |
| Sludge | High | Reduced (~30–50% less) |
| Energy input | None | UV lamps or solar |
| Cost | Low (capital + chemical) | Higher (solar = lower operating cost) |
| Mineralization | 50–70% | 80–95% |
| Industrial adoption | Mature | Growing (solar variants) |

2. **Photo-Fenton enhancement (2 marks):** UV-Vis light (especially λ < 365 nm) reduces Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺ rapidly via:
$$Fe(OH)^{2+} \xrightarrow{h\nu} Fe^{2+} + OH^{\bullet}$$
This **regenerates the catalyst** and **produces additional •OH**, making the process catalytic and continuous. Additional photolysis of Fe³⁺-carboxylate complexes also mineralizes intermediates.

3. **Solar photo-Fenton (2 marks):** The **Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC)** reactor concentrates both direct and diffuse sunlight. Demonstration plants (Plataforma Solar de Almería, Spain) have shown:
   - Treatment of **500–2000 mg/L COD** pesticide effluents to **<100 mg/L**.
   - Residence time: **30–120 minutes**.
   - Operating cost: **€2–5 per m³** (vs. €10–20 for Fenton-only).
   - Suitable for sunny regions: Mediterranean, Middle East, South India.

4. **Limitations (1 mark):** Photo-Fenton requires transparent reactor walls (quartz, borosilicate glass), Fe recovery is needed to reduce sludge, and quantum yield drops at high Fe concentrations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the chemistry of ozone decomposition in water. Explain the direct and indirect oxidation pathways with suitable reactions.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone) process. List four advantages of the process over ozonation alone.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Ozone Decomposition Chemistry:**

1. **Ozone properties (1 mark):** O₃ is an allotrope of oxygen, with a bent molecular structure and resonance forms. It is a powerful electrophilic oxidant (E° = 2.07 V).

2. **Initiation step (1 mark):** In water, O₃ reacts with hydroxide ion (the dominant initiation pathway above pH 8):
$$O_3 + OH^- \xrightarrow{k_1 = 70 \, M^{-1} s^{-1}} HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -}$$
This is the rate-determining step of the chain decomposition.

3. **Chain propagation (2 marks):**
$$O_3 + O_2^{\bullet -} \rightarrow O_3^{\bullet -} + O_2$$
$$O_3^{\bullet -} + H^+ \rightleftharpoons HO_3^{\bullet} \quad (pK_a = 6.2)$$
$$HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + O_2$$
At low pH, the superoxide radical O₂•⁻ is the dominant chain carrier, and the decomposition slows dramatically.

4. **Direct oxidation (1 mark):** Molecular O₃ attacks electron-rich functional groups selectively:
   - **Ozonolysis of double bonds (C=C):** Criegee mechanism → aldehydes, ketones, hydrogen peroxide.
   - **Aromatic rings with electron-donating groups (–OH, –NH₂):** Ring opening to muconic acid derivatives.
   - **Sulfides, amines, phenols:** Direct O₃ attack is fast.

5. **Indirect oxidation (1 mark):** The •OH radical generated during decomposition attacks pollutants non-selectively:
$$RH + OH^{\bullet} \rightarrow R^{\bullet} + H_2O \quad (k \approx 10^8 - 10^{10} \, M^{-1} s^{-1})$$
$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{peroxy intermediates} \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$$
The hydroxyl radical pathway is **non-selective** and **fast**, ideal for refractory organics.

6. **pH dependence summary (1 mark):**
   - **pH < 4:** Direct O₃ attack dominates (slow, selective).
   - **pH 7–9:** Mixed regime.
   - **pH > 9:** •OH dominates (fast, non-selective). Carbonate/bicarbonate scavenges •OH, reducing efficiency.

**Part (b) — Peroxone Process:**

1. **Process description (2 marks):**
   - The Peroxone process (US Patent 5,200,092, Glaze and Kang, 1989) combines O₃ and H₂O₂ simultaneously injected into wastewater.
   - Typical doses: O₃ = 1–10 mg/L, H₂O₂ = 0.2–0.5 × O₃ (molar).
   - Reactor: contact tank with fine-bubble diffuser at the bottom, 10–30 min contact time.

2. **Key reactions (1 mark):**
$$O_3 + H_2O_2 \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2$$
$$O_3 + HO_2^- \rightarrow OH^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -} + O_2$$
The hydroperoxide anion HO₂⁻ (conjugate base of H₂O₂, pKₐ = 11.6) is the active initiator — even at pH 7–8, a small fraction ionizes and drives •OH formation.

3. **Flow diagram (text representation, 2 marks):**
```
   O₃ Generator ──→ Venturi Injector ──┐
                                        ├─→ Contact Tank → Off-gas destructor
   H₂O₂ Storage → Dosing Pump ─────────┘
                                          ↓
                                  Biological post-treatment
```

4. **Four advantages over O₃ alone (2 marks):**
   - **Higher •OH yield:** 2–5× more hydroxyl radicals per unit O₃.
   - **Lower O₃ dose:** 20–40% reduction in O₃ consumption for same pollutant removal.
   - **Effective at near-neutral pH (7–8.5):** No need for alkaline pH adjustment.
   - **Reduced bromate formation:** BrO₃⁻ (a regulated DBPs) is suppressed because direct O₃ attack on Br⁻ is reduced.
   - **Better TOC and COD removal** for refractory organics like pharmaceuticals and pesticides.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write **mg/L**, **min⁻¹**, or **M⁻¹s⁻¹** explicitly. Marks are deducted for unitless rate constants.
> 2. **Confusing Fenton with Photo-Fenton:** Students often write the same reaction for both. **Photo-Fenton** *must* mention **UV/visible light** and **Fe³⁺ photoreduction**.
> 3. **Skipping pH:** Every AOP has an optimum pH. Always state it: Fenton = 2.5–3.5; Ozonation = 7–9 (for •OH); TiO₂/UV = 5–7.
> 4. **Not writing the *form* of reaction:** For Fenton, you must write Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH, not just "Fenton reagent oxidizes pollutants."
> 5. **Missing the radical:** Always end any AOP discussion with the **•OH radical** as the active species.
> 6. **Comparing only two processes:** When asked "compare," examiners expect at least 3–4 comparison points (mechanism, pH, cost, sludge, application).
> 7. **Neglecting scavenging:** At pH > 9, carbonate/bicarbonate scavenge •OH — this is frequently tested.
> 8. **Stoichiometry errors:** H₂O₂ dose for COD removal follows the empirical relation $[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2] \approx 2.25 \times \text{COD} - 0.625$ (mg/L), not arbitrary values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### **A. Core Concepts**
- [x] **AOPs** generate **•OH radicals** for non-selective oxidation of refractory organics.
- [x] •OH has **E° = 2.80 V** — the strongest practical aqueous oxidant.
- [x] AOPs are typically coupled with **biological post-treatment** for complete mineralization.
- [x] **Alkaline pH** promotes O₃ → •OH conversion.
- [x] **Acidic pH** (2.5–3.5) is mandatory for the **Fenton** process.

### **B. Ozone-Based Processes**
- [x] **O₃ alone:** Direct + indirect pathway. Direct attack is selective; •OH is non-selective.
- [x] **O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone):** H₂O₂ : O₃ molar ratio = 0.2–0.5. Most widely used industrial ozone-AOP.
- [x] **O₃/UV:** UV photolysis of O₃ → H₂O₂ → 2•OH. High capital cost.
- [x] **Initiation reaction:** $O_3 + OH^- \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_2^{\bullet -}$, $k_1 = 70$ M⁻¹s⁻¹.
- [x] **Chain propagation** cycles through $O_3^{\bullet -}$, $HO_3^{\bullet}$, and $O_2^{\bullet -}$.

### **C. Non-Ozone Processes**
- [x] **Fenton (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂):** Acidic pH, produces iron sludge, no external energy.
- [x] **Photo-Fenton:** UV-Vis accelerates Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ regeneration; **solar-driven** variant is the future.
- [x] **UV/H₂O₂:** Quantum yield Φ = 0.5 at 254 nm; clean, no sludge.
- [x] **TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis:** Anatase TiO₂, bandgap = 3.2 eV, λ < 388 nm; produces $e^-_{CB}$ and $h^+_{VB}$.
- [x] **Wet Air Oxidation (WAO):** High T (125–320 °C) and P (0.5–20 MPa); for very high-strength industrial effluents.

### **D. High-Yield Numbers to Memorize**
- [x] •OH reduction potential: **2.80 V**
- [x] O₃ reduction potential: **2.07 V**
- [x] O₃ half-life: **minutes to hours** (pH-dependent)
- [x] Fenton optimum pH: **2.5–3.5**
- [x] Fenton Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ molar ratio: **1 : 5 to 1 : 25**
- [x] H₂O₂ dose for COD: $[H_2O_2] = 2.25 \times COD - 0.625$ mg/L
- [x] TiO₂ bandgap: **3.2 eV** (anatase)
- [x] UV/H₂O₂ quantum yield (254 nm): **0.5 mol/Einstein**
- [x] O₃ Henry's constant (25 °C): **0.082 M·atm⁻¹**
- [x] Pseudo-first-order integrated law: $\ln(C/C_0) = -k't$

### **E. Industrial Applications (Recall Map)**
- [x] **Textile →** Fenton (color), Photo-Fenton (COD).
- [x] **Pharma →** O₃/H₂O₂, UV/H₂O₂ (micropollutants).
- [x] **Pulp & paper →** Fenton, Wet Air Oxidation.
- [x] **Drinking water →** O₃ alone, O₃/H₂O₂.
- [x] **Olive mill →** Photo-Fenton, Fenton.
- [x] **Pesticide →** TiO₂/UV, Photo-Fenton.
- [x] **Landfill leachate →** O₃/H₂O₂ + biological.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark answers, always structure with **definitions → reactions (with conditions: pH, dose) → mechanism → comparison table → applications → limitations.** Examiners reward visual organization and balanced chemical equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fenton and Photo-Fenton Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fenton and Photo-Fenton Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fenton Oxidation** is an Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP) in which hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) is catalytically decomposed by ferrous ions (Fe²⁺) in an acidic aqueous medium (optimum pH ≈ 2.5–3.5) to generate highly reactive, non-selective **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**, which possess a standard redox potential of **E° = +2.80 V** (vs NHE), making them the second strongest oxidant after fluorine.

The classical Fenton reaction is represented stoichiometrically as:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \quad (k \approx 63\text{–}76 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1})$$

**Photo-Fenton Oxidation** is the photo-assisted enhancement of the Fenton process in which the ferric ion (Fe³⁺) is photochemically reduced back to Fe²⁺ under UV/visible irradiation, simultaneously regenerating the catalyst and producing additional hydroxyl radicals, thereby accelerating the destruction of refractory organic pollutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fenton and Photo-Fenton processes are classified under **Homogeneous Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** and form a core part of Module 4 (Advanced Oxidation Processes). They are specifically tested for understanding of (a) hydroxyl radical chemistry, (b) pH-dependent speciation of iron, and (c) photo-enhancement mechanisms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Fenton reactor** as a *fire-breathing dragon* used to burn down stubborn, hard-to-decompose molecules:
- **The Dragon (•OH radical)** is the fire-breather.
- **The Spark (H₂O₂)** is the fuel.
- **The Lighter (Fe²⁺ catalyst)** is what ignites the fuel.
- **The Sunlight (UV photons)** in Photo-Fenton acts like a *recharger* that constantly reignites the lighter, making the dragon breathe fire continuously.

In conventional wastewater treatment, persistent organic pollutants (POPs) like pesticides, phenols, and dyes are like *fire-resistant steel* — they cannot be burned by ordinary flames (O₂, Cl₂, O₃). Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are the *plasma torch* that can slice through anything organic. The Fenton process manufactures these torches on-site using cheap, environmentally benign ingredients (iron salts and hydrogen peroxide).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metric:** The hydroxyl radical reacts with organic compounds at rate constants ranging from **10⁷ to 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹**, which is **6–10 orders of magnitude faster** than ozone or chlorine. This is why Fenton-based AOPs are preferred for recalcitrant compounds.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Redox potential of •OH | **E° = +2.80 V (vs NHE)** |
| Lifetime of •OH radical | **~ 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ s** in water |
| Optimum pH | **2.5 – 3.5** |
| Optimum H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ molar ratio | **10 : 1 to 20 : 1** (typical industrial) |
| Optimum temperature | **25 – 40 °C** |
| Second-order rate constant (Fenton) | **63 – 76 M⁻¹s⁻¹** at 25 °C |
| Quantum yield of Photo-Fenton | **0.14 – 0.61** (UV-A range) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fenton Reaction Mechanism — Step-by-Step

The classical Fenton mechanism is propagated via a **free-radical chain reaction** comprising initiation, propagation, and termination steps. Henri J.H. Fenton first reported the reaction in **1894**, and the radical mechanism was definitively proposed by Haber and Weiss in **1934**.

#### 2.1.1 Initiation Step (Catalyst Activation)
The ferrous ion transfers an electron to H₂O₂, cleaving the O–O bond heterolytically and yielding the hydroxyl radical:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

This is the **rate-limiting, rate-determining step** of the entire process.

#### 2.1.2 Propagation Steps
The generated radicals participate in propagation sequences:

**Reaction of Fe³⁺ with H₂O₂ (Fenton-like reaction):**

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{H}^+$$

This regenerates Fe²⁺, sustaining the catalytic cycle. However, this step is **~ 30 times slower** than the Fenton initiation, often becoming the kinetic bottleneck.

**Radical scavenging by Fe²⁺ (unwanted side reaction):**

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^-$$

This consumes the very •OH we are trying to produce — one of the key reasons why **excess Fe²⁺ is detrimental**.

#### 2.1.3 Termination Steps
Chain termination occurs via radical recombination:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$${}^{\bullet}\text{OH} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$$

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{HO}_2^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the pH window is narrow (2.5–3.5):** At pH < 2.5, H₂O₂ is stabilized by solvated protons (H₃O₂⁺ formation), reducing radical yield. At pH > 3.5, Fe³⁺ precipitates as **ferric oxyhydroxide [Fe(OH)₃]**, removing the catalyst from solution. The optimum pH strikes a balance between these extremes.

### 2.2 Photo-Fenton Enhancement Mechanism

When UV-visible light (λ ≤ 580 nm) is introduced to the Fenton system, two photochemical processes synergistically accelerate the reaction:

**Step 1 — Photolysis of Fe³⁺-hydroxy complexes (dominant pathway):**

$$\text{[Fe(OH)]}^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

**Step 2 — Photolysis of H₂O₂:**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2 \; {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

**Step 3 — Photolysis of Fe³⁺-carboxylate complexes (e.g., with oxalate, citrate):**

$$2 \; \text{[Fe(C}_2\text{O}_4)]^{3-} + h\nu \rightarrow 2 \; \text{Fe}^{2+} + 3 \; \text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-} + 2 \; \text{CO}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Photo-Fenton Advantage:** The Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ cycle is accelerated up to **3–5 times** under solar irradiation. This dramatically reduces the catalyst loading required and extends the operational pH window to **pH 4–5**, since photoreduction prevents Fe³⁺ accumulation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** All mathematical symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | Application | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$ | Primary Fenton reaction (initiation) | — |
| 2 | $r = k \cdot [\text{Fe}^{2+}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]$ | Pseudo-first-order rate of •OH generation | M s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $k_{\text{Fenton}} = 63 \text{ to } 76 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ | Rate constant at 25 °C | M⁻¹s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $E^\circ({}^{\bullet}\text{OH}/\text{H}_2\text{O}) = +2.80 \; \text{V}$ | Oxidizing power of •OH | V (vs NHE) |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{degradation}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency at time t | % |
| 6 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$ | Pseudo-first-order kinetic model | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 : \text{Fe}^{2+} = 10 : 1 \text{ to } 20 : 1$ | Optimum molar feed ratio | mol/mol |
| 8 | $\text{pH}_{\text{opt}} = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5$ | Operating pH window | — |
| 9 | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{moles of }{}^{\bullet}\text{OH produced}}{\text{moles of photons absorbed}}$ | Quantum yield (Photo-Fenton) | mol Einstein⁻¹ |
| 10 | $[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_{\text{opt}} \approx 5 \text{ to } 50 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$ | Typical catalyst concentration range | mg L⁻¹ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Effluent Treatment:** Landfill leachate, textile dye baths, pharmaceutical residues, olive mill wastewater, and petroleum refinery effluents.
- **Soil & Groundwater Remediation:** *In-situ* chemical oxidation (ISCO) using Fenton reagents injected into contaminated aquifers.
- **Sludge Conditioning:** Pre-treatment of biological sludge to enhance dewaterability (Fenton-conditioned sludge has 30–40% lower capillary suction time).
- **Micropollutant Removal:** Pharmaceuticals (e.g., diclofenac, carbamazepine), endocrine disruptors, and pesticide traces — compounds resistant to conventional biological treatment.
- **Disinfection:** Inactivation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) in hospital wastewater.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pseudo-First-Order Kinetic Model for Fenton Degradation

When organic substrate **S** reacts with •OH at a rate much faster than •OH generation, the substrate becomes the limiting species. The degradation rate law is:

$$-\frac{dC_S}{dt} = k_{\text{OH},S} \cdot [{}^{\bullet}\text{OH}] \cdot C_S$$

Since the steady-state concentration of •OH is constant during Fenton operation, we can define an apparent rate constant:

$$k_{\text{app}} = k_{\text{OH},S} \cdot [{}^{\bullet}\text{OH}]_{\text{ss}}$$

This reduces the rate law to a pseudo-first-order form:

$$-\frac{dC_S}{dt} = k_{\text{app}} \cdot C_S$$

**Step 1 — Separation of variables:**

$$-\frac{dC_S}{C_S} = k_{\text{app}} \cdot dt$$

**Step 2 — Integration from $t=0$ (where $C_S = C_0$) to $t=t$ (where $C_S = C_t$):**

$$-\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC_S}{C_S} = k_{\text{app}} \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluating the integrals:**

$$-[\ln C_S]_{C_0}^{C_t} = k_{\text{app}} \cdot t$$

$$\ln C_0 - \ln C_t = k_{\text{app}} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k_{\text{app}} \cdot t$$

Or equivalently:

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{app}} \cdot t}$$

This is the **most commonly cited KTU exam formula** for Fenton kinetics. The half-life of degradation is:

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{app}}} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_{\text{app}}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical wastewater contains **200 mg L⁻¹** of paracetamol. A Fenton oxidation experiment at pH 3.0 and 30 °C yields an apparent first-order rate constant of **$k_{\text{app}} = 0.085 \; \text{min}^{-1}$**. Calculate:

**(i)** The paracetamol concentration after 30 minutes.

**(ii)** The time required for 95% degradation.

**(iii)** The reaction rate at $t = 0$.

#### Solution:

**Part (i) — Concentration at t = 30 min:**

Using $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{app}} \cdot t}$:

$$C_t = 200 \times e^{-0.085 \times 30}$$

$$C_t = 200 \times e^{-2.55}$$

$$C_t = 200 \times 0.0781$$

$$\boxed{C_t \approx 15.62 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}}$$

This corresponds to a removal efficiency of:

$$\eta = \dfrac{200 - 15.62}{200} \times 100 = 92.2\%$$

**Part (ii) — Time for 95% degradation:**

For 95% removal, $C_t = 0.05 \cdot C_0$, so $C_t / C_0 = 0.05$:

$$t = -\dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{k_{\text{app}}} = -\dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{0.085}$$

$$t = -\dfrac{-2.996}{0.085}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 35.25 \; \text{min}}$$

**Part (iii) — Initial reaction rate:**

At $t = 0$, $C_S = C_0 = 200 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$:

$$r_0 = k_{\text{app}} \cdot C_0 = 0.085 \times 200$$

$$\boxed{r_0 = 17 \; \text{mg L}^{-1} \text{min}^{-1}}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Fenton vs. Photo-Fenton

| Parameter | Conventional Fenton | Photo-Fenton (UV/Solar) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Radical generation rate | Moderate | **3–5× higher** |
| Catalyst (Fe²⁺) required | 10–50 mg L⁻¹ | **2–10 mg L⁻¹** |
| Optimum pH | 2.5–3.5 (strict) | **3.0–5.0** (relaxed) |
| Sludge (Fe(OH)₃) generation | High | **Reduced by 40–60%** |
| Energy requirement | None | UV lamp electricity or solar |
| Operating cost | Low (chemicals) | Moderate (lamp + chemicals) |
| Mineralization efficiency | 60–80% TOC removal | **> 95% TOC removal** |
| Reaction time | 60–120 min | **15–45 min** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Photo-Fenton Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Equalization Tank]
    B --> C[Acidification Unit - pH 2.5 to 3.5]
    C --> D[Fe2+ Dosing Skid - FeSO4 Solution]
    D --> E[Fenton Reactor CSTR]
    F[H2O2 Storage and Dosing System] --> E
    E --> G[Photochemical Reactor UV-A or Solar]
    G --> H[Neutralization Basin - Lime or NaOH]
    H --> I[Sludge Settler Lamella Clarifier]
    I --> J[Sand Filtration Polishing]
    J --> K[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    I --> L[Fe(OH)3 Sludge - Dewatering]
    L --> M[Filter Press]
    M --> N[Cake Disposal]
    H --> O[pH Adjustment to 6.5 to 7.5]
    O --> J
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style G fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000

    style N fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```
