## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Fenton's reagent. What are the active species generated in the Fenton process and what is its standard redox potential?

**Model Answer:**
Fenton's reagent is a mixture of **ferrous sulphate (FeSO₄·7H₂O)** and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** in an acidic aqueous medium (pH 2.5–3.5). The active species generated is the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)**, with a standard redox potential of **E° = +2.80 V (vs NHE)**, making it the second strongest oxidizing agent after fluorine. Hydroxyl radicals non-selectively attack and mineralize refractory organic pollutants into CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic salts.

*(2 additional supporting points: H₂O₂/Fe²⁺ molar ratio 10:1–20:1; •OH reacts at 10⁷–10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Why is the Fenton process restricted to an acidic pH range? Explain with respect to iron speciation.

**Model Answer:**
The Fenton process is restricted to pH 2.5–3.5 because the catalytic activity of iron is highly pH-dependent:
- At **pH < 2.5**: Excess H⁺ ions stabilize H₂O₂ by forming H₃O₂⁺, which suppresses •OH radical generation.
- At **pH > 3.5**: Fe³⁺ (the dominant iron species) precipitates as **ferric oxyhydroxide [Fe(OH)₃]**, removing the catalyst from the aqueous phase. This is the most critical limitation.
- At **pH > 7**: Both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ precipitate as hydroxides, completely halting the reaction.

The acidic pH ensures that iron remains soluble in its catalytically active Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ ionic form, allowing continuous regeneration of the catalyst.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the reaction mechanism of the Fenton process. Discuss the role of each species and the rate-limiting step.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A textile dye effluent contains **500 mg L⁻¹** of an azo dye (Reactive Black 5). In a Photo-Fenton pilot study with $[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_0 = 20 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$, $[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]_0 = 500 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$ at pH 3.0 and 30 °C, the apparent rate constant was found to be **$k_{\text{app}} = 0.12 \; \text{min}^{-1}$**. Calculate:
- (i) The concentration of dye after 20 minutes.
- (ii) The percentage removal efficiency at $t = 30$ min.
- (iii) The half-life of degradation.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Fenton mechanism follows a free-radical chain pathway:

**Step 1 — Initiation (rate-determining):**
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \quad (k_1 = 63\text{–}76 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1})$$
Fe²⁺ donates an electron to H₂O₂, cleaving the O–O bond and generating the hydroxyl radical.

**Step 2 — Fenton-like regeneration (slow):**
$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{H}^+ \quad (k_2 \approx 0.001\text{–}0.01 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1})$$
This regenerates Fe²⁺, but is **~30× slower** than the initiation step, making it the kinetic bottleneck.

**Step 3 — Radical scavenging (wasteful):**
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- \quad (k_3 = 3.2 \times 10^8 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1})$$
Excess Fe²⁺ consumes •OH — this is why catalyst overdose is counterproductive.

**Step 4 — Termination:**
$${}^{\bullet}\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$${}^{\bullet}\text{OH} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$$

The **rate-limiting step** is Step 1 (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂) because:
- It has the smallest rate constant of the productive steps.
- All subsequent •OH generation depends on it.
- It determines the steady-state [•OH] concentration.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $C_0 = 500 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$, $k_{\text{app}} = 0.12 \; \text{min}^{-1}$

**(i) Concentration at t = 20 min:**

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{app}} \cdot t}$$

$$C_{20} = 500 \times e^{-0.12 \times 20} = 500 \times e^{-2.4}$$

$$C_{20} = 500 \times 0.0907$$

$$\boxed{C_{20} \approx 45.36 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}}$$

**(ii) Percentage removal at t = 30 min:**

$$C_{30} = 500 \times e^{-0.12 \times 30} = 500 \times e^{-3.6} = 500 \times 0.0273 = 13.66 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{500 - 13.66}{500} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 97.27\%}$$

**(iii) Half-life of degradation:**

$$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{app}}} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.12}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = 5.78 \; \text{min}}$$

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Fenton and Photo-Fenton processes. How does UV/solar irradiation enhance the degradation efficiency?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the operating parameters that affect the Fenton process. With a neat sketch, explain the components of a typical Photo-Fenton reactor.

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Fenton | Photo-Fenton |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| Light source | None | UV-A / Solar |
| Catalyst Fe²⁺ dose | 10–50 mg L⁻¹ | 2–10 mg L⁻¹ |
| Optimum pH | 2.5–3.5 | 3.0–5.0 |
| Reaction time | 60–120 min | 15–45 min |
| Sludge | High | Reduced 40–60% |
| Mineralization | 60–80% TOC | > 95% TOC |

**UV Enhancement Mechanism (3 enhancement pathways):**

1. **Fe³⁺ photoreduction:**
$$[\text{Fe(OH)}]^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$
This accelerates the rate-limiting step of the Fenton cycle.

2. **H₂O₂ direct photolysis:**
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2 \; {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

3. **Photo-decarboxylation of Fe³⁺-carboxylate complexes** when organic acids (oxalate, citrate) are present, yielding additional Fe²⁺ and CO₂.

**Overall effect:** The Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ catalytic cycle rate is **3–5× faster**, drastically improving TOC removal, reducing chemical consumption, and extending the operational pH window.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Key Operating Parameters:**

1. **pH (most critical):** Optimum 2.5–3.5. Outside this range, •OH yield collapses.
2. **H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ Molar Ratio:** Optimum 10:1–20:1. Excess H₂O₂ scavenges •OH; excess Fe²⁺ scavenges •OH.
3. **Temperature:** 25–40 °C. Higher temperatures accelerate H₂O₂ decomposition but reduce selectivity.
4. **Initial Pollutant Concentration:** Pseudo-first-order kinetics hold up to ~1000 mg L⁻¹; above this, •OH demand exceeds supply.
5. **Reaction Time:** 30–120 minutes typical; Photo-Fenton halves this.
6. **Agitation:** Adequate mixing (G = 500–1000 s⁻¹) to prevent mass-transfer limitations.

**Typical Photo-Fenton Reactor Components (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):**

The reactor train consists of: (1) **Equalization tank** for flow/load buffering, (2) **Acidification unit** with H₂SO₄ dosing, (3) **FeSO₄ dosing skid** with metering pump, (4) **H₂O₂ dosing system** (typically 30–50% w/v), (5) **Fenton CSTR** (residence time 15–45 min), (6) **Photochemical reactor** with immersed UV lamps (low-pressure mercury, λ = 254 nm) or compound parabolic collectors (CPC) for solar, (7) **Neutralization basin** with NaOH/Ca(OH)₂, (8) **Lamella clarifier** for Fe(OH)₃ sludge, (9) **Sand filter** for polishing, (10) **Sludge dewatering** via filter press.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting pH rationale (–2 marks):** Many students quote pH 2.5–3.5 but do not explain *why*. Always mention Fe(OH)₃ precipitation at high pH and H₃O₂⁺ stabilization at low pH.
> 2. **Confusing H₂O₂ role (–1 mark):** H₂O₂ is the *oxidant source* AND *•OH precursor*, not just a cleaning agent. It is consumed in the Fenton cycle.
> 3. **Wrong H₂O₂:Fe²⁺ ratio direction (–1 mark):** Excess of *either* reactant is harmful. Both excess Fe²⁺ (scavenges •OH) and excess H₂O₂ (forms HO₂• which is weaker) reduce efficiency.
> 4. **Neglecting units in kinetics (–2 marks):** Always write $k_{\text{app}}$ with units (min⁻¹ or s⁻¹) and convert time units consistently.
> 5. **Mixing Photo-Fenton and photocatalysis (–2 marks):** Photo-Fenton uses Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ catalyst and produces •OH in solution. Photocatalysis (TiO₂) uses solid semiconductor and is a *heterogeneous* AOP — completely different.
> 6. **Skipping the stoichiometric equation (–1 mark):** Always write the balanced Fenton equation as the first step of any numerical or descriptive answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- Fenton process is a **homogeneous AOP** using **Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂** at **pH 2.5–3.5**.
- The active oxidant is the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)** with **E° = +2.80 V**.
- Primary reaction: $\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$.
- Rate-limiting step is the **Fenton initiation**; $k = 63\text{–}76 \; \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$.
- Optimum **H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ ratio = 10:1 to 20:1** (molar).
- Optimum **Fe²⁺ dose = 5–50 mg L⁻¹**.
- Photo-Fenton uses **UV-A (λ ≤ 400 nm) or solar light** to photoreduce Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺, accelerating the cycle 3–5×.
- Photo-Fenton extends pH range to **3.0–5.0** and reduces sludge by **40–60%**.
- Pseudo-first-order kinetics: $\ln(C_t/C_0) = -k_{\text{app}} \cdot t$; half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k_{\text{app}}$.
- **Advantages:** Cheap reagents, simple equipment, no mass-transfer limitations, complete mineralization possible.
- **Limitations:** Strict acidic pH, iron sludge, H₂O₂ handling hazard, scavenging at high reagent doses.
- **Key Applications:** Landfill leachate, textile dyes, pharmaceutical residues, olive mill wastewater, ISCO soil remediation, sludge conditioning.
- The hydroxyl radical is **non-selective** — this is both an advantage (broad spectrum) and a limitation (scavenging by matrix constituents like carbonates, NOM).
- Total Organic Carbon (TOC) removal, not just decolorization, is the **true indicator** of Fenton performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Photo Catalytic Treatment Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solar Photo Catalytic Treatment Systems # ☀️ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solar Photocatalytic Treatment System (SPTS)** is an **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** in which a semiconductor photocatalyst — most commonly **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂)** — is activated by **solar photons** (primarily the UV-A component, 315–400 nm) to generate highly reactive transitory species, primarily the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)**, which non-selectively mineralizes refractory organic pollutants, pathogens, and emerging contaminants in wastewater into CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids. The system is formally classified under **Heterogeneous Photocatalysis**, in which the catalyst exists in a different phase (solid) from the reactants (aqueous). The "solar" prefix denotes that the photon source is the sun, making the process **renewable, low-carbon, and zero-electricity** at the point of photon generation — a critical advantage over conventional UV-lamp-driven photocatalysis. ## 1.2 The Three Pillars of a Solar Photocatalytic System | Pillar | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | **Photon Source** | Sunlight (UV-A 3–5 % of total) | Supplies energy ≥ band-gap of catalyst | | **Photocatalyst** | TiO₂ (anatase), ZnO, WO₃, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄ | Absorbs photons, generates e⁻/h⁺ pairs | | **Oxidant** | Dissolved O₂, H₂O, OH⁻ | Scavenges charge carriers, forms •OH | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun-Powered Pac-Man" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine the photocatalyst particle (TiO₂) as a tiny **"Pac-Man"** that is *asleep* in the dark. When a **sunbeam** (UV photon) hits it, the Pac-Man "wakes up" — it splits its internal energy into two hungry mouths: a **positive hole (h⁺)** and an **excited electron (e⁻)**. These mouths then **chew up water and oxygen** floating around them to spit out extremely reactive **"biting saliva"** — the **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**. These radicals are so aggressive that they rip apart the molecular structure of pollutants (dyes, pesticides, pharmaceutical residues) one bond at a time, converting them into harmless CO₂ and water — just like Pac-Man finishing every ghost in the maze. ## 1.4 Why Solar Photocatalysis? (Syllabus Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > - It is the **only AOP that uses freely available solar energy**, eliminating electrical UV-lamp costs (≈ 70–80 % of operating cost in conventional photocatalysis). > - It can operate at **ambient temperature and pressure**. > - Theoretically, **complete mineralization** (no secondary waste) is possible. > - Effective against **biorefractory compounds** that resist conventional biological treatment (Cephalosporins, Carbamazepine, Endocrine Disruptors, Azo dyes, etc.). ## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used - **Planck's Constant:** *h* = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** - **Speed of Light:** *c* = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** - **Anatase TiO₂ Band-Gap Energy:** *Eg* = **3.2 eV** (λ ≤ 387 nm, UV-A region) - **Rutile TiO₂ Band-Gap Energy:** *Eg* = **3.0 eV** (λ ≤ 413 nm) - **Standard Solar Constant (AM 1.5):** *I₀* = **1367 W/m²** - **UV-A Fraction in Sunlight:** ≈ **3–5 %** of total terrestrial solar flux - **Hydroxyl Radical Reduction Potential:** *E°(•OH/H₂O)* = **+2.85 V** vs NHE (the most powerful aqueous oxidant after fluorine). # 🧪 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six-Step Photocatalytic Mechanism (The "Heart" of the Topic) > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner loves asking the **six-step mechanism** — memorize it in the order: **Absorption → Excitation → Separation → Trapping → Radical Formation → Mineralization.** ### Step 1 — Photon Absorption The semiconductor absorbs a photon of energy **hν ≥ Eg**. Photons of lower energy pass through without effect (this is why visible-light-only catalysts like WO₃ or BiVO₄ with narrower band gaps are being researched). ### Step 2 — Electron–Hole Pair Generation The absorbed photon promotes an electron from the **Valence Band (VB)** to the **Conduction Band (CB)**, leaving a positive vacancy (hole) in the VB. ### Step 3 — Charge Carrier Separation and Migration The e⁻/h⁺ pair must migrate to the catalyst surface **before** they recombine. Recombination releases heat and wastes the photon energy. Doping the catalyst (e.g., with N, Fe, C) reduces recombination rates. ### Step 4 — Surface Trapping and Adsorption Simultaneously, organic pollutant molecules and water/oxygen adsorb on the catalyst surface — a prerequisite for reaction. ### Step 5 — Radical Formation (Oxidation / Reduction at Surface) The holes oxidize water to hydroxyl radicals; the electrons reduce adsorbed oxygen to superoxide radicals, which cascade into more •OH. ### Step 6 — Pollutant Mineralization The •OH radicals (and secondarily O₂•⁻, HO₂•) attack organic molecules via **hydrogen abstraction, electrophilic addition, and electron transfer**, eventually yielding **CO₂ + H₂O + mineral acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, H₃PO₄).** ## 2.2 Complete Chemical Reaction Set (Board-Exam Ready) $$TiO_2 + h\nu \;\longrightarrow\; e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$ $$h^+_{VB} + H_2O_{(ads)} \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH + H^+$$ $$h^+_{VB} + OH^-_{(ads)} \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH$$ $$e^-_{CB} + O_{2(ads)} \;\longrightarrow\; O_2^{\cdot-}$$ $$O_2^{\cdot-} + H^+ \;\longrightarrow\; HO_2^{\cdot}$$ $$2\,HO_2^{\cdot} \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O_2 + O_2$$ $$H_2O_2 + e^-_{CB} \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH + OH^-$$ > [!TIP] > The **ultimate non-selective oxidant** is •OH. The **ultimate electron sink** is dissolved O₂ — without O₂, e⁻ accumulates and recombination skyrockets, killing efficiency. **Always aerate solar photoreactors.** ## 2.3 Governing Kinetic Model — Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L-H) Kinetics For heterogeneous photocatalysis, the rate of pollutant disappearance is conventionally fitted to the modified L-H equation: $$r = -\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{k_r K C}{1 + K C}$$ Where: - *r* = rate of substrate degradation (mg L⁻¹ min⁻¹) - *C* = substrate concentration at time *t* (mg L⁻¹) - *k_r* = intrinsic reaction rate constant (mg L⁻¹ min⁻¹) - *K* = adsorption equilibrium constant (L mg⁻¹) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dilute Solution Limit (KC ≪ 1):** This is the regime typical of environmental wastewater treatment (µg/L to low mg/L). The denominator collapses to unity, and the equation reduces to a **pseudo-first-order** form — the form most often tested in KTU problems. Integrating under the dilute limit: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k_{app}\, t$$ Where *k_app* (min⁻¹) is the **apparent pseudo-first-order rate constant**, obtained from the slope of ln(C₀/C) vs *t*. ## 2.4 Quantum Yield / Photonic Efficiency $$\Phi = \frac{\text{moles of substrate degraded (or } \cdot OH \text{ formed)}}{\text{moles of photons absorbed by the catalyst}}$$ Solar-specific variant — **Photochemical Yield (ξ)**: $$\xi = \frac{r \cdot V}{I_{UVA} \cdot A}$$ Where: - *I_UVA* = incident UV-A irradiance on collector (W m⁻²) - *A* = illuminated collector area (m²) - *V* = reactor liquid volume (L) ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Significance / KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{max}}$ | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda_{max}$ in nm | Determines minimum photon wavelength for catalyst activation | | 2 | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g}$ (nm) | $E_g$ in eV | Quick numerical substitution for TiO₂: $\lambda_{max} \approx 387$ nm | | 3 | $r = \dfrac{k_r K C}{1 + K C}$ | $r$ = mg L⁻¹ min⁻¹, $K$ = L mg⁻¹ | Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate | | 4 | $\ln(C_0/C) = k_{app}\,t$ | $k_{app}$ in min⁻¹ or h⁻¹ | Pseudo-first-order kinetics (board exam favourite) | | 5 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{app}}$ | min | Half-life of pollutant | | 6 | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{mol degraded}}{\text{mol photon absorbed}}$ | dimensionless | Quantum efficiency | | 7 | $\xi = \dfrac{rV}{I_{UVA} A}$ | dimensionless | Solar photochemical yield | | 8 | $\text{LDR} = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{V_{tank}}$ | dimensionless | Local Dilution Ratio for CPC reactors | | 9 | $\eta_{opt} = 1 - 10^{-A_\lambda}$ | $A_\lambda$ = absorbance at λ | Fraction of photons absorbed by suspension | | 10 | $E_{EO} = \dfrac{P\, t}{V\, \log(C_0/C)}$ | kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹ | Electric-Energy-per-Order metric (for hybrid systems) | ## 2.6 Factors Affecting Solar Photocatalytic Efficiency (Frequently Asked) 1. **Catalyst Loading** — Optimum exists (typically 0.1–2.0 g/L for TiO₂). Above it, suspension becomes opaque, blocking photons. 2. **pH** — Affects catalyst surface charge (pHpzc of TiO₂ ≈ 6.25) and pollutant ionization. 3. **Initial Pollutant Concentration** — Higher C₀ → higher rate until saturation of active sites. 4. **Light Intensity (I)** — At low I, rate ∝ I (unimolecular recombination). At high I, rate ∝ √I (bimolecular recombination). 5. **Dissolved Oxygen** — Essential electron acceptor; pure N₂ sparging nearly halts the reaction. 6. **Temperature** — Mild effect (apparent activation energy ≈ 5–20 kJ/mol), confirming a **photonic, not thermal**, process. 7. **Water Matrix** — Carbonates, bicarbonates, humic acids scavenge •OH (negative effect); chlorides, sulfates milder. # 📐 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — From L-H Equation to Pseudo-First-Order Form (with full algebra) The Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression for heterogeneous photocatalysis is: $$r = -\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{k_r K C}{1 + K C}$$ **Step 1 — Identify the dilute-solution limit.** In typical wastewater scenarios, the pollutant concentration is low (e.g., pharmaceutical residues in the µg/L range), so that **KC ≪ 1**. Hence the term *KC* in the denominator becomes negligible compared to 1. **Step 2 — Apply the limit.** $$\frac{k_r K C}{1 + K C} \;\approx\; \frac{k_r K C}{1} = k_r K C$$ Define a new lumped constant: $$k_{app} = k_r K \quad \text{(units: min}^{-1}\text{)}$$ **Step 3 — Rewrite the differential equation.** $$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_{app} C$$ **Step 4 — Separate variables.** $$-\frac{dC}{C} = k_{app}\, dt$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from t = 0 (C = C₀) to t = t (C = C).** $$\int_{C_0}^{C} -\frac{dC}{C} = \int_{0}^{t} k_{app}\, dt$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate both integrals.** $$\left[-\ln C\right]_{C_0}^{C} = k_{app}\, t$$ $$-\ln C - (-\ln C_0) = k_{app}\, t$$ $$\ln C_0 - \ln C = k_{app}\, t$$ **Step 7 — Final form (the classic KTU board equation).** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k_{app}\, t$$ **Step 8 — Express in terms of percentage removal R%.** Since $C = C_0 (1 - R/100)$, we get: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - R/100}\right) = k_{app}\, t$$ This equation allows **direct comparison of photocatalyst performance** from experimental C vs t data. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Wavelength for TiO₂ Activation Given the anatase TiO₂ band-gap energy Eg = 3.2 eV, derive the maximum wavelength of solar radiation that can activate the catalyst. **Step 1 — Energy of a photon in Joules.** $$E = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Convert the band-gap energy from eV to Joules.** 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J $$E_g = 3.2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 5.126 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{J}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for λ.** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{h c}{E_g}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s})(3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s})}{5.126 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}}$$ **Step 5 — Carry out the division.** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.126 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\lambda_{max} = 3.877 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to nanometres.** $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 387\;\text{nm (UV-A region)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Only the UV-A component of solar radiation (≈ 3–5 % of total solar flux) can activate anatase TiO₂. This is the **fundamental engineering limitation** that drives research into visible-light-active (VLA) photocatalysts such as N-doped TiO₂, BiVO₄, g-C₃N₄, and Ag/AgCl plasmonic systems. ## 3.3 Derivation III — Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A solar photocatalytic reactor using Degussa P-25 TiO₂ (1.0 g/L) is used to degrade a dye **C** at initial concentration C₀ = 50 mg/L. The pseudo-first-order rate constant, determined under midday solar irradiance of 28 W/m² (UV-A), is k_app = 0.025 min⁻¹. Calculate: > (a) the time required for 90 % degradation of the dye. > (b) the half-life t₁/₂ of the dye. > (c) the concentration remaining after 2 hours. **Solution (a) — Time for 90 % degradation:** For 90 % removal, C = 0.10 C₀, so C₀/C = 10. Using the pseudo-first-order equation: $$t_{90\%} = \frac{\ln(C_0/C)}{k_{app}} = \frac{\ln(10)}{0.025}$$ $$t_{90\%} = \frac{2.3026}{0.025} = 92.1\;\text{min} \approx 1.53\;\text{h}$$ **Solution (b) — Half-life:** For 50 % removal, C₀/C = 2. $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{app}} = \frac{0.6931}{0.025} = 27.7\;\text{min}$$ **Solution (c) — Concentration after 2 hours:** t = 2 h = 120 min. $$C = C_0 \exp(-k_{app}\, t) = 50 \exp(-0.025 \times 120)$$ $$C = 50 \exp(-3.0) = 50 \times 0.04979 = 2.49\;\text{mg/L}$$ This corresponds to ≈ 95 % removal. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Photocatalyst Materials | Property | TiO₂ (Anatase) | ZnO | WO₃ | Fe₂O₃ (Hematite) | g-C₃N₄ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Band Gap (eV) | 3.2 | 3.37 | 2.7 | 2.1 | 2.7 | | λ activation (nm) | ≤ 387 (UV-A) | ≤ 368 (UV-A) | ≤ 459 (visible) | ≤ 590 (visible) | ≤ 459 (visible) | | Solar Photon Use | Poor (3–5 %) | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good | | Chemical Stability | Excellent | Poor (photocorrodes) | Good | Good | Excellent | | Cost | Low | Low | Moderate | Very Low | Very Low | | Toxicity | None | Moderate | Low | None | None | | Typical Use | Reference catalyst | Lab research | Solar H₂ & organics | Visible light, low cost | Emerging contaminant removal | # 🏗️ 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Solar Photocatalytic Treatment System. List any four advantages of using TiO₂ as a photocatalyst in solar wastewater treatment. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A Solar Photocatalytic Treatment System is a heterogeneous Advanced Oxidation Process in which a semiconductor photocatalyst (typically TiO₂ in the anatase form) is activated by solar UV-A photons (λ ≤ 387 nm) to generate electron-hole pairs that ultimately produce highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which non-selectively oxidize refractory organic pollutants in wastewater to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids. **Four advantages of TiO₂:** 1. **Strong oxidative power** — Valence-band hole potential (+2.7 V vs NHE) drives near-complete mineralization. 2. **Chemical and biological inertness** — Non-toxic, non-corrosive, safe for potable-water applications. 3. **Photostability** — Does not photocorrode (unlike ZnO or CdS); reusable for thousands of cycles. 4. **Low cost and high availability** — Commercially available as Degussa P-25; cheap white pigment used in paints. ### Question 2: Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous photocatalysis. Give one example of each relevant to solar wastewater treatment. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Homogeneous Photocatalysis | Heterogeneous Photocatalysis | |---|---|---| | **Phase of catalyst** | Same phase as reactants (dissolved ions) | Different phase (solid catalyst + aqueous reactants) | | **Catalyst** | Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ (Photo-Fenton), [Fe(OH)]²⁺ | TiO₂, ZnO, WO₃ | | **Working pH** | Acidic (pH 2.5–4) required | Wide range; near-neutral optimum | | **Catalyst recovery** | Difficult (forms sludge) | Easy (settling, filtration) | | **Light absorption** | Throughout liquid volume | Only at catalyst particle surface | | **Solar suitability** | Moderate (UV-A absorption by Fe³⁺-complex) | High (immobilized or slurry TiO₂) | | **Example** | Photo-Fenton (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ + hν) | TiO₂/UV-A slurry or CPC reactor | ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not write the six-step mechanism without chemical equations** — students lose 2 marks by giving only the verbal description. > 2. **Always state the band-gap value and the maximum wavelength** when discussing TiO₂; examiners specifically check Eg = 3.2 eV and λ = 387 nm. > 3. **In numerical problems, write units at every step** — missing units = −0.5 mark per equation. > 4. **Don't confuse Langmuir–Hinshelwood with simple first-order kinetics** — explicitly justify the dilute-solution limit before reducing the equation. > 5. **Mention dissolved O₂ as the electron sink** in any mechanism-based answer — this is the most-skipped "must-have" step. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **six-step mechanism of heterogeneous photocatalysis** for organic pollutant degradation using solar-irradiated TiO₂. Write the relevant chemical equations and highlight the role of dissolved oxygen. **(b) [7 Marks]** A solar photocatalytic batch reactor was used to degrade **Methyl Orange dye** using 1.5 g/L TiO₂ P-25. The pseudo-first-order rate constant under midday solar irradiance was determined to be **k_app = 0.038 min⁻¹**. For an initial dye concentration of 80 mg/L, calculate: (i) Time required for 95 % decolourization. (ii) The half-life (t₁/₂) of the dye. (iii) The dye concentration after 3 hours. (iv) Comment on the suitability of pseudo-first-order kinetics in solar photocatalysis. --- **Model Solution to A(a):** The photocatalytic degradation proceeds through six sequential steps: **Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** A photon of UV-A solar radiation with energy hν ≥ Eg (3.2 eV for anatase TiO₂; λ ≤ 387 nm) is absorbed by a TiO₂ particle. **Step 2 — Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** $$TiO_2 + h\nu \;\longrightarrow\; e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$ **Step 3 — Charge Separation and Migration to Surface:** The photogenerated e⁻ and h⁺ must diffuse to the catalyst surface within their lifetimes (typically nanoseconds for TiO₂) to participate in redox reactions; otherwise, they recombine, releasing heat. **Step 4 — Surface Adsorption:** Pollutant molecules (R), H₂O, OH⁻, and O₂ adsorb on the catalyst surface — a prerequisite for surface-mediated reactions. **Step 5 — Free-Radical Formation:** The **holes** oxidize adsorbed water / hydroxide: $$h^+_{VB} + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH + H^+$$ $$h^+_{VB} + OH^- \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH$$ The **electrons** reduce adsorbed **dissolved oxygen** (the critical electron sink): $$e^-_{CB} + O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; O_2^{\cdot-}$$ $$O_2^{\cdot-} + H^+ \;\longrightarrow\; HO_2^{\cdot}$$ $$2\,HO_2^{\cdot} \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O_2 + O_2$$ $$H_2O_2 + e^-_{CB} \;\longrightarrow\; \cdot OH + OH^-$$ **Step 6 — Mineralization of the Pollutant (R):** $$R + \cdot OH \;\longrightarrow\; R^{\cdot} + H_2O$$ $$R^{\cdot} + O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; ROO^{\cdot} \;\xrightarrow{\cdot OH}\;\text{intermediates}\;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 + H_2O + \text{mineral acids}$$ > **Role of Dissolved O₂:** It scavenges CB electrons, suppressing e⁻/h⁺ recombination and continuously generating additional reactive oxygen species (O₂•⁻, HO₂•, H₂O₂, and •OH). Without O₂, the reaction is almost completely quenched. --- **Model Solution to A(b):** Given: k_app = 0.038 min⁻¹, C₀ = 80 mg/L, kinetics is pseudo-first-order. **(i) Time for 95 % degradation:** For 95 % removal, C = 0.05 C₀ → C₀/C = 20. $$t_{95\%} = \frac{\ln(C_0/C)}{k_{app}} = \frac{\ln(20)}{0.038}$$ $$t_{95\%} = \frac{2.9957}{0.038} = 78.8\;\text{min} \approx 1.31\;\text{h}$$ **(ii) Half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{app}} = \frac{0.6931}{0.038} = 18.24\;\text{min}$$ **(iii) Concentration after 3 hours:** t = 3 h = 180 min. $$C = C_0 \exp(-k_{app}\, t) = 80 \exp(-0.038 \times 180)$$ $$C = 80 \exp(-6.84) = 80 \times 1.064 \times 10^{-3} = 0.085\;\text{mg/L}$$ Removal = (1 − 0.085/80) × 100 ≈ 99.89 %. **(iv) Comment on Suitability:** Pseudo-first-order kinetics is highly suitable for dilute wastewater systems (µg/L to low mg/L) where the substrate concentration is too low to saturate the catalyst's active sites (KC ≪ 1). It offers (a) simple linear regression on ln(C₀/C) vs t plots, (b) direct extraction of k_app as a single performance metric, and (c) easy comparison between different photocatalysts and operating conditions. However, at very high C₀ where active sites approach saturation, the full Langmuir–Hinshelwood equation must be used. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **design and working principle of a Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC) solar photocatalytic reactor**. Compare it with a flat-plate reactor and a slurry Thin-Film Fixed-Bed (TFFB) reactor. List any two real-world field applications of solar photocatalysis. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression** for heterogeneous photocatalysis. Show clearly how the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation, **ln(C₀/C) = k_app · t**, is obtained as a limiting case. State the practical significance of the rate constant k_app in reactor design. --- **Model Solution to B(a):** **Working Principle of CPC Reactor:** A Compound Parabolic Collector consists of two adjacent parabolic reflectors of low-concentration ratio (typically 1–5 suns) oriented such that incoming solar radiation (both direct and diffuse UV-A) is reflected onto a transparent borosilicate glass tube through which the TiO₂ aqueous suspension flows. The non-imaging (compound) parabolic geometry ensures high collection efficiency for both direct normal and diffuse radiation — a major advantage in cloudy conditions. The reactor tube is mounted at the focus of the compound-parabolic trough. Residence times of 30–120 minutes are typical, and the suspension is continuously recirculated through an aerated holding tank that also acts as the catalyst settling/recirculation reservoir. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | CPC Reactor | Flat-Plate Reactor | TFFB (Thin-Film Fixed-Bed) | |---|---|---|---| | **Concentration ratio** | 1–5 suns | 1 sun | 1 sun | | **Acceptance angle** | Wide (captures diffuse light) | Limited (mostly direct) | Limited | | **Catalyst form** | Slurry (with recovery) | Immobilized or slurry | Immobilized on support | | **Catalyst recovery** | Required (settler) | Not required | Not required | | **Volumetric efficiency** | High (long light path) | Moderate | Low | | **Optical losses** | Moderate | Low | Low | | **Best application** | Mid-to-large scale WWTPs | Lab/pilot scale | Small decentralized units | **Two Real-World Field Applications:** 1. **The Solar Detoxification Plant at the Plataforma Solar de Almería, Spain** — A 100 m² CPC field treating pesticide-contaminated agricultural wastewater at 250–1000 L batches with >95 % TOC removal. 2. **Treatment of pharmaceutical residues in hospital wastewater** — Pilot CPC reactors (3–10 m²) in Germany and France have shown >90 % removal of carbamazepine, diclofenac, and sulfamethoxazole under real solar exposure. --- **Model Solution to B(b):** **Derivation of Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate Expression:** **Assumption 1 — Adsorption Equilibration:** Pollutant (S) adsorbs on a catalyst surface site (*) at equilibrium, following the Langmuir isotherm: $$S + * \;\rightleftharpoons\; S^* ; \quad K = \frac{[S^*]}{[S][*]}$$ where K is the equilibrium adsorption constant (L mg⁻¹). **Assumption 2 — Surface Reaction as Rate-Limiting Step:** Only the adsorbed species reacts, with intrinsic rate constant k_r. **Step 1 — Fractional surface coverage (θ):** $$\theta = \frac{K C}{1 + K C}$$ where C is the bulk substrate concentration (mg L⁻¹). **Step 2 — Reaction rate (proportional to surface coverage):** $$r = k_r \theta = \frac{k_r K C}{1 + K C}$$ This is the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L-H) rate law** for heterogeneous photocatalysis. **Step 3 — Pseudo-First-Order Limit:** For dilute wastewater (KC ≪ 1): $$\frac{k_r K C}{1 + K C} \approx k_r K C = k_{app} C$$ Therefore: $$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_{app} C$$ **Step 4 — Integration:** $$-\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = \int_0^{t} k_{app}\, dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k_{app}\, t$$ **Step 5 — Half-life in this limit:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{app}}$$ **Practical Significance of k_app in Reactor Design:** - **Scale-up metric:** k_app (min⁻¹ or h⁻¹), obtained from bench-scale solar experiments, is the key input for sizing full-scale CPC field area and residence time. - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) design:** For a target removal of (1 − C/C₀) × 100, the required HRT in a continuous stirred-tank photoreactor is **HRT = −ln(1 − R/100) / k_app**. - **Catalyst comparison:** k_app allows direct ranking of photocatalysts under identical solar conditions. - **Solar variability compensation:** k_app is correlated with local UV-A irradiance, allowing empirical correlations of the form k_app = β · I_UVA^α, where α ranges from 0.5 to 1.0 depending on recombination regime — essential for year-round design in tropical Kerala conditions. --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Top 15 Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 ESE on Solar Photocatalytic Treatment Systems:** 1. **Solar Photocatalysis is a Heterogeneous AOP** using a **solid semiconductor catalyst** (TiO₂ most often) and **solar UV-A photons**. 2. **Anatase TiO₂:** Eg = **3.2 eV** → λ_max = **387 nm** (UV-A); only **3–5 % of sunlight** is usable. 3. **Six-step mechanism:** Absorption → Excitation → Separation → Surface trapping → Radical formation → Mineralization. 4. **Core reaction:** TiO₂ + hν → e⁻_CB + h⁺_VB. 5. **•OH radical** is the principal oxidant; reduction potential = **+2.85 V vs NHE** (strongest practical aqueous oxidant). 6. **Dissolved O₂ is the electron sink** — without it, e⁻/h⁺ recombination destroys efficiency. 7. **L-H kinetics** reduce to **pseudo-first-order** when KC ≪ 1 (typical of environmental concentrations). 8. **Standard equation:** **ln(C₀/C) = k_app · t** and **t₁/₂ = 0.6931/k_app**. 9. **CPC (Compound Parabolic Collector)** is the most efficient large-scale solar photoreactor — captures diffuse + direct light. 10. **Quantum yield (Φ)** and **photochemical yield (ξ)** quantify photon-to-pollutant efficiency. 11. **Optimum TiO₂ loading:** 0.1–2.0 g/L; above it, suspension opacity reduces light penetration. 12. **Visible-light-active catalysts** (WO₃, Fe₂O₃, N-doped TiO₂, g-C₃N₄) are an active research frontier to overcome the UV-A limitation. 13. **Process is photonic, not thermal** — apparent activation energy is low (5–20 kJ/mol); ambient temperature operation. 14. **Common applications:** azo dye decolourization, pharmaceutical residue removal, pesticide detoxification, E. coli disinfection, Cr(VI) reduction, cyanide oxidation. 15. **For Kerala tropical climate:** UV-A irradiance of 25–35 W/m² at midday supports high k_app values, making solar photocatalysis particularly attractive for the state's industrial and hospital wastewater streams.
